questions.study_unit_name: NTSB Part 830, FAR Part 1, FAR Part 61, FAR Part 117, FAR Part 119
questions.question_text: Which of the following meets the requirements of a “serious injury” as defined by the NTSB?
questions.choice_a: Asimple fracture of the nose or other extremity.
questions.choice_b: An injury which caused severe tendon amage.
questions.choice_c: First-degree burns over 5 percent of the body.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Serious injury means any injury that (1) requires hospitalization for more than 48 hr., commencing within 7 days from the date the injury was received; (2) results in a fracture in any bone (except simple fractures of the fingers, toes, or nose); (3) causes severe hemorrhages, nerve, muscle, or tendon damage; (4) involves any internal organ; or (5) involves second- or third-degree burns, or any burns covering more than 5% of the body surface. Answer (A) is incorrect. Simple fractures, such as of the finger, toe, or nose, are not considered a serious injury. Answer (C) is incorrect. Only second- and third-degree burns or first-degree burns over more than 5%, not just 5%, of the body are defined as a serious injury. First-degree burns are less serious than second- and third-degree burns. '

questions.study_unit_name: NTSB Part 830, FAR Part 1, FAR Part 61, FAR Part 117, FAR Part 119
questions.question_text: What period of time must a person be hospitalized before an injury may be defined by the NTSB as a “serious injury”?
questions.choice_a: 72 hours; commencing within 10 days after date of injury.
questions.choice_b: 48 hours; commencing within 7 days after date of the injury.
questions.choice_c: 10 days, with no other extenuating circumstances.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Serious injury means any injury that requires hospitalization for more than 48 hr., commencing within 7 days from the date the injury was received. Answer (A) is incorrect. A time frame requirement of 72 hr. is not defined in NTSB Part 830. Answer (C) is incorrect. Ten days is the amount of time required to file a report to the NTSB after an accident. ‘

questions.study_unit_name: NTSB Part 830, FAR Part 1, FAR Part 61, FAR Part 117, FAR Part 119
questions.question_text: Which of the following constitutes “substantial damage’ according to NTSB Part 830?
questions.choice_a: Ground damage to landing gear, wheels, or tires.
questions.choice_b: Damage to wingtips (or rotor blades, in the case of a helicopter).
questions.choice_c: Failure of ac mponsniyabighgvould. adversely: the performance, ic equire replacement.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Substantial damage means damage or failure that adversely affects the structural strength, performance, or flight characteristics of the aircraft and requires major repair or replacement of the affected component. Answer (A) is incorrect. Ground damage to landing gear, wheels, or tires is not considered “substantial damage” for the purpose of NTSB Part 830. Answer (B) is incorrect. Damage to wingtips (or rotor blades, in the case of a helicopter) is not considered “substantial damage” for the purpose of NTSB Part 830.

questions.study_unit_name: NTSB Part 830, FAR Part 1, FAR Part 61, FAR Part 117, FAR Part 119
questions.question_text: Which incident requires an immediate notification to NTSB?
questions.choice_c: Damage to property, other than the aircraft, estimated to exceed $10,000.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: The NTSB must be notified immediately and by the most expeditious means possible when an aircraft accident or any of various listed incidents occurs or when an aircraft is overdue and believed to have been in an accident. The following are considered incidents: 1. Flight control system malfunction or failure 2. Inability of any required flight crewmember to perform normal flight duties as a result of injury or illness 3. Failure of structural components of a turbine engine, excluding compressor and turbine blades and vanes 4. In-flight fire 5. Aircraft collision in flight Answer (A) is incorrect. A ground collision does not require immediate notification. Answer (C) is incorrect. Immediate notification is required for damage to property of more than $25,000, not $10,000.

questions.study_unit_name: NTSB Part 830, FAR Part 1, FAR Part 61, FAR Part 117, FAR Part 119
questions.question_text: Within what time period should the nearest NTSB field office be notified when an aircraft is involved in an accident which results in substantial damage?
questions.choice_c: 10 days.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: The NTSB must be notified immediately and by the most expeditious means possible when an aircraft accident, which results in substantial damage, or any of various listed incidents occurs or when an aircraft is overdue and believed to have been in an accident. Answer (B) is incorrect. A written report, not notification, is required after 7 days if an overdue aircraft is still missing, not after an aircraft is involved in an accident that results in substantial damage. Answer (C) is incorrect. A written accident report, not notification, is required within 10 days.

questions.study_unit_name: NTSB Part 830, FAR Part 1, FAR Part 61, FAR Part 117, FAR Part 119
questions.question_text: Within how many days must the operator of an aircraft involved in an accident file a report to the NTSB?
questions.choice_c: 10 days.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: The operator of an aircraft shall file a report on NTSB Form 6120.1 or Form 7120.2 within 10 days after an accident. Answer (A) is incorrect. A written report to the NTSB is required within 10 days, not 3 days, after an accident. Answer (B) is incorrect. A written report to the NTSB is required within 7 days for an overdue aircraft that is still missing, not for an aircraft involved in an accident.

questions.study_unit_name: NTSB Part 830, FAR Part 1, FAR Part 61, FAR Part 117, FAR Part 119
questions.question_text: When is an operator of an aircraft, which has been involved in an incident, required to submit a report to the nearest field office of the NTSB?
questions.choice_c: Only if requested to do so by the NTSB.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: The operator of an aircraft shall file a report on NTSB Form 6120.1 or Form 7120.2 on an incident for which notification is required only if requested to do so by the NTSB. Answer (A) is incorrect. A wriften report to the NTSB is required within 7 days for an overdue aircraft that is still missing, not for an incident. Answer (B) is incorrect. A written report to the NTSB is required within 10 days of an accident, not an incident, involving an aircraft.

questions.study_unit_name: NTSB Part 830, FAR Part 1, FAR Part 61, FAR Part 117, FAR Part 119
questions.question_text: What is the name of an area beyond the end of a runway which does not contain obstructions and can be considered when calculating takeoff performance of turbine-powered aircraft?
questions.choice_c: Obstruction clearance plane.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: A clearway is an area beyond the runway, not less than 500 ft. wide, centrally located about the extended centerline of the runway and under the control of the airport authorities. The clearway is expressed in terms of a clearway plane, extending from the end of the runway with an upward slope not exceeding 1.25%, above which no object or terrain protrudes. However, threshold lights may protrude above the plane if their height above the end of the runway is 26 in. or less and if they are located on each side of the runway. Answer (B) is incorrect. A stopway is an area beyond the takeoff runway, no less wide than the runway and centered upon the extended centerline of the runway, able to support the airplane during an aborted takeoff. Answer (C) is incorrect. An obstruction clearance plane is used in calculating the effective length of a runway for landing limitations, not for calculating takeoff performance in a turbine-powered aircraft.

questions.study_unit_name: NTSB Part 830, FAR Part 1, FAR Part 61, FAR Part 117, FAR Part 119
questions.question_text: “Operational control” of a flight refers to
questions.choice_a: the specific duties of any required crewmember. 'B. exercising authority over initiating, conducting, mmninathd’a night
questions.choice_c: exercising the privileges of pilot in command of an aircraft.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Operational control of a flight refers to the” exercise of authority over initiating, conducting, or terminating a flight. Answer (A) is incorrect. Crewmember refers to any person assigned to perform duty in an aircraft during flight time, which includes cabin crew as well as cockpit crew. Answer (C) is incorrect. Pilot in command refers to the pilot responsible for the operation and safety of an aircraft during flight time.

questions.study_unit_name: NTSB Part 830, FAR Part 1, FAR Part 61, FAR Part 117, FAR Part 119
questions.question_text: Which is a definition of the term “crewmember”?
questions.choice_a: Only a pilot, flight engineer, or flight navigator assigned to duty in an aircraft during flight time. ssigned to perform duty in an re
questions.choice_c: Any person assigned to duty in an aircraft during flight except a pilot or flight engineer.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: A crewmember is a person assigned to ~ perform duty in an aircraft during flight time. Answer (A) is incorrect. Only a pilot, flight engineer, or flight navigator assigned to duty in an aircraft during flight time is the definition of the term “flight crewmember,” not “crewmember.” Answer (C) is incorrect. The term “crewmember” includes the flight (or cockpit) crew as well as cabin crew.

questions.study_unit_name: NTSB Part 830, FAR Part 1, FAR Part 61, FAR Part 117, FAR Part 119
questions.question_text: What is an area identified by the term “stopway”?
questions.choice_a: An area, at least the same width as the runway, capable of supporting an airplane during a normal takeoff.
questions.choice_b: "spied for use in decelerating an iborted takeoff.
questions.choice_c: An area, not as wide as the runway, capable of supporting an airplane during a normal takeoff.
questions.explanation: ; DISCUSSION: A stopway is an area beyond the takeoff runway, no less wide than the runway and centered upon the extended centerline of the runway, able to support the airplane during an aborted takeoff without causing structural damage to the airplane. Answer (A) is incorrect. An area, at least the same width as the runway, capable of supporting an airplane during a normal takeoff describes the nonlanding portion of a runway, which is before a displaced threshold and may be suitable for taxiing, landing rollout, and takeoff of aircraft. Answer (C) is incorrect. An area, not as wide as the runway, capable of supporting an airplane during a normal takeoff describes an area that exists before a displaced threshold.

questions.study_unit_name: NTSB Part 830, FAR Part 1, FAR Part 61, FAR Part 117, FAR Part 119
questions.question_text: Which is the correct symbol for design cruising speed? OA. Vou
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: V, is design cruising speed. Design cruising speed is established by the manufacturer and is used to define the flight envelope parameters in the aircraft certification. Answer (B) is incorrect. Vg is the stalling speed, or minimum steady flight speed, at which the airplane is controllable. Answer (C) is incorrect. Va is design maneuvering speed.

questions.study_unit_name: NTSB Part 830, FAR Part 1, FAR Part 61, FAR Part 117, FAR Part 119
questions.question_text: What is V2 speed?
questions.choice_a: Takeoff decision speed. Bo Takec ff fety speed
questions.choice_c: Minimum takeoff speed.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: \V, is takeoff safety speed. Takeoff safety — speed means a referenced airspeed obtained after liftoff at which the required one-engine-inoperative climb performance can be achieved. a Answer (A) is incorrect. V;, not V2, means takeoff decision speed. Answer (C) is incorrect. Vain, Not V>, is minimum takeoff safety speed.

questions.study_unit_name: NTSB Part 830, FAR Part 1, FAR Part 61, FAR Part 117, FAR Part 119
questions.question_text: Which speed symbol indicates the maximum operating limit speed for an airplane? Mie Be Mul Mino. GiiVigiMvo.
questions.explanation: ' DISCUSSION: Vyo/Mmo is maximum operating limit speed. Maximum operating limit speed is also known as maximum Mach number, which is analogous to Vy_ of a small airplane. Answer (A) is incorrect. Vie is maximum landing gear extended speed. Answer (C) is incorrect. V.o is the maximum speed for operating the landing gear.

questions.study_unit_name: NTSB Part 830, FAR Part 1, FAR Part 61, FAR Part 117, FAR Part 119
questions.question_text: What is the correct symbol for minimum unstick speed? A Vin:
questions.choice_c: Vec.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Vyy is minimum unstick speed. Minimum unstick speed is the minimum airspeed at which the airplane can be made to take off without demonstrating hazardous characteristics while continuing the takeoff. Answer (B) is incorrect. Vip is a nonsense concept in that it is not defined in FAR 1.2. Answer (C) is incorrect. Vec/Mec is maximum speed for stability characteristics.

questions.study_unit_name: NTSB Part 830, FAR Part 1, FAR Part 61, FAR Part 117, FAR Part 119
questions.question_text: Which is the correct symbol for the stalling speed or the minimum steady flight speed at which the airplane is controllable?
questions.choice_c: Vo51-
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Vs is the stalling speed, or the minimum steady flight speed, at which the airplane is controllable. Answer (A) is incorrect. Vso is the stalling speed, or the minimum steady flight speed, in the landing configuration. Answer (C) is incorrect. Vs, is the stalling speed, or the minimum steady flight speed, obtained in a specific configuration.

questions.study_unit_name: NTSB Part 830, FAR Part 1, FAR Part 61, FAR Part 117, FAR Part 119
questions.question_text: Which is the correct symbol for the minimum steady-flight speed or stalling speed in the landing configuration?
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Vz, is the stalling speed, or the minimum steady flight speed, in the landing configuration. Answer (A) is incorrect. Vs is the stalling speed, or the minimum steady flight speed, at which the airplane is controllable. Answer (B) is incorrect. Vs, is the stalling speed, or the minimum steady flight speed, obtained in a specific configuration.

questions.study_unit_name: NTSB Part 830, FAR Part 1, FAR Part 61, FAR Part 117, FAR Part 119
questions.question_text: The symbol for the speed at which the critical engine is assumed to fail during takeoff is
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: V.; means the speed at which the critical engine Is assumed to fail during takeoff. Answer (A) is incorrect. V2 is the takeoff safety speed, not the speed at which the critical engine is assumed to fail during takeoff. Answer (B) is incorrect. V, is the maximum speed in the takeoff at which the pilot must take the first action to stop the airplane within the accelerate-stop distance, not the speed at which the critical engine is assumed to fail during takeoff.

questions.study_unit_name: NTSB Part 830, FAR Part 1, FAR Part 61, FAR Part 117, FAR Part 119
questions.question_text: The maximum speed during takeoff that the pilot may abort the takeoff and stop the airplane within the accelerate-stop distance is
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: V, means the maximum speed in the takeoff at which the pilot must take the first action (e.g., apply brakes, reduce thrust, deploy speed brakes) to stop the airplane within the accelerate-stop distance. ° Answer (A) is incorrect. V> is the takeoff safety speed, not the maximum speed during takeoff that the pilot may abort the takeoff and Stop the airplane within the accelerate-stop distance. Answer (B) is incorrect. Vee means the speed at which the critical engine Is assumed to fail during takeoff, not the maximum speed during takeoff that the pilot may abort the takeoff and stop the airplane within the accelerate-stop distance.

questions.study_unit_name: NTSB Part 830, FAR Part 1, FAR Part 61, FAR Part 117, FAR Part 119
questions.question_text: The minimum speed during takeoff, following a failure of the critical engine at Vee, at which the pilot may continue the takeoff and achieve the required height above the takeoff surface within the takeoff distance is indicated by symbol
questions.choice_c: Vor.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: V, is the minimum speed in the takeoff, following a failure of the critical engine at Ver, at which the pilot can continue the takeoff and achieve the required height above the takeoff surface within the takeoff distance. Answer (A) is incorrect. Voy is the minimum takeoff safety speed, not the minimum speed during takeoff, following a failure of the critical engine at Ver, at which the pilot may continue the takeoff and achieve the required height above the takeoff surface within the takeoff distance. Answer (C) is incorrect. V.og is the liftoff speed, not the minimum speed during takeoff, following a failure of the critical engine at Ver, at which the pilot may continue the takeoff and achieve the required height above the takeoff surface within the takeoff distance.

questions.study_unit_name: NTSB Part 830, FAR Part 1, FAR Part 61, FAR Part 117, FAR Part 119
questions.question_text: You are on a takeoff roll in a 14 CFR Part 25 certificated jet and see an engine fire warning light as you see V, pass. You know you will now need more than the calculated distance to stop, so you continue the takeoff as briefed and handle the fire ligh' as an in-flight emergency.
questions.choice_b: abort the takeoff with a plan to use maximum reverse thrust to augment the braking action.
questions.choice_c: continue the takeoff roll to the end of the runway to gain maximum speed before lifting off.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: V, means the maximum speed in the takeoff at which the pilot must take the first action (e.g., apply brakes, reduce thrust, deploy speed brakes) to stop the airplane within the accelerate-stop distance. V, also means the minimum speed in the takeoff, following a failure of the critical engine at Ver, at which the pilot can continue the takeoff and achieve the required height above the takeoff surface within the takeoff distance. As the question states, you have passed V,, therefore requiring more distance to stop. You would take off as briefed and land as soon as possible. Answer (B) is incorrect. You are beyond V, and may not have enough runway on which to stop, even with reverse thrust and braking action. Answer (C) is incorrect. You should take off as briefed, contact ATC while climbing to a safe altitude, and get clearance to return to the airport. Using all of the runway in a possible emergency situation could compromise the ability of the aircraft to meet the minimum climb gradients calculated for takeoff. This could result in the aircraft striking terrain or objects in the takeoff path.

questions.study_unit_name: NTSB Part 830, FAR Part 1, FAR Part 61, FAR Part 117, FAR Part 119
questions.question_text: What is the lowest decision height for which a Category II applicant can be certified during the original issuance of the authorization?
questions.choice_c: 200 feet AGL.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Upon original issuance of a Category II pilot authorization, the authorization contains a limitation for Category II operations of 1,600 ft. RVR (runway visual range) and a 150-ft. decision height. Answer (A) is incorrect. A 100-ft. decision height is allowed after completion of three Category II ILS approaches with a 150-ft. DH within the previous 6 months. Answer (C) is incorrect. The standard Category | ILS decision height is 200 ft.

questions.study_unit_name: NTSB Part 830, FAR Part 1, FAR Part 61, FAR Part 117, FAR Part 119
questions.question_text: A Category II ILS pilot authorization, when originally issued, is normally limited to
questions.choice_a: Category II operations not less than 1600 RVR and a 150-foot DH.
questions.choice_b: pilots who have completed an FAA-approved Category II training program.
questions.choice_c: Category I! operations not less than 1200 RVR and a 100-foot DH.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Upon original issuance of a Category II authorization, the authorization contains a limitation for Category || operations of 1,600 ft. RVR and a 150-ft. DH. This initial limitation is removed when three Category II ILS approaches within a 150-ft. DH have been flown. Answer (B) is incorrect. A Category II pilot authorization is issued as a part of a pilot’s instrument rating or ATP certificate, not a Category II training program. Answer (C) is incorrect. A 1,200 RVR and a 100-ft. DH are the Category II minimums after the initial limitation is removed by the pilot's completing three ILS approaches to a 150-ft. DH in the preceding 6 months. -

questions.study_unit_name: NTSB Part 830, FAR Part 1, FAR Part 61, FAR Part 117, FAR Part 119
questions.question_text: When may a Category II ILS limitation be removed? ILS 0-foo Sei haan!
questions.choice_b: When six ILS approaches to Category II minimums and landings have been completed in the past 6 months.
questions.choice_c: 120 days after issue or renewal.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: The Category II limitation is removed when the holder shows that, since the beginning of the sixth preceding month, (s)he has made three Category II ILS approaches with a 150-ft. decision height to a landing under actual or simulated instrument conditions. Answer (B) is incorrect. The removal of the Category II limitation requires only three, not six, Category II ILS approaches to a 150-ft. decision height. Answer (C) is incorrect. The expiration of a temporary airman certificate is 120 days. This is not related to Category II limitations.

questions.study_unit_name: NTSB Part 830, FAR Part 1, FAR Part 61, FAR Part 117, FAR Part 119
questions.question_text: How soon after the conviction for driving while intoxicated by alcohol or drugs shall it be reported to the FAA, Civil Aviation Security Division?
questions.choice_a: No later than 30 working days after the motor vehicle action.
questions.choice_b: ater than 60 days after the motor vehicle ion.
questions.choice_c: Required to be reported upon renewal of medical certificate.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Each individual convicted of driving while intoxicated by alcohol or drugs shall provide a written report of each motor vehicle action to the FAA, Civil Aviation Security Division, no later than 60 days after the motor vehicle action. Answer (A) is incorrect. The report will be sent no later than 60 days, not 30 days, after the motor vehicle action. Answer (C) is incorrect. Although conviction of driving while intoxicated by alcohol or drugs is reported on medical certificates, notification of a motor vehicle action for these offenses is required to be reported to the FAA in Oklahoma City no later than 60 days from that date.

questions.study_unit_name: NTSB Part 830, FAR Part 1, FAR Part 61, FAR Part 117, FAR Part 119
questions.question_text: An applicant who is scheduled for a practical test for an airline transport pilot certificate, in an approved flight simulator, is
questions.choice_a: required to have at least a current third-class medical certificate.
questions.choice_b: not required to have a medical certificate.
questions.choice_c: required to have a first-class medical certificate.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: When taking the practical test for an airline transport pilot certificate in a flight simulator, an applicant is not required to have a medical certificate. Answer (A) is incorrect. An applicant is required to have at least a third-class medical certificate if the ATP practical test is conducted in an airplane, not a flight simulator. Answer (C) is incorrect. An applicant is required to have a first-class medical certificate to exercise the privileges of an ATP certificate, not to take the ATP practical test.

questions.study_unit_name: NTSB Part 830, FAR Part 1, FAR Part 61, FAR Part 117, FAR Part 119
questions.question_text: An applicant who is taking a practical test for a type rating to be added to a commercial pilot certificate, in an approved simulator, is
questions.choice_a: required to have a first-class medical certificate.
questions.choice_b: required to have a second-class medical certificate.
questions.choice_c: not required to have a medical certificate.
questions.explanation: __ DISCUSSION: An applicant who is taking a practical test in a flight simulator for a type rating to be added to a commercial pilot certificate is not required to have a medical certificate. ___ Answer (A) is incorrect. An applicant is required to have a first-class medical certificate to exercise the privileges of an ATP certificate, not to take a practical test for a type rating in a flight simulator. Answer (B) is incorrect. An applicant is required to have a second-class medical certificate to exercise the privileges of a commercial pilot certificate, not to take a practical test for a type rating in a flight simulator.

questions.study_unit_name: NTSB Part 830, FAR Part 1, FAR Part 61, FAR Part 117, FAR Part 119
questions.question_text: When a type rating is to be added to an airline transport pilot certificate, and the practical test is scheduled in an approved flight training device and/or approved flight simulator, the applicant is
questions.choice_a: required to have at least a third-class medical certificate.
questions.choice_b: not required to have a medical certificate.
questions.choice_c: required to have a first-class medical certificate.
questions.explanation: ___ DISCUSSION: An applicant who is taking a practical test in a flight simulator or flight training device for a type rating to be added to an ATP certificate is not required to have a medical certificate. ___ Answer (A) is incorrect. A third-class medical certificate is required if any part of the practical test is conducted in an airplane, not if the practical test is conducted entirely in a flight simulator or flight training device. Answer (C) is incorrect. An applicant Is required to have a first-class medical certificate to exercise the privileges of an ATP certificate, not to take a practical test to add a type rating to an ATP certificate.

questions.study_unit_name: NTSB Part 830, FAR Part 1, FAR Part 61, FAR Part 117, FAR Part 119
questions.subsection: 61.29 Replacement of Lost or Destroyed Airman or Medical Certificate or Knowledge Test Report
questions.question_text: When a facsimile replacement is received for an airman’s medical certificate, for what maximum time is this document valid?
questions.choice_c: 90 days.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: A person who has lost an airman certificate or medical certificate may obtain a fax from the FAA confirming that it was issued. The fax may be carried as a certificate for a period not to exceed 60 days pending his or her receipt of a duplicate certificate. Answer (A) is incorrect. The fax that may be carried as a certificate is valid for 60 days, not 30 days. Answer (C) is incorrect. The fax that may be issued as a certificate is valid for 60 days, not 90 days.

questions.study_unit_name: NTSB Part 830, FAR Part 1, FAR Part 61, FAR Part 117, FAR Part 119
questions.subsection: 61.29 Replacement of Lost or Destroyed Airman or Medical Certificate or Knowledge Test Report
questions.question_text: Unless otherwise authorized, when is the pilot in command required to hold a type rating?
questions.choice_a: When operating an aircraft that is certificated for more than one pilot. “Baygiltioh operat gemini rcraft having a gross ~ ight of more tha
questions.choice_c: When operating a multiengine aircraft having a gross weight of more than 6,000 pounds.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: A person may not act as pilot in command of any turbojet or other large aircraft (an aircraft of more than 12,500 Ib. maximum certificated takeoff weight) unless (s)he holds a type rating for that aircraft. Answer (A) is incorrect. Although an aircraft may require two pilots for its operation, only turbojet aircraft and other aircraft in excess of 12,500 Ib. require a type rating. Answer (C) is incorrect. Large aircraft are those aircraft in excess of 12,500 Ib., not 6,000 Ib. They can be either single-engine or multi-engine aircraft.

questions.study_unit_name: NTSB Part 830, FAR Part 1, FAR Part 61, FAR Part 117, FAR Part 119
questions.subsection: 61.29 Replacement of Lost or Destroyed Airman or Medical Certificate or Knowledge Test Report
questions.question_text: An applicant who is scheduled for a practical test for an airline transport pilot certificate, in an aircraft, needs
questions.choice_c: asecond-class medical certificate.
questions.explanation: , DISCUSSION: An applicant who is scheduled for an ATP practical test in an airplane must hold at least a current third- class medical certificate. Answer (A) is incorrect. An applicant is required to have a first-class medical certificate to exercise the privileges of an ATP certificate, not to take the ATP practical test in an airplane. Answer (C) is incorrect. An applicant is required to have a second-class medical certificate to exercise the privileges of a commercial pilot certificate, not to take the ATP practical test in an airplane.

questions.study_unit_name: NTSB Part 830, FAR Part 1, FAR Part 61, FAR Part 117, FAR Part 119
questions.subsection: 61.29 Replacement of Lost or Destroyed Airman or Medical Certificate or Knowledge Test Report
questions.question_text: When a type rating is to be added to an airline transport pilot certificate, and the practical test is scheduled in an approved flight simulator and an aircraft, the applicant is fo have at least a current third-class al certificate.
questions.choice_b: required to have a current first-class medical certificate.
questions.choice_c: not required to hold a medical certificate.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: When a type rating is to be added to an ATP certificate and the practical test is scheduled in a flight simulator and an airplane, the applicant is required to have at least a current third-class medical certificate. ; Answer (B) is incorrect. An applicant is required to have a first-class medical certificate to exercise the privileges of an ATP certificate, not to take a practical test for a type rating to be added to an ATP certificate. Answer (C) is incorrect. The applicant is required to hold at least a current third-class medical certificate since a portion of the practical test will be conducted in an airplane. If the entire test will be completed in a flight simulator, an applicant does not need a medical certificate.

questions.study_unit_name: NTSB Part 830, FAR Part 1, FAR Part 61, FAR Part 117, FAR Part 119
questions.subsection: 61.29 Replacement of Lost or Destroyed Airman or Medical Certificate or Knowledge Test Report
questions.question_text: What instrument flight time may be logged by a second in command of an aircraft requiring two pilots? allah ir ~ —— ndincommandis = ontrolling the ai solely by reference to fight instruments.
questions.choice_b: One-half the time the flight is on an IFR flight plan.
questions.choice_c: One-half the time the airplane is in actual IFR conditions.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: A pilot may log instrument flight time only for that flight time during which (s)he operates the aircraft solely by reference to flight instruments under actual or simulated instrument flying conditions. Answer (B) is incorrect. Only when the pilot is flying in actual or simulated instrument flying conditions as sole manipulator of the controls may (s)he log instrument flight time. Answer (C) is incorrect. A pilot may log all flight time in which (s)he is sole manipulator of the controls in IFR conditions as instrument flight time.

questions.study_unit_name: NTSB Part 830, FAR Part 1, FAR Part 61, FAR Part 117, FAR Part 119
questions.subsection: 61.57 Recent Flight Experience: Pilot in Command
questions.question_text: To satisfy the minimum required instrument experience for IFR operations, a pilot must accomplish during the past 6 months at least ix instrument approaches, holding, — pe ike \d tracking course h the "Use of navigation = sinan Shrove fli gaining device/simulator or in the category craft to be noe,
questions.choice_b: six instrument approaches, three of which must be in the same category and class of aircraft to be flown, plus holding, intercepting, and tracking courses in any aircraft.
questions.choice_c: six instrument approaches and 6 hours of instrument time, three of which may be in a glider.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: To satisfy the minimum required instrument experience for IFR operations, a pilot must accomplish six instrument approaches, holding, and intercepting and tracking courses through the use of navigation systems in a flight simulator or flight training device (representative of the category of aircraft to be flown) or in the appropriate category of aircraft to be flown. Answer (B) is incorrect. To meet the instrument currency, all of the instrument approaches, holding, and intercepting and tracking courses must be completed in the category of aircraft to be used or in a flight simulator or training device that is representative of the aircraft to be used, not any aircraft. Answer (C) is incorrect. No minimum instrument time is required and all of the requirements must be accomplished in the category of aircraft to be used. In addition to six approaches, the pilot must also accomplish holding and intercepting and tracking courses.

questions.study_unit_name: NTSB Part 830, FAR Part 1, FAR Part 61, FAR Part 117, FAR Part 119
questions.subsection: 61.57 Recent Flight Experience: Pilot in Command
questions.question_text: A pilot, acting as second in command under 14 CFR 135, successfully completes the instrument competency check specified in FAR Part 61. How long does this pilot remain current if no further IFR flights are made?
questions.choice_c: 6 months.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: A pilot acting as either pilot in command or second-in-command will remain current for 6 months after an instrument proficiency check if no further IFR flights are made. Answer (A) is incorrect. Upon completion of an instrument proficiency check, a pilot will remain current for 6 months, not 12 months. Answer (B) is incorrect. Ninety days defines the three takeoffs-and-landings experience required to carry passengers.

questions.study_unit_name: NTSB Part 830, FAR Part 1, FAR Part 61, FAR Part 117, FAR Part 119
questions.subsection: 61.57 Recent Flight Experience: Pilot in Command
questions.question_text: Within the past 6 months, a pilot has accomplished: Two approaches in a helicopter. Two approaches in an airplane. Two approaches in a glider. What additional instrument experience must the pilot obtain prior to acting as second in command (under 14 CFR part 135) on an IFR flight? pproaches in an aircraft, approved n ic ht Simba oma eeentalive of the aircraft category), _ holding, intercepting and tracking courses : _using the navigation systems.
questions.choice_b: Passes an instrument proficiency check in any category aircraft, approved simulator or training device.
questions.choice_c: Holding, intercepting and tracking courses (using the navigation systems) in an aircraft, approved simulator or approved flight training device.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: FAR 135.245 requires that the second- in-command on an IFR flight meet the instrument experience requirements of Part 61. FAR 61.57 requires that, in the past 6 months, a pilot must have accomplished at least six instrument approaches, holding procedures, and intercepting and tracking courses through the use of navigation systems in an aircraft, flight simulator, or flight training device that is representative of the aircraft category for the instrument privileges sought. Answer (B) is incorrect. An instrument proficiency check must be in an aircraft, flight simulator, or flight training device that is appropriate to the aircraft category, not any category of aircraft. Answer (C) is incorrect. Six instrument approaches, holding, and intercepting and tracking courses must be done in the same category (airplane) of aircraft for which instrument privileges are sought; thus, the pilot must accomplish four approaches in an airplane or an airplane flight simulator or training device for his or her instrument experience to be current.

questions.study_unit_name: NTSB Part 830, FAR Part 1, FAR Part 61, FAR Part 117, FAR Part 119
questions.question_text: What recent experience is required to be eligible for the practical test for the original issue of a Category || authorization?
questions.choice_a: Within the previous 6 months, six ILS apologies flown manually to the Category |
questions.choice_b: Within the previous 12 calendar months, six ILS approaches flown by use of an approach coupler to the Category | or Category II DH.
questions.choice_c: Within the previous 6 months, six ILS approaches, three of which may be flown to the Category | DH by use of an approach coupler.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: An applicant for the original issuance of a Category II authorization must have made at least six ILS approaches since the beginning of the sixth month before the practical test. These approaches must be under actual or simulated instrument flight conditions down to the minimum landing altitude for the ILS approach in the type of airplane in which the flight is to be conducted. At least three of these approaches must be conducted manually, without the use of an autopilot (approach coupler). Answer (A) is incorrect. Only three of the six approaches must be flown manually to Category | DH. Answer (B) is incorrect. The six ILS approaches must be flown within the preceding 6, not 12, calendar months, and three of the approaches must be flown manually, not with an approach coupler.

questions.study_unit_name: NTSB Part 830, FAR Part 1, FAR Part 61, FAR Part 117, FAR Part 119
questions.question_text: To be eligible for the practical test for the renewal of a Category II authorization, what recent instrument approach experience is required?
questions.choice_a: Within the previous 6 months, six ILS approaches, three of which may be flown to the Category | DH by use of an approach coupler.
questions.choice_b: Within the previous 6 months, six ILS approaches flown by use of an approach coupler to the Category | DH.
questions.choice_c: Within the previous 12 calendar months, three ILS approaches flown by use of an approach coupler to the Category II DH.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: For a pilot to renew his or her Category II authorization, (s)he must meet the same requirements as those established for the original issuance; i.e., at least six ILS approaches must have been made under actual or simulated instrument flight conditions down to the minimum landing altitude for the ILS approach in the type of airplane in which the flight is to be conducted, and they must have been completed since the beginning of the sixth month before the test. At least three of these approaches must have been flown manually without the use of an approach coupler. Answer (B) is incorrect. Only three of the six approaches may be flown using an approach coupler. Answer (C) is incorrect. The requirement is for a total of six, not three, approaches, only three of which may be flown by use of an approach coupler. The approaches are not required to be flown down to Category II DH. Also, they must have been flown within the preceding 6, not 12, calendar months.

questions.study_unit_name: NTSB Part 830, FAR Part 1, FAR Part 61, FAR Part 117, FAR Part 119
questions.question_text: A commercial pilot has a type rating in a B-727 and B-737. A flight test is completed in a B-747 for the Airline Transport Pilot Certificate. What pilot privileges may be exercised regarding these airplanes?
questions.choice_a: Commercial - B-737; ATP - B-727 and B-747.
questions.choice_b: ATP - B-747; Commercial - B-727 and B-737.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: When a person successfully passes the ATP practical test, all type ratings will be included at the ATP certificate level, provided that the aircraft used on the practical test is the same category and class of aircraft as the type ratings. Since the B-727, -737, and -747 are all airplanes (category) and multi-engine land (class), the ATP certificate privileges may be exercised for the B-727, -737, and -747. Answer (A) is incorrect. All three airplanes, not only the B-727 and -747, will be included at the ATP certificate level, since they are all the same category and class of aircraft. Answer (B) is incorrect. All three airplanes, not only the airplane in which the practical test was taken, will be at the ATP certificate level, since they are all the same category and class of aircraft.

questions.study_unit_name: NTSB Part 830, FAR Part 1, FAR Part 61, FAR Part 117, FAR Part 119
questions.question_text: A commercial pilot has DC-3 and DC-9 type ratings. A flight test is completed for an Airline Transport Pilot Certificate in a B-727. What pilot privileges may be exercised?
questions.choice_c: ATP - B-727 only; Commercial - DC-9 and DC-3.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: When a person successfully passes the ATP practical test, all type ratings will be included at the ATP certificate level, provided that the aircraft used on the practical test is the same category and class of aircraft of the type ratings. Since the B-727, DC-3, and DC-9 are all airplanes (category) and multi-engine land (class), the ATP certificate privileges may be exercised for the B-727, DC-3, and DC-9. Answer (A) is incorrect. All three aircraft, not only the B-727 and DC-3, will be included at the ATP certificate level, since they are all the same category and class of aircraft. Answer (C) is incorrect. All three aircraft, not only the B-727, will be included at the ATP certificate level, since they are all the same category and class of aircraft.

questions.study_unit_name: NTSB Part 830, FAR Part 1, FAR Part 61, FAR Part 117, FAR Part 119
questions.question_text: In a 24-hour consecutive period, what is the maximum time, excluding briefing and debriefing, that an airline transport pilot may instruct other pilots in air transportation service?
questions.choice_c: 10 hours.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: An airline transport pilot may instruct other pilots in air transportation service, excluding briefings and debriefings, for a maximum of 8 hr. in any 24-hr. consecutive period. Answer (B) is incorrect. An airline transport pilot may instruct for a maximum of 8 hr., not 6 hr., in any 24-hr. consecutive period. Answer (C) is incorrect. An airline transport pilot may instruct for a maximum of 8 hr., not 10 hr., in any 24-hr. consecutive period.

questions.study_unit_name: NTSB Part 830, FAR Part 1, FAR Part 61, FAR Part 117, FAR Part 119
questions.question_text: The flight instruction of other pilots in air transportation service by an airline transport pilot is restricted to
questions.choice_c: 30 hours in any 7-consecutive-day period.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: The period of time that an airline transport pilot may instruct other pilots in air transportation service, excluding briefings and debriefings, is restricted to 36 hr. in any 7-consecutive-day period. Answer (B) is incorrect. An airline transport pilot may instruct other pilots in air transportation for a maximum of 8 hr., not 7 hr., in any 24-consecutive-hour period. Answer (C) is incorrect. An airline transport pilot may instruct other pilots in air transportation for a maximum of 36 hr., not 30 hr., in any 7-consecutive-day period.

questions.study_unit_name: NTSB Part 830, FAR Part 1, FAR Part 61, FAR Part 117, FAR Part 119
questions.question_text: For airport/standby reserve, all time spent in airport/standby reserve time is
questions.choice_a: not part of the flightcrew member’s flight duty period.
questions.choice_b: part of the flightcrew member's flight duty period.
questions.choice_c: part of the flightcrew member’s flight duty period after being alerted for flight assignment.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: “Airport/standby reserve” means a defined duty period during which a flightcrew member is required by a certificate holder to be at an airport for a possible assignment. A flight duty period includes the duties performed by the flightcrew member on behalf of the certificate holder that occur before a flight segment or between flight segments without a required intervening rest period. Examples of tasks that are part of the flight duty period include deadhead transportation, training conducted in an aircraft or flight simulator, and airport/ standby reserve, if the above tasks occur before a flight segment or een flight segments without an intervening required rest period. Answer (A) is incorrect. Examples of tasks that are part of the flight duty period include deadhead transportation, training conducted in an aircraft or flight simulator, and airport/standby reserve, if the above tasks occur before a flight segment or between flight segments without an intervening required rest period. Answer (C) is incorrect. Examples of tasks that are part of the flight duty period include deadhead transportation, training conducted in an aircraft or flight simulator, and airport/standby reserve, if the above tasks occur before a flight segment or meayeen flight segments without an intervening required rest period.

questions.study_unit_name: NTSB Part 830, FAR Part 1, FAR Part 61, FAR Part 117, FAR Part 119
questions.question_text: “Window of circadian low” means a period of maximum sleepiness that occurs between
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: A window of circadian low means a period of maximum sleepiness that occurs between 0200 and 0559 during a physiological night. Answer (A) is incorrect. A circadian low occurs during the hours of 0200 and 0559 of a physiological night. Answer (C) is incorrect. A circadian low does not begin until 0200 and extends to 0559 during a physiological night.

questions.study_unit_name: NTSB Part 830, FAR Part 1, FAR Part 61, FAR Part 117, FAR Part 119
questions.question_text: A “calendar day” is
questions.choice_c: a 24-hour consecutive period between Saturday and Sunday.
questions.explanation: A calendar day means a 24-hour period from 0000 through 2359 using Coordinated Universal Time or local time. Answer (B) is incorrect. A calendar day is a specific 24-hour period from 0000 through 2359 that references Coordinated Universal Time or local time. Answer (C) is incorrect. A calendar day is not restricted to the weekend; it is a specific 24-hour period from 0000 through 2359 using Coordinated Universal Time or local time.

questions.study_unit_name: NTSB Part 830, FAR Part 1, FAR Part 61, FAR Part 117, FAR Part 119
questions.question_text: “Rest period” means
questions.choice_a: an 8-hour continuous period determined prospectively during which the flightcrew member is free from all restraint by the certificate holder.
questions.choice_b: acontinuous period determined prospectively during which the flightcrew member is free from all restraint by the certificate holder.
questions.choice_c: a12-hour continuous period determined prospectively during which the flightcrew member is free from all restraint by the certificate holder.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: A rest period includes freedom from present responsibility for work should the occasion arise. Answer (A) is incorrect. A rest period is not provided an hourly limit; it is merely a continuous period during which the flightcrew member is free from all responsibility for work and restraint from the certificate holder. Answer (C) is incorrect. A rest period is not provided an hourly limit; it is merely a continuous period during which the flightcrew member is free from all responsibility for work and restraint from the certificate holder.

questions.study_unit_name: NTSB Part 830, FAR Part 1, FAR Part 61, FAR Part 117, FAR Part 119
questions.question_text: “Physiological night’s rest” means
questions.choice_a: 9 hours of rest that encompasses the hours of 0100 and 0700 at the crewmember’s home base.
questions.choice_b: 12 hours of rest that encompasses any continuous 8-hour period for uninterrupted or disturbed rest.
questions.choice_c: 10 hours of rest that encompasses the hours of 0100 and 0700 at the crewmember’s home base.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Physiological night’s rest is 10 hours of rest that must encompass the hours of 0100 and 0700 at the acclimated location, unless the flightcrew member has acclimated to a different theater. Answer (A) is incorrect. Ten hours of rest must encompass the hours of 0100 and 0700 of either the acclimated location or the flightcrew member’s home base. Answer (B) is incorrect. The physiological night’s rest must encompass the hours of 0100 and 0700 and must be 10 hours.

questions.study_unit_name: NTSB Part 830, FAR Part 1, FAR Part 61, FAR Part 117, FAR Part 119
questions.question_text: “Theater” means a geographical area in which the distance between the flightcrew member flight duty period departure point and arrival point differs by no more than
questions.choice_c: 60 degrees longitude.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Theater refers to a geographical area in which the distance between the flight crewmember flight duty period departure point and arrival point differs by no more than 60 degrees longitude. Answer (A) is incorrect. The geographical area that encompasses the “theater” can be no more than 60 degrees longitude. Answer (B) is incorrect. The geographical area that encompasses the “theater” can be no more than 60 degrees longitude.

questions.study_unit_name: NTSB Part 830, FAR Part 1, FAR Part 61, FAR Part 117, FAR Part 119
questions.question_text: “Airport standby reserve” means
questions.choice_a: aspecified 15-hour period of reserve in close proximity of assignment being available for flight duty assignments in less than 2 hours.
questions.choice_b: adefined duty period during which a flight crewmember is required by the certificate holder to be available for possible assignment.
questions.choice_c: being within 90 minutes of the airport and available for immediate flight duty assignments of 8 hours duration.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Airport standby reserve is a defined duty period during which a flight crewmember is required by the certificate holder to be available for possible assignment. Answer (A) is incorrect. Airport standby reserve is a defined duty period during which a flight crewmember is required by the certificate holder to be available for possible assignment. Answer (C) is incorrect. Airport standby reserve is a defined duty period during which a flight crewmember is required by the certificate holder to be available for possible assignment.

questions.study_unit_name: NTSB Part 830, FAR Part 1, FAR Part 61, FAR Part 117, FAR Part 119
questions.question_text: In order to be assigned for duty, each flightcrew member must report on time, in uniform, and properly prepared to accomplish all assigned duties. to the airport on time, after the designated rest period and fully prepared to accomplish assigned duties. for any flight duty period rested and prepared to perform his/her assigned duties.
questions.explanation: : DISCUSSION: Each flightcrew member must report for any flight duty period rested and prepared to perform his or her assigned duties. No certificate holder may assign and no | flightcrew member may accept assignment to a flight duty period if the flightcrew member has reported for a flight duty period too fatigued to safely perform his or her assigned duties. Answer (A) is incorrect. While arriving on time and in uniform may be an airline requirement, it is not an FAA requirement. Answer (B) is incorrect. While arriving to the airport on time may be an airline requirement, it is not an FAA requirement.

questions.study_unit_name: NTSB Part 830, FAR Part 1, FAR Part 61, FAR Part 117, FAR Part 119
questions.question_text: Flightcrew members must receive fatigue education and awareness training with all required air carrier dispatcher and every flightcrew member training activity. annually for flightcrew members and every 24 months for dispatchers, flightcrew member schedulers, and operational control individuals. annually for flightcrew member schedulers, operational control individuals, and flightcrew members and dispatchers.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Each certificate holder must develop and implement an education and awareness training program, approved by the Administrator. This program must provide annual education and awareness training to all employees of the certificate holder responsible for administering the provisions of this rule including flightcrew members, dispatchers, individuals directly involved in the scheduling of flightcrew members, individuals directly involved in operational control, and any employee providing direct management oversight of those areas. Answer (A) is incorrect. Fatigue education and awareness training must include flightcrew members, dispatchers, individuals directly involved in the scheduling of flightcrew members, individuals directly involved in operational control, and any employee providing direct management oversight of those areas. Answer (B) is incorrect. Fatigue education and awareness training must provide annual, not every 24 months, education and awareness training to all employees of the certificate holder responsible for administering the provisions of this rule including flightcrew members, dispatchers, individuals directly involved in the scheduling of flightcrew members, individuals directly involved in operational control, and any employee providing direct management oversight of those areas.

questions.study_unit_name: NTSB Part 830, FAR Part 1, FAR Part 61, FAR Part 117, FAR Part 119
questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 488 on page 46.) (Refer to Table A.) In an airplane with a minimum flight crew of two assigned, your flight time may not exceed
questions.choice_a: 9 hours if assigned to report at 0330.
questions.choice_b: 9 hours if assigned to report at 0500.
questions.choice_c: 9 hours if assigned to report at 2030.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Refer to Fig. 488, Table A. Locate 0500-1959. If assigned to report at 0500 hours, the maximum flight time may not exceed 9 hours. Answer (A) is incorrect. Reporting at 0330 falls between 0000-0459, which has a maximum flight time of 8 hours. Answer (C) is incorrect. Reporting at 2030 falls between 2000-2359, which has a maximum flight time of 8 hours.

questions.study_unit_name: NTSB Part 830, FAR Part 1, FAR Part 61, FAR Part 117, FAR Part 119
questions.question_text: The time spent resting during unaugmented operations will not be counted towards the flight duty period limitation if the rest period is at least
questions.choice_a: 3 hours long after reaching suitable accommodations.
questions.choice_b: 4 hours long after reaching suitable accommodations.
questions.choice_c: 4 hours long which can include transportation to suitable accommodations.
questions.explanation: For an unaugmented operation only, if a flightcrew member is provided with a rest opportunity (an opportunity to sleep) in a suitable accommodation during his or her flight duty period, the time that the flightcrew member spends in the suitable accommodation is not part of that flightcrew member’s flight duty period if all of the following conditions are met: 1. The rest opportunity is provided between the hours of 22:00 and 05:00 local time. 2. The time spent in the suitable accommodation is at least 3 hours, measured from the time that the flightcrew member reaches the suitable accommodation. 3. The rest opportunity is scheduled before the beginning of the flight duty period in which that rest opportunity is taken. 4. The rest opportunity that the flightcrew member is actually provided may not be less than the rest opportunity that was scheduled. 5. The rest opportunity is not provided until the first segment of the flight duty period has been completed. 6. The combined time of the flight duty period and the rest opportunity provided in this section does not exceed 14 hours. Answer (B) is incorrect. The time spent in the suitable accommodation is at least 3 hours, not 4 hours. Answer (C) is incorrect. The time spent in the suitable accommodations is at least 3 hours, not 4 hours. Also, suitable accommodation only applies to ground facilities and does not apply to aircraft onboard rest facilities.

questions.study_unit_name: NTSB Part 830, FAR Part 1, FAR Part 61, FAR Part 117, FAR Part 119
questions.question_text: Notification of the rest opportunity period during unaugmented operations, must be
questions.choice_a: given before the next to last flight segment.
questions.choice_b: given before the beginning of the flight duty period.
questions.choice_c: provided no later than after the first flight segment offered after the first flight segment is completed.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: For an unaugmented operation only, if a flightcrew member is provided with a rest opportunity (an opportunity to sleep) in a suitable accommodation during his or her flight duty period, the time that the flightcrew member spends in the suitable accommodation is not part of that flightcrew member's flight duty period if all of the following conditions are met: 1. The rest opportunity is provided between the hours of 22:00 and 05:00 local time. 2. The time spent in the suitable accommodation is at least 3 hours, measured from the time that the flightcrew member reaches the suitable accommodation. 3. The rest opportunity is scheduled before the beginning of the flight duty period in which that rest opportunity is taken. 4. The rest opportunity that the flightcrew member is actually provided may not be less than the rest opportunity that was scheduled. 5. The rest opportunity is not provided until the first segment of the flight duty period has been completed. 6. The combined time of the flight duty period and the rest opportunity provided in this section does not exceed 14 hours. Item 3. implies that notification must be given before the beginning of the flight duty period. Answer (A) is incorrect. Notification must be given and scheduled before the beginning of the flight duty period in which that rest opportunity is taken. Answer (C) is incorrect. Notification must be given and scheduled before the beginning of the flight duty period in which that rest opportunity is taken.

questions.study_unit_name: NTSB Part 830, FAR Part 1, FAR Part 61, FAR Part 117, FAR Part 119
questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 488 below.) (Refer to Table B.) For unaugmented flightcrew operations, your maximum flight duty period limit is
questions.choice_a: 13 hours if assigned to report at 0700 for 4 flight segments.
questions.choice_b: 13 hours if assigned to report at 2030 for 3 flight segments.
questions.choice_c: 10.5 hours if assigned to report at 1730 for 6 flight segments.
questions.explanation: Find the “scheduled time of start” of 0700 on the left column of Table B and notice that 4 flight segments and 13 hours are allowed. Answer (B) is incorrect. Find the “scheduled time of start” of 1700-2159 on the left column of Table B and notice that 13 hour duty period is not allowed. Answer (C) is incorrect. Find the “scheduled time of start” of 1700-2159 on the left column of Table B and notice that 6 flight segments allows 9 duty period hours.

questions.study_unit_name: NTSB Part 830, FAR Part 1, FAR Part 61, FAR Part 117, FAR Part 119
questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 488 below.) (Refer to Table C.) In an airplane with an augmented crew of three flightcrew members assigned, the maximum flight duty period is
questions.choice_a: 17 hours if assigned to report at 1200 with a class 3 rest facility available.
questions.choice_b: 16 hours if assigned to report at 0630 with a class 1 rest facility available.
questions.choice_c: 14 hours if assigned to report at 1630 with a class 2 rest facility available. questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: In an airplane with an augmented crew of three flightcrew members assigned, they can have a maximum flight duty period of 16 hours if they are assigned to report at 0630 with a class 1 rest facility available. Answer (A) is incorrect. In an airplane with an augmented crew of three flightcrew members assigned, they can have a maximum flight duty period of 15 hours if they are assigned to report at 1200 with a class 3 rest facility available. Answer (C) is incorrect. In an airplane with an augmented crew of three flightcrew members assigned, they can have a maximum flight duty period of 15 hours if they are assigned to report at 1630 with a Class 2 rest facility available.
