questions.study_unit_name: NTSB Part 830, FAR Part 1, FAR Part 61, FAR Part 117, FAR Part 119
questions.question_text: If the augmented flightcrew member is not acclimated, the maximum flight duty period given in 14 CFR part 117, Table C (not included herein) is reduced by 30 minutes. flight duty period assignment must be reduced 15 minutes by each 15 degrees of longitude difference from the previous rest location. minimum rest period must be extended by 3 hours.
questions.explanation: For flight operations conducted with an acclimated augmented flightcrew, no certificate holder may assign and no flightcrew member may accept an assignment if the scheduled flight duty period will exceed the limits specified in Table C of 14 CFR Part 117. If the flightcrew member is not acclimated: 1. The maximum flight duty period in Table C of 14 CFR Part 117 is reduced by 30 minutes. 2. The applicable flight duty period is based on the local time at the theater in which the flightcrew member was last acclimated. Answer (B) is incorrect. 14 CFR Part 117 does not allow reducing minutes based on longitude. Answer (C) is incorrect. If a flightcrew member operating in a new theater has received 36 consecutive hours of rest, that flightcrew member is acclimated.

questions.study_unit_name: NTSB Part 830, FAR Part 1, FAR Part 61, FAR Part 117, FAR Part 119
questions.question_text: After takeoff, unforeseen circumstances arise. In this case, the flight duty period may be extended by as much as 2 hours. necessary to reach the closest suitable alternate crew base airport. necessary to land at the next destination airport or alternate airport.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: For augmented and unaugmented operations, if unforeseen operational circumstances arise prior to takeoff: The pilot in command and the certificate holder may extend the maximum flight duty period permitted in Tables B or C of 14 CFR 117 up to 2 hours. The pilot in command and the certificate holder may also extend the maximum combined flight duty period and reserve availability period limits up to 2 hours. Answer (A) is incorrect. If unforeseen operational circumstances arise prior to takeoff, not after takeoff, the pilot in command and certificate holder may extend the maximum flight duty period permitted in 14 CFR 117 in Tables B or C up to 2 hours. Answer (B) is incorrect. The pilot in command and the certificate holder may extend the maximum flight duty periods specified in Tables B or C of 14 CFR 117 to the extent necessary to safely land the aircraft at the next destination airport or alternate airport, as appropriate, not the closest suitable alternate crew base airport.

questions.study_unit_name: NTSB Part 830, FAR Part 1, FAR Part 61, FAR Part 117, FAR Part 119
questions.question_text: The flight duty period may be extended due to unforeseen circumstances before takeoff by as much as 2 hours. 1 hour. 30 minutes.
questions.explanation: For augmented and unaugmented operations, if unforeseen operational circumstances arise prior to takeoff: 1. The pilot in command and the certificate holder may extend the maximum flight duty period permitted in Tables B or C of 14 CFR 117 up to 2 hours. The pilot in command and the certificate holder may also extend the maximum combined flight duty period and reserve availability period limits up to 2 hours. 2. Anextension in the flight duty period of more than 30 minutes may occur only once prior to receiving a rest period of 30 consecutive hours free from all duty within the past 168 consecutive hour periods. Answer (B) is incorrect. The flight duty period may be extended by as much as 2 hours, not 1 hour. Answer (C) is incorrect. The flight duty period may be extended by as much as 2 hours. However, if the flight duty period has been extended by more than 30 minutes, but less than 2 hours, it cannot be extended a second time to take advantage of the 2-hour extension permissible under 14 CFR 117.

questions.study_unit_name: NTSB Part 830, FAR Part 1, FAR Part 61, FAR Part 117, FAR Part 119
questions.question_text: For a short-call reserve, the reserve availability period may not exceed 12 hours. 14 hours. 16 hours.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Short-call reserve availability period may not exceed 14 hours. Answer (A) is incorrect. Short-call reserve availability period may exceed 12 hours, but not 14 hours. Answer (C) is incorrect. Short-call reserve availability may not exceed 14 hours.

questions.study_unit_name: NTSB Part 830, FAR Part 1, FAR Part 61, FAR Part 117, FAR Part 119
questions.question_text: Limiting flight time for all flightcrew members will include
questions.choice_a: instruction flight hours, commercial flying, and flying for any certificate holder.
questions.choice_b: any flying by flightcrew members for any certificate holder or 91K program manager.
questions.choice_c: flying by flightcrew members for any certificate holder or 91K program manager and any other commercial flight time.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: The limitations of 14 CFR 117 include all flying by flightcrew members on behalf of any certificate holder or 91K Program Manager during the applicable periods. Answer (A) is incorrect. Instruction flight hours are not addressed in 14 CFR 117. Answer (C) is incorrect. Instruction flight hours are not addressed in 14 CFR 117.

questions.study_unit_name: NTSB Part 830, FAR Part 1, FAR Part 61, FAR Part 117, FAR Part 119
questions.question_text: Flight duty period hours for flight crew members are limited to
questions.choice_c: 180 hours in any 672 consecutive hours.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Note that this question is asking about flight duty period hours. No certificate holder may schedule and no flightcrew member may accept an assignment if the flightcrew member's total flight duty period will exceed 60 flight duty period hours in any 168 consecutive hours and 190 flight duty period hours in any 672 consecutive hours. Answer (B) is incorrect. This question is asking about flight duty period hours. Flightcrew member's total flight duty periods may exceed 170 hours in any 672 consecutive hours. Answer (C) is incorrect. This question is asking about flight duty period hours. Flightcrew member’s total flight duty periods may exceed 180 hours in any 672 consecutive hours.

questions.study_unit_name: NTSB Part 830, FAR Part 1, FAR Part 61, FAR Part 117, FAR Part 119
questions.question_text: Flightcrew member's flight duty periods are limited to
questions.choice_c: 60 hours in any 7 days.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: No certificate holder may schedule and no flightcrew member may accept an assignment if the flightcrew member’s total flight duty period will exceed: 1. 60 flight duty period hours in any 168 consecutive hours or 2. 190 flight duty period hours in any 672 consecutive hours. Answer (B) is incorrect. No certificate holder may schedule and no flightcrew member may accept an assignment if the flightcrew member’s total flight duty period will exceed 60 hours, not 70 hours, in any 168-hour limitation. Answer (C) is incorrect. No certificate holder may schedule and no flightcrew member may accept an assignment if the flightcrew member’s total flight duty period will exceed 60 hours, not 7 days, in any 168 consecutive hours.

questions.study_unit_name: NTSB Part 830, FAR Part 1, FAR Part 61, FAR Part 117, FAR Part 119
questions.question_text: Flight duty period hours for flightcrew members are limited to
questions.choice_c: 100 hours in any 672 consecutive hours.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Note that this question is asking about flight duty period hours. No certificate holder may schedule and no flightcrew member may accept an assignment if the flightcrew member's total Flight Duty Period will exceed 60 flight duty period hours in.any 168 consecutive hours. Answer (A) is incorrect. This question is asking about flight duty period hours. Seventy hours in any 168 consecutive hours would exceed the allowable total Flight Duty Period for a flightcrew member. Answer (C) is incorrect. This question is asking about flight duty period hours. One hundred hours in any 168 consecutive hours would exceed the allowable total Flight Duty Period for a flightcrew member.

questions.study_unit_name: NTSB Part 830, FAR Part 1, FAR Part 61, FAR Part 117, FAR Part 119
questions.question_text: Flight time hours for flightcrew members are limited to
questions.choice_c: 60 hours in any 168 consecutive hours.
questions.explanation: ___ DISCUSSION: Note that this question is asking about flight time hours. No certificate holder may schedule and no flightcrew member may accept an assignment if the flightcrew member’s oe flight time will exceed 100 hours in any 672 consecutive ours. __ Answer (B) is incorrect. This question is asking about flight time hours. Flightcrew members may exceed 80 hours of flight time in any 672 consecutive hours. Answer (C) is incorrect. This question is asking about flight time hours. Flightcrew members may exceed 60 hours of flight time in any 672 consecutive hours.

questions.study_unit_name: NTSB Part 830, FAR Part 1, FAR Part 61, FAR Part 117, FAR Part 119
questions.question_text: A flightcrew member must be given a rest period before beginning any reserve or flight duty period, of
questions.choice_a: 24 consecutive hours free from any duty in the past 7 consecutive calendar days.
questions.choice_b: 36 consecutive hours in the past 168 consecutive hours.
questions.choice_c: 30 consecutive hours in the past 168 consecutive hours.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Before beginning any reserve or flight duty period a flightcrew member must be given at least 30 consecutive hours free from all duty within the past 168 consecutive hour period (i.e., 7 days). Answer (A) is incorrect. Before beginning any reserve or flight duty period a flightcrew member must be given at least 30, not 24, consecutive hours free from all duty within the past 168 consecutive hour period (i.e., 7 days). Answer (B) is incorrect. Before beginning any reserve or flight duty period a flightcrew member must be given at least 30, not 36, consecutive hours free from all duty within the past 168 consecutive hour period (i.e., 7 days).

questions.study_unit_name: NTSB Part 830, FAR Part 1, FAR Part 61, FAR Part 117, FAR Part 119
questions.question_text: To be considered acclimated in a new theater of operations, a required flight crew member must have ___ consecutive hours of rest.
questions.explanation: This regulation went into effect January 4, 2014. It reads as follows: If a flight crew member operating in a new theater has received 36 consecutive hours of rest, that flight crew member is acclimated. Answer (A) is incorrect. A period of 24 hours is not enough time to become acclimated per the regulations of 36 hours. Answer (B) is incorrect. A period of 30 hours is not enough time to become acclimated as outlined in the regulations. It is short of the required 36 hours.

questions.study_unit_name: NTSB Part 830, FAR Part 1, FAR Part 61, FAR Part 117, FAR Part 119
questions.question_text: No flightcrew member may accept an assignment without scheduled rest opportunities for
questions.choice_a: more than 3 consecutive nighttime flights that infringe on the window of circadian low.
questions.choice_b: more than 4 consecutive nighttime flights that infringe on the window of circadian low ina 168 hour period.
questions.choice_c: consecutive nighttime flights beginning after 0001 hours local home base time.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: “Window of circadian low” means a period of maximum sleepiness that occurs between 0200 and 0559 during a physiological night. A certificate holder may schedule and a flightcrew member may accept up to five consecutive flight duty periods that infringe on the window of circadian low if the certificate holder provides the flightcrew member with an opportunity to rest in a suitable accommodation during each of the consecutive nighttime flight duty periods. The rest opportunity must be at least 2 hours, measured from the time that the flightcrew member reaches the suitable accommodation, and must be between 2200 and 0800 local time, the rest is scheduled before the beginning of the flight duty period, and the rest period may not be less than scheduled. Otherwise, no certificate holder may schedule and no flightcrew member may accept more than three consecutive flight duty periods that infringe on the window of circadian low. Answer (B) is incorrect. No certificate holder may schedule and no flightcrew member may accept more than three, not four, consecutive flight duty periods that infringe on the window of circadian low. Answer (C) is incorrect. No certificate holder may schedule and no flightcrew member may accept more than three consecutive flight duty periods that infringe on the window of circadian low.

questions.study_unit_name: NTSB Part 830, FAR Part 1, FAR Part 61, FAR Part 117, FAR Part 119
questions.question_text: A provisional airport is an airport approved by the Administrator for use by an air carrier certificate holder for the purpose of
questions.choice_a: obtaining provisions and fuel when unable, due to winds, to proceed direct to the regular airport.
questions.choice_b: having the aircraft catered (foods, beverages, or supplies).
questions.choice_c: providing service to a community when the regular airport is unavailable.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: A provisional airport is an airport approved by the Administrator (FAA) for use by an air carrier certificate holder for the purpose of providing service to a community when the regular airport used by the certificate holder is unavailable. Answer (A) is incorrect. An alternate airport, not a provisional airport, is an airport used to obtain provisions and fuel when an aircraft is unable, due to winds, to proceed directly to the regular airport. Answer (B) is incorrect. A provisional airport is an airport approved by the FAA for use by an air carrier certificate holder for the purpose of providing service to a community when the regular airport is unavailable, not for the purpose of having the airplane catered.

questions.study_unit_name: NTSB Part 830, FAR Part 1, FAR Part 61, FAR Part 117, FAR Part 119
questions.question_text: An airport approved by the Administrator for use by an air carrier certificate holder for the purpose of providing service to a community when the regular airport is not available is a/an
questions.choice_c: alternate airport.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Provisional airport is defined as an airport approved by the Administrator (FAA) for use by a certificate holder for the purpose of providing service to a community when the regular airport used by the certificate holder is not available. Answer (A) is incorrect. A destination airport is the airport of intended landing when departing on a flight, not necessarily an airport approved by the FAA for use by an air carrier certificate holder for the purpose of providing service to a community when the regular airport is not available. Answer (C) is incorrect. An alternate airport means an airport at which an aircraft may land if a landing at the intended airport becomes inadvisable, not necessarily an airport approved by the FAA for use by an air carrier certificate holder for the purpose of providing service to a community when the regular airport is not available.

questions.study_unit_name: NTSB Part 830, FAR Part 1, FAR Part 61, FAR Part 117, FAR Part 119
questions.question_text: A person who is not authorized to conduct direct air carrier operations, but who is authorized by the Administrator to conduct operations as a U.S. commercial operator, will be issued
questions.choice_c: an Operating Certificate.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: A person who is not authorized to conduct direct air carrier operations, but who is authorized by the Administrator (FAA) to conduct operations as a U.S. commercial operator, will be issued an Operating Certificate. Answer (A) is incorrect. An Air Carrier Certificate is issued to a person who is authorized, rather than to a person who is not authorized, to conduct direct air carrier operations. Answer (B) is incorrect. A person who is not authorized to conduct direct air carrier operations, but who is authorized by the FAA to conduct operations as a U.S. commercial operator, will be issued an Operating Certificate, not a Supplemental Air Carrier Certificate. Additionally, there is no certificate called a Supplemental Air Carrier Certificate.

questions.study_unit_name: NTSB Part 830, FAR Part 1, FAR Part 61, FAR Part 117, FAR Part 119
questions.question_text: Which document specifically authorizes a person to operate an aircraft in a particular geographic area?
questions.choice_c: Dispatch Release.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Under Part 121 or 135, no person may operate an aircraft in a geographical area unless its operations specifications specifically authorize the certificate holder to operate in that area. Answer (B) is incorrect. An operating certificate is issued to a business to conduct Part 121 or 135 operations; it does not state where the aircraft can operate. Answer (C) is incorrect. A dispatch release authorizes a flight to begin, not the geographic area in which a person can operate an aircraft. : ras Part 119 contains certification rules for Parts 121, 125, an

questions.study_unit_name: NTSB Part 830, FAR Part 1, FAR Part 61, FAR Part 117, FAR Part 119
questions.question_text: The kinds of operation that a certificate holder is authorized to conduct are specified in the
questions.choice_b: application submitted for an Air Carrier or Operating Certificate, by the applicant.
questions.choice_c: Air Carrier Certificate or Operating Certificate. END OF STUDY UNIT
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: The kinds of operation that a certificate holder is authorized to conduct are specified in the certificate holder's operations specifications, i.e., domestic, flag, supplemental, commuter, or on-demand operations. Answer (B) is incorrect. While an applicant must inform the FAA as to what kinds of operation (s)he wants to operate when applying for an Air Carrier or Operating Certificate, the kinds of operation that a certificate holder is authorized to conduct are specified in the operations specifications. Answer (C) is incorrect. The kinds of operation that a certificate holder is authorized to conduct are specified in the operations specifications, not on the Air Carrier or Operating Certificate.

questions.study_unit_name: FAR Part 91, Civil Aviation Security, Hazardous Materials
questions.question_text: The pilot in command (PIC) of an aircraft must always
questions.choice_a: follow ATC instructions regardless of the PIC’s perceptions of the aircraft's situation.
questions.choice_b: abide by ATC instructions, even if the instructions do not make sense to the pilot.
questions.choice_c: remember the PIC is the final authority pe to the operation of the aircraft in the air or on the grounds, ; {
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: The PIC of an aircraft is directly responsible for, and is the final authority as to, the operation of that aircraft. Answer (A) is incorrect. The instructions given by ATC do not supersede the responsibilities taken on by the PIC. It is the PIC who is directly responsible for the operation of the aircraft in the air and on the ground. Answer (B) is incorrect. The PIC of an aircraft is directly responsible for, and is the final authority as to, the operation of that aircraft. The PIC is not required to accept instructions from ATC if (s)he does not understand them or believes complying with those instructions could put the aircraft, its crew, or passengers, at risk.

questions.study_unit_name: FAR Part 91, Civil Aviation Security, Hazardous Materials
questions.question_text: No person may operate a U.S. registered civil aircraft
questions.choice_a: for which an AFM or RFM is required by part 21 section 21.5 unless there is a current, approved operator’s manual available.
questions.choice_b: for which an AFM or RFM is required by _ ion ap M or RFM available.
questions.choice_c: for which an AFM or RFM is required by part 21 section 21.5 unless there is a current, approved AFM or RFM available or the manual specified in part 135 section 135.19(b).
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: According to 14 CFR 21.5, the holder of a Type Certificate or the licensee of a Type Certificate is required to make available to the owner of an aircraft at the time of delivery a current approved Airplane or Rotorcraft Flight Manual. Answer (A) is incorrect. According to 14 CFR 21.5, the holder of a Type Certificate or the licensee of a Type Certificate is required to make available to the owner of an aircraft at the time of delivery a current approved Airplane or Rotorcraft Flight Manual. The regulation does not recognize an approved operator's manual as an acceptable substitute. Answer (C) is incorrect. According to 14 CFR 21.5, the holder of a Type Certificate or the licensee of a Type Certificate is required to make available to the owner of an aircraft at the time of delivery a Current approved Airplane or Rotorcraft Flight Manual. It is not appropriate to reference 14 CFR 135.19(b) in this case, as that regulation relates to pilot deviations from the rules of that part in an emergency situation.

questions.study_unit_name: FAR Part 91, Civil Aviation Security, Hazardous Materials
questions.question_text: Pilot performance can be seriously degraded by
questions.choice_c: prescription medications only.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Over-the-counter drugs that are analgesics, such as aspirin, acetaminophen, and ibuprofen, have few side effects when taken in the correct dosage. Some people are allergic to certain analgesics or may suffer from stomach irritation. Flying usually is not restricted when taking these drugs. Flying is almost always precluded while using prescription analgesics, such as drugs containing propoxyphene, oxycodone, meperidine, and codeine since these drugs are known to cause side effects, such as mental confusion, dizziness, headaches, nausea, and vision problems. Some antibiotics can produce dangerous side effects, such as balance disorders, hearing loss, nausea, and vomiting. Even if the antibiotics are safe for use while flying, the cause requiring the antibiotic may prohibit flying. 14 CFR prohibits pilots from performing crewmember duties while using any medication that affects the body in any way contrary to safety. The safest rule is not to fly as a crewmember while taking any medication, unless approved to do so by the FAA. If there is any doubt regarding the effects of any medication, consult an AME before flying. Answer (B) is incorrect. Over-the-counter and prescribed medications can cause serious degrading issues to a pilot’s performance. Answer (C) is incorrect. Prescribed and over-the- counter medications can cause serious degrading issues to a pilot's performance.

questions.study_unit_name: FAR Part 91, Civil Aviation Security, Hazardous Materials
questions.question_text: At what maximum indicated airspeed may a reciprocating-engine-powered airplane be operated within Class D airspace?
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Unless otherwise authorized or required by ATC, no person may operate an aircraft at or below 2,500 ft. AGL within 4 NM of the primary airport of a Class C or Class D airspace area at an indicated airspeed of more than 200 Kt. Answer (A) is incorrect. The maximum indicated airspeed within Class D airspace is 200 kt., not 156 kt. Answer (B) is incorrect. The maximum indicated airspeed within Class D airspace is 200 kt., not 180 kt.

questions.study_unit_name: FAR Part 91, Civil Aviation Security, Hazardous Materials
questions.question_text: What is the maximum indicated airspeed a turbine- powered aircraft may be operated below 10,000 feet MSL?
questions.choice_c: 230 knots.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Unless otherwise authorized by ATC, no person may operate an aircraft below 10,000 ft. MSL at an indicated airspeed of more than 250 kt. (288 MPH). Answer (A) is incorrect. The maximum authorized airspeed below 10,000 ft. MSL is 288 MPH, not 288 kt. Answer (C) is incorrect. The maximum authorized holding speed for civil turbojets from above 6,000 ft. through 14,000 ft., not the maximum airspeed below 10,000 ft. MSL, is 230 kt.

questions.study_unit_name: FAR Part 91, Civil Aviation Security, Hazardous Materials
questions.subsection: 91.123 Compliance with ATC Clearances and Instructions
questions.question_text: At what maximum indicated airspeed can a reciprocating-engine airplane operate in the airspace underlying Class B airspace?
questions.choice_c: 230 knots.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: No person may operate an aircraft in the airspace underlying a Class B airspace area, or in a VFR corridor designated through a Class B airspace area, at an indicated airspeed of more than 200 kt. (230 MPH). Answer (A) is incorrect. The maximum indicated airspeed in the airspace underlying Class B airspace is 200 kt., not 180 kt. Answer (C) is incorrect. The maximum authorized airspeed in the airspace underlying a Class B airspace area is 230 MPH, not 230 Kt.

questions.study_unit_name: FAR Part 91, Civil Aviation Security, Hazardous Materials
questions.subsection: 91.123 Compliance with ATC Clearances and Instructions
questions.question_text: At what maximum indicated airspeed can a B-727 operate within Class B airspace without special ATC authorization?
questions.choice_c: 275 knots.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Unless otherwise authorized or required by ATC, no person may operate an aircraft in Class B airspace at an indicated airspeed of more than 250 Kt. Answer (A) is incorrect. The maximum authorized holding speed for civil turbojet aircraft operating from above 6,000 ft. through 14,000 ft., not for aircraft operating within Class B airspace, is 230 kt. Answer (C) is incorrect. The maximum indicated airspeed within Class B airspace is 250 kt., not 275 Kt.

questions.study_unit_name: FAR Part 91, Civil Aviation Security, Hazardous Materials
questions.subsection: 91.123 Compliance with ATC Clearances and Instructions
questions.question_text: What is the maximum indicated airspeed a reciprocating-engine-powered airplane may be operated within Class B airspace?
questions.choice_c: 250 knots.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Unless otherwise authorized or required by ATC, no person may operate an aircraft in Class B airspace at an indicated airspeed of more than 250 Kt. Answer (A) is incorrect. The maximum indicated airspeed within Class B airspace is 250 kt., not 180 kt. Answer (B) is incorrect. The maximum authorized holding speed for civil turbojet aircraft from 6,000 ft. through 14,000 ft., not for aircraft operating within Class B airspace, is 230 kt.

questions.study_unit_name: FAR Part 91, Civil Aviation Security, Hazardous Materials
questions.subsection: 91.123 Compliance with ATC Clearances and Instructions
questions.question_text: When may ATC request a detailed report on an emergency even though a rule has not been violated?
questions.choice_c: When the emergency occurs in controlled airspace.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Each pilot in command who is given priority by ATC in an emergency shall submit a detailed report of that emergency within 48 hr. to the manager of that ATC facility, if requested by ATC. Answer (B) is incorrect. During an emergency when no rule has been violated, ATC may request a detailed report only if priority is given, not anytime an emergency occurs. Answer (C) is incorrect. During an emergency when no rule has been violated, ATC may request a detailed report only if priority is given regardless of the airspace.

questions.study_unit_name: FAR Part 91, Civil Aviation Security, Hazardous Materials
questions.subsection: 91.123 Compliance with ATC Clearances and Instructions
questions.question_text: During an emergency, a pilot in command does not deviate from a 14 CFR rule but is given priority by ATC. To whom or under what condition is the pilot required to submit a written report?
questions.choice_a: To the manager of the General Aviation District Office within 10 days.
questions.choice_b: To the manager of the facility in control within 10 days. juest by ATC, submit a written report jours to the ATC manager.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Each pilot in command who is given priority by ATC in an emergency shall submit a detailed report of that emergency within 48 hr. to the manager of that ATC facility, if requested by ATC. Answer (A) is incorrect. If requested by ATC, the detailed report is to be given to the manager of the ATC facility in control at the time that the emergency occurred, not to the General Aviation District Office. Answer (B) is incorrect. A detailed written report is to be submitted only if it was requested by ATC (it is not mandatory). Also, the report must be submitted within 48 hr., not 10 days.

questions.study_unit_name: FAR Part 91, Civil Aviation Security, Hazardous Materials
questions.question_text: A pilot approaching to land at a class D airport in a turbine-powered airplane on a runway served by a VASI shall itude at or above the glide slope Raita 1g Tcseusty for a saf %
questions.choice_b: use the VASI only when weather conditions are below basic VFR.
questions.choice_c: not use the VASI unless a clearance for a VASI approach is received.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: An airplane approaching to land on a runway served by a visual approach slope indicator shall maintain an altitude at or above the glide slope until a lower altitude is necessary for a safe landing. Answer (B) is incorrect. A VASI should be used at all times, both in VFR and during the transition out of IFR weather. Answer (C) is incorrect. A VASI should be used at all times when available and is not considered an instrument approach.

questions.study_unit_name: FAR Part 91, Civil Aviation Security, Hazardous Materials
questions.question_text: A pilot of a turbine-powered airplane should climb as rapidly as practicable after taking off to what altitude?
questions.choice_c: 5,000 feet AGL.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Unless otherwise required by departure procedure or the applicable distance-from-clouds criteria, each pilot of a turbine-powered airplane and each pilot of a large airplane shall climb to an altitude of 1,500 ft. above the surface as rapidly as practicable after takeoff. Answer (A) is incorrect. Turbine-powered or large aircraft (in excess of 12,500 Ib.) should climb as rapidly as possible to 1,500 ft., not 1,000 ft., above the surface. Answer (C) is incorrect. All turbine-powered or large aircraft should climb as rapidly as possible to 1,500 ft., not 5,000 ft., above the surface. ¥ Answer (C) is correct. (14 CFR 91.129) DISCUSSION: Federal Aviation Regulations state that, at a Class D airport, each pilot operating a large or turbine-powered airplane must enter the traffic pattern at 1,500 AGL and maintain 1,500 feet AGL until further descent is required for a safe landing. Answer (A) is incorrect. Unless otherwise noted in the Chart Supplement, an uncontrolled airport would have a traffic pattern of 1,000 feet AGL. Answer (B) is incorrect. Each pilot operating a large or turbine-powered airplane must enter the traffic pattern of a Class D airport at 1,500 AGL. Military aircraft may (sometimes) enter at 2,500 feet. i Answer (A) is correct. (14 CFR 91.155) DISCUSSION: In Class E airspace less than 10,000 ft. MSL, the basic VFR weather minimums are in-flight visibility of 3 SM and a distance from clouds of 500 ft. below, 1,000 ft. above, and 2,000 ft. horizontal. Answer (B) is incorrect. The visibility requirement is 3 SM, not 5 SM, and the distances from clouds should be 1,000 ft. above and 500 ft. below. Answer (C) is incorrect. The distances from clouds should be 1,000 ft. above, not below, and 500 ft. below, not above.

questions.study_unit_name: FAR Part 91, Civil Aviation Security, Hazardous Materials
questions.question_text: A turbine-engine-powered, or large airplane, shall enter the traffic pattern at an airport in Class D airspace at
questions.choice_c: 1,500 feet AGL.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: In Class E airspace at or above 10,000 ft. MSL, the basic VFR weather minimums are in-flight visibility of 5 SM and a distance from clouds of 1,000 ft. above or below and 1 SM horizontal. Answer (A) is incorrect. Three SM, 1,000 ft. above, 500 ft. below, and 2,000 ft. horizontal are the VFR weather minimums for below, not at or above, 10,000 ft. MSL. Answer (C) is incorrect. The vertical separation from clouds is 1,000 ft. both above and below.

questions.choice_a: 3statute miles, 1,000 feet above, 500 feet © below, and 2,000 feet horizontal.

questions.choice_c: 3statute miles, 500 feet above, 1,000 feet below, and 2,000 feet horizontal.

questions.study_unit_name: FAR Part 91, Civil Aviation Security, Hazardous Materials
questions.question_text: What is the minimum flight visibility and distance from clouds for flight at 10,500 feet, in Class E airspace, with a VFR-on-Top clearance during daylight hours?
questions.choice_a: 3statute miles, 1,000 feet above, 500 feet below, and 2,000 feet horizontal. 5 statute miles, 1,000 feet above, 1,000 feet

MSL (area 4 in Fig. 128), the basic VFR weather minimums are in-flight visibility of 1 SM and a distance from clouds of 500 ft. below, 1,000 ft. above, and 2,000 ft. horizontal. Answer (B) is incorrect. Three SM, 1,000 ft. above, 2,000 ft. horizontal, and 500 ft. below are the VFR weather minimums for controlled, not uncontrolled, airspace below 10,000 ft. MSL. Answer (C) is incorrect. Five SM, 1,000 ft. above, 1 SM horizontal, and 1,000 ft. below are the VFR weather minimums for controlled and uncontrolled airspace at or above, not below, 10,000 ft. MSL.

questions.study_unit_name: FAR Part 91, Civil Aviation Security, Hazardous Materials
questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 128 on page 63.) What is the minimum in-flight visibility and distance from clouds required for a VFR-on-Top flight at 9,500 feet MSL (above 1,200 feet AGL) during daylight hours for the circle 3 area?
questions.choice_c: pe 000 feet; (F) 2,000 feet;

MSL (area 3 in Fig. 128), the basic VFR weather minimums are in- flight visibility of 3 SM and a distance from clouds of 500 ft. below, 1,000 ft. above, and 2,000 ft. horizontal. Answer (A) is incorrect. The minimum visibility required is 3 SM, not 2,000 ft. Answer (B) is incorrect. In Class E airspace at or above 10,000 ft., not below 10,000 ft., the minimum visibility is 5 SM.

questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 128 on page 63.) What is the minimum in-flight visibility and distance from clouds required in VFR conditions above clouds at 13,500 feet MSL (above 1,200 feet AGL) during daylight hours for the circle 2 area?

MSL (area 2 in Fig. 128), the basic VFR weather minimums are in-flight visibility of 5 SM and a distance from clouds of 1,000 ft. above or below and 1 SM horizontal. Answer (A) is incorrect. The minimum cloud distances for VFR in uncontrolled airspace above 1,200 ft. AGL and below, not above, 10,000 ft. MSL are 1,000 ft. above, 2,000 ft. horizontal, and 500 ft. below. Answer (B) is incorrect. Visibility minimum is 5 SM, not 3 SM.

questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 128 on page 63.) A flight is to be conducted in VFR-on-Top conditions at 12,500 feet MSL (above 1,200 feet AGL). What is the in-flight visibility and distance from clouds required for operation during daylight hours for the circle 1 area?

The distance from cloud requirements listed are for below, not above, 10,000 ft. MSL. Answer (C) is incorrect. The visibility requirement is 5 SM, not 3 SM, and the horizontal separation requirement from clouds i is 1 SM, not 2,000 ft.

questions.study_unit_name: FAR Part 91, Civil Aviation Security, Hazardous Materials
questions.subsection: 91.171 VOR Equipment Check for IFR Operations
questions.question_text: Which checks and inspections of flight instruments or instrument systems must be accomplished before an aircraft can be flown under IFR?
questions.choice_a: VOR within 30 days and altigaiebeysiome and transponder within 24 calendar months.
questions.choice_b: ELT test within 30 days, altimeter systems within 12 calendar months, and transponder within 24 calendar months.
questions.choice_c: Airspeed indicator within 24 calendar months, altimeter system within 24 calendar months, and transponder within 12 calendar months.
questions.explanation: No person may operate a civil aircraft under IFR unless (1) the VOR has been operationally checked within the preceding 30 days and (2) the altimeter systems and transponder have been tested and inspected and found to comply with FAA standards within the preceding 24 calendar months. Answer (B) is incorrect. ELTs do not have to be tested every 30 days, and the altimeter must be checked along with the transponder every 24 calendar months, not 12 months. Answer (C) is incorrect. The transponder must be inspected every 24 calendar months, not 12 months.

questions.study_unit_name: FAR Part 91, Civil Aviation Security, Hazardous Materials
questions.subsection: 91.171 VOR Equipment Check for IFR Operations
questions.question_text: Which entry shall be recorded by the person performing a VOR operational check?
questions.choice_a: Frequency, radial and facility used, and bearing error.
questions.choice_b: Flight hours and number of days since last check, and bearing error. te: ‘Date, place, bearing error, and signature.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Each person making a VOR operational check shall enter the date, place, and bearing error and sign the aircraft log or other record. Answer (A) is incorrect. The frequency and radial used are not required entry items; however, date and place must be entered along with signature. Answer (B) is incorrect. Flight hours and number of days since last check are not required entry items; however, date, place, and signature must be included.

questions.study_unit_name: FAR Part 91, Civil Aviation Security, Hazardous Materials
questions.subsection: 91.171 VOR Equipment Check for IFR Operations
questions.question_text: What is the maximum permissible variation between the two bearing indicators on a dual VOR system when checking one VOR against the other?
questions.choice_a: 4° on the ground and in flight.
questions.choice_b: 6° on the ground and in flight.
questions.choice_c: 6° in flight and 4° on the ground.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: If dual-system VOR units are installed in the aircraft, the person checking the equipment may check one system against the other. Both systems are tuned to the same VOR station and the indicated bearings to that station noted. The maximum permissible variation between the two indicated bearings is 4°. Answer (B) is incorrect. Six degrees is the maximum permissible bearing error when checking a single VOR system against a published radial while in the air, not when checking a dual VOR system. Answer (C) is incorrect. Six degrees is the maximum permissible bearing error when checking a single VOR system while in the air. Regardless of whether you are on the ground or airborne, the maximum permissible bearing error is only 4° when using a cross-check between dual VORs.

questions.study_unit_name: FAR Part 91, Civil Aviation Security, Hazardous Materials
questions.subsection: 91.171 VOR Equipment Check for IFR Operations
questions.question_text: During a VOT check of the VOR equipment, the course deviation indicator centers on 356° with the TO/FROM reading FROM. This VOR equipment may
questions.choice_a: be used if 4° is entered on a correction card and subtracted from all VOR courses.
questions.choice_b: be used during IFR flights, since the error is limits.
questions.choice_c: not be used during IFR flights, since the TO/ FROM should read TO.
questions.explanation: When using an FAA-approved VOT station or a test signal radiated by a certificated and appropriately rated radio repair station, the maximum permissible bearing error is plus or minus 4°. To use the VOT service, tune in the VOT frequency on your VOR receiver. With the course deviation indicator (CDI) centered, the omni-bearing selector should read 0° with the TO/FROM indication showing “FROM.” Since the CDI for this aircraft reads 356°, it is within the 4° permissible bearing error, So the VOR equipment may be used during IFR flights. Answer (A) is incorrect. Four degrees is the maximum permissible bearing error for a VOT check, and no correction card exists for VORs. VORs are either within or not within acceptable limits. Answer (C) is incorrect. A “TO” reading would ace if the omni-bearing selector were selected to 180°, not 0°.

questions.study_unit_name: FAR Part 91, Civil Aviation Security, Hazardous Materials
questions.subsection: 91.173 ATC Clearance and Flight Plan Required
questions.question_text: What record shall be made by the pilot performing a VOR operational check?
questions.choice_a: The date, frequency of VOR or VOT, number of hours flown since last check, and signature in the aircraft log. ir F bearing error, and signature in ‘the Mp obeatis recente ;
questions.choice_c: The date, approval or disapproval, tach reading, and signature in the aircraft log or other permanent record.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Each person making a VOR operational check shall enter the date, place, and bearing error, and sign the aircraft log or other record. Answer (A) is incorrect. Neither the frequency nor the number of hours flown since the last check need be entered in the log or record. However, bearing error and place must be entered in the log. Answer (C) is incorrect. Neither the tach reading nor approval or disapproval need be entered in the record of a VOR operational check. Also, if the VOR systems are not within the permissible bearing error, the aircraft cannot be flown in IFR operations, but it may still be flown under VFR.

questions.study_unit_name: FAR Part 91, Civil Aviation Security, Hazardous Materials
questions.subsection: 91.173 ATC Clearance and Flight Plan Required
questions.question_text: If an airborne checkpoint is used to check the VOR system for IFR operations, the maximum bearing error permissible is
questions.choice_c: plus or minus 4°.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: If neither a test signal nor a designated checkpoint on the surface is available, use an airborne checkpoint designated by the FAA Administrator or, outside the United States, by an appropriate authority. The maximum permissible bearing error is plus or minus 6° for airborne checks. Answer (B) is incorrect. The maximum bearing error is plus or minus 6°, not plus 6° or minus 4°. Answer (C) is incorrect. Plus or minus 4° is the maximum permissible bearing error when using a VOT check or a radio repair facility, not when checking a single VOR while in the air.

questions.study_unit_name: FAR Part 91, Civil Aviation Security, Hazardous Materials
questions.subsection: 91.173 ATC Clearance and Flight Plan Required
questions.question_text: A pilot is operating in Class G airspace. If existing weather conditions are below those for VFR flight, an IFR flight plan must be filed and an ATC clearance received prior to
questions.choice_a: takeoff if weather conditions are below IFR minimums.
questions.choice_c: entering IFR weather conditions.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: No person may operate an aircraft in controlled airspace under IFR unless that person has (1) filed an IFR flight plan and (2) received an appropriate ATC clearance. Answer (A) is incorrect. If IFR weather conditions exist and you wish to depart, an ATC clearance and IFR flight plan are not required in Class G airspace. Answer (C) is incorrect. An IFR flight plan and an ATC clearance are not required to fly in IMC (instrument meteorological conditions) in Class G airspace. , Answer (A) is correct. (14 CFR 91.175) DISCUSSION: When conducting an ILS approach, each pilot operating a civil aircraft shall immediately execute an appropriate missed approach procedure upon arrival at the DH (decision height) on the glide slope when the required visual references are not acquired. Answer (B) is incorrect. A visual descent point is used for nonprecision approaches (e.g., VOR/DME), not on an ILS approach. Answer (C) is incorrect. The expiration of the time listed on the approach chart is used for nonprecision approaches (e.g., VOR), not for an ILS approach.

questions.study_unit_name: FAR Part 91, Civil Aviation Security, Hazardous Materials
questions.subsection: 91.173 ATC Clearance and Flight Plan Required
questions.question_text: Assuming that all ILS components are operating and the required visual references are not acquired, the missed approach should be initiated upon
questions.choice_a: arrival at the DH on the glide slope.
questions.choice_b: arrival at the visual descent point.
questions.choice_c: expiration of the time listed on the approach chart for missed approach.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Unless otherwise authorized by the FAA, no pilot operating an aircraft under FAR Part 121, 125, 127, 129, or 135 may take off from a civil airport under IFR unless weather conditions are at or above weather minimums for IFR takeoff prescribed for that airport under FAR Part 97. If takeoff minimums are not prescribed under FAR Part 97 for a particular airport, the minimum visibility for aircraft having more than two engines is 1/2 SM visibility. Answer (A) is incorrect. One SM visibility is for aircraft, other than helicopters, having two engines or less, not three engines. Answer (C) is incorrect. Minimum ceilings are not specified in FAR Part 91 for takeoff minimums.

questions.study_unit_name: FAR Part 91, Civil Aviation Security, Hazardous Materials
questions.subsection: 91.173 ATC Clearance and Flight Plan Required
questions.question_text: When takeoff minimums are not prescribed for a civil airport, what are the takeoff minimums under IFR for a three-engine airplane? De ASM:
questions.choice_c: 300 feet and 1/2 SM.

questions.study_unit_name: FAR Part 91, Civil Aviation Security, Hazardous Materials
questions.subsection: 91.173 ATC Clearance and Flight Plan Required
questions.question_text: What action should be taken when a pilot is “cleared for approach” while being radar vectored on an unpublished route?
questions.choice_b: Remain at last assigned altitude until — stablished on a published route segment.
questions.choice_c: Descend to initial approach fix altitude.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: When operating on an unpublished route or while being radar vectored, the pilot, when (s)he receives an approach clearance, shall maintain the last altitude assigned to him or her until the aircraft is established on a segment of a published route or instrument approach procedure unless a different altitude is assigned by ATC. Answer (A) is incorrect. A pilot should maintain the last altitude assigned by ATC, not descend to minimum vector altitude. Answer (C) is incorrect. A pilot should maintain the last altitude assigned by ATC, not descend to initial approach fix altitude.

questions.study_unit_name: FAR Part 91, Civil Aviation Security, Hazardous Materials
questions.subsection: 91.173 ATC Clearance and Flight Plan Required
questions.question_text: If being radar vectored to the final approach course of a published instrument approach that specifies “NO PT,” the pilot should
questions.choice_a: advise ATC that a procedure turn will not be executed. 2 ea eRe eases ane
questions.choice_c: execute a holding-pattern type procedure turn.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: In the case of a radar vector to a final approach course or fix or an approach for which the procedure specifies “NO PT,” a pilot may not make a procedure turn unless cleared to do so by ATC. Answer (A) is incorrect. A procedure turn is not authorized or expected to be executed for this instrument approach; therefore, advising ATC of your intention to omit a procedure turn is not necessary. Answer (C) is incorrect. If the published instrument approach specifies “NO PT,” you should follow the published approach procedure and not execute a holding-pattern-type procedure turn.

questions.study_unit_name: FAR Part 91, Civil Aviation Security, Hazardous Materials
questions.subsection: 91.173 ATC Clearance and Flight Plan Required
questions.question_text: Under which condition, if any, may a pilot descend below DH or MDA when using the ALSF-1 approach light system as the primary visual reference for the intended runway?
questions.choice_a: Under no condition can the approach light system serve as a necessary visual reference for descent below DH or MDA.
questions.choice_b: Descent to the intended runway is authorized as long as any portion of the approach light system can be seen.
questions.choice_c: The approach light system can be used as a vistel raferenee Bice pt that descent me bove TDZE requires that the red light nd identifiable. ~— Qe
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: When a DH or an MDA is applicable to an instrument approach, no pilot may operate a civil aircraft at any airport below the authorized MDA or continue an approach below the authorized DH unless a visual reference, e.g., the approach light system, for the intended runway is clearly visible, except that the pilot may not descend below 100 ft. above the touchdown zone elevation using the approach lights as a reference unless the red terminating bars or the red side row bars are distinctly visible and identifiable. Answer (A) is incorrect. Approach lighting systems can be used as a reference below the DH or MDA up to 100 ft. above the TDZE, at which point the runway environment must be in sight. Answer (B) is incorrect. The approach lighting system can be used only to within 100 ft. of the TDZE, at which point the runway environment must be in sight.

questions.study_unit_name: FAR Part 91, Civil Aviation Security, Hazardous Materials
questions.subsection: 91.173 ATC Clearance and Flight Plan Required
questions.question_text: The visibility criteria for a particular instrument approach procedure is RVR 40. What minimum ground visibility may be substituted for the RVR value?
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: If RVR (runway visual range) minimums for takeoff or landing are prescribed in an instrument approach procedure, but RVR is not reported for the runway of intended operation, the RVR minimum shall be converted to ground visibility. An RVR of 40 is equivalent to 3/4 SM visibility. Answer (A) is incorrect. A 1/2 SM visibility is equivalent to an RVR of 24, not 40. Answer (C) is incorrect. A 1/4 SM visibility is equivalent to an RVR of 16, not 40.

questions.study_unit_name: FAR Part 91, Civil Aviation Security, Hazardous Materials
questions.subsection: 91.173 ATC Clearance and Flight Plan Required
questions.question_text: What altitude is a pilot authorized to fly when cleared for an ILS approach? The pilot
questions.choice_a: may begin a descent to the procedure turn altitude.
questions.choice_b: must maintain the last assigned altitude until established on a published route or segment of the approach with published attitadal
questions.choice_c: may descend from the assigned altitude only when established on the final approach course.
questions.explanation: _ DISCUSSION: When operating on an unpublished route or while being vectored to an ILS approach, the pilot, upon receiving an approach clearance, shall maintain the last altitude assigned to him or her until the aircraft is established on a segment of a published route or instrument approach procedure unless a different altitude is assigned by ATC. _ Answer (A) is incorrect. Descent to the procedure turn altitude can be commenced only when you are established on that segment of the instrument approach. Answer (C) is incorrect. The pilot does not have to be established on the final approach course to descend from the last assigned altitude, if (s)he is established on a published route segment with a specified lower minimum altitude than the last assigned altitude.

questions.study_unit_name: FAR Part 91, Civil Aviation Security, Hazardous Materials
questions.subsection: 91.173 ATC Clearance and Flight Plan Required
questions.question_text: When must the pilot initiate a missed approach procedure from an ILS approach? RRs Dao if iy visual snot wt the ot dis le, or t visual refe Seat lost.
questions.choice_b: When the time has expired after reaching the DA/DH and the runway environment is not clearly visible.
questions.choice_c: At the DA/DH when the runway is not clearly visible.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Each pilot operating a civil aircraft shall immediately execute an appropriate missed approach procedure upon arrival at the missed approach point (DA/DH on an ILS approach) if the visual runway references are not distinctly visible or at any time thereafter if those visual references are lost below the DA/DH. Answer (B) is incorrect. As soon as the DA/DH is reached on an ILS approach, regardless of the elapsed time, a missed approach procedure should be executed if visual references are not obtained or if, at any time after the DA/DH is reached, visual reference is lost. Answer (C) is incorrect. The runway itself does not have to be visible at the DH to continue with the approach. A pilot may use the approach lighting system to within 100 ft. of the TDZE or any of the following: (1) threshold lights, (2) REIL lights, (3) VASI lights, (4) TDZ lights, or (5) runway lights.

questions.study_unit_name: FAR Part 91, Civil Aviation Security, Hazardous Materials
questions.subsection: 91.173 ATC Clearance and Flight Plan Required
questions.question_text: When proceeding to the alternate airport, which minimums apply?
questions.choice_a: The IFR alternate minimums section in front of the NOAA IAP book.
questions.choice_b: 2000-3 for at least 1 hour before until 1 hour after the ETA. S. THOMCiateainiaunte chown On the TAREHat for the airport.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: No pilot operating a civil aircraft may land that aircraft when the flight visibility is less than the visibility prescribed in the standard instrument approach procedure being used. Answer (A) is incorrect. The alternate minimums listed in the NOAA IAP (National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration Instrument Approach procedure) book refer to the ceiling and visibility requirements for that airport in order to file it as an alternate, not the ceiling and visibility required to execute an instrument approach. Answer (B) is incorrect. These minimums apply to the destination airport. If your destination airport has a forecast ceiling of at least 2,000 ft. and a visibility of at least 3 SM 1 hr. before to 1 hr. after your ETA, an alternate airport need not be filed in the flight plan.

questions.study_unit_name: FAR Part 91, Civil Aviation Security, Hazardous Materials
questions.subsection: 91.173 ATC Clearance and Flight Plan Required
questions.question_text: The prescribed visibility criteria of RVR 32 for the runway of intended operation is not reported. What minimum ground visibility may be used instead of the RVR value?
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: If RVR (runway visual range) minimums for takeoff or landing are prescribed in an instrument approach procedure, but RVR is not reported for the runway of intended operation, the RVR minimum shall be converted to ground visibility. An RVR of 32 is equivalent to 5/8 SM visibility. Answer (A) is incorrect. A 3/8 SM visibility is not a published RVR value used on instrument approach procedure charts. Answer (C) is incorrect. A 3/4 SM visibility is equivalent to an RVR of 40, not 32.

questions.study_unit_name: FAR Part 91, Civil Aviation Security, Hazardous Materials
questions.subsection: 91.173 ATC Clearance and Flight Plan Required
questions.question_text: What minimum ground visibility may be used instead of a prescribed visibility criteria of RVR 16 when that RVR value is not reported? ‘A. 1/4 SM.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: If RVR (runway visual range) minimums for takeoff or landing are prescribed in an instrument approach procedure, but RVR is not reported for the runway of intended operation, the RVR minimum shall be converted to ground visibility. An RVR of 16 is equivalent to 1/4 SM visibility. Answer (B) is incorrect. A 3/4 SM visibility is equivalent to an RVR of 40, not 16. Answer (C) is incorrect. A 1/2 SM visibility is not a published RVR value used on instrument approach procedure charts.

questions.study_unit_name: FAR Part 91, Civil Aviation Security, Hazardous Materials
questions.subsection: 91.173 ATC Clearance and Flight Plan Required
questions.question_text: Which facility may be substituted for the middle marker during a Category | ILS approach?
questions.choice_c: Compass locator.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: The basic ground components of an ILS are the localizer, glide slope, outer marker, and middle marker. A compass locator or precision radar may be substituted for the outer or middle marker. Answer (A) is incorrect. VOR/DME can be substituted for the outer marker only, not the middle marker. Answer (B) is incorrect. Surveillance radar may be substituted for the outer marker only, not the middle marker.

questions.study_unit_name: FAR Part 91, Civil Aviation Security, Hazardous Materials
questions.subsection: 91.180 Operations within Airspace Designated as Reduced Vertical Separation Minimum (RVSM) Airspace
questions.question_text: Before requesting RVSM clearance, each person
questions.choice_a: shall correctly annotate the flight plan.
questions.choice_b: must file an ICAO RVSM flight plan.
questions.choice_c: should file for odd altitudes only.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Each person requesting a clearance to operate within RVSM airspace shall correctly annotate the flight plan filed with air traffic control with the status of the operation and aircraft with regard to RVSM approval. Answer (B) is incorrect. There is no separate flight plan required for RVSM operations; rather, operators must notate that the aircraft and flight crew are both authorized to use RVSM airspace. Answer (C) is incorrect. RVSM operations may be conducted at both even- and odd-thousand-foot increments.

questions.study_unit_name: FAR Part 91, Civil Aviation Security, Hazardous Materials
questions.subsection: 91.180 Operations within Airspace Designated as Reduced Vertical Separation Minimum (RVSM) Airspace
questions.question_text: A person may not file for RVSM clearance unless
questions.choice_a: the pilots have been briefed on RVSM procedures.
questions.choice_b: the operator is authorized and the aircraft has been approved.
questions.choice_c: the aircraft can maintain FL440 or FL450.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Reduced Vertical Separation Minimum (RVSM) airspace requires special qualifications. The operator and the aircraft used by the operator must be approved by the Administrator. Answer (A) is incorrect. While pilot training is required to operate in RVSM airspace, there are additional aircraft requirements as well. Answer (C) is incorrect. Within RVSM airspace, ATC separates aircraft by a minimum of 1,000 feet vertically between flight (FL) 290 and FL 410 (inclusive). The altitudes in this answer fall outside the scope of RVSM operations.

questions.study_unit_name: FAR Part 91, Civil Aviation Security, Hazardous Materials
questions.subsection: 91.180 Operations within Airspace Designated as Reduced Vertical Separation Minimum (RVSM) Airspace
questions.question_text: \f a pilot is being radar vectored in IFR conditions and loses radio communications with ATC, what action should be taken?
questions.choice_a: Fly directly to the next point shown on the IFR flight plan and continue the flight.
questions.choice_b: Squawk 7700 and climb to VFR on Top.
questions.choice_c: Fly direct to a fix, route, or airway specified in the vector clearance.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: If two-way radio communications failure occurs in IFR weather conditions and the pilot is being vectored by ATC, the pilot should proceed by the direct route from the point of radio failure to the fix, route, or airway specified in the vector clearance. Answer (A) is incorrect. The route shown on the flight plan should be used only if an assigned route, vector, or expected route has not been received. Answer (B) is incorrect. A climb should be initiated only in order to establish the highest of either the assigned, MEA, or expected altitude, and the transponder = be set to 7600, not 7700, to indicate communications ailure.

questions.study_unit_name: FAR Part 91, Civil Aviation Security, Hazardous Materials
questions.subsection: 91.180 Operations within Airspace Designated as Reduced Vertical Separation Minimum (RVSM) Airspace
questions.question_text: While in IFR conditions, a pilot experiences two- way radio communications failure. Which route should be flown in the absence of an ATC assigned route or a route ATC has advised to expect in a further clearance?
questions.choice_a: The most direct route to the filed alternate airport.
questions.choice_b: An off-airway route to the point of departure.
questions.choice_c: The route filed in the flight plan.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: If two-way radio communications failure occurs in IFR conditions, each pilot shall continue with the flight according to the following: (1) by the route assigned in the last ATC clearance; (2) if being radar vectored, by the direct route from the point of radio failure to the fix, route, or airway specified in the vector clearance; (3) in the absence of an assigned route, by the route that ATC has advised may be expected in a further clearance; or (4) by the route filed in the flight plan. __ Answer (A) is incorrect. Upon two-way radio communications failure, an approach and landing should be made or attempted at the destination airport first before going to the filed alternate airport. Answer (B) is incorrect. In the absence of an assigned route or expected route, only the filed route in the flight plan may be used. An off-airway route should not be used unless it was filed as part of the flight plan.

questions.study_unit_name: FAR Part 91, Civil Aviation Security, Hazardous Materials
questions.subsection: 91.180 Operations within Airspace Designated as Reduced Vertical Separation Minimum (RVSM) Airspace
questions.question_text: What is the purpose of having a transmitter light on a radio transmitter?
questions.choice_a: To let you know the power is on.
questions.choice_b: To inform you the transmitter is not working.
questions.choice_c: To acknowledge you are transmitting.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: The transmit light is there to acknowledge that you are transmitting. It could also inform the pilot that the radio transmit button is stuck. __ Answer (A) is incorrect. If all the other lights and frequency displays on the radio are on, the radio power is on. Answer (B) is incorrect. The transmit light is not a malfunction light. However, there is a malfunction if it does not come on when trying to transmit.

questions.study_unit_name: FAR Part 91, Civil Aviation Security, Hazardous Materials
questions.subsection: 91.180 Operations within Airspace Designated as Reduced Vertical Separation Minimum (RVSM) Airspace
questions.question_text: A pilot is flying in IFR weather conditions and has two-way radio communications failure. What altitude should be used?
questions.choice_a: Last assigned altitude, ate eee Nee advised t , or the MEA, r is highest.
questions.choice_b: An altitude that is at least 1,000 feet above the highest obstacle along the route. G: i VFR altitude that is above the MEA for each eg.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: If a pilot experiences two-way radio communications failure, (s)he should maintain the highest of the following altitudes or flight levels for the route segment being flown: (1) the altitude or flight level assigned in the last ATC clearance received, (2) the minimum altitude/flight level, or (3) the altitude or flight level ATC has advised may be expected in a further clearance. Answer (B) is incorrect. This altitude is what a MOCA, or minimum obstruction clearance altitude, provides. Answer (C) is incorrect. VFR altitudes or regulations should never be used while flying in IFR weather conditions. A VFR altitude should be used only in VFR weather conditions if VFR-on-top clearance has been approved by ATC.

questions.study_unit_name: FAR Part 91, Civil Aviation Security, Hazardous Materials
questions.subsection: 91.180 Operations within Airspace Designated as Reduced Vertical Separation Minimum (RVSM) Airspace
questions.question_text: What altitude and route should be used if the pilot is flying in IFR weather conditions and has two-way radio communications failure?
questions.choice_a: Continue on the route specified in the cl se al the highest of the following: the last assigned altitude, altitude ATC has informed the pilot to Soar ahete MEA.
questions.choice_b: Descend to MEA and, if clear of clouds, proceed to the nearest appropriate airport. If not clear of clouds, maintain the highest of the MEA’s along the clearance route.
questions.choice_c: Fly the most direct route to the destination, maintaining the last assigned altitude or MEA, whichever is higher.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: If radio communications failure occurs in IFR conditions and a clearance has been received, the pilot should fly the route assigned in the last ATC clearance and the highest of either the assigned altitude, MEA, or altitude to be expected when informed by ATC. Answer (B) is incorrect. The highest of either the MEA, expected altitude, or assigned altitude should be used. Answer (C) is incorrect. If ATC advises the pilot that (s)he may expect an altitude after reaching a clearance limit, and it is higher than the published MEA or assigned altitude, the expected altitude should be used. The route to be used should be the one assigned by ATC, as specified in a vector clearance, the route ATC has advised may be expected, or in the absence of all of these, the route as filed in the flight plan, not the most direct route.

questions.study_unit_name: FAR Part 91, Civil Aviation Security, Hazardous Materials
questions.subsection: 91.180 Operations within Airspace Designated as Reduced Vertical Separation Minimum (RVSM) Airspace
questions.question_text: After experiencing two-way radio communica- tions failure en route, when should a pilot begin the descent for the instrument approach?
questions.choice_a: Upon arrival at any initial approach fix for the instrument approach procedure but not before the flight plan ETA as amended by ATC.
questions.choice_b: Upon arrival at the holding fix depicted on the instrument approach procedure at the corrected ETA, plus or minus 3 minutes.
questions.choice_c: At the primary initial approach fix for the instrument approach procedure at the ETA shown on the flight plan or the EFC time, whichever is later.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: If two-way radio communications failure occurs en route and your clearance limit is not a fix from which an approach begins, leave the clearance limit at the expect- further-clearance time if one has been received, or if no EFC has been received, proceed to clearance limit, and proceed to the appropriate initial approach fix. Commence descent and approach to coincide as closely as possible with the ETA. Answer (B) is incorrect. An approach should begin at the initial approach fix, not at a holding fix, as close as possible to the ETA. Answer (C) is incorrect. An EFC time supersedes a flight plan ETA and should be used if one has been received (no matter if the EFC is sooner or later than the flight plan ETA).

questions.study_unit_name: FAR Part 91, Civil Aviation Security, Hazardous Materials
questions.subsection: 91.180 Operations within Airspace Designated as Reduced Vertical Separation Minimum (RVSM) Airspace
questions.question_text: A pilot is holding at an initial approach fix after having experienced two-way radio communications failure. When should that pilot begin descent for the instrument approach?
questions.choice_a: At the EFC time, if this is within plus or minus 3 minutes of the flight plan ETA as amended by ATC.
questions.choice_b: At flight plan ETA as amended by ATC.
questions.choice_c: At the EFC time as amended by ATC.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: The radio failure was experienced before the pilot entered the holding pattern at the initial approach fix. Since the pilot would not have received an expect-further-clearance (EFC) time, (s)he should commence descent for the instrument approach as close as possible to the estimated time of arrival (ETA), as calculated from the filed or amended (with ATC) estimated time en route. Answer (A) is incorrect. The radio failure occurred prior to arrival at the initial approach fix, so it is unlikely an EFC time would have been received. Additionally, the approach should begin either at the EFC time or, if one has not been received, at flight plan ETA. Answer (C) is incorrect. The radio failure occurred before the pilot entered the holding pattern at the initial approach fix; thus, (s)he would not have received holding instructions that would have contained an EFC time.

questions.study_unit_name: FAR Part 91, Civil Aviation Security, Hazardous Materials
questions.subsection: 91.187 Operation under IFR in Controlled Airspace: Malfunction Reports
questions.question_text: While flying IFR in controlled airspace, if one of the two VOR receivers fails, which course of action should the pilot in command follow?
questions.choice_a: No call is required if one of the two VOR receivers is operating properly.
questions.choice_c: Notify the dispatcher via company frequency.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: The pilot in command of each aircraft operated in controlled airspace under IFR shall report as soon as practicable to ATC any malfunction of navigational, approach, or communication equipment occurring in flight. Answer (A) is incorrect. Any malfunction of a navigational radio should be reported, no matter how slightly it may affect the conduct of the flight. Answer (C) is incorrect. Although the operations manual may require that the dispatcher be notified, the pilot in command must report the failure to ATC immediately.

questions.study_unit_name: FAR Part 91, Civil Aviation Security, Hazardous Materials
questions.subsection: 91.187 Operation under IFR in Controlled Airspace: Malfunction Reports
questions.question_text: What action is necessary when a partial loss of ILS receiver capability occurs while operating in controlled airspace under IFR?
questions.choice_a: Continue as cleared and file a written report to the Administrator if requested.
questions.choice_b: If the aircraft is equipped with other radios suitable for executing an instrument approach, no further action is necessary.
questions.choice_c: Report the malfunction immediately to ATC.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: The pilot in command of each aircraft operated in controlled airspace under IFR shall report as soon as practicable to ATC any malfunction of navigational, approach, or communication equipment occurring in flight. Answer (A) is incorrect. Any malfunction of approach equipment must be reported in flight, not by a written report. Answer (B) is incorrect. Although another type of instrument approach may be executed if permission is granted by ATC, any malfunction of approach equipment should be reported.

questions.study_unit_name: FAR Part 91, Civil Aviation Security, Hazardous Materials
questions.subsection: 91.187 Operation under IFR in Controlled Airspace: Malfunction Reports
questions.question_text: What action should be taken if one of the two VHF radios fail while IFR in controlled airspace?
questions.choice_c: Monitor the VOR receiver.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: The pilot in command of each aircraft operated in controlled airspace under IFR shall report as soon as practicable to ATC any malfunction of navigational, approach, or communication equipment occurring in flight. Answer (B) is incorrect. Although a communications failure has been experienced, it is only a partial one. One operational VHF radio is still available, and all other radios are working normally. Thus, a squawk of 7600 is not needed. Answer (C) is incorrect. An operable VHF radio is still available for communication. Monitoring a NAVAID is not needed. The only pilot action required is notification to ATC of the problem.

questions.study_unit_name: FAR Part 91, Civil Aviation Security, Hazardous Materials
questions.subsection: 91.187 Operation under IFR in Controlled Airspace: Malfunction Reports
questions.question_text: When may a pilot descend below 100 feet above the touchdown zone elevation during a Category II ILS instrument approach when only the approach lights are visible? After passing the visual descent point (VDP). w When the RVR is 1,600 feet or more.
questions.choice_c: When the red terminal bar of the approach light systems are in sight. Requirements
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Unless otherwise authorized, a pilot operating an aircraft in a Category Il approach may not descend below 100 ft. above the touchdown zone elevation using the approach lights as a reference unless the red terminating bars or the red side row bars are distinctly visible and identifiable. __ Answer (A) is incorrect. A VDP is not used in conjunction with Category II ILS instrument approaches. Answer (B) is incorrect. Although 1,600 ft. may be the required in-flight visibility, descending below 100 ft. above the touchdown zone elevation based on the approach lights also requires sight of the red terminating bars.

questions.study_unit_name: FAR Part 91, Civil Aviation Security, Hazardous Materials
questions.subsection: 91.187 Operation under IFR in Controlled Airspace: Malfunction Reports
questions.question_text: When is DME or suitable RNAV required for an instrument flight?
questions.choice_a: Ator above 24,000 feet MSL if VOR navigational equipment is required.
questions.choice_c: Above 12,500 feet MSL.
questions.explanation: _ DISCUSSION: For flights at or above FL 240, DME or Suitable RNAV is required for instrument flights when VOR navigational equipment is required. ___ Answer (B) is incorrect. The only requirement for operation in a terminal radar service area is-two-way radio communication, not DME or a suitable RNAV. Answer (C) is incorrect. The figure of 12,500 ft. MSL is not an altitude significant to airspace definition or other operating rules.

questions.study_unit_name: FAR Part 91, Civil Aviation Security, Hazardous Materials
questions.question_text: A function of the minimum equipment list is to indicate instruments or equipment which
questions.choice_a: are required to be operative for overwater passenger air carrier flights.
questions.choice_b: may be inoperative for a one-time ferry flight of a large airplane to a maintenance base.
questions.choice_c: may be inoperative prior to beginning a flight in an aircraft.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: A function of an approved minimum equipment list (MEL) is to indicate the instruments and/or equipment which may be inoperative prior to beginning a flight in an aircraft and to state under what conditions the flight can be operated with that inoperative equipment. Answer (A) is incorrect. An MEL prescribes which instruments and equipment may be inoperative prior to beginning a flight, not which items are required to be operative for overwater air carrier flights. Answer (B) is incorrect. A special flight permit, not an MEL, authorizes a one-time ferry flight of a large airplane to a maintenance base with inoperative instruments and/or equipment.

questions.study_unit_name: FAR Part 91, Civil Aviation Security, Hazardous Materials
questions.question_text: An approved minimum equipment list or FAA Letter of Authorization allows certain instruments or equipment to be inoperative
questions.choice_a: prior to beginning a flight in an aircraft if prescribed procedures are followed.
questions.choice_b: anytime with no other documentation required or procedures to be followed.
questions.choice_c: for a one-time ferry flight of a large airplane to a maintenance base without further documentation from the operator or FAA with passengers on board.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: The minimum equipment list (MEL) contains units and systems for which operation of the aircraft with some deviation from airworthiness standards or operating rules have been approved. These are components and systems that the operator or manufacturer has proven that the aircraft may safely operate without under specific conditions. Answer (B) is incorrect. The MEL lists components and systems that the operator or manufacturer has proven that the aircraft may safely operate without under specific conditions. No additional documentation is required, but specific procedures must be complied with in order for the operator to fly the aircraft. Answer (C) is incorrect. The MEL lists components and systems that the operator or manufacturer has proven that the aircraft may safely operate without under specific conditions. There is no limitation that restricts the operator to a single flight to a maintenance base, with or without passengers.

questions.study_unit_name: FAR Part 91, Civil Aviation Security, Hazardous Materials
questions.question_text: In what altitude structure is a transponder required when operating in controlled airspace?
questions.choice_a: Above 12,500 feet MSL, excluding the airspace at and below 2,500 feet AGL.
questions.choice_b: Above 10,000 feet MSL, excluding the airspace at and below 2,500 feet AGL.
questions.choice_c: Above 14,500 feet MSL, excluding the airspace at and below 2,500 feet AGL.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: No person may operate an aircraft at or above 10,000 ft. MSL, excluding the airspace at and below 2,500 ft. AGL, unless that aircraft is equipped with an operable Mode C transponder. Answer (A) is incorrect. The figure of 12,500 ft. MSL is not an altitude significant to airspace definition or other operating rules. Answer (C) is incorrect. The figure of 14,500 ft. MSL is the floor of Class E airspace (unless designated at a lower altitude), not the floor at which an operable Mode C transponder is required.

questions.study_unit_name: FAR Part 91, Civil Aviation Security, Hazardous Materials
questions.question_text: For what purpose may cockpit voice recorders and flight data recorders not be used?
questions.choice_a: Determining causes of accidents and occurrences under investigation by the NTSB.
questions.choice_b: Determining any certificate action, or civil penalty, arising out of an accident or occurrence.
questions.choice_c: Identifying procedures that may have been conducive to any accident, or occurrence resulting in investigation under NTSB Part 830.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Information obtained from flight data and cockpit voice recorders is used to assist in determining the cause of accidents or occurrences in connection with investigations under NTSB Part 830. The FAA does not use the cockpit voice recorder for any civil penalty or certificate action. Answer (A) is incorrect. Cockpit voice recorders and flight data recorders are used to determine causes of accidents or occurrences. Answer (C) is incorrect.
