Flight data recorders and cockpit voice recorders are used to identify any procedure, malfunction, or failure that may have contributed to an accident or occurrence.

questions.study_unit_name: FAR Part 91, Civil Aviation Security, Hazardous Materials
questions.subsection: 91.611 Authorization for Ferry Flight with One Engine Inoperative
questions.question_text: Information obtained from flight data and cockpit voice recorders shall be used only for determining
questions.choice_a: who was responsible for any accident or incident.
questions.choice_b: evidence for use in civil penalty or certificate action.
questions.choice_c: possible causes of accidents or incidents.
questions.explanation: Information obtained from flight data and cockpit voice recorders is used to assist in determining the cause of accidents or occurrences in connection with investigations under NTSB Part 830. The FAA does not use the cockpit voice recorder for any civil penalty or certificate action. Answer (A) is incorrect. Flight data or cockpit voice recorders are used only to determine causes of accidents or incidents, not to determine who was responsible or at fault. Answer (B) is incorrect. Flight data or cockpit voice recorders may not be used for any civil penalty or certificate action.

questions.study_unit_name: FAR Part 91, Civil Aviation Security, Hazardous Materials
questions.subsection: 91.611 Authorization for Ferry Flight with One Engine Inoperative
questions.question_text: How long is cockpit voice recorder and flight recorder data kept, in the event of an accident or occurrence resulting in terminating the flight?
questions.choice_c: 30 days.
questions.explanation: In the event of an accident or occurrence that results in the termination of the flight, any operator who has installed approved flight recorders and cockpit voice recorders shall keep the recorded information for at least 60 days or, if requested by the FAA or the NTSB, for a longer period. Answer (B) is incorrect. The information need be kept for only 60 days, not 90 days. Answer (C) is incorrect. The information must be kept for a minimum of 60 days, not 30 days.

questions.study_unit_name: FAR Part 91, Civil Aviation Security, Hazardous Materials
questions.subsection: 91.611 Authorization for Ferry Flight with One Engine Inoperative
questions.question_text: Which operational requirement must be observed when ferrying an air carrier airplane when one of its three turbine engines is inoperative?
questions.choice_a: The weather conditions at takeoff and destination must be VFR.
questions.choice_b: The flight cannot be conducted between official sunset and official sunrise.
questions.choice_c: Weather conditions must exceed the basic VFR minimums for the entire route, including takeoff and landing.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: The holder of an air carrier operating certificate or a certificate issued under Part 125 may conduct a ferry flight of an aircraft equipped with three turbine engines, one of which is inoperative, to a base for the purpose of repairing that engine only if the weather conditions at the takeoff and destination airports are VFR. Answer (B) is incorrect. A ferry flight may be conducted after sunset and before sunrise as long as the takeoff and destination airports are VFR. Answer (C) is incorrect. The weather conditions must be VFR only for takeoff and landing, not over the entire route.

questions.study_unit_name: FAR Part 91, Civil Aviation Security, Hazardous Materials
questions.subsection: 91.611 Authorization for Ferry Flight with One Engine Inoperative
questions.question_text: Which operational requirement must be observed when ferrying a large, turbine-engine-powered airplane when one of its engines is inoperative?
questions.choice_a: The weather conditions at takeoff and destination must be VFR.
questions.choice_b: Weather conditions must exceed the basic VFR minimums for the entire route, including takeoff and landing.
questions.choice_c: The flight cannot be conducted between official sunset and sunrise.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: The holder of an air carrier operating certificate or a certificate issued under Part 125 may conduct a ferry flight of an aircraft with one of its engines inoperative to a base for the purpose of repairing that engine only if the weather conditions at the takeoff and destination airports are VFR. Answer (B) is incorrect. The weather conditions must be VFR only for takeoff and landing, not over the entire route. Answer (C) is incorrect. A ferry flight may be conducted after sunset and ree sunrise as long as the takeoff and destination airports are

questions.study_unit_name: FAR Part 91, Civil Aviation Security, Hazardous Materials
questions.subsection: 91.611 Authorization for Ferry Flight with One Engine Inoperative
questions.question_text: When a turbine-engine-powered airplane is to be ferried to another base for repair of an inoperative engine, which operational requirement must be observed?
questions.choice_a: Only the required flight crewmembers may be on board the airplane.
questions.choice_b: The existing and forecast weather for departure, en route, and approach must be VFR.
questions.choice_c: No passengers except authorized maintenance personnel may be carried.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: The holder of an air carrier operating certificate or a certificate issued under Part 125 may conduct a ferry flight of an aircraft with one of its turbine engines inoperative to a base for the purpose of repairing that engine with only the required flight crewmembers on board the airplane. Answer (B) is incorrect. The weather conditions must be VFR only for takeoff and landing, not over the entire route. Answer (C) is incorrect. Only the required flight crewmembers may be aboard. Maintenance personnel, mechanics, and others may not be carried. .

questions.study_unit_name: FAR Part 91, Civil Aviation Security, Hazardous Materials
questions.subsection: 91.705 Operations within the North Atlantic Minimum Navigation Performance Specifications Airspace
questions.question_text: Which operational requirement must be observed by a commercial operator when ferrying a large, three-engine, turbojet-powered airplane from one facility to another to repair an inoperative engine?
questions.choice_a: The computed takeoff distance to reach V, must not exceed 70 percent of the effective runway length.
questions.choice_b: The existing and forecast weather for ered en route, and approach must be
questions.choice_c: No passengers may be carried.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: The holder of an air carrier operating certificate or a certificate issued under Part 125 may conduct a ferry flight of an aircraft with one of its turbine engines inoperative to a base for the purpose of repairing that engine with only the required flight crewmembers on board the airplane. No passengers may be carried. Answer (A) is incorrect. Runway length allowing V, in less than 70% of the runway is not required for ferry flights with one engine inoperative. Answer (B) is incorrect. The weather conditions must be VFR only for takeoff and landing, not over the entire route.

questions.study_unit_name: FAR Part 91, Civil Aviation Security, Hazardous Materials
questions.subsection: 91.705 Operations within the North Atlantic Minimum Navigation Performance Specifications Airspace
questions.question_text: A commercial operator plans to ferry a large, four- engine, reciprocating-engine-powered airplane from one facility to another to repair an inoperative engine. Which is an operational requirement for the three- engine flight?
questions.choice_a: The gross weight at takeoff may not exceed 75 percent of the maximum certificated gross weight.
questions.choice_b: Weather conditions at the takeoff and destination airports must be VFR.
questions.choice_c: The computed takeoff distance to reach V, must not exceed 70 percent of the effective runway length.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: The holder of an air carrier operating certificate or a certificate issued under Part 125 may conduct a ferry flight of an aircraft with one of its reciprocating engines inoperative to a base for the purpose of repairing that engine only if the weather conditions at the takeoff and destination airports are VFR. Answer (A) is incorrect. Gross weight restrictions do not exist for ferry flights with one engine inoperative. Answer (C) is incorrect. Runway length allowing V, in less than 70% of the runway is not required for ferry flights with one engine inoperative.

questions.study_unit_name: FAR Part 91, Civil Aviation Security, Hazardous Materials
questions.subsection: 91.705 Operations within the North Atlantic Minimum Navigation Performance Specifications Airspace
questions.question_text: You are assigned to ferry a large, three-engine, turbojet-powered airplane from one facility to another to repair an inoperative engine. You know you are restricted to
questions.choice_a: VFR weather for takeoff, en route, and landing.
questions.choice_c: acomputed takeoff distance to reach V1 that cannot exceed 70 percent of the effective runway length.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: According to 14 CFR Part 91.611, persons other than required flight crewmembers shall not be carried during the flight of a turbojet-powered airplane being ferried from one facility to another to repair an inoperative engine. Answer (A) is incorrect. Although 14 CFR Part 91.611 does require VFR weather for takeoff and landing, it does not require VFR weather for the en route portion of the flight of a turbojet- powered airplane being ferried from one facility to another to repair an inoperative engine. Answer (C) is incorrect. There is no requirement that a turbojet-powered airplane being ferried from one facility to another to repair an inoperative engine reach V, within the first 70% of effective runway length.

questions.study_unit_name: FAR Part 91, Civil Aviation Security, Hazardous Materials
questions.subsection: 91.705 Operations within the North Atlantic Minimum Navigation Performance Specifications Airspace
questions.question_text: Which publication includes information on operations in the North Atlantic (NAT) Minimum Navigation Performance Specifications Airspace?
questions.explanation: Operations within the NAT Minimum Navigation Performance Specifications (MNPS) Airspace is the title of both 14 CFR Part 91.705 and Appendix C of Part 91. Answer (A) is incorrect. The rules regarding operating requirements of domestic, flag, and supplemental air carriers in the United States are provided in 14 CFR Part 121. Answer (B) is incorrect. ICAO Annex 1, Chapter 2, concerns pilot certificates and ratings.

questions.study_unit_name: FAR Part 91, Civil Aviation Security, Hazardous Materials
questions.subsection: 91.705 Operations within the North Atlantic Minimum Navigation Performance Specifications Airspace
questions.question_text: How may an aircraft operate in North Atlantic (NAT) Minimum Navigation Performance Specifications Airspace with less than the minimum navigation capability required by FAR Part 91, appendix C?
questions.choice_a: By operating under VFR conditions only.
questions.choice_b: By requesting a deviation from the Administrator.
questions.choice_c: By operating only between 2400Z and 0600Z.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: No person may operate a civil aircraft of U.S. Registry in NAT airspace designated as Minimum Navigation Performance Specifications (MNPS) airspace unless the FAA authorizes deviations from the requirements of this section in accordance with Section 3 of Appendix C of FAR Part 91. Answer (A) is incorrect. NAT flights, with or without MNPS, may be conducted in IFR weather conditions as well as VFR. Answer (C) is incorrect. NAT flights, with or without MNPS, do not have time restrictions.

questions.study_unit_name: FAR Part 91, Civil Aviation Security, Hazardous Materials
questions.question_text: A person must report RVSM altitude keeping
questions.choice_a: deviations of 300 feet or more from an assigned altitude.
questions.choice_b: deviations of 245 feet or more from an assigned altitude.
questions.choice_c: deviations of 120 feet or more from an assigned altitude. 2.2 Civil Aviation Security (49 CFR)
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Each operator shall report to the Administrator each event in which the operator's aircraft has exhibited an assigned altitude deviation of 300 feet or more. Answer (B) is incorrect. Deviations of 245 feet of altimetry system error shall be reported to the Administrator. This is not a deviation of an assigned altitude. Answer (C) is incorrect. A deviation of 120 feet of an assigned altitude is not an issue and does not require reporting it to the Administrator.

questions.study_unit_name: FAR Part 91, Civil Aviation Security, Hazardous Materials
questions.question_text: When a passenger notifies the certificate holder prior to checking baggage that an unloaded weapon is in the baggage, what action is required by regulation regarding this baggage?
questions.choice_a: The baggage may be carried in the flightcrew compartment, provided the baggage remains locked, and the key is given to the pilot in command.
questions.choice_b: The baggage must remain locked and carried in an area that is inaccessible to the passenger, and only the passenger retains the key.
questions.choice_c: The baggage must remain locked and stored where it would be inaccessible, and custody of the key shall remain with a designated crewmember.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: When a passenger notifies the certificate holder prior to checking baggage that an unloaded weapon is in his or her baggage, that baggage must remain locked, and only the passenger who checked the baggage may retain the key or combination. The baggage containing the weapon is carried in an area, other than the flight crew compartment, that is inaccessible to passengers. Answer (A) is incorrect. The baggage containing the unloaded weapon must be carried in an area other than, not in, the flight crew compartment, and the person who checked the baggage, not the PIC, should retain the key. Answer (C) is incorrect. The person who checked the baggage, not a designated crewmember, retains custody of the key.

questions.study_unit_name: FAR Part 91, Civil Aviation Security, Hazardous Materials
questions.question_text: A certificate holder is notified that a person specifically authorized to carry a deadly weapon is to be aboard an aircraft. Except in an emergency, how long before loading that flight should the air carrier be notified?
questions.choice_a: Notification is not required, if the certificate holder has a security coordinator.
questions.choice_c: A minimum of 2 hours.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Except in an emergency, a certificate holder should be notified at a minimum of 1 hr. before the flight on which a person who is specifically authorized to carry a deadly weapon is to be aboard that aircraft. Answer (A) is incorrect. Except in an emergency, a certificate holder must be notified at a minimum of 1 hr. before a flight, regardless of whether the certificate holder is required to have a security coordinator. Answer (C) is incorrect. Except in an emergency, a certificate holder must be notified at a minimum of 1 hr., not 2 hr., before a flight.

questions.study_unit_name: FAR Part 91, Civil Aviation Security, Hazardous Materials
questions.question_text: Which applies to the carriage of a person in the custody of law enforcement personnel?
questions.choice_a: The air carrier is not allowed to serve beverages to the person in custody or the law enforcement escort.
questions.choice_b: No more than one person considered to be in the maximum risk category may be carried on a flight, and that person must have at least two armed law enforcement escorts.
questions.choice_c: The person in custody must be seated between the escort and the aisle.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: No more than one person who the certificate holder has been notified is in a maximum risk category may be carried on the airplane, and that person must have at least two armed law enforcement escorts. Answer (A) is incorrect. The air carrier can serve nonalcoholic beverages to the law enforcement escort. Additionally, food or beverage may be served to the person in custody with the authorization of the law enforcement escort. Answer (C) is incorrect. The law enforcement escort must be seated between the person in custody and the aisle; the person in custody is not to be seated between the escort and the aisle.

questions.study_unit_name: FAR Part 91, Civil Aviation Security, Hazardous Materials
questions.question_text: When a person in the custody of law enforcement personnel is scheduled on a flight, what procedures are required regarding boarding of this person and the escort?
questions.choice_a: They shall be boarded before all other passengers board, and deplaned after all the other passengers have left the aircraft.
questions.choice_b: They shall be boarded after all other passengers board, and deplaned before all the other passengers leave the aircraft.
questions.choice_c: They shall board and depart before the other passengers.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Each person in the custody of law enforcement personnel will be boarded before all other passengers board and deplaned after all other passengers have left the aircraft. Answer (B) is incorrect. The person in custody will be boarded before, not after, all other passengers board and deplaned after, not before, all other passengers leave the aircraft. Answer (C) is incorrect. The person in custody will be deplaned after, not before, all other passengers leave the aircraft.

questions.study_unit_name: FAR Part 91, Civil Aviation Security, Hazardous Materials
questions.question_text: Hazardous material shipped on an aircraft must be described and certified on a shipping paper. For what period of time must the originating aircraft operator retain one copy of this document?
questions.choice_c: 90 days.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: The originating aircraft operator must retain for 90 days one copy of each shipping paper describing and certifying hazardous materials. Answer (A) is incorrect. The shipping paper describing and certifying hazardous materials must be retained for 90 days, not 30, by the originating aircraft operator. Answer (B) is incorrect. The shipping paper describing and certifying hazardous materials must be retained for 90 days, not 60, by the originating aircraft operator.

questions.study_unit_name: FAR Part 91, Civil Aviation Security, Hazardous Materials
questions.question_text: Certain classes of hazardous material may be shipped by air but are not permitted aboard passenger-carrying aircraft. How must such material be labeled?
questions.choice_c: CARGO AIRCRAFT ONLY.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: If hazardous material as presented is not permitted aboard passenger-carrying aircraft, it must be labeled with a “Cargo Aircraft Only” label. Answer (A) is incorrect. Hazardous material not permitted aboard passenger-carrying aircraft must be labeled “Cargo Aircraft Only,” not “Dangerous.” Answer (B) is incorrect. Hazardous material not permitted aboard passenger-carrying aircraft must be labeled “Cargo Aircraft Only,” not “Hazardous/ Class X.”

questions.study_unit_name: FAR Part 91, Civil Aviation Security, Hazardous Materials
questions.question_text: Which class of hazardous material must be loaded aboard an aircraft in a position that allows no contact with containers of corrosive materials?
questions.choice_c: Catalytic agents.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: No person may stow a package of a corrosive material on an aircraft next to, or in a position that will allow contact with, a package of flammable solids, oxidizing materials, or organic peroxides. Answer (A) is incorrect. Not all organic chemicals are classified as organic peroxides. Answer (C) is incorrect. Catalytic agents cause a chemical reaction, and they are not necessarily only oxidizing materials.

questions.study_unit_name: FAR Part 91, Civil Aviation Security, Hazardous Materials
questions.question_text: Which procedure must be followed if an operator, when loading magnetized material, cannot avoid placing it in a position where it affects the accuracy of the magnetic compass?
questions.choice_c: Make a special compass swing and calibration.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: No person may load magnetized material (which might cause an erroneous magnetic compass reading) on an aircraft, in the vicinity of a magnetic compass, in a manner that affects its operation. If this requirement cannot be met, a special aircraft swing and compass calibration may be made. Answer (A) is incorrect. A reliable magnetic compass is required. Thus, the airplane cannot be operated until the compass is made reliable by a special compass swing and calibration. Answer (B) is incorrect. A reliable magnetic compass is required. Thus, the airplane cannot be operated until the compass is made reliable by a special compass swing and calibration.

questions.study_unit_name: FAR Part 91, Civil Aviation Security, Hazardous Materials
questions.question_text: (Refer to Legend 34 on page 77.) What is the maximum, if any, net quantity of acetyl bromide in one package that may be carried in a cargo-only aircraft?
questions.choice_c: No limit is specified.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: One gallon of acetyl bromide is the maximum quantity per container that may be carried aboard a cargo-only aircraft. Answer (A) is incorrect. One quart is the maximum per container that may be carried aboard any passenger-carrying, not cargo-only, aircraft. Answer (C) is incorrect. A maximum of 1 gal. per container may be carried aboard any cargo-only aircraft.

questions.study_unit_name: FAR Part 91, Civil Aviation Security, Hazardous Materials
questions.question_text: (Refer to Legend 34 on page 77.) If not excepted, what label, if any, must be placed on a package containing acetone?
questions.choice_c: FLAMMABLE LIQUID.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: The hazardous materials classification of acetone is flammable liquid, and it is required to be labeled (if not excepted) as a “FLAMMABLE LIQUID.” Answer (A) is incorrect. Most hazardous materials require labeling. Answer (B) is incorrect. Acetone is to be labeled “FLAMMABLE LIQUID,” not “POISON.”

questions.study_unit_name: FAR Part 91, Civil Aviation Security, Hazardous Materials
questions.question_text: (Refer to Legend 34 on page 77, Legend 35 on page 78, and Legend 36 on page 79.) What is the maximum, if any, net quantity of acetylene in one package that may be carried in a passenger-carrying aircraft?
questions.choice_c: No limit is specified.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Any amount of acetylene is forbidden aboard passenger-carrying aircraft. Answer (B) is incorrect. A total of 300 Ib. of acetylene may be carried aboard a cargo-only, not passenger-carrying, aircraft. Answer (C) is incorrect. Any amount of acetylene is forbidden aboard passenger-carrying aircraft.

questions.study_unit_name: FAR Part 91, Civil Aviation Security, Hazardous Materials
questions.question_text: (Refer to Legend 36 on page 79.) If not excepted, what label, if any, must be placed on a package containing allethrin?
questions.choice_c: CORROSIVE.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: No label is required on allethrin, which is an ORM-A (other regulated material) classification. Answer (A) is incorrect. ORM-A is the hazardous materials classification by which the proper label is placed on the container. In this case, no label is required. Answer (C) is incorrect. No label is required on allethrin.

questions.study_unit_name: FAR Part 91, Civil Aviation Security, Hazardous Materials
questions.question_text: (Refer to Legend 36 on page 79.) What is the maximum, if any, net quantity of aluminum hydride in one package that may be carried in a passenger- carrying aircraft?
questions.choice_c: Any amount is forbidden.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: The Hazardous Materials Table indicates that any amount of aluminum hydride is forbidden on passenger- Carrying aircraft. ___ Answer (A) is incorrect. Any amount of aluminum hydride is forbidden aboard a passenger-carrying aircraft. Answer (B) is incorrect. An amount of 25 Ib. of aluminum hydride is allowable only on cargo-only, not passenger-carrying, aircraft.

questions.study_unit_name: FAR Part 91, Civil Aviation Security, Hazardous Materials
questions.question_text: The aircraft operator discovers that the label on a container of hazardous material is missing. How should the appropriate replacement label be determined?
questions.choice_c: Hazardous Materials Tables of CFR 49.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Lost or detached labels for packages of hazardous materials must be replaced in accordance with the information provided on the shipping papers. Answer (B) is incorrect. The shipping papers, not the hazardous material index, are used to determine the contents of a package with a missing label. Answer (C) is incorrect. The Hazardous Materials Tables of 49 CFR Part 172 are used to determine the labeling requirements, but the shipping papers will identify the hazardous material in the container.

questions.study_unit_name: FAR Part 91, Civil Aviation Security, Hazardous Materials
questions.question_text: An operator makes a telephone report of an incident involving fire during the loading of hazardous materials. Within what period of time must a written report be submitted?
questions.choice_c: 15 days.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Each operator who transports hazardous materials shall report in writing within 15 days of the date of discovery each incident that occurs during the course of transportation (including loading, unloading, or temporary storage) in which, as a direct result of hazardous materials, fire, breakage, spillage, or suspected contamination occurs. Answer (A) is incorrect. The figure of 48 hr. is not a time limit specified in 49 CFR Part 175. Answer (B) is incorrect. Ten days is the time within which a report concerning an aircraft accident or fire, not a hazardous materials fire, must be reported.

questions.study_unit_name: FAR Part 91, Civil Aviation Security, Hazardous Materials
questions.question_text: What is the maximum weight of hazardous material (other than nonflammable compressed gas) that may be carried in an accessible cargo compartment of a passenger-carrying aircraft?
questions.choice_c: 25 pounds, if classified as ORM-D.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Not more than 50 Ib. net weight of hazardous material (other than nonflammable compressed gas) may be carried in any freight container within an accessible cargo compartment of a passenger-carrying aircraft. Answer (B) is incorrect. Up to 50 Ib., not 10 Ib., of hazardous material, even if corrosive, can be carried in an accessible cargo compartment of a passenger-carrying aircraft. Answer (C) is incorrect. There is no limitation to the amount of ORM material aboard a passenger-carrying aircraft.

questions.study_unit_name: FAR Part 91, Civil Aviation Security, Hazardous Materials
questions.question_text: What is the maximum, if any, number of packages of ORM material that may be transported in a passenger-carrying aircraft?
questions.choice_b: Anumber whose combined transportation indices total 50.
questions.choice_c: A number whose combined transportation indices total 100.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: No limitation applies to the number of packages of ORM material aboard a passenger-carrying aircraft. Answer (B) is incorrect. Combined transportation indices of 50 refer to the maximum number of packages of radioactive materials, not ORM. Answer (C) is incorrect. Combined transportation indices of 100 do not apply to any hazardous material that can be carried on a passenger-carrying aircraft.

questions.study_unit_name: FAR Part 91, Civil Aviation Security, Hazardous Materials
questions.question_text: No person may carry more than __________ of hazardous materials in a passenger-carrying aircraft (disregarding non-flammable compressed gas).
questions.choice_c: 55 pounds net weight.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: For each package containing a hazardous material acceptable for carriage aboard passenger-carrying aircraft, no more than 25 kg (55 pounds) net weight of hazardous material may be loaded in a passenger-carrying aircraft. The reference material for this question is found in Title 49 CFR Part 175, not the more commonly referenced Title 14 CFR Parts 91, 121, or 135. Answer (A) is incorrect. No person may carry more than 25 kg net, not 25 kg gross, weight of hazardous materials in a passenger-carrying aircraft (disregarding non-flammable compressed gas). Answer (B) is incorrect. No person may carry more than 55 pounds, not 25 pounds, net weight of hazardous materials in a passenger-carrying aircraft (disregarding non- flammable compressed gas).

questions.study_unit_name: FAR Part 91, Civil Aviation Security, Hazardous Materials
questions.question_text: If transported in a passenger-carrying aircraft, what is the maximum combined transportation indices of packages containing radioactive materials?
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Packages containing radioactive materials when their combined transport indices exceed 50 may not be carried in a passenger-carrying aircraft. Answer (A) is incorrect. On a passenger-carrying aircraft, the maximum combined transportation indices of packages containing radioactive materials are 50, not 100. Answer (C) is incorrect. On a passenger-carrying aircraft, the maximum combined transportation indices of packages containing radioactive materials are 50, not 25.

questions.study_unit_name: FAR Part 91, Civil Aviation Security, Hazardous Materials
questions.question_text: Who should be notified if there is a suspected radioactive contamination involving a radioactive materials shipment and it is determined that radiological advice is needed?
questions.choice_b: U.S. Energy Research and Development Administration.
questions.choice_c: Nuclear Regulatory Commission.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: If radiological advice or assistance is needed, the U.S. Energy Research and Development Administration should be notified. Answer (A) is incorrect. The Office of Hazardous Materials Regulation would be notified along with the FAA in the event of a fire, injury, or fatality. However, the U.S. Energy Research and Development Administration handles radioactive contamination. Answer (C) is incorrect. The U.S. Energy Research and Development Administration, not the Nuclear Regulatory Commission, provides radiological advice and assistance.

questions.study_unit_name: FAR Part 91, Civil Aviation Security, Hazardous Materials
questions.question_text: What is the minimum distance that a package of radioactive materials bearing the label “RADIOACTIVE YELLOW II” and having a transport index of 15, may be placed from a space continuously occupied by people?
questions.choice_c: 85 inches.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Radioactive Yellow || labeled packages having a transport index of 15 may be no closer than 85 inches to a space continuously occupied by people. Answer (A) is incorrect. Radioactive Yellow I! labeled packages having a transport index of 15 may be no closer than 85 inches, not 36 feet, to a space continuously occupied by people. Answer (B) is incorrect. Radioactive Yellow I! labeled packages having a transport index of 15 may be no closer than 85 inches, not 48 inches, to a space continuously occupied by people.

questions.study_unit_name: FAR Part 91, Civil Aviation Security, Hazardous Materials
questions.question_text: What is the maximum quantity of flammable liquid fuel that may be carried in the cabin of a small, nonscheduled, passenger-carrying aircraft being operated in a remote area of the United States?
questions.choice_c: 20 gallons.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: A small aircraft operating into a remote area in the United States may carry, in other than scheduled passenger operations, not more than 20 gal. of flammable liquid fuel. Answer (A) is incorrect. The maximum amount of flammable liquid fuel that may be carried in the cabin of a small aircraft is 20, not 10, gal. Answer (B) is incorrect. The maximum amount of flammable liquid fuel that may be carried in the cabin of a small aircraft is 20, not 15, gal.

questions.study_unit_name: FAR Part 91, Civil Aviation Security, Hazardous Materials
questions.question_text: What precaution, if any, should be taken if dry ice is carried aboard an aircraft?
questions.choice_a: This material does not require special precautions.
questions.choice_b: A waiver to carry this material should be requested from the certificating FAA district office.
questions.choice_c: Proper ventilation of the aircraft should be assured. END OF STUDY UNIT
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Dry ice is not a corrosive and therefore does not require special packaging or containment. It does require proper ventilation because its vapors displace oxygen, and there is a limit on the amount that may be carried on each type of transport aircraft. Answer (A) is incorrect. There are ventilation and maximum load limitations on dry ice. Answer (B) is incorrect. A waiver is not required; dry ice may be carried if certain precautions are taken.

questions.subsection: 3.1 Approval of Routes: Domestic and Flag Operations
questions.question_text: The North Polar Area (of operations) means the
questions.choice_a: entire area north of 60° N latitude. Answer (A) is incorrect. The North Polar Area is defined in
questions.choice_b: entire area north of 70° N latitude. 14 CFR 121.7 as being the entire area north of 78° N latitude, ’ not the area north of the 60° N latitude line, which includes much
questions.choice_c: entire area north of 78° N latitude. more terrain to the south. Answer (B) is incorrect. The North Polar Area is defined in 14 CFR 121.7 as being the entire area north of 78° N latitude, not the area north of the 70° N latitude line, which extends south of the defined North Polar Area of operations.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: In 14 CFR 121.7, the North Polar Area is defined as the entire area north of 78° N latitude.

questions.subsection: 3.1 Approval of Routes: Domestic and Flag Operations
questions.question_text: The South Polar region includes
questions.choice_a: the entire area south of 60° S latitude. Answer (B) is incorrect. The South Polar region includes
questions.choice_b: the entire area south of 70° S latitude. the entire area south of 60°, not 70°, S latitude. Answer (C) is : F : incorrect. The South Polar region only includes the entire area
questions.choice_c: the entire area north of 70° S latitude and south of 60° S latitude, not just the area north of 70° S latitude. south of 60° S latitude.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: The South Polar region encompasses the entire area south of 60° S latitude.

questions.subsection: 3.1 Approval of Routes: Domestic and Flag Operations
questions.question_text: Required aeronautical data for the approval of
questions.choice_a: runways for diversion operations and maintenance facilities.
questions.choice_b: facilities at each airport or in the immediate data must include, for the protection of the public, facilities at area sufficient to protect the passengers from each airport or in the immediate area sufficient to protect the the elements and to see to their welfare. passengers from the elements and to see to their welfare. Answer (A) is incorrect. Required aeronautical data for the
questions.choice_c: pertinent NOTAMs. approval of ETOPS routes beyond 180 minutes or operations in the North and South Polar areas does not include diversion operations and maintenance facilities. Answer (C) is incorrect. Required aeronautical data for the approval of ETOPS routes beyond 180 minutes or operations in the North and South Polar areas does not include pertinent NOTAMs.
questions.explanation: ETOPS routes beyond 180 minutes or operations in DISCUSSION: Each certificate holder conducting domestic the North and South Polar areas must include or flag operations must show that it has an approved system for obtaining, maintaining, and distributing to appropriate personnel current aeronautical data for each airport it uses to ensure a safe operation at that airport. For ETOPS beyond 180 minutes or operations in the North and South Polar areas, this aeronautical

questions.question_text: Who must the crew of a domestic or flag air carrier airplane be able to communicate with, under normal conditions, along the entire route (in either direction) of flight?
questions.choice_c: Appropriate dispatch office.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Each domestic and flag air carrier must show that a two-way air/ground radio communication system is available at points that will ensure reliable and rapid communications under normal operating conditions over the entire route (either direct or via approved point-to-point circuits) between each airplane and the appropriate dispatch office, and between each airplane and the appropriate air traffic control unit. For all domestic air carrier operations and for flag air carrier operations in the 48 contiguous states and the District of Columbia, the communications systems between each airplane and the dispatch office must be independent of any system operated by the United States. Answer (A) is incorrect. The crew of a domestic or flag air carrier airplane must be able to communicate directly with the appropriate dispatch office, not just ARINC. ARINC is largely owned by a group of airlines and is contracted by the FAA to provide communications support for ATC and meteorological services in portions of international airspace. Answer (B) is incorrect. The crew of a domestic or flag air carrier airplane must be able to communicate with the appropriate dispatch office, not any FSS, along the entire route.

questions.question_text: Which minimum level of RFFS do you need when filing an alternate using 180 minutes ETOPS rule?
questions.choice_c: ICAO Category 2.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: For ETOPS up to 180 minutes, each designated ETOPS Alternate Airport must have RFFS equivalent to that specified by ICAO as Category 4, or higher. Answer (B) is incorrect. Each designated ETOPS Alternate Airport must have RFFS equivalent to that specified by ICAO as Category 4, or higher. Answer (C) is incorrect. Each designated ETOPS Alternate Airport must have RFFS equivalent to that specified by ICAO as Category 4, or higher.

questions.question_text: For flight planning, a Designated ETOPS Alternate Airport for ETOPS up to 180 minutes, must have RFFS equivalent to that specified by
questions.choice_a: |ICAO Category 3, unless the airport's RFFS can be augmented by local fire fighting assets within 45 minutes.
questions.choice_b: ICAO Category 4, unless the airport's RFFS can be augmented by local fire fighting assets within 45 minutes.
questions.choice_c: ICAO Category 4, unless the airport's RFFS can be augmented by local fire fighting assets within 30 minutes.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: For ETOPS up to 180 minutes, RFFS equivalent to that specified in ICAO Category 4 is a requirement; however, the RFFS can be augmented by local fire fighting assets with a 30-minute response time. Answer (A) is incorrect. The RFFS requirement under ICAO is for Category 4, not Category 3. The response time for local fire fighting assets is also incorrect; the allowance is 30 minutes, not 45 minutes. Answer (B) is incorrect. The response time for local fire fighting assets is 30 minutes, not 45 minutes.

questions.question_text: ETOPS entry points mean the first entry point on the route of flight of an ETOPS flight using one- engine-inoperative cruise speed that is more than
questions.choice_a: 90 minutes from an adequate airport for airplanes having two engines.
questions.choice_b: 60 minutes from an adequate airport for airplanes having two engines.
questions.choice_c: 207 minutes from an adequate airport for airplanes having more than two engines.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: An ETOPS entry point is the point at which the airplane is farther than a distance of 60 minutes flying time with one engine inoperative from an emergency or diversion airport that is adequate for an airplane with two engines. Answer (A) is incorrect. The flying time stipulated by the rule is 60 minutes, not 90 minutes. Answer (C) is incorrect. An ETOPS entry point occurs at the point where the flying time is more than 60 minutes, not 207 minutes.

questions.question_text: Which minimum level of RFFS do you need when filing an alternate using the beyond-180 minutes ETOPS rule?
questions.choice_c: ICAO Category 7.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: For ETOPS beyond 180 minutes, each designated ETOPS Alternate Airport must have RFFS equivalent to that specified by ICAO Category 4, or higher. In addition, the aircraft must remain within the ETOPS authorized diversion time from an Adequate Airport that has RFFS equivalent to that specified by ICAO Category 7, or higher. Answer (A) is incorrect. In addition to the Category 4 requirement for the ETOPS Alternate Airport, the aircraft must remain within the ETOPS authorized diversion time from an Adequate Airport that has RFFS equivalent to that specified by ICAO Category 7, or higher. Answer (B) is incorrect. The aircraft must remain within the ETOPS authorized diversion time from an Adequate Airport that has RFFS equivalent to that specified by ICAO Category 7, or higher.

questions.question_text: What is required when listing an ETOPS Alternate Airport greater than 180 minutes?
questions.choice_a: The aircraft must have a total of four engines.
questions.choice_b: The aircraft must be equipped with three pilot crew members.
questions.choice_c: Airlines are responsible for providing a passenger recovery plan that includes safe retrieval without undue delay.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: For ETOPS greater than 180 minutes, a specific passenger recovery plan is required for each ETOPS Alternate Airport used in those operations. Answer (A) is incorrect. There is no requirement in the regulations for an aircraft to have a total of four engines to list an ETOPS Alternate Airport greater than 180 minutes from the planned course. Answer (B) is incorrect. There is no requirement in the regulations for an aircraft to have three pilot crew members to list an ETOPS Alternate Airport greater than 180 minutes from the planned course.

questions.question_text: If a required instrument on a multiengine airplane becomes inoperative, which document required under 14 CFR Part 121 dictates whether the flight may continue en route?
questions.choice_a: A Master Minimum Equipment List for the airplane.
questions.choice_c: Original dispatch release.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Each manual required by 14 CFR 121.133 must provide information for en route flight and navigation and communication procedures, including procedures for the dispatch or release or continuance of flight if any item or equipment required for the particular type of operation becomes inoperative or unserviceable en route. Answer (A) is incorrect. The Minimum Equipment List (MEL) is to indicate the instruments and/or equipment that may be inoperative prior to beginning a flight. Answer (C) is incorrect. The dispatch release must have weather reports and forecasts for the destination airport, intermediate stops, and alternate airports. It may point out if there are any items that are affecting a MEL or CDL; however, it does not dictate whether the flight may continue en route.

questions.question_text: For which of these aircraft is the “clearway” for a particular runway considered in computing takeoff weight limitations?
questions.choice_a: Those passenger-carrying transport aircraft certificated between August 26, 1957, and August 30, 1959.
questions.choice_b: Turbine-engine-powered transport airplanes certificated after September 30, 1958.
questions.choice_c: US. certified air carrier airplanes certificated after August 29, 1959.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: The takeoff distance may include a clearway distance, but the clearway distance included may not be greater than one-half of the takeoff run for turbine-engine-powered transport airplanes certificated after September 30, 1958. Answer (A) is incorrect. The clearway for a particular runway may be considered in computing takeoff distance for turbine- engine-powered, not just passenger-carrying, transport aircraft certificated after, not before, September 30, 1958. Answer (C) is incorrect. A turbine-engine-powered transport, not an air carrier airplane, certificated after August 29, 1959, may include the clearway in determining takeoff distance.

questions.subsection: 121.195 Transport Category Airplanes: Turbine Engine Powered: Landing Limitations: Destination Airports
questions.question_text: What effective runway length is required for a turbojet-powered airplane at the destination airport if the runways are forecast to be wet or slippery at the ETA?
questions.choice_a: 70 percent of the actual runway available, from a height of 50 feet over the threshold.
questions.choice_b: 115 percent of the runway length required for a dry runway.
questions.choice_c: 115 percent of the runway length required for a wet runway.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: No person may take off a turbojet-powered airplane when the appropriate weather reports and forecasts, or a combination thereof, indicate that the runways at the _ destination airport may be wet or slippery at the estimated time of arrival unless the effective runway length at the destination airport is at least 115% of the runway length required for a dry runway. Answer (A) is incorrect. Seventy percent of the actual runway available is a landing limitation for a turbopropeller airplane, not a turbojet-powered airplane landing at an airport that forecasts the runways to be wet or slippery at the ETA. Answer (C) is incorrect. If the runways are forecast to be wet or slippery at the ETA, the effective runway length for a turbojet-powered airplane is 115% of the runway length required for a dry, not wet, runway.

questions.subsection: 121.195 Transport Category Airplanes: Turbine Engine Powered: Landing Limitations: Destination Airports
questions.question_text: What restrictions must be observed regarding the carrying of cargo in the passenger compartment of an airplane operated under FAR Part 121?
questions.choice_a: All cargo must be separated from the passengers by a partition capable of withstanding certain load stresses.
questions.choice_b: All cargo must be carried in a suitable flame resistant bin and the bin must be secured to the floor structure of the airplane.
questions.choice_c: Cargo may be carried aft of a divider if properly secured by a safety belt or other tiedown having enough strength to eliminate the possibility of shifting.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Cargo may be carried aft of a bulkhead or divider in any passenger compartment provided the cargo is restrained and is properly secured by a safety belt or other tiedown having enough strength to eliminate the possibility of shifting under all normally anticipated flight and ground conditions. Answer (A) is incorrect. Cargo may be carried in the passenger compartment if it is carried in an approved cargo bin that meets certain requirements. A partition separating the cargo from the passengers is not required. Answer (B) is incorrect. The cargo bin may be attached to either seat tracks or the floor structure, not only the floor structure, of the airplane.

questions.subsection: 121.195 Transport Category Airplanes: Turbine Engine Powered: Landing Limitations: Destination Airports
questions.question_text: What requirement must be met regarding cargo that is carried anywhere in the passenger compartment of an air carrier airplane?
questions.choice_a: The bin in which the cargo is carried may not be installed in a position that restricts access to, or use of, any exit.
questions.choice_b: The bin in which the cargo is carried may not be installed in a position that restricts access to, or use of, any aisle in the passenger compartment.
questions.choice_c: The container or bin in which the cargo is carried must be made of material which is at least flash resistant.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: A cargo bin installed in the passenger compartment may not be installed in a position that restricts access to, or use of, any emergency exit or aisle in the passenger compartment. Answer (A) is incorrect. The cargo bin may not be installed in a position that restricts access to, or use of, any emergency exit, not any exit, in the airplane. Answer (C) is incorrect. The container or bin in which the cargo is carried must be made of material that is at least flame, not flash, resistant.

questions.question_text: Which restriction applies to a cargo bin in a passenger compartment? The bin
questions.choice_a: may have an open top if it is placed in front of the passengers and the cargo is secured by a cargo net.
questions.choice_b: must withstand the load factor required of passenger seats, multiplied by 1.15, using the combined weight of the bin and the maximum poignt of the cargo that may be carried in the in.
questions.choice_c: must be constructed of flame retardant material and fully enclosed.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: A cargo bin installed in a passenger compartment must withstand the load factors and emergency landing conditions applicable to the passenger seats of the airplane in which the bin is installed, multiplied by a factor of 1.15, using the combined weight of the bin and the maximum weight of cargo that may be carried in the bin. Answer (A) is incorrect. The cargo bin must be fully enclosed, not have an open top if it is placed in front of the passengers. Answer (C) is incorrect. The bin must be constructed of flame resistant, not retardant, material.

questions.question_text: Where should the portable battery-powered megaphone be located if only one is required on a passenger-carrying airplane?
questions.choice_a: The most forward location in the passenger cabin.
questions.choice_b: Inthe cabin near the over-the-wing emergency exit.
questions.choice_c: The most rearward location in the passenger cabin.
questions.explanation: One megaphone is required on each airplane with a seating capacity of more than 60 and less than 100 passengers at the most rearward location in the passenger cabin, where it would be readily accessible to a normal flight attendant seat. Answer (A) is incorrect. The portable battery-powered megaphone must be in the most rearward, not forward, location in the passenger cabin. Answer (B) is incorrect. The portable battery-powered megaphone must be in the most rearward location in the cabin, not near the over-the-wing emergency exit.

questions.question_text: How many portable battery-powered megaphones are required on an air carrier airplane with a seating capacity of 100 passengers on a trip segment when 45 passengers are carried?
questions.choice_a: Two; one at the forward end, and the other at the most rearward location in the passenger cabin.
questions.choice_b: Two; one at the most rearward and one in the center of the passenger cabin.
questions.choice_c: Two; one located near or accessible to the flight crew, and one located near the center of the passenger cabin.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Two megaphones are required in the passenger cabin on each airplane with a seating capacity of more than 99 passengers, one installed at the forward end and the other at the most rearward location, where it would be readily accessible to a normal flight attendant seat. Answer (B) is incorrect. One megaphone must be at the forward end, not in the center, and the other in the most rearward location in the passenger cabin. Answer (C) is incorrect. The megaphones are located in the passenger cabin, one at the forward end, not necessarily accessible to the flight crew, and the other at the most rearward location, not the center, of the passenger cabin.

questions.question_text: How many portable battery-powered megaphones are required on an air carrier airplane with a seating capacity of 150 passengers on a trip segment when 75 passengers are carried?
questions.choice_a: Two; one located near or accessible to the flight crew, and one located near the center of the passenger cabin.
questions.choice_b: Two; one at the most rearward and one in the center of the passenger cabin.
questions.choice_c: Two; one at the forward end, and the other at the most rearward location of the passenger cabin.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Two megaphones are required in the passenger cabin on each airplane with a seating capacity of more than 99 passengers, one installed at the forward end and the other at the most rearward location, where it would be readily accessible to a normal flight attendant seat. Answer (A) is incorrect. One megaphone must be located at the forward end, not necessarily near or accessible to the flight crew, and the other at the most rearward location, not near the center, of the passenger cabin. Answer (B) is incorrect. One megaphone must be at the forward end, not center, and the other at the most rearward location in the passenger cabin.

questions.question_text: Which factor determines the minimum number of hand fire extinguishers required for flight under 14 CFR Part 121?
questions.choice_a: Number of passengers and crewmembers aboard.
questions.choice_c: Airplane passenger seating accommodations.
questions.explanation: An airplane’s passenger seating accommodations determine the minimum number of hand fire extinguishers required for flight under 14 CFR Part 121. Answer (A) is incorrect. The minimum number of hand fire extinguishers required is determined by the airplane passenger seating accommodations, not the actual number of passengers and crew aboard a given flight. Answer (B) is incorrect. The minimum number of hand fire extinguishers required is" determined by the airplane passenger seating accommodations, not the actual number of passenger cabin occupants.

questions.question_text: Which requirement applies to emergency equipment (fire extinguishers, megaphones, first-aid kits, and crash ax) installed in an air carrier airplane?
questions.choice_a: All emergency equipment must be readily accessible to the passengers.
questions.choice_b: Emergency equipment cannot be located in a compartment or area where it is not immediately visible to a flight attendant in the passenger compartment.
questions.choice_c: Emergency equipment must be clearly identified and clearly marked to indicate its method of operation.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: All emergency equipment installed in an air carrier airplane must be clearly identified and clearly marked to indicate its method of operation. Answer (A) is incorrect. Only that emergency equipment located in the passenger compartment, not all emergency equipment, must be readily accessible to passengers. Answer (B) is incorrect. There is no requirement that the emergency equipment be visible to a flight attendant in the passenger compartment. Emergency equipment can be carried in a compartment or container as long as it is marked as to the contents.

questions.question_text: The emergency lights on a passenger-carrying airplane must be armed or turned on during
questions.choice_c: takeoff, cruise, and landing.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: The emergency lights on a passenger- carrying airplane must be armed or turned on during taxiing, takeoff, and landing. Answer (A) is incorrect. The emergency lights must be armed or turned on during taxiing, takeoff, and landing, not cruise. Answer (C) is incorrect. The emergency lights must be armed or turned on during taxiing, takeoff, and landing, not cruise.

questions.question_text: If a passenger-carrying landplane is required to have an automatic deploying escape slide system, when must this system be armed?
questions.choice_c: During taxi, takeoff, landing, and after ditching.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Automatic deploying escape slide systems must be armed during taxi, takeoff, and landing. Answer (B) is incorrect. An automatic deploying escape slide system must be armed for taxi as well as takeoff and landing. Answer (C) is incorrect. An automatic deploying escape slide system may be deployed, not armed, after ditching.

questions.question_text: If there is a required emergency exit located in the flight crew compartment, the door which separates the compartment from the passenger cabin must be unlocked during takeoff and landing.
questions.choice_b: locked at all times, except during any emergency declared by the pilot in command.
questions.choice_c: latched open during takeoff and landing.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: If it is necessary to pass through a doorway separating the passenger cabin from other areas to reach any required emergency exit from any passenger seat, the door must have a means to latch it in open position, and the door must be latched open during each takeoff and landing. Answer (A) is incorrect. The door must always be latched open, not unlocked, during takeoff and landing. Answer (B) is incorrect. The door must always be latched open, not locked, during takeoff and landing.

questions.subsection: 121.311 Seats, Safety Belts, and Shoulder Harnesses
questions.question_text: Federal Aviation Regulations require that interior emergency lights must
questions.choice_a: operate automatically when subjected to a negative G load.
questions.choice_b: be operable manually from the flight crew station and a point in the passenger compartment.
questions.choice_c: be armed or turned on during taxiing and all flight operations.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Interior emergency lights must be operable manually both from the flight crew compartment and from a point in the passenger compartment that is readily accessible to a normal flight attendant seat. Answer (A) is incorrect. Interior emergency lights must operate automatically in a crash landing, which would cause a positive, not negative, G force. Answer (C) is incorrect. The interior emergency lights must be armed or turned on during taxiing, takeoff, and landing, not all flight operations (e.g., cruise).

questions.subsection: 121.311 Seats, Safety Belts, and Shoulder Harnesses
questions.question_text: When may two persons share one approved safety belt in a lounge seat?
questions.choice_a: When one is an adult and one is a child under 3 years of age.
questions.choice_b: Only during the en route flight.
questions.choice_c: During all operations except the takeoff and landing portion of a flight.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Two persons occupying a berth may share one approved safety belt and two persons occupying a multiple lounge or divan seat may share one approved safety belt during en route flight only. Answer (A) is incorrect. The regulations do not specify an age of persons sharing a seat belt on a lounge seat. Sharing a seat belt in a lounge seat can be done only during en route flight. Answer (C) is incorrect. Two persons may share one seat belt in a lounge seat only during the en route portion of the flight, which excludes taxi, takeoff, and landing.

questions.subsection: 121.311 Seats, Safety Belts, and Shoulder Harnesses
questions.question_text: In the event of an engine emergency, the use of a cockpit check procedure by the flight crew is
questions.choice_a: encouraged; it helps to ensure that all items on the procedure are accomplished.
questions.choice_b: required by regulations to prevent reliance upon memorized procedures.
questions.choice_c: required by the FAA as a doublecheck after the memorized procedure has been accomplished.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Each certificate holder shall provide an approved cockpit check procedure for each type of aircraft. The approved procedures must include each item necessary for flight crewmembers to check for safety before starting engines, taking off, or landing, and in engine and systems emergencies. The procedures must be designed so that a flight crewmember will not need to rely upon his or her memory for items to be checked. Answer (A) is incorrect. The use of a cockpit check procedure is required, not encouraged, by regulations. Answer (C) is incorrect. The purpose of the checklist is to avoid having the flight crew rely upon memory.

questions.subsection: 121.311 Seats, Safety Belts, and Shoulder Harnesses
questions.question_text: An air carrier airplane must have an operating public address system if it
questions.choice_a: has a seating capacity of 19 passengers.
questions.choice_b: has aseating capacity for more than 19 passengers.
questions.choice_c: weighs more than 12,500 pounds.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: No person may operate an air carrier airplane with a seating capacity of more than 19 passengers unless it is equipped with an operating public address system. Answer (A) is incorrect. An air carrier airplane must have an operating public address system if it has a seating capacity of more than, not equal to, 19 passengers. Answer (C) is incorrect. An air carrier airplane is required to have an operating public address system if it has a seating capacity of more than 19 passengers, not if it weighs more than 12,500 Ib.

questions.subsection: 121.311 Seats, Safety Belts, and Shoulder Harnesses
questions.question_text: A crewmember interphone system is required on which airplane?
questions.choice_c: An airplane with more than 19 passenger seats.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: No person may operate an airplane with a seating capacity of more than 19 passengers unless the airplane is equipped with a crewmember interphone system. Answer (A) is incorrect. The crewmember interphone system requirement is based upon the number of seats, not the size of the airplane. A crewmember interphone system is required if the airplane has more than 19 passenger seats. Answer (B) is incorrect. The number of passenger seats, not the type of propulsion, determines whether a crewmember interphone system is required.

questions.subsection: 121.327 Supplemental Oxygen: Reciprocating Engine Powered Airplanes
questions.question_text: For a 2-hour flight in a reciprocating-engine- powered airplane at a cabin pressure altitude of 12,000 feet, how much supplemental oxygen for sustenance must be provided? Enough oxygen for
questions.choice_a: 30 minutes for 10 percent of the passengers.
questions.choice_b: 10 percent of the passengers for 1.5 hours.
questions.choice_c: each passenger for 30 minutes.
questions.explanation: For flights in a reciprocating-engine-powered airplane of more than 30 min. duration at cabin pressure altitudes above 8,000 ft. up to and including 14,000 ft., enough oxygen must be provided for 30 min. for 10% of the passengers. Answer (B) is incorrect. Enough oxygen for 10% of the passengers for 1.5 hr. is the oxygen required for a 2-hr. flight at a cabin pressure altitude of 12,000 ft. for a turbine-engine- powered airplane, not a reciprocating-engine-powered airplane. Answer (C) is incorrect. There must be enough oxygen for 10% of the passengers, not each passenger, for 30 min.

questions.subsection: 121.327 Supplemental Oxygen: Reciprocating Engine Powered Airplanes
questions.question_text: What is the passenger oxygen supply requirement for a flight, in a turbine-powered aircraft, with a cabin pressure altitude in excess of 15,000 feet? Enough oxygen for
questions.choice_a: each passenger for the entire flight above 15,000 feet cabin altitude.
questions.choice_c: 10 percent of the passengers for 30 minutes.
questions.explanation: For flights at cabin pressure altitudes above 15,000 ft. in a turbine-powered airplane, there must be enough oxygen available for each passenger carried during the entire flight at those altitudes. Answer (B) is incorrect. Enough oxygen for 30% of the passengers is the oxygen supply requirement for the time at cabin pressure altitudes above 14,000 ft., up to and including 15,000 ft., not above 15,000 ft. Answer (C) is incorrect. Enough oxygen for 10% of the passengers is the oxygen supply requirement for flights of more than 30 min. at cabin pressure altitudes above 10,000 ft., up to and including 14,000 ft., not for cabin pressure altitudes above 15,000 ft.

questions.subsection: 121.327 Supplemental Oxygen: Reciprocating Engine Powered Airplanes
questions.question_text: For flights above which cabin altitude must oxygen be provided for all passengers during the entire flight at those altitudes?
questions.choice_c: 14,000 feet. Pressurized Cabins
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: For flights at cabin pressure altitudes above 15,000 ft., there must be enough oxygen available for all passengers during the entire flight at those altitudes. Answer (B) is incorrect. While oxygen must be provided for all passengers during the entire flight at cabin altitudes above 16,000 ft., this oxygen requirement becomes effective above 15,000 ft. Answer (C) is incorrect. For flights at cabin pressure altitudes above 14,000 ft., up to and including 15,000 ft., enough oxygen for that part of the flight at those altitudes must be provided for 30% of the passengers.

questions.subsection: 121.327 Supplemental Oxygen: Reciprocating Engine Powered Airplanes
questions.question_text: How much supplemental oxygen for emergency descent must a pressurized turbine-powered air transport airplane carry for each flight crewmember on flight deck duty when operating at flight altitudes above 10,000 feet? A minimum of 2-hours’ supply.
questions.choice_b: Sufficient for the duration of the flight above 8,000 feet cabin pressure altitude.
questions.choice_c: Sufficient for the duration of the flight at 10,000 feet flight altitude, not to exceed 1 hour and 50 minutes.
questions.explanation: When operating a turbine-engine-powered airplane with a pressurized cabin at flight altitudes above 10,000 ft., there must be a minimum of a 2-hr. supply of supplemental oxygen for each flight crewmember on flight deck duty in the event of an emergency descent. The 2-hr. oxygen supply is that required for a constant rate of descent from the airplane’s maximum certificated operating altitude to 10,000 ft. in 10 min., followed by 1 hr. 50 min. at 10,000 ft. Answer (B) is incorrect. Oxygen requirements at a cabin pressure altitude above 8,000 ft. is for passengers In reciprocating-engine-powered airplanes, not for flight crewmembers on a turbine-engine-powered airplane in the event of an emergency descent. Answer (C) is incorrect. A minimum of 2 hr. of supplemental oxygen is required for each flight crewmember on flight deck duty. The minimum 2-hr. supply includes 10 min. for the descent to 10,000 ft., followed by 1 hr. 50 min. at 10,000 ft. .

questions.subsection: 121.327 Supplemental Oxygen: Reciprocating Engine Powered Airplanes
questions.question_text: What is the highest flight level that operations may be conducted without the pilot at the controls wearing and using an oxygen mask, while the other pilot is away from the duty station?
questions.choice_c: Above FL 250.
questions.explanation: If for any reason at any time it is necessary for one pilot to leave his or her station at the controls of the _ airplane when operating at flight altitudes above FL 250, the remaining pilot at the controls shall put on and use his or her oxygen mask until the other pilot has returned to his or her duty station. Thus, FL 250 is the highest flight level at which operations may be conducted without the pilot at the controls wearing and using an oxygen mask while the other pilot is away from the duty station. Answer (A) is incorrect. The highest flight level at which operations may be conducted without the pilot at the controls wearing and using an oxygen mask while the other pilot is away from the duty station is FL 250, not FL 240. Answer (C) is incorrect. At flight levels above FL 250, the pilot at the controls must wear and use an oxygen mask anytime the other pilot is away from the duty station.

questions.subsection: 121.327 Supplemental Oxygen: Reciprocating Engine Powered Airplanes
questions.question_text: If either pilot of an air carrier airplane leaves the duty station while flying at FL 410, the other pilot
questions.choice_a: and the flight engineer shall put on their oxygen masks and breathe oxygen.
questions.choice_b: shall put on the oxygen mask and breathe oxygen.
questions.choice_c: must have a quick-donning type oxygen mask available.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: If for any reason at any time it is necessary for one pilot to leave his or her duty station at the controls of the airplane when operating at flight altitudes above FL 250, the remaining pilot at the controls must put on and use his or her oxygen mask until the other pilot has returned to his or her duty station. Answer (A) is incorrect. Only the remaining pilot at the flight controls, not the flight engineer, is required to put on the oxygen mask and breathe oxygen. Answer (C) is incorrect. If one pilot leaves the duty station when above FL 250, the remaining pilot must put on the oxygen mask and breathe oxygen, not just have a quick-donning-type oxygen mask available. Answer (©) is correct. (14 CFR 121.333) DISCUSSION: If a turbine-engine-powered, pressurized airplane is not equipped with quick-donning-type oxygen masks, the maximum altitude authorized without one pilot at the controls wearing and using an oxygen mask is FL 250. Answer (A) is incorrect. The maximum altitude authorized without one pilot wearing and using an oxygen mask in a turbine- engine-powered, pressurized airplane that is not equipped with quick-donning-type oxygen masks is FL 250, not FL 200. Answer (B) is incorrect. The maximum altitude authorized without one pilot wearing and using an oxygen mask in a turbine-engine- powered, pressurized airplane that is not equipped with quick- donning-type oxygen masks is FL 250, not FL 300.

questions.subsection: 121.327 Supplemental Oxygen: Reciprocating Engine Powered Airplanes
questions.question_text: If a turbine-engine-powered, pressurized airplane is not equipped with quick-donning oxygen masks, what is the maximum flight altitude authorized without one pilot wearing and using an oxygen mask?
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Each flight crewmember on flight deck duty must be provided with an oxygen mask that can be rapidly placed on his or her face from its ready position, properly secured, sealed, and supplying oxygen on demand, when operating at flight altitudes above FL 250. Answer (A) is incorrect. Each flight crewmember on flight deck duty must be provided with an oxygen mask that can be rapidly placed on his or her face when operating at flight altitudes above FL 250, not FL 260. Answer (B) is incorrect. Each flight crewmember on flight deck duty must be provided with an oxygen mask that can be rapidly placed on his or her face when operating at flight altitudes above, not at, FL 250.

questions.subsection: 121.327 Supplemental Oxygen: Reciprocating Engine Powered Airplanes
questions.question_text: Each air carrier flight deck crewmember on flight deck duty must be provided with an oxygen mask that can be rapidly placed on his face when operating at flight altitudes
questions.choice_c: above FL 250.
questions.explanation: It must be possible to place a quick-donning oxygen mask on the face from its ready position, properly sealed, secured, and supplying oxygen on demand, with one hand within 5 seconds. Answer (B) is incorrect. It must be possible to put on a quick- donning oxygen mask with one hand and use it within 5 seconds, not 10 seconds. Answer (C) is incorrect. It must be possible to put on a quick-donning oxygen mask with one hand and use it within 5 seconds, not 15 seconds.

questions.subsection: 121.327 Supplemental Oxygen: Reciprocating Engine Powered Airplanes
questions.question_text: A flight crewmember must be able to don and use a quick-donning oxygen mask within
questions.choice_c: 15 seconds.

questions.subsection: 121.339 Emergency Equipment for Extended Over-Water Operations
questions.question_text: What is the minimum number of acceptable oxygen-dispensing units for first-aid treatment of occupants who might require undiluted oxygen for physiological reasons?
questions.choice_c: Three.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: There must be a minimum of two acceptable oxygen-dispensing units for first-aid treatment of occupants who might require undiluted oxygen for physiological reasons. Answer (B) is incorrect. The minimum number of oxygen- dispensing units for first-aid treatment is two, not four. Answer (C) is incorrect. The minimum number of oxygen- dispensing units for first-aid treatment is two, not three.

questions.subsection: 121.339 Emergency Equipment for Extended Over-Water Operations
questions.question_text: A passenger briefing by a crewmember shall be given, instructing passengers on the necessity of using oxygen in the event of cabin depressurization, prior to flights conducted above
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Before flight is conducted above FL 250, a crewmember shall instruct the passengers on the necessity of using oxygen in the event of cabin depressurization and shall point out to them the location and demonstrate the use of the oxygen-dispensing equipment. Answer (A) is incorrect. Only if the flight is conducted above FL 250, not FL 200, is an oxygen briefing required. Answer (B) is incorrect. Only if the flight is conducted above FL 250, not FL 240, is an oxygen briefing required.

questions.subsection: 121.339 Emergency Equipment for Extended Over-Water Operations
questions.question_text: The supplemental oxygen requirements for passengers when a flight is operated at FL 250 is dependent upon the airplane’s ability to make an emergency descent to a flight altitude of
questions.choice_c: 12,000 feet within 4 minutes or at a minimum rate of 2,500 ft/min, whichever is quicker.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: The supplemental oxygen requirements for passengers when a flight is operated at FL 250 is dependent upon the airplane’s ability to make an emergency descent to a flight altitude of 14,000 ft. or less within 4 min. Answer (A) is incorrect. The airplane must be able to descend to 14,000 ft., not 10,000 ft., in 4 min. Answer (C) is incorrect. The regulations do not specify a rate of descent in feet per minute. The determining factor is the ability to descend to 14,000 ft., not 12,000 ft., in 4 min.

questions.subsection: 121.339 Emergency Equipment for Extended Over-Water Operations
questions.question_text: Which emergency equipment is required for a flag air carrier flight between John F. Kennedy International Airport and London, England?
questions.choice_a: Alife preserver equipped with an approved survivor locator light or other flotation device for the full seating capacity of the airplane.
questions.choice_b: An appropriately equipped survival kit attached to each required life raft.
questions.choice_c: Aself-buoyant, water resistant, portable, survival-type emergency locator transmitter for each required life raft.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: No person may operate an airplane in extended overwater operations without having an appropriately equipped survival kit attached to each required life raft. Answer (A) is incorrect. There must be a life preserver, equipped with an approved survivor locator light, for each occupant of the airplane, not for the full seating capacity of the airplane. Answer (C) is incorrect. Only one self-buoyant, water- resistant, portable survival-type emergency locator transmitter is eoeue to be carried in the airplane, not one for each required ife raft.

questions.subsection: 121.339 Emergency Equipment for Extended Over-Water Operations
questions.question_text: What emergency equipment is required for extended overwater operations?
questions.choice_a: A portable survival emergency locator transmitter for each life raft.
questions.choice_b: A pyrotechnic signaling device for each life preserver.
questions.choice_c: A life preserver equipped with a survivor locator light, for each person on the airplane.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: No person may operate an airplane in extended overwater operations unless there is a life preserver, equipped with an approved survivor locator light, for each person on the airplane. Answer (A) is incorrect. Only one portable survival type emergency locator transmitter is required to be carried on the airplane, not one for each life raft. Answer (B) is incorrect. There should be at least one pyrotechnic signaling device for each life raft, not for each life preserver.

questions.question_text: Each large aircraft operating over water must have a life preserver for each
questions.choice_c: passenger seat, plus 10 percent.
questions.explanation: No person may operate a large airplane in any overwater operation unless it is equipped with life preservers or with an approved flotation means for each airplane occupant. Answer (B) is incorrect. Each large airplane operating over water must have a life preserver for each occupant, not each seat, on the airplane. Answer (C) is incorrect. Each large airplane operating over water must have a life preserver for each occupant, not each passenger seat plus 10%.

questions.question_text: For the purpose of testing the flight recorder system,
questions.choice_a: aminimum of 1 hour of the oldest recorded data must be erased to get a valid test.
questions.choice_b: a total of 1 hour of the oldest recorded data accumulated at the time of testing may be erased.
questions.choice_c: a total of no more than 1 hour of recorded data may be erased. or for Operations under IFR or Over the Top
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: For the purpose of testing the flight recorder or flight recorder system, a total of 1 hr. of the oldest recorded data accumulated at the time of testing may be erased. Answer (A) is incorrect. For the purpose of testing the flight recorder system, a maximum, not minimum, of 1 hr. of the oldest recorded data may be erased. Answer (C) is incorrect. The recorded data that is erased must be the oldest at the time of testing, not just any data.

questions.question_text: When an air carrier flight is operated under IFR or over-the-top on “victor airways,” which navigation equipment is required to be installed in duplicate?
questions.choice_c: VOR and DME.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: No person may operate an airplane under IFR or over-the-top on VHF (victor) airways unless the airplane is equipped with that radio equipment necessary to receive satisfactorily, by either of two independent systems, radio navigational signals from all primary en route and approach navigational facilities intended to be used, which are VOR/ VORTAC stations. Equipment provided to receive signals en route may be used to receive signals on approach, if it is capable of receiving both signals. Answer (B) is incorrect. ADF equipment is not required for a flight operated IFR or over-the-top on VHF (victor) airways. Answer (C) is incorrect. Only one, not two, DME is required to be installed whenever VOR navigation equipment is required.

questions.question_text: An air carrier operates a flight in VFR over-the- top conditions. What radio navigation equipment is required to be a dual installation?
questions.choice_c: VOR and DME.
