questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: No person may release a supplemental or commercial air carrier turbojet-powered airplane to an airport for which an alternate is not specified unless it has enough fuel, considering wind and other weather conditions expected, to fly to that airport and thereafter to fly for at least 2 hr. at normal cruising fuel consumption. Answer (A) is incorrect. Once the airplane arrives at the destination, it needs enough fuel for at least 2 hr. at normal cruising, not holding, fuel consumption. Answer (C) is incorrect. Thirty min. at holding airspeed at 1,500 ft. AGL is a part of the fuel requirements for turbine-powered airplanes outside the United States if an alternate is required.

questions.subsection: 121.643 Fuel Supply: Nonturbine and Turbo-Propeller-Powered Airplanes: Supplemental Operations
questions.question_text: The fuel reserve required for a turbine-engine- powered (other than turbopropeller) supplemental air carrier airplane upon arrival over the most distant alternate airport outside the 48 contiguous United States is
questions.choice_a: 30 minutes at holding speed, at 1,500 feet over the airport.
questions.choice_b: 30 minutes, over the airport, at 1,500 feet, at cruising speed.
questions.choice_c: 2 hours at the normal cruise fuel consumption rate.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: The fuel reserve required for a supplemental air carrier turbojet airplane upon arrival over the most distant alternate airport outside the 48 contiguous United States is enough fuel to fly for 30 min. at holding speed at 1,500 ft. above the alternate airport under standard temperature conditions. Answer (B) is incorrect. Upon arrival at the most distant alternate airport, the fuel requirement is based on holding, not cruising, speed. Answer (C) is incorrect. Fuel for 2 hr. at normal cruise is the requirement after arriving at an international destination airport where no alternate was specified.

questions.subsection: 121.643 Fuel Supply: Nonturbine and Turbo-Propeller-Powered Airplanes: Supplemental Operations
questions.question_text: A turbine-engine-powered flag air carrier airplane is released to an airport which has no available alternate. What is the required fuel reserve?
questions.choice_a: 2 hours at normal cruise speed in a no wind condition fuel consumption.
questions.choice_b: 2 hours at normal cruise fuel consumption.
questions.choice_c: 30 minutes, plus 10 percent of the total flight time.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: No person may release a turbine-engine- powered airplane (other than a turbopropeller airplane) to an airport for which an alternate is not specified unless it has enough fuel, considering wind and other weather conditions expected, to fly to that airport and thereafter to fly for at least 2 hr. at normal cruising fuel consumption. Answer (A) is incorrect. The required fuel reserve upon arrival at an airport which has no available alternate is 2 hr. at normal cruising fuel consumption, not normal cruising speed. Additionally, in computing the fuel requirements, wind must be considered. Answer (C) is incorrect. When referring to flag air carrier operations outside the 48 contiguous states where an alternate is specified, the flight must have sufficient fuel to fly to the destination airport plus 10% of the total time from departure to destination airport, then to the most distant alternate with an additional 30 min. fuel at holding speed at 1,500 ft. AGL at the alternate.

questions.subsection: 121.651 Takeoff and Landing Weather Minimums: IFR: All Certificate Holders
questions.question_text: Under what conditions may an air carrier pilot continue an instrument approach to the DH, after receiving a weather report indicating that less than minimum published landing conditions exist at the airport?
questions.choice_a: lf the instrument approach is conducted in a radar environment.
questions.choice_b: When the weather report is received as the pilot passes the FAF.
questions.choice_c: When the weather report is received after the pilot has begun the final approach segment of the instrument approach.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: If a pilot has begun the final approach segment of a precision instrument approach procedure and after that receives a later weather report indicating below-minimum * conditions, the pilot may continue the approach to the DH. Answer (A) is incorrect. In order to continue the approach, the pilot must have begun the final approach segment. Whether or not the approach is in a radar environment is not a factor. Answer (B) is incorrect. The question refers to an IAP with a DH, which implies a precision approach. While a precision approach does not have a FAF, the final approach segment begins with the intercept of the electronic glide slope.

questions.subsection: 121.651 Takeoff and Landing Weather Minimums: IFR: All Certificate Holders
questions.question_text: Category II ILS operations below 1600 RVR and a 150-foot DH may be approved after the pilot in command has
questions.choice_a: logged 90 hours’ flight time, 10 takeoffs and landings in make and model airplane and three Category II ILS approaches in actual or simulated IFR conditions with 150-foot DH since the beginning of the sixth preceding month, in operations under 14 CFR Parts 91 and 121.
questions.choice_b: made atleast six Category || approaches in actual IFR conditions with 100-foot DH within the preceding 12 calendar months.
questions.choice_c: logged 100 hours’ flight time in make and model airplane under 14 CFR Part 121 and three Category || ILS approaches in actual or simulated IFR conditions with 150-foot DH since the beginning of the sixth preceding month.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: A Category II pilot authorization is issued as a part of the applicant’s instrument rating or airline transport pilot certificate. Upon original issue, the authorization contains a limitation for Category II operations of 1,600 ft. RVR and a 150-ft. decision height. This limitation is removed when the holder shows that, since the beginning of the sixth preceding month, (s)he has made three Category II ILS approaches to a landing under actual or simulated instrument conditions with a 150-ft. decision height. However, Category II minimums do not apply to air carrier operations until the pilot in command has served 100 hr. as pilot in command under 14 CFR Part 121 in the type of airplane (s)he is operating. Answer (A) is incorrect. The pilot-in-command experience in the make and model of airplane and the Category II currency must have all been conducted in operations under 14 CFR Part 121 only. Answer (B) is incorrect. The requirement is at least three, not six, Category I] approaches in actual (or simulated) eopations to a 150-ft., not a 100-ft., DH within the preceding months.

questions.subsection: 121.651 Takeoff and Landing Weather Minimums: IFR: All Certificate Holders
questions.question_text: When a pilot’s flight time consists of 80 hours pilot in command in a particular type airplane, how does this affect the minimums for the destination airport?
questions.choice_a: Has no effect on destination but alternate minimums are no less than 300 feet and 1 statute mile.
questions.choice_b: Minimums are decreased by 100 feet and 1/2 statute mile.
questions.choice_c: Minimums are increased by 100 feet and 1/2 statute mile.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: If the pilot in command of an airplane has not served 100 hr. as pilot in command in operations under FAR Part 121 in the type of airplane, the minimums for the destination airport are increased by 100 ft. and 1/2 SM (or an RVR of 2,400). Answer (A) is incorrect. When the pilot has 80 hr. of pilot-in- command experience in a particular type airplane, the destination airport minimums are increased by 100 ft. and 1/2 SM; the alternate does not need to be increased, but it can be no lower than 500 ft. and 1 SM. Answer (B) is incorrect. When the pilot has 80 hr. of pilot-in-command experience in a particular type airplane, the destination airport minimums are increased, not decreased, by 100 ft. and 1/2 SM.

questions.question_text: Which in-flight conditions are required by a supplemental air carrier to conduct a day, over-the- top flight below the specified IFR minimum en route altitude?
questions.choice_a: The flight must remain clear of clouds by at least 1,000 feet vertically and 1,000 feet horizontally and have at least 3 miles flight visibility.
questions.choice_b: The flight must be conducted at least 1,000 feet above an overcast or broken cloud layer, any higher broken/overcast cloud cover is a minimum of 1,000 feet above the IFR MEA, and have at least 5 miles flight visibility.
questions.choice_c: The height of any higher overcast or broken layer must be at least 500 feet above the IFR MEA.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: A supplemental air carrier may conduct day over-the-top operations in an airplane at flight altitudes lower than the minimum en route IFR altitudes if the operation is conducted at least 1,000 ft. above the top of lower broken or overcast cloud cover, the top of the lower cloud cover is generally uniform and level, flight visibility is at least 5 SM, and the base of any higher broken or overcast cloud cover is generally uniform and level and is at least 1,000 ft. above the minimum en route IFR altitude for that route segment. Answer (A) is incorrect. The flight must remain at least 1,000 ft. above the cloud layer with a flight visibility of at least 5 SM, not 3 SM. Answer (C) is incorrect. The height of any higher ceiling must be at least 1,000 ft., not 500 ft., above the IFR MEA.

questions.question_text: The certificated air carrier operators who must attach to, or include on, the flight release form the name of each flight crewmember, flight attendant, and designated pilot in command are
questions.choice_c: flag and commercial.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Supplemental air carriers must include the name of each flight crewmember, flight attendant, and pilot designated as pilot in command in the flight release. NOTE: The FAA may delete “commercial operators” in the future. Answer (B) is incorrect. Domestic air carriers, unlike supplemental air carriers, utilize a dispatch release. Supplemental air carriers utilize a flight release. A flight release contains the crew names, but a dispatch release does not. Answer (C) is incorrect. Flag air carriers, unlike supplemental air carriers, utilize a dispatch release. Supplemental air carriers utilize a flight release. A flight release contains the crew names, but a dispatch release does not.

questions.question_text: The information required in the flight release for supplemental air carriers and commercial operators that is not required in the dispatch release for flag and domestic air carriers is the
questions.choice_c: minimum fuel supply. and Flight Plans: Domestic and Flag Operations
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Supplemental air carriers must include the name of each flight crewmember, flight attendant, and pilot designated as pilot in command in the flight release. NOTE: The FAA may delete “commercial operators” in the future. Answer (A) is incorrect. Weather reports and forecasts are found in the flight release for supplemental air carriers just as they are found in the dispatch release for flag and domestic air carriers. Answer (C) is incorrect. Minimum fuel supply information is found in the flight release for supplemental air carriers just as they are found in the dispatch release for flag and domestic air carriers.

questions.question_text: Which documents are required to be carried aboard each domestic air carrier flight?
questions.choice_a: Load manifest (or information from it) and flight release.
questions.choice_b: Dispatch release and weight and balance release.
questions.choice_c: Dispatch release, load manifest (or information from it), and flight plan.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: The pilot in command of a domestic air carrier airplane shall carry in the airplane to its destination a copy of the completed load manifest (or information from it, except information concerning cargo and passenger distribution), a copy of the dispatch release, and a copy of the flight plan. Answer (A) is incorrect. A flight release is used by supplemental air carriers, not domestic air carriers. Answer (B) is incorrect. There is no such thing as a weight and balance release. The weight and balance data are contained in the load manifest.

questions.subsection: 121.697 Disposition of Load Manifest, Flight Release, and Flight Plans: Supplemental Operations
questions.question_text: Which documents are required to be carried aboard each flag air carrier flight?
questions.choice_a: Dispatch release, flight plan, and weight and balance release.
questions.choice_b: Load manifest, flight plan, and flight release.
questions.choice_c: Dispatch release, load manifest, and flight plan.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: The pilot in command of a flag air carrier airplane shall carry in the airplane to its destination a copy of the completed load manifest (or information from it, except information concerning cargo and passenger distribution), a copy of the dispatch release, and a copy of the flight plan. Answer (A) is incorrect. There is no such thing as a weight and balance release. The weight and balance data are contained in the load manifest. Answer (B) is incorrect. A flight release is used by supplemental air carriers, not flag air carriers.

questions.subsection: 121.697 Disposition of Load Manifest, Flight Release, and Flight Plans: Supplemental Operations
questions.question_text: A domestic or flag air carrier shall keep copies of the flight plans, dispatch releases, and load manifests for at least
questions.choice_c: 30 days.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: A domestic or flag air carrier shall keep copies of the flight plans, dispatch releases, and load manifests for at least 3 months. Answer (B) is incorrect. A domestic or flag air carrier shall keep copies of the flight plans, dispatch releases, and load manifests for at least 3 months, not 6 months. Answer (C) is incorrect. A domestic or flag air carrier shall keep copies of the flight plans, dispatch releases, and load manifests for at least 3 months, not 30 days.

questions.subsection: 121.697 Disposition of Load Manifest, Flight Release, and Flight Plans: Supplemental Operations
questions.question_text: What information must the pilot in command of a supplemental air carrier flight or commercial operator carry to the destination airport?
questions.choice_c: Names of all crewmembers and designated pilot in command.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: The pilot in command of a supplemental air carrier flight shall carry in the airplane to its destination the original or signed copy of the load manifest, flight release, airworthiness release, pilot route certification, and flight plan. NOTE: The FAA may change “commercial operator” to “supplemental air carrier” in the future. Answer (A) is incorrect. Cargo and passenger distribution information is part of the weight and balance information contained in the load manifest. Answer (C) is incorrect. A list of the names of all crewmembers and the designated pilot in command is one element contained in the flight release.

questions.subsection: 121.697 Disposition of Load Manifest, Flight Release, and Flight Plans: Supplemental Operations
questions.question_text: How long shall a supplemental air carrier or commercial operator retain a record of the load manifest, airworthiness release, pilot route certification, flight release, and flight plan?
questions.choice_c: 12 months.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: A supplemental air carrier shall retain a record of the load manifest, airworthiness release, pilot route certification, flight release, and flight plan for at least 3 months. NOTE: The FAA may delete “commercial operator’ in the uture. Answer (A) is incorrect. A supplemental air carrier shall retain a record of the load manifest, airworthiness release, pilot route certification, flight release, and flight plan for at least 3 months, not 1 month. Answer (C) is incorrect. A supplemental air carrier shall retain a record of the load manifest, airworthiness release, pilot route certification, flight release, and flight plan for at least 3 months, not 12 months.

questions.subsection: 121.697 Disposition of Load Manifest, Flight Release, and Flight Plans: Supplemental Operations
questions.question_text: A person whose duties include the handling of hazardous materials must have satisfactorily completed an approved training program within the preceding
questions.choice_c: 24 calendar months.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: A person whose duties include the handling of hazardous materials must have satisfactorily completed an FAA-approved initial or recurrent hazardous materials training program within the preceding 24 months. ___ Answer (A) is incorrect. There are no regulations requiring initial or recurrent training of handling hazardous materials within the preceding 6 months. Answer (B) is incorrect. 14 CFR 121.1005 clearly states the training for handling hazardous materials has to be within the preceding 24 months. END OF STUDY UNIT

questions.question_text: An aircraft being operated outside of the United
questions.choice_a: the International Civil Aviation Organization Annex 3. Answer (C) is incorrect. The U.S. State Department has (ICAO), Annex 3, Rules of the Air. no authority over aviation activities over a foreign country.
questions.choice_c: rules of the U.S. State Department and the foreign country.
questions.explanation: States, over a foreign country, by a 14 CFR Part 135 DISCUSSION: While operating an aircraft outside of the operator must comply with U.S., over a foreign country, a FAR Part 135 operator must comply with the regulations of the foreign country. : MeO Ruane ald Answer (A) is incorrect. ICAO Rules of the Air is Annex 2, not

questions.question_text: The pilot in command may deviate from 14 CFR Part 135 during an emergency involving the safety of persons or property only
questions.choice_a: after ATC is notified of the emergency and the extent of deviation required.
questions.choice_b: to the extent required to meet that emergency.
questions.choice_c: if required to, by the emergency cockpit checklist.
questions.explanation: | DISCUSSION: In an emergency involving the safety of persons or property, the pilot in command may deviate from the rules of FAR Part 135 to the extent required to meet that emergency. Answer (A) is incorrect. ATC should be notified after, not before, the pilot in command deviates from the rules of FAR Part 135 during an emergency. Answer (C) is incorrect. The pilot in command may deviate from the rules of FAR Part 135 during an emergency to the extent required to meet that emergency, not if an emergency checklist requires the deviation.

questions.question_text: Who is responsible for keeping copies of the certificate holder's manual up to date with approved changes or additions?
questions.choice_a: Each of the certificate holder's employees who are furnished a manual.
questions.choice_b: An employee designated by the certificate holder.
questions.choice_c: A representative of the certificate holder approved by the Administrator.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Each employee of the certificate holder to whom a manual (or portions of it) is furnished is responsible for keeping his or her copy up to date with the changes and additions furnished to him or her. Answer (B) is incorrect. All employees of the certificate holder to whom copies of the manual have been furnished are responsible for keeping them up to date, not an employee designated by the certificate holder. Answer (C) is incorrect. A certificate holder must furnish a copy of the manual to a representative of the FAA (the Principal Operations Inspector), and the certificate holder, not the inspector, is responsible for keeping the inspector’s copy up to date.

questions.question_text: lf previous arrangements have not been made by the operator, where can the procedures for servicing the aircraft be found?
questions.choice_c: Pilot’s Handbook.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: The certificate holder's manual contains the procedures to be followed by the pilot in command to obtain maintenance, preventive maintenance, and servicing of the aircraft at a place where previous arrangements have not been made. Answer (A) is incorrect. The maintenance manual describes how maintenance personnel would service the airplane, not the procedures the pilot in command would follow to have the aircraft serviced at a place where previous arrangements have not been made. Answer (C) is incorrect. The Pilot's Handbook provides information on the airplane, not the procedures to follow to have the airplane serviced at a place where previous arrangements have not been made.

questions.question_text: What document contains procedures that explain how the required return-to-service conditions have been met?
questions.choice_c: Certificate holder's manual.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: The certificate holder’s manual must include procedures for ensuring that the pilot in command knows that the aircraft has undergone the required airworthiness inspections and has been approved for return to service in compliance with applicable maintenance requirements. Answer (A) is incorrect. The certificate holder’s manual, not the maintenance manual, must contain the procedures that explain how the return-to-service conditions have been met. Answer (B) is incorrect. The certificate holder’s manual, not the pilot's handbook, must contain the procedures that explain how the return-to-service conditions have been met.

questions.question_text: A certificate holder must have “exclusive use” of
questions.choice_a: at least one aircraft that meets the requirements of the specific operations authorized in the certificate holder's Operations Specifications. at least one aircraft that meets the requirements of at least one kind of operation authorized in the certificate holder's Operations Specifications. at least one aircraft that meets the requirements of each kind of operation authorized in the Operations Specifications.
questions.explanation: Each certificate holder must have the exclusive use of at least one aircraft that meets the requirements for at least one kind of operation authorized in the certificate holder’s operations specifications. In addition, for each kind of operation for which the certificate holder does not have the exclusive use of an aircraft, the certificate holder must have available for use under a written agreement (including arrange- ments for performing required maintenance) at least one aircraft that meets the requirements for that kind of operation. However, this does not prohibit the operator from using or authorizing the use of the aircraft for other than air taxi or commercial operations and does not require the certificate holder to have exclusive use of all aircraft that the certificate holder uses. Answer (A) is incorrect. The certificate holder must have exclusive use for at least one, not all, operation(s) authorized to the certificate holder. Answer (C) is incorrect. The certificate holder must have exclusive use for at least one, not all, operation(s) authorized to the certificate holder.

questions.question_text: Who is responsible for the preparation of a required load manifest? PIC or the Dispatcher. Company official designated by the Administrator. The certificate holder.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: For multi-engine aircraft, each certificate holder is responsible for the preparation and accuracy of a load manifest in duplicate containing information concerning the loading of the aircraft. The load manifest must be prepared before each takeoff. Answer (A) is incorrect. While the PIC must carry a copy of the load manifest in the aircraft to its destination, the PIC is not responsible for the preparation of the load manifest. Additionally, dispatchers are required for some FAR Part 121 operations, not FAR Part 135. Answer (B) is incorrect. The certificate holder, not any official designated by the FAA, is responsible for the preparation of a required load manifest.

questions.question_text: Where must a certificate holder keep copies of completed load manifests and for what period of time? 1 month at its principal operations base, or at a location approved by the Administrator. 30 days at its principal operations base, or another location used by it and approved by the Administrator. 30 days, at the flight’s destination.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: The certificate holder must keep copies of completed load manifests for at least 30 days at its principal operations base or at another location used by it and approved by the FAA Administrator. Answer (A) is incorrect. A certificate holder must keep copies of completed load manifests for 30 days, not 1 month, at its principal operations base or at another location used by it and approved by the FAA, not at any location. Answer (C) is incorrect. While the PIC must carry a copy of the completed load manifest to the flight’s destination, the certificate holder must keep copies of completed load manifests at its principal operations base or at another location used by it and approved by the FAA.

questions.question_text: Which is not a required item on the load manifest? List of passenger names and the weight of each. Aircraft registration number or flight number. Identification of crewmembers and their crew position.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: A list of passenger names and their weights is not required on the load manifest; only the number of passengers is required. Answer (B) is incorrect. The aircraft registration number or flight number must be included on the load manifest. Answer (C) is incorrect. The identification of crewmembers and their crew position assignments must be included on the load manifest.

questions.question_text: The load manifest must be prepared prior to each
questions.choice_a: any aircraft with a passenger seating capacity the loading of the aircraft. The manifest must be prepared before | of 10 seats or more. each takeoff. Answer (A) is incorrect. A manifest is required for multi- Sor sR UR NLS INL RISE engine aircraft only, regardless of the number of passengers
questions.choice_c: all helicopters and large aircraft operated by a carried. Answer (C) is incorrect. The FAA's load manifest commuter air carrier. requirement is based on single-engine/multi-engine, not class or size.
questions.explanation: takeoff for DISCUSSION: For multi-engine aircraft, each certificate holder is responsible for the preparation in duplicate and the ; : ‘ : accuracy of a load manifest containing information concerning

questions.question_text: Who is directly responsible for determining the
questions.choice_a: Aircraft dispatcher. already know, determine the status of each irregularity entered in
questions.choice_b: Line maintenance supervisor. the maintenance log at the end of the preceding flight. Answer (A) is incorrect. The aircraft dispatcher is responsible
questions.choice_c: The next pilot in command. for briefing the pilot in command on all available weather information and on irregularities of facilities and services. Answer (B) is incorrect. The pilot in command, not the line maintenance supervisor, is directly responsible for determining the status of each mechanical irregularity previously entered in the aircraft maintenance log.
questions.explanation: status of each mechanical irregularity previously DISCUSSION: The pilot in command shall enter or have entered in the aircraft maintenance log? entered in the aircraft maintenance log each mechanical irregularity that comes to the pilot’s attention during flight time. Before each flight, the pilot in command shall, if the pilot does not

questions.question_text: Procedures for keeping copies of the aircraft
questions.choice_a: the certificate holder's manual. Answer (B) is incorrect. A maintenance procedures
questions.choice_b: the maintenance procedures handbook. handbook establishes procedures for handling different ing maintenance items, not procedures for keeping copies of the
questions.choice_c: the Operations Specifications. maintenance log in the aircraft. Answer (C) is incorrect. The operations specifications lists the time limitations for overhauls, inspections, and other items that are subject to an airworthiness maintenance program, not procedures for keeping copies of the maintenance log in the aircraft.
questions.explanation: maintenance log in the aircraft and available to DISCUSSION: Each certificate holder shall establish a appropriate personnel shall be set forth in procedure for keeping copies of the aircraft maintenance log in the aircraft for access by appropriate personnel and shall include that procedure in the certificate holder’s manual.

questions.question_text: Where is the certificate holder required to list the
questions.choice_a: Operations Specifications. Answer (A) is incorrect. The operations specifications
questions.choice_b: Attached to the load manifest. prescribes the area in which the certificate holder can operate, types of instrument approaches that can be flown, aircraft that can be used, etc., not who can exercise operational control. Answer (B) is incorrect. The load manifest contains specific flight data, such as fuel on board, passengers, cargo, etc., not the name of each person who can exercise operational control.
questions.choice_c: Certificate holder's manual.
questions.explanation: name and title of each person authorized to exercise DISCUSSION: Each certificate holder is responsible for operational control for a particular flight? operational control and shall list, in the operations manual required, the name and title of each person authorized by it to exercise operational control.

questions.subsection: 135.85 Carriage of Persons without Compliance with the Passenger-Carrying Provisions of This Part
questions.question_text: Which person may be carried aboard an aircraft without complying with the passenger-carrying requirements of FAR Part 135?
questions.choice_a: An individual who is necessary for the safe handling of hazardous material on the aircraft.
questions.choice_b: Arepresentative of the Administrator, traveling to attend a meeting.
questions.choice_c: Amember of the United States diplomatic corps on an official courier mission.
questions.explanation: Certain types of persons may be carried aboard an aircraft without having to comply with the passenger- carrying requirements of FAR Part 135. They include 1. Acrewmember or other employee of the certificate holder 2. Aperson necessary for the safe handling of animals on the aircraft 3. Aperson necessary for the safe handling of hazardous materials (as defined in Subchapter C of Title 49 CFR) 4. Apperson performing duty as a security or honor guard accompanying a shipment made by or under the authority of the U.S. Government 5. Amilitary courier or a military route supervisor carried by a military cargo contract air carrier or commercial operator in operations under a military cargo contract, if that carriage is specifically authorized by the appropriate military service 6. An authorized representative of the FAA Administrator conducting an en route inspection 7. Apperson, authorized by the FAA Administrator, who is performing a duty connected with a cargo operation of the certificate holder Answer (B) is incorrect. A representative of the FAA Administrator conducting an en route inspection, not traveling to attend a meeting, may be carried aboard an aircraft without complying with the passenger-carrying requirements of FAR Part 135. Answer (C) is incorrect. A member of the U.S. diplomatic corps on an official courier mission carried aboard an aircraft must comply with the passenger-carrying requirements of FAR Part 135.

questions.subsection: 135.85 Carriage of Persons without Compliance with the Passenger-Carrying Provisions of This Part
questions.question_text: Which restriction must be observed regarding the carrying of cargo in the passenger compartment?
questions.choice_a: \t is packaged or covered to avoid possible injury to occupants.
questions.choice_b: All cargo must be carried in a suitable bin and secured to a passenger seat or the floor structure of the aircraft.
questions.choice_c: Cargo carried in passenger seats must be forward of all passengers.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: No person may carry cargo, including carry- on baggage, in or on any aircraft unless it is packaged or covered to avoid possible injury to occupants. Answer (B) is incorrect. Carry-on baggage can be stowed under a passenger seat as long as the seat is fitted with a means to prevent the baggage from sliding under the impact of a crash, which does not mean that the baggage must be secured to the passenger seat or the floor structure of the aircraft. Answer (C) is incorrect. Carry-on baggage can be stowed under a passenger seat, not forward of all passenger seats, as long as the seat is fitted with a means to prevent the baggage from sliding under the impact of a crash.

questions.subsection: 135.85 Carriage of Persons without Compliance with the Passenger-Carrying Provisions of This Part
questions.question_text: What requirement must be met regarding cargo that is carried anywhere in the passenger compartment of a commuter air carrier airplane?
questions.choice_a: Cargo may not be carried anywhere in the rear of the passenger compartment.
questions.choice_b: The bin in which the cargo is carried may not be installed in a position that restricts access to, or use of the aisle between the crew and the passenger compartment.
questions.choice_c: The container or bin in which the cargo is carried must be made of material which is at least flash resistant.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Cargo, including carry-on baggage, that is carried anywhere in the passenger compartment may not be located in a position that restricts access to, or use of, any required emergency or regular exit, or the use of the aisle between the crew and the passenger compartment. Answer (A) is incorrect. There is no regulation stating that cargo cannot be carried anywhere in the rear of the passenger compartment. Answer (C) is incorrect. FAR 135.87 does not specify what material the container or bin must be made of. FAR 121.285 requires the bin to be of material that is at least flame, not flash, resistant.

questions.question_text: Which is a requirement governing the carriage of cargo, on a scheduled passenger flight?
questions.choice_a: Cargo must be carried in an approved rack, bin, or compartment.
questions.choice_b: Cargo not stowed in an approved bin must be secured by a safety belt or approved tiedown device.
questions.choice_c: All cargo carried in the passenger compartment must be packaged and stowed ahead of the foremost seated passenger.
questions.explanation: Cargo not stowed in an approved bin on a scheduled passenger flight must be properly secured by a safety | belt or other approved tie-down device having enough strength to eliminate the possibility of shifting under all normally anticipated | flight and ground conditions. Answer (A) is incorrect. Cargo does not have to be carried in an approved bin if it is secured by a safety belt or an approved tie-down device. Answer (C) is incorrect. Cargo may be carried in the passenger compartment under specified conditions; it is not required to be stowed ahead of the foremost seated passenger.

questions.question_text: Which is a requirement governing the carriage of carry-on baggage?
questions.choice_a: All carry-on baggage must be restrained so that its movement is prevented during air turbulence.
questions.choice_b: Carry-on baggage must be stowed under the seat in front of the owner.
questions.choice_c: Pieces of carry-on baggage weighing more than 10 pounds must be carried in an approved rack or bin.
questions.explanation: All carry-on baggage must be restrained so that its movement is prevented during air turbulence. Answer (B) is incorrect. There is no FAR requirement that carry-on baggage be stowed under the seat in front of the owner. Answer (C) is incorrect. There is no FAR requirement that carry- on baggage weighing more than 10 Ib. be carried in an approved rack or bin.

questions.question_text: If carry-on baggage or cargo is carried in the passenger compartment, it must be
questions.choice_a: stowed ahead of the foremost seated passengers and secured by approved means.
questions.choice_b: placed in an approved rack, bin, or compartment installed in the aircraft.
questions.choice_c: located so that it does not obstruct the access to, or the use of, any required emergency or regular exit.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: If carry-on baggage is not carried in an approved rack, bin, or compartment installed in the aircraft, it must be located so that is does not obstruct the access to, or the use of, any required emergency or regular exit. Answer (A) is incorrect. There is no FAR requirement that carry-on baggage be stowed ahead of the foremost seated passengers. Answer (B) is incorrect. Carry-on baggage does not have to be placed in an approved rack, bin, or compartment in the aircraft if it is carried in accordance with other specified conditions.

questions.question_text: In a cargo-only operation, cargo must be loaded
questions.choice_a: so that it does not obstruct the aisle between the crew and cargo compartments.
questions.choice_b: in sucha manner that at least one emergency or regular exit is available to all occupants.
questions.choice_c: in such a manner that at least one emergency or regular exit is available to all crewmembers, if an emergency occurs.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: In a cargo-only operation, cargo must be loaded so that at least one emergency or regular exit is available to provide all occupants of the aircraft a means of unobstructed exit from the aircraft if an emergency occurs. Answer (A) is incorrect. Cargo must be loaded so that it does not obstruct the aisle between the crew and passenger, not cargo, compartments in a passenger-carrying flight, not in a cargo-only operation. Answer (C) is incorrect. Access to at least one emergency or regular exit must be available to all occupants, not just crewmembers, if an emergency occurs.

questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 197 on page 149 and Figure 199 on page 459.) When are the pilots required to use oxygen on this flight from EGE to SLC?
questions.choice_a: Starting 30 minutes after climbing through 10,000 feet until descending below 10,000 feet.
questions.choice_b: Starting 30 minutes after level off until descending below 10,000 feet.
questions.choice_c: Upon climbing through 12,000 feet on ascent.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Fig. 197 shows that the cruising altitude for this flight is 14,000 feet. FAR 135.89 requires each pilot of an unpressurized aircraft to use oxygen continuously above 12,000 feet. Note that this FAA figure has a typo in it. The leg between PUC and FFU incorrectly shows an altitude of 1,400 ft. when the altitude should be 14,000 ft. Answer (A) is incorrect. This flight will climb higher than 12,000 feet. Each pilot must use oxygen continuously when flying at altitudes above 10,000 ft. through 12,000 ft. for that part of the flight at those altitudes that are of more than 30 minutes duration and continuously above 12,000 feet. Answer (B) is incorrect. The level off is at 14,000 ft., which is higher than 12,000 feet. FAR 135.89 requires each pilot of an unpressurized aircraft to use oxygen continuously above 12,000 feet.

questions.question_text: Which is a pilot requirement for oxygen?
questions.choice_a: Each pilot of a pressurized aircraft operating at FL 180 and above shall have an approved quick-donning type oxygen mask.
questions.choice_b: On pressurized aircraft requiring a flight crew of two pilots, both shall continuously wear oxygen masks whenever the cabin pressure altitude exceeds 12,000 feet MSL.
questions.choice_c: On unpressurized aircraft, flying above 12,000 feet MSL, pilots shall use oxygen continuously.
questions.explanation: On unpressurized aircraft, each pilot must use oxygen continuously when flying above 12,000 ft. MSL. Answer (A) is incorrect. There is no FAR requirement that a pressurized aircraft operating at FL 180 and above have an approved quick-donning-type oxygen mask for each pilot. Answer (B) is incorrect. On pressurized aircraft requiring a flight crew of two pilots, both must use oxygen continuously, not just wear oxygen masks, whenever the cabin pressure altitude exceeds 12,000 ft. MSL.

questions.question_text: At altitudes above 10,000 feet through 12,000 feet MSL, each pilot of an unpressurized airplane must use supplemental oxygen for that part of the flight that is of a duration of more than
questions.choice_c: 45 minutes.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Each pilot of an unpressurized aircraft shall use oxygen continuously when flying at altitudes above 10,000 ft. MSL through 12,000 ft. MSL for that part of the flight at those altitudes that is of more than 30 min. duration. Answer (A) is incorrect. For that part of the flight that exceeds 30, not 20, min. in duration from 10,000 ft. to 12,000 ft. MSL, oxygen is required. Answer (C) is incorrect. For that part of the flight that exceeds 30, not 45, min. in duration from 10,000 ft. to 12,000 ft. MSL, oxygen is required.

questions.question_text: Which is a requirement for pilot use of oxygen in a pressurized airplane?
questions.choice_a: The pilot at the controls shall use oxygen continuously any time the cabin pressure altitude is more than 12,000 feet MSL.
questions.choice_b: At FL 250 and above, each pilot shall have an approved quick-donning oxygen mask.
questions.choice_c: At FL 250 and above the pilot at the controls must have an approved oxygen mask any time the other pilot is away from the duty station.
questions.explanation: Each pilot of a pressurized airplane must use oxygen continuously anytime the airplane is operated at a cabin pressure altitude above 12,000 ft. MSL. At cabin pressure altitudes above 10,000 ft. MSL through 12,000 ft. MSL, oxygen must be used continuously for flights in excess of 30 min. duration at those cabin pressure altitudes. Answer (B) is incorrect. There is no FAR requirement that each pilot have an approved quick-donning oxygen mask when operating a pressurized airplane at FL 250 and above. Answer (C) is incorrect. If one pilot leaves a pilot duty station of an aircraft at altitudes above, not at, FL 250, the remaining pilot must put on and use, not just have, an approved oxygen mask until the other pilot returns to the pilot duty station.

questions.question_text: The two pilot stations of a pressurized aircraft are equipped with approved quick-donning oxygen masks. What is the maximum altitude authorized if one pilot is not wearing an oxygen mask and breathing oxygen?
questions.choice_c: 25,000 feet MSL.
questions.explanation: The maximum altitude authorized if one pilot at the controls is not wearing an oxygen mask and breathing oxygen in a pressurized aircraft equipped with quick-donning oxygen masks for both pilot stations is 35,000 ft. MSL. Answer (A) is incorrect. At 41,000 ft., at least one pilot at the controls must wear, secured and sealed, an oxygen mask that either supplies oxygen at all times or automatically supplies oxygen whenever the cabin pressure altitude exceeds 12,000 ft. MSL. Answer (C) is incorrect. This is the maximum altitude authorized if one pilot at the controls is not wearing an oxygen mask in an aircraft that does not have quick-donning oxygen masks for both pilot stations.

questions.question_text: Above which altitude/flight level must at least one of the two pilots, at the controls of a pressurized aircraft (with quick-donning masks) wear a secured and sealed oxygen mask?
questions.explanation: Whenever a pressurized aircraft equipped with quick-donning masks is operated above FL 350, at least one of the two pilots at the controls must wear a secured and sealed oxygen mask that either supplies oxygen at all times or automatically supplies oxygen whenever the cabin pressure altitude exceeds 12,000 ft. MSL. Answer (A) is incorrect. At léast one of the two pilots at the controls of a pressurized aircraft, equipped with quick-donning masks, must wear a secured and sealed oxygen mask above FL 350, not FL 300. Answer (C) is incorrect. FL 250 is the maximum altitude/flight level above which at least one of the two pilots of a pressurized aircraft must wear a secured and sealed oxygen mask, if the aircraft is not equipped with quick-donning masks.

questions.subsection: 135.91 Oxygen for Medical Use by Passengers
questions.question_text: Which is a requirement for flight crew use of oxygen masks in a pressurized cabin airplane?
questions.choice_a: Both pilots at the controls shall use oxygen masks above FL 350.
questions.choice_b: At altitudes above 25,000 feet MSL, if one pilot leaves the pilot duty station, the remaining pilot at the controls shall use an oxygen mask.
questions.choice_c: Ataltitudes above FL 250, one of the two pilots at the controls shall use an oxygen mask continuously.
questions.explanation: If one pilot leaves the duty station of a pressurized airplane when operating at altitudes above 25,000 ft. MSL, the remaining pilot at the controls must put on and use an approved oxygen mask until the other pilot returns to the pilot duty station of the airplane. Answer (A) is incorrect. At least one pilot, not both pilots, must use an oxygen mask above FL 350. Answer (C) is incorrect. One pilot is required to use an oxygen mask continuously above FL 250 only if the other pilot leaves the duty station.

questions.subsection: 135.91 Oxygen for Medical Use by Passengers
questions.question_text: Which requirement applies when oxygen is stored in liquid form?
questions.choice_a: Smoking is not permitted within 50 feet of stored liquid oxygen.
questions.choice_b: Liquified oxygen is a hazardous material and must be kept in an isolated storage facility.
questions.choice_c: The equipment used to store liquid oxygen must be covered in the certificate holder’s approved maintenance program.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: When medical oxygen is stored in the form of a liquid, the equipment must have been under the certificate holder’s approved maintenance program since it was purchased new or since the storage container was last purged. Answer (A) is incorrect. Smoking is not permitted within 10 ft., not 50 ft., of oxygen storage and use. Answer (B) is incorrect. Liquified oxygen can be carried aboard airplanes and used for medical reasons if in compliance with FAR 135.91.

questions.subsection: 135.91 Oxygen for Medical Use by Passengers
questions.question_text: Which is a condition that must be met when a person is administered medical oxygen in flight?
questions.choice_a: The distance between a person using medical oxygen and any electrical unit must not be less than 5 feet.
questions.choice_b: A person using oxygen equipment must be seated to avoid restricting access to, or use of, any required exit.
questions.choice_c: A person being administered oxygen must be monitored by equipment that displays and records pulse and respiration.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: When a person is administered medical oxygen in flight, the equipment must be stowed and each person using the equipment must be seated so as not to restrict access to, or use of, any required emergency or regular exit or the aisle in the passenger compartment. Answer (A) is incorrect. There is no provision for distance from electrical units during medical oxygen use. Answer (C) is incorrect. Displaying and recording pulse and respiration data during administration of medical oxygen is not required.

questions.subsection: 135.91 Oxygen for Medical Use by Passengers
questions.question_text: Which is a requirement regarding the carriage and operation of oxygen equipment for medical use by passengers?
questions.choice_a: No person may smoke within 10 feet of oxygen storage and dispensing equipment.
questions.choice_b: When oxygen equipment is used for the medical treatment of a patient, the rules pertaining to emergency exit access are waived.
questions.choice_c: No person may connect oxygen bottles or any other ancillary equipment until all passengers are aboard the aircraft and seated.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: No person may smoke and no certificate holder may allow any person to smoke within 10 ft. of oxygen storage and dispensing equipment. Answer (B) is incorrect. Medical oxygen equipment must be used so as not to restrict access to, or use of, any required emergency or regular exit or the aisle in the passenger compartment. Answer (C) is incorrect. No certificate holder may allow any person other than a person trained in the use of medical oxygen equipment to connect or disconnect oxygen bottles or any other ancillary component while any passenger(s) is(are) aboard the aircraft.

questions.subsection: 135.91 Oxygen for Medical Use by Passengers
questions.question_text: If a certificate holder deviates from the provisions of regulations which pertain to medical use of oxygen by passengers, a complete report of the incident shall be sent to the FAA within
questions.choice_c: 10 days of the deviation.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: If a certificate holder deviates from the provisions of regulations that pertain to medical use of oxygen by passengers, a report shall be sent to the appropriate FAA Flight Standards District Office within 10 working days. Answer (A) is incorrect. The report is required in 10 working days, not 7 working days. Answer (C) is incorrect. The report is required in 10 working, not calendar, days.

questions.question_text: What is the lowest altitude above the terrain that an autopilot may be used during en route operations, if the Airplane Flight Manual specifies a malfunction under cruise conditions?
questions.choice_c: 100 feet.
questions.explanation: !) DISCUSSION: No person may use an autopilot during en ! route operations at an altitude above the terrain that is less than | 500 ft. or less than twice the maximum altitude loss specified in the approved Airplane Flight Manual or equivalent for a malfunction of the autopilot, whichever is higher. Answer (A) is incorrect. This would be correct only if the maximum altitude loss specified in the Airplane Flight Manual for | a malfunction of the autopilot were 500 ft. Answer (C) is incorrect. . The lowest altitude above the terrain at which an autopilot may be used during en route operations is 500 ft., not 100 ft.

questions.question_text: The maximum altitude loss specified for malfunction of a certain autopilot under cruise conditions is 50 feet. What is the lowest altitude this autopilot may be used en route?
questions.choice_c: 600 feet AGL.
questions.explanation: No person may use an autopilot at an altitude above the terrain that is less than 500 ft. or less than twice the maximum altitude loss specified in the approved Airplane Flight Manual or equivalent for a malfunction of the autopilot, whichever is higher. Answer (B) is incorrect. The lowest the aircraft may cruise is 500 ft. AGL, not 550 ft. AGL, or twice the maximum autopilot loss, whichever is higher. Answer (C) is incorrect. The altitude loss of the autopilot doubled is not added to the minimum of 500 ft. AGL.

questions.question_text: The maximum altitude loss for a malfunctioning autopilot without an approach coupler is 45 feet. If the MDA is 1,620 feet MSL and the TDZE is 1,294 feet, to which minimum altitude may you use the autopilot?
questions.choice_c: 1,570 feet MSL.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: The aircraft is allowed to descend to the higher of two altitudes. The first minimum altitude is 50 ft. below the MDA of 1,620 ft. MSL, or 1,570 ft. MSL (1,620 — 50). The second is twice the maximum altitude loss possible in an autopilot malfunction as specified in the autopilot manual. The maximum altitude loss is 90 ft. (45 x 2). Add the 90 ft. to the touchdown zone elevation of 1,294 to get a minimum altitude number of 1,384 ft. MSL. Therefore the higher or more restrictive altitude would apply, or 1,570 ft. MSL (1,570 ft. MSL is higher than 1,384 ft. MSL). Answer (A) is incorrect. Only 50 ft. below the MDA is allowed, not 90 ft. below the MDA. Answer (B) is incorrect. This is only 45 ft. above the TDZE.

questions.question_text: The maximum altitude loss for a malfunctioning autopilot with an approach coupler is 40 feet. To which minimum altitude may the autopilot be used during an ILS approach in less than basic VFR conditions?
questions.choice_c: 80 feet AGL.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: For ILS approaches, when reported weather conditions are less than basic VFR weather conditions, no person may use an autopilot with an approach coupler at an altitude that is less than 50 ft. above the terrain or the maximum altitude loss specified in the approved Airplane Flight Manual or equivalent for the malfunction of the autopilot with approach coupler, whichever is higher. Answer (A) is incorrect. The aircraft is allowed to descend to only 50 ft. AGL, not down to 40 ft. AGL (40 ft. is the maximum autopilot loss). Answer (C) is incorrect. Twice the autopilot loss, 80 ft. (2 x 40 ft.) in this case, is for non-ILS approaches.

questions.question_text: The altitude loss for a particular malfunctioning autopilot with an approach coupler is 60 feet. If the reported weather is below basic VFR minimums and an ILS approach using the approach coupler is to be used, what minimum altitude may be used?
questions.choice_c: 60 feet AGL.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: For ILS approaches, when reported weather conditions are less than basic VFR weather conditions, no person may use an autopilot with an approach coupler at an altitude above the terrain that is less than 50 ft. above the terrain or the maximum altitude loss specified in the approved Airplane Flight Manual or equivalent for the malfunction of the autopilot with approach coupler, whichever is higher. In this case, the maximum altitude loss is 60 ft. ° Answer (A) is incorrect. The lowest altitude is the greater of 50 ft. AGL or the maximum autopilot loss. In this case, the maximum altitude loss is 60 ft. Answer (B) is incorrect. The lowest altitude is the greater of 50 ft. AGL or the maximum autopilot loss. In this case, the maximum altitude loss is 60 {te not 55 ft.

questions.question_text: The maximum altitude loss for a particular malfunctioning autopilot under approach conditions is 55 feet. If the TDZE is 571 feet and the MDA is 1,100 feet, to which minimum altitude may you use this autopilot?
questions.choice_c: 1,050 feet MSL.
questions.explanation: When using an instrument approach facility other than ILS, no person may use an autopilot at an altitude above the terrain that is less than 50 ft. below the approved minimum descent altitude for that procedure, or less than twice the maximum loss specified in the approved Airplane Flight Manual or equivalent for a malfunction of the autopilot under approach conditions, whichever is higher. Note that the MDA in the question refers to a nonprecision approach. The aircraft is allowed to descend to the higher of two altitudes. The first minimum altitude is 50 ft. below the MDA (here 1,100 ft.), which is 1,050 ft. The second is twice the maximum altitude loss of 55 ft., which is 110 ft. AGL or 681 ft. MSL. Since 1,050 ft. MSL is greater than 681 ft. MSL, the minimum altitude for autopilot use is 1,050 ft. MSL. Answer (A) is incorrect. This is the 571 TDZE plus 55 ft. altitude loss, which implies that the autopilot can be used until an altitude is reached from which a malfunction will result in a ground strike. Answer (B) is incorrect. This is the MDA of 1,100 less twice the maximum autopilot loss of 55 ft., which is not provided for in the FARs.

questions.question_text: What is the minimum passenger seating configuration that requires a second-in-command?
questions.choice_c: 10 seats.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: No certificate holder may operate an aircraft without a second-in-command if that aircraft has a passenger seating configuration, excluding any pilot seat, of 10 seats or more. Answer (A) is incorrect. Excluding any pilot seat, a second- in-command Is required with a minimum passenger seating of 10, not 15. Answer (B) is incorrect. A second-in-command is required with a minimum passenger seating of 10, not 12.

questions.question_text: An autopilot may not be used in place of a second-in-command in any aircraft
questions.choice_a: being operated in commuter air carrier service.
questions.choice_b: having a passenger seating configuration, excluding any pilot’s seat, of 10 seats or more.
questions.choice_c: having a total seating capacity of 10 or more seats and being operated in commuter air service.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: No certificate holder may operate dn aircraft without a second-in-command if that aircraft has a passenger seating configuration, excluding any pilot seat, of 10 seats or more. Thus, an autopilot may not be used in place of a second- in-command in such an aircraft. Answer (A) is incorrect. An autopilot can be used in place of a second-in-command if the airplane has a passenger seating configuration, excluding any pilot seat, of less than 10 seats. This includes aircraft being operated in commuter air carrier service. Answer (C) is incorrect. An autopilot may not be used in place of a second-in-command of any aircraft having passenger seating, not total seating, capacity of 10 or more seats. Pilot seats are excluded from the seating configuration.

questions.question_text: Below what altitude, except in cruise flight, are nonsafety-related cockpit activities by flight crewmembers prohibited?
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: No flight crewmember may perform any nonsafety-related cockpit duties during the critical phases of flight. The critical phases of flight include all ground operations involving taxi, takeoff, landing, and all other flight operations conducted below 10,000 ft., except cruise flight. Answer (A) is incorrect. Flight crewmembers are prohibited from any nonsafety-related cockpit activities below 10,000 ft., not 14,500 ft., except in cruise flight. Answer (C) is incorrect. Flight crewmembers are prohibited from any nonsafety-related cockpit activities below 10,000 ft., not FL 180, except in cruise flight.

questions.subsection: 135.105 Exception to Second-in-Command Requirement: Approval for Use of Autopilot System
questions.question_text: Which is a condition that must be met by a commuter air carrier certificate holder to have an aircraft approved for operation with an autopilot system and no second-in-command?
questions.choice_a: The passenger seating configuration is 10 or more, including any pilot seat.
questions.choice_b: The autopilot system is capable of operating the controls to maintain flight and to maneuver the aircraft about the three axes.
questions.choice_c: The operation is restricted to VFR or VFR over-the-top.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Unless two pilots are required for operations under VFR, a person may operate an aircraft without a second- in-command if it is equipped with an operative approved autopilot ‘ system that is capable of operating the aircraft controls to maintain flight and maneuver it about the three axes. Answer (A) is incorrect. An autopilot system, without a second-in-command, may be used if the passenger seating configuration is 10 or more, excluding, not including, any pilot seat. Answer (C) is incorrect. IFR operations may take place under certain conditions, including that the pilot in command must have 100 hr. in make and model of aircraft and that the certificate holder’s operations specifications authorize use of an autopilot.

questions.subsection: 135.105 Exception to Second-in-Command Requirement: Approval for Use of Autopilot System
questions.question_text: A commuter air carrier certificate holder plans to assign a pilot as pilot in command of an aircraft having eight passenger seats to be used in passenger-carrying operations. Which experience requirement must that pilot meet if the aircraft is to be flown with an operative approved autopilot and no second-in-command?
questions.choice_a: 100 hours as pilot in command in the category, class, and type. 15 or more.
questions.choice_b: 50 hours and 10 landings as pilot in command in the make and model. 19 or more.
questions.choice_c: 100 hours as pilot in command in the make and model. 43.A flight attendant crewmember is required on aircraft having a passenger seating configuration, excluding any pilot seat, of 20o0r more.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: No certificate holder may use any person nor may any person serve as a pilot in command of an aircraft with an autopilot and no second-in-command operated by a commuter air carrier in passenger-carrying operations unless that person has at least 100 hr. pilot-in-command flight time in the make and model of aircraft to be flown and has met all other applicable requirements of FAR Part 135. Answer (A) is incorrect. The 100 hr. must be in the make and model of aircraft to be flown, not in the category, class, and type. Answer (B) is incorrect. One hundred hr., not 50 hr., of flight time must be in the make and model of aircraft. The number of landings, or combination of flight hours and landings, is not specified.

questions.subsection: 135.105 Exception to Second-in-Command Requirement: Approval for Use of Autopilot System
questions.question_text: Which person, other than the second-in- command, may the pilot in command permit to manipulate the flight controls?
questions.choice_a: A member of the National Transportation Safety Board who holds a pilot certificate appropriate for the aircraft.
questions.choice_b: An authorized FAA safety representative who is qualified in the aircraft, and is checking flight operations.
questions.choice_c: A pilot employed by an engineering firm who is authorized by the certificate holder to conduct flight tests.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: No pilot in command may allow any person to manipulate the flight controls of an aircraft during flight conducted under FAR Part 135, nor may any person manipulate the controls during such flight, unless that person is 1. A pilot employed by the certificate holder and qualified in the aircraft; or 2. An authorized FAA safety representative who has the permission of the pilot in command, is qualified in the aircraft, and is checking flight operations. Answer (A) is incorrect. Only a pilot qualified in the aircraft and employed by the certificate holder, not a member of the NTSB, may manipulate the controls. Answer (C) is incorrect. Flight checks by an engineering firm may be done under FAR Part 91, not under FAR Part 135, operations.

questions.question_text: Before takeoff, the pilot in command of an aircraft carrying passengers shall ensure that all passengers have been orally briefed on the normal and emergency use of oxygen
questions.choice_a: _ if the flight involves operations above 12,000 feet MSL.
questions.choice_b: regardless of the altitude at which the flight will operate. C._ if the flight involves operations at or above 12,000 feet MSL for more than 30 minutes.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Before each takeoff, each pilot in command of an aircraft carrying passengers must ensure that all passengers have been orally briefed on the normal and emergency use of oxygen if the flight involves operations above 12,000 ft. MSL. Answer (B) is incorrect. The pilot in command must ensure that all passengers have been orally briefed on the normal and emergency use of oxygen before a flight involving operations above 12,000 ft. MSL, not before any flight. Answer (C) is incorrect. The pilot in command must ensure that all passengers have been orally briefed on the normal and emergency use of oxygen if the flight involves operations above 12,000 ft. MSL, regardless of the amount of time above 12,000 ft. MSL.

questions.question_text: Before each takeoff, the pilot in command of an aircraft carrying passengers shall ensure that all passengers have been orally briefed on the
questions.choice_a: location of normal and emergency exits, oxygen masks, and life preservers.
questions.choice_b: use of safety belts, location and operation of fire extinguishers, and smoking.
questions.choice_c: use of seatbelts, smoking, and location and use of survival equipment.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Before each takeoff, each pilot in command of an aircraft carrying passengers shall ensure that all passengers have been orally briefed on Smoking, which is generally not permitted Use of safety belts The placement of seat backs in an upright position before takeoff and landing The location and means for opening the passenger entry door and emergency exits The location of survival equipment Ditching procedures and the use of required flotation equipment if the flight involves extended overwater operation The normal and emergency use of oxygen if the flight involves operations above 12,000 ft. MSL The location and operation of fire extinguishers CON SC es OLE Answer (A) is incorrect. Oxygen mask and life preserver information is required only in flight above 12,000 ft. MSL and during extended over-water operations. Answer (C) is incorrect. There is no requirement for orally briefing the passengers on the use of survival equipment.

questions.question_text: The oral before flight briefing required on passenger-carrying aircraft shall be
questions.choice_a: supplemented by an actual demonstration of emergency exit door operation by a crewmember.
questions.choice_b: presented by the pilot in command or another flight crewmember, as a crewmember demonstrates the operation of the emergency equipment.
questions.choice_c: conducted by a crewmember or the pilot in command and supplemented by printed cards for the use of each passenger.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: The oral briefing shall be given by the pilot in command or a crewmember. The briefing shall be supplemented by printed cards, which must be carried in the aircraft in locations convenient for the use of each passenger. Answer (A) is incorrect. There is no requirement to demonstrate the emergency door operation. Only a verbal description of the location and operation of the passenger entry door and emergency exits is required. Answer (B) is incorrect. There is no requirement to demonstrate the operation of the emergency equipment.

questions.question_text: Who may be allowed to carry a deadly weapon on board an aircraft operated under FAR Part 135?
questions.choice_a: Official bodyguards attached to foreign legations.
questions.choice_b: Crewmembers and/or others authorized by the certificate holder.
questions.choice_c: Employees of a municipality or a state, or of the United States.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: No person may, while on board an aircraft operated under FAR Part 135, carry on or about that person a deadly or dangerous weapon, either concealed or unconcealed, except crewmembers and other persons authorized by the certificate holder to carry arms. Answer (A) is incorrect. Official bodyguards attached to foreign legations are not exempted from the prohibition against the carriage of a deadly weapon on board an aircraft operating under FAR Part 135. Answer (C) is incorrect. Only those employees of a municipality, a state, or the U.S. authorized to carry arms may be allowed to carry a deadly weapon, not any employee.

questions.question_text: What aircraft operating under FAR Part 135 are required to have a third gyroscopic bank-and-pitch indicator installed?
questions.choice_a: All airplanes that are turbojet powered.
questions.choice_b: All multiengine airplanes that require a two pilot flight crew.
questions.choice_c: All turbine powered aircraft having a passenger seating capacity of 30 seats or more.
questions.explanation: No person may operate a turbojet aircraft unless a third gyroscopic bank-and-pitch indicator is installed. Answer (B) is incorrect. All turbojet aircraft, not only those that require a two-pilot flight crew, are required to have a third gyroscopic bank-and-pitch indicator. Answer (C) is incorrect. Only turbojet aircraft, not all airplanes having a passenger seating capacity of 30 or more, are required to have a third gyroscopic bank-and-pitch indicator.

questions.question_text: In airplanes where a third gyroscopic bank-and- pitch indicator is required, that instrument must
questions.choice_a: continue reliable operation for at least 30 minutes after the output of the airplane’s electrical generating system falls below an optimum level.
questions.choice_b: be operable by a selector switch which may be actuated from either pilot station.
questions.choice_c: continue reliable operation for a minimum of 30 minutes after total failure of the electrical generating system.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: When a third gyroscopic bank-and-pitch indicator is required, that instrument must continue reliable operation for a minimum of 30 min. after total failure of the electrical generating system. Answer (A) is incorrect. Thirty minutes of reliable operation is required of the third gyroscopic attitude indicator in turbojets after a total failure of the aircraft's electrical generating system, not when the system drops below an optimal level. Answer (B) is incorrect. The third (backup) gyroscopic attitude indicator in a turbojet must be automatically switched to the emergency power source after total electrical system failure. Manual switching is not allowed.

questions.question_text: Which aircraft must be equipped with an approved public address and crewmember interphone system?
questions.choice_a: All turbine-engine-powered aircraft having a seating configuration of more than 19 seats.
questions.choice_b: Aircraft having a passenger seating configuration, excluding any pilot seat, of more than 19 seats.
questions.choice_c: Multiengine aircraft having a passenger seating configuration of 10 seats or more.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: All aircraft having a passenger seating configuration of more than 19 seats (excluding any pilot seat) are required to be equipped with an approved public address and crewmember interphone system. Answer (A) is incorrect. The requirement for a public address and crewmember interphone system is based on passenger seating, excluding any pilot seat, not the total airplane seating configuration. Answer (C) is incorrect. Passenger seating of 10 seats or more requires a second-in-command, not a public address system.

questions.question_text: The crewmember interphone system on a large turbojet-powered airplane provides a means of two- way communications between ground personnel and at least one of two flight crewmembers in the pilot compartment, when the aircraft is on the ground. The interphone station for use by ground personnel must be located so that those using the system from that station
questions.choice_a: are always visible from within the airplane.
questions.choice_b: are able to avoid the intake areas of the engines.
questions.choice_c: may avoid visible detection from within the airplane.
questions.explanation: When a large turbojet-powered airplane is on the ground, the crewmember interphone system provides a means of two-way communication between ground personnel and at least one of two flight crewmembers in the pilot compartment. The interphone station for use by ground personnel must be located so that those using the system from that station may avoid visible detection from within the airplane. Answer (A) is incorrect. The interphone system for ground personnel use must be located to avoid visible detection, not be visible, from within the airplane. Answer (B) is incorrect. The interphone station for ground personnel use must be located to avoid visible detection from within the airplane, not to avoid the intake areas of the engines.

questions.question_text: An approved cockpit voice recorder is required equipment in
questions.choice_a: large turbine-powered airplanes having a maximum passenger capacity of 20 or more seats.
questions.choice_b: multiengine, turbine-powered airplanes having a passenger seating configuration of 20 or more seats.
questions.choice_c: all aircraft operated in commuter air carrier service having a passenger seating configuration of 20 seats or more.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: No person may operate a multi-engine, turbine-powered airplane having a passenger seating configuration of 20 or more seats unless it is equipped with an approved cockpit voice recorder. Answer (A) is incorrect. A cockpit voice recorder is required in multi-engine, turbine-powered airplanes, not only large turbine- powered airplanes, having a maximum passenger seating capacity of 20 or more seats. Answer (C) is incorrect. A cockpit voice recorder is required in multi-engine, turbine-powered airplanes, not in all aircraft operated in commuter air carrier service, having a passenger seating configuration of 20 or more seats.

questions.question_text: Information recorded during normal operation of a cockpit voice recorder in a multiengine turbine- powered airplane
questions.choice_a: may all be erased or otherwise obliterated except for the last 30 minutes.
questions.choice_b: may all be erased or otherwise obliterated except for the last 30 minutes prior to landing.
questions.choice_c: may all be erased, prior to each flight, unless the NTSB has requested that it be kept for 60 days.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Information recorded during normal operation of a cockpit voice recorder in a multi-engine turbine-powered airplane (having a seating configuration of 20 or more seats) may all be erased or otherwise obliterated except for the last 30 min. Answer (B) is incorrect. Information recorded may be erased or otherwise obliterated except for the last 30 min., not the last 30 min. prior to landing. Answer (C) is incorrect. Information recorded may be erased or otherwise obliterated except for the last 30 min., not prior to each flight.

questions.question_text: During which time period must a required voice recorder of a passenger-carrying airplane be continuously operated?
questions.choice_a: From the beginning of taxi to the end of the landing roll.
questions.choice_b: From engine start at departure airport to engine shutdown at landing airport.
questions.choice_c: From the use of the checklist before the flight to completion of the final check at the end of the flight.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Cockpit voice recorders must be operated continuously from the use of the checklist before the flight to completion of the final checklist at the end of the flight. Answer (A) is incorrect. The requirement is from the use of the checklist before the flight, not the beginning of taxi, to the completion of the final checklist, not the end of the landing roll. Answer (B) is incorrect. Prior to the engine start, a pre-start checklist is usually required, and after engine shutdown, a final check is usually required.

questions.question_text: Information recorded during normal operations by a required cockpit voice recorder
questions.choice_a: must be erased or obliterated, except for the last 30 minutes.
questions.choice_b: may be erased or obliterated, only once each flight.
questions.choice_c: may be erased or obliterated, except the last 30 minutes.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Information recorded during normal operations by a required cockpit voice recorder may be erased or obliterated, except for the last 30 min. Answer (A) is incorrect. The information recorded may, not must, be erased or obliterated, except for the last 30 min. Answer (B) is incorrect. The information recorded may be erased or obliterated, except for the last 30 min., not only once each flight.

questions.question_text: In which airplanes is a ground proximity warning system required?
questions.choice_a: All airplanes having a passenger seating configuration, excluding any pilot seat, of 10 seats or more.
questions.choice_b: Turbine-powered airplanes having a passenger seating configuration, excluding any pilot seat, of 10 seats or more.
questions.choice_c: Turbine-powered aircraft having a passenger seating configuration, including any pilot seat, of 10 seats or more.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: No person may operate a turbine-powered airplane having a passenger seating configuration, excluding any pilot seat, of 10 seats or more, unless it is equipped with a ground proximity warning system or a system that conveys warnings of excessive closure rates with the terrain and any deviations below glide slope by visual and audible means. Answer (A) is incorrect. Only a turbine-powered airplane, not a reciprocating airplane, is required to have a ground proximity warning system if it has 10 passenger seats or more. Answer (C) is incorrect. Pilot seats are excluded, not included, in the number of seats.

questions.question_text: When a ground proximity warning system is required under FAR Part 135, it must
questions.choice_a: convey warnings of any deviation below glide slope and of excessive closure rate with the terrain.
questions.choice_b: convey warnings for excessive closure rates with the terrain but not for deviation from an ILS glide slope.
questions.choice_c: alert the pilot by an audible and visual warning signal when deviation above or below glide slope occurs.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: No person may operate a turbine-powered airplane having a passenger seating configuration, excluding any pilot seat, of 10 seats or more, unless it is equipped with a ground proximity warning system or a system that conveys warnings of excessive closure rates with the terrain and any deviations below glide slope by visual and audible means. Answer (B) is incorrect. The ground proximity warning system must warn the pilot of both excessive closure rates with the terrain and any deviations below glide slope. Answer (C) is incorrect. The ground proximity warning system must alert the pilot by an audible and visual warning signal of excessive closure rates with the terrain and any deviations below, not above, the glide slope.

questions.question_text: When a ground proximity warning system is required, it must
questions.choice_a: apply corrective control pressure when deviation below glide slope occurs.
questions.choice_b: incorporate a means of alerting the pilot when a system malfunction occurs.
questions.choice_c: incorporate a backup feature that activates automatically upon total failure of the aircraft's electrical generating system.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: A ground proximity warning system must have a means of alerting the pilot when a malfunction occurs in the system. Answer (A) is incorrect. There is no requirement for auto- flight or autopilot connection to the ground proximity warning system. However, a means for alerting the pilot when a malfunction occurs is mandated by the regulation. Answer (C) is incorrect. No backup feature is required. Electrical failure does not mean that the airplane is too close to the ground or below the glide slope.

questions.question_text: In which airplanes is a Class A TAWS warning system required?
