questions.choice_a: Turbine-powered aircraft having a passenger seating configuration, including any pilot seat, of 10 seats or more.
questions.choice_b: Turbine-powered airplanes having a passenger seating configuration, excluding any pilot seat, of 10 seats or more.
questions.choice_c: All airplanes naving a passenger seating configuration, excluding any pilot seat, of 10 seats or more.
questions.explanation: _ DISCUSSION: No person may operate a turbine-powered airplane configured with 10 or more passenger seats, excluding any pilot seat, unless that airplane is equipped with an approved terrain awareness and warning system that meets the requirements for Class A equipment in Technical Standard Order (TSO)-C151. The airplane must also include an approved terrain situational awareness display. Answer (A) is incorrect. No person may operate a turbine- powered airplane configured with 10 or more passenger seats, excluding any pilot seat (not including), unless that airplane is equipped with an approved terrain awareness and warning system that meets the requirements for Class A equipment in TSO-C151. Answer (C) is incorrect. The Class A TAWS requirement is only for turbine-powered aircraft.

questions.question_text: A pressurized airplane being operated at FL 330 can descend safely to 15,000 feet MSL in all occupants other than the pilots?
questions.choice_c: 30 minutes.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Pressurized aircraft above 15,000 ft. MSL require oxygen availability to each occupant of the aircraft, other than the pilots, for 1 hr. unless, at all times during flight above that altitude, the aircraft can safely descend to 15,000 ft. MSL within 4 min., in which case only a 30-min. supply is required. Answer (A) is incorrect. One hr. of oxygen for each passenger is required if a descent to 15,000 ft. MSL cannot be made within 4 min. Answer (B) is incorrect. There is no 45-min. time period specified with respect to oxygen supply under FAR Part 135.

questions.question_text: The oxygen requirements for occupants of a pressurized airplane operated at altitudes above FL 250 is dependent upon the airplane’s ability to descend safely to an altitude of
questions.choice_b: 12,000 feet MSL at a minimum rate of 2,500 ft/min.
questions.choice_c: 15,000 feet MSL in 4 minutes.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Pressurized aircraft above 15,000 ft. MSL require oxygen availability to each occupant of the aircraft, other than the pilots, for 1 hour unless, at all times during flight above that altitude, the aircraft can safely descend to 15,000 ft. MSL within 4 min., in which case only a 30-min. supply is required. Answer (A) is incorrect. The descent requirement is to 15,000 ft. MSL, not 10,000 ft. MSL, in 4 min. Answer (B) is incorrect. The requirement calls for a descent to be made to 15,000 ft. MSL, not 12,000 ft. MSL, in 4 min. A minimum descent rate is not specified.

questions.question_text: Between what altitudes must oxygen be available to at least 10 percent of the occupants, in an unpressurized airplane, other than the pilots?
questions.choice_a: Above 12,000 feet through 16,000 feet MSL, for any time period.
questions.choice_b: Above 10,000 feet through 15,000 feet MSL, if flight at those altitudes is of more than a 30-minute duration.
questions.choice_c: 10,000 feet to 15,000 feet MSL, if flight at those altitudes is of more than a 30-minute duration.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: At altitudes above 10,000 ft. through 15,000 ft. MSL, oxygen dispensers and oxygen must be available for at least 10% of the occupants of an unpressurized aircraft, other than the pilots, for that part of the flight at those altitudes that is of more than 30-min. duration. Answer (A) is incorrect. Oxygen must be available to at least 10% of the occupants in an unpressurized airplane, other than the pilots, from above 10,000 ft. MSL through 15,000 ft. MSL, not from above 12,000 ft. MSL through 16,000 ft. MSL, if flight at those altitudes is of more than a 30-min. duration, not of any duration. Answer (C) is incorrect. Oxygen must be available to at least 10% of the occupants in an unpressurized airplane, other than the pilots, from above, not at, 10,000 ft. MSL through 15,000 ft. MSL, if flight at those altitudes is of more than a 30-min. duration.

questions.question_text: An unpressurized aircraft with 20 occupants other than the pilots will be cruising at 14,000 feet MSL for 25 minutes. For how many, if any, of these occupants must there be an oxygen supply?
questions.choice_c: None.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: At altitudes above 10,000 ft. through 15,000 ft. MSL, oxygen dispensers and oxygen must be available for at least 10% of the occupants of an unpressurized aircraft, other than the pilots, for that part of the flight at those altitudes that is of more than 30 min. duration. This flight is only 25 min. in length. Answer (A) is incorrect. No oxygen supply is required for flight between 10,000 ft. MSL and 15,000 ft. MSL for 30 min. or less. Answer (B) is incorrect. An oxygen supply for two occupants is the minimum requirement for more than 30, not 25, min. at 14,000 ft. MSL.

questions.question_text: At what altitude, in an unpressurized airplane, must all passengers be supplied oxygen?
questions.choice_c: Above 15,000 feet MSL. > a
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Above 15,000 ft. MSL, oxygen must be supplied to all passengers in an unpressurized aircraft. Answer (A) is incorrect. From 10,000 ft. MSL through 15,000 ft. MSL, oxygen is required for at least 10%, not all, of the passengers if the flight is more than 30 min. in duration. Answer (B) is incorrect. Only in flight at 14,000 ft. MSL for more than 30 min. is oxygen required for at least 10%, not all, of the passengers.

questions.subsection: 135.159 Equipment Requirements: Carrying Passengers under VFR at Night or under VFR Over-the-Top Conditions
questions.question_text: No person may operate an aircraft under 14 CFR Part 135, carrying passengers under VFR at night, unless
questions.choice_a: each flight crewmember has a flashlight having at least two size “D” batteries or the equivalent.
questions.choice_b: it is equipped with a flashlight having at least two size “D” cells or the equivalent.
questions.choice_c: each crewmember has a flashlight having at least two size “D” cells and a spare bulb.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: No person may operate an aircraft carrying passengers under VFR at night, unless it is equipped with a flashlight having at least two size “D” batteries or the equivalent. Answer (A) is incorrect. Only one flashlight having at least two size “D” batteries or the equivalent is required in the airplane, not one flashlight for each flight crewmember. Answer (C) is incorrect. Only one flashlight is required in the airplane, not one flashlight for each crewmember, and there is no requirement fora spare bulb.

questions.subsection: 135.159 Equipment Requirements: Carrying Passengers under VFR at Night or under VFR Over-the-Top Conditions
questions.question_text: In addition to fully-equipped life rafts and life preservers, what emergency equipment must be provided during extended overwater operations?
questions.choice_a: One water resistant, self-buoyant, portable survival-type emergency radio transmitter for each life raft.
questions.choice_b: Each aircraft must have at least one life raft, equipped with a survival-type emergency locator transmitter.
questions.choice_c: One pyrotechnic signaling device for each aircraft.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: No person may operate an aircraft in extended overwater operations unless there is attached to one of the required life rafts a survival-type emergency locator transmitter. Answer (A) is incorrect. Only one, not each, life raft must be equipped with a survival-type emergency locator transmitter. Answer (C) is incorrect. A pyrotechnic signaling device is required for each life raft, not one for each aircraft.

questions.subsection: 135.159 Equipment Requirements: Carrying Passengers under VFR at Night or under VFR Over-the-Top Conditions
questions.question_text: Each aircraft being operated in extended overwater operations, must have a life preserver for each
questions.choice_c: passenger seat, plus 10 percent.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Each aircraft being operated in extended overwater operations must have a life preserver equipped with an approved survivor locator light for each occupant of the aircraft. Answer (B) is incorrect. A life preserver is required for each aircraft occupant, not for each seat on the aircraft. Answer (C) is incorrect. A life preserver is required for each aircraft occupant, not for each passenger seat, plus 10%.

questions.subsection: 135.159 Equipment Requirements: Carrying Passengers under VFR at Night or under VFR Over-the-Top Conditions
questions.question_text: What emergency equipment is required for extended overwater operations?
questions.choice_a: A portable survival emergency locator transmitter for each life raft.
questions.choice_b: A pyrotechnic signaling device for each life preserver.
questions.choice_c: Alife preserver equipped with a survivor locator light, for each person on the airplane.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Each airplane being operated in extended overwater operations must have a life preserver equipped with an approved survivor locator light for each person on the airplane. Answer (A) is incorrect. An approved survival-type emergency locator transmitter is required to be attached to only one life raft, not each life raft. Answer (B) is incorrect. A pyrotechnic signaling device is required for each life raft, not each life preserver.

questions.subsection: 135.159 Equipment Requirements: Carrying Passengers under VFR at Night or under VFR Over-the-Top Conditions
questions.question_text: An airplane, operated by a commuter air carrier, flying in extended overwater operations must carry enough approved life rafts of a rated capacity and buoyancy to accommodate the occupants of the aircraft. Each life raft must be equipped with
questions.choice_b: colored smoke flares and a signal mirror.
questions.choice_c: one fishing kit for each person the raft is rated to carry.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: An airplane, operated by a commuter air carrier, flying in extended overwater operations must Carry enough approved life rafts of rated capacity and buoyancy to accommodate the occupants of the aircraft. Each life raft must be equipped with one approved pyrotechnic signaling device. Answer (B) is incorrect. Colored smoke flares are not required emergency equipment for extended overwater operations. Answer (C) is incorrect. One fishing kit for each life raft, not each person the raft is rated to carry, is required emergency equipment for extended overwater operations.

questions.subsection: 135.171 Shoulder Harness Installation at Flight Crewmember Stations
questions.question_text: Life preservers required for extended overwater operations are stored within easy reach of each passenger. under each occupant seat. within easy access of each seated occupant.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Life preservers required for extended overwater operations are stored within easy access of each seated occupant. Answer (A) is incorrect. Life preservers must be within easy access, not reach, of each seated occupant, not only passengers. Answer (B) is incorrect. Life preservers must be within easy access of each seated occupant, not necessarily under each occupant’s seat.

questions.subsection: 135.171 Shoulder Harness Installation at Flight Crewmember Stations
questions.question_text: Which is a requirement for life preservers during extended overwater operations? Each life preserver must be equipped with a dye marker. an approved survivor locator light. one flashlight having at least two size “D” cells or equivalent.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: No person may operate an aircraft in extended overwater operations unless it carries, installed in conspicuously marked locations easily accessible to the occupants, an approved life preserver equipped with an approved survivor locator light for each occupant of the aircraft. The life preserver must be easily accessible to each seated occupant. Answer (A) is incorrect. The dye marker is for each life raft, not each life preserver. Answer (C) is incorrect. The flashlight and associated batteries are required for each life raft, not each life preserver.

questions.subsection: 135.171 Shoulder Harness Installation at Flight Crewmember Stations
questions.question_text: Which airplanes must have a shoulder harness installed at each flight crewmember station?
questions.choice_a: All airplanes used in commuter air service, having a passenger seating configuration of 9, excluding any pilot seat. All airplanes operating under FAR Part 135, having a seating configuration for 10 persons. All turbojet-powered airplanes.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: No person may operate a turbojet aircraft or an aircraft having a passenger seating configuration, excluding any pilot seat, of 10 seats or more unless it is equipped with an approved shoulder harness installed for each flight crewmember station. Answer (A) is incorrect. The requirement is for all turbojet aircraft or aircraft having a passenger seating configuration, excluding any pilot seat, of 10 seats or more, regardless of scheduled or on-demand operations. Answer (B) is incorrect. Nonturbojet-powered airplanes must have a passenger seating configuration of 10 seats or more, excluding any pilot seat, not just a total of 10 seats in the airplane.

questions.subsection: 135.171 Shoulder Harness Installation at Flight Crewmember Stations
questions.question_text: Which group of aircraft must have a shoulder harness installed at each flight crewmember station?
questions.choice_a: Aircraft having a passenger seating configuration, excluding any pilot seat, of 10 seats or more. All passenger-carrying aircraft operating under FAR Part 135, having a seating configuration for 10 persons. Large aircraft being operated in commuter air service, having a passenger seating configuration of 9, excluding any pilot seat.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: No person may operate a turbojet aircraft or an aircraft having a passenger seating configuration, excluding any pilot seat, of 10 seats or more unless it is equipped with an approved shoulder harness installed for each flight crewmember station. Answer (B) is incorrect. The requirement is for all turbojet aircraft or aircraft having 10 passenger seats or more, not all passenger-carrying aircraft operating under FAR Part 135. Answer (C) is incorrect. The requirement is for all turbojet aircraft or aircraft having 10 passenger seats or more, even if under 12,500-Ib. gross takeoff weight, regardless of whether the aircraft is conducting scheduled or on-demand operations.

questions.subsection: 135.171 Shoulder Harness Installation at Flight Crewmember Stations
questions.question_text: When is a pilot not required to keep the shoulder harness fastened during takeoff and landing while at a pilot station? When operating an aircraft having a passenger seating configuration, excluding any pilot seat, of 10 seats or less. When the pilot cannot perform the required duties with the shoulder harness fastened. When serving as pilot in command or second- in-command of an aircraft having a total seating capacity of eight seats or less.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Each flight crewmember occupying a station equipped with a shoulder harness must fasten the shoulder harness during takeoff and landing, except that the shoulder harness may be unfastened if the crewmember cannot perform his or her required duties with the shoulder harness fastened. Answer (A) is incorrect. A flight crewmember may unfasten his or her shoulder harness to perform required duties that cannot be performed when in a shoulder harness. The number of passenger seats has no effect on this requirement. Answer (C) is incorrect. A flight crewmember may unfasten his or her shoulder harness to perform required duties that cannot be performed when in a shoulder harness. The number of passenger seats has no effect on this requirement.

questions.question_text: In which aircraft, or under what conditions, is airborne thunderstorm detection equipment required?
questions.choice_a: Large multiengine turbine-powered aircraft having a passenger seating configuration of 19 seats or more being operated by a commuter air carrier.
questions.choice_b: Any aircraft having a passenger seating configuration of 19 seats or more that is engaged in passenger-carrying operations under IFR or at night.
questions.choice_c: Small aircraft having a passenger seating configuration of 10 seats or more, excluding any pilot seat, that are engaged in passenger- carrying operations.
questions.explanation: No person may operate an aircraft that has a passenger seating configuration, excluding any pilot seat, of 10 seats or more in passenger-carrying operations, unless the aircraft is equipped with either approved thunderstorm detection equipment or approved airborne weather radar equipment. Answer (A) is incorrect. The requirement is for any airplane, whether single or multi-engine, piston or turbine-powered, that has a passenger seating configuration of 10, not 19, or more. Answer (B) is incorrect. The requirement is for aircraft passenger seating of 10, not 19, or more under any conditions, not just under IFR or at night.

questions.question_text: Airborne weather radar equipment must be installed in large transport category aircraft, in the conterminous 48 United States,
questions.choice_a: that are engaged in passenger-carrying operations.
questions.choice_b: that are engaged in either cargo or passenger- carrying operations.
questions.choice_c: and be fully operational, although weather forecasts indicate no hazardous conditions. 19 Passengers
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: In the conterminous 48 states, no person may operate a large transport-category aircraft in passenger- carrying operations unless approved airborne weather radar equipment is installed in the aircraft. Answer (B) is incorrect. Airborne weather radar equipment is not required for cargo operations. Answer (C) is incorrect. Day- VFR flights may be conducted with the airborne weather radar inoperative, provided it is done in accordance with the operation specifications and the minimum equipment list.

questions.question_text: How many, if any, approved first aid kits are required on an aircraft having a passenger seating configuration of 20 seats and a passenger load of 14?
questions.choice_c: Two.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: No person may operate an aircraft having a passenger seating configuration, excluding any pilot seat, of more than 19 seats unless it is equipped with one approved first- aid kit for treatment of injuries likely to occur in flight or in a minor accident. Answer (A) is incorrect. One approved first-aid kit is required on each aircraft with a seating capacity of 20 passengers or more. The number of passengers aboard is irrelevant. Answer (C) is incorrect. One approved first-aid kit is required on each aircraft with a seating capacity of 20 passengers or more. The number of passengers aboard is irrelevant.

questions.question_text: An aircraft has a passenger seating configuration of 19 seats, excluding any pilot seats. How many, if any, approved first aid kits are required?
questions.choice_c: None.
questions.explanation: _ DISCUSSION: Approved first-aid kits are required only on aircraft with a seating configuration, excluding any pilot seat, of more than 19 seats. Answer (A) is incorrect. If the passenger seating were more than 19, one first-aid kit would be required. In this case, the configuration is 19 passenger seats, so no first-aid kit is required. Answer (B) is incorrect. If the passenger seating were more than 19, one first-aid kit, not two, would be required. In this case, the configuration is 19 passenger seats, so no first-aid kit is required.

questions.question_text: When a crash ax is required equipment on an aircraft, where should it be located?
questions.choice_b: Ata location inaccessible to the passengers during normal operations.
questions.choice_c: Atalocation accessible to both the crew and passengers during normal operations.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: A crash ax must be located so that it is accessible to the crew but inaccessible to passengers during normal operations. Answer (A) is incorrect. A crash ax must be located so that it is inaccessible to passengers during normal operations but need not be in the flight crew compartment. Answer (C) is incorrect. A crash ax must be located so that it is inaccessible, not accessible, to passengers during normal operations.

questions.question_text: Federal Aviation Regulations require that interior emergency lights on aircraft having a passenger seating configuration of 20 to
questions.choice_a: operate automatically when subjected to a negative G load.
questions.choice_b: be operable manually from the flight crew station and a point in the passenger compartment.
questions.choice_c: be armed or turned on during taxiing and all flight operations.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Aircraft having a passenger seating configuration of more than 19 seats must have interior emergency lights that must be operable manually both from the flight crew station and from a point in the passenger compartment that is readily accessible to a normal flight attendant seat. Answer (A) is incorrect. Interior emergency lights operate automatically (when properly set) in the event of failure of the primary lighting power, not when subjected to a negative G load. Answer (C) is incorrect. Interior emergency lights must be armed or turned on only during taxiing, takeoff, and landing, not all flight operations (e.g., cruise flight)..

questions.question_text: To operate an aircraft with certain equipment inoperative under the provisions of a minimum equipment list, what document authorizing it must be issued to the certificate holder?
questions.choice_a: Letter of Authorization from the Regional Airworthiness Office authorizing such an operation.
questions.choice_b: Operations specifications issued by the FAA district office having certification responsibility.
questions.choice_c: Letter of Authorization issued by the FAA district office having certification responsibility.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: No person may take off an aircraft with inoperable instruments or equipment installed unless the FSDO having certification responsibility has issued the certificate holder operations specifications authorizing operations in accordance with an approved minimum equipment list. Answer (A) is incorrect. Operations specifications, not a letter of authorization, must be issued by the FSDO having certification responsibility, not the Regional Airworthiness Office, to operate under the provisions of an MEL. Answer (C) is incorrect. A letter of authorization is issued for an FAR Part 91 MEL, while operations specifications are issued for an FAR Part 135 MEL.

questions.question_text: Which document would constitute an approved change to the type design without requiring a recertification?
questions.choice_b: The Operations Specifications as approved by the Administrator.
questions.choice_c: A special flight permit.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: An approved minimum equipment list (MEL), as authorized by the operations specifications, constitutes an approved change to the type design without requiring recertification. Answer (B) is incorrect. An approved MEL, not the operations specifications, constitutes an approved change to the type design without requiring a recertification. The operations specifications authorize the use of an MEL. Answer (C) is incorrect. A special flight permit is issued to allow an aircraft to make a flight, under specified conditions with inoperable instruments or equipment, to a place where maintenance can be performed.

questions.subsection: 135.181 Performance Requirements: Aircraft Operated Over-the-Top or in IFR Conditions
questions.question_text: What performance is required of a multiengine airplane with the critical engine inoperative, while carrying passengers for hire in IFR weather conditions?
questions.choice_a: Climb at least 100 ft/min at the highest MEA of the route to be flown or 5,000 feet MSL, whichever is higher. Climb at least 50 ft/min at the MEAs of the route to be flown or 5,000 feet AGL, whichever is higher. Climb at least 50 ft/min at the MEAs of the route to be flown or 5,000 feet MSL, whichever is higher.
questions.explanation: No person may operate a multi-engine aircraft carrying passengers over-the-top or in IFR conditions at a weight that will not allow it to climb, with the critical engine inoperative, at least 50 fom when operating at the MEAs of the route to be flown or 5,000 ft. MSL, whichever is higher. Answer (A) is incorrect. The minimum climb is 50 fpm, not 100 fpm, while operating at the MEAs of the route, not the highest MEA, or 5,000 ft. MSL, whichever is higher. Answer (B) is incorrect. The airplane must be able to climb at 50 fpm at the MEAs of the route to be flown or 5,000 ft. MSL, not 5,000 ft. AGL, whichever is higher.

questions.subsection: 135.181 Performance Requirements: Aircraft Operated Over-the-Top or in IFR Conditions
questions.question_text: Which performance requirement applies to passenger-carrying land airplanes being operated over water?
questions.choice_a: Multiengine airplanes must be able to climb, with the critical engine inoperative, at least 50 ft/min at 1,500 feet above the surface. Single-engine airplanes must be operated at an altitude that will allow them to reach land in case of engine failure. Multiengine airplanes must be able to climb, with the critical engine inoperative, at least 100 ft/min at 1,000 feet above the surface.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: No person may operate a land aircraft carrying passengers over water unless it is operated at an altitude that allows it to reach land in the case of engine failure. Answer (A) is incorrect. A multi-engine airplane must be able to climb, with the critical engine inoperative, at least 50 fpm, at 1,000 ft. above the surface, not 1,500 ft. above the surface. Answer (C) is incorrect. A multi-engine airplane must be able to climb, with the critical engine inoperative, at least 50 fpm, not 100 fom, at 1,000 ft. above the surface.

questions.subsection: 135.181 Performance Requirements: Aircraft Operated Over-the-Top or in IFR Conditions
questions.question_text: The weight and CG of an aircraft used in 135 operations must have been calculated from those values established by actual weighing of the aircraft within what period of time? Multiengine aircraft, preceding 36 calendar months. Multiengine and single-engine aircraft, preceding 36 calendar months. Multiengine aircraft, last 36 calendar months; single-engine, last 24 calendar months.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: No person may operate a multi-engine aircraft unless the current empty weight and center of gravity are calculated from values established by actual weighing of the aircraft within the preceding 36 calendar months. Answer (B) is incorrect. Single-engine aircraft are not required to be actually weighed on a specific time schedule. Answer (C) is incorrect. Single-engine aircraft are not required to be actually weighed on a specific time schedule.

questions.subsection: 135.181 Performance Requirements: Aircraft Operated Over-the-Top or in IFR Conditions
questions.question_text: What are the empty weight and balance currency requirements for aircraft used in 135 operations?
questions.choice_a: The empty weight and CG of multiengine and single-engine aircraft must have been calculated from an actual weighing within the previous 36 calendar months. The empty weight and CG must have been calculated from an actual weighing within the previous 24 calendar months unless the original Airworthiness Certificate was issued within the previous 36 calendar months. The empty weight and CG of multiengine aircraft must have been calculated from an actual weighing within the previous 36 calendar months.
questions.explanation: __ DISCUSSION: No person may operate a multi-engine aircraft unless the current empty weight and center of gravity are calculated from values established by actual weighing of the aircraft within the preceding 36 calendar months. Answer (A) is incorrect. Single-engine aircraft are not required to be actually weighed on a specific time schedule. Answer (B) is incorrect. The empty weight and CG must have been calculated from an actual weighing within the previous 36 months, not 24 months, unless the original airworthiness certificate was issued within the preceding 36 calendar months.

questions.subsection: 4.4 VFRIIFR Operating Limitations and Weather Requirements
questions.question_text: What is the minimum ceiling and visibility for an airplane to operate under VFR in Class G airspace?
questions.choice_c: 1,000-foot ceiling; 2-miles flight visibility.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: No person may operate an airplane under VFR in Class G airspace when the ceiling is less than 1,000 ft. unless flight visibility is at least 2 statute miles. Answer (A) is incorrect. Basic VFR weather minimums in Class G airspace are a ceiling of 1,000 ft., not 2,000 ft., and flight visibility of 2 SM, not 1 statute mile. Answer (B) is incorrect. Basic VFR weather minimums in Class G airspace are a ceiling of ae ft., not 2,000 ft., and flight visibility of 2 SM, not 1 statute mile.

questions.subsection: 4.4 VFRIIFR Operating Limitations and Weather Requirements
questions.question_text: Which condition must be met to conduct IFR operations from an airport that is not at the location where weather observations are made?
questions.choice_a: An “Authorization Letter” permitting the procedure must be issued by the FAA district office charged with the overall inspection of the certificate holder.
questions.choice_b: A “Letter of Waiver” authorizing the procedure must be issued by the Administrator, after an investigation by the U.S. National Weather Service and the FSDO which find the standard of safety to be satisfactory.
questions.choice_c: The Administrator must issue Operations Specifications that permit the procedure.
questions.explanation: Weather observations made and furnished to pilots to conduct IFR operations at an airport must be taken at the airport where those IFR operations are conducted, unless the FAA issues operations specifications allowing the use of weather observations taken at a location not at the airport where the IFR operations are conducted. Answer (A) is incorrect. The FSDO will issue operations specifications, not an authorization letter, to permit IFR operations from an airport that is not at the location where weather observations are made. Answer (B) is incorrect. The FSDO will issue operations specifications, not a letter of waiver, after an investigation by the NWS and the FSDO which find the standard of safety to be satisfactory.

questions.subsection: 4.4 VFRIIFR Operating Limitations and Weather Requirements
questions.question_text: A pilot may make an IFR departure from an airport that does not have an approved standard instrument approach procedure if
questions.choice_a: there is a departure alternate within 60 minutes and the weather there is above landing minimums.
questions.choice_b: the Administrator has issued Operations Specifications to the certificate holder approving the procedure.
questions.choice_c: the departure airport is within 30 minutes flying time of another airport that has an approved standard instrument approach procedure.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: The FAA may issue operations specifications to the certificate holder to allow it to depart from an airport that does not have an approved standard instrument approach procedure. Answer (A) is incorrect. A pilot may make an IFR departure from an airport that does not have an approved standard instrument approach procedure only if it is authorized in the operations specifications, not if there is a departure alternate airport. Answer (C) is incorrect. A pilot may make an IFR departure from an airport that does not have an approved standard instrument approach procedure only if it is authorized in the operations specifications, not if there is a departure alternate airport.

questions.subsection: 4.4 VFRIIFR Operating Limitations and Weather Requirements
questions.question_text: No person may takeoff an aircraft under IFR from an airport that has takeoff weather minimums but that is below landing minimums unless there is an alternate airport within
questions.choice_a: 1 hour at normal indicated airspeed of the departure airport.
questions.choice_b: 1 hour at normal cruise speed in still air of the departure airport.
questions.choice_c: 1 hour at normal cruise speed in still air with one engine operating.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: No person may take off an aircraft under IFR from an airport where weather conditions are at or above takeoff minimums but are below authorized IFR landing minimums unless there is an alternate airport within 1 hr. flying time at normal cruising speed in still air. Answer (A) is incorrect. The alternate must be within 1 hr. at normal cruise speed in still air, not at normal indicated airspeed. Answer (C) is incorrect. An alternate airport within 1 hr. at normal cruise speed in still air with one engine operating is a requirement for a two-engined airplane operating under FAR Part 121, not FAR Part 135.

questions.question_text: A takeoff may not be made from an airport that is below the authorized IFR landing minimums unless
questions.choice_a: there is an alternate airport with the required IFR landing minimums within 60 minutes’ flying time, at normal cruising speed in still air.
questions.choice_b: the departure airport is forecast to have the required IFR landing minimums within 1 hour.
questions.choice_c: there is an alternate airport with the required IFR landing minimums within 60 minutes’ flying time, at normal cruising speed in still air with one engine inoperative.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: No person may take off an aircraft under IFR from an airport where weather conditions are at or above takeoff minimums but are below authorized IFR landing minimums unless there is an alternate airport within 1 hr. flying time at normal cruising speed in still air. Answer (B) is incorrect. The regulations state that an alternate airport is required within 1 hr. flying time. The FARs do not refer to the forecast weather at the departure airport. Answer (C) is incorrect. There is no requirement that flying time be calculated with one engine inoperative since some operations are conducted with single-engine aircraft.

questions.question_text: A pilot may not begin an IFR operation unless the next airport of intended landing is forecast to be at or above authorized IFR landing minimums at
questions.choice_a: the estimated time of arrival, plus or minus 1 hour.
questions.choice_c: the estimated time of arrival, plus or minus 30 minutes. 135.221 IFR: Alternate Airport Weather Minimums
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: No person may take off an aircraft under IFR or begin an IFR or over-the-top operation unless the latest weather reports or forecasts, or any combination of them, indicate that weather conditions at the estimated time of arrival at the next airport of intended landing will be at or above authorized IFR landing minimums. Answer (A) is incorrect. The forecast or weather conditions are for estimated time of arrival, not plus or minus 1 hour. Answer (C) is incorrect. The forecast or weather conditions are for estimated time of arrival, not plus or minus 30 minutes.

questions.question_text: A pilot may not designate an airport as an alternate unless the weather reports, or forecasts, or any combination of them indicate that it will be at or above alternate airport landing minimum at the
questions.choice_b: estimated time of arrival, plus or minus 1 hour.
questions.choice_c: estimated time of arrival.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: No person may designate an alternate airport unless the weather reports or forecasts, or any combination of them, indicate that the weather conditions will be at or above authorized alternate airport landing minimums for that airport at the estimated time of arrival. Answer (A) is incorrect. The alternate airport designation is based on the estimated arrival time, not time of departure. Answer (B) is incorrect. The alternate airport designation is based on the weather at the estimated arrival time, not plus or minus 1 hour.

questions.question_text: An alternate for a destination airport (circling not authorized) is not required if, for at least 1 hour before and after the ETA, the required visibility exists, and the forecast ceiling is at least
questions.choice_a: 1,500 feet above the lowest published minimum, or 2,000 feet above the airport elevation, whichever is higher.
questions.choice_b: 1,500 feet above the lowest MDA or 2,000 feet above the runway touchdown zone elevation, whichever is higher.
questions.choice_c: 1,000 feet above the lowest published minimum, or 1,500 feet above the airport elevation, whichever is higher.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: If a standard instrument approach procedure for the first airport of intended landing is prescribed and, for at least 1 hr. before and after the estimated time of arrival, the appropriate weather reports or forecasts or any combination of them indicate that visibility for that airport is forecast to be at least 3 SM, or 2 SM more than the lowest applicable visibility minimums, whichever is the greater, for the instrument approach procedure to be used at the destination airport, no alternate landing airport is required. If a circling instrument approach is not authorized for the destination airport and the ceiling will be at least 1,500 ft. above the lowest published minimum or 2,000 ft. above the airport elevation, whichever is higher, no alternate landing airport is required. Answer (B) is incorrect. If a circling approach is not authorized, the ceiling must be at least 1,500 ft. above the lowest published minimum, not necessarily the lowest MDA, or 2,000 ft. above the airport elevation, not the touchdown zone elevation. Answer (C) is incorrect. The requirement is 1,500 ft., not 1,000 ft., above the lowest published minimum altitude or 2,000 ft., not 1,500 ft., above the airport elevation.

questions.subsection: 135.225 IFR: Takeoff, Approach, and Landing Minimums
questions.question_text: If the weather forecasts do not require the listing of an alternate airport on an IFR flight, the airplane must carry sufficient fuel to fly to the destination airport and
questions.choice_a: make one missed approach and thereafter have a 45-minute reserve at normal cruising speed.
questions.choice_b: fly thereafter for 45 minutes at normal cruising speed.
questions.choice_c: fly for 45 minutes thereafter at normal cruise climb speed.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: No person may operate an aircraft in IFR conditions unless it carries enough fuel (considering weather reports or forecasts, or any combination of them) to complete the flight to the first airport of intended landing and fly after that for 45 min. at normal cruising speed, if no alternate airport is required. Answer (A) is incorrect. There is no regulatory requirement to plan for fuel used for a missed approach. Answer (C) is incorrect. The airplane must carry enough fuel to fly to the destination airport and fly after that for 45 min. at normal cruising, not climb, speed.

questions.subsection: 135.225 IFR: Takeoff, Approach, and Landing Minimums
questions.question_text: If the weather forecasts require the listing of an alternate airport on an IFR flight, the airplane must carry enough fuel to fly to the first airport of intended landing, then to the alternate, and fly thereafter for a minimum of
questions.choice_b: 45 minutes at normal cruise speed and then complete an approach and landing.
questions.choice_c: 45 minutes at normal cruise speed.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: No person may operate an aircraft in IFR conditions unless it carries enough fuel (considering weather reports or forecasts, or any combination of them) to complete the flight to the first airport of intended landing then fly to the alternate airport and fly after that for 45 min. at normal cruising speed. Answer (A) is incorrect. The airplane must have enough fuel to fly to the alternate airport and to fly after that for 45 min. at normal cruising, not holding, speed. Answer (B) is incorrect. There is no regulatory requirement that there should be enough fuel to complete an approach and landing after 45 minutes.

questions.subsection: 135.225 IFR: Takeoff, Approach, and Landing Minimums
questions.question_text: Assuming the required ceiling exists, an alternate for the destination airport is not required under 14 CFR Part 135 if, for at least 1 hour before and after the ETA, the forecast visibility is
questions.choice_a: 5 statute miles, or 3 nautical miles more than the lowest applicable visibility minimums for the instrument approach procedure to be used, whichever is greater.
questions.choice_b: 3statute miles, or 2 statute miles more than the lowest applicable visibility minimums for the instrument approach procedure to be used, whichever is greater.
questions.choice_c: 3 nautical miles, or 2 nautical miles more than the lowest applicable visibility minimums for the approach procedure to be used, whichever is greater.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: No alternate is required if the destination airport has at least one standard approach procedure and, for at least 1 hr. before and after the estimated time of arrival, the appropriate weather reports or forecasts or any combination of them indicate that visibility for that airport is forecast to be at least 3 SM, or 2 SM more than the lowest applicable visibility minimums, whichever is the greater, for the instrument approach procedure to be used at the destination airport. Answer (A) is incorrect. The visibility requirement is for only 3 SM, not 5 SM, or 2 SM, not 3 SM, more than the lowest landing visibility minimums. Answer (C) is incorrect. The visibility is measured in statute miles (SM), not nautical miles (NM).

questions.subsection: 135.225 IFR: Takeoff, Approach, and Landing Minimums
questions.question_text: An instrument approach procedure to an airport may not be initiated unless the latest weather report issued by an authorized weather reporting facility indicates that weather conditions
questions.choice_a: are at or above the circling minimums for the runway the pilot intends to use.
questions.choice_b: are at or above the authorized IFR landing minimums for that procedure.
questions.choice_c: exceed the straight-in minimums for all nonprecision approaches.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: No pilot may begin an instrument approach procedure to an airport unless the latest weather report issued by the weather reporting facility at that airport indicates that weather conditions are at or above the authorized IFR landing minimums for that procedure. Answer (A) is incorrect. The approach may be initiated only if weather conditions are above the reported minimums for the intended approach. Answer (C) is incorrect. The approach may be initiated only if weather conditions are above the reported minimums for the intended approach.

questions.subsection: 135.225 IFR: Takeoff, Approach, and Landing Minimums
questions.question_text: After passing the final approach fix ona VOR approach, a weather report is received indicating the visibility is below prescribed minimums. In this situation, the pilot
questions.choice_a: may continue the approach and land, if at the MDA, the actual weather conditions are at least equal to the minimums prescribed for the procedure.
questions.choice_b: may continue the approach and land regardless of the visibility observed at the MDA, if prior to beginning the approach, the visibility was reported at or above minimums.
questions.choice_c: should level off and continue to fly the approach to the MAP, and execute the missed approach.
questions.explanation: If a pilot has begun the final approach segment (e.g., passing the final approach fix on a VOR approach) and a weather report indicates the visibility is below the prescribed minimums, the pilot may continue the approach and land if, at the MDA, the pilot finds that the actual weather conditions are at least equal to the minimums prescribed for the procedure. Answer (B) is incorrect. If the weather is below published minimums at the MDA, the pilot must execute a missed approach. Answer (C) is incorrect. If the weather conditions at the MDA are at least equal to the published minimums, the pilot should continue the approach and land, not level off and continue to fly to the MAP and then execute the missed approach.

questions.subsection: 135.225 IFR: Takeoff, Approach, and Landing Minimums
questions.question_text: A pilot may not take off under IFR at a foreign airport unless the visibility is
questions.choice_a: 1/2 mile or more above landing minimums.
questions.choice_b: 1 mile or more and the ceiling is 500 feet or more.
questions.choice_c: atleast 1 mile.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Each pilot making an IFR takeoff or approach and landing at a foreign or military airport shall comply with applicable instrument approach procedures and weather minimums. In addition, no pilot may take off under IFR at that airport when the visibility is less than 1 statute mile. Answer (A) is incorrect. The 1/2-SM visibility limit is for aircraft landing at a foreign airport, not the required takeoff visibility minimum, which is 1 statute mile. Answer (B) is incorrect. The requirement for taking off at a foreign airport is 1 SM visibility only. No ceiling limitations are stated.

questions.subsection: 135.225 IFR: Takeoff, Approach, and Landing Minimums
questions.question_text: At a military airport, a pilot may not take off under IFR unless the reported weather conditions indicate that the
questions.choice_b: ceiling is at least 500 feet and the visibility is 1 mile or more.
questions.choice_c: airport has landing minimums.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Each pilot making an IFR takeoff or approach and landing at a military or foreign airport shall comply with applicable instrument approach procedures and weather minimums. In addition, no pilot may take off under IFR at that airport when the visibility is less than 1 statute mile. Answer (B) is incorrect. The requirement for taking off at a military airport is 1 SM visibility only. No ceiling limitations are stated. Answer (C) is incorrect. There is no requirement that the military airport have landing minimums for an IFR takeoff, unless they are required at a specific airport.

questions.subsection: 135.225 IFR: Takeoff, Approach, and Landing Minimums
questions.question_text: Which is one required condition for a pilot to take off under IFR with less-than-standard takeoff minimums at an airport where a straight-in instrument approach procedure is authorized and there is an approved weather reporting source?
questions.choice_a: The pilot must have at least 100 hours as pilot in command in the type airplane to be flown.
questions.choice_b: The certificate holder has been approved for such operation and the visibility at the time of takeoff must be at least RVR 16.
questions.choice_c: Wind direction and velocity must be such that a straight-in approach can be made to the runway served by the procedure.
questions.explanation: One required condition for a pilot to take off under IFR with less-than-standard takeoff minimums at an airport where a straight-in instrument approach procedure is authorized and there is an approved weather reporting source is that the wind direction and velocity at the time of takeoff be such that a straight-in instrument approach can be made to the runway served by the instrument approach. Other conditions are that the ground and aircraft equipment for that approach be working and that the certificate holder be approved for such operations. ___ Answer (A) is incorrect. The pilot must have 100 hr. as PIC in the make and model, not type, of airplane to be flown to act as PIC of a commuter air carrier that uses an autopilot in place of a second-in-command, not to take off under IFR with less- than-standard minimums. Answer (B) is incorrect. The only requirement for visibility is that it be equal to or better than the lowest straight-in minimum, not that it be at least RVR 16.

questions.question_text: Which is an operational requirement concerning ice, snow, or frost on structural surfaces? A takeoff may be with ice, snow, or frost adhering to the wings or stabilizing or control surfaces, but polished smooth, if the anti-icing and deicing equipment is operating. If snow, ice, or frost is adhering to the airplane’s lift or control surfaces, but polished smooth, a takeoff may be made. A takeoff may not be made if ice or snow is adhering to the wings or stabilizing or control surfaces.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: No pilot may take off an aircraft that has frost, ice, or snow adhering to any propeller, windshield, wing, or stabilizing or control surface; to a power plant installation; or to an airspeed, altimeter, rate-of-climb, or flight attitude instrument system. Answer (A) is incorrect. A takeoff may be made with frost, not ice or snow, adhering to the wings or stabilizing or control surfaces, if the frost has been polished to make it smooth. There is no requirement that the anti-icing and deicing equipment be operational. Answer (B) is incorrect. A takeoff may be made with frost, not snow or ice, adhering to the wings or stabilizing or control surfaces, if the frost has been polished smooth.

questions.question_text: A pretakeoff contamination check for snow, ice, or frost is required by FAR Part 135. This check is required to
questions.choice_a: be made within 2 minutes of starting the takeoff roll.
questions.choice_b: be completed within 5 minutes prior to beginning the takeoff.
questions.choice_c: see that the aircraft is clean. Therefore, a safe takeoff can be made during the next 5 minutes. 4.5 Flight Crewmember Requirements
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: A pretakeoff contamination check, which has been established by the certificate holder and approved by the FAA for the specific airplane type, is required to be completed within 5 min. prior to beginning the takeoff. A pretakeoff contamination check is a check to make sure the wings and control surfaces are free of frost, ice, or snow. Answer (A) is incorrect. The pretakeoff contamination check must be completed within 5 min., not made within 2 min., prior to beginning the takeoff. Answer (C) is incorrect. The pretakeoff contamination check is a check of the wings and control surfaces, not of the entire aircraft, to see if they are clean of frost, ice, or snow.

questions.question_text: What are the minimum certificate and rating requirements for the pilot in command of a multiengine airplane being operated by a commuter air carrier?
questions.choice_a: Airline transport pilot; airplane category; multiengine class.
questions.choice_b: Commercial pilot; airplane category; multiengine class; instrument rating; airplane type rating, if required.
questions.choice_c: Airline transport pilot; airplane category; multiengine class; airplane type rating, if required.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: No certificate holder may use a person as pilot in command in passenger-carrying operations of a multi-engine airplane being operated by a commuter air carrier unless that person holds an airline transport pilot certificate with appropriate category and class ratings and, if required, an appropriate type rating for that airplane. Answer (A) is incorrect. In addition to the category and class ratings, an aircraft type rating may be required for that airplane. Answer (B) is incorrect. For the pilot in command of any multi- engine aircraft used as a commuter air carrier, an appropriate airline transport, not a commercial, pilot certificate is required.

questions.question_text: What are the minimum certificate and rating requirements for the pilot in command of a multiengine airplane in commuter air carrier service under IFR? Airline transport pilot of any category; multiengine class rating. Airline transport pilot; airplane category; multiengine class rating; airplane type rating, if required. Commercial pilot; airplane category; multiengine class and instrument rating.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: No certificate holder may use a person as pilot in command in passenger-carrying operations of a multi-engine airplane being operated by a commuter air carrier unless that person holds an airline transport pilot certificate with appropriate category and class ratings and, if required, an appropriate type rating for that airplane. Answer (A) is incorrect. A type rating may be required for the appropriate category of aircraft being used, and the ATP must be airplane category. Answer (C) is incorrect. An appropriate airline transport, not a commercial, pilot certificate is required for the pilot in command of a commuter air carrier.

questions.question_text: What are the minimum certificate and rating requirements for the pilot in command of a turbojet airplane with two engines being operated by a commuter air carrier (as defined in Part 298)?
questions.choice_a: Airline transport pilot; airplane category; multiengine class rating; airplane type rating, if required.
questions.choice_b: Airline transport pilot of any category; multiengine class rating; airplane type rating.
questions.choice_c: Commercial pilot; airplane category; multiengine class rating; instrument rating; airplane type rating.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: To act as pilot in command of a turbojet airplane with two engines being operated by a commuter air carrier, the PIC must have an ATP certificate with an airplane category, a multi-engine class rating, and an airplane type rating, if required. NOTE: A commuter operation is defined in FAR 119.3 as well as Part 298. 4 Answer (B) is incorrect. The airline transport pilot’s certificate must be appropriate for the specific category (i.e., airplane vs. rotorcraft) of aircraft. Answer (C) is incorrect. To act as pilot in command of a cargo, not passenger-carrying, turbojet, the pilot must hold a commercial pilot certificate.

questions.question_text: A person is acting as a pilot in command of a turbojet-powered airplane operated in passenger- carrying service by a commuter air carrier. If 10 takeoffs and landings have been accomplished in that make and basic model, which additional pilot- in-command experience meets the requirement for designation as pilot in command?
questions.choice_c: 10 hours, and five takeoffs and landings.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Twenty-five hr. of operating experience is required for a person to serve as pilot in command of a turbojet- powered aircraft used in passenger-carrying commuter air service. A reduction of up to 50% of the required time may be made by the substitution of one takeoff and landing for each required hour of operating experience. Since 10 takeoffs and landings have been accomplished, only 15 hr. is required (25 — 10 = 15). Answer (A) is incorrect. Ten hr. of operating experience is required in a single-engine, not turbojet-powered, aircraft in order to serve as PIC for a commuter air carrier. Answer (C) is incorrect. Only 50% of the required 25 hr. of operating experience may be substituted with takeoffs and landings. Thus, 13 hr., not 10 hr., and two takeoffs and landings, not five takeoffs and landings, would meet the requirement.

questions.question_text: A person is acting as pilot in command of a multiengine, turboprop-powered airplane operated in passenger-carrying service by a commuter air carrier. If eight takeoffs and landings are accomplished in that make and basic model, which additional pilot- in-command experience meets the requirement for designation as pilot in command?
questions.choice_a: 7 hours, and two takeoffs and landings.
questions.choice_b: 10 hours, and three takeoffs and landings.
questions.choice_c: 10 hours, and two takeoffs and one landing.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: A minimum of 20 hr. operating experience is required for a person to act as pilot in command of a turboprop- powered airplane used in passenger-carrying service by a commuter air carrier. A maximum of 10 takeoffs and landings may be used to reduce the required experience to 10 flight hr. Therefore, a minimum of 10 hr. operating experience and at least two takeoffs and landings (in addition to eight previous takeoffs and landings) are needed. Answer (A) is incorrect. A minimum of 10 hr., not 7 hr., and two takeoffs and landings are needed to meet the requirements for designation as PIC. Answer (C) is incorrect. A minimum of 10 hr. and at least two takeoffs and two landings, not one, are needed to meet the requirements for designation as PIC.

questions.question_text: A pilot's experience includes 8 hours in a particular make and basic model multiengine, turboprop airplane while acting as pilot-in-command. Which additional pilot-in-command experience meets the requirements for designation as pilot in command of that airplane when operated by a commuter air Carrier in passenger-carrying service?
questions.choice_b: Five takeoffs and landings, and 2 hours.
questions.choice_c: Ten takeoffs and landings, and 2 hours.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: The pilot of a turboprop airplane must have 20 hr. of operating experience in that particular make and model of aircraft in order to serve as PIC for a commuter air carrier. A maximum reduction of 50% of this time may be made by substituting one takeoff and landing for each flight hour. In this case, a minimum of 2 additional hr. operating experience to reach 10 hr. (50% of the 20 required hr.) and 10 takeoffs and landings are required. Answer (A) is incorrect. Only 50% of the required 20 hr. may be substituted by takeoffs and landings. Thus, 10 takeoffs and landings, not 12, and 2 hr. are needed to meet the requirements for designation as PIC. Answer (B) is incorrect. Fifteen hr. is the experience required in a multi-engine, reciprocating airplane, not a multi-engine, turboprop airplane, which requires 20 hr.

questions.question_text: A person is acting as pilot in command of a multiengine, reciprocating engine-powered airplane operated in passenger-carrying service by a commuter air carrier. If five takeoffs and landings have been accomplished in that make and basic model, which additional pilot-in-command experience meets the requirement for designation as the pilot in command?
questions.choice_a: Two takeoffs and landings, and 8 hours.
questions.choice_b: Five takeoffs and landings, and 5 hours.
questions.choice_c: Three takeoffs and landings, and 7 hours.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Fifteen hr. of operating experience is required for a person to serve as pilot in command of a reciprocating multiengine-powered airplane used in passenger-carrying service by a commuter air carrier. Up to 50% of these required hours may be reduced by one takeoff and landing for each required hour of operating experience. A maximum of seven takeoffs and landings may be used to reduce the required experience to 8 flight hr. Thus, two takeoffs and landings (in addition to five previous takeoffs and landings) and 8 flight hr. are needed. Answer (B) is incorrect. An additional five takeoffs and landings would exceed the 50% of 15 hr. reduction allowed. A maximum of 7 hr. of operating experience may be substituted. Answer (C) is incorrect. An additional three takeoffs and landings would exceed the 50% of 15 hr. reduction allowed. A maximum of 7 hr. of operating experience may be substituted.

questions.question_text: A person is acting as pilot in command of a single-engine airplane operated in passenger-carrying service by a commuter air carrier. If six takeoffs and landings have been accomplished in that make and basic model, which additional pilot-in-command experience meets the requirement for designation as pilot in command?
questions.choice_c: 6 hours.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Since 10 hr. of experience is required for a person to act as pilot in command of a single-engine airplane used in passenger-carrying service by a commuter air carrier, a maximum of five takeoffs and landings may be substituted for 5 hr. operating experience. Thus, 5 hr. (10 — 5) is required to meet the PIC requirement. Answer (A) is incorrect. Only five of the six takeoffs and landings can be substituted; thus 5 hr., not 4 hr., is needed to meet the PIC requirement. Answer (C) is incorrect. Five of the six takeoffs and landings can be substituted; thus 5 hr., not 6 hr., is needed to meet the PIC requirement.

questions.question_text: To satisfy the instrument approach recency experience requirement, a second-in-command must have made at least
questions.choice_a: six approaches within the past 6 months; three must have been in the category aircraft to be flown.
questions.choice_b: six approaches within the past 6 months in an airplane, approved instrument ground trainer, or simulator representative of the aircraft to be flown.
questions.choice_c: six approaches and 6 hours of instrument time within the past 6 months, in an airplane, helicopter, approved instrument ground trainer, or simulator.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: To satisfy the instrument approach recency of experience requirement, a second-in-command must have made six approaches within the past 6 months in the same category of aircraft, or in a flight simulator or flight training device that is representative of that aircraft category, for which instrument privileges are sought. There are specific exceptions to this rule, but neither of the two alternative answer choices presented here reflect those exceptions accurately. Answer (A) is incorrect. All six of the approaches must be performed in an aircraft of the category to be flown or ina flight simulator or flight training device that is representative of the category to be flown. Answer (C) is incorrect. There is no requirement for a minimum number of hours of instrument time as long as six approaches in the same aircraft category, or an appropriate flight simulator or flight training device, have been made.

questions.question_text: No person may serve, as second-in-command of an aircraft (under part 135), unless they hold a commercial pilot certificate with the appropriate category, class rating and an instrument rating. For flight under IFR, that person must have accomplished within the last 6 months, the recent instrument requirements of
questions.choice_a: using the navigation systems for interception and tracking of courses, 6 instrument low approaches and holding.
questions.choice_b: using the navigation systems to intercept and track 3 inbound/3 outbound courses, 6 holding patterns and 6 instrument approaches.
questions.choice_c: holding procedures, using the navigation systems for intercepting and tracking courses, and 6 instrument approaches.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: FAR 135.245 indicates that the second-in- command of an aircraft operating under Part 135 must have a commercial pilot certificate with the appropriate category, class, and instrument ratings. For flight under IFR, that person must meet the recent instrument requirements outlined in Part 61. To act as pilot in command or second-in-command under IFR, Part 61 requires that within the preceding 6 months, a pilot must have performed holding procedures, intercepting and tracking courses, and 6 instrument approaches under actual or simulated instrument conditions, or have completed an instrument proficiency check. Answer (A) is incorrect. The instrument approaches do not need to be low approaches; only instrument approaches are required. Answer (B) is incorrect. No specific number of inbound and outbound courses need to be tracked, and no specific number of holding patterns need to be entered.

questions.question_text: For operations during the period beginning 1 hour after sunset and ending 1 hour before sunrise (as published in the Air Almanac), no certificate holder may use any person, nor may any person serve, as pilot in command of an aircraft carrying passengers unless that person has made three takeoffs and three landings within the preceding 90 days
questions.choice_a: as the sole manipulator of the flight controls in an aircraft of the same category and class and, if a type rating is required, of the same type in which that person is to serve.
questions.choice_b: as pilot in command of an aircraft of the same category and class and, if a type rating is required, of the same type in which that person is to serve.
questions.choice_c: as the sole manipulator of the flight controls in an aircraft of the same type in which that person is to serve.
questions.explanation: res DISCUSSION: For operations during the period beginning 1 hr. after sunset and ending 1 hr. before sunrise (as published in the Air Almanac), no certificate holder may use any person nor may any person serve as pilot in command of an aircraft carrying passengers unless that person has made three takeoffs and three landings during that period as the sole manipulator of the _ flight controls in an aircraft of the same category and class and, if a type rating is required, of the same type in which that person Is to serve, within the preceding 90 days. Answer (B) is incorrect. The pilot must make the three takeoffs and three landings within the preceding 90 days as sole manipulator of the controls, not just as PIC. Answer (C) is incorrect. The three takeoffs and three landings must be in the same category and class of aircraft in which that person is to serve. The same type of aircraft means that, if a type rating is required, then the requirement must also be in the same type of aircraft.

questions.question_text: What is one of the requirements that must be met by a pilot in command to reestablish recency of experience? DISCUSSION: No person may serve as pilot in command of an aircraft carrying passengers unless, within the preceding 90 days, that person has made three takeoffs and landings as
questions.choice_a: Atleast one full stop landing must be made the sole manipulator of the flight controls in an aircraft of the from a circling approach. same category and class and, if a type rating is required, of the same type in which that person is to serve.
questions.choice_b: Three takeoffs and landings must be made Answer (A) is incorrect. One of the requirements that must as the sole manipulator of the controls, in be met to reestablish recency of experience is three takeoffs the type, if a type rating is required, if not in and landings as sole manipulator of the controls, not a full stop the same category and class aircraft that the landing from a circling approach. Answer (C) is incorrect. One person is to serve. of the requirements that must be met to reestablish recency of in experience is three takeoffs and landings as sole manipulator
questions.choice_c: Atleast one nonprecision approach must be of the controls, not a nonprecision approach to the lowest made to the lowest minimums authorized for the certificate holder.
questions.explanation: minimums authorized for the certificate holder.

questions.question_text: An employee who performs safety-sensitive functions, for a certificate holder, who has actual knowledge of an accident involving an aircraft for which he or she performed a safety-sensitive function at or near the time of the accident shall not use DISCUSSION: An employee who performs safety-sensitive functions for a certificate holder and who has actual knowledge of an accident involving an aircraft for which (s)he performed a Safety-sensitive function at or near the time of the accident alcohol shall not use alcohol for 8 hr. following the accident, unless (s)he has been given a post-accident test under Appendix J of FAR Aunt ahourecanenthe accident Part 121 or the employer has determined that the employee’s performance could not have contributed to the accident.
questions.choice_c: until given a release by the NTSB or FAA.
questions.explanation: Answer (A) is incorrect. An employee who has actual knowledge of an accident for which (s)he performed a safety- sensitive function at or near the time of the accident must not use alcohol within 8 hr., not 4 hr., of the accident. Answer (C) is incorrect. An employee can use alcohol before 8 hr. if (s)he has been given a post-accident test under Appendix J of FAR Part 121 or the employer, not the NTSB or FAA, has determined that the employee’s performance could not have contributed to the accident.

questions.subsection: 4.6 Crewmember Flight Time and Duty Period Limitations and Rest Requirements
questions.question_text: Pilot flight time limitations under FAR Part 135 are based
questions.choice_a: on the flight time accumulated in any commercial flying.
questions.choice_b: solely on flight time accumulated in air taxi operations.
questions.choice_c: solely on flight time accumulated during commercial flying, in the last 30 day and/or 12-month period.
questions.explanation: Flight time limitations under FAR Part 135 are based on the flight time accumulated during any commercial flying time. Answer (B) is incorrect. Any and all commercial flying time, such as flight instruction, is included and counted under FAR Part 135 flight time limitations. Answer (C) is incorrect. Only commercial flight time, not all, is accumulated based on a 24-hr., 7-day, 30-day, and calendar-year period, not only a 30-day and 12-month period.

questions.subsection: 4.6 Crewmember Flight Time and Duty Period Limitations and Rest Requirements
questions.question_text: What is the maximum number of hours that a pilot may fly in 7 consecutive days as a pilot in commercial flying and as a pilot for a commuter air carrier?
questions.choice_c: 35 hours.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: No certificate holder may schedule any flight crewmember and no flight crewmember may accept an assignment for flight time in scheduled operations or in other commercial flying if that crewmember’s total flight time in all commercial flying will exceed 34 hr. in any 7 consecutive days. Answer (A) is incorrect. The maximum number of hours that a pilot may fly in 7 consecutive days as a pilot in commercial flying and as a pilot for a commuter air carrier is 34 hr., not 32 hours. Answer (C) is incorrect. The maximum number of hours that a pilot may fly in 7 consecutive days as a pilot in commercial flying and as a pilot for a commuter air carrier is 34 hr., not 35 hours.

questions.subsection: 4.6 Crewmember Flight Time and Duty Period Limitations and Rest Requirements
questions.question_text: What is the maximum number of hours that a commuter air carrier may schedule a flight crewmember to fly in scheduled operations and other commercial flying in any calendar month?
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: No certificate holder may schedule any flight crewmember and no flight crewmember may accept an assignment for flight time in scheduled operations or in other commercial flying if that crewmember’s total flight time in all commercial flying will exceed 120 hr. in any calendar month. Answer (A) is incorrect. The maximum number of hours that a commuter air carrier may schedule a flight crewmember to fly in scheduled operations and other commercial flying is 120 hr., not 100 hr., in any calendar month. Answer (B) is incorrect. The maximum number of hours that a commuter air carrier may schedule a flight crewmember to fly in scheduled operations and other commercial flying is 120 hr., not 110 hr., in any calendar month.

questions.subsection: 4.6 Crewmember Flight Time and Duty Period Limitations and Rest Requirements
questions.question_text: A person may act as pilot in command of both type A and type B aircraft under IFR if an instrument proficiency check has been passed in
questions.choice_a: either type A or B since the beginning of the 12th month before time to serve.
questions.choice_b: type A since the beginning of the 12th month, and in type B since the beginning of the 6th month before time to serve.
questions.choice_c: type A since the beginning of the 12th month, and in type B since the beginning of the 24th month before time to serve.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: For instrument currency purposes, a pilot must take in rotation the instrument proficiency check required in each type of aircraft to which that pilot is assigned, but not more than one flight check during each 6 months. Answer (A) is incorrect. An instrument proficiency check is done every 6 months, not 12 months, and the pilot would rotate the type aircraft for each 6-month check. Answer (C) is incorrect. The required instrument proficiency check ride is valid only since the beginning of the 6th calendar month. A rotation is done every 6, not 12, months.

questions.subsection: 4.6 Crewmember Flight Time and Duty Period Limitations and Rest Requirements
questions.question_text: A person may not serve as pilot in command in an IFR operation unless that person has passed an
questions.choice_a: aircraft competency, an instrument proficiency, and auto-pilot check within the previous 6 calendar months prior to the date to serve. instrument proficiency check in the airplane in which to serve, or in an approved aircraft simulator, within the previous 12 calendar months. instrument proficiency check under actual or simulated IFR conditions, since the beginning of the 6th calendar month prior to the date to serve.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: No certificate holder may use a pilot nor may any person serve as a pilot in command of an aircraft under IFR unless, since the beginning of the 6th calendar month before that service, that pilot has passed an instrument proficiency check. Answer (A) is incorrect. The aircraft competency and — autopilot checks must have been performed within the previous 12, not 6, calendar months. Answer (B) is incorrect. The instrument proficiency check is required within the previous 6, not 12, calendar months.

questions.subsection: 4.6 Crewmember Flight Time and Duty Period Limitations and Rest Requirements
questions.question_text: A person is assigned as pilot in command to fly both single-engine and multiengine airplanes and has passed the initial instrument proficiency check in a multiengine airplane. Which requirement applies regarding each succeeding instrument check? The instrument check must be taken every 6 calendar months in both a single-engine and a multiengine airplane. The instrument check must be taken alternately in single-engine and multiengine airplanes every 6 calendar months. The instrument check may be taken in either a single-engine or multiengine airplane if taken at intervals of 6 calendar months.
questions.explanation: If the pilot in command is assigned to pilot both single-engine and multi-engine aircraft, that pilot must initially take the instrument proficiency check in a multi-engine aircraft and each succeeding check alternately in single-engine and multi-engine aircraft, but not more than one flight check during each 6-month period. Answer (A) is incorrect. After a pilot initially takes the required instrument proficiency check in a multi-engine airplane, subsequent instrument checks at 6-month intervals must be alternated between the single-engine airplane and the multi- engine airplane. Answer (C) is incorrect. Each succeeding instrument check must alternate between the single-engine and multi-engine airplane, not be in either one.

questions.subsection: 4.6 Crewmember Flight Time and Duty Period Limitations and Rest Requirements
questions.question_text: A pilot in command is authorized to use an autopilot system in place of a second-in-command. During the instrument proficiency check, that person is required to demonstrate (without a second-in- command) the ability to comply with complex ATC instructions with, but not without, the autopilot. properly conduct air-ground communications with, but not without, the autopilot. properly conduct instrument operations competently both with, and without, the autopilot.
questions.explanation: If the pilot in command is authorized to use an auto pilot system in place of a second-in-comman4d, that pilot must show during the required instrument proficiency check that (s)he is able (without a second-in-command), both with and without using the autopilot, to conduct instrument operations competently. Answer (A) is incorrect. The pilot must be able both to conduct air-ground communications and to comply with complex air traffic control instructions without, as well as with, the use of the autopilot. Answer (B) is incorrect. The pilot must be able both to conduct air-ground communications and to comply with complex air traffic control instructions without, as well as with, the use of the autopilot.

questions.subsection: 4.6 Crewmember Flight Time and Duty Period Limitations and Rest Requirements
questions.question_text: A pilot in command who is authorized to use an autopilot system, in place of a second-in-command, may take the autopilot check concurrently with the instrument proficiency check, but at 12-month intervals. in any aircraft appropriately equipped, providing the check is taken at 6-month intervals. concurrently with the competency check, providing the check is taken at 12-month intervals.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Each pilot taking the autopilot check must show that while using the autopilot, the airplane can be operated as proficiently as it would be if a second-in-command were present to handle air-ground communications and air traffic control instructions. The autopilot check need be demonstrated only once every 12 calendar months during the required instrument proficiency check. Answer (B) is incorrect. The instrument proficiency (and related autopilot) check must be taken in the type aircraft flown in FAR Part 135 operations, and it must be taken every 12, not 6, months. Answer (C) is incorrect. The autopilot check must be taken in conjunction with the instrument proficiency, not the competency, check every 12 months.

questions.subsection: 135.299 Pilot in Command: Line Checks: Routes and Airports
questions.question_text: To serve as pilot in command in an IFR operation, a person must have passed a line check
questions.choice_a: consisting of a flight over the route to be flown, with at least three instrument approaches at representative airports, within the past 12 calendar months, in one type of aircraft which that pilot is to fly. within the past 12 months, which included a portion of a civil airway and one instrument approach at one representative airport, in one of the types of aircraft which that pilot is to fly. since the beginning of the 12th month before that service, which included at least one flight over a Civil airway, or approved off-airway route, or any portion of either, in one type of aircraft which that pilot is to fly.
