questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: No certificate holder may use a pilot nor may any person serve as a pilot in command of a flight unless, since the beginning of the 12th calendar month before that service, that pilot has passed a line check in one of the types of aircraft that (s)he is to fly. The flight check shall consist of at least one flight over one route segment and include takeoffs and landings at one or more representative airports; if authorized for IFR flight, at least one flight shall be flown over a civil airway, an approved off-airway route, or a portion of either of them. Answer (A) is incorrect. There is no requirement for instrument approaches to be performed for the line check of routes and airports. Answer (B) is incorrect. There is no requirement for instrument approaches to be performed for the line check of routes and airports.

questions.subsection: 135.299 Pilot in Command: Line Checks: Routes and Airports
questions.question_text: What are the minimum requirements for the line check required of each pilot in command authorized for IFR air taxi operations? The line check shall be given over
questions.choice_a: one route segment in each type of airplane the pilot is to fly and includes takeoffs and landings at one or more representative airports. a civil airway or an approved off-airway route, or a portion of either of them, in one type of airplane the pilot is to fly and includes takeoffs and landings at one or more representative airports. a civil airway or an approved off-airway route in each make and model airplane the pilot is to fly and includes takeoffs and landings at one or more representative airports.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: No certificate holder may use a pilot nor may any person serve as a pilot in command of a flight unless, since the beginning of the 12th calendar month before that service, that pilot has passed a line check in one of the types of aircraft that (s)he is to fly. The flight check shall consist of at least one flight over one route segment and include takeoffs and landings at one or more representative airports; if authorized for IFR flight, at least one flight shall be flown over a civil airway, an approved off-airway route, or a portion of either of them. Answer (A) is incorrect. The line check is conducted in one, not each, type of aircraft that the pilot will fly, and for IFR operations the flight must be flown over a civil airway, an approved off-airway route, or a portion of either of them, not necessarily a route segment. Answer (C) is incorrect. The line check is conducted in one type of aircraft, not each make and model airplane, that the pilot is to fly.

questions.subsection: 135.299 Pilot in Command: Line Checks: Routes and Airports
questions.question_text: No certificate holder may use a person as pilot in command unless that person has passed a line check since the beginning of the 12th month before serving as pilot in command. since the beginning of the 6th month before serving as pilot in command. within the past 6 months.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: No certificate holder may use a pilot nor may any person serve as a pilot in command of a flight unless, since the beginning of the 12th calendar month before that service, that pilot has passed a line check in one of the types of aircraft that (s)he is to fly. Answer (B) is incorrect. An instrument proficiency check, not a line check, is required since the beginning of the 6th month before serving as PIC. Answer (C) is incorrect. A line check must have been passed since the beginning of the 12th month before serving as PIC, not within the past 6 months.

questions.question_text: The training required for flight crewmembers who have not qualified and served in the same capacity on an aircraft is
questions.choice_c: initial training.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Initial training is required for flight crewmembers who have not qualified and served in the same capacity on an aircraft. Answer (A) is incorrect. Upgrade training is required for a crewmember who has qualified and served as second-in- command on a particular aircraft type before (s)he serves as pilot in command on that aircraft. Answer (B) is incorrect. Transition training is required for a crewmember who has qualified and served in the same capacity on another aircraft.

questions.question_text: A crewmember who has served as second-in- command on a particular aircraft type (e.g., BE-1900), may serve as pilot in command upon completing which training program?
questions.choice_c: Initial training.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: A crewmember who has served as second- in-command on a particular aircraft type (e.g., BE-1900) may serve as pilot in command upon completing upgrade training. Answer (B) is incorrect. Transition training is required for crewmembers who have qualified and served in the same capacity on another aircraft. Answer (C) is incorrect. Initial training is required for crewmembers who have not qualified and served in the same capacity on an aircraft.

questions.question_text: The training required for crewmembers who have been qualified and served in the same capacity on another aircraft is
questions.choice_c: upgrade training.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: The training required for crewmembers who have been qualified and have served in the same capacity on another aircraft is transition training. Answer (A) is incorrect. Difference training is required for crewmembers who have qualified and served on a particular type aircraft prior to becoming qualified in the same capacity on a particular variation of that aircraft. Answer (C) is incorrect. Upgrade training is required for a crewmember who has qualified and served as second-in-command on a particular aircraft type before (s)he serves as pilot in command on that aircraft.

questions.question_text: The certificate holder must give instruction on such subjects as respiration, hypoxia, gas expansion, and decompression to crewmembers who serve in operations above
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: The certificate holder must give instruction on such subjects as respiration, hypoxia, gas expansion, and Se Gh aa to crewmembers who serve in operations above Answer (A) is incorrect. The certificate holder must give instruction on such subjects as respiration, hypoxia, gas expansion, and decompression to crewmembers who serve in operations above FL 250, not FL 180. Answer (B) is incorrect. The certificate holder must give instruction on such subjects as respiration, hypoxia, gas expansion, and decompression EAM els who serve in operations above FL 250, not

questions.question_text: The air carrier must give instruction on such subjects as gas bubble formation, hypoxia, decompression, and length of consciousness without supplemental oxygen at altitude to crewmembers serving on aircraft operated above
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Crewmembers who serve in operations above FL 250 must receive instruction on subjects such as gas bubbie formation, hypoxia, decompression, and length of consciousness without supplemental oxygen at altitude. Answer (B) is incorrect. Instruction on subjects such as gas bubble formation, hypoxia, and decompression must be given to crewmembers serving on aircraft operating above FL 250, not FL 200. Answer (C) is incorrect. Instruction on subjects such as gas bubble formation, hypoxia, and decompression must be given to crewmembers serving on aircraft operati FL 250, not FL 180. g perating above

questions.explanation: landing distance that may be used by a reciprocating- DISCUSSION: FAR 135.397 prescribes that FARs 135.375 engine-powered, small transport category airplane to and 135.377 are applicable to reciprocating-powered small land on Rwy 1 (dry) at the destination airport? airplanes. FAR 135.375 requires a reciprocating airplane to be able to land in 60% of the effective length of the runway at the destination airport.

questions.subsection: 135.379 Large Transport Category Airplanes: Turbine Engine Powered: Takeoff Limitations
questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 1 above.) What is the maximum planning landing distance that may be used by a reciprocating-engine-powered, small transport category airplane to land on Rwy 6 (dry) at the destination airport?
questions.choice_c: 6,370 feet.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: FAR 135.397 prescribes that FARs 135.375 and 135.377 are applicable to reciprocating-powered small airplanes. FAR 135.375 requires a reciprocating airplane to be able to land in 60% of the effective length of the runway at the destination airport. The effective length of Rwy 6 on Fig. 1 is 9,100 ft. (10,350 — 1,250).The effective runway length begins at the point where the obstruction clearance plane intersects the centerline of the runway. Thus, the maximum landing distance is 5,460 ft. (9,100 x 60%). Answer (B) is incorrect. This is 60% of the actual, not the effective, runway length. Answer (C) is incorrect. This is 70%, not 60%, of the effective runway length.

questions.subsection: 135.379 Large Transport Category Airplanes: Turbine Engine Powered: Takeoff Limitations
questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 1 above.) What is the maximum landing distance that may be used by a reciprocating- engine-powered, small transport category airplane to land on Rwy 24 (dry) at the destination airport?
questions.choice_c: 6,405 feet.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: FAR 135.397 prescribes that FARs 135.375 and 135.377 are applicable to reciprocating-powered small airplanes. FAR 135.375 requires a reciprocating airplane to be able to land in 60% of the effective length of the runway at the airport of intended landing. The effective length of Rwy 24 on Fig.1 is 9,150 ft. (10,350 — 1,200). The effective runway length begins at the point where the obstruction clearance plane intersects the centerline of the runway. Thus, the maximum landing distance is 5,490 ft. (9,150 x 60%). Answer (B) is incorrect. This is 60% of the actual, not the effective, runway length. Answer (C) is incorrect. This is 70%, not 60%, of the effective runway length.

questions.subsection: 135.379 Large Transport Category Airplanes: Turbine Engine Powered: Takeoff Limitations
questions.question_text: For which of these aircraft may part of the “clearway” distance, for a particular runway, be considered in computing the takeoff distance?
questions.choice_b: Turbine-engine-powered transport airplanes, certificated after September 30, 1958.
questions.choice_c: U.S. certified transport airplane, certificated before August 26, 1957.
questions.explanation: For turbine-engine-powered large transport category airplanes certificated after Sept. 30, 1958, the takeoff distance may include a clearway distance, but the clearway distance included may not be greater than one-half of the takeoff run. __ Answer (A) is incorrect. Not all passenger-carrying transport aircraft may consider part of the clearway distance in computing the takeoff distance. Answer (C) is incorrect. Turbine-engine- powered large transport category airplanes certificated after Sept. 30, 1958, not any U.S.-certified transport airplane certificated before Aug. 26, 1957, can include part of the clearway distance when computing the takeoff distance.

questions.subsection: 135.385 Large Transport Category Airplanes: Turbine Engine Powered: Landing Limitations: Destination Airports
questions.question_text: Which takeoff computation must not exceed the length of the runway plus the length of the stopway for a turbine-engine-powered small transport category airplane?
questions.choice_c: Acceleration-climb distance.
questions.explanation: The acceleration-stop distance must not exceed the length of the runway plus the length of any stopway for a turbine-powered, small-transport-category airplane. Answer (A) is incorrect. The takeoff distance usually is shorter than the computed acceleration-stop distance. The latter must be no greater than the length of the runway plus the length of any stopway. Answer (C) is incorrect. The acceleration-stop, not acceleration-climb, distance must not exceed the length of the runway plus the length of any stopway.

questions.subsection: 135.385 Large Transport Category Airplanes: Turbine Engine Powered: Landing Limitations: Destination Airports
questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 1 on page 178.) What is the maximum landing distance that may be used by a turbine-engine-powered, small transport category airplane to land on Rwy 24 (dry) at the destination airport?
questions.choice_c: 6,210 feet.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: FAR 135.397 prescribes that FARs 135.385 and 135.387 are applicable to turbine-engine-powered small airplanes. FAR 135.385 requires a turbine-engine-powered airplane to be able to land in 60% of the effective length of the runway at the destination airport. The effective length of Rwy 24 on Fig. 1 is 9,150 ft. (10,350 — 1,200). The effective runway length begins at the point where the obstruction clearance plane intersects the centerline of the runway. Thus, the maximum landing distance is 5,490 ft. (9,150 x 60%). Answer (A) is incorrect. This is 60% of the effective runway length of Rwy 6, not Rwy 24. Answer (C) is incorrect. This is 60% of the actual, not the effective, runway length.

questions.subsection: 135.385 Large Transport Category Airplanes: Turbine Engine Powered: Landing Limitations: Destination Airports
questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 1 on page 178.) What is the maximum landing distance that may be used by a turbine-engine-powered, small transport category airplane to !and on Rwy 6 (wet) at the destination airport?
questions.choice_c: 5,460 feet.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: FAR 135.397 prescribes that FARs 135.385 and 135.387 are applicable to turbine-engine-powered small airplanes. FAR 135.385 requires a turbine-engine-powered airplane to be able to land in 60% of the effective length of a dry (or a wet) runway at the destination airport. The effective length of Rwy 6 on Fig. 1 is 9,100 ft. (10,350 — 1,250). The effective runway length begins at the point where the obstruction clearance plane intersects the centerline of the runway. Thus, the maximum landing distance is 5,460 ft. (9,100 x 60%). Answer (A) is incorrect. This is 100%, not 60%, of the effective runway length. Answer (B) is incorrect. This is 115% of 5,460 feet, which would be required for landing a turbojet airplane, not a turbine-engine-powered airplane, on Rwy 6 (wet) at the destination airport. Only turbojet airplanes are required to increase the available runway length for landing on a wet runway.

questions.subsection: 135.385 Large Transport Category Airplanes: Turbine Engine Powered: Landing Limitations: Destination Airports
questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 1 on page 178.) What is the maximum landing distance that may be used by a turbopropeller-powered, small transport category airplane to land on Rwy 24 (dry) at the destination airport?
questions.choice_c: 9,150 feet.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: FAR 135.397 prescribes that FARs 135.385 and 135.387 are applicable to turbopropeller-powered small airplanes. FAR 135.387 requires a turbopropeller-powered airplane to be able to land in 60% of the effective length of the runway at a destination airport. The effective length of Rwy 24 on Fig. 1 is 9,150 ft. (10,350 — 1,200). The effective runway length begins at the point where the obstruction clearance plane intersects the centerline of the runway. Thus, the maximum landing distance is 5,490 ft. (9,150 x 60%). Answer (A) is incorrect. This is 67%, not 60%, of the effective runway length. Answer (C) is incorrect. This is the full effective runway length, not the requirement for landing.

questions.subsection: 135.385 Large Transport Category Airplanes: Turbine Engine Powered: Landing Limitations: Destination Airports
questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 2 above.) What is the maximum landing distance that may be used by a turbopropeller-powered, small transport category airplane to land on Rwy 19 (dry) at the destination airport?
questions.choice_c: 5,160 feet.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: FAR 135.397 prescribes that FARs 135.385 and 135.387 are applicable to turbopropeller-powered small airplanes. FAR 135.385 requires a turbine-engine-powered airplane to be able to land in 60% of the effective length of the runway at the destination airport. The effective length of Rwy 19 on Fig. 2 is 8,600 ft. (9,700 — 1,100). The effective runway length begins at the point where the obstruction clearance plane intersects the centerline of the runway. Thus, the maximum landing distance is 5,160 ft. (8,600 x 60%). Answer (A) is incorrect. This is 70%, not 60%, of the effective runway length. Answer (B) is incorrect. This is 60% of the actual, not the effective, runway length.

questions.subsection: 135.385 Large Transport Category Airplanes: Turbine Engine Powered: Landing Limitations: Destination Airports
questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 2 above.) What is the maximum landing distance that may be used by a turbine- engine-powered, small transport category airplane to land on Rwy 1 (dry) at the destination airport?
questions.choice_c: 5,845 feet.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: FAR 135.397 prescribes that FARs 135.385 and 135.387 are applicable to turbine-engine-powered small airplanes. FAR 135.385 requires a turbine-engine-powered airplane to be able to land in 60% of the effective length of the runway at the destination airport. The effective length of Rwy 1 on Fig. 2 is 8,350 ft. (9,700 — 1,350). The effective runway length begins at the point where the obstruction clearance plane intersects the centerline of the Ay Thus, the maximum landing distance is 5,010 ft. (8,350 x Oo}. Answer (B) is incorrect. This is 60% of the actual, not the effective, runway length. Answer (C) is incorrect. This is 70%, not 60%, of the effective runway length.

questions.subsection: 135.385 Large Transport Category Airplanes: Turbine Engine Powered: Landing Limitations: Destination Airports
questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 2 above.) What is the maximum landing distance that may be used by a turbine- engine-powered, small transport category airplane to land on Rwy 19 (dry) at the destination airport?
questions.choice_c: 6,020 feet.
questions.explanation: FAR 135.397 prescribes that FARs 135.385 and 135.387 are applicable to turbine-engine-powered small airplanes. FAR 135.385 requires a turbine-engine-powered airplane to be able to land in 60% of the effective length of the runway at the destination airport. The effective length of Rwy 19 on Fig. 2 is 8,600 ft. (9,700 — 1,100). The effective runway length begins at the point where the obstruction clearance plane intersects the centerline of the aes Thus, the maximum landing distance is 5,160 ft. (8,600 x QO). Answer (B) is incorrect. This is 60% of the actual, not the effective, runway length. Answer (C) is incorrect. This is 70%, not 60%, of the effective runway length.

questions.subsection: 135.387 Large Transport Category Airplanes: Turbine Engine Powered: Landing Limitations: Alternate Airports
questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 2 on page 180.) May a small transport category, turbine-engine-powered airplane that has a computed landing distance of 5,500 feet use one or both of the runways depicted in the illustration at the destination airport?
questions.choice_a: Neither Rwy 1 nor Rwy 19 may be used if dry conditions exist.
questions.choice_b: Only Rwy 19 may be used provided dry conditions exist.
questions.choice_c: Rwy 1 or Rwy 19 may be used whether conditions are wet or dry.
questions.explanation: FAR 135.397 prescribes that FARs 135.385 and 135.387 are applicable to turbine-engine-powered small airplanes. FAR 135.385 requires a turbine-engine-powered airplane to be able to land in 60% of the effective length of the runway at the destination airport. The effective length of Rwy 1 on Fig. 2 is 8,350 ft. (9,700 — 1,350). For Rwy 19, the effective length is 8,600 ft. (9,700 — 1,100). The effective runway length begins at the point where the obstruction clearance plane intersects the centerline of the runway. Thus, the maximum landing distance is 5,010 ft. (8,350 x 60%) for Rwy 1 and 5,160 ft. (8,600 x 60%) for Rwy 19. Thus, neither Rwy 1 nor 19 may be used; i.e., the maximum landing distance for each is less than 5,500 feet. Answer (B) is incorrect. Rwy 19’s maximum landing distance is 5,160 feet. Answer (C) is incorrect. Neither Rwy 1 nor 19 may be used.

questions.subsection: 135.387 Large Transport Category Airplanes: Turbine Engine Powered: Landing Limitations: Alternate Airports
questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 2 on page 180.) May a small transport category, turboprop airplane that has a computed landing distance of 6,000 feet use either or both runways depicted in the illustration at the destination airport?
questions.choice_a: Only Rwy 19 may be used if dry conditions exist.
questions.choice_b: Neither Rwy 1 nor Rwy 19 may be used under any conditions.
questions.choice_c: Either Rwy 1 or Rwy 19 may be used whether conditions are wet or dry.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: FAR 135.397 prescribes that FARs 135.385 and 135.387 are applicable to turbine-engine-powered small airplanes. FAR 135.385 requires a turbine-engine-powered airplane to be able to land in 60% of the effective length of the runway at the destination airport. The effective length of Rwy 1 on Fig. 2 is 8,350 ft. (9,700 — 1,350). For Rwy 19, the effective length is 8,600 ft. (9,700 — 1,100). The effective runway length begins at the point where the obstruction clearance plane intersects the centerline of the runway. Thus, the maximum landing distance is 5,010 ft. (8,350 x 60%) for Rwy 1 and 5,160 ft. (8,600 x 60%) for Rwy 19. Thus, neither Rwy 1 nor 19 may be used; i.e., the maximum landing distance for each is less than 6,000 feet. Answer (A) is incorrect. Rwy 19’s maximum landing distance is 5,160 feet. Answer (C) is incorrect. Neither Rwy 1 nor 19 may be used.

questions.subsection: 135.387 Large Transport Category Airplanes: Turbine Engine Powered: Landing Limitations: Alternate Airports
questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 2 on page 180.) What con- dition meets FAR Part 135 operational requirements for a small, transport category, turboprop airplane to land at the destination airport that has the runway environment given in the illustration?
questions.choice_a: The airport may be listed as the destination airport if the landing distance does not exceed 5,160 feet for Rwy 19.
questions.choice_b: The airport may NOT be listed as the destination airport if the landing distance exceeds 5,100 feet for Rwy 19.
questions.choice_c: The airport may be listed as the destination airport if the landing distance does not exceed 5,350 feet for either runway, wet or dry conditions.
questions.explanation: FAR 135.397 prescribes that FARs 135.385 and 135.387 are applicable to turbine-engine-powered small airplanes. FAR 135.385 requires a turboprop airplane to be able to land in 60% of the effective length of the runway at the destination airport. The effective length of Rwy 1 on Fig. 2 is 8,350 ft. (9,700 — 1,350). For Rwy 19, the effective length is 8,600 ft. (9,700 — 1,100). The effective runway length begins at the point where the obstruction clearance plane intersects the centerline of the runway. Thus, the maximum landing distance is 5,010 ft. (8,350 x 60%) for Rwy 1 and 5,160 ft. (8,600 x 60%) for Rwy 19. Thus, Rwy 19 may be used within 5,160 ft. landing distance. Answer (B) is incorrect. The landing distance may be up to 5,160 ft. on Rwy 19. Answer (C) is incorrect. Rwy 1 landing distance cannot exceed 5,010 ft., and Rwy 19 landing distance cannot exceed 5,160 feet.

questions.subsection: 135.387 Large Transport Category Airplanes: Turbine Engine Powered: Landing Limitations: Alternate Airports
questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 2 on page 180.) What is the maximum landing distance that may be used for a non-transport category, turbopropeller-driven airplane to land on Rwy 1 (dry) at the alternate airport?
questions.choice_c: 6,020 feet.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: FAR 135.387 requires a turboprop airplane to be able to land in 70% of the effective length of the runway at alternate airports. The effective length of Rwy 1 on Fig. 2 is 8,350 ft. (9,700 — 1,350). The effective runway length begins at the point where the obstruction clearance plane intersects the centerline of the runway. Thus, the maximum landing distance is 5,845 ft. (8,350 x 70%). es (A) is incorrect. This is 60%, not 70%, of the effective runway length. Answer (C) is incorrect. This is 70% of the effective runway length of Rwy 19, not Rwy 1.

questions.subsection: 135.387 Large Transport Category Airplanes: Turbine Engine Powered: Landing Limitations: Alternate Airports
questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 1 above.) What is the maximum planning landing distance that may be used by a turbopropeller-powered, small transport category airplane to land on Rwy 24 (dry) at the alternate airport?
questions.choice_c: 6,405 feet.
questions.explanation: FAR 135.397 prescribes that FARs 135.385 and 135.387 are applicable to turbine-engine-powered small airplanes. FAR 135.387 requires a turbopropeller-powered airplane to be able to land in 70% of the effective length of the runway at an alternate airport. The effective length of Rwy 24 on Fig. 1 is 9,150 ft. (10,350 — 1,200). The effective runway length begins at the point where the obstruction clearance plane intersects the centerline of the runway. Thus, the maximum planning landing distance is 6,405 ft. (9,150 x 70%). Answer (A) is incorrect. This is 60%, not 70%, of the effective runway length. Answer (B) is incorrect. This is 60% of the actual, not the effective, runway length.

questions.subsection: 135.387 Large Transport Category Airplanes: Turbine Engine Powered: Landing Limitations: Alternate Airports
questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 1 above.) What is the maximum landing distance that may be used by a turbopropeller-powered, small transport category airplane to land on Rwy 6 (dry) at the alternate airport?
questions.choice_c: 6,370 feet.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: FAR 135.397 prescribes that FARs 135.385 and 135.387 are applicable to turbine-engine-powered small airplanes. FAR 135.387 requires a turbopropeller-powered airplane to be able to land in 70% of the effective length of the runway at an alternate airport. The effective length of Rwy 6 on Fig. 1 is 9,100 ft. (10,350 — 1,250). The effective runway length begins at the point where the obstruction clearance plane intersects the centerline of the runway. Thus, the maximum landing distance is 6,370 ft. (9,100 x 70%). Answer (A) is incorrect. This is 60%, not 70%, of the effective runway length. Answer (B) is incorrect. This is 60%, not 70%, of the actual, not the effective, runway length.

questions.question_text: If a certificate holder makes arrangements for another person to perform aircraft maintenance, that maintenance shall be performed in accordance with the
questions.choice_a: certificate holder’s manual and FAR Parts 43, 91, and 135.
questions.choice_b: provisions of a contract prepared by a certificate holder and approved by the supervising FAA district office.
questions.choice_c: provisions and standards outlined in the certificate holder’s manual.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: The certificate holder shall ensure that any maintenance, preventive maintenance, or alteration that is performed by another person is performed under the certificate holder’s manual and under FARs 43, 91, and 135. Answer (B) is incorrect. The FAA allows other persons to perform maintenance, preventive maintenance, or alterations only in accordance with the certificate holder’s manual requirements. Answer (C) is incorrect. While the maintenance must be performed in accordance with the certificate holder’s manual, it must also be performed in accordance with FAR Parts 43, 91, and 135.

questions.question_text: Who is responsible for submitting a Mechanical Reliability Report? Each certificate holder.
questions.choice_b: Director of maintenance at the facility that discovers the reportable condition.
questions.choice_c: Chief inspector at the facility where the condition is found. 4.11 Hazardous Materials Training Program
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Each certificate holder shall report the occurrence or detection of each failure, malfunction, or defect in an aircraft. Answer (B) is incorrect. Someone other than a maintenance facility may discover the discrepancy. An example would be an engine shutdown during flight due to foreign object ingestion or icing. Upon landing, the certificate holder shall submit a mechanical reliability report. Answer (C) is incorrect. Someone other than a maintenance facility may discover the discrepancy. An example would be an engine shutdown during flight due to foreign object ingestion or icing. Upon landing, the certificate holder shall submit a mechanical reliability report.

questions.question_text: A person whose duties include the handling or carriage of dangerous articles and/or magnetized materials must have satisfactorily completed an approved training program established by the certificate holder within the previous
questions.choice_c: 24 calendar months. END OF STUDY UNIT
questions.explanation: No certificate holder may use any person to perform, and no person may perform, any assigned duties and responsibilities for the handling or carriage of hazardous materials (e.g., dangerous articles and/or magnetized materials), unless within the preceding 24 calendar months that person has satisfactorily completed initial or recurrent training in an appropriate training program established by the certificate holder. Answer (A) is incorrect. Any person involved in the handling or carriage of dangerous articles or magnetized materials must have completed an approved training program within the previous 24 calendar months, not 6 calendar months. Answer (B) is incorrect. Any person involved in the handling or carriage of dangerous articles or magnetized materials must have completed an approved training program within the previous 24 calendar months, not 12 calendar months.

questions.question_text: Which of the following is considered a primary flight control?
questions.choice_c: Dorsal fin.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: The primary flight control surfaces allow the pilot to control an airplane’s movement around its lateral, longitudinal, and vertical axes. The primary flight controls are the elevator, ailerons, and rudder. Answer (A) is incorrect. Slats are high-lift devices, not a flight control device. Answer (C) is incorrect. A dorsal fin is not a flight control, but rather is used to provide directional stability.

questions.question_text: Which of the following are considered primary flight controls?
questions.choice_c: Outboard ailerons.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: The primary flight control surfaces allow the pilot to control an airplane’s movement around its lateral, longitudinal, and vertical axes. The primary flight controls are elevators, ailerons, and rudders. Answer (A) is incorrect. Tabs are secondary, not primary, flight controls. Answer (B) is incorrect. Flaps are secondary, not primary, flight controls.

questions.question_text: Which of the following is considered an auxiliary flight control?
questions.choice_c: Leading-edge flaps.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Leading-edge flaps, speed brakes, spoilers, trailing-edge flaps, and leading-edge slats are auxiliary (secondary) wing flight control surfaces. Answer (A) is incorrect. A ruddervator is a primary, not auxiliary, flight control surface that incorporates both a rudder and an elevator into one surface. This is done on “V”-tail-type airplanes. Answer (B) is incorrect. Upper rudders (found on the B-727) are standby rudders, which are used in the event of a hydraulic system failure. These are primary, not auxiliary, flight controls.

questions.question_text: When are inboard ailerons normally used? Low-speed flight only. High-speed flight only.
questions.choice_c: Low-speed and high-speed flight.
questions.explanation: ; DISCUSSION: Some large aircraft use two sets of ailerons (inboard and outboard). At high airspeeds, the aerodynamic loads from use of the outboard ailerons twist the wingtips. The outboard ailerons are locked in the neutral position during high- speed flight. Answer (A) is incorrect. The inboard ailerons are used during both high- and low-speed flight. Answer (B) is incorrect. The inboard ailerons are used during both high- and low-speed flight.

questions.question_text: When are outboard ailerons normally used? Low-speed flight only. High-speed flight only. Low-speed and high-speed flight.
questions.explanation: Some large aircraft use two sets of ailerons (inboard and outboard). At high airspeeds, the aerodynamic loads from use of the outboard ailerons twist the wingtips. The outboard ailerons are locked in the neutral position during high- speed flight. Answer (B) is incorrect. The outboard ailerons are locked during high-speed flight. Answer (C) is incorrect. The outboard ailerons are locked during high-speed flight.

questions.question_text: Why do some airplanes equipped with inboard/ outboard ailerons use the outboards for slow flight only?
questions.choice_a: Increased surface area provides greater controllability with flap extension.
questions.choice_b: Aerodynamic loads on the outboard ailerons tend to twist the wingtips at high speeds.
questions.choice_c: Locking out the outboard ailerons in high- speed flight provides variable flight control feel.
questions.explanation: Some large aircraft use two sets of ailerons (inboard and outboard). At high airspeeds, the aerodynamic loads from use of the outboard ailerons twist the wingtips. The outboard ailerons are locked in the neutral position during high- speed flight. Answer (A) is incorrect. While using the outboard ailerons during slow flight does increase roll stability, if they are used in high-speed flight, the aerodynamic loads would twist the wingtips. Answer (C) is incorrect. Locking out the outboard ailerons reduces the aerodynamic loads on the wings, preventing overstressing at high airspeeds.

questions.question_text: What is the purpose of a control tab? Move the flight controls in the event of manual reversion. Reduce control forces by deflecting in the proper direction to move a primary flight control. Prevent a control surface from moving to a full- deflection position due to aerodynamic forces.
questions.explanation: When hydraulic pressure is lost to the primary controls, the control tabs unlock from their actuating cylinders and allow control wheel inputs. This method of secondary control is known as manual reversion. Answer (B) is incorrect. Servo, not control, tabs reduce control forces by deflecting primary flight controls in the proper direction. Answer (C) is incorrect. Anti-servo, not control, tabs prevent a control surface from moving to a full-deflection position due to aerodynamic forces.

questions.question_text: What is the purpose of an elevator trim tab? Provide horizontal balance as airspeed is increased to allow hands-off flight. Adjust the speed tail load for different airspeeds in flight allowing neutral control forces. Modify the downward tail load for various airspeeds in flight eliminating flight-control pressures.
questions.explanation: _ DISCUSSION: The elevator trim tab acts on the elevators, which in turn act upon the entire airplane. This allows the pilot to adjust the angle of attack of the elevators, thus modifying the downward tail load for any airspeed in flight and eliminating the need to exert continuous flight-control pressures. Answer (A) is incorrect. The elevator trim tab permits hands-off flight at any airspeed, not only when the airspeed is increasing, and provides longitudinal, not horizontal, balance. Answer (B) is incorrect. The elevator trim tab adjusts the downward, not speed, tail load for various airspeeds in flight allowing neutral control forces.

questions.question_text: Which direction from the primary control surface does an elevator adjustable trim tab move when the control surface is moved?
questions.choice_c: Remains fixed for all positions.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Once adjusted, an elevator adjustable trim tab remains fixed for all positions relative to the primary control surface as the primary control surface is moved. Answer (A) is incorrect. Adjustable trim tabs are adjusted in the opposite direction of the primary control surface as a means of trimming the control surface. Once adjusted, their relationship remains constant with respect to the primary control surface. Answer (B) is incorrect. Once adjusted, trim tabs remain fixed to the primary control surface as the primary control surface is moved.

questions.question_text: What is the purpose of a servo tab?
questions.choice_a: Move the flight controls in the event of manual reversion.
questions.choice_b: Reduce control forces by deflecting in the proper direction to move a primary flight control.
questions.choice_c: Prevent a control surface from moving to a full- deflection position due to aerodynamic forces.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: The servo tab aids the pilot in moving a primary control surface and in holding it in the desired position. Answer (A) is incorrect. In the event of manual reversion on some transport category aircraft, the control, not servo, tabs would move the flight controls. Answer (C) is incorrect. To preclude full deflection of control surfaces is the purpose of the anti-servo, not servo, tab.

questions.question_text: Which direction from the primary control surface does a servo tab move?
questions.choice_c: Remains fixed for all positions.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Movement of the servo tab in one direction causes the primary control surface to move in the opposite direction. If a servo tab on an aileron is deflected upward, the aileron is deflected downward, increasing the angle of attack on that wing and resulting in greater lift on that wing. Answer (A) is incorrect. An anti-servo tab, as found on the trailing edge of stabilators, moves in the same direction as the stabilator to provide a feel to the pilot control pressures. Answer (C) is incorrect. Servo tabs move in response to the pilot's control movements.

questions.question_text: What is the purpose of an anti-servo tab?
questions.choice_a: Move the flight controls in the event of manual reversion.
questions.choice_b: Reduce control forces by deflecting in the proper direction to move a primary flight control.
questions.choice_c: Prevent a control surface from moving to a full- deflection position due to aerodynamic forces.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Since the anti-servo tab moves in the same direction as the control surface, it produces a stabilizing force that increases control force pressures. Answer (A) is incorrect. In the event of manual reversion, the control, not anti-servo, tabs would move the flight controls. Answer (B) is incorrect. A servo, not an anti-servo, tab reduces control forces by deflecting in the proper direction to move a primary flight control.

questions.question_text: Which direction from the primary control surface does an anti-servo tab move?
questions.choice_c: Remains fixed for all positions.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: An anti-servo tab, as found on the trailing edge of a stabilator, moves in the same direction as the primary flight control surface. Answer (B) is incorrect. A servo, not an anti-servo, tab moves in the opposite direction from the primary control surface. Answer (C) is incorrect. Trim, not anti-servo, tabs remain fixed during control inputs.

questions.question_text: The primary purpose of high-lift devices is to increase the
questions.choice_c: drag and reduce airspeed.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: High-lift devices increase lift at low air- speeds, allowing lowered landing and takeoff speeds. Answer (A) is incorrect. Increasing the lift component is an objective of high-lift devices which increase the ratio of L/D. The primary purpose of high-lift devices is to increase lift at low speeds. Answer (C) is incorrect. Increasing the drag to reduce airspeed is the function of spoilers, not high-lift devices.

questions.question_text: Which is a purpose of leading-edge flaps?
questions.choice_a: Increase the camber of the wing. @ Reduce lift without increasing airspeed.
questions.choice_c: Direct airflow over the top of the wing at high angles of attack.
questions.explanation: The leading-edge flap is similar in operation to the plain flap. When actuated, the leading edge of the wing extends in a downward direction to increase the camber of the wing, providing greater lift at low flight speeds. _ Answer (B) is incorrect. Leading-edge flaps increase, not decrease, the lift at slow airspeeds. Answer (C) is incorrect. A slot, not a leading-edge flap, directs airflow over the top of the wing at high angles of attack.

questions.question_text: What is the primary function of the leading edge flaps in landing configuration during the flare before touchdown?
questions.choice_c: Increase profile drag.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: The primary function of the leading edge flaps in the landing configuration during the flare before touchdown is to prevent flow separation at low airspeeds, therefore allowing lower landing and takeoff speeds. Answer (B) is incorrect. To decrease the rate of sink, the coefficient of lift (C_) must increase. C, is increased by the use of leading edge lift devices, slats, flaps, and other devices with the correct power setting. Answer (C) is incorrect. Spoilers, not leading edge flaps, increase profile drag and are usually deployed after touchdown to reduce lift.

questions.question_text: What effect does the leading edge slot in the wing have on performance?
questions.choice_b: Changes the stalling angle of attack to a higher angle.
questions.choice_c: Decelerates the upper surface boundary layer air.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: The leading edge slot in a wing conducts the flow of high energy air into the boundary layer on the upper surface and delays airflow separation to some higher angle of attack. Thus, the slot simply delays stall to a higher angle of attack. Answer (A) is incorrect. At low angles of attack, there is little or no profile drag increase. At high angles of attack, the slot delays the stall characteristics of the wing. Answer (C) is incorrect. The leading edge slot actually increases airflow on the upper wing surface to allow higher angles of attack.

questions.question_text: Which is a purpose of leading-edge slats on high- performance wings?
questions.choice_a: Increase lift at relative slow speeds.
questions.choice_b: Improve aileron control during low angles of attack.
questions.choice_c: Direct air from the low-pressure area under the leading edge along the top of the wing.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Leading-edge slats direct air from the high- pressure area under the wing to the upper surface, resulting in improved stall characteristics at lower speeds. They also increase lift at relatively slow airspeeds. Answer (B) is incorrect. The leading edge slats are used during high, not low, angle of attack situations, such as takeoffs and landings. Answer (C) is incorrect. The area under the leading edge is high pressure, not low pressure.

questions.question_text: Which is a purpose of leading-edge slats on high- performance wings?
questions.choice_a: Decrease lift at relative slow speeds.
questions.choice_b: Improve aileron control during low angles of attack.
questions.choice_c: Direct air from the high-pressure area under the leading edge along the top of the wing.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Leading-edge slats direct air from the high- pressure area under the wing to the upper surface, resulting in improved stall characteristics at lower speeds. They also increase lift at relatively slow airspeeds. ___ Answer (A) is incorrect. The purpose of leading-edge-lift slats is to increase, not decrease, the lift at low speeds. Sometimes these are referred to as high-lift devices. Answer (B) is incorrect. The leading-edge slats are used during high, not low, angle of attack situations, such as takeoffs and landings.

questions.question_text: What is a purpose of flight spoilers?
questions.choice_a: Increase the camber of the wing.
questions.choice_b: Increase the rate of descent without increasing airspeed.
questions.choice_c: Direct airflow over the top of the wing at high angles of attack.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Spoilers are mounted on the upper surface of each wing, and they reduce lift without increasing airspeed. Their purpose Is to spoil or disrupt the smooth flow of air over the wing to reduce the lifting force of the wing, thus increasing the rate of descent without increasing airspeed. Answer (A) is incorrect. Flaps, not spoilers, increase the camber of the wing. Answer (C) is incorrect. Slots and slats, not ee direct airflow over the top of the wing at high angles of attack.

questions.question_text: For which purpose may flight spoilers be used?
questions.choice_a: Reduce the wings’ lift upon landing.
questions.choice_b: Increase the rate of descent without increasing aerodynamic drag.
questions.choice_c: Aid in longitudinal balance when rolling an airplane into a turn.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: The purpose of spoilers is to spoil or disrupt the smooth flow of air over the wing to reduce the lifting force of the wing. Thus, flight spoilers may be used to reduce the wings’ lift upon landing. Answer (B) is incorrect. Spoilers will increase the aerodynamic drag. Answer (C) is incorrect. Longitudinal balance or = he is primarily achieved by the horizontal stabilizer, not a spoiler.

questions.question_text: Flaps have more effect on
questions.choice_c: symmetrical wings.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: The effectiveness of flaps on a wing configuration depends on many different factors. One important factor is the amount of the wing area affected by the flaps. The thicker the wing shape, the more effect the flaps have. Answer (B) is incorrect. Because surface area of the wing directly corresponds to flap effectiveness, thin wings are affected less, not more, than thicker wings. Answer (C) is incorrect. Symmetrical wings are generally less effective at producing lift due to their lack of positive upper camber. Because of this, flaps will result in less lifting force than if the wing shape were not symmetrical.

questions.question_text: Which is a purpose of ground spoilers? Reduce the wings’ lift upon landing. ae Aid in rolling an airplane into a turn.
questions.choice_c: Increase the rate of descent without gaining airspeed.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Spoilers are designed to spoil or disrupt the smooth flow of air over the wing to reduce the lifting force of the wing. Thus, ground spoilers are used to reduce the wings’ lift upon landing. Answer (B) is incorrect. A flight spoiler, not a ground spoiler, can be used to roll an airplane into a turn. Answer (C) is incorrect. Flight spoilers, not ground spoilers, are used to increase the rate of descent without gaining airspeed.

questions.question_text: Split flaps as opposed to plain flaps
questions.choice_a: produce more lift with less drag.
questions.choice_b: produce only slightly more lift, but much more drag.
questions.choice_c: enhance takeoff performance in hot temperatures and high altitudes.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Split flaps produce slightly more lift than plain flaps, but they also produce more drag due to the turbulent airflow behind the wing. Answer (A) is incorrect. Although split flaps do produce slightly more lift than plain flaps, they produce more drag, not less. Answer (C) is incorrect. Both split flaps and plain flaps produce only slightly more lift when extended, but they create much greater drag. This combination of effects does not allow them to significantly enhance takeoff performance in high- temperature or high-altitude scenarios.

questions.question_text: Fowler flaps as compared to split flaps
questions.choice_a: generate more nose down pitching moment. Na develop much more drag.
questions.choice_c: cause very little change in the airplane structure.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: The Fowler flap causes the greatest change in pitching moment, and the split flap causes the least. Answer (B) is incorrect. The split flap actually produces significantly more drag than the Fowler flap. Answer (C) is incorrect. Fowler flaps extend laterally, increasing the wing’s surface area. This movement significantly changes the airplane’s structure.

questions.question_text: Fowler flaps as compared to split flaps
questions.choice_a: generate more nose down pitching moment. a develop much less lift.
questions.choice_c: cause very little change in the airplane wing structure and characteristics.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: The Fowler flap causes the greatest change in pitching moment, and the split flap causes the least. Answer (B) is incorrect. The Fowler flap develops much more lift than the split flap. Answer (C) is incorrect. The Fowler flap design not only changes the camber of the wing, it also increases the wing area; this design significantly changes the airplane’s wing structure and characteristics.

questions.question_text: Upon landing, spoilers
questions.choice_a: decrease directional stability on the landing rollout.
questions.choice_b: function by increasing tire to ground friction.
questions.choice_c: should be extended after the thrust reversers have been deployed.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: When spoilers are deployed on landing, most of the wing’s lift is destroyed. This action transfers the airplane's weight to the landing gear so that the wheel brakes are more effective. Answer (A) is incorrect. Spoilers increase, not decrease, directional stability on the landing rollout by transferring the airplane’s weight to the landing gear so the wheel brakes and nosewheel steering are more effective. Answer (C) is incorrect. Upon landing, spoilers should be extended immediately after touchdown, not after the thrust reversers have been deployed.

questions.question_text: One method of boundary layer control is accomplished by injecting
questions.choice_a: ajet of air into the leading edge to energize the boundary layer.
questions.choice_b: alow speed jet of air into the boundary layer.
questions.choice_c: ahigh speed jet of air into the boundary layer.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Boundary layer control devices are an additional means of increasing the maximum lift coefficient of a section of the airfoil. Injecting a high-speed jet of air into the boundary layer when operating at high angles of attack can delay the onset of a stall. Answer (A) is incorrect. Injecting air into the leading edge does not aid in preventing boundary layer separation. In fact, this may increase boundary layer separation by decreasing the laminar flow of air across the upper wing surface. Answer (B) is incorrect. An injection of low-speed air into the boundary layer will not have any impact on preventing boundary layer separation.

questions.question_text: On most airplanes, the first 50% of flap deflection causes
questions.choice_a: less than 50% of the total change in lift.
questions.choice_b: more than 50% of the total change in lift.
questions.choice_c: linear lift increases at the AFM specified speed.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: In most airplanes, the first 50% of flap deflection provides over half of the total lift capability of the flaps. The last 50% of flap deflection provides over half of the total drag increase of the flaps. Answer (A) is incorrect. In most airplanes, the first 50% of flap deflection provides over half, not less than half, of the total lift capability of the flaps. Answer (C) is incorrect. In most airplanes, the first 50% of flap deflection provides over half of the total lift capability of the flaps, not a linear lift increase.

questions.question_text: Swept wings causes a significant
questions.choice_c: flap actuation reliability issue.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Swept wings cause a reduction in effectiveness of flaps. Answer (A) is incorrect. Swept wings suffer from a reduction of flap effectiveness, not an increase in their effect. Answer (C) is incorrect. Flap actuation is not a reliability issue in swept wings, although the effectiveness of the flaps is reduced by the wing sweep.

questions.question_text: Compared to a straight wing, swept wing flaps are
questions.choice_c: more effective.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: The use of swept wings hinders the effectiveness of trailing edge control surfaces and high lift devices. Answer (A) is incorrect. The swept wing reduces the effectiveness of flaps. They are not equally effective to flaps installed on a straight wing. Answer (C) is incorrect. The swept wing reduces the effectiveness of flaps. They are not more effective than flaps installed on a straight wing.

questions.question_text: It is important to combine the boundary layer control with flaps since
questions.choice_a: the flap deflection tends to reduce the angle of attack for maximum lift.
questions.choice_b: the flap deflection tends to increase the angle of attack for maximum lift.
questions.choice_c: the boundary layer controls are only effective at low power settings.
questions.explanation: __ DISCUSSION: Boundary layer control devices combined with flaps maintain high velocity smooth airflow with a maximum lift capability. _ Answer (B) is incorrect. The boundary layer control tends to increase the angle of attack, and the flaps help counter that increase by reducing, not increasing, the angle of attack for maximum lift. Answer (C) is incorrect. High speed air is required to maintain high velocity in the boundary | : of the airflow. ry layer to allay separation

questions.question_text: What is the difference between the Fowler flap system and split flap system?
questions.choice_b: Fowler flaps produce the greatest change in pitching moment.
questions.choice_c: Split flaps cause the greatest change in twisting loads.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: The types of flaps producing the greatest increases in aircraft lift coefficient, such as the Fowler flaps, produce the greatest twisting moment. The split flap causes more drag than lift and causes the least twisting moment. Answer (A) is incorrect. Fowler flaps produce a minimal amount of drag. Answer (C) is incorrect. Fowler flaps cause the oe change in twisting moments while split flaps cause the east.

questions.question_text: The tucking (downward motion) of the nose of an airplane during extension of Fowler flaps is
questions.choice_a: a sign of contamination and a no flap landing may be necessary.
questions.choice_c: abnormal.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: A Fowler flap arrangement causes the greatest change in twisting moment. This causes the tucking of the nose. Answer (A) is incorrect. Aerodynamic twisting moments are normal and expected with a Fowler flap arrangement. Answer (C) is incorrect. Aerodynamic twisting moments are normal and are expected with the greatest change in moment from the Fowler flap.

questions.question_text: The first 50% of the flap deflection causes
questions.choice_a: more than half of the total change in lift.
questions.choice_b: more than half of the total change in drag.
questions.choice_c: very little affect on lift.
questions.explanation: The first 50% of flap deflection causes more than half the total change in the maximum coefficient of lift (Ct, ). Answer (B) is incorrect. The last 50% of flap deflection causes more than half of the total change in drag, not the first 50% of flap deflection. Answer (C) is incorrect. The first 50% of flap deflection causes more than half the total change in lift.

questions.question_text: Leading-edge, high-lift devices are
questions.choice_a: more effective on the highly swept wing than trailing edge flaps.
questions.choice_b: less effective than a trailing edge device on a swept wing.
questions.choice_c: more effective than flaps because of the increase in drag at relatively low angles of attack.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Leading-edge, high-lift devices are more effective on the highly swept wing than trailing edge flaps because they are more effective at creating additional lift. Trailing edge flaps normally cause more drag than lift, especially when fully deployed. Answer (B) is incorrect. Leading-edge, high-lift devices are more effective on a highly swept wing because they provide a powerful flow pattern. Answer (C) is incorrect. Slots will provide a negligible change in the pitching moment and no significant change in drag at low angles of attack.

questions.question_text: What will be the ratio between airspeed and lift if the angle of attack and other factors remain constant and airspeed is doubled? Lift will be
questions.choice_c: four times greater.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Lift is proportional to the square of the airplane’s velocity, if the angle of attack and other factors remain constant. For example, an airplane traveling at 200 kt. has four times the lift of the same airplane traveling at 100 kt. Answer (A) is incorrect. Lift is proportional to the square of the airplane’s velocity; it is not constant. Answer (B) is incorrect. As airspeed is doubled, the lift will be four times greater, not doubled.

questions.question_text: What true airspeed and angle of attack should be used to generate the same amount of lift as altitude is increased? The same true airspeed and angle of attack.
questions.choice_b: A higher true airspeed for any given angle of attack.
questions.choice_c: A lower true airspeed and higher angle of attack.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: At an altitude of 18,000 ft., the air has one- half the density of air at sea level. Thus, in order to maintain the same amount of lift as altitude increases, an airplane must fly at a greater true airspeed for any given angle of attack. Answer (A) is incorrect. True airspeed must be increased, not constant, as altitude increases. Answer (C) is incorrect. True airspeed must increase, not decrease, for any given angle of attack.

questions.question_text: How can an airplane produce the same lift in ground effect as when out of ground effect?
questions.choice_c: A higher angle of attack.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: The reduction of the wingtip vortices due to ground effect alters the spanwise lift distribution and reduces the induced angle of attack and induced drag. The wing will require a lower angle of attack in ground effect to produce the same lift coefficient as when out of ground effect. Answer (A) is incorrect. If the same angle of attack is maintained, an increase in lift coefficient will result. Answer (C) is incorrect. A lower, not higher, angle of attack is required to produce the same lift in ground effect as out of ground effect.

questions.question_text: By changing the angle of attack of a wing, the pilot can control the airplane’s
questions.choice_b: lift and airspeed, but not drag.
questions.choice_c: lift, airspeed, and drag.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Changes in the angle of attack can affect lift, airspeed, and drag. Answer (A) is incorrect. Gross weight cannot be controlled by angle of attack; however, the aircraft load factor may be altered with a change in angle of attack. Answer (B) is incorrect. Typically, at low angles of attack, the drag coefficient is low and small changes in angle of attack create only slight changes in the drag coefficient. At high angles of attack, small changes in the angle of attack cause significant changes in drag.

questions.question_text: In a jet powered airplane (fan or pure jet) the angle of attack at stall is
questions.choice_b: very different from power on and power off.
questions.choice_c: essentially the same power on or power off.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: For turbine-powered airplanes, the angle of attack at stall is essentially the same whether power on or power off. Answer (A) is incorrect. The reason the angle of attack is essentially the same for turbine-powered airplanes whether power on or power off is the lack of accelerated airflow moving over the wing surfaces. Altitude is not as significant a factor. Answer (B) is incorrect. The angle of attack at stall is essentially the same whether power on or power off in a turbine-powered airplane, not different depending on the power setting.

questions.question_text: An increase in airspeed above the stall speed will require
questions.choice_a: arelative decrease in the relative wind.
questions.choice_b: acorresponding decrease in angle of attack and co-efficient of lift.
questions.choice_c: acorresponding increase in co-efficient of lift and drag.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: An increase in airspeed above the stall speed ae a decrease of both angle of attack and the coefficient of ift. Answer (A) is incorrect. An increase in airspeed will result in an increase in relative wind, not a decrease. Answer (C) is incorrect. An increase in airspeed above stall speed will require a decrease in the coefficient of lift, not an increase. Although itis true, as this distractor answer suggests, that total drag will increase as speed increases.

questions.question_text: Lift coefficient must be maximum at the
questions.choice_c: maximum mach operating limit (Vimo)
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: The lift coefficient will be at its maximum value at the speed the airplane stalls. Answer (B) is incorrect. The airplane’s maximum maneuvering speed is based on a stall speed that occurs prior to an increase in load factor that would exceed the limit load for the aircraft. It is not predicated on maximum lift coefficient. Answer (C) is incorrect. Vimo is an operational limit speed, not a speed that corresponds to the maximum coefficient of lift, or the airplane’s stalling speed.

questions.question_text: When at high angle of attack the boundary layer tends to
questions.choice_b: loop around the upper camber line.
questions.choice_c: decrease air pressure along the chord.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: When at a high angle of attack, the boundary layer tends to stagnate and stop. The airflow will separate from the surface and a stall occurs. Answer (B) is incorrect. As the boundary layer becomes Stagnate and stops, the airflow will separate and loop around the upper surface and a stall occurs. Answer (C) is incorrect. As the boundary layer separates from the body (curving above the upper camber of the wing) the pressure rises, resulting in a loss of lift as the critical angle of attack is reached.

questions.question_text: Propeller driven airplanes can develop more lift at slower airspeeds with high power on because
questions.choice_a: the dynamic pressure is created downstream of the propellers.
questions.choice_b: the high power conditions decreases the maximum lift angle of attack.
questions.choice_c: the high power reduces the lift required from the wings.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: When the propeller driven airplane is at low airspeeds and high power, the dynamic pressure is much greater and causes considerably greater lift than at zero thrust. Answer (B) is incorrect. The high power conditions increase, not decrease, the maximum lift angle of attack. Answer (C) is incorrect. The wings still require the same amount of lift; however, the dynamic pressure provided from the propellers helps satisfy that requirement.

questions.question_text: What is the effect on total drag of an aircraft if the airspeed decreases in level flight below that speed for maximum L/D?
questions.choice_a: Drag increases because of increased induced drag.
questions.choice_b: Drag increases because of increased parasite drag.
questions.choice_c: Drag decreases because of lower induced drag.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Total drag is at a minimum for the maximum lift-drag ratio (L/Dyax) at one specific angle of attack and lift coefficient. As airspeed decreases, the induced drag will increase because a greater angle of attack is required to maintain the lift coefficient. The amount of induced drag varies inversely as the square of the airspeed. Answer (B) is incorrect. Parasite drag varies directly, not inversely, with airspeed. Answer (C) is incorrect. Drag increases, not decreases, from any speed other than that for maximum L/D.

questions.question_text: What performance is characteristic of flight at maximum L/D in a propeller-driven airplane?
questions.choice_c: Maximum endurance.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: At maximum L/D, total drag is at a minimum. This occurs at a particular angle of attack and lift coefficient at which the proportion between speed and power required is the greatest. Thus, the maximum range and engine-out glide distance for a propeller-driven airplane is at maximum L/D. Answer (B) is incorrect. Best angle of climb (e.g., to clear an obstacle) is at a high angle of attack with both high lift and high drag coefficients, which would not result in a maximum L/D ratio. Answer (C) is incorrect. Maximum endurance would be obtained at the point of minimum power required, since this would require the lowest fuel flow to keep the airplane in steady, level flight. This point is not at maximum L/D.

questions.question_text: What flight condition should be expected when an aircraft leaves ground effect?
questions.choice_a: An increase in induced drag requiring a higher angle of attack.
questions.choice_b: A decrease in parasite drag permitting a lower angle of attack.
questions.choice_c: Anincrease in dynamic stability.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: The reduction of the wingtip vortices due to ground effect alters the spanwise lift distribution and reduces the induced angle of attack and induced drag. When leaving ground effect, the airplane will require an increase in angle of attack to maintain the same lift coefficient, which will increase induced drag. Answer (B) is incorrect. At slow airspeeds when taking off, induced, not parasite, drag predominates. Answer (C) is incorrect. When leaving ground effect, expect a decrease, not increase, in stability and a nose-up change in moment.

questions.question_text: What is the relationship between induced and parasite drag when the gross weight is increased?
questions.choice_a: Parasite drag increases more than induced drag.
questions.choice_b: Induced drag increases more than parasite drag.
questions.choice_c: Both parasite and induced drag are equally increased.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Increasing airplane weight increases the induced drag (more lift is required) more than the parasite drag because a higher angle of attack is required to support the increased weight. Answer (A) is incorrect. Parasite drag increases less, not more, than induced drag as airplane weight increases. Answer (C) is incorrect. Induced drag increases more than parasite drag with increases in airplane gross weight.

questions.question_text: If the boundary layer separates drag will decrease. the airflow separates from the wing surface and stall occurs. ice will not sublimate in this area.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: The boundary layer is the thick boundary that exists between the flow of air and the solid surface of the airfoil. If the boundary layer separates, the airflow separates as well, causing an increase in drag and a loss of lift that results in a stall. Answer (A) is incorrect. Drag increases with the separation of the boundary layer. It does not decrease due to the separation. Answer (C) is incorrect. The separation of the boundary layer affects airflow, lift, and drag in a relatively short duration manner. The result is an increase in drag and a stall. The sublimation of ice is not a factor in this situation.

questions.question_text: An airplane flying at L/Dyax will have the same glide speed for all weights. different glide ratios dependent on gross weight. the same glide performance at all weights.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Maximum glide performance (i.e., distance or range) is found at L/Dyax. Because this aerodynamic reference is determined at one specific angle of attack and lift coefficient, weight does not affect glide performance as long as the correct speed is maintained during the glide. Answer (A) is incorrect. An airplane’s glide speed increases with increases in weight. Answer (B) is incorrect. The maximum glide ratio for an airplane is found at L/Dyax. L/Dmax is a fixed aerodynamic point and does not vary based on weight.

questions.question_text: Any angle of attack lower or higher than L/Dyyax increases total drag. S requires less thrust for steady state flight. means less maneuvering margin before stalling.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Any angle of attack lower or higher than that for L/Dwax increases the total drag for a given airplane. Answer (B) is incorrect. A lower or higher angle of attack will result in an increase in drag, which will require more thrust to maintain steady state flight, not less. Answer (C) is incorrect. An airplane can stall at any airspeed, in any attitude, based on exceeding the critical angle of attack, not a margin of maneuverability that exists between L/Dyyax and a published stalling speed.

questions.question_text: At the end of a power-on approach in a propeller driven airplane, if the pilot sharply reduces power, the airplane may continue to float for a long distance down the runway. the airplane may drop suddenly due to reduced lift. the airplane’s engine will quit running if the power is reduced suddenly.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Airplanes with large propellers spinning rapidly in low pitch create considerable drag and may drop suddenly. In such airplanes, it is preferable to maintain power through the landing flare and touchdown. Answer (A) is incorrect. The airspeed will decay rapidly, not slowly, due to the increased drag caused by the propellers. Answer (C) is incorrect. The airplane should continue to run even if the power is reduced suddenly.

questions.question_text: A bank angle of 15° will increase induced drag by about 3% 7% 15%
questions.explanation: The equation used to determine this is: Loadifactot =; === ==) aes mast aCe (bank angle) elie hack, wh yl Load factor Cos (15) = 1.0353 Gs 1.0353° = 1.072 (1.072 — 1) x 100 = 7.2% Induced drag increase To express as a percentage Answer (A) is incorrect. The increase of 3% would require a bank of about 5° at 1.015 Gs. Answer (C) is incorrect. The increase of 15% would require a bank of about 20° at 1.095 Gs. .

questions.question_text: A bank angle of 30° will increase induced drag by about
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: A 33% increase in induced drag will be caused by a coordinated turn with a bank angle of 30°. The equation used to determine this is: = ee ee Foad factor. «Oosibank angle) ake be ag Cos (0) = 1:1547 Load factor = Induced drag increase = 1.1547° = 1.3333 To express as a percentage = (1.3333 — 1) x 100 = 33.3% Answer (A) is incorrect. An 18% increase in induced drag will be caused by a coordinated turn with a bank angle of 23°. Answer (C) is incorrect. A 24% increase in induced drag will be caused by a coordinated turn with a bank angle of about 28°.

questions.question_text: A bank angle of 45° will increase induced drag by
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: A 100% increase in induced drag will be caused by a coordinated turn with a bank angle of 45°. The equation used to determine this is: BAe enn ee Load factor Cos (bank angle) a ere Cos (45) = 1.4142 Load factor = Induced drag increase = 1.4142° = 1.9999 To express as a percentage = (1.9999 — 1) x 100 = 99.9% Answer (A) is incorrect. A 33% increase in induced drag will be caused by a coordinated turn with a bank angle of 30°. Answer (C) is incorrect. A 76% increase in induced drag will be caused by a coordinated turn with a bank angle of 40°.

questions.question_text: A bank angle of 60° will increase induced drag by
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: A 300% increase in induced drag will be caused by a coordinated turn with a bank angle of 60°. The equation used to determine this is: = ae ieee Load factor = == (bank angle) =") 19999 ed ME Load factor Cos (60) Induced drag increase = 1.9999° = 3.9996 To express as a percentage = (3.9996 — 1) x 100 = 299.9% Answer (A) is incorrect. A 200% increase in induced drag will be caused by a coordinated turn with a bank angle of 50°. Answer (C) is incorrect. A 100% increase in induced drag will be caused by a coordinated turn with a bank angle of 45°.

questions.question_text: Which is a purpose of wing-mounted vortex generators?
questions.choice_a: Reduce the drag caused by supersonic flow over portions of the wing.
questions.choice_b: Increase the onset of drag divergence and aid in aileron effectiveness at high speed.
questions.choice_c: Break the airflow over the wing so the stall will progress from the root out to the tip of the wing.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Even when jet aircraft do not fly at Mach 1 or greater, airflow over some portions of the upper surface of the wing may exceed Mach 1 and create a shockwave of turbulent air. This turbulent air causes a portion of the wing to partially stall, which causes a substantial increase in drag. Wing-mounted vortex generators are installed to reduce this drag caused by supersonic airflow over portions of the wing. Answer (B) is incorrect. Although the vortex generators are most effective at high speeds, the increased drag that they produce is not their purpose. Answer (C) is incorrect. A stall strip breaks the airflow over the wing so the stall will progress from the root out to the tip of the wing.

questions.question_text: What affects indicated stall speed?
questions.choice_b: Load factor, angle of attack, and power.
questions.choice_c: Angle of attack, weight, and air density.
questions.explanation: ; DISCUSSION: An airplane’s indicated stall speed is affected by total airplane weight, weight distribution within the airplane, angle of bank, pitch attitude, control coordination, drag, and power settings, but NOT by angle of attack or air density. Answer (B) is incorrect. Indicated stall speed is not affected by the angle of attack. Answer (C) is incorrect. Indicated stall speed is not affected by angle of attack or air density.

questions.question_text: Which is a purpose of wing-mounted vortex generators?
questions.choice_a: Delays the onset of drag divergence at high speeds and aids in maintaining aileron effectiveness at high speeds.
questions.choice_b: Breaks the airflow over the wing so the stall will progress from the root out to the tip of the wing.
questions.choice_c: Increase the onset of drag divergence and aid in aileron effectiveness at low speed.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Rows of vortex generators located on the upper surface of the wing just upstream of the ailerons delay the onset of drag divergence at high speeds and also aid in maintaining aileron effectiveness at high speeds. Answer (B) is incorrect. Vortex generators do not break the airflow over the wing so the stall will progress from the root out to the tip of the wing. Answer (C) is incorrect. Vortex generators delay, not increase, the onset of drag divergence.

questions.question_text: How can turbulent air cause an increase in stalling speed of an airfoil?
questions.choice_a: An abrupt change in relative wind.
questions.choice_b: A decrease in angle of attack.
questions.choice_c: Sudden decrease in load factor.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Turbulence can cause an aircraft to stall at a significantly higher airspeed than in stable conditions. A vertical gust or wind shear can cause a sudden change in the relative wind and result in an abrupt increase in the angle of attack and, thus, result in a stall. Although a gust may not be maintained long enough for a stall to develop, the aircraft may stall while the pilot is attempting to control the flight path, particularly during an approach in gusty conditions. Answer (B) is incorrect. Decreased angle of attack decreases, not increases, the possibility of a stall. Answer (C) is incorrect. A sudden decrease in load factor decreases, not increases, the stalling speed.

questions.question_text: Airflow separation over the wing can be delayed by using vortex generators,
questions.choice_a: directing high pressure air over the top of the wing or flap through slots and making the wing surface smooth.
questions.choice_b: directing a suction over the top of the wing or flap through slots and making the wing surface smooth.
questions.choice_c: making the wing surface rough and/or directing high pressure air over the top of the wing or flap through slots.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Airflow separation over the wing can be delayed by various methods of replacing the slow-moving laminar airflow next to the wing’s surface with faster-moving air. One method is the use of vortex generators, which are attached to the upper surface of the wing. Vortex generators make the wing surface rough, creating a turbulent flow that increases the speed of the air. Another method is to direct high-pressure air over the top of the wing or flap through slots. Answer (A) is incorrect. A smooth wing surface does not delay airflow separation. Vortex generators create a turbulent flow of air, which increases the speed of the airflow next to the wing’s surface. Answer (B) is incorrect. Vortex generators create a turbulent flow of air making the wing surface rough, not smooth. Additionally, slots direct high-pressure air, not a suction, over the top of the wing or flap.

questions.question_text: An airplane stall warning device must sense
questions.choice_c: angle of attack.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: The aircraft stalls when the critical angle of attack is exceeded. The angle of attack is measured as the angle between the chord line and the relative wind. In order to accurately warn of an impending stall, a stall warning device must sense the relative wind. Answer (B) is incorrect. Coincident wind is not a factor in determining that a stall is imminent. Answer (C) is incorrect. A stall warning device does not directly measure angle of attack. However, angle of attack indicators are frequently employed to avoid situations that would cause the stall warning system to initiate a warning.

questions.question_text: How does Vs (KTAS) speed vary with altitude?
questions.choice_a: Remains the same at all altitudes.
questions.choice_c: Varies inversely with altitude.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Due to the lesser pressure at altitude, a higher true airspeed is required to create the same pressure differential between impact (pitot) pressure and static air pressure. Consequently, true airspeed increases as altitude increases. Answer (A) is incorrect. True airspeed (TAS) increases with altitude; it does not remain unchanged. Answer (C) is incorrect. True airspeed varies directly with altitude, not inversely. As altitude increases, true airspeed increases.

questions.question_text: Stall warning devices must sense
questions.choice_c: dynamic pressures.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Stall warning devices must sense pressure distribution on the wing in order to reliably indicate an impending stall. Answer (A) is incorrect.
