Static pressure is not a factor in determining when a stall is imminent. Answer (C) is incorrect. The measurement of dynamic pressures is important to the indication of airspeed, not stall warnings. This is primarily because the stall occurs as a result of exceeding an angle of attack at a specified pressure distribution. The combination that results in a stall can occur at any airspeed.

questions.question_text: An aircraft in a steady state, constant altitude turn
questions.choice_b: experiences no changes in stall speeds.
questions.choice_c: experiences higher stall speeds.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: The stalling speed of an aircraft is higher in a level turn than in straight-and-level flight. This is due to the higher angle of attack required to maintain altitude, because a portion of vertical lift has been re-directed to horizontal lift. Answer (A) is incorrect. Airspeed indication errors result from a variety of factors, including excessive angle of attack, which is not typically experienced during a steady state, constant altitude turn. Answer (B) is incorrect. An airplane in a steady state, constant altitude turn will experience a stall at a higher airspeed than the same airplane in straight-and-level flight.

questions.question_text: Fundamental recovery from a stall requires
questions.choice_c: decreasing the angle of attack.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: To recover from a stall, you must decrease the angle of attack. While this action may involve lowering the pitch of the nose, increasing airspeed, and/or increasing power, decreasing the angle of attack is the fundamental way to recover from a stall. Answer (A) is incorrect. Increasing power alone will not cause the airplane to recover from a stall. Answer (B) is incorrect. Increasing airspeed alone will not cause the airplane to recover from a stall.

questions.question_text: The stall speed of an airplane
questions.choice_a: is constant regardless of weight or airfoil configuration.
questions.choice_b: is affected by weight and bank angle.
questions.choice_c: is not affected by dynamic pressures and lift coefficient.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: The stalling speed of an airplane is affected by both weight and bank angle. For example, the stalling speed of an aircraft is higher in a level turn than it is in straight-and-level flight. A Answer (A) is incorrect. The stall speed of an airplane is not constant. Stall speed is affected by bank, weight, load factor, and power. Answer (C) is incorrect. Dynamic pressure and coefficient of lift are both considerations in wing design and performance. Their relationship does indeed have an effect on the stall speed of an airplane.

questions.question_text: A sudden pull-up after takeoff
questions.choice_a: can result in a stall or spin. 2 will cause an increase in initial rate of climb.
questions.choice_c: will delay rotation, which may degrade acceleration.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: If the airplane is subject to a sudden pull-up or steep turn after becoming airborne, the result may be a stall, spin, or reduction in initial rate of climb. Answer (B) is incorrect. A sudden pull-up after takeoff may result in a reduction, not increase, in initial rate of climb. Answer (C) is incorrect. A delayed rotation is a typical takeoff error; however, when a sudden pull-up is initiated, the chances of a stall or spin are of concern.

questions.question_text: An airplane will always stall at the same
questions.choice_c: airspeed. 5.8 Load Factor
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: An aircraft stall results from a rapid decrease in lift caused by separation of airflow from the wing's surface brought on by exceeding the critical angle of attack. A stall can occur at any pitch attitude or airspeed. The stalling speed of a particular aircraft is not a fixed value for all flight situations, but a given aircraft always stalls at the same angle of attack regardless of airspeed, weight, load factor, or density altitude. Answer (B) is incorrect. Regardless of the power setting or airspeed, a given aircraft will always stall at the same angle of attack. Answer (C) is incorrect. Regardless of the airspeed, a given aircraft will always stall at the same angle of attack.

questions.question_text: What is load factor?
questions.choice_a: Lift multiplied by the total weight.
questions.choice_b: Lift subtracted from the total weight.
questions.choice_c: Lift divided by the total weight.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: A load factor is the ratio of the total airload acting on the airplane to the gross weight of the airplane. For example, if an airplane has a gross weight of 2,000 Ib. and, during flight, is subjected to aerodynamic forces that increase the total load the wing must support to 4,000 Ib., the load factor is 2 (4,000 + 2,000). In this example, the airplane wing is producing lift that is equal to twice the gross weight of the airplane. Answer (A) is incorrect. Lift is divided, not multiplied, by the total weight. Answer (B) is incorrect. Lift is divided by, not subtracted from, the total weight.

questions.question_text: |f an aircraft with a gross weight of 2,000 pounds were subjected to a total load of 6,000 pounds in flight, the load factor would be
questions.choice_c: 9Gs.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Load factor is the ratio of the total airload acting on the airplane to the gross weight of the airplane. In this problem, the total airload is 6,000 Ib., and the gross weight is 2,000 Ib. The load factor is 3 (6,000 + 2,000). Load factors are usually expressed in terms of “G,” or pull of gravity, in this case, three times the actual weight. Answer (A) is incorrect. Calculating 6,000 Ib. + 2,000 Ib. = 3, not 2. Answer (C) is incorrect. Calculating 6,000 Ib. + 2,000 Ib. = 3, not 9.

questions.question_text: For a given angle of bank, the load factor imposed on both the aircraft and pilot in a coordinated constant-altitude turn
questions.choice_a: is directly related to the airplane’s gross weight.
questions.choice_b: varies with the rate of turn.
questions.choice_c: is constant.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: In any airplane at any airspeed, if a coordinated constant altitude turn is maintained, the load factor for a given degree of bank is constant, which is the resultant load of gravity and centrifugal force. Answer (A) is incorrect. Load factor is not affected by changes in gross weight. Answer (B) is incorrect. For any given angle of bank, the rate of turn varies with the airspeed; thus, there is no change in centrifugal force for any given bank. The load factor remains constant.

questions.question_text: Upon which factor does wing loading during a level coordinated turn in smooth air depend?
questions.choice_c: True airspeed.
questions.explanation: The load factor for a given airplane during a level coordinated turn is determined solely by the angle of bank. Answer (A) is incorrect. In a coordinated turn, the rate of turn does not have any impact on the load factor, which is determined solely by the angle of bank. Answer (C) is incorrect. True airspeed has no impact on the load factor.

questions.question_text: Vue speed
questions.choice_c: varies directly with altitude.
questions.explanation: __ DISCUSSION: V,- remains the same. In an airplane, Vye is fixed and is marked on the airspeed indicator. It is not altered by altitude. _ Answer (B) is incorrect. Vye varies inversely with altitude in helicopters, not airplanes. Answer (C) is incorrect. The radial red line on the airspeed indicator is the value of Vy-. That value will not change with altitude.

questions.question_text: What result does a level turn have on the total lift required and load factor with a constant airspeed?
questions.choice_a: Lift required remains constant, and the load factor increases.
questions.choice_b: Both total lift required and load factor increase.
questions.choice_c: Lift required increases, and the load factor decreases. 5.9 Pallet Weight
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: In a constant airspeed, level turn both the total lift required and the load factor increase. Answer (A) is incorrect. The amount of lift required increases in a level turn at constant airspeed. It does not remain constant. Answer (C) is incorrect. The load factor increases in a constant airspeed, level turn. It does not decrease.

questions.question_text: What is the maximum allowable weight that may be carried on a pallet which has the dimensions of 76 x 74 inches? Floor load limit - 176 Ib./sq. ft. Pallet weight - 77 Ib. Tie-down devices - 29 Ib.
questions.choice_c: 6,796.8 pounds.
questions.explanation: The calculation is as follows: Pallet area: 76 x 74 = 5,624 sq. in. Pallet sq. ft.: 5,624 + 144 = 39.056 sq. ft. Max. weight: 39.056 x 176 = 6,873.8 Ib. Max. cargo weight: 6,873.8 — 77 — 29 = 6,767.8 Ib. Answer (B) is incorrect. This is the maximum allowable weight of the pallet, which is a total of the weight of the pallet and tie-down devices, plus the amount carried on the pallet. Answer (C) is incorrect. This does not include the weight of the tie-down devices as part of the total loaded pallet weight.

questions.question_text: What is the maximum allowable weight that may be carried on a pallet which has the dimensions of 36 x 48 inches? Floor load limit - 169 Ib./sq. ft. Pallet weight - 47 Ib. Tie-down devices - 33 Ib.
questions.choice_c: 1,981.0 pounds.
questions.explanation: The calculation is as follows: Pallet area: 36 x 48 = 1,728 sq. in. Pallet sq. ft.: 1,728 + 144 = 12 sq. ft. Max. weight: 12 x 169 = 2,028 Ib. Max. cargo weight: 2,028 — 47 — 33 = 1,948 lb. Answer (B) is incorrect. This does not include the weight of the pallet as part of the total loaded pallet weight. Answer (C) is incorrect. This does not include the weight of the tie-down devices as part of the total loaded pallet weight.

questions.question_text: What is the maximum allowable weight that may be carried on a pallet which has the dimensions of 81 x 83 inches? Floor load limit - 180 Ib./sq. ft. Pallet weight - 82 Ib. Tie-down devices - 31 Ib.
questions.choice_c: 8,290.8 pounds.
questions.explanation: The calculation is as follows: Pallet area: 81 x 83 = 6,723 sq. in. Pallet sq. ft.: 6,723 + 144 = 46.688 sq. ft. Max. weight: 46.688 x 180 = 8,403.8 Ib. Max. cargo weight: 8,403.8 — 82 — 31 = 8,290.8 Ib. Answer (A) is incorrect. This is the maximum allowable weight of the pallet, which is a total of the weight of the pallet and tie-down devices, plus the amount carried on the pallet. Answer (B) is incorrect. This does not include the weight of the tie-down devices as part of the total loaded pallet weight.

questions.question_text: What is the maximum allowable weight that may be carried on a pallet which has the dimensions of Floor load limit - 112 Ib./sq. ft. Pallet weight - 45 Ib. Tie-down devices - 29 Ib.
questions.choice_c: 1,347.8 pounds.
questions.explanation: The calculation is as follows: Pallet area: 36.5 x 48.5 = 1,770.25 sq. in. Pallet sq. ft.: 1,770.25 + 144 = 12.293 sq. ft. Max. weight: 12.293 x 112 = 1,376.8 Ib. Max. cargo weight: 1,376.8 — 45 — 29 = 1,302.8 Ib. Answer (A) is incorrect. This does not include the weight of the pallet (45 Ib.) as part of the total loaded pallet weight. Answer (C) is incorrect. This does not include the weight of the tie-down devices (29 Ib.) as part of the total loaded pallet weight.

questions.question_text: What is the maximum allowable weight that may be carried on a pallet which has the dimensions of Floor load limit - 121 Ib./sq. ft. Pallet weight - 47 Ib. Tie-down devices - 33 Ib.
questions.choice_c: 1,696.2 pounds.
questions.explanation: The calculation is as follows: Pallet area: 42.6 x 48.7 = 2,074.62 sq. in. Pallet sq. ft.: 2,074.62 + 144 = 14.407 sq. ft. Max. weight: 14.407 x 121 = 1,743.2 Ib. Max. cargo weight: 1,743.2 — 47 — 33 = 1,663.2 Ib. Answer (A) is incorrect. This does not include the weight of the tie-down devices (33 |b.) as part of the total loaded pallet weight. Answer (C) is incorrect. This does not include the weight of the pallet (47 Ib.) as part of the total loaded pallet weight.

questions.question_text: What is the minimum floor load limit that an aircraft must have to carry the following pallet of cargo? Pallet dimensions are 116.8 x 87.7 inches Pallet weight - 137 Ib. Tie-down devices - 49 Ib. Cargo weight - 12,262.4 Ib.
questions.choice_c: 179 lb./sq. ft.
questions.explanation: The calculation is as follows: Pallet area: 116.8 x 87.7 = 10,243.36 sq. in. Pallet sq. ft.: 10,243.36 + 144 = 71.13 sq. ft. questions.question_text: At which speed will increasing the pitch attitude cause an airplane to climb?
questions.choice_c: Any speed.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: At speeds below L/Dyax, an increase in pitch will slow the airplane down (i.e., the region of reverse command). At L/Dyax and above, an increase in pitch will increase climb or decrease descent. Answer (A) is incorrect. Below L/Duax, performance decreases, not increases, with increases in pitch. Answer (C) is incorrect. Below L/Dmax, performance decreases, not increases, with increases in pitch.

questions.question_text: At what speed, with reference to L/Dyax, does maximum rate-of-climb for a jet airplane occur?
questions.choice_a: Aspeed greater than that for L/Dmax.
questions.choice_b: Aspeed equal to that for L/Dyax.
questions.choice_c: Aspeed less than that for L/Dmax.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: The maximum rate-of-climb for a jet airplane will occur at a speed greater than that for L/Duax. This occurrence is accounted for, in part, by the increase in power available with speed. Answer (B) is incorrect. A speed equal to that of L/Dyax is the maximum climb angle, not rate-of-climb. Answer (C) is incorrect. The maximum rate-of-climb is at a speed greater, not less, than L/Dmax:

questions.question_text: What effect does an increase in airspeed have on a coordinated turn while maintaining a constant angle of bank and altitude?
questions.choice_a: The rate of turn will decrease resulting in a decreased load factor.
questions.choice_b: The rate of turn will increase resulting in an increased load factor.
questions.choice_c: The rate of turn will decrease resulting in no changes in load factor.
questions.explanation: aM DISCUSSION: Increasing airspeed while maintaining a coordinated turn with a constant angle of bank and altitude will decrease the rate of turn. For any given bank angle, the rate of turn varies with the airspeed; the higher the speed, the slower the rate of turn. This variation compensates for added centrifugal force, allowing the load factor to remain the same. Answer (A) is incorrect. At a constant bank angle, the higher airspeed will decrease the rate of turn to compensate for added centrifugal force, allowing the load factor to remain the same, not decrease. Answer (B) is incorrect. For any bank angle, the rate of turn varies with the airspeed; the higher the speed, the slower, not faster, the rate of turn.

questions.question_text: During a skidding turn to the right, what is the relationship between the component of lift and centrifugal force?
questions.choice_a: Centrifugal force is less than the horizontal lift component, and the load factor is increased.
questions.choice_b: Centrifugal force is greater than the horizontal lift component.
questions.choice_c: Centrifugal force and the horizontal lift component are equal, and the load factor is decreased.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: When centrifugal force is greater than the horizontal component of lift, the aircraft skids to the outside of the turn. Answer (A) is incorrect. Centrifugal force is greater than the horizontal lift component in a skidding turn. It is a slipping turn that results from centrifugal force being less than the horizontal component of lift. Answer (C) is incorrect. Centrifugal force and the horizontal component would be equal only in a coordinated turn. In a skidding turn, the centrifugal force must be greater than the horizontal component of lift.

questions.question_text: If no corrective action is taken by the pilot as angle of bank is increased, how is the vertical component of lift and sink rate affected?
questions.choice_a: Lift increases and the sink rate increases.
questions.choice_b: Lift decreases and the sink rate decreases.
questions.choice_c: Lift decreases and the sink rate increases.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: As the angle of bank is increased, the horizontal component of lift increases, which decreases the vertical component. As this happens, weight is greater than the vertical component of lift, and lift decreases as the sink rate increases. Answer (A) is incorrect. Lift will decrease, not increase. Answer (B) is incorrect. The sink rate increases, not decreases, as the lift decreases.

questions.question_text: What is the relationship of the rate of turn with the radius of turn with a constant angle of bank but increasing airspeed?
questions.choice_a: Rate will decrease and radius will increase.
questions.choice_b: Rate will increase and radius will decrease.
questions.choice_c: Rate and radius will increase.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: As airspeed is increased, the angle of attack needs to be decreased, which will cause the rate of turn to decrease. At the same time, the increase in airspeed results in an increase of the turn radius. _ Answer (B) is incorrect. To maintain a constant angle of bank while increasing airspeed, the radius of turn will increase, not decrease. Answer (C) is incorrect. To maintain a constant angle of bank while increasing airspeed, the rate of turn will decrease, not increase.

questions.question_text: How can the pilot increase the rate of turn and decrease the radius at the same time?
questions.choice_a: Steepen the bank and increase airspeed.
questions.choice_b: Steepen the bank and decrease airspeed.
questions.choice_c: Shallow the bank and increase airspeed.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: At slower airspeeds, an airplane can make a turn in less distance (smaller radius) and at a faster rate. Thus, to decrease the radius and increase the rate, one steepens the bank and decreases airspeed. __ Answer (A) is incorrect. A decrease, not increase, in airspeed decreases the turn radius. Answer (C) is incorrect. A steeper, not shallower, bank and a decrease, not increase, in airspeed increase the rate of turn.

questions.question_text: Why must the angle of attack be increased during a turn to maintain altitude?
questions.choice_a: pompersate for loss of vertical component of ift.
questions.choice_b: Increase the horizontal component of lift equal to the vertical component.
questions.choice_c: Compensate for increase in drag.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: The lift during a turn is divided into two components, one vertical and the other horizontal. This division reduces the amount of lift that is opposing gravity. The angle of attack must be increased until the vertical component of lift is equal to the weight in order to maintain altitude. Answer (B) is incorrect. Angle of attack is increased in order to increase the vertical component of lift to equal weight. Answer (C) is incorrect. Additional thrust (power), not angle of attack, is used to compensate for increase in drag.

questions.question_text: Generally, the turning performance of an airplane is defined by
questions.choice_a: structural and power limits at high altitude.
questions.choice_b: aerodynamic and structural limits at low altitude.
questions.choice_c: control and structural limits at high altitude.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Each of the three limiting factors (aerodynamic, structural, and power) may combine to define the turning performance of an airplane. Generally, aerodynamic and structural limits predominate at low altitude while aerodynamic and power limits predominate at high altitude. Answer (A) is incorrect. Aerodynamic, structural, and power limitations may combine to define the turning performance of an airplane. Generally, structural limitations are limited to lower altitudes due to the thinner air at higher altitudes. At higher altitudes, aerodynamic and power limitations generally apply. Answer (C) is incorrect. Aerodynamic, structural, and power limitations may combine to define the turning performance of an airplane. Generally, structural and aerodynamic limits predominate at lower altitudes due to thicker air. Control is not one of the recognized limiting factors of turning performance.

questions.question_text: An increase in airspeed followed by a decrease in control effectiveness may result in
questions.choice_a: a decrease in downwash on the horizontal tail, resulting in a “tuck under.”
questions.choice_b: an increase in downwash on the horizontal tail, resulting in a “tuck under.”
questions.choice_c: the need for retrimming due to the increased speed. 5.12 Stability
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: When airflow separation occurs on the wing due to shock wave formation resulting from an increase in speed, there will be a loss of lift and subsequent loss of downwash aft of the affected area. If there is a decrease in downwash on the horizontal tail, it will create a diving moment and the aircraft will “tuck under.” Answer (B) is incorrect. A decrease in downwash, rather than an increase in downwash, may cause a “tuck under.” Answer (C) is incorrect. There will not necessarily be a need for retrimming, rather the decrease in control effectiveness resulting by a large increase in airspeed may cause a “tuck under.”

questions.question_text: Describe dynamic longitudinal stability.
questions.choice_c: Motion about the vertical axis.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Longitudinal stability is the quality that makes an airplane stable about its lateral axis. It involves the pitching motion as the airplane’s nose moves up and down in flight. Dynamic stability is the overall tendency that the airplane displays after its equilibrium is disturbed. Answer (A) is incorrect. Motion about the airplane’s longitudinal axis is lateral, not longitudinal, stability. Answer (C) is incorrect. Motion about the vertical axis is directional, not longitudinal, stability.

questions.question_text: What is a characteristic of longitudinal instability?
questions.choice_c: Aircraft constantly tries to pitch down.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Longitudinal stability is the quality that makes an airplane stable about its lateral axis (pitching). A longitudinally unstable airplane has a tendency to dive or climb (oscillations) progressively into a very steep dive or climb. Answer (B) is incorrect. Longitudinal stability refers to pitch, not bank, oscillations. Answer (C) is incorrect. The aircraft constantly trying to pitch down is a characteristic of improper elevator trim, not longitudinal instability.

questions.question_text: Identify the type stability if the aircraft attitude remains in the new position after the controls have been neutralized.
questions.choice_c: Neutral longitudinal static stability.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: If the airplane’s nose remains in the new position after the elevator control is pressed forwardand released, the aircraft displays neutral longitudinal static stability. Answer (A) is incorrect. A negative longitudinal static stability means the airplane tends to move even farther from the original position. Answer (B) is incorrect. With neutral longitudinal dynamic stability, the airplane continues to oscillate without a tendency to increase or decrease.

questions.question_text: Identify the type stability if the aircraft attitude tends to move farther from its original position after the controls have been neutralized.
questions.choice_c: Positive static stability.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: When the airplane’s nose moves farther from its original position, it has the undesirable characteristic of negative static stability. Answer (B) is incorrect. Negative dynamic stability refers to oscillations that become progressively larger. Answer (C) is incorrect. If the aircraft had positive static stability, it would return to its original position.

questions.question_text: Identify the type stability if the aircraft attitude tends to return to its original position after the controls have been neutralized.
questions.choice_c: Neutral dynamic stability.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Imagine that you trim the airplane for hands- off control in level flight and then momentarily give the controls a slight push to nose the airplane down. If within a brief period the nose rises to the original position and then stops, the airplane is statically stable. It is positively statically stable because it has returned to its original position after being displaced. Answer (A) is incorrect. Positive dynamic stability refers to oscillations being dampened or decreasing. Answer (C) is incorrect. Neutral dynamic stability refers to oscillations continuing without a tendency to increase or decrease.

questions.question_text: The engine thrustline
questions.choice_a: has very limited influence on flight stability.
questions.choice_b: has no effect on stability in turbojets or fanjets.
questions.choice_c: canbe stabilizing or destabilizing.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: The vertical location of the thrust line defines one of the direct contributions to stability. If the thrustline is below the center of gravity (CG), thrust produces a positive or nose-up moment and the effect is destabilizing. If the thrust line is located above the CG, a negative moment is created and the effect is stabilizing. Answer (A) is incorrect. Engine thrustline can cause an aircraft to be stable or unstable. Answer (B) is incorrect. The vertical location of the thrust line defines one of the direct aaah to stability and is more noticeable in turbojets or anjets.

questions.question_text: Some airplanes can experience compressibility effects at flight speeds well below the speed of sound due to
questions.choice_a: improperly compensated and adjusted trim surfaces.
questions.choice_c: surfaces that produce different local flow velocities.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Every aircraft has a distinct aerodynamic shape that develops lift differently. Local flow velocities over some surfaces will be greater than the flight speed because of the way this airflow is handled. Answer (A) is incorrect. All components of the aircraft are affected by compressibility in some manner, and the effect of the interference between the various surfaces of the aircraft must be considered. However, it is the aerodynamic shape of the surfaces, not the improper adjustments, that typically cause the compressibility effects at speeds below that of sound. Answer (B) is incorrect. Compressibility effects relate to the air pressure and density changes that take place at high speeds, not the structural pressures on the airframe in the event of turbulence.

questions.question_text: The engine’s thrust line above the center of gravity
questions.choice_c: has little effect on the stability of the aircraft.
questions.explanation: If the thrust line is located above the center of gravity, a negative moment is created and the effect is stabilizing. Answer (A) is incorrect. If the thrust line is located above the CG,a negative moment is created and the effect is stabilizing. Answer (C) Is Incorrect. If the thrust line is located above the CG, a negative moment is created and the effect is stabilizing.

questions.question_text: The engine’s thrust line below the center of gravity
questions.choice_c: has little effect on the stability of the aircraft. 5.13 Effects of Rearward CG
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: If the thrust line is below the center of gravity, thrust produces a positive, or nose-up, moment and the effect is destabilizing. Answer (B) is incorrect. The engine’s thrust line below the CG produces a positive, or nose-up, moment, which does not stabilize the aircraft. Answer (C) is incorrect. The engine’s thrust line below the CG produces a positive, or nose-up, moment, which does have an effect on the stability of the aircraft. The vertical location of the thrust line defines one of the direct contributions to stability.

questions.question_text: What are some characteristics of an airplane loaded with the CG at the aft limit?
questions.choice_a: Lowest stall speed, lowest cruise speed, and highest stability.
questions.choice_b: Highest stall speed, highest cruise speed, and least stability.
questions.choice_c: Lowest stall speed, highest cruise speed, and least stability.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: The airplane will stall at a higher speed with a forward CG location. The critical angle of attack is reached at a higher speed due to increased wing loading. Conversely, the airplane will stall at a lower speed with an aft CG loading. The airplane will cruise faster with an aft CG location because of reduced drag. The airplane will become less stable as the CG is moved rearward. Answer (A) is incorrect. An aft CG causes an airplane to have the highest, not lowest, cruise speed and the least, not highest, stability. Answer (B) is incorrect. An aft CG causes the airplane to have the lowest, not highest, stall speed.

questions.question_text: What characteristic should exist if an airplane is loaded to the rear of its CG range?
questions.choice_c: Unstable about the lateral axis.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: The rearward CG limit is largely determined by considerations of stability. When loaded to the rear of its CG limit, an airplane will become unstable about the lateral axis, i.e., longitudinally unstable. At this point when the nose is momentarily pulled up, it may alternately climb and dive, becoming steeper with each oscillation. Answer (A) is incorrect. An aft location of the CG has a greater effect on the longitudinal stability, not the lateral (aileron) controllability. Answer (B) is incorrect. An aft CG has a greater effect on the longitudinal stability, not vertical (rudder) controllability. Answer (A) is the best answer. (PHAK Chap 5) DISCUSSION: An airplane loaded with the CG at the aft limit will fly more efficiently. The drag is reduced because a smaller angle of attack and less downward deflection are required of the horizontal stabilizer. Answer (B) is incorrect. Having an aft CG does not effect the balance of the airplane along its lateral axis. The airplane will fly more efficiently with an aft CG because of the need for less downforce on the horizontal stabilizer and the corresponding decrease in drag. Answer (C) is incorrect. An aft CG allows the airplane to fly more efficiently with less downforce required from the horizontal stabilizer. An aft CG can also create a heavy feel in the longitudinal axis, but this is less exaggerated as long as the CG remains within the aircraft’s limits.

questions.question_text: An airplane loaded with the CG at the aft limit will
questions.choice_b: be very unbalanced in lateral control forces.
questions.choice_c: feel heavy in the longitudinal axis.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: The elevator or stabilator normally produce the down force required to hold the nose up against the nose- down pitching moment of the wing. As the CG moves aft, the amount of down force needed to counter the nose-down pitching moment decreases, therefore the elevator or stabilator does not need to develop as much down force. Answer (B) is incorrect. The further aft CG becomes, the less down force is needed to maintain level flight. This will require less movement and more less pressure on the controls to maintain level flight. Answer (C) is incorrect. The trim would have to be set for a lower nose-up attitude with aft CG however this would not make the controls very heavy.

questions.question_text: At the aft CG limit the horizontal stab and elevator
questions.choice_a: must develop less down force to balance the decreased forward loading.
questions.choice_b: will require less movement to maintain level flight.
questions.choice_c: can be trimmed to closer to neutral and be very heavy on the controls.

questions.question_text: What effect does an uphill runway slope have upon takeoff performance?
questions.choice_c: Decreases takeoff distance.
questions.explanation: The upslope of a runway impedes acceleration, resulting in a longer ground run on takeoff. Answer (B) is incorrect. Indicated airspeed for takeoff remains the same. Answer (C) is incorrect. The takeoff distance is increased, not decreased.

questions.question_text: What effect does landing at high elevation airports have on groundspeed with comparable conditions relative to temperature, wind, and airplane weight?
questions.choice_c: The same as at low elevation.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: An airplane at altitude will land at the same indicated airspeed as at sea level but, because of the reduced density (comparable conditions), the true airspeed (TAS) will be greater. Thus, with a higher TAS, the ground speed will be higher at high elevation airports than at low elevation. Answer (B) is incorrect. At high elevation, there is reduced air density, and thus TAS will increase. As TAS increases, groundspeed will increase, not decrease. Answer (C) is incorrect. Under comparable conditions, TAS will increase and cause the groundspeed to be higher, not the same, at higher elevation.

questions.question_text: Which condition reduces the required runway for takeoff?
questions.choice_c: Increased headwind component.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: The effect of a headwind is to allow the airplane to reach lift-off speed at a lower groundspeed. A headwind that is 10% of the takeoff airspeed will reduce the takeoff distance approximately 19%. Answer (A) is incorrect. Higher-than-recommended airspeed before rotation increases, not reduces, the required runway for takeoff. Answer (B) is incorrect. Lower-than-standard air density increases, not decreases, the required runway for takeoff.

questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 229 on page 237 and Figure 231 on page 238.) Given the following conditions, what is the takeoff climb limit? Airport OAT: 38°C Airport Pressure Altitude: 14 ft. Flaps: fom Engine Bleed for packs: On Anti-ice: Off
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: First find 38°C on the horizontal axis, by estimating 8/10ths of the distance between 30°C and 40°C. Next go straight up to the 0 ft. pressure altitude line (this is the best estimate for 14 ft.) and, at this point, go straight across to the climb limit brake release weight of 136,000 pounds. Answer (B) is incorrect. The climb limit brake release weight is 136,000 pounds. Answer (C) is incorrect. The climb limit brake release weight is 136,000 pounds.

questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 229 on page 237.) Given the following conditions, what is the takeoff field limit weight? Temperature (OAT): 30°C Pressure Altitude: 5,431 ft. Field length available: 12,000 ft. Runway slope, Up: 1% Wind (KTS) Headwind: 20 Engine Bleed for Packs: On Anti-ice: Off
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: On the Takeoff Field Limit Chart, first find 12,000 ft. on the horizontal axis for field length available. Go up to the first reference line, then follow the runway slope line to 1% up. Then go straight up to the next reference line and follow the wind line up to 20 kts. headwind. From this point, draw a line straight up the rest of the chart. Now find 30°C on the horizontal axis for the Airport OAT, and go straight up to the pressure altitude lines and stop halfway between the 5,000 ft. and 6,000 ft. pressure altitude lines. Next go horizontally across to the limit weight reference line, then follow the direction of limit weight lines until you intersect with the line you previously drew straight up the chart, from the headwind lines. From the intersection of these two lines, go horizontally across to find the field length limit brake release weight of 160,000 pounds. _ Answer (B) is incorrect. The field length limit brake release weight is 160,000 pounds. Answer (C) is incorrect. The field length limit brake release weight is 160,000 pounds.

questions.question_text: Which performance factor decreases as airplane gross weight increases, for a given runway?
questions.choice_c: Accelerate-stop distance.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Critical engine failure speed (i.e., takeoff decision speed, or V;) is the speed to which the airplane can be accelerated, lose an engine, and then either continue the takeoff with the remaining engine(s) or stop, in the same total runway distance. Critical engine failure speed decreases as the airplane gross weight increases due to the increased mass that must decelerate and stop in the event of a rejected takeoff. Answer (B) is incorrect. An increase in aircraft gross weight usually results in an increase in the rotation speed (Vr). Answer (C) is incorrect. The accelerate-stop distance will increase as the aircraft gross weight increases.

questions.question_text: Under which condition during the landing roll are the main wheel brakes at maximum effectiveness?
questions.choice_a: When wing lift has been reduced.
questions.choice_c: When the wheels are locked and skidding.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: During the landing roll, the main wheel brakes are at maximum effectiveness when wing lift has been reduced and the normal force on the wheels approaches the weight of the airplane. At this point, the braking friction force is at maximum. Answer (B) is incorrect. At high groundspeeds, the lift is greater and the normal force on the wheels is small; thus, the braking friction force is small. Answer (C) is incorrect. When the wheels are locked and skidding, the braking friction force is small.

questions.question_text: Arriving over the runway threshold 100 ft. high (150 HAT) would add approximately how many feet to the landing distance?
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: A common approach error is excessive height over the threshold. This could be the result of an unstable approach or a stable but high approach. It also may occur during an instrument approach where the missed approach point is close to or at the runway threshold. Excessive height over the threshold will most likely result in a touchdown beyond the normal aiming point. An extra 50 feet of height over the threshold will add approximately 1,000 feet to the landing distance. It is essential that the airplane arrive at the approach threshold window exactly on altitude (50 feet above the runway). Answer (A) is incorrect. Arriving over the runway threshold 50 feet high would cause an added 1,000 feet of landing distance. Answer (B) is incorrect. Arriving over the runway threshold 75 feet high would cause an added 1,500 feet of landing distance.

questions.question_text: Approaching the runway 1° below glidepath can add how many feet to the landing distance?
questions.choice_c: 1,000 feet.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: If the pilot crosses the threshold at an angle shallower than the normal 3°, the shallow approach angle effectively increases the landing distance and should be avoided. An approach angle of 2° instead of a recommended 3° will add 500 feet to landing distance. Answer (A) is incorrect. Approaching the runway 0.5° below glidepath, not 1° below glidepath, can add approximately 250 feet to the landing distance. Answer (C) is incorrect. Approaching the runway 2° below glidepath, not 1° below glidepath, can add approximately 1,000 feet to the landing distance.

questions.question_text: Arriving over the runway 10 knots over Vrer would add approximately how many feet to the dry landing distance?
questions.choice_c: 2,800 feet.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Ten knots of excess airspeed will delay touchdown by approximately 2,500 feet due to floating in the flare and add approximately 300 feet to the ground roll after touchdown on a dry runway. Thus, the total amount added to the dry landing distance would be approximately 2,800 feet. Answer (A) is incorrect. Arriving over the runway 3 knots over Veer, Not 10 knots, would add approximately 800 feet to the dry landing distance. Answer (B) is incorrect. Arriving over the runway 6 knots over Vrer, Not 10 knots, would add approximately 1,700 feet to the dry landing distance.

questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 232 below.) Given the following conditions, what is the takeoff climb limit? Airport OAT: 30°C Airport Pressure Altitude: 5431 ft. Flaps: sy Engine Bleed for packs: On Anti-ice: Off
questions.choice_c: 120,000 Ib. Takeoff Climb Limit Flaps 25 Based on engine bleed for packs on and anti-ice off 150 co a =] So (=) = ~ <= © uw S Lu ” < W | WwW a Ww ; a = = _ a = —! O With engine bleed for packs off, increase weight by 2300 Ib. With engine anti-ice on, decrease weight by 400 Ib. With engine and wing anti-ice on, decrease weight by 2300 Ib. (optional system).
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Find 30°C on the horizontal axis. Go straight up and estimate 5,431 ft., halfway between the 5,000 ft. and 6,000 ft. pressure altitude lines. Now go straight across to the climb limit brake release weight of 116,000 pounds. Answer (B) is incorrect. The climb limit brake release weight is 116,000 pounds. Answer (C) is incorrect. The climb limit brake release weight is 116,000 pounds. _\VANAAW ALA AAA AV VAAKYA 3 4oN =AIRPORT PRESSUR 60 AIRPORT OAT Figure 232. — Takeoff Climb Limit.

questions.question_text: You are rolling out after touchdown and decide you really need to abort your landing, and takeoff. Your airplane is at 116 knots and your engines have spooled down to 71% idle. You need a V, of 142 to safely lift off and climb. The airplane will require 6 seconds to accelerate after the engines spool up to takeoff thrust, which requires 4 seconds. How much runway will you require for a safe landing abort from your decision point? (Use an average of 129 knots ground speed.)
questions.choice_c: 3,601 feet.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Given that 1 NM = 6,076.1 feet, use the following formula to determine the distance covered during the 10 seconds required for the engines to spool up to takeoff thrust and for the airplane to accelerate to V,: (Average speed in knots) x 6,076.1 feet per NM 3,600 seconds per hour x (Number of seconds) = Distance in feet feb nee x 10 = 2,177.3 feet Answer (A) is incorrect. A runway length of 1,738 feet would be required if 8 seconds, not 10 seconds, were required for the engines to spool up to takeoff thrust and for the airplane to accelerate to V2. Answer (C) is incorrect. A runway length of 3,601 feet would be required if 16.5 seconds, not 10 seconds, were required for the engines to spool up to takeoff thrust and for the airplane to accelerate to V5.

questions.question_text: Excessive takeoff speeds may result in approximately a
questions.choice_a: 4% takeoff distance increase for each 1% of additional takeoff speed.
questions.choice_b: 1% takeoff distance increase for each 2% of additional takeoff speed.
questions.choice_c: 2% takeoff distance increase for each 1% of additional takeoff speed.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Takeoff distance varies with the square of takeoff velocity if one assumes acceleration is constant. Rudimentary physics uses the following equation to determine the relationship between velocity, acceleration, and distance for uniformly accelerated objects. S = Acceleration distance in feet V = Velocity in feet per second, after uniformly accelerating from a velocity of zero Acceleration in feet per second I a Assume the aircraft has a takeoff velocity of 150 kt. (253.5 feet per second) with an acceleration of .2 g (6.434 feet per second). A 1% increase in takeoff velocity is computed by multiplying the takeoff velocity by 0.01. 253.5 x .01 = 2.53 203:5 + 2:93'= 296.03 Now compute the new takeoff distance. 256.037 65,551.36 Sr = 1(gi4a4)). Ti TE BOB TS eee Takeoff distance increased by 100 feet (2%). 100 + 4,994 = .02 Answer (A) is incorrect. A 4% increase in takeoff speed would result in a 4% increase in takeoff distance. Answer (B) is incorrect. A 1% increase in takeoff speed would result in a 2% increase in takeoff distance.

questions.question_text: You touchdown long with a speed of 145 knots on a 9,001 foot runway and the braking is not working, so you decide to takeoff and climbout. The engines require 5 seconds to spool up and then the airplane requires 10 seconds of acceleration to liftoff again. The 5,000 foot markers flash by. Do you have enough runway to liftoff? (Use 142 knots for average groundspeed due to the tailwind.)
questions.choice_a: Yes, there will be a margin of about 850 feet with almost 3 seconds of decision time.
questions.choice_b: Yes, there will be a margin of almost 1,401 feet which allows about 5.8 seconds of decision time.
questions.choice_c: No, the runway is 1,104 feet too short and my decision is about 3 seconds too late.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Given that 1 NM = 6,076.1 feet, use the following formula to determine the distance covered during the 15 seconds required for the engines to spool up to takeoff thrust and for the airplane to accelerate to V.: (Average speed in knots) x 6,076.1 feet per NM 3,600 seconds per hour x (Number of seconds) = Distance in feet [(142 x 6,076.1) + 3,600] x 15 = 3,595 feet Since 5,000 ft. of runway remains, you have a margin of about 1,405 ft. of runway length. At the speed you are moving down the runway, you will have a little less than 6 seconds of time to make and implement this decision. Answer (A) is incorrect. This answer would require approximately 17 seconds of delay instead of 15 seconds. Answer (C) is incorrect. This answer would require an average speed of 242 kt. instead of 142 kt.

questions.question_text: One typical takeoff error is
questions.choice_a: delayed rotation which may extend the climb distance.
questions.choice_b: premature rotation which may increase takeoff distance.
questions.choice_c: extended rotation which may degrade acceleration.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Premature rotation may increase takeoff distance. If the aircraft does not have sufficient airspeed to fly, it may settle back onto the runway. Answer (A) is incorrect. Delayed rotation will result in a higher takeoff speed, not an extended climb distance. Answer (C) is incorrect. An extended rotation does not degrade acceleration, although over-rotation can create additional drag that can degrade acceleration. Establishing an appropriate attitude for V, or Vy will allow the aircraft to safely leave ground effect and accelerate normally.

questions.question_text: During takeoff
questions.choice_a: pitch control is a relatively minor skill requirement.
questions.choice_b: pitch rotation is not an important factor in performance calculations for distance and obstacle clearances.
questions.choice_c: insufficient pitch rotation may result in increased rolling resistance and excessive landing gear speeds.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Insufficient rotation may provide added rolling resistance or require that the airplane accelerate to some excessive speed prior to becoming airborne. Answer (A) is incorrect. Any item which alters the takeoff velocity or acceleration during takeoff roll will affect the takeoff distance. Having the proper skillset to rotate appropriately is an important skill requirement. Answer (B) is incorrect. Pitch rotation is an important factor and technique for takeoff performance.

questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 230 on page 243.) Given the following conditions, what is the takeoff field limit weight? Temperature (OAT): 38°C Pressure Altitude: 14 ft. Field length available: 9,401 ft. Runway slope: 0% Wind (KTS) Tailwind: 3 Engine bleed for packs: On Anti-ice: Off
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: On the Takeoff Field Limit - Dry Runway chart, first find 9,400 ft. on the horizontal axis for Field Length Available. Go straight up to the first reference line and, as there is no runway slope, continue straight up to the second reference line for wind correction. Follow the tailwind line back to 3 kts. (approximately 1/4 of the distance from 0 to —10 kts. tailwind). Now draw a line straight up the chart. Next find 38°C on the horizontal axis for Airport OAT by estimating 8/10ths of the distance between 30°C and 40°C. From here, go straight up to the 0 ft. pressure altitude line (this is the best estimate for 14 ft.). Then go horizontally across to the limit weight reference line and follow the direction of limit weight lines until you intersect with the line you previously drew straight up the chart from the tailwind lines. From the intersection of these two lines, go horizontally across to find the field length limit brake release weight of 169,000 pounds. - _ Answer (A) is incorrect. The field length limit brake release weight is 169,000 Ib. Answer (C) is incorrect. The field length limit brake release weight is 169,000 Ib.

questions.question_text: A landing weight increase of 10% will result in a
questions.choice_a: 7%. would result from a landing weight increase of about 7%, not B 9 10%. Answer (C) is incorrect. An increase of 15% landing OY: y Secs distance would result from a landing weight increase of about
questions.explanation: landing distance increase of approximately DISCUSSION: A 10% increase in landing weight will result in an increase in landing distance by 10%. x Answer (A) is incorrect. An increase of 7% landing distance

questions.question_text: A landing weight increase of 10% will result in
questions.choice_c: 33% increase in kinetic energy.
questions.explanation: A 10% increase in landing weight will produce a 21% increase in the kinetic energy of the airplane that must be dissipated during the landing roll. Answer (A) is incorrect. A 3% increase in landing weight will produce about a 7% increase in the kinetic energy of the airplane that must be dissipated during the landing roll. Answer (C) is incorrect. A 16% increase in landing weight will produce a 33% increase in the kinetic energy of the airplane that must be dissipated during the landing roll.

questions.question_text: Friction braking becomes effective at
questions.choice_c: 70-80% of touchdown speed.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: In the initial high speed part of the landing roll, maximum deceleration would be obtained by creating the greatest possible aerodynamic drag. By the time the aircraft has slowed to 70 or 80% of the touchdown speed, aerodynamic drag decays but braking action will then be effective. Answer (A) is incorrect. Braking action becomes effective between 70 and 80% of touchdown speed. Answer (B) is incorrect. Aerodynamic drag does not decay enough for effective braking action until the aircraft has slowed to 70 or 80% of the touchdown speed.

questions.question_text: Aerodynamic braking is only effective to approximately
questions.choice_c: 60% to 70% of touchdown speed.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Aerodynamic drag will be of importance only for the initial 20% to 30% of speed reduction from the point of touchdown. Answer (B) is incorrect. At 40% to 50% of touchdown speed, aerodynamic drag has no consequence to braking. Answer (C) is incorrect. At speeds less than 60% to 70% of the landing speed, aerodynamic drag is of little consequence, and brakes will be the principal source of deceleration regardless of the runway surface.

questions.question_text: For airplanes with relatively low thrust-to-weight ratios, a 10% increase in takeoff gross weight would result in a takeoff distance increase of approximately
questions.choice_c: 25 to 30% or more.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: For the airplane that has a 10% takeoff gross weight, with a relatively low thrust-to-weight ratio, the result in the increase in takeoff distance would be approximately 25% to 30%. Answer (A) is incorrect. For the airplane with a 5% takeoff gross weight, with a relatively low thrust-to-weight ratio, the result in the increase in takeoff distance would be approximately 14%. Answer (B) is incorrect. For the airplane with an 8% takeoff gross weight, with a relatively low thrust-to-weight ratio, the result in the increase in takeoff distance would be approximately 23%.

questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 233 on page 245.) Given the following conditions, what is the obstacle climb limit? Obstacle height: 522i: Distance from brake release: 9000 ft. OAT: 38°C Pressure Altitude: 427 ft. Wind (Kts.) Headwind: 8 kts. Flaps: os Engine Bleed for packs: On Anti-ice: Off

Go straight across to 9,000 ft. for distance from brake release, halfway between the 8,000 ft. and 10,000 ft. curves. From this point, go straight up to the reference line for OAT and follow the temperature line up to 38°C by estimating 8/10ths of the distance from 30°C to 40°C. Now go straight up to the pressure altitude reference line and follow the pressure altitude line to approximately 427 ft., halfway between 0 ft. and 1,000 feet. From here, go straight up to the wind reference line and follow the headwind line up to 8 kts., just before the 10 kt. line. Go straight up to the obstacle limit weight of 92,500 pounds. Answer (B) is incorrect. The obstacle limit weight is 92,500 pounds. Answer (C) is incorrect. The obstacle limit weight is 92,500 pounds.

questions.question_text: A headwind that is 10% of the landing speed will decrease the landing distance
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: A headwind which is 10% of the landing airspeed will reduce the landing distance 19%. Answer (A) is incorrect. A headwind which is 5% of the landing airspeed will reduce the landing distance by hie approximately 11%. Answer (C) is incorrect. A headwind which is 15% of the landing airspeed will reduce the landing distance by approximately 24%.

questions.subsection: 0.50 inches, not 0.25 inches. Answer (B) is incorrect. This weight
questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 235 on page 249 and Figure 236 on page 250.) Given the following conditions, what is the maximum Slush/Standing Water takeoff weight? Dry field/obstacle limit weight: 180,000 Ib. Slush/standing water depth: .25 inches Temperature (OAT): 30°C Field pressure altitude: 5431 ft. Field length available: 9000 ft. No reverse thrust
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Go to Fig. 235 (no reverse thrust), for Slush/ Standing Water Takeoff with no reverse thrust. On the first table for weight adjustments, find 180 (1,000 Ib.) in the first column. Go across to the weights under 0.25 inches. Interpolate for 5,500 ft. to find a weight adjustment of -40.15. To find the Slush/ Standing Water Takeoff limit weight, subtract 40.15 (1,000 Ib.) from 180 (1,000 Ib.): 180,000 — 40,150 = 139,850 pounds. Now check the V, (MCG) Limit Weight on the second table. First you must calculate the adjusted field length for an OAT of 30°C, which is 9,000 — 750 = 8,250 feet. On the V,; (MCG) table, you must interpolate for 5,500 ft. pressure altitude for each of 8,200 ft. and 8,600 ft. field length to find 137.86 (1,000 Ib.) and 159.32 (1,000 Ib.). Now interpolate these two weights for 8,250 ft. adjusted field length to find 140.54 (1,000 Ib.), or 140,540 pounds. The lesser of the two weights is 139,850 Ib., so this is the maximum Slush/Standing Water takeoff weight. Answer (A) is incorrect. This weight would result if you used 0.50 inches, not 0.25 inches. Answer (B) is incorrect. This weight would result if you used the Slush/Standing Water Takeoff Chart for Maximum Reverse Thrust, not No Reverse Thrust.

questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 235 on page 249 and Figure 236 on page 250.) Given the following conditions, what is the maximum Slush/Standing Water takeoff weight? Dry field/obstacle limit weight: 180,000 Ib. Slush/standing water depth: .50 inches Temperature (OAT): 30°C Field pressure altitude: 54371 ft. Field length available: 9000 ft. Maximum reverse thrust
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Go to Fig. 236 (maximum reverse thrust), on the Slush/Standing Water Takeoff chart, find 180 (1,000 Ib.) in the first column of the first table for weight adjustments. Go across and find the weights for 5,000 ft. and 10,000 ft., under 0.5 inches. Interpolate for 5,500 ft. to find -43.55 (1,000 Ib.). To find the slush/standing water takeoff limit weight, subtract 43,550 Ib. from 180,000, giving a weight of 136,450 pounds. Now check the V, (MCG) Limit weight. First you must calculate the adjusted field length available for an OAT of 30°C, which is 9,000 — 750 = 8,250 feet. On the V, (MCG) table, interpolate the weights for 5,000 ft. pressure altitude and 10,000 ft. pressure altitude for each of 8,200 ft. and 8,600 ft. adjusted field length (note for 5,000 ft. pressure altitude and 8,600 ft., there is no weight limit listed, so you should use the Dry field/obstacle limit weight) to find 186.69 (1,000 Ib.) and 178.54 (1,000 Ib). As these figures are both above 136,450 Ib., the weight for V, (MCG) is not the limiting factor and the maximum slush/standing water takeoff ° weight is 136,450 pounds. Answer (A) is incorrect. This weight would result if you used 0.25 inches, not 0.50 inches. Answer (C) is incorrect. The maximum slush/standing water takeoff weight is 136,450, not 138,550 pounds.

questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 237 on page 253 and Figure 238 on page 254.) Given the following conditions, what are the takeoff V speeds? Weight: 170,000 Ib. Flaps: 15° Temperature (OAT): 16°C Field pressure altitude: 5431 ft. Runway slope: 1% Wind (KTS) Headwind: 10 KTS Runway condition: Dry Runway For Vp: More than or equal to .1 Ve = round up Vp (ex: 140 .1 = 141)
questions.choice_a: V; 139 kts., Ve 137 kts., V2 141 kts.
questions.choice_b: V; 138 kts., Vp 138 kts., V2 141 kts.
questions.choice_c: V; 137 kts., Ve 137 kts., V2 140 kts.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Go to Fig. 237 (Takeoff Speeds — Dry Runway), enter the Takeoff Speeds table at 170,000 Ib. and go across to Flaps 15 to find V; 136, Vz 136, and V2 141. Find the adjustments in the second table by entering the table at 16°C and interpolating for 5,500 ft. pressure altitude. Rounding up for any Vp or above, gives V, 139, Vp 138, and V2 141. Adjusting V;, for runway slope and wind gives V, 140; however, V, cannot exceed Ve, SO V; is 138, Ve is 138, and V2 is 141. Answer (A) is incorrect. These speeds would result if you did not adjust V, to be equal to Vp (i.e., it cannot be higher), and Ve is not rounded up after interpolating for 5,500 ft. pressure altitude. Answer (C) is incorrect. These speeds would result if you only adjusted V, for runway slope and wind. Vp and V2 are not rounded up after interpolating for 5,500 ft. pressure altitude.

questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 237 on page 253 and Figure 238 on page 254.) Given the following conditions, what are the takeoff V speeds? Weight: 170,000 Ib. Flaps: 10° Temperature (OAT): PAS Field pressure altitude: 427 ft. Runway slope: 0% Wind (KTS) headwind: 8 KTS Runway condition: Wet Runway For Vg more than or equal to .1 Vp, round up Vp to the next value (example: 140 + .1 = 141)
questions.choice_a: V; 134 kts., Ve 140 kts., V2 145 kts.
questions.choice_b: V, 140 kts., Vz 140 kts., V2 145 kts.
questions.choice_c: V;, 138 kts., Vp 141 kts., V2 145 kts.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Go to Fig. 238 (Takeoff Speeds — Wet Runway), enter the Takeoff Speeds Chart at 170,000 Ib. and, under Flaps 10, find V, 133 kts., Ve 139 kts., and V2 145 knots. Enter the adjustments table at 27°C and interpolate for 500 feet. Round down to 133 kts. for V;, up to 140 kts. for Vp (must always round up if .1 or above) and no adjustment for V, 145 knots. No adjustments are necessary for runway slope, but one knot should be added for wind effects. The final speeds are V, 134 kts., Ve 140 kts., and V2 145 knots. Answer (B) is incorrect. Speeds for Vp and V2 are correct; however, V; is 134 kts., not 140 knots. Answer (C) is incorrect. V, is 134 kts., not 138 kts., and Ve is 140 kts., not 141 knots.

questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 240 above.) Given the following conditions, what is the stab trim setting? Weight: 160,000 Ib. CG (%MAC): 24% Flaps: alate
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: On the Stab Trim Setting chart, go to the second table for Flaps 10, 15, and 25. Find the weight row of 160-180 (1,000 Ib.) and go across to 24% MAC, where you will come to 5 1/2%. Answer (A) is incorrect. This percentage would result if you used the table for Flaps 1 and 5, not Flaps 15. Answer (C) is incorrect. This percentage would result if you used 30% MAC, not 24% MAC.

questions.question_text: Which maximum range factor decreases as weight decreases?
questions.choice_c: Airspeed.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: For propeller-driven airplanes, maximum range is obtained at maximum lift-drag (L/D) ratio, the point at which the proportion between speed and power required is greatest. As weight decreases, so does this optimum airspeed due to a reduction in the maximum L/D. Additionally, for jet aircraft, airspeed must be reduced as weight decreases to obtain maximum range. Answer (A) is incorrect. The factors of maximum range are weight, altitude, and aerodynamic configuration of the airplane, not angle of attack. Answer (B) is incorrect. Maximum range altitude may increase, not decrease, with a decrease in weight.

questions.question_text: What should a pilot do to maintain “best range” airplane performance when a tailwind is encountered?
questions.choice_c: Decrease speed.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: A pilot flying an airplane in a tailwind should decrease speed to take advantage of the wind. In a headwind, the pilot should increase speed to reduce the effect of the wind. Answer (A) is incorrect. A pilot would increase speed with a headwind, not a tailwind. Answer (B) is incorrect. A pilot would maintain speed in a calm wind, not tailwind, condition.

questions.question_text: Which procedure produces the minimum fuel consumption for a given leg of the cruise flight?
questions.choice_c: Increase altitude for a headwind, decrease altitude for a tailwind.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Airplanes flying in a headwind should fly at higher airspeeds to reduce the effect of the headwind. Conversely, airplanes flying in a tailwind should slow to take advantage of the wind with lower power settings. Answer (B) is incorrect. Tailwind increases performance, and slower TAS decreases fuel consumption. Answer (C) is incorrect. Airspeed, not altitude, should be adjusted according to the wind.

questions.question_text: At what speed, with reference to L/Dyax, does maximum range for a jet airplane occur?
questions.choice_a: A speed less than that for L/Dmax.
questions.choice_b: A speed equal to that for L/Dmax.
questions.choice_c: A speed greater than that for L/Dmax.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Maximum range for a jet airplane occurs when the proportion between velocity and thrust required is greatest, and this point is at a speed greater than that for L/Dmax. Answer (A) is incorrect. A speed greater, not less, than L/Dyaxwill obtain maximum range for a jet airplane. Answer (B) is incorrect. A speed equal to that of L/Dyax is a jet airplane's maximum endurance, not maximum range.

questions.question_text: Maximum range performance of a turbojet aircraft is obtained by which procedure as aircraft weight reduces?
questions.choice_c: Increasing speed or decreasing altitude.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Maximum range is the consideration of maximum flying distance. “Cruise control” of an airplane implies that the airplane is operated to maintain the recommended long- range cruise condition throughout the flight. As fuel is consumed, the gross weight will vary and optimum airspeed, altitude, and power setting can vary. As the gross weight decreases, the optimum airspeed and power may decrease, or the optimum altitude may increase. Answer (A) is incorrect. As weight decreases, the optimum speed decreases, not increases, or altitude increases. Answer (C) is incorrect. As weight decreases, speed decreases, not increases, or altitude increases, not decreases.

questions.question_text: The increase in specific range with altitude of the turbojet airplane can be attributed to three factors. One of those factors is
questions.choice_a: an increase in altitude in the troposphere results in higher energy air flow.
questions.choice_b: an increase in proportion of velocity verses thrust required.
questions.choice_c: decreased engine turbine speeds.
questions.explanation: An increase in altitude will increase the proportion of V/Tr and provide a greater TAS for the same Tr. Answer (A) is incorrect. An increase in altitude in the troposphere will produce lower inlet air temperature and air flow. Answer (C) is incorrect. An increase in altitude increases the engine RPM speed.

questions.question_text: The most important factor affecting the specific range of a turbojet airplane is
questions.choice_c: the decrease of the proportion of V/Tr (where V = velocity and Tr = thrust required).
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: The effect of altitude on the range of the turbojet airplane is of great importance because no other single factor can cause such large variations of a specific range. Answer (B) is incorrect. A decrease in RPM is the result of altitude, not a factor affecting the range. There will be a decrease in engine RPM at altitude, but it will require an increase in engine RPM or the same amount of thrust. Answer (C) is incorrect. There will actually be an increase in the proportion of V/Tr and a greater TAS for the same Tr.

questions.question_text: The specific fuel consumption of a turbojet climbing above the tropopause
questions.choice_a: will continue to decrease as altitude increases.
questions.choice_b: will remain constant as altitude increases.
questions.choice_c: will increase as altitude increases.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Altitudes much above the tropopause bring about a general deterioration of overall engine efficiency and the specific fuel consumption begins to increase with altitude. Answer (A) is incorrect. Above the tropopause, the temperature is constant and altitudes slightly above the tropopause cause no further decrease in fuel consumption. In fact, fuel consumption will begin to increase with altitude. Answer (B) is incorrect. Generally, the specific fuel consumption decreases steadily with altitude until the tropopause is reached. Fuel consumption tends to increase above the tropopause.

questions.question_text: The most efficient cruise control in a turbojet is
questions.choice_c: Constant altitude cruise.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: The climbing cruise takes advantage of the benefits of optimum altitudes.during cruise and results in a more efficient flight path. Answer (B) is incorrect. A flight idle descent is very efficient; however, it would not provide a usable cruise. Answer (C) is incorrect. A constant altitude cruise requires a reduction of airspeed and engine thrust to maintain optimum lift. It does not take advantage of the higher altitudes rendering it less efficient than the climbing cruise.

questions.question_text: The specific range for a turbojet at 40,000 feet is approximately
questions.choice_a: 150% greater than obtained at sea level.
questions.choice_b: 150% greater than obtained at ground level.
questions.choice_c: 125% greater than obtained at sea level.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: The typical turbojet airplane obtains a specific range at 40,000 feet, which is approximately 150% greater than that obtained at sea level. Answer (B) is incorrect. This performance is measured above sea level, not above ground level. Answer (C) is incorrect. At 40,000 feet, the turbojet will have a 150%, not 125%, higher specific range than at sea level.

questions.question_text: At 3,000 feet with a temperature of 95°F anda calibrated airspeed of 100 knots, your true airspeed is
questions.choice_c: 5 knots higher. 5.16 Propeller Pitch and Engine Performance
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Using an E6B flight computer, convert 95°F to 35°C from the temperature conversion scale. Line up 3,000 feet pressure altitude with 35°C in the pressure altitude window. Find calibrated airspeed of 100 on the outer scale of the inner wheel. This will be indicated as the number 10. Read the true airspeed above 10. It should be lined up almost with 109. The closest answer is 10 knots higher than the calibrated airspeed of 100 knots. Answer (A) is incorrect. A true airspeed of 15 knots higher would require a calibrated airspeed of 105 knots. Answer (C) is incorrect. A true airspeed of 5 knots higher would require a calibrated airspeed of 96 knots.

questions.question_text: What is the reason for variations in geometric pitch along a propeller or rotor blade?
questions.choice_a: \t permits a relatively constant angle of attack along its length when in cruising flight.
questions.choice_b: It prevents the portion of the blade near the hub or root from stalling during cruising flight.
questions.choice_c: It permits a relatively constant angle of incidence along its length when in cruising flight.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Variations in the geometric pitch of the blades permit the propeller to operate with a relatively constant angle of attack along its length when in cruising flight. Propeller blades have variations to change the blade in proportion to the differences in speed of rotation along the length of the propeller and thereby keep thrust more nearly equalized along this length. Answer (B) is incorrect. If there were no variation in geometric pitch, the propeller tips, not the root, would be stalled during cruising flight. Answer (C) is incorrect. Variations in geometric pitch permit a constant angle of attack, not incidence, along its length.

questions.question_text: What effect does high relative humidity have upon the maximum power output of modern aircraft engines?
questions.choice_a: Neither turbojet nor reciprocating engines are affected.
questions.choice_b: Reciprocating engines will experience a significant loss of BHP.
questions.choice_c: Turbojet engines will experience a significant loss of thrust.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Water vapor, i.e., high relative humidity, will cause a significant reduction of power output of reciprocating engines but a negligible loss of thrust for a turbojet engine. Answer (A) is incorrect. Both reciprocating and turbojet engines are affected by high relative humidity. Answer (C) is incorrect. Turbojet engines will have a negligible, not significant, loss of thrust.

questions.question_text: Under normal operating conditions, which combination of MAP and RPM produce the most severe wear, fatigue, and damage to high performance reciprocating engines?
questions.choice_c: High RPM and high MAP.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: The most severe rate of wear and fatigue damage occurs at high RPM and low MAP on engines found on transport category airplanes, such as a DC-6. High RPM produces high centrifugal loads and reciprocating inertia loads. Thus, operating time at maximum RPM and MAP must be held to a minimum, and operation at maximum RPM and low MAP must be avoided. See page 145 of Aerodynamics for Naval Aviators. Note that this is the opposite from what we learn as commercial pilots regarding current high-performance engines. Answer (B) is incorrect. High, not low, RPM and low, not high, MAP produce the most severe wear to high-performance reciprocating engines. Answer (C) is incorrect. A high RPM and a low, not high, MAP produce the most severe wear to high- performance reciprocating engines.

questions.question_text: Where is the critical altitude of a supercharged- reciprocating engine?
questions.choice_a: The highest altitude at which a desired manifold pressure can be obtained.
questions.choice_b: Highest altitude where the mixture can be leaned to best power ratio.
questions.choice_c: The altitude at which maximum allowable BMEP can be obtained.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Because the density of air decreases as altitude increases, the supercharger compresses the thin air to maintain the preset power (manifold pressure) as the airplane climbs. When the critical altitude is reached, the turbine is rotating at its highest speed and can no longer compensate for decreasing manifold pressure after that altitude is exceeded. Answer (B) is incorrect. Critical altitude is the highest altitude at which a manifold pressure, not fuel mixture, can be obtained. Answer (C) is incorrect. BMEP is pressure representing the mean gas load on the piston during the power stroke, not the air pressure in the manifold.

questions.question_text: What is controlled by the waste gate of a turbocharged-reciprocating engine?
questions.choice_c: Throttle opening. 5.18 Multi-Engine Airplane Operation
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: The waste gate allows exhaust gas to be directed to drive the turbine of the turbocharger. The waste gate is normally controlled automatically to maintain the required amount of exhaust gas flow so the turbocharger can maintain the desired manifold pressure. At the critical altitude, the exhaust gas waste gate is fully closed, and the turbine is operating at maximum speed. Answer (A) is incorrect. The waste gate controls exhaust gas discharge, not a supercharger gear ratio. Answer (C) is incorrect. The throttle opening sets the desired manifold pressure; it does not operate a waste gate that is part of the turbocharger system.

questions.question_text: Under what condition should stalls never be practiced in a twin-engine airplane?
questions.choice_c: With full flaps and gear extended.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: The pilot of a twin-engine airplane should become familiar with imminent stalls entered with various flap settings, power settings, and landing gear positions. Because of possible loss of control, stalls with one engine inoperative or at idle power and the other developing effective power are not to be practiced. Answer (B) is incorrect. You may be required to practice stalls with climb power on. Answer (C) is incorrect. Stalls should be practiced with full flaps and gear extended, as in a stall on approach to landing.

questions.question_text: What does the blue radial line on the airspeed indicator of a light, twin-engine airplane represent?
questions.choice_c: Minimum controllable airspeed for single- engine operation.
questions.explanation: The airspeed indicator of a light, twin- engine airplane is marked (in addition to other normally marked airspeeds) with a red radial line at the minimum controllable airspeed with the critical engine inoperative, and a blue radial line at the best rate-of-climb airspeed with one engine inoperative. Answer (B) is incorrect. The blue radial line is the maximum single-engine rate, not angle, of climb. Answer (C) is incorrect. Minimum controllable airspeed for single-engine operation is marked by a red, not blue, radial line.

questions.question_text: What performance should a pilot of a light, twin- engine airplane be able to maintain at Vic?
questions.choice_c: Heading, altitude, and ability to climb 50 ft./ min.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Vy represents the minimum controllable airspeed with a critical engine inoperative. FAA regulations require that, in an airplane at Vic, the pilot must at least be able to stop the turn that results when the critical engine is suddenly made inoperative within 20° of the original heading using max rudder deflection and a max bank of 5° into the operative engine. Also, after recovery, the pilot must maintain the airplane in Straight flight with not more than 5° bank wing lowered toward the operating engine. This does not mean that the airplane must be able to climb or even to hold altitude. It means only that a heading can be maintained. Answer (B) is incorrect. The ability to maintain altitude is not a requirement of Vic. Answer (C) is incorrect. The ability to hold altitude and climb is not a requirement of Vic.

questions.question_text: What effect, if any, does altitude have on Vc for an airplane with unsupercharged engines?
questions.choice_c: Decreases with altitude.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: For an airplane with unsupercharged engines, Vyc decreases as altitude is increased; i.e., directional control can be maintained at a lower airspeed than at sea level. Vue is a function of power, which decreases with altitude. It is possible for the airplane to reach a stall speed prior to the loss of directional control. Answer (A) is incorrect. Vic is always affected by altitude. ye (B) is incorrect. Vic decreases, not increases, with altitude.

questions.question_text: Under what condition is Vic the highest?
questions.choice_a: Gross weight is at the maximum allowable value.
questions.choice_b: CG is at the most rearward allowable position.
questions.choice_c: CG is at the most forward allowable position.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Vc is greater when the center of gravity is at the most rearward allowable position. Since the airplane rotates around its center of gravity, the moments are measured using that point as a reference. A rearward CG would not affect the thrust moment but would shorten the arm to the center of the rudder’s horizontal lift, which would mean that a higher force or airspeed would be required to provide the necessary rudder effectiveness. Answer (A) is incorrect. Maximum gross weight decreases, not increases, Vc. Answer (C) is incorrect. A forward CG increases rudder effectiveness and reduces Vyc.

questions.question_text: What is the resulting performance loss when one engine on a twin-engine airplane fails?
questions.choice_a: Reduction of cruise airspeed by 50 percent.
questions.choice_b: Reduction of climb performance of 80-90 percent or more.
questions.choice_c: Reduction of all performance by 50 percent.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: When one engine fails on a light twin, the most obvious problem is the loss of 50% of power. When power is increased in both engines in level flight and airspeed is held constant, the airplane will climb at a rate depending on the power added. When one engine fails, however, not only does it lose power, but the drag increases considerably because of asymmetric thrust. The operating engine must carry the full burden alone, and climb performance may be reduced by 80-90% or more. Answer (A) is incorrect. The power loss affects climb capability much more than it does cruise speed. Answer (C) is incorrect. Power is reduced by 50%, climb performance is reduced by 80-90% or more.

questions.question_text: Which engine is the “critical” engine of a twin- engine airplane?
questions.choice_a: The one with the center of thrust closest to the centerline of the fuselage.
questions.choice_b: The one designated by the manufacturer because it develops most usable thrust.
questions.choice_c: The one with the center of thrust farthest from the centerline of the fuselage.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: On twin-engine airplanes, if the propellers rotate in the same direction (most U.S.-designed engines rotate clockwise when viewed from the rear) and both engines develop an equal amount of thrust, the asymmetric propeller thrust, or “P-factor,” results in a center of thrust at the right side of each engine. The turning (or yawing) force of the right engine is greater than the left engine because the center of thrust is much farther away from the centerline of the fuselage; it has a longer arm. Thus, when the right engine is operative and the left engine is inoperative, the turning (or yawing) force is greater than with a good left engine and an inoperative right engine.
