Directional control may be difficult when the left (or critical) engine is suddenly inoperative. Answer (B) is incorrect. The critical engine of a twin- engine airplane is the one with the center of thrust closer to the centerline of the fuselage (propellers rotate in same direction), not one designated by the manufacturer. Answer (C) is incorrect. The critical engine is the one with the center of thrust closer to, not farther from, the centerline of the fuselage.

questions.question_text: Which engine is the “critical” engine of a twin- engine airplane?
questions.choice_a: The engine whose failure has the most adverse effect on directional control.
questions.choice_b: The engine whose failure has the least adverse effect on directional control.
questions.choice_c: The engine that is operating when used by the manufacturer to determine Vyc.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: On twins with each engine rotating in conventional, clockwise rotation as viewed from the pilot’s seat, the “critical” engine will be the left engine. The descending propeller blade of each engine will produce greater thrust (P-factor) than the ascending blade when the airplane is operated under power and at positive angles of attack. The descending propeller blade of the right engine is also a greater distance from the center of gravity and therefore has a longer moment arm than the descending propeller blade of the left engine. As a result, failure of the left engine will result in the most asymmetrical thrust (adverse yaw). Answer (B) is incorrect. The “critical” engine of a twin-engine airplane is the one whose failure has the most, not least, adverse effect on directional control. Answer (C) is incorrect. The “critical” engine is the engine whose failure will have the most adverse effect on directional control, not the engine that is operating.

questions.question_text: In a light, twin-engine airplane with one engine inoperative, when is it acceptable to allow the ball of a slip-skid indicator to be deflected outside the reference lines?
questions.choice_a: When practicing imminent stalls in a banked attitude of over 60°.
questions.choice_b: While maneuvering at minimum controllable airspeed or less to avoid overbanking.
questions.choice_c: When operating at any airspeed of Vc or greater with only enough deflection to zero the side slip.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Flight tests have shown that holding the ball of the turn coordinator in the center while maintaining heading with wings level increases Vc as much as 20 kt. due to increased drag. Banking into the operative engine sufficiently to achieve zero sideslip (usually 5° or less) reduces Vic and ensures that the airplane will be in a minimum drag configuration for best climb performance. Engine-out flight with the ball centered is never advisable. Answer (A) is incorrect. Stalls should never be practiced with one engine inoperative. Answer (B) is incorrect. With a 5° bank toward the good engine and the ball deflected toward the good engine, the airplane will be in a zero sideslip. There is no tendency for overbanking.

questions.question_text: Which condition has the effect of reducing critical engine failure speed?
questions.choice_a: Slush on the runway or inoperative antiskid.
questions.choice_c: High density altitude.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Slush on the runway may dictate adjustments, such as a reduction in critical engine failure speed (V,), an increase in required runway length, or a penalty in runway limited weight. An inoperative antiskid system may have the same requirements according to the airplane flight manual or company operations manual. Answer (B) is incorrect. Low gross weight may allow an increase in critical engine failure speed. Answer (C) is incorrect. An increase in density altitude requires a higher takeoff velocity (TAS) and critical engine failure speed.

questions.question_text: What is the safest and most efficient takeoff and initial climb procedure in a light, twin-engine airplane? Accelerate to
questions.choice_a: Best engine-out, rate-of-climb airspeed while on the ground, then lift off and climb at that speed.
questions.choice_b: Vyc, then lift off at that speed and climb at maximum angle-of-climb airspeed.
questions.choice_c: An airspeed slightly above Vyc, then lift off and climb at the best rate-of-climb airspeed.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: On normal takeoffs, liftoff should never take place until the airspeed reaches and exceeds Vic. The FAA recommends Vyc 5 kt. before liftoff. The safest and most efficient climb: procedure is one in which the airplane leaves the ground slightly above Vic, accelerates to Vy (best rate of climb), and climbs at Vy for both engines, not single-engine. Answer (A) is incorrect. Liftoff should occur slightly above Vuc, and the climb should be at the best rate-of-climb airspeed for both engines, not for an engine out. Answer (B) is incorrect. Liftoff should occur slightly above Vic, and the climb should be at the best rate, not angle, of climb.

questions.question_text: If an engine failure occurs at an altitude above single-engine ceiling, what airspeed should be maintained? Baie
questions.explanation: When an engine failure occurs at an altitude above the single-engine ceiling, altitude will be lost. In an airplane not capable of maintaining altitude with an engine inoperative under existing circumstances, the airspeed should be maintained at Vyse So as to conserve altitude as long as possible. _ Answer (A) is incorrect. Vyse should be maintained, not the minimum controllable airspeed. Answer (C) is incorrect. The best rate-, not angle-, of-climb airspeed should be maintained.

questions.question_text: What procedure is recommended for an engine- out approach and landing?
questions.choice_a: The flightpath and procedures should be almost identical to a normal approach and landing.
questions.choice_b: The altitude and airspeed should be considerably higher than normal throughout the approach.
questions.choice_c: Anormal approach, except do not extend the landing gear or flaps until over the runway threshold.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: In most light twins, a single-engine approach can be accomplished with the flight path and procedures almost identical to a normal approach and landing. However, neither full flaps nor the landing gear should be extended until the landing is assured. Answer (B) is incorrect. Higher altitude and airspeed result in floating, and unnecessary runway use should be avoided. Answer (C) is incorrect. Landing gear and full flaps should be extended when the landing is ensured, not over the runway threshold.

questions.question_text: Which part(s) in the turbojet engine is subjected to the high temperatures and severe centrifugal forces?
questions.choice_c: Compressor rotor(s) or impeller(s).
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: The turbine wheel is a dynamically balanced unit consisting of blades attached to a rotating disk. The disk, in turn, is attached to the main power transmitting shaft of the engine. The jet gases leaving the turbine nozzle vanes act on the blades of the turbine wheel, causing the assembly to rotate at a very high rate of speed. The high rotational speed imposes severe centrifugal loads on the turbine wheel, and at the same time the elevated temperatures result in a lowering of the strength of the material. Consequently, the engine speed and temperature must be controlled to keep turbine operation within safe limits. Answer (B) is incorrect. The turbine vanes are not subjected to centrifugal force. Answer (C) is incorrect. The compressor rotor(s) or impeller(s) are not subjected to high temperatures.

questions.question_text: Which place in the turbojet engine is subjected to the highest temperature?
questions.choice_c: Turbine inlet.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: There is a relatively slow rise in temperature as the air passes through the compressor inlet to the compressor discharge. The air is discharged into the combustion chamber. At the front of the combustion chamber are the fuel spray nozzles. The temperature increases rapidly in the combustion chamber and is cooled by a secondary airflow. The turbine inlet is subjected to the highest temperature in the turbojet engine. High-temperature, high-energy combustion gas is delivered through the turbine inlet from the combustion chamber. The temperature decreases through the turbine and exhaust nozzle. Answer (A) is incorrect. The compressor discharge has a relatively low, not high, temperature in the turbojet engine. Answer (B) is incorrect. The fuel spray nozzles are located in front of the combustion chamber and are not subjected to the highest temperature.

questions.question_text: What effect would a change in ambient temperature or air density have on gas-turbine-engine performance?
questions.choice_a: As air density decreases, thrust increases.
questions.choice_c: As temperature increases, thrust decreases.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: A high ambient air temperature at a given pressure altitude relates to a high density altitude or a decrease in air density. Thrust is reduced because of low air density and low mass flow. Also, thrust and fuel flow are reduced further because of high compressor inlet temperature. Answer (A) is incorrect. As air density decreases, thrust decreases, not increases. Answer (B) is incorrect. Thrust decreases, not increases, with an increase in temperature.

questions.question_text: What effect, if any, does high ambient temperature have upon the thrust output of a turbine engine?
questions.choice_a: Thrust will be reduced due to the decrease in air density.
questions.choice_b: Thrust will remain the same, but turbine temperature will be higher.
questions.choice_c: Thrust will be higher because more heat energy is extracted from the hotter air.
questions.explanation: A high ambient temperature at a given pressure altitude relates to a high density altitude or a decrease in air density. Thrust is reduced because of the low air density and low mass flow. Also, thrust and fuel flow are reduced further because of high compressor inlet temperature. Answer (B) is incorrect. The compressor inlet, not turbine, temperature will increase at high ambient temperature. Answer (C) is incorrect. Thrust is reduced, not increased, because the hotter air is less dense, with a low mass flow.

questions.question_text: As outside air pressure decreases, thrust output will
questions.choice_a: increase due to greater efficiency of jet aircraft in thin air.
questions.choice_b: remain the same since compression of inlet air will compensate for any decrease in air pressure.
questions.choice_c: decrease due to higher density altitude.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Altitude is one factor that strongly affects the performance of the turbojet engine. An increase in altitude produces a decrease in density and pressure. This high density altitude causes a decrease in the engine air mass flow and, thus, a decrease in thrust output. Answer (A) is incorrect. Thrust output decreases, not increases, with a decrease in air pressure. Answer (B) is incorrect. The compression of inlet air does not compensate for the low air mass flow caused by higher density altitudes.

questions.question_text: The most important restriction to the operation of turbojet or turboprop engines is
questions.choice_c: limiting torque.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: The operating characteristics of the turbojet or turboprop engine provide various operating limitations. Limiting exhaust gas temperature is the most important restriction to the operation of the turbojet or turboprop engine. Answer (A) is incorrect. The turbine section is the most critical element of the turbojet engine. Temperature control is more restrictive than turbine speed, which may operate above 10,000 RPM continuously. Answer (C) is incorrect. Torque is a performance measure used on turbopropeller airplanes but is not generally applicable to turbojet engines. The most important restriction is temperature, even though, in cooler weather, a torque limitation may be reached before the temperature limitation in a turbopropeller airplane.

questions.question_text: Equivalent shaft horsepower (ESHP) of a turboprop engine is a measure of
questions.choice_c: propeller thrust only.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: In addition to providing jet thrust, the turboprop engine requires additional turbine stages to continue the expansion of gases in the turbine section and to extract a very large percent of the exhaust gas energy as shaft power. The turboprop is primarily a power-producing machine, and the jet thrust is a small amount of the output propulsive power. The turboprop engine is rated by ESHP, which is a measure of shaft horsepower and jet thrust. Answer (A) is incorrect. ESHP is a measure of power, not temperature. Answer (C) is incorrect. Propeller thrust is not a measurement of ESHP.

questions.question_text: What effect will an increase in altitude have upon the available equivalent shaft horsepower (ESHP) of a turboprop engine?
questions.choice_a: Lower air density and engine mass flow will cause a decrease in power.
questions.choice_b: Higher propeller efficiency will cause an increase in usable power (ESHP) and thrust.
questions.choice_c: Power will remain the same but propeller efficiency will decrease.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: The ESHP will vary with altitude in a manner similar to the thrust output of the turbojet because the higher altitude produces much lower density and engine mass flow. __ Answer (B) is incorrect. Propeller efficiency decreases, not increases, with an increase in altitude. Answer (C) is incorrect. Sesh will decrease, not remain the same, with an increase in altitude.

questions.question_text: Minimum specific fuel consumption of the turboprop engine is normally available in which altitude range?
questions.choice_c: The tropopause to 45,000 feet.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: The minimum specific fuel consumption is obtained at relatively high power setting and high altitudes. The low inlet air temperature reduces the specific fuel consumption, and the lowest is obtained near altitudes of 25,000 to 35,000 ft. (or to the tropopause). Answer (A) is incorrect. The altitude range is from 25,000 to 35,000 ft., not 10,000 to 25,000 ft. Answer (C) is incorrect. The tropopause is the highest, not lowest, altitude range for minimum specific fuel consumption.

questions.question_text: A rule of thumb states that a 1,000 foot increase in density altitude for a turbojet with a high thrust-to- weight ratio will cause a takeoff distance increase of
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Some appropriate rules of thumb may be derived to illustrate the differences between various airplane types. A 1,000-foot increase in density altitude will cause approximately a 7% increase in takeoff distance for a turbojet with high thrust-to-weight ratio. Answer (B) is incorrect. At a given gross weight, every 1,000-foot increase in density altitude results in a 5% increase in landing distance, not a decrease in takeoff distance. Each 1% excess in velocity incurs approximately 2% additional takeoff distance. Answer (C) is incorrect. The approximate increase in eer distance for a turbojet with low thrust-to-weight ratio is 2%.

questions.question_text: For an airplane with a relatively high thrust- to-weight ratio, a 10% increase in takeoff gross weight would result in a takeoff distance increase of approximately
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: The takeoff distance will vary at least as the square of the gross weight varies. For example, a 10% increase in takeoff gross weight would cause a 5% increase in takeoff velocity, at least a 9% decrease in acceleration, and at least a 21% increase in takeoff distance. For the airplane with a high thrust-to-weight ratio, the increase in takeoff distance would be approximately 21% to 22%. Answer (A) is incorrect. An increase of 30% or more would require approximately a 15% increase in takeoff gross weight. Answer (C) is incorrect. An increase of 11% would require approximately 5% increase in takeoff gross weight.

questions.question_text: High Density altitude conditions result in
questions.choice_c: lower true airspeeds.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: The difference you will see at high density altitude (DA) is your true airspeed (TAS) and, with no wind, ground speed will be higher than at sea level for the same indicated airspeed. For example, you when you rotate at 67 knots indicated, at sea level, you will be at 67 knots TAS. At 8,000 ft. density altitude, however, 67 KIAS is 76 KTAS. With no wind, you'll be rotating 9 knots faster ground speed. Answer (B) is incorrect. Indicated airspeeds would be the same at high or low density altitudes. Answer (C) is incorrect. Lower true airspeeds will be at lower density altitudes.

questions.question_text: Aerodynamic studies prove
questions.choice_a: turbojet powered airplanes and reciprocating engine powered are airplanes equally sensitive to density altitude.
questions.choice_b: turbojet powered airplanes are more sensitive to density altitude than reciprocating engine powered airplanes.
questions.choice_c: reciprocating engine powered airplanes are more sensitive to density altitude than turbojet powered airplanes.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: The turbojet airplane is much more sensitive to density altitude than the reciprocating powered airplane because the effect of density altitude on powerplant thrust depends much on the type of powerplant. An increase in altitude above standard sea level will bring an immediate decrease in power output for the unsupercharged or the turbojet and turboprop engines. Answer (A) is incorrect. The effect of density altitude on powerplant thrust depends much on the type of powerplant. A reciprocating engine airplane is less sensitive to density altitude than a turbojet because reciprocating engines can be supercharged. Answer (C) is incorrect. The effect of density altitude on powerplant thrust depends much on the type of powerplant. A reciprocating engine airplane is less sensitive to density altitude than a turbojet because reciprocating engines can be supercharged. Thus, a 1,000-ft. increase in density altitude will cause an approximate 3.5% increase in takeoff distance for the supercharged reciprocating airplane when below critical altitude, while it could cause upwards of 7% for a turbojet.

questions.question_text: A turboprop engine
questions.choice_b: has superior performance during takeoff than a turbojet.
questions.choice_c: has low propulsive efficiency and low thrust available.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: The performance of the turboprop illustrates the typical advantages of the propeller-engine combination. Higher propulsive efficiency combined with high thrust and low speeds provides superior range, endurance, and takeoff performance relative to the turbojet. Answer (A) is incorrect. All propeller equipped powerplants have nearly constant power with speed unlike a turbojet which decreases slightly. Answer (C) is incorrect. Turboprop engines have higher propulsive efficiency and high thrust, along with low speeds. They provide range, endurance, and takeoff performance superior to a turbojet.

questions.question_text: What characterizes a transient compressor stall?
questions.choice_a: Loud, steady roar accompanied by heavy shuddering.
questions.choice_b: Sudden loss of thrust accompanied by a loud whine.
questions.choice_c: Intermittent “bang,” as backfires and flow reversals take place.
questions.explanation: , DISCUSSION: As a compressor stall occurs, the pressure rises and the compressor does not furnish discharge at a pressure equal to the combustion chamber pressure. As a result, a flow reversal or backfire takes place. If the stall is transient and intermittent, the indication will be the intermittent “bang” as backfire and flow reversal take place. Answer (A) is incorrect. This describes a developed and steady stall, not a transient stall. Answer (B) is incorrect. A transient stall is characterized by an intermittent “bang,” not a loud whine.

questions.question_text: What indicates that a compressor stall has developed and become steady?
questions.choice_c: Complete loss of power with severe reduction in airspeed.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: As a compressor stall occurs, the pressure rises and the compressor does not furnish discharge at a pressure equal to the combustion chamber pressure. As a result, a flow reversal or backfire takes place. If the stall develops and becomes steady, strong vibrations and a loud roar develop from the continuous flow reversal. Answer (B) is incorrect. An occasional loud “bang” and flow reversal describe an indication of a transient stall, not a stall that has developed and become steady. Answer (C) is incorrect. A compressor stall will not cause a complete loss of power.

questions.question_text: Which type of compressor stall has the greatest potential for severe engine damage?
questions.choice_c: Steady, continuous flow reversal stall.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: In a steady compressor stall, strong vibrations and a loud roar develop from the continuous flow reversal. The increase in compressor power required tends to reduce RPM, and the reduced airflow and increased fuel flow cause a rapid, immediate rise in exhaust gas temperature. The possibility of engine damage is immediate with the steady stall. Answer (A) is incorrect. An intermittent “backfire” stall does not have as great a potential for severe engine damage as a steady stall. Answer (B) is incorrect. A transient “backfire” stall does not have as great a potential for severe engine damage as a steady stall.

questions.question_text: What recovery would be appropriate in the event of compressor stall?
questions.choice_a: Reduce the throttle and then rapidly advance the throttle to decrease the angle of attack on the compressor blades, creating more airflow.
questions.choice_b: Reduce the throttle and then slowly advance the throttle again and decrease the aircraft's angle of attack.
questions.choice_c: Advance the throttle slowly to increase airflow and decrease the angle of attack on one or more compressor blades.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: To recover from a compressor stall, the pilot should reduce the throttle, decrease the aircraft’s angle of attack, and increase airspeed. These steps will allow the compressor blades to recover from the stall that precipitated the issue. Answer (A) is incorrect. The angle of attack of the compressor blades is a result of the vector caused by the inlet air velocity and the rotational velocity of the blades. Recovery from a compressor stall should be accomplished by quickly reducing power, decreasing the aircraft's angle of attack, and increasing airspeed. Answer (C) is incorrect. It is necessary to reduce the aircraft's angle of attack and increase airspeed to recover from a compressor stall. It is not possible for the pilot to decrease the angle of attack on individual compressor blades.

questions.question_text: What limits turbine engines from developing compressor stalls?
questions.choice_c: TKS system.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: To prevent compressor stalls, compressor bleed valves are contained in the system to restrict the engine until it is at an rpm that allows it to respond to a rapid acceleration demand without distress. Answer (A) is incorrect. Deice valves and heated fuel do not contribute to the prevention of compressor stalls in turbine engines. Answer (C) is incorrect. The TKS system is an anti-icing system that prevents ice from building up on the aircraft. It does not play a role in the prevention of compressor stalls.

questions.question_text: What prevents turbine engines from developing compressor stalls?
questions.choice_c: Fuel heat. 5.21 Mach Speed Flight
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: The compressor bleed valves and the fuel nozzle and spray patterns along with their governors help restrict the fuel flow and chamber pressure until the engine RPM is at acceptable tolerances. Answer (A) is incorrect. The TKS system is a chemical deicing system that is primarily used as an anti-icing system. It does not reduce or prevent compressor stalls. Answer (C) is incorrect. If warm fuel is flown to high altitudes, the fuel will cool and may cause ice crystals. Fuel heat will help eliminate these crystals, and that reduces the possibility of flameouts, but does not directly affect compressor stalls. The compressor bleed valves help prevent turbine engines from developing stalls.

questions.question_text: At what Mach range does the subsonic flight range normally occur?
questions.choice_c: From 1.20 to 2.50 Mach.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Since there is a possibility of having both subsonic and supersonic flows existing on the aircraft, it is convenient to define certain regimes of flight. Subsonic flight range occurs in Mach numbers below 0.75. Answer (B) is incorrect. From 0.75 to 1.20 Mach is the transonic, not subsonic, flight regime. Answer (C) is incorrect. From 1.20 to 2.50 Mach is part of the supersonic, not subsonic, flight regime.

questions.question_text: Within what Mach range does transonic flight regimes usually occur?
questions.choice_c: 1.20 to 2.50 Mach.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Since there is a possibility of having both subsonic and supersonic flows existing on the aircraft, it is convenient to define certain regimes of flight. Transonic flight regimes occur in Mach numbers from 0.75 to 1.20. Answer (A) is incorrect. Mach 0.50 to 0.75 is part of the subsonic, not transonic, flight regime. Answer (C) is incorrect. Mach 1.20 to 2.50 is part of the supersonic, not transonic, flight regime.

questions.question_text: What is the free stream Mach number which produces first evidence of local sonic flow?
questions.choice_c: Critical Mach number.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: By definition, critical Mach number is the “free stream Mach number which produces first evidence of local sonic flow.” Therefore, shock waves, buffet, airflow separation, etc., take place above critical Mach number. Answer (A) is incorrect. The free stream Mach number that produces first evidence of local sonic flow is the critical, not supersonic, Mach number. Answer (B) is incorrect. The free stream Mach number that produces first evidence of local sonic flow is the critical, not transonic, Mach number.

questions.question_text: What is the highest speed possible without supersonic flow over the wing?
questions.choice_c: Transonic index.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: The highest speed possible without supersonic flow over the wing is termed the critical Mach number. Answer (A) is incorrect. The initial buffet speed occurs above the critical Mach number when an area of supersonic flow is created over the wing. Answer (C) is incorrect. The critical Mach number, not the transonic index, is the highest speed possible without supersonic flow over the wing.

questions.question_text: What is the movement of the center of pressure when the wingtips of a sweptwing airplane are shock- stalled first?
questions.choice_c: Outward and forward.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: On a sweptwing airplane, the wing center of pressure shifts when the airplane is shock-stalled. On a sweptwing airplane when the wingtips are shock-stalled first, the center of pressure will move forward and inward. Answer (A) is incorrect. When the wingtips are shock-stalled first, the center of pressure moves forward, not aft. Answer (C) is incorrect. When the wingtips are shock-stalled first, the center of pressure moves inward, not outward.

questions.question_text: What is the result of a shock-induced separation of airflow occurring symmetrically near the wing root of a sweptwing aircraft?
questions.choice_a: A high-speed stall and sudden pitchup.
questions.choice_c: Severe porpoising.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: When airflow separation occurs on the wing due to shock wave formation, there will be a loss of lift anda subsequent loss of downwash aft of the affected area. If the shock-induced separation occurs symmetrically near the wing root, a decrease in downwash behind this area is caused by a loss of lift. A decrease in downwash on the horizontal tail will create a diving moment and the aircraft will perform a “Mach tuck.” Answer (A) is incorrect. There is a sudden pitch down, not up, when a shock-induced separation of airflow occurs symmetrically near the wing root of a sweptwing aircraft. Answer (C) is incorrect. There is a diving moment, not severe porpoising, when a shock-induced separation of airflow occurs symmetrically near the wing root of a sweptwing aircraft.

questions.question_text: What is the principal advantage of a sweepback design wing over a straightwing design?
questions.choice_a: The critical Mach number will increase significantly.
questions.choice_b: Sweepback will increase changes in the magnitude of force coefficients due to compressibility.
questions.choice_c: Sweepback will accelerate the onset of compressibility effect.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: The sweep of a surface produces a beneficial effect in high-speed flight since higher flight speeds may be obtained before components of speed perpendicular to the leading edge produce critical conditions on the wing. One of the most important advantages of a sweepback wing design is an increase in the critical Mach number. Answer (B) is incorrect. Sweepback will reduce, not increase, changes in the magnitude of force coefficients due to compressibility. Answer (C) is incorrect. Sweepback will delay, not accelerate, the onset of compressibility effect.

questions.question_text: What is one disadvantage of a sweptwing design?
questions.choice_a: The wing root stalls prior to the wingtip section.
questions.choice_b: The wingtip section stalls prior to the wing root.
questions.choice_c: Severe pitchdown moment when the center of pressure shifts forward.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: The use of a swept wing design produces certain disadvantages that are important from the standpoint of both airplane design and flight operations. One disadvantage is that, when sweepback is combined with taper, there is an extremely powerful tendency for the wingtip section to stall prior to the wing root. Answer (A) is incorrect. A swept wing design has a tendency for the wingtip, not wing root, to stall first. Answer (C) is incorrect. When the center of pressure shifts forward, there is a pitch up, not down, moment.

questions.question_text: A jet airplane operating at a cruise speed of .75 Mach with outside air temperature of -42 degrees C has a true airspeed of
questions.choice_c: 455 knots.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: To obtain TAS from .75 Mach, use the calculator side of your flight computer and set OAT at -42°C over the Mach index. Find the Mach number on the “minutes” scale, and read TAS above on “miles” scale, or 446 kt. Answer (A) is incorrect. A TAS of 429 kt. would be correct for an OAT of -60°C. Answer (C) is incorrect. A TAS of 455 kt. would be correct for an OAT of —35°C.

questions.question_text: Swept wings improve specific fuel consumption. increases the critical Mach number. increases the speed of sound quotient.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Swept wings can produce major effects on the aerodynamic characteristics. One of the most important advantages of the sweep is an increase in critical Mach number, which will delay the onset of compressibility effects at which drag will increase dramatically. Answer (A) is incorrect. Improved specific fuel consumption is a byproduct of the swept wings, however the most important advantage of the swept wing is the increase in the critical Mach number, which delays the onset of compressibility effects. Answer (C) is incorrect. The Mach number typically exceeds the critical Mach number by 5-10%. At that speed the airflow is induced by a shockwave (the speed of sound). The airflow on a swept wing causes the airflow to hit the wing leading edge at an angle less than 90°, thus delaying the airflow separation and reducing the onset of a shockwave rather than increasing it.

questions.question_text: For significant benefit, wing sweep must be at least 30 to 35°. 45 to 50°. 55° or more to substantially delay compressibility effects.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Sweepback will reduce the magnitude of change in the aerodynamic force coefficients due to compressibility. The use of sweepback on a transonic aircraft will reduce and delay the drag rise and preserve the maneuverability of the aircraft in transonic flight. If sweepback is to be used at all at least 30° to 35° must be used to produce any significant benefit. Answer (B) is incorrect. The benefits of sweepback begin at 30° to 35° of sweep. Answer (C) is incorrect. The benefits of sweepback begin at 30° to 35° of sweep. There are stability issues with sweepback wings of 55° or more.

questions.question_text: “Drag rise” usually occurs 5 to 10% above the critical Mach number. 2 to 3% above the critical Mach number. at the Mach convergence number.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: The Mach number which produces a sharp change in the drag coefficient is termed force divergence or drag rise. Drag rise usually occurs 5% to 10% above the critical Mach number. Answer (B) is incorrect. A 2% to 3% above critical Mach number would be the beginning of supersonic flow and would not produce drag rise. Answer (C) is incorrect. The Mach convergence number is at Mach 1, not beyond it. Drag rise is beyond Mach 1.

questions.question_text: Above the critical Mach number, you can expect rudder control vibrations. wingtip flutter. airflow separation.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Aircraft above the critical Mach number can cause airflow separation. Answer (A) is incorrect. All components of the aircraft are affected by compressibility in a manner somewhat similar to that of basic airfoil. The tail, fuselage, nacelles, etc., and the effect of the interference between the various surfaces of the aircraft must be considered. Aircraft above the critical Mach number should always expect some sort of airflow separation which may result in rudder control vibrations. Answer (B) is incorrect. All components of the aircraft are affected by compressibility in a manner somewhat similar to that of basic airfoil. The tail, fuselage, nacelles, etc. and the effect of the interference between the various surfaces of the aircraft must be considered. Aircraft above the critical Mach number should always expect some sort of airflow separation which may result in wingtip flutter.

questions.question_text: As flight approaches the speed of sound, all changes in velocity and pressure will take place quite suddenly. the airflow ahead of the aircraft is aggressively influenced and is forced out of the way. all changes in velocity and pressure will take place quite slowly.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: The airflow ahead of the object is not influenced until the air particles are suddenly forced out of the way by the object quite sharply and suddenly as flight approaches the speed of sound. Answer (B) is incorrect. As the flight speed nears the speed of sound, a compression wave will form at the leading edge. The airflow ahead of the aircraft is not influenced or moved out of the way. The air velocity and pressure change very quickly. Answer (C) is incorrect. All changes in velocity and pressure will take place quite suddenly when speeds are approaching the speed of sound.

questions.question_text: A “buzz” felt in the flight controls in cruise flight might be an indication of
questions.choice_c: “Force Rise.”
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: “Drag rise” is associated with buffet, trim and stability changes and a decrease in control surface effectiveness. Aileron, rudder, and elevator surfaces subjected to this buffet may “buzz.” Answer (B) is incorrect. The Critical Mach Tuck, or “tuck under,” may result from drag rise, which causes a decrease in downwash on the horizontal tail. Answer (C) is incorrect. “Drag rise,” not “force rise,” may result in a “buzz,” which is a result of the critical Mach number being exceeded.

questions.question_text: “Drag divergence” usually occurs
questions.choice_a: 5% to 10% above the critical Mach number.
questions.choice_b: 2% to 3% above the critical Mach number.
questions.choice_c: at the Mach convergence number.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: The Mach number that produces a sharp change in the drag coefficient is termed drag divergence or drag rise. Drag rise usually occurs 5% to 10% above the critical Mach number. Answer (B) is incorrect. The Mach number that produces a sharp change in the drag coefficient is termed drag divergence or drag rise. A level of 2% to 3% above the critical Mach number is not enough to produce drag divergence. Answer (C) is incorrect. The Mach convergence number is a Mach number and a convergence trend.

questions.question_text: Trim and stability changes in cruise
questions.choice_a: may be caused by drag divergence effects.
questions.choice_b: are caused by the “tuck under” effect.
questions.choice_c: may be caused by drag convergence effects.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Airflow separation can create significant variations in the aerodynamic forces. At speeds over the critical Mach number, trim and stability changes along with a decrease in control effectiveness may occur. These changes are caused by drag divergence effects. Answer (B) is incorrect. Trim and stability changes may cause “tuck under” (Mach tuck) if not corrected. Trim and stability changes are usually what cause Mach tuck. Answer (C) is incorrect. Drag divergence, not convergence, effects may be a cause of trim and stability changes in cruise.

questions.question_text: Transonic airflow across an aircraft
questions.choice_a: may be partly subsonic and partly supersonic.
questions.choice_c: will cause less compressibility effects.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: In the transonic regime, it is very probable that flow on the aircraft components may be partly subsonic and partly supersonic. Answer (B) is incorrect. Transonic airflow may be partly subsonic and partly supersonic. Answer (C) is incorrect. The principal differences between subsonic and supersonic flow are the compressibility effects.

questions.question_text: The number which produces a sharp change in the drag coefficient is the
questions.choice_c: tuck Mach number.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: The Mach number that produces a sharp change in the drag coefficient is termed drag divergence, force divergence, or drag rise. Drag rise usually occurs 5% to 10% above the critical Mach number. Answer (B) is incorrect. The critical Mach number is the free stream Mach number, which produces first evidence of local sonic flow. Answer (C) is incorrect. The tuck Mach number refers to the speed at which the nose will pitch down due to the center of lift moving aft over the wing. This movement is created by local sonic flows over the wing.

questions.question_text: A jet airplane operating at cruise of .80 Mach with an OAT of -40°C has a true airspeed of
questions.choice_c: 429 knots.
questions.explanation: __ DISCUSSION: Using an E6B flight computer, rotate the right inner wheel cutout until the Mach number index is seen. Line the arrow of the Mach number index with the temperature -40°C line. Find the 80 on the minute scale on the outer portion of the inner wheel. Above the 80 on the “miles” scale is almost 48 (three long marks past 45). Common sense tells us it can't be 48 knots, but can be 480 knots. The closest answer to the FAA’s answers is 479 knots TAS. Answer (B) is incorrect. Mach .77 would be about 452 knots TAS with -40°C. Answer (C) is incorrect. Mach .70 would be about 429 knots TAS with -40°C

questions.question_text: A jet airplane operating at cruise of .82 Mach with an OAT of -44°C has a true airspeed of
questions.choice_c: 494 knots. 5.22 Thrust Reversers
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Using an E6B flight computer, rotate the right inner wheel cutout until the Mach number index is seen. Line the arrow of the Mach number index with the temperature -44°C. Find the 82 on the minute scale on the outer portion of the inner wheel. Above the 82 on the “miles” scale is a little past 48 (48.8). Common sense tells us it cannot be 48.8 knots, but can be 488 knots. The closest answer to the FAA’s answers is 486 knots TAS. Answer (A) is incorrect. Mach .79 would be about 477 knots true airspeed with -44°C. Answer (C) is incorrect. Mach .83 would be about 494 knots true airspeed with -44°C.

questions.question_text: How should thrust reversers be applied to reduce landing distance for turbojet aircraft?
questions.choice_c: After applying maximum wheel braking.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Reverse thrust of turbojet engines will usually employ some form of vanes, buckets, or clamshells in the exhaust to turn or direct the exhaust gases forward. Reverse thrust must be applied immediately after ground contact for maximum effectiveness in reducing landing distance. Answer (B) is incorrect. Thrust reversers should be applied immediately after, not prior to, ground contact to reduce landing distance. Answer (C) is incorrect. Thrust reversers should be applied immediately after ground contact, not after applying maximum wheel braking, to reduce landing distance.

questions.question_text: How should reverse thrust propellers be used during landing for maximum effectiveness in stopping?
questions.choice_a: Gradually increase reverse power to maximum as rollout speed decreases.
questions.choice_b: Use maximum reverse power as soon as possible after touchdown.
questions.choice_c: Select reverse-pitch after landing and use idle power setting of the engines.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Reverse thrust of propellers is obtained by rotating the blade angle well below the low pitch stop and applying engine power. The action is to extract a large amount of momentum from the airstream and create negative thrust. Maximum effectiveness is achieved by use of maximum reverse power immediately after the airplane is in contact with the runway. Answer (A) is incorrect. Maximum effectiveness is achieved by use of maximum reverse power as soon as possible after touchdown, not as the rollout speed decreases. Answer (C) is incorrect. Power should be at maximum, not idle, to add to drag and braking friction to increase the net retarding force.

questions.question_text: A definition of the term “viscous hydroplaning’ is where
questions.choice_a: the airplane rides on standing water.
questions.choice_b: a film of moisture covers the painted or rubber- coated portion of the runway.
questions.choice_c: the tires of the airplane are actually riding on a mixture of steam and melted rubber.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Viscous hydroplaning can occur on a thin film of water if it covers the painted or rubber-coated portion of the runway. Answer (A) is incorrect. Dynamic hydroplaning occurs when the airplane tires ride on standing water. Answer (C) is incorrect. Reverted rubber hydroplaning occurs when the tires of the airplane are actually riding on a mixture of steam and melted rubber.

questions.question_text: Compared to dynamic hydroplaning, at what speed does viscous hydroplaning occur when landing on a smooth, wet runway?
questions.choice_a: At approximately 2.0 times the speed that dynamic hydroplaning occurs.
questions.choice_c: At the same speed as dynamic hydroplaning.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Viscous hydroplaning may occur at lower speeds and at lower water depths than dynamic hydroplaning. Answer (A) is incorrect. Dynamic hydroplaning usually occurs at a higher speed than viscous hydroplaning. Answer (C) is incorrect. Viscous hydroplaning often occurs on smooth runway pavements or where rubber deposits are present, usually in the touchdown area where a thin film of water can significantly reduce the coefficient of friction. This usually occurs at a speed lower than dynamic hydroplaning.

questions.question_text: At what minimum speed (rounded off) could dynamic hydroplaning occur on main tires having a pressure of 121 PSI?
questions.choice_c: 110 knots.
questions.explanation: The minimum speed in knots for dynamic hydroplaning can be calculated by the following formula: a WS) FS y| Tire pressure (in PSI) Therefore, for a tire pressure of 121 PSI, the minimum speed is 96 knots. Ci Sax LAL eal to fes Veo} cra ilgll Answer (A) is incorrect. Hydroplaning at 90 kt. will occur at a tire pressure of 106 PSI. Answer (C) is incorrect. Hydroplaning at 110 kt. will occur at a tire pressure of 159 PSI.

questions.question_text: At what minimum speed will dynamic hydroplaning begin if a tire has an air pressure of 70 PSI?
questions.choice_c: 75 knots.
questions.explanation: The minimum speed in knots for dynamic hydroplaning can be calculated by the following formula: Sess y| Tire pressure (in PSI) Cee ouex y 70 S/N MOS OF) =a wees Answer (A) is incorrect. Hydroplaning will occur at 85 kt. with a tire pressure of 95 PSI. Answer (B) is incorrect. Hydroplaning will occur at 80 kt. with a tire pressure of 84 PSI.

questions.question_text: Which term describes the hydroplaning which occurs when an airplane’s tire is effectively held off a smooth runway surface by steam generated by friction?
questions.choice_c: Viscous hydroplaning.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Reverted rubber hydroplaning is the result of a locked wheel skid in which reverted rubber becomes a barrier between the tire and the runway. Steam generated by the heat of friction will hold the wheel off a smooth runway surface. Answer (B) is incorrect. Dynamic hydroplaning occurs when there is standing water or slush on the runway that forms a wedge that lifts the tire away from contact with the runway surface. Answer (C) is incorrect. Viscous hydroplaning occurs on a thin film of water on a smooth (e.g., painted or rubber coated) runway surface.

questions.question_text: What is the best method of speed reduction if hydroplaning is experienced on landing?
questions.choice_a: Apply full main wheel braking only.
questions.choice_b: Apply nosewheel and main wheel braking alternately and abruptly.
questions.choice_c: Apply aerodynamic braking to the fullest advantage.
questions.explanation: During hydroplaning, the tire(s) is(are) not in contact with the runway surface, so there is no friction, and application of the brakes would do nothing. Aerodynamic braking must be used to its fullest advantage. Answer (A) is incorrect. Applying full main wheel braking may increase or compound the problems associated with hydroplaning. If any brakes are used, a pumping or modulating motion like an antiskid system can be used. Aerodynamic braking is recommended. Answer (B) is incorrect. Abrupt use of either the nosewheel or the mainwheel brakes will lock the wheels and compound the problem.

questions.question_text: What effect, if any, will landing at a higher- than-recommended touchdown speed have on hydroplaning?
questions.choice_a: No effect on hydroplaning, but increases landing roll.
questions.choice_b: Reduces hydroplaning potential if heavy braking is applied.
questions.choice_c: Increases hydroplaning potential regardless of braking.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Landing at a higher-than-recommended touchdown speed increases the hydroplaning potential because the wings provide lift and reduce the contact between the tires and the runway, allowing a layer of water to form between the tire and the runway. Answer (A) is incorrect. Landing at a higher airspeed increases the hydroplaning potential. Answer (B) is incorrect. Landing at a higher airspeed increases, not reduces, the hydroplaning potential, regardless of braking.

questions.question_text: Which is an effect of ice, snow, or frost formation on an airplane?
questions.choice_c: Decreased angle of attack for stalls.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: When ice, snow, or particularly frost forms on the upper surface of a wing, it provides a surface texture of considerable roughness. The increase in surface roughness increases skin friction drag and reduces the kinetic energy of the boundary layer, causing incipient stalling of the wing; i.e., separation will occur at angles of attack lower than for the clean, smooth wing. In some aircraft, stall will occur prior to activation of stall warning devices. Answer (A) is incorrect. The formation of ice, snow, or frost on the wings of an aircraft increases, not decreases, stall speed. Answer (B) is incorrect. The formation of ice, snow, or frost on the wings of some aircraft may increase, not decrease, pitchup tendencies.

questions.question_text: Which is an effect of ice, snow, or frost formation on an airplane?
questions.choice_c: Increased angle of attack for stalls.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: When ice, snow, or particularly frost forms on the upper surface of a wing, it provides a surface texture of considerable roughness. The increase in surface roughness increases skin friction drag and reduces the kinetic energy of the boundary layer, causing an increase in power required and stall speed. Answer (B) is incorrect. The formation of ice, snow, or frost on the wings of some aircraft may increase pitchup, not pitchdown, tendencies. Answer (C) is incorrect. The formation of ice, snow, or frost on the wings of an aircraft decreases, not increases, the angle of attack for stalls. In some aircraft, stall will occur prior to activation of stall warning devices.

questions.question_text: The adverse effects of ice, snow, or frost on aircraft performance and flight characteristics include decreased lift and
questions.choice_c: An increased stall speed.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: The adverse effects of ice, snow, or frost on aircraft performance and flight characteristics are generally reflected in the form of decreased lift, increased stall speed, decreased thrust, trim changes, and altered stall characteristics and handling qualities. Answer (A) is incorrect. The adverse effects of ice, snow, or frost on aircraft performance and flight characteristics include a decrease, not an increase, in thrust. Answer (B) is incorrect. The adverse effects of ice, snow, or frost on aircraft performance and flight characteristics include an increase, not a decrease, in stall speed.

questions.question_text: Test data indicate that ice, snow, or frost having a thickness and roughness similar to medium or coarse sandpaper on the leading edge and upper surface of a wing can
questions.choice_a: reduce lift by as much as 40 percent and increase drag by 30 percent.
questions.choice_b: reduce lift by as much as 30 percent and increase drag by 40 percent.
questions.choice_c: increase drag and reduce lift by as much as 40 percent.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Test data indicate that ice, snow, or frost formations having a thickness and surface roughness similar to medium or coarse sandpaper on the leading edge and upper surface of a wing can reduce wing lift by as much as 30% and increase drag by 40%. These changes in lift and drag significantly increase stall speed, reduce controllability, and alter aircraft flight characteristics. Answer (A) is incorrect. Ice, snow, or frost on the leading edge and upper surface of a wing can reduce lift by 30%, not 40%, and increase drag by 40%, not 30%. Answer (C) is incorrect. Ice, snow, or frost on the leading edge and upper surface of a wing can reduce lift by 30%, not 40%.

questions.question_text: Freezing Point Depressant (FPD) fluids used for deicing
questions.choice_b: are intended to provide ice protection on the ground only.
questions.choice_c: onthe ground cause no performance degradation during takeoff.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: FPD fluids are used to aid the ground deicing process and to provide a protective film of FPD (anti-icing) to delay formations of frost, snow, or other ice until after takeoff. During flight, an aircraft's deice/anti-ice equipment (if installed) provides ice protection. Answer (A) is incorrect. FPD fluids used during ground deicing are not intended for, and do not provide, ice protection during flight. Answer (C) is incorrect. Some large aircraft do experience performance degradation during takeoff, and on small airplanes, degradation is significant.

questions.question_text: Freezing Point Depressant (FPD) fluids are highly soluble in water; however,
questions.choice_a: \ce is slow to absorb it but fast to melt when in contact with FPD.
questions.choice_b: Ice absorbs it very fast but is slow to melt when in contact with it.
questions.choice_c: Ice is slow to absorb it, and to melt when in contact with it.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: FPD fluids are very soluble in water. However, the rate at which ice will absorb FPD or melt is very slow. Thus, ice formations adhering to a wing surface must be melted by repeated application of FPD fluids or by application of heated fluid. Answer (A) is incorrect. The rate at which ice will melt when in contact with FPD is very slow, not fast. Answer (B) is incorrect. The rate at which ice will absorb FPD is very slow, not fast.

questions.question_text: Freezing Point Depressant (FPD) fluid residue on engine fan or compressor blades
questions.choice_a: Can increase performance and cause stalls or surges.
questions.choice_b: Could cause FPD vapors to enter the aircraft but would have no effect on engine thrust or power.
questions.choice_c: Can reduce engine performance and cause surging and/or compressor stalls.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: FPD fluid residue on engine fan or compressor blades can reduce engine performance or cause a stall or a surge. In addition, this residue could increase the possibility of, or the quantity of, glycol vapors entering the aircraft through the engine bleed air system. Answer (A) is incorrect. FPD fluid residue on engine fan or compressor blades can reduce, not increase, performance. Answer (B) is incorrect. FPD fluid residue on engine fan or compressor blades can reduce, rather than have no effect on, performance.

questions.question_text: What is the effect of Freezing Point Depressant (FPD) fluid residue on engine fan or compressor blades?
questions.choice_a: It could cause FPD vapors to enter the aircraft but would have no effect on engine thrust or power.
questions.choice_b: It can increase performance and cause stalls or surges.
questions.choice_c: It can reduce engine performance and cause surging and/or compressor stalls.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: FPD fluid residue on engine fan or compressor blades can reduce engine performance and cause surging and/or compressor stalls. In addition, this residue could increase the possibility of, or the quantity of, glycol vapors entering the aircraft through the engine bleed air system. Answer (A) is incorrect. FPD fluid residue on engine fan or compressor blades can reduce engine thrust or power in addition to allowing FPD vapors to enter the aircraft. Answer (B) is incorrect. FPD fluid residue on engine fan or compressor blades can reduce, not increase, engine performance and cause stalls or surges.

questions.question_text: The practice developed and accepted by the North American air carrier industry using traditional North American fluids is to ensure that the freeze point of the remaining film is below ambient temperature by at least A P10°F.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: The practice developed and accepted by the North American air carrier industry using traditional North American fluids is to ensure that the remaining film has a freeze point of at least 20°F below ambient temperature. Answer (A) is incorrect. The remaining film should have a freeze point of at least 20°F, not 10°F, below ambient temperature. Answer (C) is incorrect. The remaining film should have a freeze point of at least 20°F, not 20°C, below ambient temperature.

questions.question_text: Anti-icing fluid should provide freezing point protection to
questions.choice_c: A freezing point no greater than 20°F below the ambient or airplane surface temperature.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: The practice developed and accepted by the air carrier industry is to ensure that the residual surface film of anti-icing fluid should provide freezing point protection to at least 20°F (11°C) below the colder of ambient or aircraft surface (skin) temperature. Answer (A) is incorrect. Anti-icing fluid should have a freezing point no greater than 20°F below ambient or surface temperature, whichever is lower, regardless of ambient temperature. Answer (B) is incorrect. Anti-icing fluid should have a freezing point no greater than 20°F below ambient or surface temperature, whichever is lower, regardless of outside temperature.

questions.question_text: Snow on top of deicing or anti-icing fluids
questions.choice_a: Need not be considered as adhering to the aircraft. ;
questions.choice_b: Must be considered as adhering to the aircraft.
questions.choice_c: Must be considered as adhering to the aircraft, se a safe takeoff can be made as it will blow off.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Ice, snow, or frost on top of deicing or anti- icing fluids must be considered as adhering to the aircraft. Takeoff should not be attempted. Answer (A) is incorrect. Ice, snow, or frost on top of deicing or anti-icing fluids must be considered as adhering to the aircraft. Answer (C) is incorrect. Takeoff should not be attempted.

questions.question_text: The purpose of diluting ethylene glycol deicing fluid with water in non-precipitation conditions is to
questions.choice_c: Increase the minimum freezing point (onset of crystallization).
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Pure ethylene glycol will freeze at warmer temperatures than that diluted with water. For this reason, all currently available commercial and military deicing fluids contain small quantities of water. Answer (A) is incorrect. The eutectic point, or minimum freezing point (onset of crystallization), is a fixed value that occurs with a specific mixture of glycol and water for which the freezing temperature is at its lowest. Answer (C) is incorrect. The minimum freezing point (onset of crystallization), or eutectic point, is a fixed value that occurs with a specific mixture of glycol and water for which the freezing temperature is at its lowest.

questions.question_text: What is the minimum glycol content of Type 1 deicing/anti-icing fluid?
questions.choice_c: 80 percent.
questions.explanation: Type 1 deicing/anti-icing fluids in the concentrated form contain a minimum of 80% glycols and are considered “unthickened” because of their relatively low viscosity. These fluids are used for deicing or anti-icing but provide very limited anti-icing protection. Answer (A) is incorrect. The minimum glycol content of Type 1 fluid is 80%, not 30%. Answer (B) is incorrect. Fifty percent is the minimum glycol content of Type 2, not Type 1, fluid.

questions.question_text: What is the minimum glycol content of Type 2 deicing/anti-icing fluid?
questions.choice_c: 80 percent.
questions.explanation: Type 2 deicing/anti-icing fluids contain a minimum of 50% glycols and are considered “thickened” because of added thickening agents that enable the fluid to be deposited in a thicker film and to remain on the aircraft surfaces until the time of takeoff. These fluids are used for deicing and anti-icing, and provide greater protection than do Type 1 fluids against ice, frost, or snow formation in conditions conducive to aircraft icing on the ground. Answer (A) is incorrect. The minimum glycol content of Type 2 fluid is 50%, not 30%. Answer (C) is incorrect. Eighty percent is the minimum glycol content of Type 1, not Type 2, fluid.

questions.question_text: Which is a disadvantage of the one-step over the two-step process when deicing/anti-icing an airplane?
questions.choice_c: More fluid is used with the one-step method when large deposits of ice and snow must be flushed off airplane surfaces.
questions.explanation: The one-step process for deicing/anti-icing an airplane consists of applying heated fluid onto the airplane surfaces to remove accumulated ice, snow, or frost and prevent their subsequent buildup. Its primary disadvantage is that, when large deposits of ice and snow must be flushed off of airplane surfaces, the total fluid usage will be greater than for a two-step process. In the two-step procedure, you may use water or a very diluted Type 1 fluid to first deice the wing and then the Type 2, thus using less fluid. Answer (A) is incorrect. The primary advantage of the one- step process is that it is quick and uncomplicated. Answer (B) is incorrect. Since the one-step process uses heated fluid, the holding time is decreased, not increased.

questions.question_text: Which procedure increases holding time when deicing/anti-icing an airplane using a two-step process?
questions.choice_a: Heated Type 1 fluid followed by cold Type 2 fluid.
questions.choice_b: Cold Type 2 fluid followed by hot Type 2 fluid.
questions.choice_c: Heated Type 1 or 2 fluid followed by cold Type 1 fluid.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: The two-step process consists of separate _ deicing and anti-icing steps. First, a heated, diluted Type 1 fluid is applied to remove accumulated ice, snow, or frost. Then a cold, more concentrated fluid is applied. Using a Type 2 fluid for the second step increases the holding time (i.e., the time the fluid will protect the aircraft from ice, snow, and frost) significantly due to its higher viscosity. Answer (B) is incorrect. Using a heated, not cold, fluid in the first step helps melt any accumulated ice, snow, or frost, and using a cold, not hot, fluid in the second step leaves greater amounts of residual film, increasing holding time. Answer (C) is incorrect. Using a Type 2 fluid for the second step increases the holding time (i.e., the time the fluid will protect the aircraft from ice, snow, and frost) significantly due to its higher viscosity.

questions.question_text: Which of the following will decrease the holding time during anti-icing using a two-step process?
questions.choice_c: Increase the viscosity of Type 1 fluid.
questions.explanation: The two-step process consists of separate deicing and anti-icing steps. First, a heated, diluted Type 1 fluid is applied to remove accumulated ice, snow, or frost. Then a cold, more concentrated fluid is applied. Using a heated Type 2 fluid in the second step will leave less amounts of residual film, decreasing holding time. Answer (B) is incorrect. Decreasing the water content of the anti-icing fluid increases its viscosity, which will increase, not decrease, holding time. Answer (C) is incorrect. Increasing the viscosity of Type 1 fluid requires lowering its temperature. This will increase, not decrease, holding time because it will leave greater amounts of residual film.

questions.question_text: What should the deice/anti-ice fluid temperature be during the last step of a two-phase process?
questions.explanation: The two-step process consists of separate deicing and anti-icing steps. First, a heated, diluted Type 1 fluid is applied to remove accumulated ice, snow, or frost. Then a cold, more concentrated fluid is applied. Using a cold fluid in the second step will leave greater amounts of residual film, increasing the effectiveness of the deice/anti-ice fluid. Answer (A) is incorrect. Using a cold, not hot, fluid in the second step will leave greater amounts of residual film, increasing the effectiveness of the deice/anti-ice fluid. Answer (B) is incorrect. Using a cold, not warm, fluid in the second step will leave greater amounts of residual film, increasing the effectiveness of the deice/anti-ice fluid.

questions.question_text: Deicing procedures and equipment developed for large transport airplanes
questions.choice_a: Will not be appropriate for the smaller aircraft, used under FAR Part 135.
questions.choice_b: Will be appropriate for all of the smaller aircraft, used under FAR Part 135.
questions.choice_c: May not be appropriate for some of the smaller aircraft, used under FAR Part 135.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Deicing procedures and equipment developed for large transport airplanes may not be appropriate for some of the smaller aircraft used under FAR Part 135. Answer (A) is incorrect. Deicing procedures and equipment developed for large transport airplanes may not be appropriate for some, not all, of the smaller aircraft used under FAR Part 135. Answer (B) is incorrect. Deicing procedures and equipment developed for large transport airplanes may be appropriate for some, not all, of the smaller aircraft used under FAR Part 135.

questions.question_text: Even a small amount of frost, ice, or snow may
questions.choice_b: hinder lift production to a point where takeoff will be impossible.
questions.choice_c: decrease takeoff ground run. 5.25 Pitot System
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Frost does not change the aerodynamic shape of the wing. The roughness of the wing’s surface spoils the smooth flow of air, causing the airflow to slow down. This slowing of the air causes early airflow separation over the affected airfoil. This results in a loss of lift. A small amount of frost on airfoils may prevent an aircraft from becoming airborne at normal takeoff speed or hinder the production of lift to the point that takeoff will be impossible altogether. It is also possible that if the aircraft becomes airborne, it could have an insufficient margin of airspeed above stall so that moderate gusts or turning the aircraft could produce incipient or complete stalling. Answer (A) is incorrect. Test data indicate that ice, snow, or frost having a thickness and roughness similar to medium or coarse sandpaper on the leading edge and upper surface of a wing can reduce lift by as much as 30% and increase drag by 40%. This will decrease takeoff performance, not increase it. Answer (C) is incorrect. Extra drag caused by ice, snow, or frost having a thickness and roughness similar to medium or coarse sandpaper on the leading edge and upper surface of a wing can reduce lift by as much as 30% and increase drag by 40%. This will require an increased takeoff ground run. Even with an increased takeoff ground run, the aircraft may not take off at all.

questions.question_text: During an en route descent, both the ram air input and drain hole of the pitot system become completely blocked by ice. What airspeed indication can be expected?
questions.choice_b: Indicated airspeed remains at the value prior to icing.
questions.choice_c: Decrease in indicated airspeed.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: If the ram air input and the drain hole are blocked, the pressure is trapped in the system, and the airspeed indicator will react as an altimeter. During a descent, the airspeed indication will decrease. Answer (A) is incorrect. Indicated airspeed will decrease, not increase, in a descent. Answer (B) is incorrect. Indicated airspeed will remain at the same value during level, not descending, flight.

questions.question_text: What can a pilot expect if the pitot system ram air input and drain hole are blocked by ice?
questions.choice_a: The airspeed indicator may act as an altimeter.
questions.choice_b: The airspeed indicator will show a decrease with an increase in altitude.
questions.choice_c: No airspeed indicator change will occur during climbs or descents.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: If the ram air and the drain hole are blocked, the pressure is trapped in the system, and the airspeed indicator will react as an altimeter: higher indicated airspeed at higher altitudes and lower indicated airspeed at lower altitudes. Answer (B) is incorrect. The airspeed indicator will show an increase, not decrease, with an increase in altitude. Answer (C) is incorrect. Differential pressure between the pitot tube and static air source changes and so does indicated airspeed.

questions.question_text: How will the airspeed indicator react if the ram air input to the pitot head is blocked by ice, but the drain hole and static port are not?
questions.choice_b: Indication will rise to the top of the scale.
questions.choice_c: Indication will remain constant but will increase in aclimb.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: If the ram air input to the pitot head is blocked by ice but the drain hole and static port remain open, the pressure in the line to the airspeed indicator will vent out through the drain hole. Thus, the airspeed indication will drop to zero. Answer (B) is incorrect. The airspeed indication will drop to zero, not rise to the top of the scale, if only the ram air input is blocked. Answer (C) is incorrect. The pressure in the airspeed line will vent out through the vent hole, and the indication will drop to zero. It will not act as an altimeter.

questions.question_text: If both the ram air input and drain hole of the pitot system become blocked, the indicated airspeed will
questions.choice_c: remain constant regardless of altitude change.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: If both the pitot tube input and the drain hole on the pitot system are blocked, the airspeed indication will increase in a climb and decrease in a descent. Answer (B) is incorrect. During a climb, it will indicate an increase, not decrease, due to the stronger differential pressure in the blocked pitot tube relative to the static vents. Answer (C) is incorrect. Indicated airspeed will change with changes in altitude.

questions.question_text: When setting the altimeter, pilots should disregard effects of nonstandard atmospheric temperatures and pressures. corrections for static pressure systems. corrections for instrument error.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: When setting the altimeter, pilots should disregard the effects of nonstandard atmospheric temperatures and pressures. The altimeter setting system provides a means that corrects for pressure variations. Since instrument flight in controlled airspace is accomplished at assigned indicated altitudes, airplane separation is maintained because all aircraft using the same altimeter setting are equally affected by nonstandard temperatures and pressures equally at various levels. Answer (B) is incorrect. Altimeters are subject to errors in the static pressure system. A pilot should set the current reported altimeter setting on the altimeter setting scale. The altimeter should read within 75 ft. of field elevation. If not, it is questionable and should be evaluated by a repair station. Answer (C) is incorrect. Altimeters are subject to instrument errors. A pilot should set the current reported altimeter setting on the altimeter setting scale. The altimeter should read within 75 ft. of field elevation. If not, it is questionable and should be evaluated by a repair station.

questions.question_text: When the temperature is -20°C at 15,000 feet indicated, you know that altimeters automatically compensate for temperature variations. the altimeter is indicating higher than true altitude. the altimeter is indicating lower than true altitude. 5.27 Glass Cockpit Systems
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: The ISA for 15,000 feet is -15°C. When the temperature is colder than standard, the altimeter will indicate a higher than true altitude. Answer (A) is incorrect. Altimeters do not compensate for temperature variations. Answer (C) is incorrect. When the temperature is warmer than standard, the altimeter will indicate a lower than true altitude.

questions.question_text: Moving map displays May or may not be accurate. Are certified to be accurate on a small scale setting. . Are very accurate and require no current chart overlays for precise navigation.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: The moving map display is designed to provide supplemental navigation information but is not approved as a substitute for primary navigation instruments. While the primary navigation CDI and related system components are required to meet certification standards for accuracy of information, moving map displays are not. Thus, a moving map may or may not be accurate depending on the accuracy of the information being fed to it by the navigation source. Answer (B) is incorrect. Moving map displays are not required to meet any certification standards in terms of accuracy. Also, the scale setting of the moving map has no impact on its accuracy. The accuracy of the displayed position is based on the navigation sensitivity supplied by the unit feeding positional information to the moving map. Answer (C) is incorrect. Not only is amoving map not required to meet any certification standards for accuracy, but database currency is essential for effective situational awareness. In IFR operations, database currency is a requisite of using the device.

questions.question_text: An airspeed vertical tape display
questions.choice_a: Is blank if the aircraft is in icing condition.
questions.choice_b: Will freeze when a stall is encountered.
questions.choice_c: Scrolls down to show an increase in airspeed.
questions.explanation: The vertical airspeed tape is configured so that lower speeds are at the bottom of the tape and higher speeds are at the top of the tape. This means that while airspeed is increasing, the airspeed tape is actually scrolling down so that higher numbers are appearing. _ Answer (A) is incorrect. The airspeed tape will indicate a failure if the pitot and/or static inputs are blocked by ice, but it will not automatically go blank as soon as icing conditions are encountered. Answer (B) is incorrect. The airspeed tape is constantly in motion as long as pitot-static inputs are being received.

questions.question_text: The message “NAV service not communicating” on a moving map display means that the
questions.choice_a: NAV source will be reset when the message is acknowledged.
questions.choice_b: Selected NAV source is not publishing data to the display.
questions.choice_c: GPS is inoperative.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Failure indications on the moving map can be quite subtle. The moving map will reflect a loss of position information by the removal of the aircraft symbol, compass labels, and other small differences. A message of some sort will also appear, alerting the pilot that the navigation source is not providing data to the display unit. Answer (A) is incorrect. A moving map display is not a control unit; it is only a display unit. Acknowledging a message has no effect on the system that sent the message. The only way to reset the NAV source in this case would be to troubleshoot the source itself, not the moving map display. Answer (C) is incorrect. This message does not necessarily mean that the GPS is inoperative, merely that it is not communicating with the moving map display. You could easily troubleshoot the GPS unit by reviewing the satellite status page to ensure it was operating correctly.

questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 998 on the inside back cover.) You see the indication in the figure on your PFD, but your standby indicator reads 120 knots and the power is set for 120-knot cruise in level flight. You decide the
questions.choice_a: pitot tube may be plugged with ice or a bug.
questions.choice_b: standby indicator is defective because there is no red ‘X’ on the speed tape display.
questions.choice_c: airspeed means attitude is incorrect.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Because your attitude, power setting, and standby airspeed indicator represent a predictable flight situation, you should assume that the PFD airspeed indication is invalid. Neither the Air Data Computer (ADC) nor its components have failed because no red X is present on the PFD. It is most likely that a clog has occurred in the pitot line feeding the ADC, but after the split in the main line that also feeds the standby indicator. Answer (B) is incorrect. A red X would only appear over the PFD airspeed indication if the ADC or its components had failed entirely. It is possible that the pitot line feeding the ADC is blocked, thus rendering the ADC and the PFD airspeed indication inaccurate. Because the aircraft’s attitude and power setting agree with the indication on the standby indicator, it is more reliable than the PFD indication. Answer (C) is incorrect. The power setting and the standby airspeed indicator reflect normal cruise flight. By cross-checking your other cockpit instrumentation, you can quickly rule out the possibility of an incorrect flight attitude.

questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 999 on the inside back cover.) The MFD in the figure has lost
questions.choice_c: contact with the ADC.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: In the lower right-hand corner of Fig. 999, the message “NAV Source Not Communicating” indicates that the moving map is not currently displaying positional information. Other clues to this are the removal of the aircraft symbol and the compass rose. Answer (A) is incorrect. Even though no traffic is shown on the moving map, no indication is provided that traffic information is not being shown due to a failure of either the traffic system or the moving map itself. Answer (C) is incorrect. The Air Data Computer (ADC) provides airspeed, altitude, vertical speed, and other flight information to the PFD. A failure of the ADC would result in red Xs on the PFD but have no bearing on the MFD.

questions.question_text: Automated flight decks or cockpits can
questions.choice_a: enhance basic pilot flight skills. e decrease the work load in terminal areas.
questions.choice_c: often create much larger pilot errors than traditional cockpits.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: The automated flight deck can be a great help when flying in high-workload situations, such as in a busy terminal area or when executing a missed approach in adverse weather conditions. Answer (A) is incorrect. The automated flight deck can assist a pilot or provide enhanced information to the pilot, but it cannot directly affect the pilot's basic skills. Answer (C) is incorrect. Although errors can occur when piloting an aircraft with an automated flight deck, the automated systems do not make those errors more common or more serious than similar errors that occur in an aircraft with a nonautomated flight deck.

questions.question_text: Automation in aircraft has proven
questions.choice_a: to be able to create larger errors.
questions.choice_c: effective in preventing complacency.
questions.explanation: ; DISCUSSION: The Veillette-Decker study on automation found that while automation made the promise of reducing human mistakes, in some instances it actually created larger errors. Answer (B) is incorrect. Automation can actually foster deterioration of basic piloting skills and lead to much larger errors at times. Answer (C) is incorrect. Automation can actually lead to increased complacency.

questions.question_text: Automation has been found to
questions.choice_a: create much larger errors at times.
questions.choice_c: substitute for a lack of aviation experience.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: The Veillette-Decker study on automation found that while automation made the promise of reducing human mistakes, in some instances it actually created larger errors. Answer (B) is incorrect. Automation by itself does not improve crew situational awareness; rather, automation can actually foster deterioration of basic piloting skills and lead to much larger errors at times.
