The glide path indications are red and white lights where two whites and two reds indicate that the aircraft is on the glide path. Three or more white lights indicates too high and three or more red lights indicate too low. Answer (A) is incorrect. One light projection unit with two colors, red and white, is a pulsating system (PVASI). Answer (C) is incorrect. The row of four lights are perpendicular to the runway, not parallel to the runway.

questions.question_text: How often are NOTAMs broadcast to pilots on a scheduled basis?
questions.choice_a: 15 minutes before and 15 minutes after the hour.
questions.choice_b: Between weather broadcasts on the hour.
questions.choice_c: Hourly, appended to the weather broadcast.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: NOTAMs (D) are broadcast to pilots automatically, appended to the hourly weather broadcast. NOTAM (L) information is not attached to the hourly weather reports but is distributed locally and maintained at each FSS for facilities in its area only. FDC NOTAMs are transmitted only once and are kept on file at the FSS until published or canceled. Answer (A) is incorrect. NOTAMs (D) are appended to the hourly weather report, not broadcasted 15 min. before and after the hour. Answer (B) is incorrect. NOTAMs (D) are appended to the hourly weather broadcast, not a separate broadcast between weather reports.

questions.question_text: What are FDC NOTAMs?
questions.choice_a: Conditions of facilities en route that may cause delays.
questions.choice_b: Time critical aeronautical information of a temporary nature from distant centers.
questions.choice_c: Regulatory amendments to published IAPs and charts not yet available in normally published charts.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: FDC (Flight Data Center) NOTAMs are regulatory in nature and issued to establish restrictions to flight or amend charts or published instrument approach procedures. FDC NOTAMs are published as needed and indexed in the Notice to Airmen Publication. Answer (A) is incorrect. NOTAM (D) contains information on navigational facilities en route that may cause delays. NOTAMs (D) are appended to the hourly weather reports and are available from any FSS for the duration of their validity or until published. Answer (B) is incorrect. Time critical aeronautical information of a temporary nature from distant centers will be included in a NOTAM (D), or distant. NOTAMs (D) are appended to the hourly weather reports and are available from any FSS for the duration of their validity or until published.

questions.question_text: NOTAMs (D) are used to disseminate what type of information?
questions.choice_a: Temporary flight restrictions, changes to sectional aeronautical charts, and changes to instrument approach procedures.
questions.choice_b: Taxi closures, personnel and equipment near or crossing runways, airport lighting aids that do not affect instrument approach criteria, and airport rotating beacon outages.
questions.choice_c: Time critical information of a permanent nature that is not yet available in normally published charts.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: NOTAMs (D) disseminate various local and distant information. At the local level, NOTAMs (D) disseminate information such as taxiway closures, personnel and equipment near or crossing runways, airport lighting aids that do not affect instrument approach criteria, and airport rotating beacon outages. Answer (A) is incorrect. Temporary flight restrictions and charting changes between regular printing cycles are disseminated in FDC NOTAMs. Answer (C) is incorrect. Time critical information of a permanent nature that is not yet available in normally published charts is disseminated by an FDC NOTAM.

questions.question_text: What type of information is disseminated by NOTAMs (D)?
questions.choice_a: Status of navigation aids, ILSs, radar service available, and other information essential to planning.
questions.choice_b: Information specific to military airports that are part of the NAS.
questions.choice_c: Temporary flight restrictions, changes in status in navigational aids, and updates on equipment such as VASI.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: NOTAMs (D) disseminate various local and distant information. Distant NOTAMs are disseminated for all navigational facilities that are part of the National Airspace System (NAS) and for all public-use airports, seaplane bases, and heliports listed in the Chart Supplement. They include the status of navigation aids, ILSs, radar service available, and other information essential to planning. Answer (B) is incorrect. Military NOTAMs, not NOTAMs (D), are issued for military airports in the National Airspace System (NAS). Answer (C) is incorrect. Temporary flight restrictions are normally disseminated in FDC NOTAMs.

questions.question_text: What information is contained in the Notices to Airmen Publication (NTAP)?
questions.choice_c: Current NOTAMs (D), FDC NOTAMs, and military NOTAMs.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: The NTAP contains NOTAMs (D) that are expected to remain in effect for an extended period and FDC NOTAMs that are current at the time of publication. ___ Answer (B) is incorrect. Military NOTAMs are not published in the NTAP. Answer (C) is incorrect. While current NOTAMs (D) and bere NOTAMs are published in the NTAP, military NOTAMs are not.

questions.question_text: Land and Hold Short Operations (LAHSO) include landing and holding short
questions.choice_b: of some designated point on the runway.
questions.choice_c: only of an intersecting runway or taxiway.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: LAHSO include landing and holding short of an intersecting runway, an intersecting taxiway, or some other designated point on a runway other than an intersecting runway or taxiway. Answer (A) is incorrect. LAHSO include landing and holding short of an intersecting runway or some other designated point on a runway, not only an intersecting taxiway. Answer (C) is incorrect. LAHSO include landing and holding short of some designated point on a runway, not only an intersecting runway or taxiway.

questions.question_text: A Land and Hold Short Operations (LAHSO) clearance, that the pilot accepts
questions.choice_b: does not preclude a rejected landing.
questions.choice_c: precludes a rejected landing.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: A LAHSO clearance, once accepted, must be adhered to, just as any other ATC clearance, unless an amended clearance is obtained or an emergency occurs. ALAHSO clearance does not preclude a rejected landing. Answer (A) is incorrect. A LAHSO clearance must be adhered to unless an amended clearance is obtained or an emergency occurs. Additionally, a LAHSO clearance does not preclude a rejected landing. Answer (C) is incorrect. A LAHSO clearance does not preclude a rejected landing.

questions.question_text: In conducting Land and Hold Short Operations (LAHSO), the pilot should have readily available
questions.choice_a: the published Available Landing Distance (ALD), landing performance of the aircraft, and slope of all LAHSO combinations at the destination airport.
questions.choice_b: the published runway length and slope for all LAHSO combinations at the airport of intended landing.
questions.choice_c: the landing performance of the aircraft, published Available Landing Distance (ALD) for all LAHSO combinations at the airport of intended landing, plus the forecast winds.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: To conduct LAHSO, you should become familiar with all available information concerning LAHSO at the destination airport. You should have readily available the published ALD and runway slope information for all LAHSO runway combinations at each airport of intended landing. Additionally, knowledge about landing performance of your airplane permits you to readily determine that the ALD for the assigned runway is sufficient for safe LAHSO. Answer (B) is incorrect. To conduct LAHSO, you should have the available landing distance (ALD), not the published runway length. Answer (C) is incorrect. To conduct LAHSO, a pilot would want to have the current, not forecast, winds to assist in determining if the landing performance would be sufficient for safe LAHSO.

questions.question_text: The airport markings, signage, and lighting associated with Land and Hold Short Operations (LAHSO) consist of
questions.choice_a: yellow hold-short markings, red and white signage, and in-pavement lights.
questions.choice_b: red and white signage, yellow hold-short markings, and at some airports, in-pavement lights.
questions.choice_c: red and black signage, in-pavement lights, and yellow hold-short markings.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Situational awareness Is vital to the success of LAHSO. Situational awareness includes a thorough understanding of the airport markings, signage, and lighting associated with LAHSO. These visual aids consist of yellow hold- short markings, red and white signage, and, at some airports, in- pavement lighting. Answer (A) is incorrect. Not all airports will have in-pavement lighting. Answer (C) is incorrect. The signage is red and white, not red and black, and not all airports will have in-pavement lighting.

questions.question_text: Bird strikes in flight will be reported to the
questions.choice_a: nearest state or federal wildlife office on company letterhead.
questions.choice_c: nearest FSS via telephone. END OF STUDY UNIT
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: The FAA strongly encourages pilots, airport operations, aircraft maintenance personnel, Air Traffic Control personnel, engine manufacturers, or anyone else who has knowledge of a strike to report it to the National Wildlife Strike Database (NWSD). Anyone reporting a strike can print the FAA’s Bird/Other Wildlife Strike Report Form 5200-7. Paper copies of Form 5200-7 may be obtained from the appropriate Airports District Offices (ADO), Flight Standards District Offices (FSDO), and Flight Service Stations (FSS) or from the Aeronautical Information Manual (AIM). Answer (A) is incorrect. The federal wildlife office does not investigate or record aviation wildlife strikes. Answer (C) is incorrect. The FSS would be able to supply you with the appropriate form to fill out, but they do not investigate or record wildlife strikes.

questions.question_text: When a composite flight plan indicates VFR for the first portion of the flight, what is the procedure for the transition?
questions.choice_a: The VFR portion is automatically canceled and the IFR portion is automatically activated when the pilot reports IFR conditions.
questions.choice_b: The pilot should advise ATC to cancel VFR and activate the IFR portion of the flight.
questions.choice_c: The pilot should close the VFR portion with the nearest FSS and request the IFR clearance at least 5 minutes prior to IFR.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: When a composite flight plan indicates VFR for the first portion of the flight, the pilot should report the departure time to the FSS where the composite flight plan was filed. To transition, the pilot should close the VFR portion with the nearest FSS and request the IFR clearance at least 5 min. prior to the point at which change from VFR to IFR is proposed. Answer (A) is incorrect. VFR flight plans are never automatically closed; it is the pilot’s responsibility to close a VFR flight plan. An IFR clearance must be requested and received before entering IFR conditions in controlled airspace. It is not automatically activated when the pilot reports IFR conditions. Answer (B) is incorrect. The pilot should cancel a VFR flight plan with the nearest FSS, not ATC.

questions.question_text: How are random RNAV routes below FL 390 defined on the IFR flight plan?
questions.choice_a: Define route waypoints using degree-distance fixes based on appropriate navigational aids for the route and altitude at 250 NM intervals.
questions.choice_b: List the initial and final fix with at least one waypoint each 200 NM.
questions.choice_c: Review and enter appropriate arrival and departure transition fixes or navigation aids for the altitude being flown. Define the random route waypoints by using degree-distance fixes based on navigation aids appropriate for the altitude being flown.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: When filing an IFR flight plan for a proposed random RNAV route below FL 390, in block 8, Route of Flight, you must define the route waypoints by using degree-distance fixes based on navigational aids which are appropriate for the route and altitude. This question requires how a random route is to be defined on an IFR flight plan, not how the flight is to be planned. Answer (A) is incorrect. The distances should be within 200 NM, not 250 NM. Answer (B) is incorrect. The route must contain a minimum of one route waypoint for each ARTCC through whose area the RNAV route will be flown. These waypoints must be located within 200 NM of the preceding center’s boundary, not every 200 NM. .

questions.question_text: When a composite flight plan indicates IFR for the first portion of the flight, what is the procedure for the transition?
questions.choice_a: The IFR portion is automatically canceled and the VFR portion is automatically activated when the pilot reports VFR conditions.
questions.choice_b: The pilot should advise ATC to cancel the IFR portion and contact the nearest FSS to activate the VFR portion.
questions.choice_c: The pilot should advise ATC to cancel the IFR portion and activate the VFR portion.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: When a composite flight plan indicates IFR for the first portion of flight and VFR for the latter portion, the pilot will normally be cleared to the point at which the change is proposed. Once the pilot has reported over the clearance limit and does not desire further IFR clearance, the pilot should advise ATC to cancel the IFR portion and contact the nearest FSS to activate the VFR portion. Answer (A) is incorrect. A pilot can operate on an IFR flight plan in VFR conditions without the IFR portion being automatically canceled. When a composite flight plan indicates IFR for the first portion, the pilot will normally be cleared to the point at which transition is proposed. Here the pilot should advise ATC to cancel the IFR portion and contact the nearest FSS to activate the VFR portion. Answer (C) is incorrect. The pilot should contact the nearest FSS, not ATC, to activate the VFR portion.

questions.question_text: Which IFR fix(es) should be entered on a composite flight plan?
questions.choice_a: All compulsory reporting points en route.
questions.choice_b: The VOR’s that define the IFR portion of the flight.
questions.choice_c: The fix where the IFR portion is to be terminated.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: On composite flight plans, the IFR portion should include the items of a standard IFR flight plan. These items include points of transition from one airway to another, fixes defining direct route segments, and the clearance limit fix where the IFR portion is to be terminated. Answer (A) is incorrect. Compulsory reporting points are not listed on an IFR flight plan unless they define a point of transition, direct route segments, or the clearance limit fix. Also, there are no compulsory reporting points for a VFR flight. Answer (B) is incorrect. IFR fixes can be defined as intersections, waypoints, and DME distance, along with VORs.

questions.question_text: How should an off-airway direct flight be defined on an IFR flight plan?
questions.choice_a: The initial fix, the true course, and the final fix.
questions.choice_b: All radio fixes over which the flight will pass.
questions.choice_c: The initial fix, all radio fixes which the pilot wishes to be compulsory reporting points, and the final fix.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: All or any portion of an off-airway direct flight must be defined on an IFR flight plan by indicating the radio fixes over which the flight will pass. These fixes automatically become compulsory reporting points for the flight, unless advised otherwise by ATC. Answer (A) is incorrect. True course is not an item that is reported on an IFR flight plan. The initial fix and the final fix are listed as radio fixes that define the start and finish points of a flight. Answer (C) is incorrect. Initial and final fixes are required to define random RNAV, not direct flight, routes. All radio fixes that define the route of a direct flight automatically become compulsory reporting points, not only those the pilot desires.

questions.question_text: How should the route of flight be defined on an IFR flight plan?
questions.choice_a: Asimplified route via airways or jet routes with transitions.
questions.choice_b: A route via airways or jet routes with VOR’s and fixes used.
questions.choice_c: A route via airways or jet routes with only the compulsory reporting points.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: It is vitally important that the route of flight be defined accurately and completely on an IFR flight plan. To simplify definition of the route and to facilitate ATC, pilots are requested to file via airways or jet routes with transitions. Answer (B) is incorrect. To simplify the route, all VORs and fixes are not used to define a route on an IFR flight plan. Answer (C) is incorrect. Compulsory reporting points may not define the transitions between airways or jet routes.

questions.question_text: What is the suggested time interval for filing and requesting an IFR flight plan?
questions.choice_a: File at least 30 minutes prior to departure and request the clearance not more than 10 minutes prior to taxi.
questions.choice_b: File at least 30 minutes prior to departure and request the clearance at least 10 minutes prior to taxi.
questions.choice_c: File at least 1 hour prior to departure and request the clearance at least 10 minutes prior to taxi.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Pilots should file IFR flight plans at least 30 min. prior to estimated time of departure to prevent possible delays in receiving a departure clearance from ATC. Certain airports have established pretaxi clearance programs through which pilots of IFR airplanes may contact clearance delivery or ground control not more than 10 min. prior to taxi time. Answer (B) is incorrect. A pilot can request the clearance not more than 10 min., not at least 10 min., prior to taxi. Answer (C) is incorrect. Pilots should file at least 30 min., not 1 hr., prior to departure and request the clearance not more than 10 min., not at least 10 min., prior to taxi.

questions.question_text: What is one limitation when filing a random RNAV route on an IFR flight plan?
questions.choice_a: The waypoints must be located within 200 NM of each other.
questions.choice_b: The entire route must be within radar environment.
questions.choice_c: The waypoints may only be defined by degree-distance fixes based on appropriate navigational aids.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Random RNAV routes can be approved only when the entire route is in a radar environment. ATC will monitor each random RNAV flight, but navigation on the route is the responsibility of the pilot. Answer (A) is incorrect. VOR/VORTAC (H) facilities, not waypoints, must be within 200 NM of each other when operating above FL 450 to define a direct, not random RNAV, route. Answer (C) is incorrect. Random RNAV waypoints may be defined by degree-distance fixes based on appropriate navigational aids or by latitude-longitude coordinate navigation, independent of VOR/TACAN references, operating at and above FL 390 in the conterminous U.S.

questions.question_text: Under what condition may a pilot file an IFR flight plan containing a special or privately owned IAP?
questions.choice_c: Upon signing a waiver of responsibility.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Pilots planning flights to locations served by a special or privately owned IAP should obtain advance approval from the owner of the procedure. These IAPs may require special crew training qualifications or other special considerations to execute the approach. Approval by the owner is necessary because special procedures are for the exclusive use of the owner, and these approaches may be based on privately owned navigational aids. Answer (A) is incorrect. ATC is not required to question pilots to determine whether they have the owner’s permission to use the procedure. Answer (C) is incorrect. A pilot is responsible for the safe operation of the airplane. To sign a waiver of responsibility is contrary to the pilot’s responsibilities.

questions.question_text: Under what condition may a pilot cancel an IFR flight plan prior to completing the flight?
questions.choice_a: Anytime it appears the clearance will cause a deviation from FARs.
questions.choice_b: Anytime within controlled airspace by contacting ARTCC.
questions.choice_c: Only if in VFR conditions in other than Class A airspace.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: An IFR flight plan may be canceled at any time the flight is operating in VFR conditions outside Class A airspace by the pilot’s stating, “Cancel my IFR flight plan.” Once accepted by ATC, a pilot should change to the appropriate communications frequency, VFR transponder code, and VFR altitude. Answer (A) is incorrect. Anytime a clearance appears to deviate from an FAR, the pilot should request clarification from ATC and an amended clearance. Answer (B) is incorrect. All aircraft in Class A airspace (above FL 180) or when operating in IMC must be operating under an IFR flight plan.

questions.question_text: In what way are SIDs depicted in plan view?
questions.choice_a: “Vectors” provided for navigational guidance or “Pilot NAV” with courses the pilot is responsible to follow. “Vectors” and “Pilot NAV” for pilots to use at their discretion. Combined textual and graphic form which are mandatory routes and instructions.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: SID procedures will be depicted in one of two basic forms. “Vectors” are established where ATC will provide radar navigational guidance to a filed/assigned route or to a fix depicted on the SID. “Pilot NAV” indicates where the pilot is primarily responsible for navigation on the SID route. These may contain vector instructions which pilots are expected to comply with until notified to resume normal navigation on the filed/assigned route or SID procedure. Answer (B) is incorrect. SIDs are departure procedures and must be followed as depicted, not at the pilot’s discretion. If a pilot does not wish to use a SID, then (s)he must notify ATC. Answer (C) is incorrect. While SIDs are published in textual and graphic form, they are mandatory only if the SID was given as part of a clearance.

questions.question_text: What is the difference between an obstacle departure procedure (ODP) and a standard instrument departure (SID)? An ODP provides obstacle clearance protection but does not include ATC-related climb requirements for traffic separation. An ODP typically provides for traffic separation in busy terminal areas, but a SID provides both obstacle clearance and aircraft separation. An ODP provides both obstacle and aircraft separation, but a SID is designed for ATC to provide aircraft separation on departure routes.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Obstacle departure procedures are only used for obstruction clearance and do not include ATC-related climb requirements. The primary emphasis of ODP design is to use the least onerous route of flight to the en route structure while attempting to accommodate typical departure routes. Answer (B) is incorrect. ODP does not use ATC for traffic separation. It is only used for obstruction clearance. Answer (C) is incorrect. ODP only provides obstacle clearance and not aircraft separation.

questions.question_text: What is the primary purpose of a STAR? Provide separation between IFR and VFR traffic. Simplify clearance delivery procedures. Decrease traffic congestion at certain airports.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: A STAR is an ATC coded IFR arrival route established for use by arriving IFR traffic at certain airports. Its purpose is to simplify clearance delivery procedures. Answer (A) is incorrect. Separation between IFR and VFR traffic is provided by Class B or TRSA radar service, not a STAR. Answer (C) is incorrect. A STAR is used to simplify clearance delivery procedures, thus decreasing radio frequency, not traffic, congestion at certain airports.

questions.question_text: When does ATC issue a STAR? Only when ATC deems it appropriate. Only to high priority flights. Only upon request of the pilot.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Pilots of IFR airplanes destined to locations for which STARs have been published may be issued a clearance containing a STAR whenever ATC deems it appropriate. A pilot must have at least a textual description in his or her possession. It is the responsibility of each pilot to accept or refuse an issued STAR. Answer (B) is incorrect. Any type of IFR flight can be issued a STAR. High priority flights will normally be handled in an expeditious manner by ATC. Answer (C) is incorrect. A STAR is a clearance delivery procedure that is issued by ATC. A pilot has the responsibility to accept or refuse that clearance. A pilot can list a STAR in the flight plan, but ATC will issue one only if appropriate.

questions.question_text: What is the normal procedure for IFR departures at locations with pretaxi clearance programs?
questions.choice_a: Pilots request IFR clearance when ready to taxi. The pilot will receive taxi instruction with clearance.
questions.choice_b: Pilots request IFR clearance when ready to taxi. Pilots will receive taxi clearance, then receive IFR clearance while taxiing or on runup.
questions.choice_c: Pilots request IFR clearance 10 minutes or less prior to taxi, then request taxi clearance from ground control.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Pretaxi clearance programs allow pilots of departing IFR airplanes to receive their IFR clearances before they start taxiing for takeoff. Pilot participation is not mandatory. Participating pilots should request IFR clearance 10 min. or less prior to taxi, then request taxi clearance from ground control. Answer (A) is incorrect. At a location with a pretaxi clearance program, the pilot will first receive the IFR clearance, then taxi instructions. The pilot should request the IFR clearance 10 min. or less prior to taxi time. Answer (B) is incorrect. At a location with a pretaxi clearance program, the pilot will first be given the IFR clearance, then the taxi clearance. The pilot should request the IFR clearance 10 min. or less prior to taxi time.

questions.question_text: What is the purpose of the term “hold for release” when included in an IFR clearance?
questions.choice_a: A procedure for delaying departure for traffic volume, weather, or need to issue further instructions.
questions.choice_b: When an IFR clearance is received by telephone, the pilot will have time to prepare for takeoff prior to being released.
questions.choice_c: Gate hold procedures are in effect and the pilot receives an estimate of the time the flight will be released.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: ATC may issue “hold for release” instructions in a clearance to delay an airplane’s departure for traffic management reasons (i.e., weather, traffic volume, etc.). The pilot may not depart until (s)he receives a release time or is given additional instructions by ATC. Answer (B) is incorrect. When a pilot receives an IFR clearance via telephone, it is normally because (s)he is departing from an uncontrolled airport. In this case, ATC issues a clearance void time, not a hold for release. Answer (C) is incorrect. Gate hold procedures are in effect whenever departure delays exceed (or are expected to exceed) 15 min. This procedure is not a way for ATC to delay an airplane’s departure.

questions.question_text: What minimum information does an abbreviated departure clearance “cleared as filed” include?
questions.choice_a: Clearance limit, transponder code, and DP, if appropriate.
questions.choice_b: Destination airport, en route altitude, transponder code, and DP, if appropriate.
questions.choice_c: Clearance limit and en route altitude.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: The minimum information in an abbreviated IFR departure clearance will include the destination airport. The en route altitude, transponder code and departure procedure (DP), as well as any additional instruction will also be included. Answer (A) is incorrect. This option does not specify the destination airport, which is included in an abbreviated departure clearance. Answer (C) is incorrect. This option does not specify the destination airport or a DP. The destination airport is always included in an abbreviated departure clearance. DPs are also included whenever they are appropriate.

questions.question_text: What action should the pilot take when “gate hold” procedures are in effect?
questions.choice_a: Contact ground control prior to starting engines for sequencing.
questions.choice_b: Taxi into position and hold prior to requesting clearance.
questions.choice_c: Start engines, perform pretakeoff check, and request clearance prior to leaving the parking area.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Pilots should contact ground control or clearance delivery prior to starting engines as gate hold procedures will be in effect whenever departure delays exceed or are anticipated to exceed 15 min. Pilots should monitor the ground control or clearance delivery frequency for engine startup advisories or new proposed start time if the delay changes. The sequence for departure will normally be maintained in accordance with the initial call-up. __ Answer (B) is incorrect. “Taxi into position” means that the pilot is on the active runway and ready for takeoff. This is not a position where ATC would issue an IFR clearance. Answer (C) is incorrect. Pilots should contact ground control for sequencing before, not after, starting engines.

questions.question_text: Aircraft are expected to depart no earlier than 5 minutes before and no later than 5 minutes after the EDCT. depart no earlier than 5 minutes before and no later than 15 minutes after the EDCT. depart no earlier than 15 minutes before and no later than 15 minutes after the EDCT.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: The Expect Departure Clearance Time (EDCT) is the runway release time assigned to an aircraft included in traffic management programs. Aircraft are expected to depart no earlier than 5 minutes before and no later than 5 minutes after the EDCT. Answer (B) is incorrect. Aircraft are expected to depart no earlier than 5 minutes before and no later than 5, not 15, minutes after the EDCT. Answer (C) is incorrect. Aircraft are expected to depart no earlier than 5, not 15, minutes before and no later than 5, not 15, minutes after the EDCT.

questions.question_text: What special consideration is given for turbine- powered aircraft when “gate hold” procedures are in effect? They are given preference for departure over other aircraft. They are expected to be ready for takeoff when they reach the runway or warmup block. They are expected to be ready for takeoff prior to taxi and will receive takeoff clearance prior to taxi.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: When gate hold procedures are in effect, ATC will consider that pilots of turbine-powered aircraft are ready for takeoff when they reach the runway or warmup block. Answer (A) is incorrect. When gate hold procedures are in effect, sequencing of all airplanes is based on the initial call-up to ground control or clearance delivery. Answer (C) is incorrect. A pilot of any airplane should be ready to taxi, not take off, prior to taxi, and takeoff clearance is received prior to takeoff, not taxi.

questions.question_text: Pilots should state their position on the airport when calling the tower for takeoff from a runway intersection. from a runway intersection, only at night. from a runway intersection, only during instrument conditions.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: In order to enhance airport capacities, reduce taxiing distances, minimize departure delays, and provide for more efficient movement of air traffic, controllers may initiate intersection takeoffs as well as approve them when the pilot requests. A pilot should state his or her position on the airport when calling the tower for takeoff from a runway intersection. Answer (B) is incorrect. A pilot should state his or her position on the airport when calling the tower for takeoff from a runway intersection at all times. Answer (C) is incorrect. A pilot should state his or her position on the airport when calling the tower for takeoff from a runway intersection under all, not just instrument, conditions.

questions.question_text: When should transponders be operated on the ground while taxiing? Only when ATC specifically requests your transponder to be activated. Any time when the airport is operating under IFR. All the time when at an airport with ASDE-X.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: If operating at an airport with Airport Surface Detection Equipment - Model X (ASDE-X), transponders should be transmitting continuously with altitude reporting while moving on the airport surface. ASDE-X is a multi-sensor surface surveillance system the FAA is acquiring for airports in the United States. Answer (A) is incorrect. If you are operating at an airport with ASDE-X, your transponder should be transmitting continuously with altitude reporting while moving on the airport surface, not only when ATC specifically requests your transponder to be activated. Answer (B) is incorrect. If you are operating at an airport with ASDE-X, your transponder should be transmitting continuously with altitude reporting while moving on the airport surface, not only when the airport is operating under IFR.

questions.question_text: When taxiing on an airport with ASDE-X, you should
questions.choice_a: operate the transponder only when the airport is under IFR or at night during your taxi.
questions.choice_b: operate the transponder with altitude reporting all of the time during taxiing.
questions.choice_c: be ready to activate the transponder upon ATC request while taxiing.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: If operating at an airport with Airport Surface Detection Equipment - Model X (ASDE-X), transponders should be transmitting continuously with altitude reporting while moving on the airport surface. ASDE-X is a multi-sensor surface surveillance system the FAA is acquiring for airports in the United States. Answer (A) is incorrect. When taxiing on an airport with ASDE-X, you should operate the transponder with altitude reporting all the time during taxiing, not only when the airport is under IFR or at night. Answer (C) is incorrect. When taxiing on an airport with ASDE-X, you should operate the transponder with altitude reporting all the time during taxiing, not just upon ATC request.

questions.question_text: Hot Spots are depicted on airport diagrams as
questions.choice_a: squares or rectangles around “HS and a number.”
questions.choice_b: circles or polygons around “HS and a number.”
questions.choice_c: triangles or blocks filled with “HS” and a number.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: An airport surface Hot Spot is a location on an airport movement area with a history or potential risk of collision or runway incursion. Heightened attention by pilots and drivers is necessary. Hot Spots are depicted on airport diagrams as open circles or polygons and designated as “HS 1,” “HS 2,” etc. Answer (A) is incorrect. Hot Spots are depicted on airport diagrams as open circles or polygons, not squares or rectangles, and designated as “HS 1,” “HS 2,” etc. Answer (C) is incorrect. Hot Spots are depicted on airport diagrams as open circles or polygons, not triangles or blocks, and designated as “HS 1,” “HS 2,” etc.

questions.question_text: An airport movement area is
questions.choice_b: an area requiring permission from ATC to move an aircraft.
questions.choice_c: defined as ramps and aprons.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: A movement area is defined as the runways, taxiways, and other areas of an airport and heliport which are utilized for taxiing or hover taxiing, air taxiing, takeoff and landing of aircraft, exclusive of loading ramps and parking areas. At those airports and heliports with an operating control tower, specific approval for entry onto the movement area must be obtained from ATC. Answer (A) is incorrect. An ATC control zone (CTR or controlled traffic region) is a volume of controlled airspace, normally around an airport, which extends from the surface to a specified upper limit and is established to protect air traffic operating to and from that airport. A movement area does not include the airspace above it. Answer (C) is incorrect. A movement area is exclusive of ramps and aprons.

questions.question_text: Under what condition does a pilot receive a “void time” specified in the clearance? On an uncontrolled airport. a When “gate hold” procedures are in effect.
questions.choice_c: If the clearance is received prior to starting engines.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: A pilot will receive a “void time’ in an IFR clearance when operating from an uncontrolled airport. If the flight has not departed prior to the void time, the clearance is void. The pilot who is still on the ground at the void time must advise ATC of his or her intentions as soon as possible, normally within a maximum of 30 min. Answer (B) is incorrect. Gate hold procedures are in effect whenever departure delays exceed or are anticipated to exceed 15 min. Pilots should contact ground control or clearance delivery prior to starting engines to receive departure clearance and engine startup advisories. Answer (C) is incorrect. Clearances can be issued before starting the airplane’s engine(s). Only when aera from an uncontrolled airport will the clearance contain a void time.

questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 243 on page 324 and Figure 244 on page 325.) At Fort Lauderdale, HS 5 probably points out a potential operational risk of an airplane
questions.choice_a: mistaking taxiway B for runway O9L and making the takeoff roll on the taxiway.
questions.choice_b: landing runway O9L and taxiing on C.
questions.choice_c: landing runway 27R and clearing taxiway B2, which crosses runway 13.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: An airport hot spot is defined as a location on an airport movement area with a history of potential risk of collision or runway incursion, and where heightened attention by pilots and drivers is necessary. Refer to the hot-spot description to the right of Fort Lauderdale, FL airport, HS 5. This gives the description of the hazard that is present, taxiway departure risk. If a pilot is not careful, a departure could be done on taxiway B instead of Rwy O9L. Answer (B) is incorrect. Taxiway C is near the very beginning of Rwy O9L. Landing on Rwy O9L and then taxiing on taxiway C would be improbable. Answer (C) is incorrect. Taxiway B2 does not cross runway 13. There is no hot spot circle near taxiway B2 or runway 13.

questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 243 on page 324 and Figure 244 on page 325.) At Fort Lauderdale-Hollywood International, HS 1 probably points out a potential incursion risk of an airplane
questions.choice_a: taxiing across runway O9L at taxiway Bravo for a runway 31 departure.
questions.choice_b: taxiing onto runway O9L instead of runway 13 as instructed for takeoff.
questions.choice_c: landing on runway 13 and clearing at taxiway Bravo and crossing runway 30.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: An airport hot spot is defined as a location on an airport movement area with a history of potential risk of collision or runway incursion, and where heightened attention by pilots and drivers is necessary. Refer to the hot spot description to the right of Fort Lauderdale, FL airport, HS 1. It list the hot spot as “Twy E at Rwy O9L-27R.” Fig. 244 shows HS 1 near the intersection of Rwy 13 and O9L. This could be an area of potential incursion risk for a pilot to taxi onto Runway O9L for takeoff instead of Runway 13, the runway the pilot was cleared to take off on. Answer (A) is incorrect. HS 1 is not indicating that taxiway Bravo is a hot spot as there is no hot spot circle on a Bravo taxiway. Answer (C) is incorrect. There is not a runway 30 to cross.

questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 241 on page 327 and Figure 242 on page 328.) At Long Beach (LGB), HS 2 probably points out a potential incursion hazard of an airplane
questions.choice_a: landing on runway 25R and taxiing on K3.
questions.choice_b: taxiing across runway 34L at taxiway G.
questions.choice_c: landing on runway 25R and clearing at taxiway B, which crosses runway 30.
questions.explanation: An airport hot spot is defined as a location on an airport movement area with a history of potential risk of collision or runway incursion, and where heightened attention by pilots and drivers is necessary. Refer to the first page of Fig. 241 and the column to the right of Long Beach, CA, Long Beach (Daugherty Fld) (LGB). Look at HS 2. This hot spot refers to Rwy 12-30 and Rwy 07L-25R, taxiway B and taxiway K. Landing long on Rwy 25R would have the aircraft exit the runway at taxiway B. Taxiway B crosses Rwy 30. The hot spot is taxiway B with the circle around it. Answer (A) is incorrect. Taxiway K3 has no hot-spot circle around that area and it runs into taxiway K. There are on hot spots in those areas. Answer (B) is incorrect. The hot spot for Rwy 34L is taxiway F as indicated by the circle around runway 34 and taxiway F.

questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 241 on page 327 and Figure 242 on page 328.) At Long Beach (LGB), HS 5 probably points out a potential operational risk of an airplane
questions.choice_a: landing runway 7R and mistakenly crossing runway 34L while taxiing on taxiway F.
questions.choice_b: landing runway 25R and mistakenly turning onto runway 34R instead of taxiway K.
questions.choice_c: landing runway 7R and crossing runway 12.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: An airport hot spot is defined as a location on an airport movement area with a history of potential risk of collision or runway incursion, and where heightened attention by pilots and drivers is necessary. Refer to the hot-spot description to the right of Long Beach (LGB) airport, HS 5. The hazard is located at taxiway F and the approach end of runway 34. If a pilot is not careful, (s)he may taxi on taxiway F and cross runway 34L. Answer (B) is incorrect. If landing on runway 25R, there is no taxiway K to exit on. Answer (C) is incorrect. If a pilot landed long on runway 7R, (s)he may cross runway 12, but there is no hot- spot circle in that area and no HS 5 label near that runway.

questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 242 on page 328.) ATC instructs you to taxi to runway 12 via taxiway K, cross runway 34R, and hold short of runway 30. You know
questions.choice_a: to taxi on K from the terminal ramp, cross 34R, and then hold short of runway 30.
questions.choice_b: you must call prior to crossing runway 34R.
questions.choice_c: to taxi to runway 12 via taxiway G and D after clearing runway 34R.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Clearance must be obtained prior to taxiing on a runway, taking off, or landing during the hours an Airport Traffic Control Tower is in operation. A clearance must be obtained prior to crossing any runway. ATC will issue an explicit clearance for all runway crossings. ATC will not use the word “cleared” in conjunction with authorization for aircraft to taxi. All hold-short instructions must be read back to ATC. Answer (B) is incorrect. ATC gave an explicit clearance for crossing runway 34R, therefore you do not need to call ATC prior to crossing runway 34R. Answer (C) is incorrect. ATC gave instructions to taxi to runway 12 via taxiway K, not via taxiway G and D.

questions.question_text: An ATC “instruction”
questions.choice_a: is the same as an ATC “clearance.”
questions.choice_b: is a directive issued by ATC for the purpose of requiring a pilot to take a specific action.
questions.choice_c: must be “read back” in full to the controller and confirmed before becoming effective.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: An ATC instruction is a directive issued by ATC for the purpose of requiring a pilot to take a specific action, e.g., “Turn left, heading 250,” “Go around,” “Clear the runway,” etc. Answer (A) is incorrect. An ATC clearance is an authorization by ATC for an aircraft to proceed under specified traffic conditions within controlled airspace. An instruction is more specific; e.g., “Turn left heading 250.” Answer (C) is incorrect. Although instructions should be (and some are required to be) read back to the controller, they are effective immediately.

questions.question_text: What is the pilot’s responsibility for clearance or instruction readback?
questions.choice_a: Except for SIDs, read back altitude assignments, altitude restrictions, and vectors.
questions.choice_b: If the clearance or instruction is understood, an acknowledgment is sufficient.
questions.choice_c: Read back the entire clearance or instruction to confirm the message is understood.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Pilots should read back the parts of ATC clearances and instructions containing altitude assignments or vectors as a means of mutual verification. Altitudes, altitude restrictions, and vectors should be read back in the same sequence as they are given in the clearance or instruction. Altitudes contained in charted procedures, such as SIDs, should not be read back unless they are specifically stated by the controller. Answer (B) is incorrect. The best way to know that the clearance or instruction is understood is to read back the “numbers” as a double-check between the pilot and ATC. This practice reduces the kinds of communication errors that occur when a number is either misheard or incorrect. Answer (C) is incorrect. The pilot's responsibility is to read back the clearances and instructions containing altitude assignments, altitude restrictions, and vectors, not the entire clearance or instruction.

questions.question_text: What action should a pilot take when a clearance is received from ATC that appears to be contrary to a regulation?
questions.choice_a: Read the clearance back in its entirety.
questions.choice_c: Do not accept the clearance.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: FAR 91.3(a) states: “The pilot in command of an aircraft is directly responsible for, and is the final authority as to, the operation of that aircraft.” If ATC issues a clearance that would cause a pilot to deviate from a rule or regulation or, in the pilot’s opinion, would place the airplane in jeopardy, it is the pilot's responsibility to request a clarification from ATC. Answer (A) is incorrect. Reading the clearance back in its entirety does not inform ATC of the possible conflict to a regulation. A pilot should actively seek clarification if there is any doubt. Answer (C) is incorrect. Not accepting a clearance is not the proper procedure to use when, in a pilot’s opinion, it would conflict with a regulation. First, a pilot should receive a clarification from ATC, then ask for an amended clearance, if necessary.

questions.question_text: What action should a pilot take if within 3 minutes of a clearance limit and further clearance has not been received?
questions.choice_a: Assume lost communications and continue as planned.
questions.choice_b: Plan to hold at cruising speed until further clearance is received.
questions.choice_c: Start a speed reduction to holding speed in preparation for holding.
questions.explanation: i DISCUSSION: When an airplane is within 3 min. from a clearance limit and a clearance beyond the fix has not been received, the pilot is expected to start a speed reduction so the airplane will initially cross the fix at or below the maximum holding speed in preparation for holding. id he: Answer (A) is incorrect. If two-way communications are lost, the pilot is required to hold at the clearance limit in a standard pattern on the course that was used to approach the fix. If an expected further clearance time was received, (s)he should plan on leaving the fix at that time. If none was given and the fix is an IAP, (s)he should plan his or her arrival as close as possible to the estimated time of arrival. Answer (B) is incorrect. Cruising speed may be greater than maximum holding speed. For all propeller-driven airplanes (including turboprop), the maximum holding speed is 175 KIAS. For turbojet airplanes, the maximum holding airspeed is 200 KIAS from minimum holding altitude through 6,000 ft.; 230 KIAS from above 6,000 ft. through 14,000 ft.; and 265 KIAS above 14,000 ft.

questions.question_text: What report should the pilot make at a clearance limit?
questions.choice_a: Time and altitude/flight level arriving or leaving.
questions.choice_b: Time, altitude/flight level, and expected holding speed.
questions.choice_c: Time, altitude/flight level, expected holding speed, and inbound leg length.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: The pilot should report to ATC the time and altitude/flight level at which the airplane reaches the clearance limit, and (s)he should report leaving the clearance limit. Answer (B) is incorrect. ATC does not need the expected holding speed reported since it will be at or below the maximum holding airspeed. Answer (C) is incorrect. Inbound leg lengths are set by time or DME distance. At or below 14,000 ft. MSL, there is a 1-min. inbound leg. Above 14,000 ft. MSL, the inbound leg is 1 1/2 min.

questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 242 on page 331.) You received these ATC taxi instructions: “Taxi to Runway 30 via Lima and hold short of Runway 25L.” Your airplane is on the ramp by the terminal and NWS on the east side of the airport. Your taxi route
questions.choice_a: requires crossing of Runway 25L at Lima.
questions.choice_c: requires crossing Runway 34R en route to the assigned runway.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: While ATC has instructed you to hold short of Runway 25L, your taxi route to Runway 30 will eventually require you to cross Runway 25L at taxiway Lima. Answer (B) is incorrect. Your taxi route will keep you west of Runway 34R at all times, but hot spot 4 (HS 4) is east of Runway 34R. Answer (C) is incorrect. Your taxi route requires you to cross Runway 25L, not Runway 34R, en route to Runway 30.

questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 242 on page 331 and Figure 241 on page 332.) As you rolled out long on Runway 30 after landing at Long Beach (LGB), you slowed and turned left on very wide pavement and now see Taxiway D signs on both sides of your pavement. You notice your heading is about 250°. Tower is urging you to turn left on D, cross 16R/34L, then taxi to G and hold short of Runway 30. You now know you
questions.choice_a: exited onto Runway 25R and transited HS 2.
questions.choice_c: exited at Taxiway J and transited HS 4.
questions.explanation: Runway 25R, which is 150 feet wide, intersects Runway 30 near the departure end. If you turned left onto Runway 25R from Runway 30, you would initially see the intersection of Taxiway D, which crosses the runway at an angle. Before the intersection, both sides of the runway would be marked with direction signs featuring a black “D” on a yellow background. Turning left onto Taxiway D and crossing Runway 16R/34L would require you to transit hot spot 2 (HS 72). Answer (B) is incorrect. If you had exited on Taxiway G, ATC would not have instructed you to turn left onto Taxiway D and cross Runway 16R/34L. Answer (C) is incorrect. Exiting at Taxiway J near hot spot 4 (HS 4) would require exiting near the midpoint of Runway 30, not near the end, as would be the case after a long rollout.

questions.question_text: As you call for taxi instructions, the key words to understand are
questions.choice_c: taxi to and “expedite.”
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: When giving taxi instructions, ATC will specify the destination on the airport, issue a taxi route, and state any hold-short instructions or runway crossing clearances if the taxi route will cross a runway. A clearance must be obtained prior to crossing any runway, and pilots should always read back any instruction to hold short of or cross a specific runway. Therefore, the key words to understand are “hold short of’ and “cross.” Answer (A) is incorrect. In order to preclude misunderstandings in radio communications, ATC will not use the word “cleared” in conjunction with authorization for aircraft to taxi. Answer (C) is incorrect. The key words to understand are “hold short of’ and “cross,” not “taxi to” and “expedite.”

questions.question_text: You must
questions.choice_b: read back runway hold-short instructions only if you are unsure of their meaning.
questions.choice_c: read back runway hold-short instructions.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: ATC is required to obtain a read-back from the pilot of all runway hold-short instructions. Answer (A) is incorrect. Acknowledging hold-short instructions is not enough. You are required to read back all runway hold-short instructions. Answer (B) is incorrect. You must read back all hold-short instructions, whether you are sure or unsure of their meaning.

questions.question_text: When ATC clears you onto or across a runway,
questions.choice_a: scan the full runway and the runway approach corridor prior to entering the runway.
questions.choice_b: proceed as quickly as possible to help with traffic flow.
questions.choice_c: call ATC as soon as you are in position or clear of the runway for further instructions.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Whenever a pilot is going to enter a runway, whether that runway is active or not, (s)he should be vigilant when doing so. The pilot should scan the full length of the runway and look for aircraft on final approach or landing roll-out. (S)he should also check both right and left corners of the downwind and base legs of the pattern. It is good practice to check and call out, “clear left, clear right, and clear to cross.” Answer (B) is incorrect. Taxiing as quickly as possible leads to incidents or accidents. It is not recommended. Answer (C) is incorrect. When in position, you should only call ATC if takeoff clearance is not received within a reasonable amount of time after clearance to line up and wait. Contacting ATC when clear of the runway is not necessary, unless further instructions have not been received.

questions.question_text: When ATC issues a clearance to descend “at pilot's discretion,” the pilot
questions.choice_a: can descend when the pilot wishes.
questions.choice_b: can climb back to the original altitude, if needed.
questions.choice_c: must maintain a descent rate of 2,000 ft/min.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: When a pilot receives the clearance to descend “at pilot’s discretion,” the pilot may descend at any time and any rate and may level off at any altitude during the descent. However, the pilot may not return to any vacated altitude. Answer (B) is incorrect. Once the descent is started, the pilot cannot return to the vacated altitude. Answer (C) is incorrect. The pilot can descend at any rate (s)he wishes.

questions.question_text: What action should a pilot take if asked by ARTCC to “VERIFY 9,000” and the flight is actually maintaining 8,000?
questions.choice_c: Report maintaining 8,000.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: At times a controller will ask a pilot to verify that (s)he is at a particular altitude. The pilot should confirm that (s)he is at the altitude stated by the controller, or (s)he should inform the controller of the actual altitude being maintained, if different. If asked by ARTCC to “VERIFY 9,000” and the flight is actually maintaining 8,000, the pilot should report maintaining 8,000. Answer (A) is incorrect. A pilot should not take action to change his or her actual altitude to the altitude stated in the controller's verification request unless the controller specifically authorizes a change. Answer (B) is incorrect. A pilot should not take action to change his or her actual altitude to the altitude stated in the controller’s verification request unless the controller specifically authorizes a change.

questions.question_text: Which reports are required when operating IFR in radar environment? DISCUSSION: When informed by ATC that his or her airplane is in “RADAR CONTACT,” the pilot should discontinue position reports over designated reporting points. A pilot will
questions.choice_a: Position reports, vacating an altitude, unable report at all times vacating an altitude, inability to climb/descend to climb 500 ft/min, and time and altitude at a rate of at least 500 fpm, time and altitude upon reaching a reaching a holding fix or point to which cleared. holding fix or point to which cleared, a change in average true
questions.choice_b: Position reports, vacating an altitude, unable airspeed exceeding 5% or 10 kt., leaving any assigned holding fix to climb 500 ft/min, time and altitude reaching or point, and a missed approach. eats: a holding fix or point to which cleared, and a Answer (A) is incorrect. Position reports are not required in a change in average true airspeed exceeding radar environment. A pilot must also report a change in average 5 percent or 10 knots. TAS (exceeding 5% or 10 kt.), leaving any assigned holding fix or j point, and a missed approach. Answer (B) is incorrect. Position
questions.choice_c: Vacating an altitude, unable to climb reports are not required in a radar environment. A pilot must also 500 ft/min, time and altitude reaching a holding fix or point to which cleared, a change in average true airspeed exceeding 5 percent or 10 knots, and leaving any assigned holding fix or point.
questions.explanation: report when leaving any assigned holding fix or point and when the approach has been missed.

questions.question_text: When a speed adjustment is necessary to maintain separation, what minimum speed may ATC request of a turbine-powered aircraft operating below
questions.choice_b: 210 knots. Answer (A) is incorrect. The maximum airspeed of any . Sapens airplane operating below 2,500 ft. AGL within 4 NM of the primary airport in Class C or Class D airspace, in a VFR- designated corridor through Class B airspace, or in airspace underlying Class B airspace is 200 kt. Answer (C) is incorrect. The maximum airspeed of any airplane operating below 10,000 ft. MSL and within Class B airspace is 250 kt.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: ATC will issue speed adjustments to pilots of radar-controlled aircraft to achieve or maintain required or 10,000 feet? desired spacing. ATC will express all speed adjustments in knots based on indicated airspeed in 10-kt. increments. ATC A, 1000 knots may request a minimum speed of 210 kt. of a turbine-powered ; : airplane operating below 10,000 ft. MSL.

questions.question_text: When a speed adjustment is necessary to maintain separation, what minimum speed may ATC request of a turbine-powered aircraft departing an
questions.choice_b: 210 knots. Answer (A) is incorrect. An“applicable airspeed for any ATC ote operation is not 188 kt. All airspeeds used by ATC/FARs are expressed in 10-kt. increments. Answer (B) is incorrect. The minimum airspeed that ATC can request of a turbine-powered airplane operating below 10,000 ft. MSL, excluding departing airplanes, is 210 kt.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: ATC will issue speed adjustments to pilots of radar-controlled airplanes to achieve or maintain required or airport? desired spacing. ATC will express all speed adjustments in knots based on indicated airspeed in 10-kt. increments. For departing Be MAS AOS. turbine-powered aircraft, the minimum speed that ATC may : request is 230 kt.

questions.question_text: If ATC requests a speed adjustment that is not within the operating limits of the aircraft, what action must the pilot take?
questions.choice_a: Maintain an airspeed within the operating limitations as close to the requested speed as possible.
questions.choice_b: Attempt to use the requested speed as long as possible, then request a reasonable airspeed from ATC.
questions.choice_c: Advise ATC of the airspeed that will be used.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: The pilot retains the authority to reject the application of speed adjustment by ATC. If the speed adjustment is not within the operating limits of the airplane, the pilot is expected to advise ATC of the airspeed that will be used. Answer (A) is incorrect. While a pilot should maintain at least the minimum safe airspeed for any particular operation, a pilot is expected to advise ATC of the airspeed being used when it differs from ATC’s requested speed adjustment. Answer (B) is incorrect. A pilot who uses an airspeed that is not within the operating limits of the airplane not only is in violation of FARs but also is risking the safety of all on board the airplane. A pilot must operate the airplane in a safe manner and advise ATC of the airspeed that will be used.

questions.question_text: An airplane operating in cruise at 10,000 feet, 200 knots calibrated with an OAT of 68°F has a true airspeed of
questions.choice_c: 244 knots. 7.7_VFR-on-Top
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Using an E6B flight computer, find the temperature conversion scale and covert 68°F to Celsius. 68°F equates to 20°C. Line up 10 in the pressure altitude window with the temperature scale above, at 20°C. Find 20 on the outer scale of the inner wheel. Across from 20 is the second hash mark past the 24. This represents 244 knots. Answer (A) is incorrect. An airspeed of 16 knots higher, or 216 knots, would require a calibrated airspeed of almost 178 knots. Answer (B) is incorrect. An airspeed of 33 knots higher, or 233 knots, would require a calibrated airspeed of almost 192 knots.

questions.question_text: Under what conditions may a pilot on an IFR flight plan comply with authorization to maintain “VFR-on- Top’?
questions.choice_a: Maintain IFR flight plan but comply with visual flight rules while in VFR conditions.
questions.choice_b: Maintain VFR altitudes, cloud clearances, and comply with applicable instrument flight rules.
questions.choice_c: Maintain IFR altitudes, VFR cloud clearances, and comply with applicable instrument flight rules.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: A pilot on an IFR flight plan operating in VFR weather conditions may request VFR-on-top instead of an assigned altitude. When ATC authorizes maintenance of VFR- on-top, pilots on IFR flight plans must maintain VFR altitudes, maintain VFR cloud clearances and visibilities, and comply with applicable instrument flight rules. These rules include minimum IFR altitudes, position reporting, radio communications, courses to be flown, and adherence to ATC clearances. Answer (A) is incorrect. Not only will a pilot remain on the IFR flight plan and comply with VFR altitudes, visibility, and cloud clearances, (s)he must also comply with applicable IFR rules, €.g., position reporting, minimum IFR altitudes. Answer (C) is incorrect. While operating on a VFR-on-top clearance, a pilot must maintain VFR, not IFR, altitudes.

questions.question_text: What cloud clearance must be complied with when authorized to maintain “VFR-on-Top”?
questions.choice_a: May maintain VFR clearance above, below, or between layers.
questions.choice_b: Must maintain VFR clearance above or below.
questions.choice_c: May maintain VFR clearance above or below, but not between layers.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: ATC authorization to maintain VFR-on-top is not intended to restrict pilots so that they must operate only above a layer. Instead, it permits operation above, below, or between layers, or in areas where there is no meteorological obscuration. Answer (B) is incorrect. An authorization to maintain VFR- on-top allows a pilot to maintain VFR clearance not only above or below, but also between layers. Answer (C) is incorrect. An authorization to maintain VFR-on-top allows a pilot to maintain VFR clearance above, below, and between layers.

questions.question_text: What separation or service by ATC is afforded pilots authorized “VFR-on-Top”?
questions.choice_a: The same afforded all IFR flights.
questions.choice_c: Traffic advisories only.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Separation will be provided between all aircraft operating on IFR flight plans except during VFR-on- top operations outside Class B or Class C airspace. Under these conditions, ATC may issue traffic advisories, but it is the responsibility of the pilot to be vigilant in order to see and avoid other aircraft. Answer (A) is incorrect. Separation will be provided for all IFR flights except those operating with a VFR-on-top clearance. In that case, only traffic advisories may be provided. Answer (B) is incorrect. When radar is employed for separation of aircraft at the same altitude, a minimum of 3 mi. separation is provided between airplanes operating within 40 mi. of the radar antenna site, and 5 mi. between aircraft operating beyond 40 mi. from the antenna site.

questions.question_text: In what airspace will ATC not authorize “VFR-on- Top’?
questions.choice_c: Class A airspace.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: ATC will not authorize VFR or VFR-on-top operations in Class A airspace. Class A airspace extends from FL 180 up to, and including, FL 600. Answer (A) is incorrect. While there may be some restrictions due to separation, a pilot can operate VFR-on-top in Class C airspace. Answer (B) is incorrect. While there may be some restrictions due to separation, a pilot can operate VFR-on-top in Class B airspace.

questions.question_text: Under what condition should a pilot on IFR advise ATC of minimum fuel status?
questions.choice_a: When the fuel supply becomes less than that required for IFR.
questions.choice_b: If the remaining fuel suggests a need for traffic or landing priority.
questions.choice_c: If the remaining fuel precludes any undue delay.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: A pilot should advise ATC of minimum fuel status when the remaining fuel supply has reached a state which precludes any undue delay upon reaching destination. A pilot must be aware that this is not an emergency situation but an advisory that indicates an emergency situation is possible should any undue delay occur. Answer (A) is incorrect. A pilot must ensure the minimum amount of fuel is on board the airplane for the planned IFR flight and alternatives, if needed, during the flight planning phase. Answer (B) is incorrect. If the remaining fuel suggests a need for traffic or landing priority, the pilot should declare an emergency, not minimum fuel status, due to low fuel and report fuel remaining in minutes.

questions.question_text: What does the term “minimum fuel” imply to ATC?
questions.choice_a: Traffic priority is needed to the destination airport.
questions.choice_b: Emergency handling is required to the nearest suitable airport.
questions.choice_c: Upon reaching the destination, the aircraft can accept little or no delay.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: When a pilot advises ATC of a minimum fuel status, ATC will handle this report as an advisory that indicates an emergency situation is possible should an undue delay occur. A minimum fuel advisory does not imply a need for traffic priority. Answer (A) is incorrect. A pilot should declare an emergency due to low fuel and report fuel remaining in minutes if traffic priority is needed to the destination airport. Answer (B) is incorrect. Emergency handling to the nearest suitable airport is required in a distress or urgent type of emergency.

questions.question_text: You should advise ATC of minimum fuel status when your fuel supply has reached a state where, upon reaching your destination, you cannot accept any undue delay.
questions.choice_a: This will ensure your priority handling by ATC.
questions.choice_b: ATC will consider this action as if you had declared an emergency.
questions.choice_c: If your remaining usable fuel supply suggests the need for traffic priority to ensure a safe landing, declare an emergency due to low fuel and report fuel remaining in minutes.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Advising ATC of minimum fuel status is not considered an emergency, nor does it imply a need for traffic priority. However, if the remaining usable fuel supply suggests the need for traffic priority to ensure a safe landing, you should declare an emergency due to low fuel and report fuel remaining in minutes. Answer (A) is incorrect. A minimum fuel advisory does not imply a need for traffic priority, only that you cannot accept any undue delay. Answer (B) is incorrect. Declaring minimum fuel is not an emergency situation but merely an advisory that indicates an emergency situation is possible should any undue delay occur.

questions.question_text: Which range of codes should a pilot avoid
questions.choice_a: 0000 through 1000. facilities. This applies to nondiscrete code 7500 and all discrete ‘B. 7200 and 7500 series. codes in the 7600 and 7700 series. Remember that 7500 is for hijack, 7600 for lost radio communications, 7700 for general emergency, and 7777 for military interceptor operations.
questions.explanation: switching through when changing transponder codes? DISCUSSION: When making routine code changes, pilots should avoid inadvertent selection of codes 7500, 7600, or 7700, which can cause momentary false alarms at automated ground

questions.question_text: What is the hijack code? Answer (B) is correct. (AIM Para 6-3-4) DISCUSSION: Transponder code 7500 means: “! am being
questions.choice_a: 7200 hijacked/forced to a new destination.” Code 7500 will never be : assigned by ATC without prior notification from the pilot that Anytime the pilot is doubtful of a condition that
questions.choice_b: 7500 his or her airplane is being subjected to unlawful interference. Code 7500 will trigger special emergency indicators in all radar When fuel endurance or weather will require
questions.choice_c: 7777 ATC facilities. §9. What minimum condition is suggested for
questions.explanation: declaring an emergency? DISCUSSION: An emergency is defined as either a distress or an urgency condition. A distress condition can be a fire, mechanical failure, or structural damage. An airplane is in at least an urgency condition when the pilot becomes doubtful about position, fuel endurance, weather, or any other condition could adversely affect flight safety. that could adversely affect flight safety. A pilot should declare an en route or landing priority. an emergency under any of these circumstances, not after the situation has developed into a distress condition. When distress conditions such as fire, Answer (B) is incorrect. If fuel endurance or weather mechanical failure, or structural damage will require an en route or landing priority, the situation has OCCUTS. progressed beyond an urgency condition and is now a distress condition. Answer (C) is incorrect. Distress condition implies a serious and/or imminent danger requiring immediate assistance. This situation is the step after an urgency condition, which is one that has the potential to become a distress condition.

questions.question_text: It is the responsibility of the pilot and crew to
questions.choice_a: 50 feet or less to another aircraft. or flight crew to determine whether a near midair collision did
questions.choice_b: 500 feet or less to another aircraft. actually occur and to initiate a near midair collision report. Answer (A) is incorrect. A near midair collision is reported
questions.choice_c: 1,000 feet or less to another aircraft. if the possibility of a collision occurs as a result of proximity of less than 500 ft., not 50 ft., to another airplane. Answer (C) is incorrect. Proximity of greater than 500 ft. is not considered a near midair collision and would not be reported as such.
questions.explanation: report a near midair collision as a result of proximity of DISCUSSION: A near midair collision is defined as an at least incident associated with the operation of an airplane in which a possibility of collision occurs as a result of proximity of less than 500 ft. to another airplane. It is the responsibility of the pilot and/

questions.subsection: 7.9 Traffic Alert and Collision Avoidance System (TCAS)
questions.question_text: Under what condition does ATC issue safety alerts? When collision with another aircraft is imminent. If the aircraft altitude is noted to be in close proximity to the surface or an obstacle. . When weather conditions are extreme and wind shear or large hail is in the vicinity.
questions.explanation: ATC will issue a safety alert when an airplane under its control is at an altitude which, in ATC’s judgment, places the aircraft in unsafe proximity to terrain, obstructions, or another aircraft. A terrain or obstruction alert is issued to an airplane that is at an altitude believed to place the aircraft in unsafe proximity to terrain or obstructions. An aircraft conflict alert is issued when an airplane under a controller's direction is at an altitude believed to place the aircraft in unsafe proximity to another airplane not under that controller’s direction. var{ Answer (A) is incorrect. A safety alert is issued to a pilot if ATC believes that his or her airplane is at an altitude which will place it in unsafe proximity to another airplane, not that a collision is imminent. Answer (C) is incorrect. When weather conditions are extreme and wind shear or large hail is in the vicinity, a convective SIGMET, not a safety alert, is broadcast.

questions.subsection: 7.9 Traffic Alert and Collision Avoidance System (TCAS)
questions.question_text: TCAS | provides traffic and resolution advisories. proximity warning. recommended maneuvers to avoid conflicting traffic.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: TCAS | provides only a proximity warning to assist the pilot in the visual acquisition of intruder aircraft. No recommended avoidance maneuvers are provided or authorized as a direct result of a TCAS | warning. Answer (A) is incorrect. TCAS II, not TCAS |, provides traffic and resolution advisories. TCAS | provides only a proximity warning. Answer (C) is incorrect. TCAS II, not TCAS |, provides recommended maneuvers to avoid conflicting traffic and resolution advisories. TCAS | provides only a proximity warning.

questions.subsection: 7.9 Traffic Alert and Collision Avoidance System (TCAS)
questions.question_text: TCAS II provides traffic and resolution advisories. w proximity warning. maneuvers in all directions to avoid the conflicting traffic.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: TCAS |! provides traffic and resolution advisories. Resolution advisories provide recommended maneuvers in a vertical direction (climb or descend only) to avoid conflicting traffic. Answer (B) is incorrect. TCAS |, not TCAS II, provides only a proximity warning. TCAS II provides traffic and resolution advisories. Answer (C) is incorrect. TCAS II provides recommended maneuvers only in a vertical direction, not in all directions, to avoid conflicting traffic.

questions.subsection: 7.9 Traffic Alert and Collision Avoidance System (TCAS)
questions.question_text: Each pilot who deviates from an ATC clearance in response to a TCAS advisory is expected to notify ATC and maintain the course and altitude resulting from the deviation, as ATC has radar contact. request a new ATC clearance. expeditiously return to the ATC clearance in effect prior to the advisory, after the conflict is resolved.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Each pilot who deviates from an ATC clearance in response to a TCAS II resolution advisory shall notify ATC of that deviation as soon as practicable and expeditiously return to the current ATC clearance when the traffic conflict is resolved. Answer (A) is incorrect. Since ATC is not responsible for IFR aircraft separation during a deviation, the pilot should notify ATC of the deviation and expeditiously return to the clearance in effect after the conflict is resolved. Answer (B) is incorrect. Since deviations from clearances should be kept to the minimum necessary to satisfy a TCAS advisory, the pilot should notify ATC of the deviation and expeditiously return to the clearance in effect after the conflict is resolved.

questions.question_text: Each pilot who deviates from an ATC clearance in response to a TCAS Il, resolution advisory (RA) is expected to
questions.choice_a: maintain the course and altitude resulting from the deviation, as ATC has radar contact.
questions.choice_b: request ATC clearance for the deviation.
questions.choice_c: notify ATC of the deviation as soon as practicable.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Each pilot who deviates from an ATC clearance in response to a TCAS II resolution advisory (RA) shall notify ATC of that deviation as soon as practicable and expeditiously return to the current ATC clearance when the traffic conflict is resolved. Answer (A) is incorrect. Since ATC is not responsible for IFR aircraft separation during a deviation, the pilot should notify ATC of the deviation and expeditiously return to the clearance in effect after the conflict is resolved. Answer (B) is incorrect. ATC clearance for a deviation in response to a TCAS advisory is not required, although the pilot is expected to notify ATC of the deviation as soon as practicable.

questions.question_text: With no traffic identified by TCAS when in 10 miles of visibility, you
questions.choice_a: can rest assured that no other aircraft is near.
questions.choice_b: must continually scan for other traffic.
questions.choice_c: must scan only for hot air balloons and gliders.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: TCAS does not alter or diminish the pilot’s basic authority and responsibility to ensure safe flight. Since TCAS does not respond to aircraft which are not transponder equipped or aircraft with a transponder failure, TCAS alone does not ensure safe separation in every case. Thus, due diligence is required, and pilots should always be scanning for traffic. Answer (A) is incorrect. TCAS alone does not ensure safe separation in every case. Answer (C) is incorrect. TCAS does not respond to aircraft which are not transponder equipped or those aircraft with a transponder failure. Pilots must continually scan for all kinds of other traffic including, but not limited to, hot air balloons and gliders.

questions.question_text: When should ADS-B equipment be operated on the ground while taxiing?
questions.choice_a: Only when ATC specifically requests your ADS-B equipment be activated.
questions.choice_b: Any time when the airport is operating under IFR conditions.
questions.choice_c: All the time when on the airport surface. 7.11_ ATC -- Approach and Landing
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: ADS-B uses highly accurate GPS signals. Because of this, ADS-B will work where radar often will not work, even in mountainous terrain. It can also function at low altitudes and on the ground, meaning it can be used to monitor traffic on the taxiways and runways. Answer (A) is incorrect. ADS-B is highly accurate. It can be used in mountainous terrain and at low altitudes, even on the ground. Having the system on will enable the controllers to monitor traffic on taxiways and runways. Answer (B) is incorrect. The ADS-B system should be left on while on the ground in all weather conditions. This will enable ATC to monitor traffic on taxiways and runways.

questions.question_text: Which reports are always required when on an IFR approach not in radar contact?
