questions.choice_a: Leaving FAF inbound or outer marker inbound and missed approach.
questions.choice_b: Leaving FAF inbound, leaving outer marker inbound or outbound, and missed approach.
questions.choice_c: Leaving FAF inbound, leaving outer marker inbound or outbound, procedure turn outbound and inbound, and visual contact with the runway.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: When not in radar contact, a pilot is required to report when leaving final approach fix (FAF) inbound on final approach (nonprecision approach) or when leaving the outer marker inbound on final approach (precision approach). At all times, a pilot will report a missed approach. Answer (B) is incorrect. A pilot is required to report leaving the outer marker inbound, not outbound, on final approach. Answer (C) is incorrect. A pilot is not required to report leaving the outer marker outbound, the execution of a procedure turn, and/or visual contact with the runway.

questions.question_text: What action(s) should a pilot take if vectored across the final approach course during an IFR approach?
questions.choice_a: Continue on the last heading issued until otherwise instructed.
questions.choice_b: Contact approach control, and advise that the flight is crossing the final approach course.
questions.choice_c: Turn onto final, and broadcast in the blind that the flight has proceeded on final.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: If a pilot is being vectored to the final approach course, these vectors are used for spacing and separating aircraft. Pilots must not deviate from the headings issued by approach control. Pilots will normally be informed when it is necessary to vector across the final approach course for spacing or other reasons. If approach course is imminent and the pilot has not been informed of crossing the final approach course, the pilot should contact approach control and advise that the flight is crossing the final approach course. Answer (A) is incorrect. The pilot should maintain last heading issued but should also advise approach control that the flight is crossing the final approach course. Answer (C) is incorrect. A pilot should broadcast in the blind that the flight has turned onto final when operating VFR at an uncontrolled airport.

questions.question_text: When cleared for an IFR approach to an uncontrolled airport with no FSS, what precaution should the pilot take after being advised to change to advisory frequency?
questions.choice_a: Monitor ATC for traffic advisories as well as UNICOM.
questions.choice_b: Broadcast position and intentions on the Common Traffic Advisory Frequency and monitor the frequency.
questions.choice_c: Wait until visual contact is made with the airport and then broadcast position and intentions to land on UNICOM.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: When making an IFR approach to an uncontrolled airport with no FSS, after ATC advises, “Change to advisory frequency approved,” the pilot should broadcast his or her intentions, including the type of approach and the time at which (s)he arrives over the FAF or outer marker inbound. The pilot should monitor the CTAF for reports from other pilots and for continual updates to position reports. Answer (A) is incorrect. After ATC advises the pilot to change to advisory frequency, ATC will no longer be able to provide traffic advisories. Answer (C) is incorrect. A pilot should always broadcast intentions and continually update position reports on the CTAF, not wait until visual contact is made with the airport. Waiting is unsafe and can contribute to an accident.

questions.question_text: When is radar service terminated while vectored for an IFR approach at an uncontrolled airport?
questions.choice_a: Only upon landing or advised to change to advisory frequency.
questions.choice_b: When aligned on the final approach course.
questions.choice_c: When cleared for the approach.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Radar service is terminated while the airplane is being vectored for an IFR approach at an uncontrolled airport when the landing is completed or when the pilot is advised to change to advisory frequency, whichever occurs first. Answer (B) is incorrect. When an airplane is established on the final approach course, radar separation will be maintained and the pilot will be expected to complete the approach utilizing the approach aid designated in the clearance (ILS, VOR, etc.). Answer (C) is incorrect. When an airplane is cleared for the approach, approach control will continue to maintain radar separation, and the pilot is expected to complete the approach nite the approach aid designated in the clearance (ILS, VOR, etc.).

questions.question_text: While being vectored to the final approach course of an IFR approach, when may the pilot descend to published altitudes?
questions.choice_a: Anytime the flight is on a published leg of an approach chart.
questions.choice_b: When the flight is within the 10-mile ring of a published approach.
questions.choice_c: Only when approach control clears the flight for the approach.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: While being vectored to the final approach course and being cleared for the approach, a pilot may descend to published altitudes anytime the flight is established on a segment of a published route or IAP. After the airplane is so established, published altitudes apply to descent within each succeeding route or approach segment unless a different altitude is assigned by ATC. Answer (A) is incorrect. One must be cleared for the approach to be able to descend to published altitudes. Answer (B) is incorrect. When the flight is within the 10-mile ring of a published approach, a pilot will maintain the minimum altitude for IFR and maintain altitude issued by approach control until established on a published segment of the approach.

questions.question_text: What is the difference between a visual and a contact approach? A visual approach is an IFR authorization while a contact approach is a VFR authorization. Both are the same but classified according to the party initiating the approach. A visual approach may be initiated by ATC while a contact approach can only be initiated by the pilot.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Visual approaches are initiated by ATC to reduce pilot/controller workload and expedite traffic by shortening flight paths to the airport. A contact approach must be initiated by the pilot. A pilot operating clear of clouds who has at least 1 SM flight visibility and can reasonably expect to continue to the destination airport in those conditions may request ATC authorization for a contact approach. Answer (A) is incorrect. Both a visual and a contact approach are initiated from an IFR flight plan. Answer (B) is incorrect. A visual approach is one in which the pilot has a preceding aircraft or the airport in sight and can maintain basic VFR weather minimums. A contact approach is used by a pilot in lieu of conducting a standard or special instrument approach to an airport.

questions.question_text: When may a pilot execute a missed approach during an ASR approach? Anytime at the pilot’s discretion. Only at the MAP. Only when advised by the controller.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: An ASR approach is one in which a controller provides azimuth guidance only. The pilot is furnished headings to fly to align the airplane with the extended line of the runway. A pilot may execute a missed approach during an ASR anytime at the pilot’s discretion. Answer (B) is incorrect. The controller will instruct the pilot to execute a missed approach at the MAP or anytime during the approach that the controller considers that safe guidance cannot be provided. Answer (C) is incorrect. A pilot can execute a missed approach anytime at the pilot’s discretion, not only when advised by the controller.

questions.question_text: Except during an emergency, when can a pilot expect landing priority? When cleared for an IFR approach. When piloting a large, heavy aircraft. In turn, on a first-come, first-serve basis.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: A clearance for a specific type of approach to an airplane operating on an IFR flight plan does not mean that landing priority will be given over other traffic. Air traffic control towers handle all airplanes, regardless of the type of flight plan, on a first-come, first-served basis. A landing sequence will be issued to each airplane as soon as possible to enable the pilot to adjust his or her flight path properly. Answer (A) is incorrect. A clearance for an IFR approach does not mean landing priority will be given over other traffic. Answer (B) is incorrect. A large, heavy aircraft will be sequenced for landing on a first-come, first-served basis, with no special priority over other traffic.

questions.question_text: How should a pilot describe braking action? 00 percent, 50 percent, 75 percent, or 100 percent. Zero-zero, fifty-fifty, or normal. Nil, poor, fair, or good.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Pilots should describe the quality of braking action by using the terms “nil,” “poor,” “fair,” and “good” or a combination of these terms. In describing braking action for a portion of a runway, pilots should use descriptive terms that are easily understood. Answer (A) is incorrect. To describe braking action, a pilot should use nil (not 00%), poor (not 50%), fair (not 75%), or good (not 100%). Answer (B) is incorrect. To describe braking action, a pilot should use the terms nil (not zero-zero), poor (not fifty-fifty), fair, or good (not normal).

questions.question_text: What action is expected of an aircraft upon landing at a controlled airport?
questions.choice_a: Continue taxiing in the landing direction until advised by the tower to switch to ground control frequency.
questions.choice_b: Exit the runway at the nearest suitable taxiway and remain on tower frequency until instructed otherwise.
questions.choice_c: Exit the runway at the nearest suitable taxiway and switch to ground control upon crossing the taxiway holding lines.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: After landing, unless otherwise instructed by the control tower, continue to taxi in the landing direction, proceed to the nearest suitable taxiway, and exit the runway without delay. A pilot who has just landed shouid not change from the tower frequency to the ground control frequency until directed to do so by the controller. Answer (A) is incorrect. Upon landing, the pilot should exit the runway at the nearest suitable taxiway to clear the runway for other traffic and then switch to ground control when directed. Answer (C) is incorrect. While the crossing of the taxiway hold lines indicates clearing of the active runway, a pilot should not switch to ground control until directed to do so by the controller. Switching without permission may be confusing to ATC.

questions.question_text: Upon landing, you should exit the runway
questions.choice_a: Without delay, but not onto another runway unless authorized by ATC.
questions.choice_b: Atthe first high-speed exit and hold short of the taxiway.
questions.choice_c: Without undue delay, but not directly onto the ramp. END OF STUDY UNIT
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: You should exit the runway without delay at the first available taxiway or on a taxiway as instructed by ATC. Pilots shall not exit the landing runway onto another runway unless authorized by ATC. At airports with a any operating control tower, pilots should not stop or reverse course on the runway without first obtaining ATC approval: Answer (B) is incorrect. When exiting a runway, the aircraft must be completely past the runway hold short line. If holding short of the taxiway, the aircraft would not be clear of the runway environment. Answer (C) is incorrect. While pilots should exit the runway without any undue delay, ramp space is not near runways.

questions.question_text: When the approach procedure involves a procedure turn the maximum speed that should be observed from first overheading the course reversal IAF through the procedure turn is
questions.choice_c: 200 knots IAS.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: When the approach procedure involves a procedure turn, the pilot should observe a maximum speed of not greater than 200 kt. IAS, from first overheading the course reversal IAF through completing the procedure turn maneuver, to ensure containment within the obstruction clearance area. Answer (A) is incorrect. The maximum speed for the procedure turn maneuver is 200 kt. IAS, not 180 kt. IAS. Answers (B) are incorrect. Airspeed limitations are based on indicated airspeed (IAS), not true airspeed (TAS).

questions.question_text: A minimum instrument altitude for enroute operations off of published airways which provides obstruction clearance of 1,000 feet in nonmountainous terrain areas and 2,000 feet in designated mountainous areas within the United States is called
questions.choice_c: Off-Route Obstruction Clearance Altitude (OROCA).
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: OROCA is an off-route altitude that provides obstruction clearance with a 1,000-ft. buffer in nonmountainous terrain areas and a 2,000-ft. buffer in designated mountainous areas within the U.S. This altitude may not provide signal coverage from ground-based navigation systems, ATC radar, or communications coverage. Answer (A) is incorrect. MOCAs represent the lowest published altitude in effect between radio fixes on VOR airways, off-airway routes, or route segments that meets obstacle clearance requirements for the entire route segment and ensures acceptable navigational signal coverage only within 25 statute (22 nautical) miles of a VOR. Answer (B) is incorrect. MSAs provide obstruction clearance of 1,000 ft., not 2,000 ft., in designated mountainous terrain.

questions.question_text: An off-route altitude which provides obstruction clearance of 1,000 feet in nonmountainous terrain areas and 2,000 feet in designated mountainous areas within the United States is called
questions.choice_c: Minimum Safe/Sector Altitude (MSA).
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Off-route obstruction clearance altitude (OROCA) is an off-route altitude that provides obstruction clearance with a 1,000-ft. buffer in nonmountainous terrain areas and a 2,000-ft. buffer in designated mountainous areas within the U.S. This altitude may not provide signal coverage from ground- based navigation systems, ATC radar, or communications coverage. Answer (A) is incorrect. MVAs in designated mountainous areas may provide only a 1,000-ft., not a 2,000-ft., obstacle clearance when required for flying terminal routes or for vectoring for an instrument approach. Answer (C) is incorrect. MSAs provide obstruction clearance of 1,000 ft., not 2,000 ft., in designated mountainous terrain.

questions.question_text: Pilots should notify controllers on initial contact that they have received the ATIS broadcast by stating “Have Numbers.” stating “Have Weather.” repeating the alphabetical code word appended to the broadcast.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Pilots should notify controllers on initial contact that they have received the ATIS broadcast by repeating the alphabetical code word appended to the broadcast. Answer (A) is incorrect. The use of the phrase “Have Numbers” means that the pilot received only the wind, runway, and altimeter information. It does not indicate receipt of the ATIS broadcast and should never be used for this purpose. Answer (B) is incorrect. “Have Weather” does not indicate receipt of the ATIS broadcast and should never be used for this purpose.

questions.question_text: An airport may not be qualified for alternate use if the airport has AWOS-3 weather reporting. the airport is located next to a restricted or prohibited area. the NAVAIDs used for the final approach are unmonitored.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Not all airports can be used as an alternate. An airport may not be qualified for alternate use if the NAVAIDs used for the final approach are unmonitored. Answer (A) is incorrect. An airport can qualify for alternate use if the airport has AWOS-3 weather reporting. An airport may not qualify for alternate use if the NAVAIDs used for the final approach are unmonitored. Answer (B) is incorrect. An airport can qualify for alternate use if the airport is located next to a restricted or prohibited area. An airport may not qualify for alternate use if the NAVAIDs used for final approach are unmonitored.

questions.question_text: Precipitation static problems caused by defective dischargers may include
questions.choice_a: Complete loss of VHF communications, 30 degree compass errors, high-pitched audio squeals, and erratic instrument readings. Complete loss of UHF communications, 50 degree compass errors, low-pitched audio squeals, and limited instrument readouts. Little interference in VHF communications, 20 degree compass errors, medium-pitched audio squeals, and negligible instrument indication changes.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Precipitation static has the potential to create problems from the serious, such as the complete loss of very- high frequency (VHF) communications and erroneous magnetic compass readings of up to 30°, to the annoyance of high-pitched audio squealing, erratic instrument readings, and St. Elmo’s fire. Answer (B) is incorrect. Precipitation static can cause 30° compass errors, high-pitched audio squeals, and erratic instrument readings. Answer (C) is incorrect. Precipitation static can cause 30° compass errors, high-pitched audio squeals, and erratic instrument readings.

questions.question_text: Under the stabilized approach concept, what is the maximum acceptable descent rate during the final stages of an approach? 1,000' per minute for precision and 1,200' per minute for non-precision. 1,200' per minute above 1,000' AGL and 500' per minute below 1,000' AGL for all approaches. 1,000' per minute for either precision or non- precision.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Research has shown that the margin of safety is seriously reduced when descending at greater than 1,000' per minute during the final stages of any instrument approach procedure. Thus, the stabilized approach concept states that descents above this rate should not be attempted during this critical stage of flight. Answer (A) is incorrect. A descent rate of 1,200' per minute would compromise the safety margin of the flight. Descent rates above 1,000' per minute should not be utilized in the final stages of any instrument approach procedure. Answer (B) is incorrect. A descent rate of 1,200' per minute would compromise the safety margin of the flight. Descent rates above 1,000' per minute should not be utilized in the final stages of any instrument approach procedure.

questions.question_text: When flying an aircraft with advanced avionics The pilot should fly by engaging the autopilot as first choice. The pilot should know what autopilot modes are engaged. Engaging the autopilot is the safe flight control activation.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: The pilot should engage the autopilot, then verify that the selected modes are engaged by monitoring the annunciator panel. Answer (A) is incorrect. The autopilot is a tool, not a crutch. The autopilot should not be assumed to be free of error or misprogramming until the pilot verifies it is tracking the course/ clearance as anticipated. Answer (C) is incorrect. The pilot should always be prepared to fly the airplane manually if the autopilot fails or is not tracking the course/clearance as expected.

questions.question_text: You notice ATC is unusually quiet and one of your VHF transmit lights is illuminated. You suspect
questions.choice_b: your VHF transmitter is keyed and you probably have a stuck microphone.
questions.choice_c: the radio is performing a self-test function.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: If your radio has been quiet and the VHF transmit light is illuminated, you may have a stuck microphone. You are transmitting on the frequency and simultaneously preventing others from transmitting on that same frequency. Answer (A) is incorrect. The VHF transmit light being — illuminated suggests the radio is transmitting, not inoperative. The receiver may work fine, but the radio is in the transmit mode, not the receive mode. Answer (C) is incorrect. VHF radios are not designed to perform self-tests at arbitrary times. It is more likely you have a stuck microphone that is monopolizing the frequency by transmitting on it.

questions.question_text: If you notice that one of your VHF transmitter lights is illuminated, you know
questions.choice_a: The radio is performing a self test.
questions.choice_b: It should be illuminated when you are talking to ATC.
questions.choice_c: It means the radio is on.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: The transmit light is there to acknowledge that you are transmitting and may inform the pilot that the radio is transmitting when not expected. The transmit light is not a malfunction light or a test-in-progress light. Answer (A) is incorrect. The radio performs a self test when the unit is initially turned on, but this is not indicated by the transmit light. Answer (C) is incorrect. All the other radio lights and frequency displays on the radio indicate that the radio is on.

questions.question_text: What is the purpose of an off-route clearance altitude (OROCA) depicted on an IFR enroute navigation chart?
questions.choice_a: The OROCA is intended for emergencies and situational awareness.
questions.choice_b: The OROCA provides signal coverage for ground-based NAVAIDs, air traffic control radar and communications coverage.
questions.choice_c: The OROCA provides minimum altitude for RNAV off-route planning.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: The OROCA is an off-route altitude that provides obstruction clearance with a 1,000 foot buffer in non- mountainous terrain areas and a 2,000 foot buffer in designated mountainous areas within the U.S. OROCAs are intended primarily as a pilot tool for emergencies and SA. Answer (B) is incorrect. The OROCA is an off-route altitude that may not provide signal coverage from ground-based navigational aids, air traffic control radar, or communications coverage. Answer (C) is incorrect. OROCAs depicted on en route charts do not provide the pilot with an acceptable altitude for terrain and obstruction clearance for the purposes of off-route, random RNAV direct flights in either controlled or uncontrolled airspace.

questions.question_text: Where does the DME indicator have the greatest error between the ground distance and displayed distance to the VORTAC?
questions.choice_a: High altitudes close to the VORTAC.
questions.choice_b: Low altitudes close to the VORTAC.
questions.choice_c: Low altitudes far from the VORTAC.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: The mileage readout on the DME is the direct distance from the airplane to the VORTAC and is commonly referred to as slant-range distance. The difference between a measured distance on the surface and the DME slant-range is known as slant-range error and is greatest at high altitudes close to the VORTAC. Answer (B) is incorrect. At low altitudes close to the VORTAC, the slant-range error is less than at high altitudes close to the VORTAC. Answer (C) is incorrect. The slant-range error is at its smallest at low altitudes far from the VORTAC.

questions.question_text: How does DME change with altitude?
questions.choice_c: Decreases.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: GPS systems provide distance as the horizontal measurement from the waypoint (WP) to the aircraft. At 3,000 feet and 0.5 miles, the DME (slant range) distance would read 0.6 NM while the GPS distance would show the actual horizontal distance of 0.5 DME. This error is smallest at low altitudes and/or long ranges. It is greatest when the aircraft is closer to the facility, at which time the DME receiver will display altitude in NM above the facility. DME increases with altitude. Answer (A) is incorrect. DME does not remain the same when altitude changes due to the errors with a DME system caused by slant range distance. Slant range distance refers to the distance from the aircraft's antenna to the ground station. Answer (C) is incorrect. As the aircraft climbs, the DME distance Increases as you are moving away from your WP.

questions.question_text: What DME indications should a pilot observe when directly over a VORTAC site at 12,000 feet?
questions.choice_c: 2.3 DME miles. 8.3. VOR/VORTAC Identifier
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: When directly over a VORTAC site, the DME will display altitude in nautical miles (NM) above the site. One NM equals approximately 6,000 ft. At 12,000 ft. directly above the VORTAC, the DME will indicate 2 DME. Answer (A) is incorrect. The DME would indicate 0 DME if it were sitting on top of the VORTAC site. Answer (C) is incorrect. The DME would indicate 2.3 if the airplane were at 13,800 ft. (6,000 x 2.3) above the VORTAC site.

questions.question_text: What would be the identification when a VORTAC is undergoing routine maintenance and is considered unreliable?
questions.choice_a: A test signal, “TESTING,” is sent every 30 seconds.
questions.choice_b: Identifier is preceded by “M” and an intermittent “OFF” flag would appear.
questions.choice_c: The identifier would be removed.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: The only positive method of identifying a VOR is by its Morse code identification or by the recorded automatic voice identification, which is always indicated by the use of the word “V-O-R’” following the range’s name. During periods of maintenance, the facility may radiate a T-E-S-T code, or the identification code would be removed. Answer (A) is incorrect. A facility may send a T-E-S-T code, not the test signal “TESTING,” during periods of maintenance. Answer (B) is incorrect. An identifier preceded by “M” designates an identification group for the Microwave Landing System (MLS).

questions.question_text: Which indication may be received when a VOR is undergoing maintenance and is considered unreliable?
questions.choice_b: Identifier is preceded by “M” and an intermittent “OFF” flag might appear.
questions.choice_c: An automatic voice recording stating the VOR is out-of-service for maintenance.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: The only positive method of identifying a VOR is by its Morse code identification or by the recorded automatic voice identification, which is always indicated by the use of the word “V-O-R’” following the range’s name. During periods of maintenance, the facility may radiate a T-E-S-T code, or the identification code would be removed. Answer (B) is incorrect. An identifier preceded by “M” designates an identification group for the Microwave Landing System (MLS). Answer (C) is incorrect. When a VOR is undergoing maintenance and is considered unreliable, the automatic voice recording used to identify the station is removed, not replaced with a message that the VOR is out-of-service.

questions.question_text: What angular deviation from a VOR course centerline is represented by a full scale deflection of the CDI?
questions.choice_c: 4° off course.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: The CDI is the course deviation indicator. This indicator is composed of an instrument face with a needle hinged to move across the instrument face. The needle centers when the aircraft is on the selected radial or its reciprocal. Each dot on the face of the instrument represents 2° off course. The outer edge of the center circle is 2° off course. Full needle deflection to either side of the center position indicates the aircraft is 10° or more off course (assuming that there is normal needle sensitivity). Answer (A) is incorrect. Full scale deflection is to the last dot on the outside of the scale, the fifth dot. Off the scale would be beyond the fifth dot. The aircraft would then be 12° or more off course. Answer (C) is incorrect. If the needle were on the second dot to the left or right of the center of the scale, the aircraft would be 4° off course.

questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 139 on page 371.) What is the lateral displacement of the aircraft in nautical miles from the radial selected on the No. 1 NAV?
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: On VORs, the displacement from course is approximately 200 ft. per dot per NM. At 30 NM from the station, one dot deflection indicates approximately 1 NM displacement of the airplane from the course centerline. At 60 NM, it would be 2 NM for every dot of displacement. Since displacement here is 2 1/2 dots, the airplane would be 5 NM from the centerline. Answer (B) is incorrect. This would be indicated by a displacement of almost 4 dots. Answer (C) is incorrect. This would be indicated by a full deflection.

questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 139 on page 371.) On which radial is the aircraft as indicated by the No. 1 NAV?
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: The course selector in Fig. 139 is set on 350° with a FROM reading, indicating that, if the course deflection bar were centered, the airplane would be on R-350. Since a total deflection is approximately 10° to 12°, one-half deflection is 5° to 6°. Here, deflection is less than one-half, so it is about 5°. The course deflection bar indicates that this airplane is to the left of R-350, which would be R-345. Answer (A) is incorrect. R-175 would require a TO indicator. Answer (B) is incorrect. R-165 would require a TO indicator and a left deflection.

questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 139 on page 371.) What is the lateral displacement in degrees from the desired radial on the No. 2 NAV? Aromas: Baca
questions.explanation: Since on a standard 5-dot VOR indicator, a full deflection of 5 dots is about 10°, 2 dots means a 4° deflection. Answer (A) is incorrect. A 1° lateral displacement is indicated by a 1/2-dot displacement of the CDI. Answer (B) is incorrect. A a nel displacement is indicated by a 1-dot displacement of the }

questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 139 on page 371.) Which OBS selection on the No. 1 NAV would center the CDI and change the ambiguity indication to a TO?
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: The course selector in Fig. 139 is set to 350° with a FROM indication, which means the airplane would be on R-350, if the course deviation bar were centered. However, the deviation bar indicates that the airplane is 5° (2° per dot) to the left of R-350, or R-345. Thus, to center the CDI and change the ambiguity indicator to TO, the OBS should be set to the reciprocal of 345°, which is 165° (345°-180°). Answer (A) is incorrect. An OBS setting of 175° would change the ambiguity indication to TO, but it would not center the CDI. Answer (C) is incorrect. An OBS setting of 345° would Sa the CDI, but the ambiguity indication would be FROM, not

questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 139 above.) Which OBS selection on the No. 2 NAV would center the CDI?
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: The course selector in Fig. 139 is set to 170° (it is not an HSI; it is a VOR) and the TO-FROM indicator indicates FROM, which means the airplane would be on R-170 if the course deviation bar were centered. Since the bar indicates a left deflection (2° per dot), the airplane is to the right of the radial, or on R-174. Answer (B) is incorrect. A right, not left, deflection would indicate R-166. Answer (C) is incorrect. The TO-FROM indicator is on FROM, not TO.

questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 139 above.) Which OBS selection on the No. 2 NAV would center the CDI and change the ambiguity indication to a TO?
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: The course selector in Fig. 139 is set to 170° (it is not an HSI; it is a VOR) and the TO-FROM indicator indicates FROM, which means the airplane would be on R-170 if the course deviation bar were centered. Since the bar indicates a left deflection (2° per dot), the airplane is to the right of the radial, or on the 174° radial. To obtain a TO indication, one would have to change the OBS selection by 180° from 174° to 354°. Answer (A) is incorrect. A right deflection would currently mean the airplane is on R-166. To change the ambiguity indicator to a TO, 180° must be added to the current radial. Answer (B) is incorrect. A right, not left, deflection would mean the airplane is currently on R-166.

questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 142 on page 373 and Figure 143 on page 373.) To which aircraft position does HS! presentation “A” correspond? At
questions.explanation: On Figs. 142 and 143, HSI “A” has a | VOR course selection of 090°, with a TO indication, meaning the airplane is to the left of the 360/180 radials. It has a right deflection, which means it is north of the 270/090 radials. The airplane heading is 205°, which means airplane 1 is described. Answer (B) is incorrect. Airplane 8 is to the right of the 360/180 radials, which would require a FROM indication. Answer (C) is incorrect. Airplane 11 is to the right of the 360/180 radials and is south of the 270/090 radials, which would require a FROM indication and a left deviation indication.

questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 142 on page 373 and Figure 143 on page 373.) To which aircraft position does HSI presentation “B” correspond?
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: On Figs. 142 and 143, HSI “B” has a VOR course selection of 270° with a FROM indication, meaning that the airplane is to the left of the 360/180 radials. Since it has a right deflection, the airplane is south of R-270. Given a heading of 135°, airplane 19 is described. Answer (A) is incorrect. Airplane 9 would require a left, not right, course deflection bar indication and a TO indication. Answer (B) is incorrect. Airplane 13 is to the right of the 360/180 radials and would require a TO indication.

questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 142 on page 373 and Figure 143 on page 373.) To which aircraft position does HSI presentation “C” correspond? Gx t2
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: On Figs. 142 and 143, HSI “C” has a VOR course selection of 360° with a TO indication, meaning the airplane is south of the 270/090 radials. Since the course deflection bar is to the left, the airplane is to the east of the 180° radial. Given a 310° heading, airplane 12 is described. Answer (A) is incorrect. Airplane 6 has a heading of 360° and is north of the 270/090 radials, which would require a FROM indication. Answer (B) is incorrect. Airplane 7 is north of the 270/090 radials, which would require a FROM indication.

questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 142 on page 373 and Figure 143 on page 373.) To which aircraft position does HSI presentation “D” correspond?
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: On Figs. 142 and 143, HSI “D” has a VOR course selection (OBS) of 180°. Its FROM indication means the airplane is south of R-270/090. Since the course deflection bar is to the left, the airplane is west of R-180. Given the heading of 180°, the position describes airplane 17. Answer (A) is incorrect. Airplane 4 is to the north of the 270/090 radials, which would require a TO indication. Answer (B) is incorrect. The course deflection bar on airplane 15 would have a centered deflection bar and a heading of 360°.

questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 142 on page 373 and Figure 143 on page 373.) To which aircraft position does HSI presentation “E” correspond?
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: On Figs. 142 and 143, HSI “E” has a VOR course selection of 360°. Its FROM indication means the airplane is north of R-270/090. Given the course deflection bar to the left, the airplane is to the east of the 360° radial. Given the 360° heading, the position describes airplane 6. Answer (A) is incorrect. Airplane 5 would have a centered deflection bar and has a heading of 180°. Answer (C) is incorrect. Airplane 15 is to the south of the 270/090 radials, which woud peste a TO indication, and the deflection bar would be centered.

questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 142 on page 373 and Figure 143 on page 373.) To which aircraft position does HSI presentation “F” correspond?
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: On Figs. 142 and 143, HSI “F” has a VOR course selection of 180° with a FROM indication, meaning that the airplane is south of the 270/090 radials. Since the course deflection bar is centered, the airplane is on R-180. Given a heading of 045° (at the top of the HSI), airplane 16 is described. Answer (A) is incorrect. Airplane 10 is north of the 270/090 radials and east of the 360/180 radials, which would require a TO indication and a right course deflection. Answer (B) is incorrect. Airplane 14 is to the east of R-180, which would require a right course deflection.

questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 141 below, and Figure 140 on page 375.) To which aircraft position(s) does HSI presentation “A” correspond?
questions.choice_c: 6only.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: On Figs. 140 and 141, HSI “A” has a heading of 360° with no localizer deviation, which means the airplane is on the localizer. Airplanes 6 and 9 are on the localizer with a 360° heading. Answer (B) is incorrect. The indication will be the same on both the front course and the back course. Answer (C) is incorrect. The indication will be the same on both the front and the back course.

questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 141 below, and Figure 140 on page 375.) To which aircraft position(s) does HS! presentation “B” correspond? iN. lal

Both airplanes 5 and 13 are described. Note the back course setting. If the front course 270° (instead of 90°) had been set, normal (rather than reverse) sensing would be indicated. Answer (A) is incorrect. Airplane 11 has a 270° heading. Answer (C) is incorrect. Airplanes 7 and 11 have 270° headings.

questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 141 below, and Figure 140 on page 375.) To which aircraft position does HSI presentation “C” correspond?
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: On Figs. 140 and 141, HSI “C” has a heading of 090° with a centered course deflection bar, which means the airplane is on the localizer with a 090° heading, which is airplane 12. The back course setting (090) has no effect because the deflection bar is centered. Answer (A) is incorrect. Airplane 9 has a 360° heading. Answer (B) is incorrect. Airplane 4 has a 270° heading.

questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 141 below, and Figure 140 on page 375.) To which aircraft position does HSI presentation “D” correspond?
questions.choice_b: 10 G2 Figure 141. — Aircraft Position and Direction of Flight.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: On Figs. 140 and 141, HSI “D” has a heading of 310° in a back course setting (090), resulting in reverse sensing. It has a right deflection, meaning the airplane is south of the localizer. Thus, airplane 2 is described. Answer (A) is incorrect. Airplane 1 is on a 225° heading, not a 310° heading, and is north of the localizer. Answer (B) is neo teck Airplane 10 has a 135° heading and is north of the ocalizer.

questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 141 below, and Figure 140 on page 377.) To which aircraft position(s) does HSI presentation “E” correspond?

questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: On Figs. 140 and 141, HSI “E” has a heading of 045°. It has a right deflection with a back course HSI setting of 090. This results in reverse sensing, meaning the airplane is south of the localizer. Thus, airplanes 3 and 8 are described. Answer (A) is incorrect. Both airplanes 8 and 3 have a 045° heading and are south of the localizer. Answer (C) is incorrect. Both airplanes 8 and 3 have a 045° heading and are south of the localizer.

questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 141 below, and Figure 140 on page 377.) To which aircraft position does HSI presentation “F” correspond?
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: On Figs. 140 and 141, HSI “F” has a localizer setting at 270° with a centered bar and a 270° heading, which is airplane 4. Answer (B) is incorrect. Airplane 11 has a left course deviation bar. Answer (C) is incorrect. Airplane 5 has a 090° heading, not 270°. It also should have a right deflection because it is south of the localizer.

questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 141 below, and Figure 140 on page 377.) To which aircraft position(s) does HSI presentation “G” correspond?
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: On Figs. 140 and 141, HSI “G” has a localizer setting at 270° with a left deviation, meaning the airplane is north of the localizer. With a 270° heading, airplanes 7 and 11 are described. Answer (A) is incorrect. Airplane 11 is also north of the localizer with a 270° heading. Answer (C) is incorrect. Airplanes 5 and 13 have a 090° heading and are also south, not north, of the localizer.

questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 141 below, and Figure 140 on page 377.) To which aircraft position does HS! presentation “H” correspond? OR N = @

Given a heading of 215°, airplane 1 is described. Answer (A) is incorrect. Airplane 8 has a heading of 045° and is located south of the localizer. Answer (C) is incorrect. questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 123 below.) You receive this ATC clearance: “...HOLD EAST OF THE ABC VORTAC ON THE ZERO NINER ZERO RADIAL, LEFT TURNS...” What is the recommended procedure to enter the holding pattern?
questions.choice_c: Teardrop only.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: You are cleared to hold east of the ABC VORTAC with left turns on R-090. The holding side will be south of R-090, and you are entering from the southwest on a 55° heading (Fig. 123). Draw the 70° line through the VOR and on R-340, R-160. A parallel entry is appropriate for R-160 to R-270. R-270 to R-340 Teardrop R-340 to R-160 Direct R-160 to R-270 Parallel Answer (B) is incorrect. A direct entry would be appropriate if you were coming in on R-340 to R-160. Answer (C) is incorrect. A teardrop entry would be appropriate if you were coming in from R-270 to R-340.

questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 123 below.) You receive this ATC clearance: *“,.CLEARED TO THE ABC VORTAC. HOLD SOUTH ON THE ONE EIGHT ZERO RADIAL...” What is the recommended procedure to enter the holding pattern?
questions.choice_c: Parallel only. Figure 123. — Aircraft Course and DME Indicator.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: You are cleared to hold south on the 180° radial with right turns, which means you will be to the east of R-180. Since you are coming in from the southwest (R-240), you can make a direct entry. R-290 to R-360 Teardrop R-360 to R-110 Parallel R-110 to R-290 Direct Answer (A) is incorrect. A teardrop entry would be appropriate only from R-290 to R-360. Answer (C) is incorrect. A parallel entry would be appropriate only from R-360 to R-110.

questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 123 above.) You receive this ATC clearance: “..CLEARED TO THE XYZ VORTAC. HOLD NORTH ON THE THREE SIX ZERO RADIAL, LEFT TURNS...” What is the recommended procedure to enter the holding pattern?
questions.choice_c: Teardrop only.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Holding north of the XYZ VORTAC with left turns on R-360 means you are on the east side of the radial. Since you are coming in from the southwest (R-240), you will be making a teardrop entry. R-180 to R-250 Teardrop R-250 to R-070 Direct R-070 to R-180 Parallel Answer (A) is incorrect. A parallel approach would be appropriate only if coming in between R-070 and R-180. Answer (B) is incorrect. A direct entry is appropriate only between R-250 and R-070.

questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 123 on page 378.) You receive this ATC clearance: “,.CLEARED TO THE ABC VORTAC. HOLD WEST ON THE TWO SEVEN ZERO RADIAL...” What is the recommended procedure to enter the holding pattern?
questions.choice_c: Teardrop only.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: You are cleared to hold west on R-270 with right turns, so you will be south of R-270. Since you are coming in on R-240, you need to make a direct entry. R-020 to R-090 Teardrop R-090 to R-200 Parallel R-200 to R-020 Direct Answer (A) is incorrect. A parallel entry is appropriate only on R-090 to R-200. Answer (C) is incorrect. A teardrop entry is appropriate only when coming in on R-020 to R-090.

questions.question_text: The maximum speed a propeller-driven airplane may hold at is
questions.choice_c: 156 knots.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: The maximum holding airspeed for all aircraft (including a propeller-driven airplane) is 265 kt. when holding at an altitude above 14,000 ft. MSL. Answer (B) is incorrect. While 230 kt. is the maximum holding airspeed from 6,001 ft. to 14,000 ft., a higher airspeed of 265 kt. is permitted when holding at an altitude above 14,000 ft. Answer (C) is incorrect. This is not a maximum holding speed for any altitude.

questions.question_text: Maximum holding speed for a turbojet airplane above 14,000 feet is
questions.choice_c: 265 knots.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: The maximum airspeed above 14,000 ft. for holding patterns is 265 kt. for all aircraft, including a turbojet airplane. This speed limit can be increased if necessary for safety reasons through discussion with ATC. Answer (A) is incorrect. This is not a maximum holding speed at any altitude. Answer (B) is incorrect. This is the maximum holding speed for all aircraft from 6,001 ft. to 14,000 ft., not above 14,000 ft.

questions.question_text: Maximum holding speed for a civil turbojet aircraft at a joint use airport (civil/Navy) between 7,000 and 14,000 feet is
questions.choice_c: 230 knots.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: The maximum holding speed for all aircraft, including a civil turbojet, from 6,001 ft. to 14,000 ft. is 230 kt. Holding speeds are based on altitudes at joint-use airports. Answer (A) is incorrect. This is the maximum holding speed of all aircraft from the MHA (minimum holding altitude) through 6,000 ft., not from 6,001 ft. to 14,000 ft. Answer (B) is incorrect. This is the maximum holding speed for all aircraft from 14,001 ft. and above.

questions.question_text: What is the maximum holding speed for a civil turbojet holding at a civil airport at 15,000 ft. MSL, unless a higher speed is required due to turbulence or icing and ATC is notified?
questions.choice_c: 250 knots.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: The maximum holding speed for all aircraft, including a civil turbojet, above 14,000 ft. MSL is 265 kt. A higher airspeed may be used if necessary due to turbulence or icing and provided ATC is notified. Answer (B) is incorrect. This is the maximum holding speed for any aircraft holding at an altitude between 6,001 ft. and 14,000 ft., not above 14,000 ft. Answer (C) is incorrect. This is not a maximum holding airspeed at any altitude.

questions.question_text: An aircraft holding at an altitude of 14,000 feet should expect to operate at which holding pattern airspeed?
questions.choice_c: 230 knots.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: The maximum holding speed for all aircraft from 6,001 ft. to 14,000 ft. is 230 kt. Answer (A) is incorrect. This is not a maximum holding airspeed at any altitude. Answer (B) is incorrect. This is not a maximum holding speed at any altitude.

questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 124 below.) A pilot receives this ATC clearance: “..CLEARED TO THE ABC VORTAC. HOLD WEST ON THE TWO SEVEN ZERO RADIAL...” What is the recommended procedure to enter the holding pattern?
questions.choice_c: Direct only. Figure 124. — Aircraft Course and DME Indicator.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: When holding west on the 270° radial, use right turns because left turns were not stated in the clearance. The holding pattern will be to the south of the 270° radial, so the VORTAC (the fix) is at the end of the inbound leg. To determine entry procedures, draw a 70° line through the holding fix such that the 70° line crosses through the outbound leg one-third of the leg length from abeam the fix. R-020 to R-090 Teardrop R-090 to R-200 Parallel R-200 to R-020 Direct Since you are approaching the holding fix from the northwest (R-330) on a 155° heading, you will make a direct entry. Answer (A) is incorrect. The parallel or teardrop entries are alternatives only when approaching on R-090. Answer (B) is incorrect. The parallel entry is appropriate only when approaching on R-090 to R-200.

questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 124 above.) A pilot receives this ATC clearance: “..CLEARED TO THE XYZ VORTAC. HOLD NORTH ON THE THREE SIX ZERO RADIAL, LEFT TURNS...” What is the recommended procedure to enter the holding pattern?
questions.choice_c: Direct.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Visualize a 360° radial with left turns. The pattern will be to the east of the radial with the holding fix being the VORTAC. Since you are approaching from the northwest with a heading of 155° (Fig. 124), you will be able to make a direct entry. R-180 to R-250 Teardrop R-250 to R-070 Direct R-070 to R-180 Parallel Answer (A) is incorrect. If you were approaching on R-180 to R-250, you would make a teardrop entry. Answer (B) is incorrect. If you were approaching on R-070 to R-180, you would fly through the VOR, parallel the holding course, turn right to the holding course, and make a parallel entry.

questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 124 above.) A pilot receives this ATC clearance: “..CLEARED TO THE ABC VORTAC. HOLD SOUTH ON THE ONE EIGHT ZERO RADIAL...” What is the recommended procedure to enter the holding pattern?
questions.choice_c: Direct only.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: If you are holding south on the 180° radial, you will have right turns because left turns are not specified and you will be on the east side of the radial (to cross the VORTAC at the end of your inbound leg). Draw the 70° line through the VORTAC on R-110, R-290. Since you are inbound on R-330 (Fig. 124), you would make a teardrop entry. R-290 to R-360 Teardrop R-360 to R-110 Parallel R-110 to R-290 Direct Answer (B) is incorrect. A parallel entry is appropriate from R-360 to R-110. Answer (C) is incorrect. A direct entry is appropriate from R-110 to R-290.

questions.question_text: When using a flight director system, what rate of turn or bank angle should a pilot observe during turns in a holding pattern?
questions.choice_a: 3° per second or 25° bank, whichever is less.
questions.choice_b: 3° per second or 30° bank, whichever is less.
questions.choice_c: beue per second or 25° bank, whichever is ess.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: The maximum rate of turn or bank to be used when using a flight director system should be 3° per second or 25° bank, whichever is less. Answer (B) is incorrect. Maximum bank while holding using a flight director is 25° bank, not 30° bank. Answer (C) is incorrect. Maximum bank while holding using a flight director is 25° bank or 3° per second, not 1-1/2° per second, whichever is less.

questions.question_text: When entering a holding pattern above 14,000 feet, the initial outbound leg should not exceed
questions.choice_c: 1-1/2 minutes or 10 NM, whichever is less. 8.7 _iFR Charts and Chart Supplements
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: When at an altitude greater than 14,000 ft. MSL, the initial outbound leg should not exceed 1-1/2 minutes. Timing for subsequent outbound legs should be adjusted as necessary to achieve proper inbound leg time (1-1/2 minutes inbound). Answer (A) is incorrect. An initial outbound leg of 1 min. should be used only when below 14,000 ft. Answer (C) is incorrect. A DME distance is issued only by the specified controller for aircraft equipped with DME capability. A DME distance is not required unless specified by the controller.

questions.question_text: All 14 CFR Part 139 airports must report
questions.choice_b: noise complaint statistics for each departure procedure or runway.
questions.choice_c: declared distances for each runway.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Declared distances for a runway represent the maximum distances available and suitable for meeting takeoff and landing distance performance requirements. These distances are determined in accordance with FAA runway design standards by adding to the physical length of paved runway any clearway or stopway and subtracting from that sum any lengths necessary to obtain the standard runway safety areas, runway object free areas, or runway protection zones. All 14 CFR Part 139 airports report declared distances for each runway. Answer (A) is incorrect. The operator of an aircraft that is involved in an accident or incident, not a 14 CFR Part 139 airport, must file a report within 10 days of the accident or incident, not annually. Answer (B) is incorrect. Individual airports, not all 14 CFR Part 139 airports, are responsible for determining noise abatement requirements, establishing their own operating procedures, tracking complaints, and conducting their own enforcement.

questions.question_text: What is the latest point at which a missed approach climb must be executed for obstacle clearance protection?
questions.choice_a: Foran ILS, on glidepath at the decision altitude.
questions.choice_b: Fora straight-in localizer, no later than the departure end of the runway (DER).
questions.choice_c: Fora straight-in localizer, or ILS, at the Middle Marker.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: For obstacle clearance to be assured, the pilot must execute the missed approach at the Missed Approach Point (MAP). Any missed approach after the Decision Altitude (DA), Decision Height (DH), or Minimum Decision Altitude (MDA) involves additional risk until established on the published missed approach procedure course and altitude. Answer (B) is incorrect. The MAP must be well prior to the departure end of the runway. Answer (C) is incorrect. The Middle Marker would be too far and too high away from the runway to be able to possibly see the runway environment for landing. If the missed approach procedures involved a turn-and-do-this at the Middle Marker, obstacle clearance is not guaranteed.

questions.explanation: ; page 383.) In addition to VOR and DME, what other DISCUSSION: The bottom of Fig. 111 provides a Chart © avionics equipment is required to be operational, at Supplement excerpt for Houston Intercontinental (IAH). The line takeoff, to fly the VOR/DME RWY 32R approach at above Radio Aids to Navigation identifies [AH as a TCA, which IAH? is now called Class B airspace. questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 112 on page 385.) The Cugar Four Arrival ends
questions.choice_c: when cleared to land.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: The Cugar Four Arrival is provided in Fig. 112. At the bottom of the STAR is the textual description of the STAR. From CUGAR INT, the pilot should proceed via [AH R-305 to BANTY INT. From there, the pilot should expect vectors to the final approach course. Thus, the Cugar Four Arrival ends at BANTY INT. Answer (B) is incorrect. The Cugar Four Arrival ends at BANTY INT, not at IAH VORTAC. Answer (C) is incorrect. A clearance to land will be given during the instrument approach procedure, which is after the end of the Cugar Four Arrival.

questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 112 on page 385.) When is the earliest time the pilot may initiate a descent from 460 feet MSL to land at IAH?
questions.choice_a: Anytime after GALES INT if the runway environment is visible.
questions.choice_b: Only after the IAH 1.3 DME if the runway environment is visible.
questions.choice_c: Only after the IAH 1 DME if the runway environment is visible.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Fig. 112 provides the IAP chart for the VOR/ DME RWY 32R approach. In the left center of the chart is the profile view. At the [AH 1.3 DME is a bold V, which identifies a visual descent point (VDP). At the VDP, a normal descent from the MDA to the runway touchdown point may begin, provided the runway environment is visible. The VDP will normally be identified by DME on VOR and LOC procedures. Answer (A) is incorrect. GALES INT is the final approach fix (FAF), which indicates the point at which a pilot may initiate a descent to, not from, the MDA. Answer (C) is incorrect. At the IAH, 1 DME is the MAP, which must be initiated if the runway environment is not in sight. The earliest time the pilot may initiate a descent from the MDA of 460 ft. MSL at IAH is at the VDP, not the MAP.

questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 112 on page 385.) At what point must the missed approach be initiated on the VOR/ DME RWY 32R approach at IAH, if still IMC?
questions.choice_c: IAH 1 DME.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: A missed approach must be initiated on the VOR/DME RWY 32R approach (Fig. 112) at IAH if still IMC at the MAP or IAH 1 DME. Answer (A) is incorrect. The missed approach procedure must be initiated at the MAP if still IMC, not at any time after the FAF. Answer (B) is incorrect. The missed approach procedure must o initiated at IAH 1 DME if still IMC, not IAH 1.3 DME (VDP).

questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 112 on page 385.) What action should the pilot take if communications were lost during the Cugar Four Arrival, after turning on the 305 radial of IAH?
questions.choice_a: Proceed direct to IAH VORTAC, then outbound on the IAH R-125 for a procedure turn for final approach.
questions.choice_b: From BANTY INT, proceed to the IAF on the IAH R-290, then continue on the IAH 10 DME Arc to final approach.
questions.choice_c: Proceed direct to IAH VORTAC, then to either IAF on the IAH 10 DME Arc to final approach.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Refer to Fig. 112. If radio communications were lost during the Cugar Four Arrival to [AH after turning on the R-305 of IAH, the pilot would continue to the end of the Cugar Four Arrival, which is BANTY INT. If no other information was given, the pilot should proceed to IAH VORTAC, then to either IAF on the IAH 10 DME arc to the final approach course. Answer (A) is incorrect. No procedure turn is indicated for the VOR/DME RWY 32R approach; thus, no procedure turn can be made to reverse course. Answer (B) is incorrect. The pilot should proceed as filed to IAH, from BANTY INT, not to the nearest IAF.

questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 112 on page 387.) While arcing left on the IAH 10 DME Arc, the pilot experiences a left cross wind component. Where should the bearing pointer be referenced relative to the 90° (wingtip) position to maintain the 10 DME range?
questions.choice_c: Ahead of the left wingtip reference.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: While arcing left on the IAH 10 DME arc, a left crosswind component will cause the airplane to be blown away from the 10 DME arc. Thus, the airplane must be crabbed to the left to maintain the 10 DME arc, and the bearing pointer will point ahead of the left 90° wingtip reference. Answer (A) is incorrect. The bearing pointer should be on the left wingtip reference in a calm wind, not a left crosswind. Answer (B) is incorrect. The bearing pointer should be behind the left wingtip reference when correcting for a right, not left, crosswind component.

questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 112 on page 387.) What effect on approach minimums, if any, does an inoperative MALSR have for an aircraft with an approach speed of 120 knots at [AH?
questions.choice_c: Increases RVR to 6,000 feet.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Refer to Legend 11 on page 365 for the Inoperative Components or Visual Aids Table. Item (3) is used for a VOR/DME approach. In the table, locate MALSR and move right to determine that all approach categories are affected; then move right to determine that the visibility minimum is increased 1/2 SM (or an RVR of 2,400 ft.). Refer to the VOR/ DME RWY 32R IAP in Fig. 112 and locate the minimums section. An approach speed of 120 kt. is a Category B (91-120) airplane, and the visibility minimum is an RVR of 2,400 ft. With the MALSR inoperative, the approach minimum increases the visibility to 1 SM or an RVR of 5,000 ft. Answer (A) is incorrect. With an inoperative MALSR, there is no change to the MDA, but an increase of 1/2 mi. to the required visibility. Answer (C) is incorrect. An airplane with an approach speed of 141-165 kt. (Category D) or greater, not 120 kt., would have the visibility increased to RVR of 6,000 ft.

questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 112 on page 387.) How should the pilot identify the MAP on the IAH VOR/DME RWY 32R?
questions.choice_a: After time has elapsed from FAF.
questions.choice_c: IAH 1 DME.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Fig. 112 provides the IAP chart for the VOR/ DME RWY 32R approach at IAH. The left center of the chart provides the profile view of the approach. The MAP is at the end of the solid line and is identified at |AH 1 DME. VOR/DME MAP is identified by DME. Answer (A) is incorrect. Timing from the FAF is not used ona VOR/DME approach. Answer (B) is incorrect. [AH 1.3 DME is the VDP, not MAP.

questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 114 on page 446.) The changeover point on V394 between DAG VORTAC and POM VORTAC is
questions.choice_c: 64 DME miles from DAG VORTAC.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Refer to Fig. 114. On the lower portion of the chart, locate POM VORTAC and move right to follow V394 (V210-394) to the changeover point indicated by “I.” The number at the top is the distance to DAG VORTAC, or 64 DME. Answer (A) is incorrect. When the changeover point is NOT located at the midway point, aeronautical charts will depict the location and give mileage to each VORTAC. Answer (B) is incorrect. The 38 DME from DAG VORTAC is APLES INT, not the changeover point.

questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 114 on page 446.) The minimum crossing altitude at APLES INT southwest bound on V394 is
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Fig. 114 provides an en route low-altitude chart. In the upper portion, locate DAG VORTAC (in the right center) and move left along V394 to APLES INT (38 DME from DAG VORTAC). The flag with an X indicates a minimum crossing altitude (MCA). Below APLES INT, it reads “V210-394 9100 SW,” which means the minimum crossing altitude is 9,100 ft. when flying southwest on V394. Answer (A) is incorrect. This is the minimum en route altitude (MEA) from DAG VORTAC to APLES INT on V394, not the MCA at APLES INT. Answer (C) is incorrect. This is the MEA after APLES INT, not the MCA at APLES INT.

questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 114 on page 446 and Figure 163A on page 389.) (Refer to the lower panel.) What is the minimum en route altitude on V210, when crossing the POM VORTAC southwest bound and continuing on the same airway?
questions.choice_c: 5,300 feet.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Fig. 114 provides an en route low-altitude chart. In the bottom portion, locate POM VORTAC (center). The flag with the X indicates a minimum crossing altitude (MCA). Since no MCA is given southwest bound on V210, the minimum en route altitude (MEA) is also the MCA. The MEA is to the right of the POM VORTAC symbol with an arrow pointing southwest, which is 5,300 ft. Answer (A) is incorrect. This is the MEA from MEANT INT to CALBE INT when southwest bound on V210, not the MCA at POM VORTAC. Answer (B) is incorrect. This is the MCA on V210 northeast, not southwest, bound.

LAWRS means a limited aviation weather reporting station where observers report cloud height, weather, obstructions to vision, temperature and dew point (in most cases), surface winds, altimeter, and pertinent remarks. questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Pahokee is on the chart on the right side of Fig. 210 about one-third up from the bottom and near the center. To the southwest of PHK VORTAC is a blue serrated-edged box, which indicates an ARTCC remote site with a discrete VHF frequency of 133.55. Answer (A) is incorrect. The Miami ARTCC remote site located near Pahokee has a discrete VHF frequency of 133.55, not 123.45. Answer (C) is incorrect. The Miami ARTCC remote site located near Pahokee has a discrete VHF frequency of 133.55, not 135.35.

questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 259 on page 401.) What effect on approach minimums, if any, does an inoperative MALSR have for an aircraft with an approach speed of 120 knots at IAH?
questions.choice_c: Increases RVR to 5,000 feet.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: This is where the FAA probably wants to test your resource management skills, as some figures are left out that would assist in getting this answer correct. Refer to Legend 11 on page 365 for the Inoperative Components or Visual Aids Table. Item (1) is used for ILS, MLS, and PAR approaches. In item (1) of the table, locate MALSR and move right to determine that all approach categories are affected; then move right to determine that the visibility minimum is increased 1/4 SM, or an RVR of 1,600 ft. (This can be found in Legend 7 on page 364). Refer to the ILS or LOC RWY 33R IAP in Fig. 259 and locate the minimums section. An approach speed of 120 kt. is for a Category B (91-120) airplane, and the visibility minimum is an RVR of 1/2 SM, or an RVR of 2,400 ft. With the MALSR inoperative, the approach minimum increases the visibility to 3/4 SM, or an RVR of 4,000 ft. Answer (A) is incorrect. With an inoperative MALSR, there is no change to the MDA but an increase of 1/4 SM to the required visibility. Answer (C) is incorrect. Adding 1/2 SM to the RVR would make the RVR visibility 1 SM, or 5,000 feet.

questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 259 on page 401.) Which approach lighting is available for Rwy 33R?
questions.choice_c: MALSR with RAIL.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: The second box on the second line of the heading section of Fig. 259 indicates RWY 33R is serviced by a medium-intensity approach lighting system with runway alignment indicator lights. Answer (A) is incorrect. Medium-intensity runway lights (MIRL) are runway edge, not approach, light systems. Answer (B) is incorrect. Touchdown zone (TDZ) and centerline lights (CL) are in-runway lights, not approach lights.

The airport elevation, not the touchdown zone elevation, is 1,135 feet. Answer (C) is incorrect. The touchdown zone elevation for RWY 25L is 1,126 feet, not 1,458 feet.
