questions.subsection: 119.3 and should expect a tower frequency of
questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 186 on page 456.) The NAVAID box at Mormon Mesa (MMM) has a black square in the upper left corner. What does this indicate?
questions.choice_a: That Hazardous Inflight Weather Advisory Service is available.
questions.choice_b: That the National Observatory transmits a time signal on the VOR frequency.
questions.choice_c: That the facility has voice communications capability on the frequency.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Mormon Mesa communication (NAVAID) box is located at the center of the left-hand chart of Fig. 186. The black box in the upper left corner of the box indicates that Hazardous Inflight Weather Advisory Service (HIWAS) is available on the VOR frequency. Answer (B) is incorrect. HIWAS, not the National ; Observatory, transmits on the VOR frequency. Answer (C) is incorrect. Voice communications capability is indicated by a lack of an underline under the frequency.

questions.subsection: 119.3 and should expect a tower frequency of
questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 175 on page 453.) Four airways (V298, V25, V448, and V204) near YKM have a series of dots that overlay the airway. What do these dots indicate?
questions.choice_a: That the airways penetrate a Prohibited and Restricted Airspace.
questions.choice_b: That 2 miles either side of the airway, where shaded, is a Controlled Firing Area.
questions.choice_c: That the airways penetrate a Military Operations Area (MOA) and a special clearance must be received from ATC.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Yakima (YKM) VORTAC is located at the top of the right-hand chart of Fig. 175. V25 is on the 338 radial, and V/298 is on the 310 radial. The series of dots that overlay these airways indicates that they penetrate prohibited and restricted airspace. Answer (B) is incorrect. Controlled firing areas are not depicted on any IFR or VFR charts. Answer (C) is incorrect. The series of dots indicates the airways have restrictions because they penetrate restricted or prohibited areas, not an MOA.

questions.subsection: 119.3 and should expect a tower frequency of
questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 121 on page 448.) On the airway J220 (BUF R-158) SE of Buffalo, the MAA is 39,000 feet. What is the MAA on J547 between BUF and PMM?
questions.explanation: The jet route system consists of routes established from 18,000 ft. MSL to 45,000 ft. MSL inclusive. Jet routes are identified by the letter “J” followed by the airway number. Since there is no lower MAA established for J547 between BUF and PM\M, the MAA is the upper limit of the jet route. Answer (A) is incorrect. This is the upper limit of Class A airspace, not a jet route. Answer (B) is incorrect. The upper limit of a jet route is 45,000 ft. MSL, not 43,000 ft. MSL.

questions.subsection: 119.3 and should expect a tower frequency of
questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 374 on page 405.) You are inbound to DEN from Dallas/Fort Worth (DFW), Center gives you a vector and a frequency for Denver Approach Control, but you miss-copy the frequency. You determine you probably were assigned
questions.choice_a: 119.3 and should expect a tower frequency of 124.3.
questions.choice_b: 120.35 and should expect a tower frequency of 132535:
questions.choice_c: 120.35 and should expect a tower frequency of 124.3.
questions.explanation: You are approaching from the south. As indicated on the Denver INTL instrument approach plate, under DENVER APP CON, the frequency above (SOUTH) is the frequency 120.35 for approach control, if you are flying from the south to the airport. In the block to the right of the approach control frequencies is DENVER TOWER frequency 124.3. Answer (A) is incorrect. The approach control frequency 119.3 is for aircraft flying north to the airport. Answer (B) is incorrect. The tower frequency is 124.3, not 132.35.

questions.question_text: What functions are provided by ILS?
questions.choice_c: Guidance, range, and visual information.
questions.explanation: ds DISCUSSION: The ILS system may be divided functionally into the following three parts: 1. Guidance information -- localizer, glide slope 2. Range information -- marker beacon, DME 3. Visual information -- approach lights, runway lights Answer (A) is incorrect. Azimuth and distance information are provided by a TACAN. Answer (B) is incorrect. A localizer/ DME approach provides azimuth and range information.

questions.question_text: Within what frequency range does the localizer transmitter of the ILS operate?
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: The frequencies assigned for localizer transmission are from 108.10 to 111.95 MHz. Answer (A) is incorrect. Communications frequencies are above 117.95 MHz. Answer (C) is incorrect. The frequency band in which VORs operate is 108.10 to 117.95 MHz.

questions.question_text: Which component associated with the ILS is identified by the first two letters of the localizer identification group?
questions.choice_c: Outer compass locator.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: The outer compass locator is identified by the first two letters of the coded localizer identification group. Answer (A) is incorrect. Marker beacons are not identified by letters; only compass locators are so identified. Answer (B) is incorrect. A middle compass locator is identified by the last two letters of the localizer identification group, not the first two letters.

questions.question_text: Which component associated with the ILS is identified by the last two letters of the localizer group?
questions.choice_c: Outer compass locator.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Compass locators transmit two-letter identification groups. The outer locator transmits the first two letters of the localizer identification group, and the middle locator transmits the last two letters of the localizer identification group. Thus, the middle compass locator is identified by the last two letters of the localizer identification group. Answer (A) is incorrect. A simple marker beacon is not identified by letters; only compass locators are so identified. Answer (C) is incorrect. An outer compass locator is identified by the first two letters of the localizer identification group, not the last two letters.

questions.question_text: If installed, what aural and visual indications should be observed over the ILS back course marker?
questions.choice_a: Aseries of two dot combinations and a white marker beacon light.
questions.choice_b: Continuous dashes at the rate of one per second and a white marker beacon light.
questions.choice_c: Aseries of two dash combinations and a white marker beacon light.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: The back course marker (BCM), where installed, indicates the back course final approach fix. The BCM is modulated at 3000 Hz and identified with two dots at a rate of 72 to 75 two-dot combinations per min. and a white marker beacon light. Answer (B) is incorrect. This is a nonsense indication. This description most closely resembles an ILS outer marker, which has continuous dashes at the rate of two per second. Answer (C) is incorrect. This is not a marker indication of any type. This description most closely resembles an ILS middle marker, which is identified by alternate dot/dash combinations at the rate of two per second.

questions.question_text: What aural and visual indications should be observed over an ILS inner marker?
questions.choice_a: Continuous dots at the rate of six per second.
questions.choice_b: Continuous dashes at the rate of two per second.
questions.choice_c: Alternate dots and dashes at the rate of two per second.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: The inner marker indicates a point at which an airplane is at a designated decision height on the glide slope between the middle marker and the landing threshold during a Category II ILS approach. It is identified with continuous dots keyed at the rate of six dots per second and a white marker beacon. Answer (B) is incorrect. Continuous dashes at the rate of two per second indicate the ILS outer marker, not inner marker. Answer (C) is incorrect. Alternate dots and dashes at the rate of two per second indicate the ILS middle marker, not inner marker.

questions.question_text: What aural and visual indications should be observed over an ILS middle marker?
questions.choice_a: Continuous dots at the rate of six per second identified as a high-pitched tone.
questions.choice_b: Continuous dashes at the rate of two per second identified as a low-pitched tone.
questions.choice_c: Alternate dots and dashes at the rate of two per second identified as an intermediate tone.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: The middle marker indicates a position approximately 3,500 ft. from the landing threshold and has an aural signal of alternating dots and dashes at two per second at an intermediate tone. Answer (A) is incorrect. Continuous dots at the rate of six per second indicate an ILS inner marker. Answer (B) is incorrect. Continuous dashes at the rate of two per second indicate an ILS outer marker.

questions.question_text: What aural and visual indications should be observed over an ILS outer marker?
questions.choice_a: Continuous dots at the rate of six per second.
questions.choice_b: Continuous dashes at the rate of two per second.
questions.choice_c: Alternate dots and dashes at the rate of two per second.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: An ILS outer marker is identified by continuous dashes at the rate of two per second. Answer (A) is incorrect. Continuous dots at the rate of six per second indicate an ILS inner marker. Answer (C) is incorrect. Alternating dots and dashes at the rate of two per second indicate an ILS middle marker.

questions.question_text: The lowest ILS Category II minimums are
questions.choice_c: DH 150 feet and RVR 1,500 feet.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: The pilot-in-command minimums for the lowest Category II minimums are a DH of 100 ft. and an RVR of 1,200 ft. Answer (A) is incorrect. A DH of 50 ft. is for Category III operations, not Category II. Answer (C) is incorrect. A DH of 150 ft. is for a pilot’s initial Category || authorization (for the initial 6-month period) only and is not the lowest DH for Category II operations.

questions.question_text: If the middle marker for a Category | ILS approach is inoperative,
questions.choice_a: the RVR required to begin the approach is increased by 20%.
questions.choice_b: the DA/DH is increased by 50 feet.
questions.choice_c: the inoperative middle marker has no effect on straight-in minimums.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Category | ILS approaches do not require a middle marker. The approach minimums are not affected by its absence. Answer (A) is incorrect. A middle marker is not required for Category | ILS approaches. The approach minimums as they pertain to RVR are not affected by its absence. Answer (B) is incorrect. A middle marker is not required for Category | ILS approaches. The approach minimums as they pertain to DA/DH are not affected by its absence.

questions.question_text: What is the lowest Category IIIA minimum?
questions.choice_c: RVR 700 feet.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: The lowest Category IIIA authorization is for no DH and an RVR of not less than 700 ft. Answer (A) is incorrect. A DH of 50 ft. is for some Category Ill operations, and an RVR of 1,200 ft. is for Category II operations. Answer (B) is incorrect. An RVR of 1,000 ft. does not exist for any category approach.

questions.question_text: How does the SDF differ from an ILS LOC?
questions.choice_b: SDF -- offset from runway plus 4°, ILS -- aligned with runway.
questions.choice_c: SDF -- 15° usable off course indications, ILS -- 35n4
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: The course width for an ILS localizer is from 3° to 6°, whereas the SDF is either 6° or 12° wide. Answer (B) is incorrect. An SDF may not be aligned with the centerline of the runway. Answer (C) is incorrect. The usable off-course indications are limited to 35° for both types of approaches.

questions.question_text: In addition to the localizer, glideslope, marker beacons, approach lighting, and HIRL, which ground components are required to be operative for a Category II instrument approach to a DH below 150 feet AGL?
questions.choice_a: Radar, VOR, DME, runway exit lights, and RVR.
questions.choice_c: Each required ground component.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Unless otherwise authorized by the Administrator, no person may operate a civil aircraft in a Category || operation unless each ground component required for the operation is installed and operating. Answer (A) is incorrect. Radar and DME are not required ground based components for Category II ILS operations. Answer (B) is incorrect. Runway end identifier lights (REIL) are used to provide rapid identification of the approach end of a runway and are not a required ground component for Category II ILS operations.

questions.question_text: Which ground components are required to be operative for a Category II approach in addition to LOC, glideslope, marker beacons, and approach lights?
questions.choice_a: Radar, VOR, DME, taxiway lead-off lights and RVR.
questions.choice_b: All of the required ground components.
questions.choice_c: RCLS and REIL.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: The specific requirements for conducting Category || approach operations are detailed in U.S. Department of Transportation Order 8400.13D, which acknowledges that different levels of operation may be authorized based on the equipment used by a specific operator and the ground equipment available at specific runways. Specific requirements can be found in an operator's OpSpec, Mspec, or LOA. Answer (A) is incorrect. There is no requirement for DME or radar equipment in Category II approach operations. Answer (C) is incorrect. There is no requirement for Runway End Identifier Lights for Category || approach operations.

questions.question_text: Which ground components are required to be operative for a Category II approach in addition to LOC, glide slope, marker beacons, and approach lights?
questions.choice_c: HIRL, TDZL, RCLS, and RVR.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Category II ILS operations require these ground components to be operative: LOC, glide slope, marker beacons, approach lights, high-intensity runway lights (HIRL), touchdown zone lights (TDZL), runway centerline lighting system (RCLS), and runway visual range (RVR). If any of these is inoperative, an increase in DH and/or visibility may be required. Answer (A) is incorrect. Radar is not a required Category Il ILS ground component. Answer (B) is incorrect. Runway end identifier lights (REIL) are used to provide rapid identification of the approach end of a runway. They are not a required ground component for Category II ILS.

questions.question_text: (Refer to Legend 9 on page 409.) The rate of descent for a 3.5° angle of descent glideslope is
questions.choice_c: 740 ft/min at 120 knots groundspeed.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Reference the rate of descent table in the instrument approach chart legend (Legend 9). Match “3.5” in the left most column with “120” in the upper most row to determine a rate of descent of 740 ft/min. Answer (A) is incorrect. The rate of descent at 105 knots groundspeed when flying a 3.5° glideslope would calculate to be only 650 ft/min, not 740 ft/min. Answer (B) is incorrect. The speed value is given as an airspeed. To calculate the required rate of descent, it is necessary to use groundspeed, not airspeed.

Per Fig. 138, two dots at 1.9 NM indicate 140 ft. above the glide slope and ; : 710 ft. to the right of the localizer.

questions.choice_a: 1,550 feet to the left of the localizer centerline ve o the outer marker. Two dots on the localizer mean and 210 feet below the glide slope. Answer (B) is incorrect. The airplane is to the left, not right, of the localizer and below, not above, the glide slope. Answer (C)

questions.choice_c: 775 feet to the left of the localizer centerline localizer. and 420 feet below the glide slope.

Which displacement from the localizer and DISCUSSION: The airplane is below the glide slope by glide slope at the outer marker is indicated? one dot and to the left of the localizer by two dots at the outer marker. According to Fig. 138, one dot on the glide slope is

questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 134 on page 413.) What are the required weather minimums to execute the CONVERGING ILS RWY 9R approach procedure?
questions.choice_c: Ceiling 800 feet and 2 miles visibility.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: In order to execute an instrument approach under Part 121 or 135, the conditions at the airport of landing must be at or above landing minimums. By referring to the minimums section of the approach chart for Fig. 134, you can see that, for the converging ILS RWY 9R approach, the decision height will be 721 ft. with visibility of 2 1/2 SM and a HAT of 700 ft. Since ATIS broadcasts ceiling in relation to AGL, not MSL, a ceiling of 700 ft. is related to the HAT figure on the approach chart. Therefore, if the ATIS broadcasts a ceiling at or above 700 ft. and a visibility of at least 2 1/2 SM, you will be at or above the required minimums to execute this approach. Answer (B) is incorrect. This defines the minimums required to land while operating under VFR, not the IFR minimums to execute the converging ILS RWY 9R instrument approach. Answer (C) is incorrect. This defines the minimums required to file a specific airport with a nonprecision approach as an alternate and not required minimums to execute the converging ILS RWY 9R instrument approach.

questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 134 on page 413.) What are the required weather minimums to execute the converging ILS RWY 9R approach procedure?
questions.choice_c: Atleast 3,000 ft. and 1 mile visibility.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: In order to execute an instrument approach under Part 121 or 135, the conditions at the airport of landing must be at or above landing minimums. Refer to the minimums section of the approach chart in Fig. 134, which shows that for the converging ILS RWY 9R approach, the decision height will be 721 ft. with visibility of 2 1/2 SM and a HAT of 700 ft. Since visibility is the controlling factor for starting an approach, 2 1/2 mile visibility is the best answer. Answer (A) is incorrect. The controlling factor for starting an approach is visibility. So for 121 or 135 operators, they cannot start the approach unless the visibility is at or above the minimums on the approach. Fig. 134 shows that visibility must be at least 2 1/2 SM to execute the converging ILS RWY 9R approach. Answer (C) is incorrect. The ceiling must be at or above 700 ft. and visibility must be at least 2 1/2 SM. Since visibility is the controlling factor to start an approach, the best answer is 2 1/2 miles.

questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 134 on page 413.) What is the final approach fix for the CONVERGING ILS RWY 9R approach procedure?
questions.choice_c: 1,800 feet MSL and glide slope interception.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: For precision approaches, the glide slope intercept altitude depicted with a lightning bolt-type arrow indicates the final approach fix. Answer (A) is incorrect. By looking at the planview of the chart (Fig. 134), BWINE intersection is labeled as an IAF or initial approach fix, not a final approach fix. Answer (B) is incorrect. KELEE intersection identifies only the outer marker. Note that the lightning bolt arrow is depicted just prior to KELEE intersection.

questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 134 on page 413.) What is the MINIMUM airborne equipment required to execute the CONVERGING ILS RWY 9R approach procedure?
questions.choice_c: Localizer only.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: In order to execute an ILS approach that provides both lateral and vertical guidance, an aircraft requires both a localizer and a glide slope. Answer (A) is incorrect. The heading section of this chart does not include DME; therefore, DME is not required. Answer (C) is incorrect. In the minimums section of the chart, the initials NA are labeled in the straight-in localizer box; therefore, a localizer-only approach is not authorized.

questions.question_text: How does the LDA differ from an ILS LOC?
questions.choice_b: LDA -- offset from runway plus 3°, ILS -- aligned with runway.
questions.choice_c: LDA -- 15° usable off course indications, ILS -- SOM
questions.explanation: ; DISCUSSION: The localizer-type directional aid (LDA) is of comparable utility and accuracy to a localizer but is not part of a complete ILS. The LDA does not have to be aligned with the runway. The straight-in minimum may be published if alignment does not exceed 30° between the course and the runway. ILS localizers are aligned with the runway. Answer (A) is incorrect. An SDF, not LDA, is fixed at either 6° or 12° wide. Answer (C) is incorrect. The usable off-course indications are limited to 35° for both types of approaches within 10 NM.

questions.question_text: What facilities may be substituted for an inoperative middle marker during a Category | ILS approach?
questions.choice_b: The middle marker has no effect on straight-in minimums.
questions.choice_c: Compass locator, PAR, and ASR.
questions.explanation: A compass locator or precision radar (PAR) may be substituted for the middle marker. Since an inoperative middle marker does not affect the straight-in minimums of an ILS approach, no substitution is required. Answer (A) is incorrect. Surveillance radar (ASR) can be substituted for the outer marker, not the middle marker. Answer (C) is incorrect. Surveillance radar (ASR) can be substituted for the outer marker, not the middle marker.

questions.question_text: Which “rule-of-thumb” may be used to approximate the rate of descent required for a 3° glidepath?
questions.choice_c: 10 times groundspeed in knots.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: As a general rule, you can calculate the approximate rate of descent required for a 3° glide slope by multiplying your groundspeed (in knots) by 5. Answer (B) is incorrect. Eight times the groundspeed would be used to calculate the rate of descent required for a 4.5°, not a 3°, glide slope. Answer (C) is incorrect. Ten times the groundspeed would be used to calculate the rate of descent required for a 6°, not a 3°, glide slope.

questions.question_text: When is the course deviation indicator (CDI) considered to have a full-scale deflection?
questions.choice_a: When the CDI deflects from full-scale left to full-scale right, or vice versa.
questions.choice_b: When the CDI deflects from the center of the scale to full-scale left or right.
questions.choice_c: When the CDI deflects from half-scale left to half-scale right, or vice versa.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: A full-scale CDI deflection occurs when the CDI moves full left or right, i.e., is fully deflected to one side or the other. Answer (A) is incorrect. When the CDI deflects full-scale left to full-scale right (or vice versa), you have two full-scale deflections. Answer (C) is incorrect. A deflection from half-scale left to half-scale right (or vice versa) is two half-scale deflections, not a full-scale deflection.

questions.question_text: When simultaneous approaches are in progress, how does each pilot receive radar advisories?
questions.choice_c: One pilot on tower frequency and the other on approach control frequency.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Whenever simultaneous approaches are in progress, radar advisories will be provided on the tower frequency. Answer (B) is incorrect. Pilots will be advised to monitor the tower, not approach control, frequency to receive radar advisories and instructions. Answer (C) is incorrect. Both pilots will receive radar advisories on the tower frequency.

questions.question_text: When simultaneous ILS approaches are in progress, which of the following should approach control be advised of immediately?
questions.choice_a: Any inoperative or malfunctioning aircraft receivers.
questions.choice_c: If radar monitoring is desired to confirm lateral separation.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: When advised that simultaneous ILS approaches are in progress, pilots shall advise approach control immediately if receivers are malfunctioning or inoperative or if simultaneous approach is not desired. Answer (B) is incorrect. Simultaneous approaches are issued at any time according to ATC needs, and it is not the responsibility of the pilot to request such an approach. Answer (C) is incorrect. Radar monitoring is always provided during simultaneous approaches.

questions.question_text: Precision runway monitoring requires pilot responsibility to monitor 2 simultaneous radios. pilot responsibility to monitor 2 ILS receivers. detailed performance during the “decision region”: 1/3 dot localizer and 1/2 dot glideslope.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: The PRM requires pilots to monitor both the primary and the monitor frequencies. This system prevents missed calls due to a blocked frequency. Answer (B) is incorrect. The pilot is responsible for monitoring two communication frequencies, not two ILS receivers. Answer (C) is incorrect. The pilot is responsible to monitor two communication frequencies during a PRM approach. There is no additional responsibility for performance in the “decision region.”

questions.question_text: Precision Runway Monitoring (PRM) is an airborne RADAR system for monitoring approaches to two runways. a RADAR system for monitoring approaches to closely spaced parallel runways. a high update rate RADAR system for monitoring multiple aircraft ILS approaches to a single runway.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Precision Runway Monitoring (PRM) is a RADAR system with a high update rate that closely monitors approaches to closely spaced parallel runways. PRM is certified to produce simultaneous independent approaches to closely spaced parallel runways. Answer (A) is incorrect. Precision Runway Monitoring (PRM) is not an airborne RADAR system; it is a ground-based RADAR system with a high update rate that closely monitors approaches to closely spaced parallel runways. Answer (C) is incorrect. Precision Runway Monitoring (PRM) is not for monitoring multiple aircraft ILS approaches to a single runway. Precision Runway Monitoring (PRM) is a RADAR system with a high update rate that closely monitors approaches to two closely spaced parallel runways.

questions.question_text: Utilizing PRM may require monitoring two communication frequencies. specialized training and monitoring 2 ILS frequencies. tracking parameters within decision region to 1/3 dot localizer and 1/2 dot glide slope displayed.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: The pilot is responsible to monitor two communication frequencies during a PRM approach: the primary and the monitor frequency. Answer (B) is incorrect. There is no requirement to monitor two ILS frequencies. The pilot must instead monitor two communication frequencies: the primary and the monitor. Answer (C) is incorrect. The PRM requires a pilot to monitor two specific communication frequencies. There is no requirement for tracking parameters in the decision region as this answer option suggests.

questions.question_text: How can the pilot determine, for an ILS runway equipped with MALSR, that there may be a penetration of the obstacle identification surfaces (OIS), and care should be taken in the visual segment to avoid any obstacles? The runway has a visual approach slope indicator (VASI). The published visibility for the ILS is no lower than 3/4 SM. The approach chart has a visual descent point (VDP) published.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: If there is penetration of the obstacle identification surfaces (OIS), the published visibility for the ILS approach can be no lower than 3/4 SM. Answer (A) is incorrect. If there is penetration of the obstacle identification surfaces for a particular approach, the way to determine that is to refer to the visibility minimums for the approach. A VASI-equipped runway is not an indication of the penetration of obstacle identification surfaces. Answer (C) is incorrect. If there is penetration of the obstacle identification surfaces for a particular approach, the way to determine that is to refer to the visibility minimums for the approach. An approach chart that has a published visual descent point is not an indication of the penetration of obstacle identification surfaces.

questions.question_text: Pilots are responsible for knowing
questions.choice_a: if the RNP missed approach is normal or reduced.
questions.choice_b: if the RNP registration is completed.
questions.choice_c: if they can conduct an RNP approach with an arc at a designated airspeed.
questions.explanation: he DISCUSSION: Pilots are responsible for knowing if they can conduct an RNP approach with an arc at a designated airspeed. Some RNP approaches have a curved path, also called a radius-to-a-fix (RF) leg. Since not all aircraft have the capability to fly these arcs, pilots are responsible for knowing if they can conduct an RNP approach with an arc or not. Aircraft speeds, winds, and bank angles have been taken into consideration in the development of the procedures. Answer (A) is incorrect. Missed approach procedures for the RNP are developed and based on standard approach speeds and a 200 ft./NM climb gradient. Any deviation from those standards will be indicated on the approach procedure, and the operator should ensure (s)he can comply with any published restrictions prior to conducting the operations. Answer (B) is incorrect. Special aircraft and aircrew authorization required procedures, like the RNP instrument approach procedures, are to be conducted by aircrews meeting special training requirements in aircraft that meet the specified performance and functional requirements.

questions.question_text: When cleared to execute a published side- step maneuver, at what point is the pilot expected to commence this maneuver?
questions.choice_b: At the MDA published or a circling approach.
questions.choice_c: As soon as possible after the runway environment is in sight.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Pilots are expected to commence the side- step maneuver as soon as possible after the runway or runway environment is in sight. Answer (A) is incorrect. The side-step maneuver can only and should be performed as soon as possible after the runway or runway environment is in sight. Answer (B) is incorrect. The side-step maneuver can only and should be performed as soon as possible after the runway or runway environment is in sight.

questions.question_text: When cleared to execute a published sidestep maneuver for a specific approach and landing on the parallel runway, at what point is the pilot expected to commence this maneuver?
questions.choice_a: Atthe published minimum altitude for a circling approach.
questions.choice_b: As soon as possible after tiie runway or runway environment is in sight.
questions.choice_c: Atthe localizer MDA minimums and when the runway is in sight.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Pilots are expected to commence the side- step maneuver as soon as possible after the runway or runway environment is in sight. Answer (A) is incorrect. The side-step maneuver can only and should be performed as soon as possible after the runway or runway environment is in sight, which may be below the published minimum altitude for a circling approach. Answer (C) is incorrect. The side-step maneuver should be performed as soon as possible after the runway or runway environment is in sight, which should be before the localizer MDA minimums.

questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 206 on page 417.) TNA 90 is a CAT C aircraft and has received a clearance to fly the LOC RWY 19L, sidestep to RWY 19R. What are the minimums?
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: The sidestep minimums for SIDESTEP RWY 19R for category C aircraft is listed in the Profile View of the approach plate. It is 340 - 1 1/2. Answer (B) is incorrect. Category A and B is 340/50. Answer (C) is incorrect. The Height Above Touchdown is 332, not the Minimum Descent Altitude.

questions.question_text: Which is a valid localizer transmitter frequency?
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: The localizer transmitter operates between 108.10 MHz and 111.95 MHz, on odd tenths, with 50 kHz spacing between each frequency. The frequency 108.3 would be 108.30 on the radio display. Answer (A) is incorrect. The frequency of 109.2 MHz is an even tenth number frequency. A valid localizer frequency has an odd tenth number frequency. Answer (C) is incorrect. The frequency of 117.6 MHz is outside the operational frequency range of 108.10 to 111.95 MHz.

questions.question_text: The Glidepath Qualification Surface (GQS) limits the height of obstructions between
questions.choice_a: the Final Approach Point and the Missed Approach Point.
questions.choice_b: any Initial Approach Point and the Final Approach Fix.
questions.choice_c: Decision Altitude and the Runway Threshold.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: When obstructions exceed the height of the GQS, an approach procedure with positive vertical guidance is not authorized. The GQS limits the height of obstructions between the Decision Altitude (DA) and the Runway Threshold (RWT). Answer (A) is incorrect. Between the Final Approach Point and the Missed Approach Point is the descent phase of the approach and is a higher altitude than that of the Decision Altitude and the Runway Threshold. The extremely low altitude at the Decision Altitude and the Runway Threshold requires strict height limits of any obstructions. Answer (B) is incorrect. Any Initial Approach Fix and the Final Approach Fix are at an altitude that most obstructions are cleared.

questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 290 on page 423.) Where is the final approach fix for the simultaneous close parallel ILS PRM RWY 26 approach at PHL?
questions.choice_a: FOSOM at 2,000 feet MSL and on course.
questions.choice_b: HOLEY descending to 2,000 feet MSL and established on course.
questions.choice_c: 2,000 feet MSL and glideslope intercept.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: The final approach fix (FAF) is indicated by the lightning bolt on this precision approach. The FAF altitude is indicated as 2,000 feet at the glideslope intercept. This is indicated by the lightning bolt symbol in the Profile view of the approach plate. Answer (A) is incorrect. FOSOM is labeled in the overhead view as an Initial Approach Fix (IAF) and is not the FAF. Answer (B) is incorrect. HOLEY is labeled in the overhead view as an Initial Approach Fix (IAF) and is not the FAF.

questions.choice_a: 111.3 MHz. spacing between each frequency. The frequency 111.3 would be
questions.choice_b: 113.25 MHz. Answer (B) is incorrect. The frequency of 113.25 MHz
