questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 292 on page 425.) With
questions.choice_a: at least 2400 RVR to fly the approach. Answer (A) is incorrect. The landing minimum for runway
questions.choice_b: areported RVR of 1800 to fly the approach. visual range is 2,400 RVR and not a minimum to fly the approach. Answer (C) is incorrect. The landing minimum for an
questions.choice_c: atleast 2400 RVR to land. aircraft without the use of a FD, AP, or HUD is 2,400 RVR.
questions.explanation: no equipment inoperative (no MEL items) in your DISCUSSION: There is a note at the top of the approach autopilot and flight director equipped airplane, you plate that reads: “.. . **RVR 1800 authorized with use of FD or know you must have AP or HUD to DA.” This indicates the use of 1,800 feet RVR is authorized when using a Flight Director (FD), an Autopilot (AP), or a Heads Up Display (HUD) to Decision Altitude (DA).

questions.question_text: Aircraft navigating by GPS are considered, on the flight plan, to be
questions.choice_c: FMS/EFIS equipped.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Aircraft navigating by GPS are considered to be RNAV-equipped aircraft; thus, the appropriate equipment suffix must be included on the flight plan. Answer (B) is incorrect. Aircraft navigating by GPS are considered to be RNAV, not astrotracker, equipped. Answer (C) is incorrect. Aircraft navigating by GPS are considered to be RNAV, not FMS/EFIS, equipped.

questions.question_text: The GPS Approach Overlay Program permits pilots to use GPS avionics when IFR for flying existing instrument approach procedures, except
questions.choice_c: SDF, LOC and LDA.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: The GPS Approach Overlay Program permits pilots to use GPS avionics under IFR for flying existing IAPs, except SDF, LOC, and LDA procedures. Answer (A) is incorrect. The GPS Approach Overlay Program permits pilots to use GPS avionics under IFR for flying existing IAPs except SDF procedures. Answer (B) is incorrect. The GPS Approach Overlay Program permits pilots to use GPS avionics under IFR for flying existing IAPs except LOC, not TACAN, procedures.

questions.question_text: The Instrument Approach Procedure Chart top margin identification is VOR or GPS RWY 25, AL-5672 (FAA), LUKACHUKAI, ARIZONA. In what phase of the approach overlay program is this GPS approach?
questions.choice_c: Phase ll.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Phase III of the GPS Approach Overlay Program begins when IAPs are retitled, such as “VOR or GPS RWY 25.” In this phase, ground-based NAVAIDs are not required to be operational, and the associated aircraft avionics (e.g., VOR) need not be installed, operational, turned on, or monitored. GPS approaches will be requested and approved using the GPS title, such as “GPS RWY 25.” Answer (A) is incorrect. Under Phase |, the IAP chart title would be VOR RWY 25, not VOR or GPS RWY 25. Answer (C) is incorrect. Under Phase II, the IAP chart title would be VOR RWY 25.

questions.question_text: Without Receiver Autonomous Integrity Monitoring (RAIM) capability, the accuracy of the GPS derived
questions.choice_a: altitude information should not be relied upon to determine aircraft altitude.
questions.choice_c: velocity information should be relied upon to determine aircraft groundspeed.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: RAIM is used to determine if a satellite is providing corrupted information. Without RAIM capability, the accuracy of the GPS-derived altitude information should not be relied upon to determine aircraft altitude since the vertical error can be quite large. Answer (B) is incorrect. RAIM is used to determine if a satellite is providing corrupted information. Without RAIM, the GPS position should NOT be relied upon since the position solution can be greatly affected. Answer (C) is incorrect. RAIM is used to determine if a satellite is providing corrupted information.

questions.question_text: Overriding an automatically selected sensitivity during a GPS approach will
questions.choice_b: require flying point-to-point on the approach to comply with the published approach procedure.
questions.choice_c: have no effect if the approach is flown manually.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: When within 2 NM of the final approach waypoint with the approach mode armed, the approach mode will switch to active, which results in RAIM changing to approach sensitivity and a change in CDI sensitivity. Overriding an automatically selected sensitivity during an approach will cancel the approach mode annunciation. Answer (B) is incorrect. Flying point-to-point on the approach does NOT ensure compliance with the published approach procedure. Answer (C) is incorrect. Manually setting CDI sensitivity does not automatically change RAIM sensitivity on some GPS receivers.

questions.question_text: If a visual descent point (VDP) is published on a GPS approach, it
questions.choice_a: will not be included in the sequence of waypoints.
questions.choice_b: will be coded in the waypoint sequence and identified using ATD.
questions.choice_c: must be included in the normal waypoints.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: If a visual descent point (VDP) is published on a GPS approach, it will not be included in the sequence of waypoints. Pilots are expected to use normal piloting techniques for beginning the visual descent. Answer (B) is incorrect. A VDP will NOT be included in the waypoint sequence. Answer (C) is incorrect. If a VDP is published on a GPS approach, it will NOT be included in the normal waypoint sequence.

questions.question_text: Authorization to conduct any GPS operation under IFR requires that
questions.choice_a: the equipment be approved in accordance with TSO C-115a.
questions.choice_b: the pilot must review appropriate weather, aircraft flight manual (AFM), and operation of the particular receiver.
questions.choice_c: procedures must be established for use in the event that the loss of RAIM capability is predicted to occur.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Authorization to conduct any GPS operation under IFR requires, among others, that procedures be established for use in the event that the loss of RAIM capability is predicted to occur. In situations in which this event is encountered, the pilot must rely on other approved equipment, delay departure, or cancel the flight. Answer (A) is incorrect. To conduct any GPS operation under IFR, the equipment must be approved in accordance with TSO C-129 or equivalent. TSO C-115a does not meet the requirements of TSO C-129. Answer (B) is incorrect. Before conducting any GPS operation under IFR, the pilot must be thoroughly familiar with, not just review, the GPS equipment installed, the GPS operation manual, and the aircraft flight manual. Additionally, prior to any GPS IFR operation, the pilot must review the appropriate NOTAMs, not only the weather.

questions.question_text: Authorization to conduct any GPS operation under IFR requires that
questions.choice_a: the pilot review appropriate weather, aircraft flight manual (AFM), and operation of the particular GPS receiver.
questions.choice_b: air carrier and commercial operators must meet the appropriate provisions of their approved operations specifications.
questions.choice_c: the equipment be approved in accordance with TSO C-115a.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Authorization to conduct any GPS operation under IFR requires that, among others, air carriers and commercial operators meet the appropriate provisions of their approved operations specifications. Answer (A) is incorrect. Before conducting any GPS operation under IFR, the pilot must be thoroughly familiar with, not just review, the GPS equipment installed, the GPS operation manual, and the aircraft flight manual. Additionally, prior to any GPS IFR operation, the pilot must review the appropriate NOTAMs, not only the weather. Answer (C) is incorrect. To conduct any GPS operation under IFR, the equipment must be approved in accordance with TSO C-129 or equivalent. TSO C-115a does not meet the requirements of TSO C-129.

questions.question_text: GPS instrument approach operations, outside the United States, must be authorized by
questions.choice_a: the FAA-approved aircraft flight manual (AFM) or flight manual supplement.
questions.choice_c: the FAA Administrator only.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: GPS instrument approach operations outside the U.S. must be authorized by the appropriate sovereign authority. Answer (A) is incorrect. The aircraft flight manual or the supplement explains how to operate the GPS system installed in the airplane; it does not authorize that GPS be used for any type of GPS operation. Answer (C) is incorrect. GPS instrument approach operations inside, not outside, the U.S. must be authorized by the FAA Administrator.

questions.question_text: A pilot employed by an air carrier and/or commercial operator may conduct GPS/WAAS instrument approaches A._ if they are not prohibited by the FAA-approved aircraft flight manual and the flight manual supplement.
questions.choice_b: only if approved in their air carrier/commercial operator operations specifications.
questions.choice_c: only if the pilot was evaluated on GPS/WAAS approach procedures during their most recent proficiency check.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: In order to use GPS/WAAS instrument _ approaches, pilots employed by an air carrier or in commercial operations must meet the appropriate provisions of their approved operations specifications. Answer (A) is incorrect. Although the aircraft flight manual can prohibit specific activities via placards or approved , maneuver lists, pilots employed by an air carrier or in commercial operations can legally conduct GPS/WAAS instrument approaches provided they meet the appropriate provisions of their approved operations specifications. Answer (C) is incorrect. Pilots employed by an air carrier or in commercial operations must meet the appropriate provisions of their approved operations specifications in order to use GPS/WAAS instrument approaches. There is currently no requirement that the pilot be evaluated on GPS/WAAS approaches on each proficiency check to allow them the option of utilizing these approaches.

questions.question_text: Pilots are not authorized to fly a published RNAV or RNP procedure unless it is retrievable by the procedure name from
questions.choice_a: the aircraft navigation database, or manually loaded with each individual waypoint in the correct sequence.
questions.choice_b: the aircraft navigation database, or manually loaded with each individual waypoint and verified by the pilot(s).
questions.choice_c: the aircraft navigation database.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Pilots are not authorized to fly a published RNAV or RNP procedure (instrument approach, departure, or arrival procedure) unless it is retrievable by the procedure name from the aircraft navigation database and conforms to the charter procedure. Answer (A) is incorrect. Pilots may not fly a published RNAV or RNP procedure unless it is retrievable by the procedure name from the aircraft navigation database. Manually loading each waypoint in the correct sequence is not an acceptable substitute for the established method of retrieving the procedure. Answer (B) is incorrect. Pilots may not fly a published RNAV or RNP procedure unless it is retrievable by the procedure name from the aircraft navigation database. Manually loading each waypoint and verifying them is not an acceptable substitute for the established method of retrieving the procedure.

questions.question_text: When using GPS for navigation and instrument approaches, a required alternate airport must have
questions.choice_a: an approved instrument approach procedure, besides GPS, that is expected to be operational and available at the ETA.
questions.choice_b: aGPS approach that is expected to be operational and available at the ETA.
questions.choice_c: authorization to fly approaches under IFR using GPS avionics.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: When using GPS for navigation and instrument approaches, any required alternate airport must have an approved instrument approach procedure, other than GPS, that is expected to be operational and available at the ETA and that the airplane is equipped to fly. Answer (B) is incorrect. When using GPS for navigation and instrument approaches, any required alternate must have an approved instrument approach procedure, other than a GPS approach, that is expected to be operational and available at the ETA. Answer (C) is incorrect. When using GPS for navigation and instrument approaches, any required alternate airport must have an approved instrument approach procedure, other than GPS, that is expected to be operational and available at the ETA. The alternate airport is not required to have an approved GPS approach.

questions.question_text: If Receiver Autonomous Integrity Monitoring (RAIM) is not available prior to beginning a GPS approach, the pilot should
questions.choice_a: continue to the MAP and hold until the satellites are recaptured.
questions.choice_b: continue the approach, expecting to recapture the satellites before reaching the IAF.
questions.choice_c: use a navigation or approach system other than GPS for an approach.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: If RAIM is not available when setting up for a GPS approach, you should inform ATC and select another type of approach using another type of navigation aid. Answer (A) is incorrect. If a RAIM failure occurs prior to the final approach waypoint (FAWP), not when setting up for a GPS approach, you should proceed to the missed approach waypoint via the FAWP, perform the missed approach, and contact ATC as soon as possible, not hold until satellites are recaptured. Answer (B) is incorrect. If RAIM is not available when setting up for a GPS approach, you should advise ATC and select another type of approach using another type of navigation aid, not proceed to the IAF and hold until satellite reception is satisfactory.

questions.question_text: A GPS missed approach requires that the pilot take action to sequence the receiver
questions.choice_c: just prior to the MAWP.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: A GPS missed approach requires that you take action to sequence the receiver past the missed approach waypoint (MAWP) to the missed approach portion of the procedure. You must be thoroughly familiar with the activation procedure for the particular GPS receiver installed in the airplane and must initiate appropriate action after the MAWP. Answer (A) is incorrect. A GPS missed approach requires that you take action to sequence the receiver after, not over, the MAWP. Answer (C) is incorrect. Activating the missed approach prior to the MAWP will cause the CDI sensitivity to change to terminal (+1 NM) immediately, and the receiver will continue to navigate to the MAWP but not sequence past the MAWP. Thus, you should sequence the receiver after, not prior to, the MAWP.

questions.question_text: If the missed approach is not activated, the GPS receiver will display
questions.choice_a: an extension of the outbound final approach course, and the ATD will increase from the MAWP.
questions.choice_b: an extension of the outbound final approach course.
questions.choice_c: an extension of the inbound final approach course.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: If the missed approach is not activated, the GPS receiver will display an extension of the inbound final approach course, and the along track distance (ATD) will increase from the MAWP until it is manually sequenced after crossing the MAWP. Answer (A) is incorrect. If the missed approach is not activated, the GPS receiver will display an extension of the inbound, not outbound, final approach course, and the ATD will increase from the MAWP. Answer (B) is incorrect. If the missed approach is not activated, the GPS receiver will display an extension of the inbound, not outbound, final approach course, and the ATD will increase from the MAWP.

questions.question_text: Missed approach routing in which the first track is via a course rather than direct to the next waypoint requires
questions.choice_a: that the GPS receiver be sequenced to the missed approach portion of the procedure.
questions.choice_b: manual intervention by the pilot, but will not be required, if RAIM is available.
questions.choice_c: additional action by the operator to set the course.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Missed approach routings in which the first track is via a course rather than direct to the next waypoint require additional action by the pilot to set the course. Being familiar with all of the inputs required is especially critical during this phase of flight. Answer (A) is incorrect. Missed approach routings in which the first track is via a course rather than direct to the next waypoint requires that the pilot take additional action to set the course, not that the GPS receiver be sequenced to the missed approach portion of the procedure. Answer (B) is incorrect. Missed approach routings in which the first track is via a course rather than direct to a waypoint requires additional action by the pilot to set the course, even if RAIM is available.

questions.question_text: If flying a published GPS departure,
questions.choice_a: the data base will contain all of the transition or departures from all runways.
questions.choice_b: and if RAIM is available, manual intervention by the pilot should not be required.
questions.choice_c: the GPS receiver must be set to terminal course deviation indicator sensitivity.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: The GPS receiver must be set to terminal (+1 NM) CDI sensitivity, and the navigation routes must be contained in the database in order to fly published IFR charted departures and SIDs. Answer (A) is incorrect. The database may not contain all of the transitions or departures from all runways, and some GPS receivers do not contain SIDs in the database. Answer (B) is incorrect. Certain segments of a SID may require some manual intervention by the pilot, even if RAIM is available, especially when radar vectored to a course or required to intercept a specific course to a waypoint.

questions.question_text: When the use of RNAV equipment using GPS input is planned to be used for an instrument approach at a destination airport, any required alternate airport must have an available instrument approach procedure that does not require the use of GPS except when the RNAV system has an IRU input. require the use of GPS except when dual, independent GPS receivers are installed. require the use of GPS except when the RNAV system has a WAAS input.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Any required alternate airport must have an available instrument approach procedure that does not require the use of GPS. The exception to that rule applies to RNAV systems using WAAS equipment. Answer (A) is incorrect. The restriction stipulates that the alternate airport must have an instrument approach procedure available that does not require GPS, unless the RNAV system utilizes WAAS equipment. There is no exception for RNAV systems with an IRU input. Answer (B) is incorrect. The restriction stipulates that the alternate airport must have an instrument approach procedure available that does not require GPS, unless the RNAV system utilizes WAAS equipment. Dual, independent GPS receivers that are not WAAS compliant would not meet the conditions for the exception.

questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 251 on page 431.) You are cleared to HNL and plan to use the RNAV (RNP) RWY 26L approach. Assuming you have received the training, you
questions.choice_a: should be prepared to program the FMS/GPS with the radio frequency to fly this approach.
questions.choice_b: can use the GPS and radio frequency communications to fly this approach to minimums.
questions.choice_c: know your FMS/GPS must have GPS and radius-to-fix capability.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: A key component necessary to realize the benefits of RNP approaches is the ability to fly curved flight tracks within a precisely defined airspace area. Accordingly, RNP approach procedures require the FMS/GPS to have the capability to receive GPS signals and to fly radius-to-fix (RF) segments. This is indicated by the statement “RF and GPS REQUIRED?” in the notes section of the approach chart. Answer (A) is incorrect. GNSS Landing System (GLS) approaches such as those using the Local Area Augmentation System (LAAS), not RNAV (RNP) approaches, require the pilot to select a five-digit channel number associated with the approach. RNAV (RNP) approaches use standard GPS signals and do not require tuning a specific radio frequency or channel. Answer (B) is incorrect. Precision Approach Radar (PAR) and ILS with Precision Runway Monitoring (ILS-PRM) approaches, not RNAV (RNP) approaches, require the use of radio communications. RNAV (RNP) approaches use standard GPS signals and do not require tuning a specific communication frequency other than the frequency assigned by ATC.

questions.question_text: To conduct an RNAV (GPS) approach to LPV minimums, the aircraft must be furnished with
questions.choice_a: aGPS/WAAS receiver approved for an LPV approach by the AFM supplement.
questions.choice_b: a GPS (TSO-C129) receiver certified for IFR operations.
questions.choice_c: anIFR approach-certified system with required navigation performance (RNP) of 0.5.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: WAAS avionics must be certified in accordance with Technical Standard Order (TSO) TSO-C145 or TSO-C146. GPS/WAAS operation must be conducted in accordance with the FAA-approved aircraft flight manual (AFM) and flight manual supplements. The flight manual supplements will state the level of approach procedure that the receiver supports. Answer (B) is incorrect. Stand-alone GPS equipment used in IFR operations for approach to LPV minimums must meet the standards set forth in TSO-C145 or TSO-C146, not TSO-C129. Answer (C) is incorrect. An approach certified system must provide RNP of 0.3, not 0.5.

questions.question_text: In order to use a substitute means of guidance on departure procedures, pilots of aircraft with RNAV systems using DME/DME/IRU without GPS input must
questions.choice_a: ensure their aircraft navigation system position is confirmed within 2,000 feet of the initialization point.
questions.choice_b: ensure their aircraft navigation system position is confirmed within 1,000 feet of pushback.
questions.choice_c: ensure their aircraft navigation system position is confirmed within 1,000 feet at the start point of takeoff roll.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Pilots using DME/DME/IRU without GPS/ GNSS must ensure the aircraft navigation system position is confirmed within 1,000 feet at the start point of the takeoff roll. Answer (A) is incorrect. The tolerance is 1,000 feet, not 2,000 feet, and the reference point is the start of the takeoff roll, not the initialization point. Answer (B) is incorrect. The reference point for the aircraft navigation system position is the start of the takeoff roll, not the pushback position.

questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 252 on page 433.) In reviewing the RNAV (GPS) RWY 4 approach to LEW, you note the lack of a shaded fan from the 1.6 NM to RW04 point to the runway threshold. This indicates
questions.choice_a: the visual segment below the MDA/DA is not clear of obstacles on a 34:1 slope.
questions.choice_b: you can descend on a 20:1 slope and remain clear of all obstacles.
questions.choice_c: the runway does not have a visual glideslope landing aid.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: In the legend at the front of a U.S. Terminal Procedures approach book, in the middle of the page, you will see a very small shaded area (a shaded fan) that indicates that the visual segment below MDA/DA is clear of obstacles on a 34:1 slope. If there is no shaded fan, this indicates a 34:1 slope is not clear of obstacles. For an example of this shaded area, refer to Fig. 251 on page 431. It is the small shaded area near the runway end in the profile view. Answer (B) is incorrect. A 20:1 slope would put obstacles very close to the glideslope and could be hazardous. Answer (C) is incorrect. A shaded fan would indicate that the visual segment below MDA/DA is clear of obstacles on a 34:1 slope, not that there is no visual glideslope landing aid.

questions.question_text: During flight planning, you selected an alternate airport serviced by only an RNAV (GPS/WAAS) approach. There are no prohibitions on the instrument approach procedure chart against using the airport as an alternate, and your aircraft is WAAS equipped. Therefore, the airport may
questions.choice_a: be selected as an alternate using the LNAV minima line.
questions.choice_b: not be selected as an alternate since it only has an RNAV (GPS) approach.
questions.choice_c: be selected as an alternate using the LPV minima line.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Installation of WAAS avionics does not require the aircraft to have other equipment appropriate to the route to be flown. There are certain restrictions on WAAS operations. Pilots may plan to use any instrument approach authorized for use with WAAS avionics at a required alternate. Answer (B) is incorrect. Unlike TSO-C129 avionics, which were certified as a supplement to other means of navigation, WAAS avionics are evaluated without reliance on other navigation systems. Installation of WAAS avionics does not require the aircraft to have other equipment appropriate to the route to be flown. Answer (C) is incorrect. When using WAAS at an alternate airport, flight planning must be based on flying the RNAV (GPS) LNAV minima line or minima on a GPS approach procedure or conventional approach procedure with “or GPS” in the title.

questions.question_text: Prior to conducting RNAV (GPS) approaches to localizer performance with vertical (LPV) guidance minimums, an air carrier pilot must
questions.choice_a: not be prohibited by the FAA approved flight manual from conducting these approaches.
questions.choice_b: be authorized by operations specification to conduct those approaches.
questions.choice_c: have successfully flown an RNAV (GPS) approach during their last proficiency check.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Like any other authorization given to air carriers and Part 91 operators, the authorization to use VNAV on a conventional non-precision approach, RNAV approaches, or LNAV/VNAV approaches is found in that operator's OpSpecs, Aircraft Flight Manual (AFM), or other FAA-approved documents. The type of equipment installed in the aircraft, the redundancy of that equipment and its operational status, the level of flight crew training, and the level of the operator's FAA authorization are all factors that can affect a pilot’s ability to use VNAV information on an approach. Answer (A) is incorrect. The company’s OpSpecs, Flight Operations Manual (FOM), and the Aircraft Flight Manual (AFM) for the pilot’s aircraft would dictate the specific operational conditions and procedures by which this type of approach could be flown. Answer (C) is incorrect. Before an RNAV (GPS) approach can be done, authorization to do this approach must be given. This authorization is found in the OpSpecs, Aircraft Flight Manual (AFM), or other FAA-approved documents.

questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 250 on page 435.) During the preflight brief, you receive a WAAS [MAY NOT BE AVBL] NOTAM for the RNAV (GPS) RWY 2 approach at KCHA. For planning purposes only, you know that you can descend to the
questions.choice_c: LPV DA minima of 882 ft. and 2,400 RVR in your CAT B aircraft.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: WAAS [MAY NOT BE AVBL] NOTAMs are predictive in nature and published for flight planning purposes only. Upon arrival at a location that has been NOTAMed WAAS [MAY NOT BE AVBL], if the WAAS avionics indicated LNAV/ VNAV or LPV service is available, then vertical guidance may be used to complete the approach using the displayed level of service. Should an outage occur during the approach, reversion to LNAV minimums may be required. Answer (A) is incorrect. Vertical navigation (VNAV) utilizes an internally generated glideslope based on WAAS. Answer (C) is incorrect. LPV approaches take advantage of the refined accuracy of WAAS for lateral and vertical guidance.

questions.question_text: “Unreliable”, as indicated in the following GPS NOTAMS: SFO 12/051 SFOWAAS LNAV/VNAV AND LPV MNM UNRELBL WEF0512182025-0512182049 means
questions.choice_a: within the time parameters of the NOTAM, the predicted level of service will not support LPV approaches.
questions.choice_b: satellite signals are currently unavailable to support LPV and LNAV/VNAV approaches.
questions.choice_c: within the time parameters of the NOTAM, the predicted level of service will not support RNAV approaches.
questions.explanation: . DISCUSSION: The term “unreliable” (UNRELBL) is an advisory to pilots indicating that the expected level of WAAS service may not be available. This NOTAM indicates that both LNAV/VNAV and LPV approaches may or may not be available. Answer (B) is incorrect. When satellite signals are not available, the name of the satellite and the words “SATELLITE UNAVBL’ will be included in the NOTAM (i.e., “PACIFIC SATELLITE UNAVBL’). Answer (C) is incorrect. This NOTAM does not support RNAV approaches.

questions.question_text: You arrive at the initial fix for the LPV approach into XYZ. The preflight briefer issued you an unreliable advisory on the approach before you took off. Your avionics indicates good signals and full GPS service is available. You
questions.choice_a: know you can fly the approach down to LPV minimums.
questions.choice_b: cannot use that approach because of the advisory from FSS.
questions.choice_c: must revert to another approach system such as VOR.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: WAAS UNRELIABLE NOTAMs are predictive in nature and published for flight planning purposes only. Upon arrival at a location that has been NOTAMed WAAS UNRELIABLE, if the WAAS avionics indicate LNAV/VNAV or LPV service is available, then vertical guidance may be used to complete the approach using the displayed level of service. Should an outage occur during the approach, reversion to LNAV minimums may be required. Answer (B) is incorrect. WAAS UNRELIABLE NOTAMs are predictive in nature and published for flight planning purposes only. Upon arrival at a location that has been NOTAMed WAAS UNRELIABLE, if the WAAS avionics indicate LNAV/VNAV or LPV service is available, then vertical guidance may be used to complete the approach using the displayed level of service. Answer (C) is incorrect. WAAS UNRELIABLE NOTAMs are predictive in nature and published for flight planning purposes only. Upon arrival at a location that has been NOTAMed WAAS UNRELIABLE, if the WAAS avionics indicate LNAV/VNAV or LPV service is available, then vertical guidance may be used to complete the approach using the displayed level of service.

questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 249 on page 437.) You arrive at PILOC on the RNAV (GPS) RWY 19R approach to BET. The briefer gave you an UNRELIABLE advisory for the approach before takeoff. Your avionics indicate good signals, and GPS service is available.
questions.choice_a: You can only fly the approach to LNAV DA minimums of 459 because of the advisory.
questions.choice_b: You can only fly the approach down to the LNAV MDA of 560.
questions.choice_c: You can use the LPV DA minimums of 368 and 2400 RVR in your category B airplane.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: The term “unreliable” is used in conjunction with GPS and WAAS NOTAMS. It is an advisory to pilots indicating the expected level of WAAS service (LNAV/VNAV/ LPV) may not be available. WAAS unreliable NOTAMs are predictive in nature and published for flight planning purposes. Once commencing an approach at locations NOTAMed WAAS UNRELIABLE, if the WAAS avionics indicate LNAV/VNAV or LPV service is available, then vertical guidance may be used to complete the approach using the displayed level of service. Should an outage occur during the approach, reversion to LNAV minima may be required. As the WAAS NOTAN is for planning purposes, you will know you can descend to the LNAV DA of 459 ft. as shown on the chart in Fig. 249. This applies to all category of aircraft. Answer (A) is incorrect. Once commencing an approach at locations NOTAMed WAAS UNRELIABLE, if the WAAS avionics indicate LNAV/VNAV or LPV service is available, then vertical guidance may be used to complete the approach using the displayed level of service. Answer (B) is incorrect. Should an outage occur during the approach, reversion to LNAV minima may be required. As the WAAS NOTANM is for planning purposes you will know you can descend to the LNAV DA of 459 ft. as shown on the chart in Fig. 249. ’

questions.subsection: 8.11 Ground Based Augmentation System (GBAS) Landing System (GLS)
questions.question_text: To utilize the localizer performance with vertical guidance (LPV) decision altitude (DA) minima on a RNAV (GPS) instrument approach procedure, the aircraft must be equipped with an appropriately certified
questions.choice_a: GPS receiver with mode S transponder.
questions.choice_c: GPS receiver with mode Z transponder.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: LPV identifies WAAS approach with vertical guidance (APV) approach minimums with electronic lateral and vertical guidance capability. The lateral guidance is equivalent to localizer accuracy, and the protected area is considerably smaller than the protected area for the present LNAV and LNAV/ VNAV lateral protection. Aircraft can fly this minima line with a statement in the AFM that the installed equipment supports LPV approaches. TSO-145A/146A certifies the GPS/WAAS receiver. Answer (A) is incorrect. Mode S transponders are covered under TSO-C112e and are not certified under TSO-145A/146A. Answer (C) is incorrect. It is the GPS that must be TSO certified and must have a minimum mode C transponder.

questions.subsection: 8.11 Ground Based Augmentation System (GBAS) Landing System (GLS)
questions.question_text: What does “UNREL’ indicate in the following GPS and WAAS NOTAM: BOS BOS WAAS LPV AND LNAV/VNAV MNM UNREL WEF 0305231700 - 0305231815?
questions.choice_a: Satellite signals are currently unavailable to support LPV and LNAV/VNAV approaches to the Boston airport.
questions.choice_b: The predicted level of service, within the time parameters of the NOTAM, may not support LPV approaches.
questions.choice_c: The predicted level of service, within the time parameters of the NOTAM, will not support LNAV/VNAV approaches. 8.11 Ground Based Augmentation System (GBAS) Landing System (GLS)
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: The FAA’s WAAS UNRELIABLE NOTAMs are predictive in nature and are published for flight planning purposes. The date/time group of numbers at the end of this NOTAM indicate a specific window when the system may be unreliable. Answer (A) is incorrect. The FAA’s WAAS UNRELIABLE NOTAMs are predictive in nature and are published for flight planning purposes. The date/time group of numbers at the end of this NOTAM indicate a specific window when the system may be unreliable. The outage is not current, but predicted for a future time frame that will last for 1 hour and 15 minutes. Answer (C) is incorrect. GPS and WAAS NOTAMSs are designed to provide information regarding the viability of LNAV, LNAV/VNAV, and LPV approaches.

questions.subsection: 8.11 Ground Based Augmentation System (GBAS) Landing System (GLS)
questions.question_text: GBAS provides
questions.choice_a: glidepath database alignment for non-linear approaches.
questions.choice_b: ground-based assessment of runway and taxiing airport traffic.
questions.choice_c: precision navigation guidance for GLS.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: The GLS provides precision navigation guidance for exact alignment and descent of aircraft on approach to a runway. It provides differential augmentation to the Global Navigation Satellite System (GNSS). GBAS is the ICAO term for Local Area Augmentation System (LAAS). Answer (A) is incorrect. The LAAS was developed as an “ILS look-alike” system from the pilot perspective and provides precision guidance for exact alignment and descent of aircraft on approach to a runway. Answer (B) is incorrect. The equipment consists of a GBAS Ground Facility (GGF), four reference stations, a VHF Data Broadcast (VDB) uplink antenna, and an aircraft GBAS receiver. The system has it for approach purposes au It does not assess ground-based runway and taxiing airport raffic.

questions.subsection: 8.11 Ground Based Augmentation System (GBAS) Landing System (GLS)
questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 293 on page 439.) What must be operational for N711JB to execute the VOR or GPS RWY 13L/13R approach to JFK?
questions.choice_b: Lead-in Light System, Radar and DME.
questions.choice_c: Lead-in Light System, VOR/DME or GPS.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: In the middle and to the right on the Instrument Approach Plate is the indication “DME or RADAR REQUIRED.” The approach requires at least one of these supported avionics to fly the procedure. There is a note at the top of the approach plate that reads “For inoperative LDIN, procedure not authorized.” LDIN is the acronym for “Lead-in Light System.” Answer (A) is incorrect. WAAS is not needed for this approach. Answer (B) is incorrect. Radar and DME should be fanny OR DME. Also needed is a VOR or GPS along with the

questions.subsection: 8.11 Ground Based Augmentation System (GBAS) Landing System (GLS)
questions.question_text: GBAS approaches are
questions.choice_a: automatically tuned and displayed after selection of the 3-letter procedure identifier.
questions.choice_b: flown the same as an LDA with glideslope selection and identification.
questions.choice_c: flown using the same techniques as an ILS once selected and identified.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Pilots will select the five-digit GBAS channel number of the associated approach within the Flight Management System (FMS) menu or manually select the five digits (system dependent). Following the procedure selection, confirmation that the correct LAAS procedure is loaded can be done by cross checking the charted Reference Path Indicator (RPI) or approach ID with the cockpit displayed RPI or audio identification of the RPI with Morse Code (for some systems). The pilot will fly the GLS approach using the same techniques as an ILS once selected and identified. Answer (A) is incorrect. Pilots will select the five-digit GBAS channel number, not a 3-letter procedure identifier. Answer (B) is incorrect. The pilot will fly the GLS approach using the same techniques as an ILS once selected and identified.

questions.subsection: 8.11 Ground Based Augmentation System (GBAS) Landing System (GLS)
questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 322 on page 441.) The highest terrain shown in the planview section of the LDA/DME RWY 25 approach to Eagle County Regional is
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: The highest of the values that correspond to an actual obstacle on this instrument approach plate is 11,573. It is located on the NE side of the approach plate, just above the (IAF) VOAXA. Answer (B) is incorrect. The figure 11,320 feet is lower than the obstacle found in the NE portion of the approach plate. Answer (C) is incorrect. The figure 12,200 feet is the minimum enroute altitude. It can also be seen on the profile of the approach plate. The aircraft must be at or above 12,200 feet when crossing WEHAL at 15.3 DME.

questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 94 on page 472, Figure 95 on
questions.choice_c: 2 hours 18 minutes.
questions.explanation: page 473, and Figure 96 on page 445.) What is the DISCUSSION: To determine the ETE from Chicago Midway ETE from Chicago Midway Airport to Greater Buffalo Airport to Greater Buffalo Intl., you must complete the flight log Intl?

questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 94 on page 472, Figure 95 on page 473, and Figure 96 on page 445.) What are the fuel requirements from Chicago Midway Airport to Greater Buffalo Intl?
questions.choice_c: 1,454 pounds.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: To determine the fuel requirements from Chicago Midway to Greater Buffalo, you must complete the fuel portion of the flight log on Fig. 94. Use 610 PPH fuel flow from level-off to start of descent and 710 PPH fuel flow for the flight to the alternate (ROC) and the reserve requirements. Time Fuel (Ib.) Level-off 0:19:00G 327.0G GlJ 0:05:48 59.0 GIJ/CRL COP 0:19:12 195.2 CRL 0:19:22 196.9 CRL/YXU COP 0:16:10 164.4 YXU 0:16:10 164.4 YXU/BUF COP 0:15:25 156.7 BUF R-282/30 0:06:59 falkO Descent and approach 0:14:00G 121.5G ROC 0:20:00G 236.7 Reserve 0:45:00 532.9 B22 G = Given Answer (B) is incorrect. This weight does not include the fuel required to fly to the alternate airport (ROC). Answer (C) is incorrect. This weight does not include the alternate and reserve fuel requirements.

questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 94 on page 472, Figure 95 on page 473, and Figure 96 on page 445.) What TAS should be maintained to arrive over CRL VORTAC 42 minutes after level-off?
questions.choice_c: 171 knots.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: To determine the required TAS to arrive over CRL VORTAC 42 min. after level-off, you need to determine the required groundspeed. The distance is 19 NM (L/O to GlJ) + 129 NM (GIJ to CRL) = 148 NM. The required groundspeed is 211 kt. [148 + (42/60)]. Next, compute the TAS on the wind side of your computer: 1. Set the average magnetic wind direction of 232° (231° to GlJ and 233° to CRL) under the true index, and place a pencil mark at 51 kt. (windspeed) up from the grommet. 2. Place the average MC of 086° (090° to GlJ, 082° to Glu/ CRL COP, and 087° to CRL) under the true index. 3. Slide the grid so the groundspeed of 211 kt. is under the grommet. 4. Read the TAS of 171 kt. under the pencil mark. Answer (A) is incorrect. This would be required if the wind speed were 59 kt., not 51 kt. Answer (B) is incorrect. This would be required if the wind speed were 55 kt., not 51 kt.

questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 94 on page 472, Figure 95 on page 473, and Figure 96 on page 445.) What action should be taken by the pilot, if communications are lost, while IMC, after takeoff on RWY 13L at Chicago Midway Airport?
questions.choice_a: Return and land immediately at Chicago Midway Airport.
questions.choice_b: Complete initially assigned turn south of DPA R-096, maintain 3,000 feet or lower if assigned. Then 10 minutes after departure, climb to FL 190, direct to GlJ, then flight plan route.
questions.choice_c: Complete initially assigned turn within 4 DME of Midway and maintain 3,000 feet or lower, if assigned. Then 10 minutes after departure, climb to FL 190, direct to GlJ, and then flight plan route.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: In the event of a two-way communication failure, ATC service will be provided on the basis that the pilot is operating in accordance with FAR 91.185. If the failure occurs after takeoff on RWY 13L at Chicago Midway, while in IMC, the pilot should follow the departure route description for the Midway Four Departure. The flight plan in Fig. 94 indicates the airplane is RNAV equipped (/R); thus it has DME. The pilot should complete the initially assigned turn within 4 DME of Midway and maintain 3,000 ft. or an assigned lower altitude. Then 10 min. after departure, (s)he should climb to FL 190, direct to GIJ VORTAC, and follow the flight plan route. Answer (A) is incorrect. To return and land immediately at Chicago Midway would mean that the pilot has also declared an emergency along with the loss of communications. A loss of communications is not an emergency in itself. Answer (B) is incorrect. A pilot would complete the initially assigned turn south of DPA R-096 if the airplane was not DME equipped. N6OJB is ie ae a as indicated in the flight plan with the equipment suffix of “/R.”

questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 97 on page 475.) How can the FAF on the RNAV RWY 32 approach at BUF be identified?
questions.choice_a: The RNAV receiver will indicate 175.1° and
questions.choice_b: The RNAV receiver will indicate a change from TO a FROM and 0 deflection of the course needle.
questions.choice_c: Two flashes/second on the OM beacon light.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: The pilot will input the waypoint location of the FAF by use of degree-distance fix based on navigational aids or by latitude/longitude coordinates. The waypoint crossing is noted similar to that of crossing a VORTAC station. The RNAV receiver will indicate a change from TO to FROM, and there should be no deflection of the course needle. Answer (A) is incorrect. The waypoint is defined to the RNAV computer as 175.1° and 2.5 DME from BUF VORTAC. Answer (C) is incorrect. The OM is part of a precision approach and is a radio transmitter. An RNAV approach is a nonprecision approach, and the FAF on the RNAV RWY 32 approach to Buffalo is a waypoint.

questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 97 on page 475.) Which of the following will define the position of the RNAV MAP for Greater Buffalo Intl.?
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: The RNAV coordinates (degree-distance fix) for the RNAV RWY 32 MAP at Greater Buffalo Intl. are 116.4 BUF 286.9°, 3.5 NM. Answer (B) is incorrect. The coordinates 42°56.44' N - 78°38.48' W are close to the coordinates of the BUF VORTAC, which is 42°55.74' N - 78°38.78' W. Answer (C) is incorrect. The coordinates 42°56.26' N - 78°43.57' W are the coordinates of Greater Buffalo Intl. itself, not the MAP.

questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 97 on page 475.) In the profile view of the RNAV or GPS RWY 32 approach to Buffalo Intl, between CYUGA and the MAP, the following appears: 42.91°. What is it?
questions.choice_a: The required pitch attitude change at CYUGA, to ensure arriving at 1,220 feet and 1.5 miles at the same time.
questions.choice_b: It indicates that 2.91° below level is recommended on the attitude indicator.
questions.choice_c: The Final Approach Angle for Vertical Path Computers.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: The symbol 42.91° on the profile view of the IAP chart indicates the final approach angle for RNAV vertical path computers, which is 2.91° for the RNAV or GPS RWY 32 IAP. Answer (A) is incorrect. The symbol 42.91° indicates the final approach angle for vertical path computers, not the required pitch attitude change. Answer (B) is incorrect. The symbol 4.2.91° indicates the final approach angle for vertical path computers, not the pitch down attitude on an attitude indicator.

questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 97 on page 475.) What is the procedure for initiating the missed approach on the RNAV RWY 32 approach at BUF? A.. Climbing left turn, select GANIS Waypoint and establish a direct course, climbing to 2,800 feet.
questions.choice_b: Select and maintain R-302 of BUF VORTAC climbing to 2,800 feet.
questions.choice_c: Establish and maintain R-286.9 of BUF VORTAC climbing to 2,800 feet.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: The missed approach procedure is described in the profile section of the RNAV RWY 32 approach. At the MAP, the pilot should begin a climbing left turn, select GANIS waypoint, and establish a direct course, while climbing to 2,800 feet. Answer (B) is incorrect. The missed approach procedure is a climbing left turn to 2,800 ft. direct to GANIS waypoint, not BUF VORTAC. GANIS waypoint is located on the R-302 and 12.1 DME from BUF VORTAC. Answer (C) is incorrect. The missed approach procedure is a climbing left turn to 2,800 ft. direct to GANIS waypoint, not a track along R-286.9 of BUF VORTAC. The MAP is located on R-286.9 and 3.5 DME from BUF VORTAC.

questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 97A on page 476, Figure 97B on page 477, and Figure 97C on page 477.) N60JB desired to list ROC as an alternate for BUF. The active RWY at ROC was expected to be RWY 28. What weather forecast was required at Greater Rochester Intl, for N60JB to list it as an alternate?
questions.choice_c: Nonprecision approach 800-2 1/4, precision approach 600-2.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Fig. 97C lists the alternate minimums for Rochester, NY (ROC). For the ILS RWY 28 (precision approach), the alternate minimums for Cat. A and B aircraft is 800-2. Standard alternate minimums of 800-2 for nonprecision approach can be used since there is no listing for the LOC RWY 28 at ROC. Thus, to list ROC as an alternate, the weather forecast must be 800-2 for both precision and nonprecision approaches. Answer (B) is incorrect. While 600-2 are the standard alternate minimums for a precision approach, ROC has nonstandard alternate minimums for the ILS RWY 28, which is 800-2 for Cat. A and B aircraft. Answer (C) is incorrect. While 600-2 are the standard alternate minimums for a precision approach, ROC has nonstandard alternate minimums for the ILS RWY 28, which is 800-2 for Cat. A and B aircraft. Since LOC RWY 28 is not listed, the standard alternate minimums of 800-2, not 800-2 1/4, can be used.
