questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 98 on page 483, Figure 99 on page 484, Figure 100 on page 485, and Figure 102 on page 487.) Determine the TAS required to arrive at CUGAR, 31 minutes after level-off.
questions.choice_c: 258 knots.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: To determine the required TAS to arrive at CUGAR INT 31 min. after level-off, you need to determine the required groundspeed. The distance is 114 NM (L/O to IAH R-305) + 13 NM (turn-off to CUGAR) = 127 NM. Average groundspeed required is 246 kt. [127 + (31/60)]. Then compute the TAS on the wind side of your computer: 1. Set the magnetic wind direction of 223° under the true index and place a pencil mark at 42 kt. (wind speed) up from the grommet. 2. Place MC of 154° (V369) under true index. (Because the MC of the last 13 NM of this leg differs, we will check our answer at the end using the MC of each leg.) Slide the grid so the groundspeed of 246 kt. is under the grommet. Read the TAS of 264 kt. under the pencil mark. Using a TAS of 264 kt., the groundspeed for the first leg is 245 kt., and the second leg is 266 kt. Time for the first leg is 28 min., and for the second leg is 3 min., for a total of 31 min. cor alg MC Answer (A) is incorrect. This would make the arrival at CUGAR approximately 30 min., not 31 min., after level-off. Answer (C) is incorrect. This would make the arrival at CUGAR 32 min., not 31 min., after level-off.

questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 99 on page 484 and Figure 101 on page 486.) Which frequency should be selected to check airport conditions and weather prior to departure at DFW Intl?
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: The Automatic Terminal Information Service (ATIS) provides airport noncontrol and weather information. On Fig. 99, the ATIS information is depicted with the Dallas- Ft. Worth airport information to the left center of the chart. The Chart Supplement on Fig. 101 for Dallas-Ft. Worth lists the ATIS frequencies under Communications. The ATIS frequency for departing airplanes is 135.5 MHz. Answer (A) is incorrect. This is the listed ATIS frequency for arriving, not departing, airplanes. Answer (B) is incorrect. This is also a listed ATIS frequency for arriving, not departing, airplanes.

questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 99 on page 484, Figure 100 on page 485, and Figure 101 on page 486.) The frequency change from departure control to ARTCC after departing DFW Intl for |AH is
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: The departure control frequency is listed in the Chart Supplement on Fig. 101 under Communications. The regional departure control frequency for a flight from DFW Intl. south to IAH is 127.75. The ARTCC frequency is found on Fig. 100 on the upper right-hand side. It is in a jagged-edged box that identifies that it is an ARTCC remote site with the discrete VHF and UHF frequencies. This is Ft. Worth Scurry on 127.95 MHz. Answer (A) is incorrect. The ATIS frequency for departing airplanes is 135.5, and 126.0 MHz is the ARTCC discrete frequency for northwest of DFW. Answer (B) is incorrect. The departure control frequency for eastbound traffic from DFW is 118.55 MHz.

questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 99 on page 484.) Where is the VOR changeover point on V369 between DFW Intl and TNV?
questions.choice_c: TORNN Int.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: The changeover point (COP) is located midway between the navigation facilities for straight route segments, or at the intersection of radials or courses forming a dogleg. When the COP is not located at the midway point, the chart will depict the COP location and give the mileage to the radio aids. The DFW VORTAC is 162 NM from the TNV VORTAC; thus, the COP is halfway at 81 NM. Answer (A) is incorrect. The Ft. Worth/Houston ARTCC boundary would indicate a radio communication frequency change, not a navigational frequency change. Answer (C) is incorrect. TORNN intersection is 85 NM from DFW VORTAC. Even though a DME distance fix is shown for TORNN, there is no special VOR changeover indicator that designates TORNN as a COP; thus, the midway point (81 NM) should be used.

questions.subsection: 2.7 min., for a total of 28.3 min.
questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 100 on page 485 and Figure 101 on page 486.) What is the magnetic variation at both DFW Intl and IAH?
questions.choice_c: O8W.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: The magnetic variation is found at the end of the VORTAC information line for both DFW Intl. and IAH. Both locations indicate “O8E,” which is a magnetic variation of 08°E. Answer (B) is incorrect. Zero degrees magnetic variation is the Agonic line, the line where the variation between magnetic and true north is zero, which is well east of Texas. Answer (C) is incorrect. The magnetic variation is 08°E (not west).

questions.subsection: 2.7 min., for a total of 28.3 min.
questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 100 on page 485 and Figure 102 on page 487.) How should the pilot identify the position to leave V369 for the Cugar Four Arrival?
questions.choice_c: 141 DME miles from DFW.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: The BILEE transition indicates that from BILEE INT the pilot should maintain the R-334 of TNV VORTAC, then intercept and track inbound on the R-305 of IAH VORTAC to CUGAR intersection for the Cugar Four Arrival. Answer (B) is incorrect. There is no DME fix for 21 NM from TNV VORTAC on either the STAR or the IFR En Route Chart. Answer (C) is incorrect. A pilot should have changed over to TNV VORTAC, and there is no 141 DME fix from DFW on V369.

questions.subsection: 2.7 min., for a total of 28.3 min.
questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 258 on page 488.) What is the procedure for the missed approach on the RNAV (GPS) RWY 32 approach at BUF?
questions.choice_a: Climbing straight ahead to 3,000 feet to TOMDE while selecting the MAP mode, left turn to 239° track to SUSKE and hold.
questions.choice_b: Climb to 3,500 feet and hold at SUSKE on the 239° track from TOMDE.
questions.choice_c: Select MAP mode, climb to 3,000 feet and hold using left turns at SUSKE on the 239° track from TOMDE.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: The GPS will not automatically cycle to the missed approach procedure. Instead, it has to be manually sequenced. While climbing straight ahead to 3,000 feet to TOMDE, manually select the MAP mode and once arriving at TOMDE track R-239 to SUSKE and hold. Answer (B) is incorrect. The proper altitude is 3,000 feet, not 3,500 feet, as indicated in the missed approach instructions. Answer (C) is incorrect. The holding pattern at SUSKE is right turns, not left turns, as indicated by the arrow heads on the MAP hold position.

questions.subsection: 2.7 min., for a total of 28.3 min.
questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 258 on page 488.) How can the GPS LNAV/MDA MAP for the RNAV (GPS) RWY 32 approach at Buffalo Niagara Intl (BUF) be identified?
questions.choice_a: GPS annunciator will switch to FROM.
questions.choice_c: At 1.4 NM to Rwy 32.
questions.explanation: Wey eed ou Ss The GPS receiver annunciator will switch to Answer (B) is incorrect. GPS receivers will not automatically sequence to a MAP during an approach. Answer (C) is incorrect. The point at 1.4 NM is a visual descent point (VDP) from the end of the runway.

questions.subsection: 2.7 min., for a total of 28.3 min.
questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 98 on page 483, Figure 99 on page 484, Figure 100 on page 485, and Figure 102 on page 487.) Determine the TAS required to arrive at CUGAR, 29 minutes after level-off.
questions.choice_c: 295 knots.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: To determine the required TAS to arrive at CUGAR INT 29 min. after level-off, you need to determine the required groundspeed. The distance is 114 NM (L/O to turn-off) + 13 NM (turn-off to CUGAR) = 127 NM. Average groundspeed required is 263 kt. [127 + (29/60)]. Then compute the TAS on the wind side of your computer: 1. Set the magnetic wind direction of 223° under the true index and place a pencil mark at 42 kt. (wind speed) up from the grommet. 2. Place MC of 154° (V369) under true index. (Because the MC of the last 13 NM of this leg differs, we will check our answer at the end using the MC of each leg.) Slide the grid so the groundspeed of 263 kt. is under the grommet. Read the TAS of 281 kt. under the pencil mark. Using a TAS of 285 kt., the groundspeed for the first leg is 267 kt., and the second leg is 287 kt. Time for the first leg is 25.6‘min., and for the second leg is 2.7 min., for a total of 28.3 min. De Ole oe Answer (B) is incorrect. A TAS of 290 kt. would complete the trip from level-off to CUGAR in approximately 28 min., not 29 min. Answer (C) is incorrect. A TAS of 291 kt. would complete ue trip from level-off to CUGAR in approximately 27 min., not min.

questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 103 on page 495, Figure 104 on page 496, Figure 105 on page 497, Figure 106 on page 498, and Figure 107 on page 499.) Determine the ETE for the flight from Tucson Intl to Los Angeles Intl.
questions.choice_c: 2 hours 19 minutes.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: To determine the estimated time en route, you must complete the flight log in Fig. 103. Convert the true wind direction to magnetic. On Fig. 104, the Chart Supplement excerpt for Tucson Intl. indicates a magnetic variation of 12°E (listed under Radio Aids to Navigation). On Fig. 107 for Los Angeles, the magnetic variation is listed as 15°E. Easterly variation is subtracted from true headings. Ground- Distance MC speed _ Time/Min. Level-off 73G X Xx :25G GBN 31 289 189 10 Int. J104 76 298 192 24 PKE 47 POU TE 188 aS TNP 54 256 188 ST I7/ Start descent 81 248 189 :26 Downe 3 LAX 52G Xx x :18G G = Given Answer (A) is incorrect. Two hr. 10 min. would require a TAS of approximately 240 kt., not 23 kt. Answer (C) is incorrect. Two hr. 19 min. would require a TAS of approximately 225 Kt., not 233 kt.

questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 348 on page 507.) What effect on the takeoff run can be expected on RWY 11R at Tucson Intl?
questions.choice_a: Takeoff length shortened to 6,986 feet by displaced threshold.
questions.choice_b: Takeoff run shortened by 0.7 percent runway slope to the SE.
questions.choice_c: Takeoff run will be lengthened by the
questions.explanation: The first line of runway information provides information common to the entire runway. At the end of RWY 11R-29L on Fig. 348, the information states, “0.7% up.” That indicates there is a 0.7% upslope grade on the runway, which will increase the distance required for the takeoff run. Answer (A) is incorrect. A displaced threshold will shorten the usable runway for landing, not for takeoff. Answer (B) is incorrect. The takeoff run is lengthened, not shortened, due to the 0.7% upslope.

questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 103 on page 495, Figure 104 on page 496, Figure 105 on page 497, and Figure 106 on page 498.) Estimate the total fuel required to be on the aircraft, prior to taxi at Tucson Intl. (Use 13°E for problem magnetic variation.)
questions.choice_c: 2,447 pounds.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: To determine the total fuel required from Tucson Intl. to Los Angeles Intl., you must complete the fuel portion of the flight log on Fig. 103. Use 676 PPH total fuel flow from level-off to start of descent. Use 726 PPH total fuel flow for reserve and alternate requirements. Time Fuel (Ib.) Level-off :25G 350G GBN 10 fal Int. J104 :24 270 PKE edS 169 TNP SA, 192 Start descent :26 293 Descent and approach :18G 170G BUR :19G 230 Reserve 145 545 Total 2,332 G = Given Answer (A) is incorrect. This includes the fuel required for a missed approach, not the fuel required to fly to the alternate airport (BUR). Answer (C) is incorrect. This includes the fuel required for a missed approach. While this should be included as a practical matter, it is not required by the FARs.

questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 103 on page 495 and Figure 104 on page 496.) If communications are lost soon after takeoff on RWY 11R at Tucson Intl, what altitude restrictions apply in IMC conditions?
questions.choice_a: Fly assigned heading for vectors to intercept appropriate transition, maintain 17,000 feet to GBN, then climb to assigned altitude.
questions.choice_b: Fly assigned heading for vectors to intercept the Gila Bend transition; climb to 17,000 feet or lower assigned altitude; climb to FL 220, 10 minutes after departure.
questions.choice_c: Fly assigned heading for vectors to intercept the Gila Bend transition; climb to 17,000 feet; 10 minutes after departure, climb to FL 220.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: If communications are lost soon after takeoff on RWY 11R at TUS in IMC conditions, you must fly the Tucson Three Departure as described in Fig. 104. Fly the assigned heading for vectors to intercept the Gila Bend transition. Climb to maintain 17,000 ft. or lower assigned altitude. Next, climb to FL 220, 10 min. after departure. Answer (A) is incorrect. You must maintain 17,000 ft. or an assigned lower altitude, and you can climb to FL 220, 10 min. after departure, not only after you reach GBN VORTAC. Answer (C) is incorrect. You must climb to maintain 17,000 ft. or an assigned lower altitude.

questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 103 on page 495 and Figure 361 on page 508.) What are the takeoff minimums for RWY 11R at Tucson Intl that apply to N91JB with a planned climb rate of 1,000 feet per minute at 140 knots?
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: A climbing rate at 140 kt. groundspeed (GS), the aircraft would cover 2.33 NM per minute (140 kt. + 60 = 2.33 NM per minute). Climbing 1,000 feet each minute would be 429 feet per NM (1,000 + 2.33 = 429.2 feet per NM). In Fig. 361, you will see two sets of takeoff minimums listed. Both allow use of standard departure weather minimums if you have a high enough climb gradient. The aircraft would require 400 feet per NM to use the standard departure minimums. The other takeoff minimum listed is climbing at 429 feet per NM. The standard minimums can be used for all of the runways. The standard minimums for aircraft is 1 SM, other than helicopters, having two engines or less. The BE-1900 has two engines. Answer (B) is incorrect. The FARs indicate the standard minimums of 1 SM for aircraft, other than helicopters, having two engines or less. Answer (C) is incorrect. The FARs indicate the standard minimums of 1 SM for aircraft, other than helicopters, having two engines or less.

questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 104 on page 496.) Determine the DEP CON frequency for the TUS3.GBN SID after takeoff from RWY 11R at Tucson Intl.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: In the TUS3.GBN SID, the pilot would be vectored to track outbound on the R-280 from TUS. The communication section of the Chart Supplement indicates that DEP CON frequency after takeoff from RWY 11R and heading 280° will be 125.1 MHz. __ Answer (B) is incorrect. This is for departures from RWY 11 with a departure heading between 286° to 089°. Answer (C) is incorrect. This is for departures from RWY 29 with a departure heading between 066° to 274°.

questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 103 on page 495.) What CAS should be used to maintain the fixed TAS at the proposed altitude?
questions.choice_c: 172 knots.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: The outside air temperature is on Fig. 103 below the wind speed and direction on the flight log. The temperature is indicated as ISA —3, which is International Standard Atmosphere temperature —3°C. The ISA temperature for FL 220 is calculated by subtracting the standard lapse rate (2°C/1,000 ft.) from standard sea-level temperature of 15°C. The OAT is —32°C [15 — (22 x 2) —3]. In the center of the computer side of the flight computer, on the right side, put the air temperature of —32°C over the pressure altitude of 22,000 ft. (given in block 7 of the flight plan). On the outer scale, find TAS of 233 kt. (from block 4), which is over calibrated airspeed (CAS) on the inner scale of 167 KCAS. Answer (A) is incorrect. This will maintain the fixed TAS if the OAT is —3°C, not -32°C. Answer (C) is incorrect. This will maintain the fixed TAS if the OAT is -50°C, not —32°C.

questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 104 on page 496 and Legend 10 on page 509.) Using an average groundspeed of 140 knots, what minimum indicated rate of climb must be maintained to meet the required climb rate (feet per NM) to 9,000 as specified on the SID?
questions.choice_c: 583 ft/min.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: The SID in Fig. 104 has a note that indicates, “Gila Bend transition requires a minimum climb of 250 ft. per NM to 9,000 ft.” Refer to the Rate-of-Climb Table in Legend 10 to determine the rate-of-climb (in ft./min.) in order to climb at 250 ft. per NM. In the left-hand column, move down to 250 ft. per NM and then move right to the 140 kt. groundspeed column to determine a rate-of-climb of 583 ft./min. Answer (A) is incorrect. A climb rate of 349 ft./min. would be required if the average groundspeed were approximately 84 kt. Answer (B) is incorrect. A climb rate of 560 ft./min. would be required if the average groundspeed were approximately 135 kt.

questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 104 on page 496.) How can the pilot receive the latest NOTAM’s for the TUS-LAX flight?
questions.choice_c: Request ADCUS on any FSS or Tower frequency.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Pilots can receive the latest NOTAMs for their flight by contacting the nearest FSS in person, telephoning, or contacting on radio. For the latest information from TUS-LAX, the pilot should contact FSS on 122.2 MHz, as listed in the Chart Supplement on Fig. 104. Answer (A) is incorrect. ATIS provides noncontrol airport traffic and weather information, not NOTAMs. Answer (C) is incorrect. ADCUS means to “advise customs” of an international flight and is handled by an FSS, not by a tower.

questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 104 on page 496.) What distance is available for takeoff on RWY 11R at Tucson Intl?
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Runway information is shown on two lines. Information common to the entire runway is shown on the first line while information concerning the runway ends is shown on the second or following line. On Fig. 104, the runway length of RWY 11R-29L is 9,129 ft., which is the distance available for takeoff on RWY 11R. Answer (A) is incorrect. This is the length of RWY 03-21. Answer (C) is incorrect. This is the length of RWY 11L, not RWY 11R.

questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 106 on page 498 and Figure 107 on page 499.) Which approach control frequency is indicated for the TNP.DOWNES3 Arrival with LAX as the destination?
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: The DOWNE3 Arrival on Fig. 106 indicates in the northwest corner of the chart the approach control and ATIS frequencies for the arrival. The approach control frequency is 124.5 MHz. Answer (A) is incorrect. This frequency is used for arrivals from 045° to 089°, which are not on the DOWNES Arrival. Answer (B) is incorrect. This frequency is used for arrivals from 090° to 224°, which are not on the DOWNES Arrival.

questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 106 on page 498 and Figure 107 on page 499.) At what point does the flight enter the final approach phase of the ILS RWY 25L at LAX?
questions.choice_c: Intercept of glide slope.
questions.explanation: The FAA question refers you to Fig. 106, which is the STAR. To answer this question, you should refer to the IAP chart, Fig. 107. The final approach phase for a precision approach begins with the intercept of the glide slope, at which time a descent may begin on the glide slope to the decision height. Thus, fora precision approach, the final approach fix is the intercept of the glide slope. Answer (A) is incorrect. FUELR INT is an initial approach fix (IAF) and does not mark the point at which the flight enters the final approach phase. Answer (B) is incorrect. HUNDA INT is a fix at which the pilot should intercept the glide slope, but the intersection itself does not mark the point at which the airplane enters the final approach phase.

questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 106 on page 498 and Figure 107 on page 499.) What is the DH for the ILS RWY 25L at LAX if the pilot has completed the initial Category ll certification within the preceding 6 months, but has flown no CAT II approaches?
questions.choice_c: 301 feet.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: The FAA question refers you to Fig. 106, which is the STAR. To answer this question, you should refer to the IAP chart, Fig. 107. Upon initial Category II certification, the authorization contains a limitation for Category || operations of 1,600 ft. RVR and a 150-ft. DH. The limitation is removed when the pilot shows that since the beginning of the sixth month (s)he has made three Category II ILS approaches with a 150-ft. DH. The first line of the minima section in Fig. 107 indicates the 150-ft. DH. Thus, the DH for the ILS RWY 25L at LAX is 251 ft. Answer (A) is incorrect. A DH of 201 ft. is available once the initial Category II limitation is removed. Answer (C) is incorrect. A DH of 150 ft., not 200 ft., is the limitation placed upon an original issue for Category II operations.

questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 106 on page 498 and Figure 107 on page 499.) The radio altimeter indication for the DH at the inner marker on the ILS RWY 25L approach at LAX is
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: The FAA question refers you to Fig. 106, which is the STAR. To answer this question, you should refer to the IAP chart, Fig. 107. The radio altimeter indication for the DH at the inner marker on the ILS RWY 25L approach is indicated by “RA” followed by the appropriate indication within parentheses in the minima section. In this approach, the RA is 111 ft. Answer (A) is incorrect. The touchdown zone elevation (TDZE) is 101 ft. Answer (C) is incorrect. The decision height, expressed in MSL, is 201 ft.

questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 106 on page 498 and Figure 107 on page 499.) If the glide slope indication is lost upon passing HUNDA INT on the ILS RWY 25L approach at LAX, what action should the pilot take?
questions.choice_a: Continue the approach as an LOC, and add 100 feet to the DH.
questions.choice_b: Immediately start the missed approach direct to INISH INT.
questions.choice_c: Continue to the MAP, and execute the missed approach as indicated.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: The FAA question refers you to Fig. 106, which is the STAR. To answer this question, you should refer to the IAP chart, Fig. 107. Note that there are no LOC minimums for this approach. Thus, if the glide slope indication is lost on the ILS RWY 25L approach at LAX, the pilot should continue to the MAP at or above the DH and then execute the missed approach as indicated. Answer (A) is incorrect. There are no LOC minimums for this approach. A pilot must have a glide slope indication or execute the missed approach procedure. Answer (B) is incorrect. Protected obstacle clearance areas for missed approaches are made on the assumption that the missed approach procedure is started at the MAP. No consideration is made for an abnormally early turn, unless otherwise directed by ATC.

questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 106 on page 498 and Figure 107 on page 499.) How can DOWNE INT be identified?
questions.choice_c: LAX R-249 and SLI R-327.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Refer to Fig: 106 or 107. DOWNE intersection is a DME fix which is 15 DME from I-LAX. The DME is installed with the ILS and established fixes on the localizer course. ILS is identified by three letters preceded by the letter “I.” ___ Answer (B) is incorrect. LAX 15 DME defines a circle, not an intersection. Answer (C) is incorrect. LAX R-249 and SLI R-327 do not intersect. DOWNE INT can be identified by LAX R-068 and SLI R-327.

questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 107 on page 499.) How should the IFR flight plan be closed upon landing at LAX?
questions.choice_c: LAX tower will close it automatically.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: When operating on an IFR flight plan to an airport with a functioning control tower, the flight plan is automatically closed upon landing. In this case, LAX tower will close the IFR flight plan automatically. Answer (A) is incorrect. An IFR flight plan is closed with a flight service station only if there is no operating tower. Answer (B) is incorrect. An IFR flight plan is closed with an FSS only if there is no operating tower.

questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 106 on page 498 and Figure 107 on page 499.) What approach lights are available for the ILS RWY 25L approach at LAX?
questions.choice_c: HIRL and TDZ/CL.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: The FAA question refers you to Fig. 106, which is the STAR. To answer this question, you should refer to the Chart Supplement excerpt, Fig. 107. Refer to the Chart Supplement excerpt and locate the runway data for RWY 07R-25L. The first line has information common to the runway. The second line contains specific information about the runway ends. RWY 25L indicates “ALSF2,” which translates to high-intensity approach lighting system with sequenced flashing lights, Category II configuration. Answer (B) is incorrect. RWY 25R has an out-of-service (see Airport Remarks) MALSR and a displaced (not displayed) threshold. Answer (C) is incorrect. High-intensity runway lights (HIRL), touchdown zone lights (TDZ), and centerline lights (CL) are runway, not approach, lighting systems.

questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 255A on page 501, Figure 255B on page 502, Figure 256 on page 503, Figure 257 on page 504, and Figure 257A on page 505.) If the glide slope indication is lost upon passing LIMMA INT on the ILS RWY 25L approach at LAX, what action should the pilot take?
questions.choice_a: Continue to the MAP, and execute the missed approach as indicated.
questions.choice_b: Continue the approach as an LOC, and add 100 feet to the DH.
questions.choice_c: Immediately start the missed approach left turn to CATLY INT.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: When the glide slope indication is lost during the ILS approach, the pilot can continue on the LOC approach. The pilot will execute the missed approach at the missed approach point for the LOC approach. Answer (B) is incorrect. The pilot will continue on the LOC approach, but the missed approach point is not determined by adding 100 feet to the DH. Answer (C) is incorrect. The pilot will not begin the missed approach left turn to CATLY INT until the MAP is reached and the climb to 800 feet is completed.

questions.question_text: (Refer to Legend 43 on page 510 and Legend 43A on page 511.) The filed flight plan, for N91JB, block 13, indicates that BUR is the alternate, for LAX. The RWYs at LAX are closed and expected to remain closed for 2 hours when N91JB arrives. N91JB requests 4,000 feet, Tower Enroute Control (TEC) with radar vectors to BUR. If radar vectors are not available, what route can be expected from LAX to BUR?
questions.choice_c: Direct SMO, UR, SILEX BUR.
questions.explanation: Legend 43A is the listing of city (airport) pairs for TEC. Locate Coast TRACON (center left of legend) and move down to the first line of airports where you will see BUR VNY WHFP listed; the route is listed in the third column. If radar vectors are not available, the expected TEC route from LAX to BUR is LAX LAX316 SILEX. Answer (A) is incorrect. The TEC route from LAX to BUR is LAX LAX316 SILEX, not direct SMO VNY BUR. Answer (C) is incorrect. The TEC route from LAX to BUR is LAX LAX316 SILEX, not SMO UR.

questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 177A on page 500.) Flight 61 is a 14 CFI part 121 carrier passenger turbojet aircraft planning to depart Lewiston-Nez County airport on RWY 29 using the POTOR TWO departure. There are no abnormal company policies or procedures. With 180 knots groundspeed, what takeoff conditions are required to depart?
questions.choice_a: 300 feet per minute climb with both engines operative.
questions.choice_b: 750 feet per minute climb with 1 engine operative.
questions.choice_c: 900 feet per minute climb with 1 engine operative.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Refer to the note in Fig. 177A for the POTOR TWO departure. For RWY 26 and 29, the required climb gradient is 300 feet per NM to 4,700 feet. In parentheses, you will see the equivalent climb rates in feet per minute (fpm) for speeds of 150 kts. (750 fpm) and 200 kts. (1,000 fpm). We are climbing at 180 kts. groundspeed and the fpm climb for that ground speed is not given, but it can be calculated. (180 kts. + 60) = 3 miles covered per minute (You have to climb at least 300 feet for every NM); 3 NM per minute x 300 feet per NM = 900 feet per minute. Answer (A) is incorrect. The climb must be a minimum of 300 feet per NM, not 300 feet per minute. Answer (B) is incorrect. The takeoff condition of 750 fpm is at 150 kts., not 180 knots.

questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 257B on page 506.) What is the DH for the ILS RWY 24R CAT II at LAX if the pilot has completed initial CAT II certification within the preceding 6 months, but has flown no CAT II approaches?
questions.choice_c: RA 220/315 feet.
questions.explanation: A pilot is restricted to a 150 foot decision height until the CAT II ILS limitation is removed. Refer to Fig. 257B. The published minimums are an Radar Altimeter height (RA) of 115 feet which corresponds to a decision height (DH) of 100 feet, and a decision altitude (DA) of 220 feet. A 100 feet DH is too low to meet the CAT II ILS limitation of 150 feet. Fifty additional feet must be added requiring a new minimums that would be RA 165 and a DA of 270 feet. Answer (A) is incorrect. RA 115 / 220 feet are the minimums for a pilot without a CAT II ILS limitation. Answer (C) is incorrect. This answer is higher than the limitations set forth in the regulations for this CAT II ILS approach.

questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 103 on page 495, Legend 43 on page 510, and Legend 43A on page 511.) The RWYs at LAX are closed and expected to remain closed for 2 hours when N91JB arrives. N91JB requests 4,000 feet, Tower Enroute Control (TEC) with radar vectors to BUR. What altitude can N91JB expect based upon the type aircraft?
questions.choice_c: 6,000 feet.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: According to the flight plan, the flight is from TUC to LAX and includes the Downe Three Arrival. The alternate airport is BUR via TEC (Tower Enroute Control). TEC does not include all city pairs; rather, it is intended to connect geographic areas by TEC. The closest pairing provided is the Coast Tracon to Burbank (BUR). The Downe Three Arrival terminates with vectors to final landing East; accordingly, the CSTJ2 Route ID should be filed (LAXE). N91JB is a turboprop with a cruise speed greater than 190 knots; therefore, the altitude for this route is 5,000 feet. Answer (A) is incorrect. This is the altitude for a non-jet aircraft. Answer (C) is incorrect. This altitude is not for an aircraft at LAX VOR that is expecting vectors for Runways 6 or 7.

questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 168 on page 516, Figure 169 on
questions.choice_a: 2 hours 15 minutes. which is PMM R-261 at 47 NM.
questions.choice_b: 2 hours 18 minutes. Our flight log below has been expanded to provide more detail. To convert the wind direction from true to magnetic, we
questions.choice_c: 2 hours 21 minutes. used 1°W variation to PMM; 3°W variation from PMM to YXU, and 8°W variation from YXU to BUF.
questions.explanation: page 517, Figure 169A on page 518, Figure 171 on DISCUSSION: To determine the ETE from Chicago Pal- page 452, and Figure 172 on page 521.) What is the Waukee (PWk) to Greater Buffalo International (BUF), you must ETE for PTZ 70 from Chicago Pal-Waukee Airport to complete the flight log in Fig. 168. In the remarks box of the flight Greater Buffalo Intl Airport? plan, the magnetic variation at PWK (1°W), FNT (3°W), and BUF (8°W) are given. This information is used to convert the true wind direction to magnetic direction. Also given is the level-off point,

questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 168 on page 516, Figure 169 on page 517, Figure 169A on page 518, Figure 171 on page 452, and Figure 172 on page 521.) What is the computed fuel usage for PTZ 70 from start of taxi at hee Pal-Waukee to landing at Greater Buffalo ntl?
questions.choice_c: 2,550 pounds.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: To determine the fuel usage from start of taxi at Chicago Pal-Waukee to landing at Greater Buffalo, you must complete the fuel portion of the flight log in Fig. 168. The 410 Ib. of fuel given in the log to level-off includes taxi fuel. Use 676 PPH from level-off to the start of the descent. Time Fuel (Ib.) Level-off 0:24:00G 410.0G PMM 0:13:21 150.4 PMM/FNT COP 0:16:06 181.4 FNT 0:15:47 177.8 FNT/YXU COP 0:15:45 ALON. XU 0:15:20 172.8 BUF R-282/40 0:18:55 213.1 BUF 0:19:00G 163.0G 1,646.0 G = Given Answer (B) is incorrect. This weight of fuel includes the fuel required to fly from BUF to SYR. Answer (C) is incorrect. This weight includes the fuel required to fly from BUF to SYR and the 45 min. of fuel reserve requirement.

questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 168 on page 516, Figure 169 on page 517, and Figure 169A on page 518.) What action should be taken by the pilot if communications are lost after departure from RWY 16 at PWK if VMC?
questions.choice_a: Continue the flight under VMC and land as soon as practicable.
questions.choice_b: Climb to 3,000 feet; after 3 minutes, turn direct to PMM and climb to FL 190.
questions.choice_c: Start right turn within 1 mile of the departure end of RWY, remain east of ORD VOR/DME R-345, and maintain 3,000 feet; 3 minutes after departure, turn direct to PMM, and climb to FL 190.
questions.explanation: If two-way radio communication failure occurs in VMC, you should continue the flight under VFR and land as soon as practicable. Answer (B) is incorrect. If radio communication failure occurs while you are in, or when you encounter, VMC, you must continue the flight under VFR and land as soon as practicable. Answer (C) is incorrect. While you should follow the SID instructions to ensure separation from approaches to RWY 14R at O’Hare, you must continue the flight under VFR. You should follow the routing and altitude restrictions if you lose communications in IMC, not VMC.

questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 168 on page 516, Figure 171 on page 452, Figure 172 on page 521, and Figure 173 on page 523.) What TAS should PTZ 70 maintain to arrive at FNT 30 minutes after passing PMM?
questions.choice_c: 260 knots.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: To determine the required TAS to arrive at FNT 30 min. after passing PMM, you need to determine the required groundspeed. The distance is 109 NM; thus the groundspeed required is 218 kt. (109 NM + 0.5 hr.). Next, compute the TAS on the wind side of your computer: 1. Set the magnetic wind direction of 023° under the true index, and place a pencil mark at 61 kt. (windspeed) up from the grommet. 2. Place the average MC of 075° (J547) under the true index. 3. Slide the grid so the groundspeed of 218 kt. is under the grommet. 4. Read the TAS of 260 kt. under the pencil mark. Answer (A) is incorrect. A TAS of 255 kt. would complete the trip from PMM to FNT in approximately 31 min., not 30 min. Answer (B) is incorrect. A TAS of 265 kt. would complete the trip from PMM to FNT in approximately 29 min., not 30 min.

questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 169A on page 518.) The PIC of PTZ 70 will use 25° of bank during the turn after departing RWY 16 at PWK. What is the maximum TAS that the aircraft may maintain during the turn and remain east of the ORD VOR/DME R-345 under a no wind condition?
questions.choice_c: 164 knots.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: The table at the top of the Pal-Waukee Two Departure (Fig. 169A) provides the maximum TAS for a given bank angle required during the turn after takeoff to remain east of the ORD VOR/DME R-345 under a no wind condition. Under a bank angle of 25° is the maximum TAS of 162 kt. Answer (A) is incorrect. This would be the maximum TAS if you were to use a bank angle of slightly less than 25°, not 25°. Answer (C) is incorrect. This would be the maximum TAS if you were to use a bank angle slightly greater than 25°, not 25°.

questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 169A on page 518.) To remain east of the ORD VOR/DME R-345, while flying the PAL-WAUKEE TWO DEPARTURE, requires a turn radius of
questions.choice_c: less than 5,000 feet.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: To remain east of the ORD VOR/DME R-345 while flying the Pal-Waukee Two Departure after a takeoff on RWY 16 requires a turn radius of less than 5,000 ft. This information is found as a note on the left side of the chart, above the departure route description. Answer (A) is incorrect. A turn radius of less than 5,000 ft., not over 5,000 ft., is required by the SID. Answer (B) is incorrect. A turn radius of less than 5,000 ft., not 5,000 ft., is required by the SID.

questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 169A on page 518.) What action should be taken by the PIC of PTZ 70 if the communication radios fail after takeoff from RWY 16 at PWK while in IMC conditions?
questions.choice_a: Climb to 3,000 feet on RWY heading; after 3 minutes, turn direct to PMM and climb to FL 190.
questions.choice_b: Start right turn within 1 mile of the departure end of RWY 16 and remain east of the 345 radial of the ORD VOR/DME while climbing to 3,000 feet; after 3 minutes, turn direct to PMM and climb to FL 190.
questions.choice_c: Set 7600 in Mode 3 of the transponder, turn direct to Northbrook (the IAF), climb to 2,700 feet, and fly the ILS RWY 16 to land at PWK.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: If radio communication failure occurs after takeoff from RWY 16 at PWK while in IMC and you have been cleared for the Pal-Waukee Two Departure, you should fly the SID as described in the narrative. Thus, you should start a right turn within 1 NM of the departure end of RWY 16 to remain east of the ORD VOR/DME R-345 while climbing to 3,000 ft. or assigned altitude. Next, proceed direct to PMM and, 3 min. after departure, climb to FL190. Answer (A) is incorrect. When departing RWY 16, you must make a right turn within 1 NM of the departure end, not maintain runway heading. Answer (C) is incorrect. While you would squawk 7600, your routing clearance is the Pal-Waukee Two Departure, not a direct turn to OBK VORTAC to execute the ILS RWY 16 approach to land at PWK.

questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 172A on page 522.) The airport diagram of Greater Buffalo Intl Airport has a symbol (appears to be a triangle balanced on top of another triangle) located close to the end of RWYs 14 and 32. What do these symbols indicate?
questions.choice_b: That special takeoff and landing minimums apply to RWYs 14 and 32.
questions.choice_c: RWY radar reflectors.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: The symbol that appears to be a triangle balanced on top of another triangle located at the approach end of both runways 14 and 32 in the Greater Buffalo airport diagram indicates runway radar reflectors. Answer (A) is incorrect. Helicopter landing areas are indicated by a circle, square, or triangle that has a black background and either a white “+” or an “H” in the center, not by one triangle on top of another triangle. Answer (B) is incorrect. Nonstandard takeoff minimums and alternate, not landing, Na are depicted on the IAP chart, not the airport diagram chart.

questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 173A on page 524 and Legend 9 on page 526.) During the approach (ILS RWY 10 at SYR) while maintaining an on glide slope indication with a groundspeed of 110 knots, what was the approximate rate of descent for PTZ 70?
questions.choice_c: 690 feet per minute.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Fig. 173A provides the IAP chart for the ILS RWY 10 approach at SYR. In the lower left corner of the profile view is GS 3.00°, or a 3° glide slope. Refer to the Rate-of-Descent Table in Legend 9 to determine the rate of descent to maintain a 3° glide slope with a groundspeed of 110 kt. Since a groundspeed of 110 kt. is not shown on the table, you must interpolate. Find 3° in the angle- of-descent column, and move right to a groundspeed of 105 kt. to determine a rate of descent of 555 fpm. For a groundspeed of 120 kt., the rate of descent is 635 fpm. Interpolate for 110 kt. to determine a rate of descent of approximately 585 fpm. Answer (A) is incorrect. This is the approximate rate of descent with a groundspeed of 110 kt. while maintaining a 2.5°, not 3°, glide slope. Answer (C) is incorrect. This is the approximate rate of descent with a groundspeed of 110 kt. while maintaining a 3.5°, not 3°, glide slope.

questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 169A on page 518.) To remain east of the ORD VOR/DME R-345, while flying the PAL-WAUKEE TWO DEPARTURE, in an aircraft flying at 140 kts TAS in the climb in calm winds, requires a bank angle of
questions.choice_b: about standard rate no matter what the winds are.
questions.choice_c: about 25° or more.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Referring to Fig. 169A, in the upper right hand corner, there is a No Wind Bank Angle chart for 122 KTS and 143 KTS. Interpolating for 140 KTS TAS would be 19.286°. The closest answer would be 20°. It is just as easy to look at this chart and notice that 140 KTS TAS is closer to 143 KTS TAS at 20° than it is to 122 KTS TAS at 15°. Answer (B) is incorrect. This is obviously false since a strong enough wind from the east would blow your aircraft past the R-345 radial even at a 45° bank. Answer (C) is incorrect. The 25° section in the chart is for an airspeed of 162 KTS. This is well over the airspeed of 140 KTS TAS that is given in the question.

questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 171 on page 452.) The facility (Kankakee) that is located 9 miles NE of Chicago Midway or 27 miles SSE of Northbrook (OBK) is a/an
questions.choice_b: Automated Weather Observing System (AWOS/ASOS) with frequency.
questions.choice_c: Flight Service, Remote Communications Outlet.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: The facility labeled Kankakee that is located southeast of OBK VORTAC is an FSS remote communications outlet (RCO). Answer (A) is incorrect. AIRINC transmitter/frequencies are not shown on en route charts. Answer (B) is incorrect. AWOS/ ASOS frequencies are not shown on high-altitude en route charts.

questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 179 on page 531, Figure 180 on
questions.explanation: page 532, Figure 181 on page 455, Figure 182 on DISCUSSION: First you must calculate the ETE from SWL page 534, and Figure 183 on page 536.) What TAS to SIE using the current flight plan on Fig. 179. The magnetic would EAB 90 need to maintain from SWL to SIE in variation at PHF is 7°W and. at PHL is 10°W. From SWL to SIE, an attempt to cut 3 minutes off of the flight plan (SWL- we will use a magnetic variation of 10°W to convert the true wind SIE) ETE? direction to magnetic wind. Wind Ground-

questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 179 on page 531, Figure 180A on page 533, Figure 181 on page 455, Figure 182 on page 534, and Figure 182A on page 535.) The time enroute from Newport News/Williamsburg Intl to Philadelphia Intl via the flight plan of EAB 90 is
questions.choice_c: 1 hour 31 minutes.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: To determine the ETE from PHF to PHL, you must complete the flight log in Fig. 179. In the remarks box of the flight plan, the magnetic variation at PHF (7°W) and PHL (10°W) are given. This information is used to convert the true wind direction to magnetic direction. i Our flight log has been expanded to provide more detail. To convert wind direction from true to magnetic, we used 7°W to SWL and 10°W to PHL. Wind Ground- Distance MC (mag) speed Time ORF 40G X X X 0:19:00G SAWED 42 030 307/70 217 0:11:36 SWL a 039 307/70 228 0:09:44 SWL/SIE COP 20 034 310/70 218 0:05:30 SIE 50 036 310/70" (2214 0:13:34 VCN 28 353 310/70 180 0:09:20 OOD “ir 301 310/70 167 0:06:06 PHL 30G X Xx X 0:16:00G 1:30:50 G = Given Answer (A) is incorrect. An ETE of 1 hr. 27 min. would require a TAS of approximately 245 kt., not 236 kt. Answer (B) is incorrect. An ETE of 1 hr. 29 min. would require a TAS of 240 kt., not 236 kt.

questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 179 on page 531, Figure 180 on page 532, Figure 181 on page 455, Figure 182 on page 534, and Figure 182A on page 535.) The planned fuel usage from Newport News/Williamsburg Intl to Philadelphia Intl for EAB 90 is
questions.choice_c: 1,084 pounds.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: To determine the planned fuel usage from PHF to PHL, you must complete the fuel portion of the flight log on Fig. 179. Use 689 PPH total fuel flow from level-off (ORF) to start of descent (OOD). Time Fuel (Ib.) ORF 0:19:00G 312.0G SAWED 0:11:36 133.2 SWL 0:09:44 idles SWL/SIE COP 0:05:30 63.2 SIE 0:13:34 155.8 VCN 0:09:20 107.2 OOD 0:06:06 70.0 PHL 0:16:00G 177.0G 1,130.2 G = Given Answer (B) is incorrect. This would be the fuel usage between PHF and PHL if the time from level-off to start of descent was 54 min., not 56 min. Answer (C) is incorrect. This would be the fuel usage between PHF and PHL if the time from level-off to start of descent was 52 min., not 56 min.

questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 179 on page 531, Figure 180 on page 532, Figure 181 on page 455, Figure 182 on page 534, and Figure 183 on page 536.) The required fuel from Newport News/Williamsburg Intl to Philadelphia Intl for EAB 90 is
questions.choice_c: 2,003 pounds.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: To determine the fuel required from PHF to PHL, you must complete the fuel portion of the flight log on Fig. 179. Use 689 PPH total fuel flow from level-off (ORF) to start of descent (OOD) and 739 PPH total fuel flow for alternate and reserves requirements. Time Fuel (Ib.) ORF 0:19:00G 312.0 GSAWED 0:11:36 Ao3e2 SWL 0:09:44 111.8 SWL/SIE COP 0:05:30 63.2 SIE 0:13:34 155.8 VCN 0:09:20 107.2 OOD 0:06:06 70.0 PHL 0:16:00G 177.0 GACY 0:18:00G 22427 Reserves 0:45:00 554.3 1,906.2 G = Given Answer (A) is incorrect. This is the required fuel from using a fuel flow of 689 PPH, not 739 PPH, for the alternate and reserve requirements. Answer (C) is incorrect. This includes the 95 Ib. required for a missed approach. While this should be included as a practical matter, it is not required by the FARs.

questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 182A on page 535.) EAB 90 is a CAT B aircraft and received a clearance to fly the LOC RWY O9R approach, to circle to land RWY 27R. The Baldn fix was received. What are the minimums?
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Fig. 182A is the ILS RWY 9R IAP at PHL. The BALDN fix minimums for circling are listed in the upper right corner of the planview section. For a Cat. B aircraft that received the BALDN fix, the circling minimums are 600-1. Answer (A) is incorrect. These are the circling minimums with the BALDN fix received for a Cat. A, not Cat. B, aircraft. Answer (C) is incorrect. These are the circling minimums without, not with, the BALDN fix identified.

questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 182A on page 535.) EAB 90 is a CAT B aircraft and received a clearance to fly the LOC RWY O9R, to land RWY O9R. The Baldn fix was received. What are the minimums?
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Fig. 182A is the ILS RWY 9R IAP at PHL. The BALDN fix minimums are located at the top center of the planview section. A clearance to fly the LOC RWY O9R, to land RWY O9R, means a straight-in approach. For a Cat. B aircraft that received the BALDN fix, the S-LOC 9R minimums are 520/24. Answer (B) is incorrect. The MDA for a S-LOC 9R approach with the BALDN fix received is 520 ft., not 600 ft. Answer (C) is incorrect. These are the minimums without, not with, the BALDN fix received for a S-LOC 9R approach.

questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 182A on page 535.) EAB 90 is a CAT B aircraft and received a clearance to fly the LOC RWY O9R to sidestep and land RWY O9L. The Baldn fix was received. What are the minimums?
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Fig. 182A is the ILS RWY 9R IAP for PHL. The BALDN fix minimums for a side step to RWY 9L are listed in the top center of the planview section below the S-LOC 9R minimums. The minimums for a Cat. B aircraft to fly the LOC RWY 9R and sidestep to land on RWY 9L with the BALDN fix received are 600-1. Answer (A) is incorrect. These are the minimums without, not with, the BALDN fix received for a side-step RWY 9L approach. Answer (B) is incorrect. These are the minimums for a S-LOC OR, not a side-step 9L, approach with the BALDN fix received.

questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 182A on page 535.) The PIC on EAB 90 has not flown 100 hours as PIC in the BE 1900 (CAT B aircraft). What are the minimums for the PIC when flying the ILS RWY O9R, at PHL?
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: FAR 135.225 requires that, if the PIC of a turbine-powered airplane has less than 100 hr. as PIC in that type of airplane (i.e., BE 1900), the MDA or DH is increased by 100 ft., and the visibility by 1/2 SM (or 2,400 ft. RVR). Thus, the PIC’s minimums when flying the ILS RWY O9R approach at PHL are 321/42. Answer (B) is incorrect. These are the minimums if the PIC has at least, not less than, 100 hr. as PIC in the BE 900. Answer (C) is incorrect. The visibility increases by 1/2 SM, or 2,400 ft. RVR, not 1,800 ft. RVR, if the PIC has less than 100 hr. as PIC in the BE 1900.

questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 182A on page 535.) The PIC of EAB 90 has 89 hours and 29 landings as PIC in the BE 1900, while operating under Part 135. The PIC has 1,234 hours and 579 landings as PIC in the DC-3 while operating under Part 135. What are the minimums for the ILS RWY 9R approach at PHL, for this PIC?
questions.explanation: - DISCUSSION: FAR 135.225 requires that, if the PIC of a turbine-powered airplane has less than 100 hr. as PIC in that type of airplane (i.e., BE 1900), the MDA or DH is increased by 100 ft., and the visibility is increased by 1/2 SM (or 2,400 ft. RVR). Thus, the PIC’s minimums when flying the ILS RWY 9R approach at PHL are 321/42. The DC-3 time does not count toward the 100-hr. requirement because it is a different type of airplane. Answer (A) is incorrect. These are the minimums if the PIC has at least, not less than, 100 hr. as PIC in the BE 1900. Answer (B) is incorrect. Visibility increases by 2,400 ft. RVR (1/2 SM), not 600 ft. RVR, if the PIC has less than 100 hr. as PIC in the BE 1900.

questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 183 on page 536 and Figure 183A on page 537.) When the weather at PHL goes below the PIC’s minimums, the flight diverts to ACY. Upon arrival at ACY, EAB 90 is cleared for the ILS RWY 13 approach. The PIC has 89 hours of PIC time in the BE 1900. What are the PIC’s minimums?
questions.explanation: FAR 135.225 requires that, if the PIC of a turbine-powered airplane has less than 100 hr. as PIC in that type of airplane (i.e., BE 1900), the MDA or DH is increased by 100 ft. and the visibility is increased by 1/2 SM (or 2,400 ft. RVR), but not to exceed the ceiling and visibility minimums when used as an alternate airport. Thus, the PIC’s minimums for the ILS RWY 13 approach at ACY are 376/42. Answer (A) is incorrect. These are the alternate minimums for the ILS RWY 13 to list the airport as an alternate, not to fly the approach, for Cat. D, not Cat. B, aircraft. Answer (B) is incorrect. These are the minimums if the PIC has at least, not less than, 100 hr. as PIC in the BE 1900.

questions.question_text: (Refer to Legend 15 on page 538.) Newport News/Williamsburg Intl is an FAR Part 139 airport. The Chart Supplement contains the following entry: ARFF Index A. What is the minimum number of aircraft rescue and fire fighting vehicles, and the type and amount of fire fighting agents that the airport should have?
questions.choice_a: Two vehicles and 600 pounds dry chemical (DC) or Halon 1211 or 500 pounds of DC plus 100 gallons of water.
questions.choice_b: One vehicle and 500 pounds of dry chemical (DC) or Halon 1211 or 450 pounds DC plus 100 gallons of water.
questions.choice_c: One vehicle and 500 pounds of dry chemical (DC) or Halon 1211 or 350 pounds DC and 1,000 gallons of water.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Legend 15 is a Chart Supplement legend. Look at the table under item 16 and find the airport index, A, on the left margin. The minimum number of vehicles is one, and the type and amount of fire-fighting agents are 500 Ib. of DC or HALON 1211 or 450 Ib. DC plus 100 gal. of water (H2O). Answer (A) is incorrect. An airport having an ARFF Index C, not A, is required to have at least two vehicles. Answer (C) is incorrect. An airport having an ARFF Index A is required to have one vehicle and 500 Ib. DC or HALON 1211 or 450 Ib., not 350 Ib., DC and 100 gal., not 1,000 gal., of water.

questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 293 on page 547.) The La Guardia Weather goes below minimums and New York Approach Control issues a clearance to N711JB, via radar vectors, to ASALT Intersection. What is the lowest altitude that Approach Control may clear N711JB to cross ASALT Intersection?
questions.choice_c: 2,000 feet.
questions.explanation: Fig. 293 is the VOR or GPS RWY 13L/13R IAP at JFK. Look at the profile view and locate ASALT Int. at the top left corner. Next to ASALT is 3000 with a line above and below the number, which means a mandatory altitude. To the left is a cross to reference a footnote, which is just below. The note states that, at ASALT Int., 3,000 ft. is a mandatory altitude unless advised otherwise by ATC, and 2,000 ft. is the minimum altitude. Answer (A) is incorrect. This is the mandatory altitude to cross ASALT, unless advised otherwise by ATC, which may use 2,000 ft. as the lowest altitude. Answer (B) is incorrect. The lowest altitude at which ATC may clear an aircraft to cross ASALT is 2,000 ft., not 2,500 ft.

questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 161A on page 546.) The distance from Canarsie (CRI) to RWY 13R at JFK is
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Fig. 161A is the VOR or GPS RWY 13L/13R IAP at JFK. Look at the profile view and locate CRI. Follow the approach course to the circle (which is a symbol for lead-in light clusters) and read the note. The distance from CRI to the lead- in lights is 1.7 NM. The arc distance via the lead-in lights to RWY 13R is 3.7 NM. Thus, the distance from CRI to RWY 13R is 5.4 NM (1.7 + 3.7). Answer (B) is incorrect. This is the distance to RWY 13R if the arc distance of 3.7 NM is added to the distance from CRI to the MAP, not from CRI to the first cluster of lead-in lights. Answer (C) is incorrect. This is the distance to RWY 13L, not RWY 13R, if the arc distance of 4.7 NM is added to the distance from CRI to the MAP, not from CRI to the first cluster of lead-in lights.

questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 161A on page 546 and Figure 293 on page 547.) For landing on RWY 31L at JFK, how much RWY is available?
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Fig. 161A is the VOR or GPS RWY 13L/13R IAP at JFK. The airport diagram is at the lower right corner of the chart. At the top portion are the available landing distances for various runways at JFK. For a landing on RWY 31L, there is 11,248 ft. of available runway. Answer (B) is incorrect. This is the available landing distance for RWY 13R, not RWY 31L. Answer (C) is incorrect. This is the total runway length, not the landing distance available, for RWY 31L.

questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 192 on page 458.) On the airway J10 between OBH and LBF, the MAA is 41,000 feet. What is the MAA on J197 between FSD and OBH?
questions.explanation: The jet route system consists of routes established from 18,000 ft. MSL to 45,000 ft. MSL inclusive. Jet routes are identified by the letter “J” followed by the airway number. Since there is no lower MAA established for J197 between FSD and OBH, the MAA is the upper limit of the jet route. Answer (A) is incorrect. The upper limit of a jet route is 45,000 ft. MSL, not 43,000 ft. MSL. Answer (C) is incorrect. This is the upper limit of Class A airspace, not a jet route.

questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 193 on page 554, Figure 193A on page 555, and Figure 194 on page 556.) The entry points for the (NORTHEAST GATE) LANDR ONE and SAYGE ONE arrivals are approximately
questions.choice_c: 13 NM apart.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Fig. 194 is JEPP LANDR ONE and SAYGE ONE STAR chart. In the upper left corner is a box titled “Arrival Overview.” At the bottom of that box is a note that states the entry points for parallel arrivals (e.g., northeast gate LANDR ONE and SAYGE ONE) are approximately 12 NM apart. Answer (A) is incorrect. The entry points for the northeast gate LANDR ONE and SAYGE ONE arrivals are approximately 12 NM, not 11 NM, apart. Answer (C) is incorrect. The entry points for the northeast gate LANDR ONE and SAYGE ONE arrivals are approximately 12 NM, not 13 NM, apart.

questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 193 on page 554, Figure 193A on page 555, Figure 194 on page 556, Figure 195 on page 557, Figure 195A on page 558, Figure 196 on page 559, and Figure 196A on page 560.) While being radar vectored for the ILS/DME RWY 35R, Denver Approach Control tells PIL 10 to contact the tower, without giving the frequency. What frequency should PIL 10 use for tower?
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Fig. 195 (JEPP) and Fig. 195A (NOS) are the IAP charts for the ILS/DME RWY 35R approach at DEN. In both charts, the communication frequencies are shown in the upper left corner. Under Denver Tower is the frequency 124.3. Answer (A) is incorrect. This is the ground control, not tower, frequency for RWY 35R. Answer (B) is incorrect. This is the tower frequency for RWY 35L, not RWY 35R.

questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 190 on page 551, Figure 195 on page 557, Figure 195A on page 558, Figure 196 on page 559, and Figure 196A on page 560.) The PIC of PIL 10 has 87.5 hours and 26 landings as PIC in the B-767, while operating under Part 121. The PIC has 1,876 hours and 298 landings, as PIC in the L-1011 while operating under Part 121. What are the minimums for the ILS/DME RWY 35R approach at DEN for the PIC?
questions.explanation: If the PIC has not served 100 hr. as PIC in the type of airplane (s)he is operating under Part 121, the MDA or DH must be increased by 100 ft., and the visibility by 1/2 SM (or 2,400 ft. RVR). However, the PIC time requirement may be reduced (not more than 50%) by substituting one landing in that type of airplane, while operating under Part 121, for one required hour of PIC time, if the PIC has at least 100 hr. as PIC in another type of airplane while operating under Part 121. Since the PIC has over 100 hr. as PIC in the L-1011 while operating under Part 121, (s)he can substitute each landing in the B-767 for one required hour of PIC time. Thus, the PIC meets the 100-hr. PIC requirement and can use the published minimums of 5567/18 for the ILS/DME RWY 35R approach at DEN. Answer (B) is incorrect. These would be the PIC minimums for the ILS/DME RWY 35R approach at DEN if the PIC had less than, not more than, 100 hr. as PIC in the L-1011. Answer (C) is incorrect. These are not minimums for the ILS/DME RWY 35R approach at DEN for a PIC with or without 100 hr. as PIC in the B-767 under Part 121.

questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 195 on page 557, Figure 195A on page 558, Figure 196 on page 559, and Figure 196A on page 560.) When PIL 10 becomes visual, at 3.8 NM from the end of Runway 35R, if the aircraft is on glide slope and on course what should the pilot see for a Visual Glideslope Indicator?
questions.choice_a: Two white and two red lights on the left side of the runway, in a row.
questions.choice_b: One white and one red light on the left or right side of the runway, in a row.
questions.choice_c: Two red and two white lights, in a row, on the right side of the runway.
questions.explanation: Fig. 196A is the Chart Supplement excerpt for Denver Intl. The runway data begin on the line titled RWY 17L-35R. Go down one line to RWY 35R, which has information concerning the approach end of the runway. The Visual Glideslope Indicator is given as PAPI (P4R), which means that the PAPI is a four-light system installed on the right side of the runway. Thus, when on the glide slope, the pilot will see two red and two white lights, in a row, on the right side of the runway. Answer (A) is incorrect. The PAPI on RWY 35R is on the right, not left, side of the runway. Answer (B) is incorrect. RWY 35R has a four-light, not a two-light, PAPI system.

questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 195 on page 557, Figure 195A on page 558, Figure 196 on page 559, and Figure 196A on page 560.) All of the runways at Denver Intl have what type of Visual Glideslope Indicators?
questions.choice_c: APAP.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Fig. 196A is the Chart Supplement excerpt for DEN. Look at the second line of each runway data line for the runway end data which list lighting systems for each runway end. Note that all runways at DEN have PAPI (precision approach path indicator) type of Visual Glideslope Indicators. Answer (A) is incorrect. All runways at DEN are equipped with PAPI, not PVASI (pulsating/steady burning visual approach slope indicator), type of Visual Glideslope Indicators. Answer (C) is incorrect. All runways at DEN are equipped with PAPI, not APAP (alignment of panels on approach path), type of Visual Glideslope Indicators.

questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 202 on page 565 and Figure 206 on page 570.) PTL 55 received the following clearance from Bay Approach Control. PTL 55 is’ cleared ILS RWY 19L at SFO, sidestep to RWY 19R. What is the lowest minimum descent altitude (MDA) and the lowest visibility that PTL 55 may accomplish the sidestep?
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Fig. 206 is the IAP chart for the ILS RWY 19L at SFO. Look at the minimums section at the lower left portion of the chart. On the left side of the table, locate the SIDESTEP RWY 19R row. Since PTL 55 has a speed of 1.3 Veo of 165 Kt., it is an approach category D airplane (i.e., 1.3 Vso of 141 kt. or more, but less than 166 kt.). The minimums for a Cat. D airplane for the side step to RWY 19R approach are 340-2. Answer (A) is incorrect. These (note that 1 SM is equivalent to an RVR of 5,000 ft.) are the minimums for Cat. A and B, not Cat. D, aircraft for the side step to RWY 19R approach. Answer (B) is incorrect. These are the minimums for a Cat. C, not Cat. D, aircraft for the side step to RWY 19R approach.

The PIC has 759 hours and 312 landings, as PIC, in the B-767 while operating under Part 121. What are the ON for the ILS RWY 19L approach at SFO, for the ?
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: If the PIC has not served 100 hr. as PIC in the type of airplane (s)he is operating under Part 121, the MDA or DH must be increased by 100 ft. and the visibility by 1/2 SM (or 2,400 ft. RVR). However, the PIC time requirement may be reduced (not more than 50%) by substituting one landing in that type of airplane, while operating under Part 121, for one required hour of PIC time if the PIC has at least 100 hr. as PIC in another type of airplane while operating under Part 121. Since the PIC has over 100 hr. as PIC in the B-767 while operating under Part 121, (s)he can substitute each landing in the B-747 for one hour of required PIC time. Thus, the PIC meets the 100-hr. PIC requirement and can use the published minimums of 208/40 for the ILS RWY 19L approach at SFO. Answer (A) is incorrect. These would be the minimums for the PIC if (s)he had less than, not more than, 100 hr. as PIC in another type of airplane while operating under Part 121. Answer (C) is incorrect. These would be the minimums if SFO was used as the alternate, not destination, and the PIC had less than, not more than, 100 hr. as PIC in another type of airplane while operating under Part 121.

questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 205 on page 568 and Legend 15 on page 574.) San Francisco Intl (SFO) is an FAR Part 139 airport. What is the minimum number of aircraft rescue and fire fighting vehicles, and the type and amount of fire fighting agents that the airport should have?
questions.choice_a: Three vehicles and 500 pounds of dry chemical (DC) or Halon 1211 or 450 pounds DC and 4,000 gallons of water.
questions.choice_b: Three vehicles and 500 pounds of dry chemical (DC) or Halon 1211 or 500 pounds of DC plus 5,000 gallons of water.
questions.choice_c: Three vehicles and 500 pounds of dry chemical (DC) or Halon 1211 or 450 pounds DC plus 6,000 gallons of water.
questions.explanation: Fig. 205 is the Chart Supplement excerpt for SFO. First, look for the aircraft rescue and fire-fighting (ARFF) index on the second line, which is ARFF Index E. Next, Legend 15 is a Chart Supplement legend. Look at the table under item 16. Locate the Airport Index column on the left side and move down to Index E. The minimum number of vehicles is three, and the airport is required to have at least 500 Ib. of dry chemical (DC) or HALON 1211 or 450 Ib. DC plus 6,000 gal. of water (HO). Answer (A) is incorrect. Three vehicles and 500 Ib. DC or HALON 1211 or 450 Ib. DC and 4,000 gal. of water are required at OAK (ARFF Index D), not SFO (ARFF Index E). Answer (B) is incorrect. An airport with an ARFF Index E is required to have three vehicles and 500 Ib. DC or HALON 1211 or 450 Ib., not 500 Ib., DC plus 6,000 gal., not 5,000 gal., of water.

questions.subsection: 1.3 times the Vsp speed, of PTL 55, is 165 knots.
questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 205 on page 568 and Figure 206A on page 571.) At San Francisco Intl (SFO), the runway hold position signs are
questions.choice_a: all on the left-hand side of the taxiways.
questions.choice_b: all on the right-hand side of the taxiways.
questions.choice_c: on either side of the taxiways.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Fig. 206A is the airport diagram for SFO. On the right side of the diagram, you will find a note that states, “Several runway hold position signs are on the right rather than the left side of the taxiways.” Answer (A) is incorrect. While normally, runway hold position signs are on the left side of the taxiways, SFO has some on the right side as noted in the Chart Supplement. Answer (B) is incorrect. Several, not all, runway hold position signs are on the right, rather than the left, side of the taxiways.

questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 205 on page 568 and Figure 206 on page 570.) What is the maximum weight that PTL 55 may weigh for landing at San Francisco Intl (SFO)?
questions.choice_c: 720,000 pounds.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Fig. 206 is the ILS RWY 19L approach to SFO; thus, we assume that PTL 55 will be landing on RWY 19L. Fig. 205 is the Chart Supplement excerpt for SFO. Look for the section titled Airport Remarks and go to the tenth line. There, RWY 01R-19L (among others) is listed along with aircraft and maximum weight. The maximum weight for a B-747 is 710,000 Ib. Answer (B) is incorrect. The maximum weight for a B-747 on RWY 19L at SFO is 710,000 Ib., not 715,000 Ib. Answer (C) is incorrect. The maximum weight for a B-747 on RWY 19L at SFO is 710,000 Ib., not 720,000 Ib.

questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 182 on page 534, Figure 214 on page 580, Figure 216 on page 583, Figure 216A on page 584, Figure 217 on page 464, and Figure 218 on page 465.) The time enroute between BDL and PHL for TNA 90 is
questions.choice_c: 50 minutes.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: To determine the ETE from BDL to PHL, you must complete the flight log in Fig. 214. In the remarks box of the flight plan, the magnetic variation is given at BDL (14°W) and PHL (10°W). This information is used to convert the true wind direction to magnetic direction. Our flight log below has been expanded to provide more detail. To convert the wind direction from true to magnetic, we use 14°W to SHERL Int. and 10°W to PHL. Wind Ground- Distance MC (mag) speed Time YODER 45G X X X 0:15:00G CCC 30 2S 34D 0m PAGZ 6 0:03:05 SHERL 43 213 354/55 481 0:05:21 MANTA 29 236 350/55 460 0:03:47 BRIGS 36 239 350/55 457 0:04:43 VCN 38 ZO ile O00 DOmmeEa eee OLO0226 PHL 46G X X X 0:14:00G 252312 G = Given Answer (A) is incorrect. An ETE of 54 min. requires a TAS of approximately 410 kt., not 440 kt. Answer (C) is incorrect. An ETE of 50 min. requires a TAS of approximately 480 kt., not 440 kt.

questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 214 on page 580.) In block 3 of the flight plan, the G following MD90/ indicates the aircraft is equipped with
questions.choice_a: GPS/GNSS that has oceanic, en route, and terminal capability.
questions.choice_b: Traffic Alert and Collision Avoidance System (TCAS) with /R capability.
questions.choice_c: Electronic Flight Instrument System (EFIS).
questions.explanation: In block 3 of the flight plan, the aircraft type followed by a “/G” means that the airplane is equipped with a Global Positioning System (GPS)/Global Navigation _ Satellite System (GNSS) that has oceanic, en route, and terminal capability. Answer (B) is incorrect. TCAS is indicated by a “T/” prefix to the aircraft type, not a “/G” suffix. Answer (C) is incorrect. In Oct. 1996, the equipment suffix “/G” was changed from EFIS with /R capability to GPS/GNSS with oceanic, en route, terminal, and GPS approach capability. Currently, there is no equipment suffix specifically for EFlIS-equipped aircraft.

questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 214 on page 580, Figure 216 on page 583, Figure 216A on page 584, Figure 217 on page 464, and Figure 218 on page 465.) The total fuel required to be onboard TNA 90 before starting to taxi at BDL is
questions.choice_c: 11,851 pounds.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: To determine the total fuel required to be on board TNA 90 before starting to taxi at BDL, you must complete the fuel portion of the flight log on Fig. 214. Use 6,150 PPH total fuel flow from level-off (YODER) to start of descent (VCN) and 5,900 PPH total fuel flow for reserve and alternate requirements. Time Fuel (Ib.) YODER 0:15:00G 2,560.0G CEE 0:03:53 398.0 SHERL 0:05:21 548.4 MANTA 0:03:47 387.8 BRIGS 0:04:43 483.5 VCN 0:05:28 560.3 PHL 0:14:00G 1,190.0G ACY 0:12:00G 1,180.0 Reserves 0:45:00 4,425.0 14) TSO G = Given Answer (A) is incorrect. This includes the 244 Ib. of fuel required for a missed approach. While this should be included as a practical matter, it is not required by the FARs. Answer (C) is incorrect. This is the approximate fuel needed if a fuel flow of 6,510 PPH, not 6,150 PPH, is used from YODER to VCN.

questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 215A on page 582.) The airport diagram of Bradley Int! Airport has a symbol (appears to be a triangle balanced on top of another triangle) located close to the approach end of RWY 19. What does this symbol indicate?
questions.choice_b: Practice hover area for the Army National Guard helicopters.
questions.choice_c: Two course lights, back to back, which flash beams of light along the course of an airway.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Fig. 215A is the airport diagram for BDL. The symbol that appears to be one triangle on top of another triangle located off the approach end of RWY 19 indicates that runway radar reflectors are at that position. Answer (B) is incorrect. Symbols are not used to indicate a practice hover area for helicopters. Answer (C) is incorrect. Two course lights, back to back, which flash beams of light along the course of an airway are used to mark airway segments in a few remote mountainous areas, not at an airport.
