questions.choice_c: 6,130 pounds.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: To determine the estimated fuel to be used between BDL and PHL, you must complete the fuel portion of the flight log on Fig. 214. Use 6,150 PPH fuel flow rate from level-off (YODER) to start of descent (VCN). Time Fuel (Ib.) YODER 0:15:00G 2,560.0G CCG 0:03:53 398.0 SHERL 0:05:21 548.4 MANTA 0:03:47 387.8 BRIGS 0:04:43 483.5 VCN 0:05:28 560.3 PHL 0:14:00G 1,190.0G 6,128.0 G = Given Answer (A) is incorrect. This includes the 45 min. reserve fuel requirement. Reserve fuel should not be included when calculating only the fuel to be used from BDL to PHL. Answer (B) is incorrect. This includes the fuel required for the missed approach and the 45 min. reserve. These requirements are not es when calculating only the fuel to be used from BDL to

questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 215 on page 581 and Legend 15 on page 593.) Windsor Locks/Bradley Intl, is an FAR Part 139 airport. What is the minimum number of aircraft rescue and fire-fighting vehicles, and what type and amount of fire-fighting agents is the airport required to have?
questions.choice_a: Two vehicles and 600 pounds dry chemical (DC), or Halon 1211 or 500 pounds of DC plus 4,000 gallons of water.
questions.choice_b: Three vehicles and 500 pounds of dry chemical (DC), or Halon 1211 or 450 pounds DC plus 3,000 gallons of water.
questions.choice_c: Three vehicles and 500 pounds of dry chemical (DC), or Halon 1211 or 450 pounds DC and 4,000 gallons of water.
questions.explanation: Fig. 215 is the Chart Supplement excerpt for BDL. First, look at the aircraft rescue and fire-fighting (ARFF) index on the third line, which is ARFF Index D. Next, Legend 15 is a Chart Supplement legend. Look at : the table under item 16. Locate the Airport Index column on | the left side and move down to Index D. The minimum number of vehicles is three, and the airport is required to have at least 500 Ib. of dry chemical (DC) or HALON 1211 or 450 Ib. DC plus 4,000 gal. of water (H2O). Answer (A) is incorrect. BDL is required to have three, not two, vehicles. Answer (B) is incorrect. Three vehicles and 500 Ib. DC or HALON 1211 or 450 Ib. DC plus 3,000 gal. of water are required for an airport with an ARFF Index C, not ARFF Index D.

questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 182A on page 585 and Figure 214 on page 580.) TNA 90 is a CAT C aircraft and has received a clearance to fly the ILS 9R approach and sidestep to RWY 9L at PHL. What are the minimums?
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Fig. 182A is the IAP chart for the ILS RWY 9R approach at PHL. The minimums section is located below the profile view near the bottom of the chart. Locate the row titled SIDESTEP RWY 9L and move right to the Cat. C column. The minimums are listed as 680/1-3/4. BALDN fix minimums are not used since the FAA’s question did not specify “BALDN fix is received,” as in our questions 102 and 103. Answer (A) is incorrect. These are the Cat. C minimums for the S-LOC RWY 9R with the BALDN fix identified, not the SIDESTEP RWY 9L without BALDN fix identified. Answer (B) is incorrect. These are Cat. C minimums for the SIDESTEP RWY 9L with, not without, the BALDN fix identified.

questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 182A on page 585 and Figure 214 on page 580.) TNA 90 is a CAT C aircraft and has received clearance to fly the LOC RWY O9R and circle to land RWY 27R. Baldn fix is received. What are the minimums?
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Fig. 182A is the IAP chart for the ILS RWY 9R approach at PHL. The BALDN fix minimums for a circling approach are listed in the upper right corner of the planview section. The Cat. C minimums for an aircraft cleared to fly the LOC RWY O9R and circle to land on RWY 27R are 600/1-1/2. Answer (A) is incorrect. These are the circling minimums with BALDN fix identified for a Cat. D, not Cat. C, airplane. Answer (C) is incorrect. These are the circling minimums if BALDN fix cannot be identified, not if BALDN fix is identified.

questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 182A on page 585 and Figure 214 on page 580.) TNA 90 is a CAT C aircraft and has received a clearance to fly the LOC RWY O9R; Baldn fix is received and TNA 90 is cleared to land O9R. What are the minimums?
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Fig. 182A is the IAP chart for the ILS RWY 9R approach at PHL. The BALDN fix minimums for the S-LOC 9R approach are listed in the top center of the planview section. For a Cat. C airplane, the BALDN fix minimums for a S-LOC 9R are 520/40. Answer (B) is incorrect. These are the minimums for a Cat. C airplane if BALDN fix cannot be identified, not if BALDN fix is identified. Answer (C) is incorrect. These are the BALDN fix minimums for a Cat. C airplane conducting a side step to RWY 9L or a circling approach, not an S-LOC 9R approach.

questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 182A on page 585 and Figure 214 on page 580.) The PIC of TNA 90 has 49 hours and 102 landings as PIC in the MD-90 while operating under Part 121. The PIC also has 959 hours and 246 landings, as PIC, in the B-727 while operating under Part 121. What are the minimums for the ILS RWY 9R approach at PHL, for this PIC?
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: If the PIC has not served 100 hr. as PIC in the type of airplane (s)he is operating under Part 121, the MDA or DH must be increased by 100 ft., and the visibility by 1/2 SM (or 2,400 ft. RVR). However, the PIC time requirement may be reduced (not more than 50%) by substituting one landing in that type of airplane, while operating under Part 121, for one required hour of PIC time if the PIC has at least 100 hr. as PIC in another type of airplane while operating under Part 121. E; Since the PIC has over 100 hr. as PIC in the B-727 while operating under Part 121, (s)he can substitute each of only 50 landings (i.e., 50% of required time) in the MD-90 for one hour of required PIC time. This will give the PIC only 99 hr., which means the PIC minimums are raised for the ILS RWY 9R approach at PHL. The PIC minimums are 321/42. Answer (A) is incorrect. These are the published minimums, which can be used only if the PIC has 100 hr. or more, not less than 100 hr., as PIC in the MD-90. Answer (C) is incorrect. RVR visibility is increased by 2,400 ft., not 600 ft.

questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 182A on page 585 and Figure 214 on page 580.) The PIC on TNA 90 (CAT C aircraft operated under FAR Part 121) has not flown 100 hours as PIC in the MD-90. What are the minimums while flying the ILS RWY 9R to land O9R at PHL?
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Fig. 182A is the IAP chart for the ILS RWY QR approach at PHL. If the PIC has not served 100 hr. as PIC in the type of airplane (s)he is operating under Part 121, the MDA or DH must RVR) by 100 ft., and the visibility by 1/2 SM (or 2,400 ft. Thus, if the PIC of TNA 90 has not flown 100 hr. as PIC in the MD-90, the minimums to conduct the ILS RWY 9R at PHL are 321/42. Answer (A) is incorrect. If the PIC has less than 100 hr. as PIC in the MD-90, the RVR visibility must be increased by 2,400 ft., not 1,200 ft. Answer (B) is incorrect. If the PIC has less than 100 hr. as PIC in the MD-90, the RVR visibility must be increased by 2,400 ft., not 1,800 ft.

questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 214 on page 580, Legend 42 on page 594, and Legend 42B on page 596.) The filed flight plan for TNA 90 indicates, if it becomes necessary to divert to the alternate, that tower enroute (TEC), radar vectors, and 3,000 feet are requested to ACY. If radar vectors are not available, what route can be expected from PHL to ACY?
questions.choice_a: Direct SAVVY Intersection, V166 OOD, V184 AGYe
questions.choice_b: Direct WILJR Intersection, VCN, V184 ACY.
questions.choice_c: OOD VCN V184 ACY.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: The TEC city pairs for PHL are shown in Legend 42B. Locate Philadelphia on the left margin; then locate Atlantic City (ACY) under the destination column on the right margin, which is the second line under PHL. The route listed is OOD VCN V184 ACY, which is the expected route if radar vectors are not available. __ Answer (A) is incorrect. The TEC routing from PHL to ACY without radar vectors is direct OOD, not direct SAVVY V166 OOD. Answer (B) is incorrect. The TEC routing from PHL to ACY without radar vectors is direct OOD, not direct WILJR.

questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 214 on page 580, Legend 42 on page 594, and Legend 42B on page 596.) The filed flight plan for TNA 90 indicates, if it becomes necessary to divert to the alternate, that tower enroute (TEC), radar vectors, and 3,000 feet are requested to ACY. What is the maximum altitude that TNA 90 may be cleared to under TEC?
questions.choice_c: 4,000 feet.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: The TEC city pairs for PHL are shown in Legend 42B. Locate Philadelphia on the left margin and then locate Atlantic City on the right margin (destination), which is the second line under PHL. The highest altitude for the TEC route is 3,000 feet. Answer (A) is incorrect. The highest altitude for the TEC route from PHL to ACY is 3,000 ft., not 2,000 feet. Answer (C) is incorrect. The highest altitude for the TEC route from Philadelphia to Allentown, not Atlantic City, is 4,000 feet.

questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 183 on page 586, Figure 183A on page 587, and Figure 214 on page 580.) The PIC, of TNA 90, has 75 hours as PIC of this type airplane. The MD-90 is a Category C aircraft. What is the lowest ceiling/visibility that may be forecast to use ACY as an alternate on the flight plan, and what is the lowest visibility that may exist at ACY prior to the final approach segment to continue the ILS RWY 13 approach?
questions.choice_c: 700-2 and 18 RVR.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Fig. 183A is the IAP chart for the ILS RWY 13 approach at ACY. Below the minimums section (lower left corner), there is a black triangle with a white “A” inside, which indicates nonstandard alternate minimums. Fig. 183 lists the nonstandard alternate minimums for ACY. Since the nonstandard alternate minimums for the ILS RWY 13 apply to Cat. D and E aircraft only, a Cat. C aircraft can use the standard alternate minimums for a precision approach of 600-2. If the PIC has not served 100 hours as PIC in that type of airplane, the minimum landing visibility at an alternate airport cannot be less than 1 SM (or 5,000 ft. RVR). Answer (A) is incorrect. The lowest visibility that may exist at ACY prior to the final approach segment to continue to ILS RWY 13 approach is 1 SM, not 2 SM. Answer (C) is incorrect. A ceiling/visibility of 700-2 is the minimum that may be forecast to use ACY as an alternate on a flight plan for a Cat. D, not Cat. C, airplane; the minimum RVR visibility must be 5,000 ft., not 1,800 feet.

questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 255A on page 588, Figure 255B on page 589, Figure 256 on page 590, Figure 257 on page 591, and Figure 257A on page 592.) At what point does the flight enter the final approach segment of the ILS RWY 25L approach at LAX?
questions.choice_a: LIMMA INT with the flaps at approach and gear down.
questions.choice_b: FUELR INT and the flaps at approach.
questions.choice_c: Intercepting the glideslope.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: The Maltese cross and a lightning bolt appear at LIMMA INT. The configuration of the aircraft (gear and flaps) are never part of the FAF definition for approach designers. The correct answer for this approach is at glideslope intercept. Answer (A) is incorrect. The configuration of the aircraft (gear and flaps) are never part of the FAF definition for approach designers. Answer (B) is incorrect. FUELR is more of a step- down fix from 8,000 feet to 7,000 feet, is 26.4 DME from the runway, and is not known as the final approach segment.

questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 214 on page 580, Figure 183 on page 586, and Figure 183A on page 587.) The weather at PHL goes below the PIC’s minimums and TNA 90 (a CAT C aircraft operating under FAR Part 121) diverts to the alternate ACY. Upon arrival at ACY, TNA 90 is cleared for an ILS RWY 13 approach. The PIC has less than 100 hours of PIC time in the MD-90. What are the landing minimums?
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: If the PIC has not served 100 hours as PIC in the type of airplane (s)he is operating under Part 121, the MDA or DH and visibility need not be increased above those applicable to the airport when used as an alternate, but in no event may the landing minimums be less than 300 ft. and 1 SM (or 5,000 ft. RVR). Fig. 183A is the IAP chart for the ILS RWY 13 approach at ACY. The TDZE for RWY 13 is found in the airport diagram at the lower right of the chart. The TDZE for RWY 13 is 76 ft. Thus, the landing minimums for the PIC of TNA 90 for the ILS RWY 13 approach are 376/50. Answer (A) is incorrect. The RVR visibility must be at least 5,000 ft., not remain the same. Answer (C) is incorrect. An RVR visibility of 4,200 ft. would be required if ACY were the destination, not the alternate, airport.

questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 40 on page 651.) What is the climb performance with both engines operating? Bressuireraltitude = ee. Sik week ote oe 9,500 feet Temperature (OAM) iawn cy ctl tera setae —5°C CALC Sy Free tar ee RB ne pa te SRE: ON
questions.choice_c: 335 feet/minute.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Interpolate where 9,500-ft. pressure altitude will be by first locating the 9,000-ft. pressure altitude (the thicker line between 8,000- and 10,000-ft. pressure altitudes) and counting up five lines from the 9,000-ft. pressure altitude. Then, draw a line from that reference point across the chart and identify where —5°C intersects the 9,500-ft. line. From that point, draw a line straight down to the “Rate of Climb” reference line, where you will arrive at 915 feet/minute. It is necessary to read the note sections in Fig. 40 in order to finish computing the answer. Because the heater is on, the rate of climb is reduced by 315 feet/minute. Thus, 915 — 315 = 600 feet/minute. Answer (B) is incorrect. A rate of climb of 925 ft./min. results from incorrectly interpreting the chart and forgetting to subtract 315 ft./min. as indicated by the note section of the chart. Answer (C) is incorrect. A rate of climb of 335 ft./min. results from interpolating at a pressure altitude of approximately 10,750 ft., rather than the 9,500 ft. indicated.

questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 15 on page 653, Figure 16 on page 654, and Figure 17 on page 655.) What is the two-engine rate of climb after takeoff in climb configuration for Operating Conditions BE-21?
questions.choice_c: 2,300 ft/min.
questions.explanation: Fig. 15 gives the following operating conditions for BE-21: OAT at takeoff +10°C Airport pressure altitude 2,000 ft. Initial climb weight 16,600 Ib. Ice vanes Retracted Fig. 16 gives the two-engine climb chart. Find +10°C OAT at the bottom left of the graph and proceed up vertically to the 2,000-ft. airport pressure altitude. From that point, move right horizontally to the aircraft weight reference line. Note 16,600 Ib. is the maximum weight, so you proceed horizontally to the right side of the graph, which indicates 2,300 fpm. Answer (A) is incorrect. The climb rate at a pressure altitude of 16,000 ft. and OAT of —22°C is 1,350 fom. Answer (B) is incorrect. The climb rate at 16,000 Ib., not 16,600 Ib., is 2,450 fom.

questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 15 on page 653, Figure 16 on page 654, and Figure 17 on page 655.) What is the single-engine climb gradient after takeoff in climb configuration for Operating Conditions BE-22?
questions.choice_c: 5.6 percent gradient.
questions.explanation: Fig. 15 gives the following operating conditions for BE-22: OAT at takeoff o°Cc Airport pressure altitude 1,000 ft. Initial climb weight 14,000 Ib. Ice vanes Extended Fig. 17 gives the single-engine climb chart. Find 0°C OAT at the bottom left of the graph and proceed up vertically to the 1,000-ft. airport pressure altitude. From that point, move right horizontally to the aircraft weight reference line. Move up and to the right (parallel to the guide line) to 14,000 Ib.; then move horizontally to the right side of the graph, which indicates 870 fom. Adjust the rate of climb for the ice vanes extended as in the note above the graph. Thus, the single-engine rate of climb with ice vanes extended is 755 fpm (870 — 115). Find 755 fpm on the right end of the chart and move right horizontally to the reference line to determine a climb gradient of 5.6%. Answer (A) is incorrect. This percentage is the climb gradient before the adjustment is made for the ice vanes being extended. Answer (B) is incorrect. This percentage is the climb gradient at a weight of 13,600 Ib., not 14,000 Ib., with the ice vanes retracted, not extended.

questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 15 on page 653, Figure 16 on page 654, and Figure 17 on page 655.) What is the two-engine rate of climb after takeoff in climb configuration for Operating Conditions BE-23?
questions.choice_c: 2,490 ft/min.

Ice vanes Retracted Fig. 16 gives the two-engine climb chart. Find +20°C OAT at the bottom left of the graph and proceed up vertically to the 3,000-ft. airport pressure altitude. From that point, move right horizontally to the aircraft weight reference line. Move up and to the right (parallel to the guide line) to 15,000 Ib.; then move horizontally to the right side of the graph, which indicates 2,600 fom. Answer (A) is incorrect. The climb rate at a pressure altitude of 10,500 ft., not 3,000 ft., is 1,500 fom. Answer (C) is incorrect. The climb rate with an OAT of +30°C, not +20°C, is 2,490 fpm.

From that point, move right horizontally to the aircraft weight reference line. Move up and to the right (parallel to the guide line) to 16,000 Ib.; then move horizontally to the right side of the graph, which indicates 2,100 fpm. Answer (B) is incorrect. questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 15 below, Figure 16 on page 654, and Figure 17 on page 655.) What is the single- engine rate of climb after takeoff in climb configuration for Operating Conditions BE-25?

Ice vanes Extended Fig. 17 gives the single-engine climb chart. Find —10°C OAT at the bottom left of the graph and proceed up vertically to the 5,000-ft. airport pressure altitude. From that point, move right horizontally to the aircraft weight reference line. Move up and to the right (parallel to the guide line) to 14,000 Ib.; then move horizontally to the right side of the graph, which indicates 780 fom. Next, adjust the rate of climb for the ice vanes extended as indicated in the note above the graph. Thus, the single-engine rate of climb with ice vanes extended is 665 fpm (780 — 115). Answer (A) is incorrect. The single-engine climb rate at a weight of 16,000 Ib., not 14,000 Ib., is 385 fom. Answer (B) is incorrect. The single-engine climb rate before the adjustment is made for the ice vanes being extended is 780 fpm.

questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 15 on page 653 and Figure 18 on page 657.) What are the time, fuel, and distance from the start of climb to cruise altitude for Operating Conditions BE-23?
questions.choice_c: 15.0 minutes; 240 pounds; 46 NM.
questions.explanation: ; DISCUSSION: Fig. 15 provides the following operating conditions for BE-23: OAT at takeoff +20°C OAT at cruise ISA Airport pressure altitude 3,000 ft. Cruise altitude 20,000 ft. Initial climb weight 15,000 Ib. Ice vanes Retracted Fig. 18 provides a chart to determine time, fuel, and distance to cruise climb. At the bottom of the chart, find ISA and move up and to the left on the ISA line to the cruise altitude of 20,000 ft. Then move right horizontally to the initial climb weight of 15,000 Ib. Then move down vertically to the bottom of the graph to determine the time to climb of 14.5 min. Move down vertically to the fuel-to-climb line to determine fuel burned of 235 Ib., and move down vertically to the next line to determine a distance to climb of 44 NM. Repeat this process using an OAT of +20°C and the airport pressure altitude of 3,000 ft. to determine the time to climb of 0.5 min., fuel burned of 25 Ib., and a distance of 4 NM. Next, subtract the results of the airport altitude from the results of the cruise altitude. Thus, from 3,000 ft. to 20,000 ft., it will take 14 min. (14.5 — 0.5), burn 210 Ib. (235 — 25) of fuel, and cover a distance of 40 NM (44 — 4). Answer (A) is incorrect. Fuel burned of 180 Ib. would require an OAT at takeoff of +40°C, not +20°C, or an OAT at cruise of —36°C, not ISA. Answer (C) is incorrect. To climb in 15.0 min. would require an OAT at cruise of —19°C, not ISA.

questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 15 on page 653 and Figure 18 on page 657.) What are the time, fuel, and distance from the start of climb to cruise altitude for Operating Conditions BE-24?
questions.choice_c: 10.0 minutes; 170 pounds; 30 NM.
questions.explanation: Fig. 15 provides the following operating conditions for BE-24: OAT at takeoff E251 OAT at cruise Ore Airport pressure altitude 4,000 ft. Cruise altitude 14,000 ft. Initial climb weight 16,000 Ib. Ice vanes Retracted Fig. 18 provides a chart to determine time, fuel, and distance to cruise climb. At the bottom of the chart, find 0°C OAT and move up vertically to the cruise altitude of 14,000 ft. Then move right horizontally to the initial climb weight of 16,000 Ib. Then move down vertically to the bottom of the graph to determine the time to climb of 12.8 min. Move down vertically to the fuel- to-climb line to determine fuel burned of 210 Ib., and move down vertically to the next line to determine a distance to climb of 38 NM. Repeat this process using an OAT of +25°C and the airport pressure altitude of 4,000 ft. to determine the time to climb of 2.0 min., fuel burned of 40 Ib., and a distance of 8 NM. Next, subtract the results of the airport altitude from the results of the cruise altitude. Thus, from 4,000 ft. to 14,000 ft., it will take 10.8 min. (12.8 — 2.0), burn 170 Ib. (210 — 40) of fuel, and cover a distance of 30 NM (38 — 8). Answer (A) is incorrect. To climb in 12.0 min. would require a cruise OAT of +5°C, not 0°C. Answer (B) is incorrect. To climb in 9.0 min. would require a cruise OAT of —2°C, not 0°C, or an OAT at takeoff of +34°C, not +25°C.

questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 15 on page 653 and Figure 18 on page 657.) What are the time, fuel, and distance from the start of climb to cruise altitude for Operating Conditions BE-25?
questions.choice_c: 12.5 minutes; 195 pounds; 38 NM. 10.9 Single Engine Service Ceiling
questions.explanation: Fig. 15 provides the following operating conditions for BE-25: OAT at takeoff —10°C OAT at cruise —40°C Airport pressure altitude 5,000 ft. Cruise altitude 22,000 ft. Initial climb weight 14,000 Ib. Ice vanes Extended Fig. 18 provides a chart to determine time, fuel, and distance to cruise climb. For operation with the ice vanes extended, add 10°C to the OAT before entering chart (Note 2 in Fig. 18). At the bottom of the chart, find -30°C (-40 + 10) OAT and move up vertically to the cruise altitude of 22,000 ft. Then move right horizontally to the initial climb weight of 14,000 Ib. Then move down vertically to the bottom of the graph to determine the time to climb of 14.5 min. Move down vertically to the fuel-to-climb line to determine fuel burned of 235 Ib., and move down vertically to the next line to determine a distance to climb of 44 NM. Repeat this process using an OAT of +0°C (—10 + 10) and the airport pressure altitude of 5,000 ft. to determine the time to climb of 2.0 min., fuel burned of 40 Ib., and a distance of 6 NM. Next, subtract the results of the airport altitude from the results of the cruise altitude. Thus, from 5,000 ft. to 22,000 ft., it will take 12.5 min. (14.5 — 2.0), burn 195 Ib. (235 — 40) of fuel, and cover a distance of 38 NM (44 — 6). Answer (A) is incorrect. The time to climb to a cruise altitude of 21,000 ft., not 22,000 ft., is 11.5 min. Answer (B) is incorrect. The time to climb to a cruise altitude of 19,000 ft., not 22,000 ft., is 8.0 min.

questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 19 on page 661 and Figure 20 on page 661.) At what altitude is the service ceiling with one engine inoperative for Operating Conditions BE-26?
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Fig. 19 provides operating conditions for BE-26. Given an OAT of —8°C, weight of 15,500 Ib., and the bleed air ON, determine the service ceiling with one engine inoperative. Fig. 20 provides a chart to determine the service ceiling with bleed air ON or OFF. Use the graph on the left-hand side for bleed air ON. Find OAT —8°C at the bottom of the chart and move up vertically to 15,500 Ib. Then move horizontally to the left edge of the chart to determine a pressure altitude service ceiling with one engine inoperative of 13,000 ft. Answer (B) is incorrect. A 14,200-ft. service ceiling with one engine inoperative would occur with an OAT of —18°C, not -8°C. Answer (C) is incorrect. A 13,600-ft. service ceiling with one engine inoperative would occur with an OAT of —13°C, not —8°C.

questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 19 on page 661 and Figure 20 on page 661.) Which statement is true regarding performance with one engine inoperative for Operating Conditions BE-27?
questions.choice_a: Climb rate at the MEA is more than 50 ft/min.
questions.choice_b: Service ceiling is below the MEA.
questions.choice_c: Bleed air OFF improves service ceiling by 3,000 feet.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Fig. 19 provides operating conditions for BE-27. Given an OAT of +30°C, weight of 16,600 Ib., the bleed air ON, and an MEA of 5,500 ft., determine the service ceiling with one engine inoperative. Fig. 20 provides a chart to determine the service ceiling with bleed air ON or OFF. Use the graph on the left-hand side for bleed air ON. Find OAT +30°C at the bottom of the chart and move up vertically to 16,600 Ib. Then move horizontally to the left edge of the chart to determine a pressure altitude service ceiling (maximum altitude for 50 fpm rate of climb) with one engine inoperative of 5,000 ft. Thus, the service ceiling of 5,000 ft. is below the MEA of 5,500 ft. t: Answer (A) is incorrect. A climb rate of 50 fom would occur below the service ceiling of 5,000 ft., which is below, not at, the MEA of 5,500 ft. Answer (C) is incorrect. Using the right- hand chart in Fig. 20, the service ceiling with the bleed air OFF is 7,100 ft., which is an improvement of only 2,100 ft. (7,100 — 5,000), not 3,000 ft.

questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 19 on page 661 and Figure 20 on page 661.) At what altitude is the service ceiling with one engine inoperative for Operating Conditions BE-28?
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Fig. 19 provides operating conditions for BE-28. Given an OAT of +5°C, weight of 16,000 Ib., and the bleed air OFF, determine the service ceiling with one engine inoperative. Fig. 20 provides a chart to determine the service ceiling with bleed air ON or OFF. Use the graph on the right-hand side for bleed air OFF. Find OAT +5°C at the bottom of the chart and move up vertically to 16,000 Ib. Then move horizontally to the left edge of the chart to determine a pressure altitude service ceiling with one engine inoperative of 11,800 ft., which is 2,800 ft. above the 9,000-ft. MEA. Answer (A) is incorrect. The 11,800-ft. service ceiling with one engine inoperative is 2,800 ft., not 1,500 ft., above the route segment MEA of 9,000 ft. Answer (B) is incorrect. A 10,400-ft. service ceiling with one engine inoperative would occur with an OAT of +15°C, not +5°C.

questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 19 on page 661 and Figure 20 on page 661.) Which statement is true regarding performance with one engine inoperative for Operating Conditions BE-29?
questions.choice_a: Service ceiling is more than 100 feet above the MEA.

questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Fig. 19 provides operating conditions for BE-29. Fig. 20 provides a chart to determine the service ceiling with bleed air ON or OFF. Use the graph on the right-hand side for bleed air ON. Find OAT +18°C at the bottom of the chart and move up vertically to 16,300 Ib. Then move horizontally to the left edge of the chart to determine a pressure altitude service ceiling with one engine inoperative of 7,700 ft., which is more than 100 ft. above the MEA of 7,000 ft. Answer (B) is incorrect. The service ceiling is the maximum altitude for a rate of climb at 50 fpm, which is above the MEA of 7,000 ft. with the bleed air ON 7,700 ft. and the bleed air OFF 9,500 feet. Answer (C) is incorrect. At the MEA of 7,000 ft., the airplane is climbing at a rate above 50 fpm.

MEA (11,600 — Answer (A) is incorrect. A 9,600-ft. service ceiling with one engine inoperative would occur with the bleed air ON, not OFF. Answer (B) is incorrect. questions.subsection: 10.10 En Route Cruise Time and Fuel
questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 21 on page 667, Figure 22 on page 667, Figure 23 on page 668, Figure 24 on page 669, and Figure 25 on page 670.) What is the en route time of the cruise leg for Operating Conditions BE-31?
questions.choice_c: 1 hour 19 minutes.
questions.explanation: oe DISCUSSION: Fig. 21 provides the operating conditions for BE-31. Weight 15,000 Ib. Pressure altitude 22,000 ft. Temperature (OAT) -19°C True course sO: Wind 180°/30 kt. Cruise distance 280 NM Determine the standard (ISA) temperature at 22,000 ft. to be —29°C [15°C — (22 x 2)]. An OAT of -19°C is ISA +10°C. Fig. 23 provides cruise data for ISA +10°C. Find 22,000 ft. under the pressure altitude column on the left edge of the chart, and move right horizontally to determine the TAS for both 16,000 Ib. (221 kt.) and 14,000 Ib. (235 kt.). Interpolate for 15,000 Ib. to determine a TAS of 228 Kt. On the wind side of the flight computer, set the wind direction of 180° under the true index and place a mark (wind dot) 30 kt. above the grommet. Set true course (110°) under the true index and slide the wind dot under TAS of 228 kt. Groundspeed is read under the grommet as 216 kt. On the calculator side, set the groundspeed under the solid pointer triangle and locate 280 NM on the outer scale. Below that, read the time en route of 77 min., or 1 hr. 17 min. Note: Do not attempt to use Fig. 22 to solve these problems, as Fig. 22 relates to landing, not cruise, wind components. Answer (A) is incorrect. This is the approximate en route time at an OAT of ISA —-10°C, not ISA +10°C. Answer (C) is incorrect. This is the approximate en route time at a pressure altitude of 25,000, not 22,000, ft. and 14,000, not 15,000, Ib.

questions.choice_c: 870 pounds.
questions.explanation: Fig. 21 provides the operating conditions for BE-31. Weight 15,000 Ib. Pressure altitude 22,000 ft. Temperature (OAT) -19°C True course 110° Wind 180°/30 kt. Cruise distance 280 NM Determine the standard (ISA) temperature at 22,000 ft. to be —29°C [15°C — (22 x 2)]. An OAT of -19°C is ISA +10°C. Fig. 23 provides cruise data for ISA +10°C. Find 22,000 ft. under the pressure altitude column on the left edge of the chart, and move right horizontally to determine the TAS and total fuel flow for both 16,000 Ib. (221 kt. and 632 Ib./hr.) and 14,000 Ib. (235 kt. and 634 Ib./hr.). Interpolate for 15,000 Ib. to determine a TAS of 228 kt. and total fuel flow of 633 Ib./hr. On the wind side of the flight computer, set the wind direction of 180° under the true index and place a mark (wind dot) 30 kt. above the grommet. Set true course (110°) under the true index and slide the wind dot under TAS of 228 kt. Groundspeed is read under the grommet as 216 kt. On the calculator side, set the groundspeed under the solid pointer triangle and locate 280 NM on the outer scale. Below that, read the time en route of 77 min. Fuel consumption is the total fuel flow multiplied by the time (hr.), which is 812 Ib. [633 x (77 + 60)]. Note: Do not attempt to use Fig. 22 to solve these problems, as Fig. 22 relates to landing, not cruise, wind components. Answer (B) is incorrect. The fuel consumption for a cruise en route time of 71, not 77, min. given a total fuel flow of 633 Ib./hr. is 749 lb. Answer (C) is incorrect. The fuel consumption for a cruise en route time of 83, not 77, min. given a total fuel flow of 633 Ib./hr. is 870 Ib.

questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 21 on page 667, Figure 22 on page 667, Figure 23 on page 668, Figure 24 on page 669, and Figure 25 on page 670.) What is the fuel consumption during the cruise leg for Operating Conditions BE-34?
questions.choice_c: 737 pounds.
questions.explanation: Fig. 21 provides the operating conditions for BE-34. Weight 16,000 Ib. Pressure altitude 23,000 ft. Temperature (OAT) -—31°C True course 020° Wind 340°/25 kt. Cruise distance 230 NM Determine the standard (ISA) temperature at 23,000 ft. to be —31°C [15°C — (23 x 2)]. An OAT of —31°C is ISA. Fig. 24 provides cruise data for ISA. Find 16,000 Ib. at the top of the chart, and move down vertically to determine the TAS and total fuel flow for both 22,000 ft. (233 kt. and 676 Ib./hr.) and 24,000 ft. (223 kt. and 630 Ib./hr.). Interpolate for 23,000 ft. to determine a TAS of 228 kt. and total fuel flow of 653 Ib./hr. On the wind side of the flight computer, set the wind direction of 340° under the true index and place a mark (wind dot) 25 kt. above the grommet. Set true course (020°) under the true index and slide the wind dot under TAS of 228 kt. Groundspeed is read under the grommet as 208 kt. On the calculator side, set the groundspeed under the solid pointer triangle and locate 230 NM on the outer scale. Below that, read the time en route of 66 min. Fuel consumption is the total fuel flow multiplied by the time (hr.), which is 718 Ib. [653 x (66 + 60)). Note: Do not attempt to use Fig. 22 to solve these problems, as Fig. 22 relates to landing, not cruise, wind components. Answer (A) is incorrect. The fuel consumption for a cruise en route time of 61, not 66, min. given a total fuel flow of 653 Ib./hr. is 668 Ib. Answer (C) is incorrect. The fuel consumption for a cruise en route time of 68, not 66, min. given a total fuel flow of 653 Ib./hr. is 737 Ib.

questions.subsection: 10.10 En Route Cruise Time and Fuel
questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 21 on page 667, Figure 22 on page 667, Figure 23 on page 668, Figure 24 on page 669, and Figure 25 on page 670.) What is the en route time of the cruise leg for Operating Conditions BE-35?
questions.choice_c: 1 hour 10 minutes.
questions.explanation: 1é DISCUSSION: Fig. 21 provides the operating conditions for BE-35. Weight 11,000 Ib. Pressure altitude 14,000 ft. Temperature (OAT) -3°C True course 305° Wind 040°/50 kt. Cruise distance 300 NM Determine the standard (ISA) temperature at 14,000 ft. to be —13°C [15°C — (14 x 2)]. An OAT of -3°C is ISA +10°C. Fig. 23 provides cruise data for ISA +10°C. Find 14,000 ft. under the pressure altitude column on the left edge of the chart, and move right horizontally to determine the TAS for both 12,000 Ib. (251 kt.) and 10,000 Ib. (255 kt.). Interpolate for 11,000 Ib. to determine a TAS of 253 Kt. On the wind side of the flight computer, set the wind direction of 040° under the true index and place a mark (wind dot) 50 kt. above the grommet. Set true course under the true index and slide the wind dot under TAS of 253 kt. Groundspeed is read under the grommet as 253 kt. On the calculator side, set the groundspeed under the solid pointer triangle and locate 300 NM on the outer scale. Below that, read the time en route of 71 min., or 1 hr. 11 min. Note: Do not attempt to use Fig. 22 to solve these problems, as Fig. 22 relates to landing, not cruise, wind components. Answer (A) is incorrect. This requires a TAS of 273 kt., which is a TAS higher than on any chart provided. Answer (B) is incorrect. This is the en route time at an OAT of ISA —10°C, not ISA +10°C.

questions.subsection: 10.10 En Route Cruise Time and Fuel
questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 21 on page 667, Figure 22 on page 667, Figure 23 on page 668, Figure 24 on page 669, and Figure 25 on page 670.) What is the fuel consumption during the cruise leg for Operating Conditions BE-35?
questions.choice_c: 954 pounds.
questions.explanation: Fig. 21 provides the operating conditions for BE-35. Weight 11,000 Ib. Pressure altitude 14,000 ft. Temperature (OAT) —3°C True course 305° Wind 040°/50 kt. Cruise distance 300 NM Determine the standard (ISA) temperature at 14,000 ft. to be —13°C [15°C — (14 x 2)]. An OAT of -3°C is ISA +10°C. Fig. 23 provides cruise data for ISA +10°C. Find 14,000 ft. under the pressure altitude column on the left edge of the chart, and move right horizontally to determine the TAS and total fuel flow for both 12,000 Ib. (251 kt. and 818 Ib./hr.) and 10,000 Ib. (255 kt. and 818 Ib./hr.). Interpolate for 11,000 Ib. to determine a TAS of 253 kt. and a total fuel flow of 818 Ib./hr. On the wind side of the flight computer, set the wind direction of 040° under the true index and place a mark (wind dot) 50 kt. above the grommet. Set true course (305°) under the true index and slide the wind dot under TAS of 253 kt. Groundspeed is read under the grommet as 253 kt. On the calculator side, set the groundspeed under the solid pointer triangle and locate 300 NM on the outer scale. Below that, read the time en route of 71 min. Fuel consumption is the total fuel flow multiplied by the time (hr.), which is 968 Ib. [818 x (71 + 60)]. Note: Do not attempt to use Fig. 22 to solve these problems, as Fig. 22 relates to landing, not cruise, wind components. Answer (A) is incorrect. The fuel consumption for a cruise en route time of 66, not 71, min. given a total fuel flow of 818 Ib./hr. is 900 Ib. Answer (B) is incorrect. The fuel consumption for a cruise en route time of 76, not 71, min. given a total fuel flow of 818 Ib./hr. is 1,030 Ib. .

questions.subsection: 10.11 Time, Fuel, and Distance to Descend
questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 26 on page 672.) What are the time and distance to descend from 16,500 feet to 3,500 feet?
questions.choice_c: 8.7 minutes, 33 NM.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Fig. 26 provides a chart to determine time, fuel, and distance required to descend. Find 16,500 ft. on the left edge of the chart, and move right horizontally to the solid diagonal line; then move down vertically to the bottom edge of the chart to determine a time of 11.1 min. Continue vertically down to the second reference line to determine a distance of 41 NM. Repeat the process for 3,500 ft. to determine a time of 2.4 min. and a distance of 8 NM. Thus, to descend from 16,500 ft. to 3,500 ft. will take 8.7 min. (11.1 — 2.4) and 33 NM (41 — 8). Answer (A) is incorrect. The time to descend from 16,500 ft. to 2,550, not 3,500, ft., is 9.3 min. Answer (B) is incorrect. The time to descend from 16,500 ft. to 3,000, not 3,500, ft., is 9.1 min.

questions.subsection: 10.11 Time, Fuel, and Distance to Descend
questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 26 on page 672.) What are the time and distance to descend from 23,000 feet to 600 feet with an average 15-knot headwind?
questions.choice_c: 14.9 minutes, 59 NM.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Fig. 26 provides a chart to determine time, fuel, and distance required to descend. The chart provides no adjustment for the 15-kt. headwind. Find 23,000 ft. on the left edge of the chart, and move right horizontally to the solid diagonal line; then move down vertically to the bottom edge of the chart to determine a time of 15.4 min. Continue vertically down to the second reference line to determine a distance of 62 NM. Repeat the process for 600 ft. to determine a time of 0.5 min. and a distance of 2 NM. Thus, to descend from 23,000 ft. to 600 ft. will take 14.9 min. (15.4 — 0.5) and 59 NM (61 — 2). Answer (A) is incorrect. The time to descend from 23,000 ft. to 1,700, not 600, ft., is 14.2 min. Answer (B) is incorrect. The time to descend from 23,000 ft. to 1,100, not 600, ft., is 14.6 min.

questions.subsection: 10.11 Time, Fuel, and Distance to Descend
questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 26 on page 672.) What are the distance and fuel consumption to descend from 22,000 feet to 4,500 feet?
questions.choice_c: 56 NM, 125 pounds.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Fig. 26 provides a chart to determine time, fuel, and distance required to descend. Find 22,000 ft. on the left edge of the chart, and move right horizontally to the solid diagonal line; then move down vertically below the bottom edge of the chart to the first reference line to determine a fuel consumption of 142 Ib. Continue vertically down to the second reference line to determine a distance of 58 NM. Repeat the process for 4,500 ft. to determine a fuel consumption of 30 Ib. and a distance of 10 NM. Thus, to descend from 22,000 ft. to 4,500 ft. will take 112 Ib. (142 — 30) of fuel and 48 NM (58 — 10). Answer (A) is incorrect. The distance to descend from 22,000 ft. to 6,500 ft., not 4,500 ft., is 44 NM. Answer (C) is incorrect. The distance to descend from 22,000 ft. to 800 ft., not 4,500 ft., is 56 NM.

questions.subsection: 10.11 Time, Fuel, and Distance to Descend
questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 26 on page 672.) What are the distance and fuel consumption to descend from 13,500 feet to 1,500 feet?
questions.choice_c: 38 NM, 100 pounds.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Fig. 26 provides a chart to determine time, fuel, and distance required to descend. Find 13,500 ft. on the left edge of the chart, and move right horizontally to the solid diagonal line; then move down vertically below the bottom edge of the chart to the first reference line to determine a fuel consumption of 90 Ib. Continue vertically down to the second reference line to determine a distance of 32.5 NM. Repeat the process for 1,500 ft. to determine a fuel consumption of 10 Ib. and a distance of 3.5 NM. Thus, to descend from 13,500 ft. to 1,500 ft. will take 80 Ib. (90 — 10) of fuel and 29 NM (32.5 — 3.5). Answer (A) is incorrect. The fuel consumption to descend from 13,500 ft. to 350, not 1,500, feet is 87 Ib. Answer (C) is incorrect. The distance to descend from 16,500, not 13,500, feet is 38 NM.

questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 26 on page 672.) What are the time and distance to descend from 18,000 feet to 2,500 feet?
questions.choice_c: 10.0 minutes, 36 NM. 10.12 Landing Distance
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Fig. 26 provides a chart to determine time, fuel, and distance required to descend. Find 18,000 ft. on the left edge of the chart, and move right horizontally to the solid diagonal line; then move down vertically to the bottom edge of the chart to determine a time of 12 min. Continue vertically down to the second reference line to determine a distance of 45 NM. Repeat the process for 2,500 ft. to determine a time of 1.7 min. and a distance of 6 NM. Thus, to descend from 18,000 ft. to 2,500 ft. will take 10.3 min. (12 —1.7) and 39 NM (45 - 6). Answer (B) is incorrect. The time to descend from 18,000 ft. to 3,200 ft., not 2,500 ft., is 9.8 min. Answer (C) is incorrect. thee to descend from 18,000 ft. to 3,000 ft., not 2,500 ft., is .O min.

questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 27 on page 677 and Figure 28 on page 678.) What is the landing distance over a 50- foot obstacle for Operating Conditions B-36?
questions.choice_c: 950 feet.
questions.explanation: Fig. 27 gives the following operating conditions for B-36: Pressure altitude (ft.) SL Temperature (OAT) +30°C Weight (Ib.) 16,000 Wind component (kt.) 20 HW Runway length (ft.) 4,000 Fig. 28 provides a chart to determine the normal landing distance. Find OAT +30°C at the bottom left of the chart and move up vertically to a pressure altitude of SL (sea level). Then move right horizontally to the first reference line. Move down and to the right (parallel to the guide line) to a weight of 16,000 Ib. Move right horizontally to the second reference line and move down (headwind) and to the right (parallel to the guide line) to 20 kt. Then move right horizontally to the third reference line and move up and to the right (parallel to the guide line) to the right edge of the chart for a 50-ft. obstacle. The landing distance is 1,900 ft. Answer (B) is incorrect. The landing distance at a weight of 13,000 Ib., not 16,000 Ib., is 1,625 ft. Answer (C) is incorrect. The ground roll, not landing, distance over a 50-ft. obstacle is 950 ft.

questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 27 on page 677 and Figure 28 on page 678.) What are the approach speed and ground roll when landing under Operating Conditions B-36?
questions.choice_c: 112 knots and 900 feet.
questions.explanation: Fig. 27 gives the following operating conditions for B-36: Pressure altitude (ft.) SL Temperature (OAT) +30°C Weight (Ib.) 16,000 Wind component (kt.) 20 HW Runway length (ft.) 4,000 Fig. 28 provides a chart to determine the normal landing distance. Find OAT +30°C at the bottom left of the chart and move up vertically to a pressure altitude of SL (sea level). Then move right horizontally to the first reference line. Move down and to the right (parallel to the guide line) to a weight of 16,000 Ib. Move right horizontally to the second reference line and move down (headwind) and to the right (parallel to the guide line) to 20 kt. Then move right horizontally to the edge of the chart. The ground roll distance is 950 ft. The box above the graph in Fig. 28 provides information on approach speed. Find the approach speed for both 16,100 Ib. (113 kt.) and 14,000 Ib. (107 kt.). Interpolate for 16,000 Ib. to determine an approach speed of 112.7 or 113 kt. Answer (B) is incorrect. The approximate total landing distance over a 50-ft. obstacle, not ground roll, is 1,950 ft. Answer (C) is incorrect. An approach speed of 112 kt. is fora weight of 15,750, not 16,000, Ib.

questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 27 on page 677 and Figure 28 on page 678.) What is the remaining runway length when stopped after landing over a 50-foot obstacle for Operating Conditions B-37?
questions.choice_c: 2,600 feet.
questions.explanation: Fig. 27 gives the following operating conditions for B-37: Pressure altitude (ft.) 1,000 Temperature (OAT) +16°C Weight (Ib.) 14,500 Wind component (kt.) 10 TW Runway length (ft.) 4,500 Fig. 28 provides a chart to determine the normal landing distance. Find OAT +16°C at the bottom left of the chart and move up vertically to a pressure altitude of 1,000 ft. Then move right horizontally to the first reference line. Move down and to the right (parallel to the guide line) to a weight of 14,500 Ib. Move right horizontally to the second reference line and move up (tailwind) and to the right (parallel to the guide line) to 10 kt. Then move right horizontally to the third reference line and move up and to the right (parallel to the guide line) to the right edge of the chart for a 50-ft. obstacle. The landing distance is 2,500 ft. The remaining runway length when stopped is the runway length minus the landing distance, or 2,000 ft. (4,500 — 2,500). Answer (A) is incorrect. The total landing, not runway remaining, distance over a 50-ft. obstacle is 2,500 ft. Answer (C) is incorrect. The remaining runway length with a 10-kt. headwind, not tailwind, is 2,600 ft.

questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 27 on page 677 and Figure 28 on page 678.) What are the approach speed and ground roll when landing under Operating Conditions B-37?
questions.choice_c: 107 knots and 1,350 feet.
questions.explanation: Fig. 27 gives the following operating conditions for B-37: Pressure altitude (ft.) 1,000 Temperature (OAT) +16°C Weight (Ib.) 14,500 Wind component (kt.) 10 TW Runway length (ft.) 4,500 Fig. 28 provides a chart to determine the normal landing distance. Find OAT +16°C at the bottom left of the chart and move up vertically to a pressure altitude of 1,000 ft. Then move right horizontally to the first reference line. Move down and to the right (parallel to the guide line) to a weight of 14,500 Ib. Move right horizontally to the second reference line and move up (tailwind) and to the right (parallel to the guide line) to 10 kt. Then move horizontally to the right edge of the chart. The ground roll distance is 1,400 ft. The box above the graph provides information on approach speed. Find the approach speeds for both 16,100 Ib. (113 kt.) and 14,000 Ib. (107 kt.). Interpolate for 14,500 Ib. to determine an approach speed of 108 kt. Answer (B) is incorrect. The ground roll with a 10-kt. headwind, not tailwind, is 900 ft. Answer (C) is incorrect. The approach speed at a weight of 14,000, not 14,500, Ib. is 107 kt.

questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 27 on page 677 and Figure 28 on page 678.) What is the landing distance over a 50- foot obstacle for Operating Conditions B-38?
questions.choice_c: 1,800 feet.
questions.explanation: Fig. 27 gives the following operating conditions for B-38: Pressure altitude (ft.) 2,000 Temperature (OAT) 0°Cc Weight (Ib.) 13,500 Wind component (kt.) 15 HW Runway length (ft.) 3,800 Fig. 28 provides a chart to determine the normal landing distance. Find OAT 0°C at the bottom left of the chart and move up vertically to a pressure altitude of 2,000 ft. Then move right horizontally to the first reference line. Move down and to the right (parallel to the guide line) to a weight of 13,500 Ib. Move right horizontally to the second reference line and move down (headwind) and to the right (parallel to the guide line) to 15 kt. Then move right horizontally to the third reference line and move up and to the right (parallel to the guide line) to the right edge of the chart for a 50-ft. obstacle. The landing distance is 1,700 ft. Answer (A) is incorrect. The landing distance over a 50-ft. obstacle in a calm wind, not a 15-kt. headwind, is 1,850 ft. Answer (C) is incorrect. The landing distance over a 50-ft. obstacle at a weight of 14,500, not 13,500, Ib. is 1,800 ft.

questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 27 on page 677 and Figure 28 on page 678.) What is the total runway used when touchdown is at the 1,000-foot marker for Operating Conditions B-38?
questions.choice_c: 1,800 feet.
questions.explanation: Fig. 27 gives the following operating conditions for B-38: Pressure altitude (ft.) 2,000 Temperature (OAT) 0°C Weight (Ib.) 13,500 Wind component (kt.) 15 HW Runway length (ft.) 3,800 Fig. 28 provides a chart to determine the normal landing distance. Find OAT 0°C at the bottom left of the chart and move up vertically to a pressure altitude of 2,000 ft. Then move right horizontally to the first reference line. Move down and to the right (parallel to the guide line) to a weight of 13,500 Ib. Move right horizontally to the second reference line and move down (headwind) and to the right (parallel to the guide line) to 15 kt. Then move right horizontally to the edge of the chart. The ground roll distance is 800 ft. The total runway used is the distance from the threshold of the runway to the touchdown point plus the ground roll distance or 1,800 ft. (1,000 + 800). Answer (A) is incorrect. The total runway used at a weight of 14,500, not 13,500, Ib. and a calm wind, not a 15-kt. headwind, is 2,000 ft. Answer (B) is incorrect. The total runway used with a 20-kt., not a 15-kt., headwind is 1,700 ft.

questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 27 on page 677 and Figure 28 on page 678.) What is the remaining runway length when stopped after landing over a 50-foot obstacle for Operating Conditions B-39?

The remaining runway length when stopped at a weight of 15,500, not 15,000, Ib., is 2,400 ft.

questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 44 on page 693.) What is the new CG if the weight is removed from the forward compartment under Loading Conditions WS-1?
questions.choice_c: 30.0 percent MAC.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Note that 2,500 Ib. is to be removed from the forward compartment. (See Fig. 44.) The forward compartment is Station 352.1. questions.question_text: You are rolling on takeoff in a 14 CFR Part 25 certified jet, and you see an engine oil pressure caution light as you approach V,. You should
questions.choice_a: abort the takeoff using max reverse thrust and braking.
questions.choice_b: abort the takeoff, understanding that you may have only asymmetrical reverse thrust available.
questions.choice_c: continue the takeoff and handle the light according to the checklist.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Abort the takeoff, understanding that you may have only asymmetrical reverse thrust available. Answer (A) is incorrect. Max reverse thrust may not be available. In the event of engine failure, you may only have asymmetrical reverse thrust. Answer (C) is incorrect. V; is the decision speed. Engine failure or certain circumstances below this airspeed should result in an aborted takeoff; above this speed, the takeoff run should be continued.

questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 51 below, and Figure 52 on page 708.) What is the total time from starting to the alternate through completing the approach for Operating Conditions L-1?
questions.choice_c: 29 minutes.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: The requirement is total time to fly to an alternate given certain operating conditions. Operating conditions L-1 on Fig. 51 are Distance (NAM) 110 Holding time at alternate 15 min. Note the use of NAM, which means you should ignore the wind component given in the operating conditions. See explanation of NAM on page 686. Using the alternate planning chart (Fig. 52), under 110 NAM, the time to the alternate is 29 min. Next, add the 15-min. holding time to determine that the total time from starting to the alternate through completing the approach is 44 min. (29 + 15). Answer (A) is incorrect. A time of 30 min. is to the alternate (excluding holding) for 120 NAM, not 110 NAM. Answer (C) is incorrect. A time of 29 min. has not been adjusted for the 15-min. holding time.

questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 51 below, and Figure 52 on page 710.) What is the approximate landing weight for Operating Conditions L-2?
questions.choice_c: 69,600 pounds.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Landing weight is required given a starting weight and information regarding operation to approach and landing. Landing weight will be starting weight less fuel burn. Consult Fig. 51 for operating conditions L-2, which indicate 70,000 Ib., 190 NAM, a 40-Kt. tailwind, and 15 min. holding. On Fig. 52 under 190 NAM, a fuel burn of 4,200 Ib. is given, which includes 15 min. of holding time per note 1 (at the bottom of Fig. 52). Note the use of NAM, which means you should ignore the wind component given in the operating conditions. See explanation of NAM on page 686. Thus, the landing weight is 65,800 Ib. (70,000 — 4,200). Answer (A) is incorrect. A weight of 65,200 Ib. assumes a fuel burn of 4,800 Ib., which relates to 250 NAM, not 190 NAM. Answer (C) is incorrect. A weight of 69,600 Ib. assumes a fuel burn of 400 Ib., which is unrealistic.

(Refer to Figure 53 on page 733, Figure 54 on page 731, and Figure 55 on page 732.) What is the takeoff EPR for Operating Conditions R-1?
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Refer to operating conditions R-1 in Fig. 53. Find pressure altitude using Fig. 54 at field elevation 100 ft. and altimeter setting 29.50 in. Pressure altitude = 500 ft. (100 + 400). Find the EPR by entering the takeoff EPR chart (Fig. 55). Move right horizontally in the OAT column to +50°F and then move down vertically to determine the temperature limit EPR of 2.04. Next, find the pressure altitude of 500 ft., which is between SL and 1,000 ft. Interpolate to determine that the pressure limit EPR at a pressure altitude of 500 ft. is 2.035. The takeoff EPR is the smaller of the two limits, which is 2.035. No EPR bleed corrections are necessary, and none of the other data in condition R-1 are relevant. Answer (A) is incorrect. This is the temperature limit, not takeoff, EPR. Answer (B) is incorrect. This is the pressure limit EPR for sea level, not 500 ft.

questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 53 on page 733, Figure 54 on page 731, and Figure 55 on page 732.) What is the takeoff safety speed for Operating Conditions R-1?
questions.choice_c: 133 knots.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Takeoff safety speed is V2. Refer to operating conditions R-1 in Fig. 53. Find pressure altitude using Fig. 54 at field elevation 100 ft. and altimeter setting 29.50 in. Pressure altitude = 500 ft. (100 + 400). On Fig. 55, enter V,, Ve, V2 chart under pressure altitude. Find —1 to 1 pressure altitude line; then proceed to the right to the +50°F OAT (+50°F is between —65°F and +91°F). Go down that column to flaps 15°, to 90,000 Ib. (90) gross weight, then right to V, (takeoff safety speed). V2 is 128 kt. Answer (B) is incorrect. This is the rotation (Vg) speed, not the safety speed. Answer (C) is incorrect. This is the V> speed using 5°, not 15°, flaps.

questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 53 on page 733, Figure 54 on page 731, and Figure 55 on page 732.) What is the takeoff EPR for Operating Conditions R-2?
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Refer to operating conditions R-2 in Fig. 53. Find pressure altitude using Fig. 54 at field elevation 4,000 ft. and coo et setting 1,032 mb. Pressure altitude = 3,500 ft. (4,000 — Find the EPR by entering the takeoff EPR chart (Fig. 55). Move right horizontally in the OAT column to —15°C and then move down vertically to determine the temperature limit EPR of 2.19. Next, find the pressure altitude of 3,500 ft., which is between 3,000 ft. and 4,000 ft. Interpolate to determine that the pressure limit EPR at a pressure altitude of 3,500 ft. is 2.19. Since both temperature and pressure EPR are the same, the takeoff EPR is 2.19. No EPR bleed air corrections are necessary, and none of the other data in condition R-2 are relevant. Answer (B) is incorrect. This is the temperature limit EPR at approximately —12°C, not -15°C. Answer (C) is incorrect. This is the pressure limit EPR at 3,000 ft., not 3,500 ft.

questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 53 on page 733, Figure 54 on page 731, and Figure 55 on page 732.) What is the takeoff EPR for Operating Conditions R-3?
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Refer to operating conditions R-3 in Fig. 53. Find pressure altitude using Fig. 54 at field elevation 950 ft. and Peal setting 29.40 in. Pressure altitude = 1,450 ft. (950 + Find the EPR by entering the takeoff EPR chart (Fig. 55). Move right horizontally in the OAT column to +59°F and then move down vertically to determine the temperature limit EPR of 2.01. Next, find the pressure altitude of 1,450 ft., which is between 1,000 ft. and 2,000 ft. Interpolate to determine that the pressure limit EPR at a pressure altitude of 1,450 ft. is 2.083. The takeoff EPR is the smaller of the two limits, which is 2.01. The EPR bleed correction for the air-conditioning OFF is +.03, so the corrected takeoff EPR is 2.04 (2.01 + .03). Answer (A) is incorrect. This is the takeoff EPR with the air- conditioning ON, not OFF. Answer (B) is incorrect. This is the pressure limit, not takeoff, EPR.

questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 53 on page 733, Figure 54 on page 731, and Figure 55 on page 732.) What is the takeoff EPR for Operating Conditions R-4?

Pressure altitude = 1,900 ft. (2,000 — Find the EPR by entering the takeoff EPR chart (Fig. 55). Move right horizontally in the OAT column to 0°C and then move down vertically to determine the temperature limit EPR of 2.11. Next, find the pressure altitude of 1,900 ft., which is between 1,000 ft. and 2,000 ft. Interpolate to determine that the pressure limit EPR at a pressure altitude of 1,900 ft. is 2.105. The takeoff EPR is the smaller of the two limits, which is 2.105. No EPR bleed corrections are necessary, and none of the other data in condition R-4 are relevant. Answer (A) is incorrect. This is the pressure limit EPR for 1,000 ft., not 1,900 ft. Answer (C) is incorrect. This is the temperature limit, not takeoff, EPR.

questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 53 on page 733, Figure 54 on page 731, and Figure 55 on page 732.) What is the takeoff EPR for Operating Conditions R-5? Ax. 1,98 B.1.95 CG; 1.96
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Refer to operating conditions R-5 in Fig. 53. Find pressure altitude using Fig. 54 at field elevation 50 ft. and altimeter setting 30.15 in. Pressure altitude = —150 ft. (50 — 200). Find the EPR by entering the takeoff EPR chart (Fig. 55). Move right horizontally in the OAT column to +95°F and then move down vertically to determine the temperature limit EPR of 1.95. Next, find the pressure altitude of —150 ft., which is between SL and —1,000 ft. Interpolate to determine that the pressure limit EPR at a pressure altitude of —150 ft. is 2.003. Use the smaller of the two limits, which is 1.95. The EPR bleed air correction for the air-conditioning OFF is +.03. The takeoff EPR is 1.98 (1.95 + .03). None of the other data in condition R-5 are relevant. Answer (B) is incorrect. This is the takeoff EPR with the air- conditioning ON, not OFF. Answer (C) is incorrect. This is the pressure limit EPR at —1,000 ft., not —150 ft.

questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 53 on page 733, Figure 54 on page 731, and Figure 55 on page 732.) What is the rotation speed for Operating Conditions R-2?
questions.choice_c: 146 knots.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Rotation speed is Ve. Refer to operating conditions R-2 in Fig. 53. Find pressure altitude using Fig. 54 at field elevation 4,000 ft. and altimeter setting 1,032 mb. Pressure altitude = 3,500 ft. (4,000 — 500). On Fig. 55, enter the V;, Ve, V2 chart under pressure altitude. Find the 3-to-5 pressure altitude line; then proceed to the right to the —15°C OAT (—15°C is between —54°C and —-8°C). Go down that column to the flaps 5° and 110,000 Ib. (110) gross weight, then right to Vp (rotation speed). Ve is 146 kt. Answer (A) is incorrect. This is the Vz speed using the pressure altitude line of 5 to 7, not 3 to 5. Answer (B) is incorrect. This is the Vg speed using 1°, not 5°, flaps.

questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 53 on page 733, Figure 54 on page 731, and Figure 55 on page 732.) What are V,, Ve, and V2 speeds for Operating Conditions R-3?
questions.choice_c: 136, 138, and 143 knots.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: The takeoff decision speed is V;, the rotation speed is Vp, and the takeoff safety speed is V2. Refer to the operating conditions R-3 in Fig. 53. Find pressure altitude using Fig. 54 at field elevation 950 ft. and altimeter setting 29.40 in. Pressure altitude = 1,450 ft. (950 + 500). On Fig. 55, enter the V;, Vr, V2 chart under pressure altitude. Find the 1-to-3 pressure altitude line; then proceed to the right to the +59°F OAT (+59°F is between +47°F and +89°F). Go down that column to 5° flaps and gross weight of 100,000 (100) Ib. V, is 136 kt., Ve is 138 kt., and V2 is 142 kt. V, adjustments must be made for wind and runway slope as indicated at the bottom of the chart. Add 1 kt. for the 20-kt. headwind and add 1 kt. for the 1% up-runway slope. The adjusted V, is 138 kt. (136 + 1 + 1). Answer (A) is incorrect. V; and Vp of 143 kt. and V2 of 147 kt. are the values using 1°, not 5°, flaps. Answer (C) is incorrect. V, of 136 kt. has not been adjusted for the wind component or the runway slope.

questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 53 on page 733, Figure 54 on page 731, and Figure 55 on page 732.) What are rotation and V, bug speeds for Operating Conditions R-5?
questions.choice_c: 134 and 141 knots.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Rotation speed is Vp, and takeoff safety speed is V,. Refer to operating condition R-5 in Fig. 53. Find pressure altitude using Fig. 54 at field elevation 50 ft. and altimeter setting 30.15 in. Pressure altitude = —150 ft. (50 — 200). On Fig. 55, enter the V;, Vr, V2 chart under pressure altitude. Find the —1-to-1 pressure altitude line; then proceed to the right to +95°F OAT (+95°F is between 92°F and 105°F). Go down that column to flaps 1°. Since the gross weight is 95,000 (95) Ib., you must interpolate between 90 and 100 gross weight. Ve at 90,000 Ib. is 134 and at 100,000 Ib. is 143. Interpolate to determine the actual Vp (rotation) speed is 138 kt. The same procedure provides the V2 speed of 143 kt. Answer (B) is incorrect. Ve of 136 kt. is for a gross weight below 95,000 Ib., and V2 of 138 kt. is for a gross weight of 90,000 Ib. Answer (C) is incorrect. Vp at 90,000 Ib. is 134 kt., and 141 kt. is V2 below 95,000 Ib.

questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 53 on page 733, Figure 54 on page 731, and Figure 55 on page 732.) What are critical engine failure and takeoff safety speeds for Operating Conditions R-4?
questions.choice_c: 122 and 130 knots.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: V, is the minimum speed in the takeoff, following a failure of the critical engine at Ver, at which the pilot can continue the takeoff and achieve the required height above the takeoff surface within the takeoff distance. The takeoff safety speed is V2. Refer to operating condition R-4 in Fig. 53. Find pressure altitude using Fig. 54 at field elevation 2,000 ft. and altimeter setting 1,017 mb. Pressure altitude = 1,900 ft. (2,000 — 100). On Fig. 55, enter the V;, Vp, V2 chart under pressure altitude. Find the 1-to-3 pressure altitude line; then proceed to the right to the 0°C OAT (0°C is between —54°C and +8°C). Go down that column to flaps 1° and gross weight of both 80 and 90. You will find V, at 80 of 122 kt. and at 90 of 131 kt. Because the actual gross weight is 85 (85,000 Ib.), interpolate to determine the correct V; speed of 127 kt. Interpolate for V, to determine a speed of 134 kt. Next, adjust V, for wind and slope. Subtract 2 kt. for the 10-kt. tailwind and subtract 2 kt. for the 2% down-runway slope. Thus, V, is 123 kt. (127 — 2 — 2). _ Answer (A) is incorrect. V, at 90,000 Ib. before any adjustments is 131 kt., and 133 kt. is Vp (not V2) at 90,000, not 85,000, Ib. Answer (C) is incorrect. V; at 80,000 Ib. before adjusting for the wind and runway slope is 122 kt., and 130 Kt. is V2 at 80,000, not 85,000, Ib. .

questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 56 on page 737, Figure 57 on page 737, and Figure 58 on page 738.) What is the ground distance covered during en route climb for Operating Conditions V-1?
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Refer to V-1 in Fig. 56. The chart in Fig. 58 is used for en route climb ISA +10°C. Find pressure altitude of 33,000 ft. at the left edge of the chart; then move right horizontally to the brake release weight of 110,000 Ib. The distance is 154 NM (lower left number), and the time is 26 min. (upper left number). Adjust the distance for the 20-kt. average headwind component by multiplying the wind by the time (in hours) to determine a distance of 9 NM [20 x (26 + 60)]. A headwind will reduce the distance; thus, the ground distance covered is 145 NM (154 — 9). Answer (B) is incorrect. This is the ground distance covered with an average headwind component of 40, not 20, kt. Answer (C) is incorrect. This is the ground distance covered for a cruise pressure altitude of 32,000, not 33,000, feet.

questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 56 on page 737, Figure 57 on page 737, and Figure 58 on page 738.) What is the ground distance covered during en route climb for Operating Conditions V-2?
questions.choice_c: 69 NM.

Find pressure altitude of 27,000 ft. at the left edge of the chart; then move right horizontally to the brake release weight of 95,000 Ib. The distance is 65 NM (lower left number), and the time is 13 min. (upper left number). Adjust the distance for the 20-kt. average tailwind component by multiplying the wind by the time (in hours) to determine a distance of 4 NM [20 x (13 + 60)]. A tailwind will increase the distance; thus, the ground distance covered is 69 NM (65 + 4). Answer (A) is incorrect. This is the approximate ground distance covered at ISA +10°C, not ISA. Answer (B) is incorrect. This is the ground distance covered before adjusting for the 20-kt. tailwind component.

questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 56 on page 737, Figure 57 on page 737, and Figure 58 on page 738.) How much fuel is burned during en route climb for Operating Conditions V-1?

Find pressure altitude of 33,000 ft. at the left edge of the chart; then move right horizontally to the brake release weight of 110,000 Ib. The fuel used is 4,100 Ib. (upper right number). Adjust the fuel burned for the 2,000-ft. airport elevation (at the bottom of the chart), which is —100 Ib. Thus, the fuel burned during the en route climb is 4,000 Ib. (4,100 — 100). Answer (A) is incorrect. This amount is the fuel burned before the adjustment is made for high-elevation airports. Answer (B) is incorrect. This amount is the fuel burned during the en route climb at ISA, not ISA +10°C.

A headwind will decrease the distance; thus, the ground distance covered is 79 NM. Answer (A) is incorrect. This is the ground distance covered for a 30-kt. tailwind, not headwind. Answer (C) is incorrect. This is the approximate ground distance covered at a pressure altitude of 30,000, not 35,000, feet.

questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 56 on page 737, Figure 57 on page 737, and Figure 58 on page 738.) What is the ground distance covered during en route climb for Operating Conditions V-4?

questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 56 on page 737, Figure 57 on page 737, and Figure 58 on page 738.) What is the ground distance covered during en route climb for Operating Conditions V-5?

The distance is 70 NM (lower left number), and the time is 13 min. (upper left number). Adjust the distance for the 40-kt. average headwind component by multiplying the wind by the time (in hours) to _ determine a distance of 9 NM [40 x (13 + 60)]. A headwind will decrease the distance; thus the ground distance covered is 61 NM (70 - 9). Answer (A) is incorrect. This is the ground distance covered before adjusting for the 40-kt. headwind. Answer (B) is incorrect. This is the ground distance covered at a pressure altitude of 27,000, not 31,000, ft.

questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 56 on page 737, Figure 57 on page 737, and Figure 58 on page 738.) What is the aircraft weight at the top of climb for Operating Conditions V-3?
questions.choice_c: 82,200 pounds.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Refer to V-3 in Fig. 56. The chart in Fig. 57 is used for en route climb at ISA. Find pressure altitude of 35,000 ft. at the left edge of the chart; then move right horizontally to the brake release weight of 85,000 Ib. The fuel burned is 2,600 Ib. (upper right number). Adjust the fuel burned for the 2,000-ft. airport elevation (at bottom of chart), which is —100 Ib. The total fuel burned during the climb is 2,500 Ib. The aircraft weight at the top of the climb is the brake release weight minus the fuel burned during climb, or 82,500 Ib. (85,000 — 2,500). Answer (A) is incorrect. The aircraft weight at the top of the climb is 82,100 Ib. when 2,900, not 2,500, Ib. of fuel is burned during the en route climb. Answer (C) is incorrect. The aircraft weight at the top of the climb is 82,200 Ib. when 2,800 Ib. of fuel is burned during a climb at a temperature of ISA +10°C, not ISA.

questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 56 on page 737, Figure 57 on page 737, and Figure 58 on page 738.) How much fuel is burned during en route climb for Operating Conditions V-2?

Find pressure altitude of 27,000 ft. at the left edge of the chart; then move right horizontally to the brake release weight of 95,000 Ib. The fuel burned is 2,400 Ib. (upper right number). Adjust the fuel burned for the 3,000-ft. airport elevation (at bottom of chart), which is interpolated between 2,000 ft. and 4,000 ft. to be -150 Ib. Thus, the fuel burned during the en route climb is 2,250 Ib. Answer (B) is incorrect. The fuel burned during an en route climb at ISA +10°C, not ISA, and with no adjustment made for the airport elevation is 2,600 Ib. Answer (C) is incorrect. The fuel burned during an en route climb before adjusting for the airport elevation is 2,400 Ib.

(2,400 — 300). _ Aircraft weight at the top of the climb is the brake release weight minus fuel burned, or 102,900 Ib. (105,000 — 2,100). Answer (B) is incorrect. questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 59 below, and Figure 60 on page 741.) What is the max climb EPR for Operating Conditions T-1? AL 1-82
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Refer to operating conditions T-1 in Fig. 59. Use the Max Climb and Max Continuous EPR chart in Fig. 60. Find the Max Climb column at the left edge of the chart. Move down vertically to 10,000 ft. (SL to 30,000) and move right horizontally to the total air temperature (TAT) column of +10°C. The temperature limit EPR is 1.90. At the bottom center of the chart, find the pressure limit EPR altitude of 10,000 ft. (5,660 ft. and above). The number above is the pressure limit EPR of 2.30. Use the smaller of the two limits, which is 1.90. Next, apply the bleed air correction for engine anti-ice ON, which is —.08. Thus, the max climb EPR is 1.82. Answer (B) is incorrect. This is the max continuous, not climb, EPR. Answer (C) is incorrect. This is the max continuous, not climb, EPR without the bleed air correction for the engine anti-ice ON.

questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 59 below, and Figure 60 on page 741.) What is the max continuous EPR for Operating Conditions T-2?
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Refer to operating conditions T-2 in Fig. 59. Use the Max Climb and Max Continuous chart in Fig. 60. Find the Max Cont. column at the left side of the chart and move down vertically to the altitude of 5,000 ft. (1,500 to 20,000). Move right horizontally to the TAT column of 0°C to determine the temperature limit EPR of 2.10. At the bottom center of the same chart, find the pressure limit altitude of 5,000 ft. (4,000) to determine the pressure limit EPR of 2.20. Use the smaller of the two limits, which is 2.10. Make the necessary bleed air corrections for the engine anti- ice ON (—.08), wing anti-ice “2 engines ON” (—.04), and the air- conditioning OFF (+.04). Thus, the max continuous EPR is 2.02 (2.10 — .08 — .04 + .04). Answer (A) is incorrect. This is the max continuous EPR before making the necessary bleed air corrections. Answer (B) is incorrect. This is the max continuous temperature limit EPR for an altitude from SL to 1,500 ft. and 20,000 ft. to 30,000 ft., not 5,000 ft.

questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 59 below, and Figure 60 on page 741.) What is the max cruise EPR for Operating Conditions T-3? Aa 2:14

Make the necessary bleed air corrections for the engine anti- ice ON (-.08), and the wing anti-ice “2 engine ON” (—.04). Thus, the max cruise EPR is 1.90. Answer (A) is incorrect. This is the max cruise EPR at an altitude of either 35,000 ft. or 37,000 ft., not 25,000 ft., and no bleed air corrections applied. Answer (B) is incorrect. questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 61 above, and Figure 62 on page 742.) What is the trip time for Operating Conditions X-1?
questions.choice_c: 4 hours.

Find the trip distance of 2,000 NM at the bottom of the chart, and move up vertically to the reference line. Move down (tailwind) and to the left (parallel to the guide line) to 50 kt. Move up vertically to the second set of pressure altitude lines to 27,000 ft. (27). Move left horizontally to the reference line; then move down and to the left (parallel to the guide line) to +10°C. Then move horizontally to the left edge of the chart to determine a trip time of 4 hr. Answer (A) is incorrect. A trip time of 4 hr. 5 min. is for a temperature of ISA, not ISA +10°C. Answer (B) is incorrect. The trip time in calm wind, not a 50-kt. tailwind, is 4 hr. 15 min.

questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 73 on page 761 and Figure 75 on page 761.) What is the go-around EPR for Operating Conditions L-1?
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Refer to operating conditions L-1 in Fig. 73. The top portion in Fig. 75 provides a chart to determine the go- around EPR. Find the TAT column on the left side of the chart, and move right horizontally to the temperature of +15°C. Move down vertically to determine the temperature limit EPR of 2.00. At the bottom of the chart, find the pressure altitude of 500 ft., which is between SL and 1,000 ft. Interpolate to determine that the pressure limit EPR at a pressure altitude of 500 ft. is 2.01. Use the smaller of the two limits, which is 2.00. Make the necessary EPR bleed corrections as indicated in the top right corner of the chart. The adjustment for the air- conditioning OFF is +.03; thus the go-around EPR is 2.03 (2.00 + 03). Answer (A) is incorrect. This is the pressure limit, not the go- around, EPR. Answer (C) is incorrect. This is the pressure limit EPR (2.01), not the lower temperature limit EPR (2.00), with the appropriate EPR bleed correction.

questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 73 on page 761 and Figure 75 on page 761.) What is the go-around EPR for Operating Conditions L-3?
