At the bottom of the chart, find the pressure altitude of 2,500 ft., which is between 2,000 and 3,000 ft. Interpolate to determine that the pressure limit EPR at a pressure altitude of 2,500 ft. is 2.12. Use the smaller of the two limits, which is 2.12. ___ Make the necessary EPR bleed corrections as indicated in the top right corner of the chart. The adjustment for the one- engine wing anti-ice ON is —.06; thus, the go-around EPR is 2.06 (2.12 — .06). Answer (B) is incorrect. This is the go-around EPR when the temperature limit EPR is used in error. The smaller of the two limits (in this question, the pressure limit) must be used. Answer (C) is incorrect. This is the go-around EPR for two- PROG, not one, wing anti-ice ON and the air-conditioning OFF, no ‘

questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 73 on page 761 and Figure 75 on page 761.) What is the go-around EPR for Operating Conditions L-2?
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Refer to operating conditions L-2 in Fig. 73. The top portion in Fig. 75 provides a chart to determine the go- around EPR. Find the reported OAT column on the left side of the chart, and move right horizontally to the temperature of +27°F. Move down vertically to determine the temperature limit EPR of 2.10. At the bottom of the chart, find the pressure altitude of 3,100 ft., which is between 3,000 and 4,000 ft. Interpolate to determine that the pressure limit EPR at a pressure altitude of 3,100 ft. is 2.115. Use the smaller of the two limits, which is 2.10. Make the necessary EPR bleed corrections as indicated in the top right corner of the chart. The adjustment for the two- engine wing anti-ice ON is —.04; thus, the go-around EPR is 2.06 (2.10 — .04). Answer (A) is incorrect. This is the pressure limit, not go- around, EPR. Answer (B) is incorrect. This is the temperature limit EPR without the EPR bleed correction for the two-engine wing anti-ice ON.

questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 73 on page 761, Figure 74 on page 763, and Figure 75 on page 761.) What is Veer for Operating Conditions L-1?
questions.choice_c: 145 knots.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Refer to operating conditions L-1 in Fig. 73. Fig. 74 provides a wind component chart, and Fig. 75 provides a chart to determine the landing speed (Veer). Find the gross weight of 100,000 (100) Ib. at the left side of the landing speed chart (Fig. 75, lower right), and move right horizontally to the 30° flap setting column to determine Vper of 135 kt. At the bottom of the chart, the note states the wind factor (1/2 of the headwind component) is added to Vper. The headwind component is determined by using the chart in Fig. 74. Find the 50° angle between the wind direction and runway (350-300), and move up and to the right to the 20-kt. wind velocity arc. Then move left horizontally to the left edge of the chart to determine a headwind component of 13 kt. Thus, the wind factor is 6.5 kt. (13 + 2). The corrected Veer is 142 kt. (135 + 7). Answer (B) is incorrect. Veer is 144 kt. with 25°, not 30°, flap position and 0, not 13, kt. headwind component. Answer (C) is incorrect. Veer is 145 kt. with a 20-kt., not 13-kt., headwind component.

questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 73 on page 761, Figure 74 on page 763, and Figure 75 on page 761.) What is the reference speed for Operating Conditions L-2?
questions.choice_c: 148 knots.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Refer to operating conditions L-2 in Fig. 73. Fig. 74 provides a wind component chart, and Fig. 75 provides a chart to determine the landing speed (Vper). Find the gross weight of 95,000 (95) Ib. at the left side of the landing speed chart (Fig. 75, lower right), and move right horizontally to the 25° flap setting column to determine Vper of 140 kt. At the bottom of the chart, the note states the wind factor (1/2 of the headwind component) is added to Vper. The headwind component is determined by using the chart in Fig. 74. Find the 50° angle between the wind direction and runway [(360-350) + 40], and move up and to the right to the 15-kt. wind velocity arc. Then move left horizontally to the left edge of the chart to determine a headwind component of 9.5 kt. Thus, the wind factor is 4.75 kt. (9.5 + 2). The corrected Vper is 145 kt. (140 + 5). Answer (A) is incorrect. Veer is 140 kt. before the wind factor of 5 kt. is added. Answer (C) is incorrect. Vaer is 148 kt. using the wind velocity, not headwind component, of 15 kt.

questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 73 on page 761 and Figure 75 on page 761.) What is the go-around EPR for Operating Conditions L-4?
questions.explanation: ube DISCUSSION: Refer to operating conditions L-4 in Fig. 73. The top portion in Fig. 75 provides a chart to determine the go- around EPR. Find the TAT column on the left side of the chart, and move right horizontally to the temperature of -10°C. Move down vertically to determine the temperature limit EPR of 2.16. At the bottom of the chart, find the pressure altitude of 2,100 ft., which is between 2,000 and 3,000 ft. Interpolate to determine that the pressure limit EPR at a pressure altitude of 2,100 ft. is 2.096. Use the smaller of the two limits, which is 2.096. Make the necessary EPR bleed corrections as indicated in the top right corner of the chart. The adjustment for the two- engine wing anti-ice ON is —.04; thus, the go-around EPR is 2.056 (2.096 — .04). Answer (B) is incorrect. This is the go-around EPR if the temperature, not pressure, limit is the lower of the two EPR limits. Answer (C) is incorrect. This is the EPR before the EPR bleed correction for the two-engine wing anti-ice ON.

questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 73 on page 761, Figure 74 on page 763, and Figure 75 on page 761.) What is Veer +10 for Operating Conditions L-4?
questions.choice_c: 148 knots.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Refer to operating conditions L-4 in Fig. 73. Fig. 74 provides a wind component chart, and Fig. 75 provides a chart to determine the landing speed (Vper). Find the gross weight of 105,000 (105) Ib. at the left side of the landing speed chart (Fig. 75, lower right), and move right horizontally to the 40° flap setting column to determine Veer of 134 kt. At the bottom of the chart, the note states that the wind factor (1/2 of the headwind component) is added to Vrer. The headwind component is determined by using the chart in Fig. 74. Find the 50° angle between the wind direction and runway [(360 — 340) + 30], and move up and to the right to the 10-kt. wind velocity arc. Then move horizontally to the left edge of the chart to determine a headwind component of 6.5 kt. Thus, the wind factor is 3.25 kt. (6.5 + 2). The corrected Vref is 138 kt. (134 + 4). Thus, Veer + 10 is 148 kt. (138 + 10). Answer (A) is incorrect. Veer + 10 is 152 kt. at a flap setting of 30°, not 40°. Answer (B) is incorrect. Veer, NOt Veer + 10, is 138 kt.

questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 73 on page 761, Figure 74 on page 763, and Figure 75 on page 761.) What is the maneuvering speed for Operating Conditions L-5?
questions.choice_c: 130 knots.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Refer to operating conditions L-5 in Fig. 73. Fig. 74 provides a wind component chart, and Fig. 75 provides a chart to determine the maneuvering speed. Find the flap position of 30° at the left side of the flap extension/maneuvering speed chart (Fig. 75, lower left), and move right to the normal maneuver column to determine the maneuvering speed of Vere. Use the landing speed chart to determine Vper. Find the gross weight of 85,000 (85) Ib. at the left side of the landing speed chart (Fig. 75, lower right), and move right horizontally to the 30° flap setting column to determine Ver of 124 kt. At the bottom of the chart, the note states the wind factor (1/2 of the headwind component) is added to Vrer. ___ The headwind component is determined by using the chart in Fig. 74. Find the 40° angle between the wind direction and runway (130 — 90), and move up and to the right to the 15-kt. wind velocity arc. Then move horizontally to the left edge of the chart to determine a headwind component of 11.5 kt. Thus, the wind factor is 5.75 kt. (11.5 + 2). The corrected Vpe¢ is 130 kt. (124 + 6). Answer (A) is incorrect. Veer is 124 kt. before the wind factor of 6 kt. (1/2 of the headwind component) is added. Answer (B) is incorrect. Veer is 137 kt. when the wind velocity, not the headwind component, is used to determine the wind factor.

questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 73 on page 761 and Figure 75 on page 761.) What is the go-around EPR for Operating Conditions L-5?
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Refer to operating conditions L-5 in Fig. 73. The top portion in Fig. 75 provides a chart to determine the go- around EPR. Find the reported OAT column on the left side of the chart, and move right horizontally to the temperature of +55°F. Move down vertically to determine the temperature limit EPR of 2.00. At the bottom of the chart, find the pressure altitude of 1,200 ft., which is between 1,000 and 2,000 ft. Interpolate to determine that the pressure limit EPR at a pressure altitude of 1,200 ft. is 2.05. Use the smaller of the two limits, which is 2.00. No EPR bleed corrections are necessary; thus the go-around EPR is 2.00. Answer (B) is incorrect. This is the go-around EPR if the pressure, not temperature, limit is the lower of the two limits, the air-conditioning bleed air is OFF, not ON, and the two-engine wing anti-ice is ON, not OFF. Answer (C) is incorrect. This is the pressure limit, not go-around, EPR.

questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 73 on page 761, Figure 74 on page 763, and Figure 75 on page 761.) What is the maneuvering speed for operating conditions L-4?
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Refer to operating conditions L-4 in Fig. 73. Fig. 74 provides a wind component chart and Fig. 75 provides a chart to determine the maneuvering speed. Find the flap position of 40° at the left side of the flap extension/maneuvering speed chart (Fig. 75, lower left), and move right to the normal maneuver column to determine the maneuvering speed of Vrer. Use the landing speed chart to determine Vper. Find the gross weight of 105,000 (105) Ib. at the left side of the landing speed chart (Fig. 75, lower right) and move right horizontally to the 40° flap setting column to determine Vper of 134 kt. At the bottom of the chart, the note states the wind factor (1/2 of the headwind component) is added to Ver. The headwind component is determined by using the chart in Fig. 74. Find the 50° angle between the wind direction and runway (30° — 340°), and move down and to the left to the 10-kt. wind velocity arc. Then move horizontally to the left edge of the chart to determine a headwind component of 7 kt. Thus, the wind factor is 3.5 kt. (7 + 2). The corrected Vper is 137.5 kt. (134 + 3.5). Answer (B) is incorrect. A speed of 130 kt. would be determined if the wind correction factor were subtracted rather than added to Ver. Answer (C) is incorrect. A speed of 127 kt. would be determined if the total wind speed, rather than the wind correction factor, were subtracted rather than added to Vper.

questions.question_text: Arriving over the runway threshold 50 ft. high and 10 knots over Veer will increase landing distance on dry runway by
questions.choice_c: 5,100 ft. END OF STUDY UNIT
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: An extra 10 knots increases the landing distance by 10 (10 x 250 = 2,500 ft). For each knot, you increase stopping distance by 20-30 feet (30 x 10 = 300 ft.). For each 50 feet high, you would add 1,000 ft. to the landing distance. Thus, the landing distance is increased by 3,800 ft. (2,500 + 300 + 1,000). Answer (A) is incorrect. The pilot must take into consideration that excess approach speed carried through the threshold window and onto the runway will increase the minimum stopping distance. Runway conditions, airspeed, and height above threshold all have to be factored into the landing distance. The landing distance is increased by 3,800 ft. (2,500 + 300 + 1,000), not 1,500 ft. Answer (C) is incorrect. The landing distance is increased by 3,800 ft. (2,500 + 300 + 1,000), not 5,100 ft.

With the load weights shown in Figure 414, you fill the fuel tanks to the maximum fuel to remain under maximum gross weight and compute the center of gravity. Your computations indicate that
questions.choice_a: ata CG of 200.1, your loading is satisfactory for flight operations.
questions.choice_b: ataCG of 180.19, you need to redistribute your loads.
questions.choice_c: ata CG of 190.27, you only need to change the cargo pod loading.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Refer to Fig. 414 to fill in the required information. Use the first column by replacing the pilot weight and moment and the TO (takeoff) weight and moment. It is not necessary to go beyond the pilot section of Fig. 411. Refer to Fig. 405 and note the maximum ramp weight (gross weight) is 8,785 lbs. Refer to Fig. 410 and note the 200-Ib. pilot has a 27.1 moment. Refer to Fig. 411 and note that full fuel is 2,224 Ibs. with a 451.7 moment. Fill in the pilot weight of 200 Ibs. and a 27.1 moment. Fill in 2,224 Ibs. of full fuel with a 451.7 moment. The calculated ramp weight is 7,429 Ibs. with a 1,408.2 moment. The CG is calculated by taking the total moment and dividing by the aircraft weight. The moment is inch- pound + 1,000; therefore, we have to multiply 1,408.2 by 1,000 to get 14,082,000. Divide this by the total ramp weight (14,082,000 * 7,429 = 189.554 or 189.6). Refer to Fig. 415 and note the aircraft is slightly out of CG. This can be brought into CG by changing the cargo pod loading. _ Answer (A) is incorrect. A CG of 200.1 would require a pilot’s weight to be slightly over 170 Ibs. Answer (B) is incorrect. At a CG of 180.19, the pilot would have to weigh more than 200 Ibs.

questions.subsection: 1.94 6. (Refer to Figure 440 on page 815.) With a
questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 438 on page 814.) With an actual runway length of 6,400 feet with 8° flaps, a 1.% downslope, a 200 foot clearway, and 4 knots of tailwind, the Reference A is IN PAV BameZ202
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Locate the runway length scale on the left side of Fig. 438. At the 6,400-ft. mark, draw a straight line to the clearway REF LINE. From this point, draw a diagonal straight line to the 200-ft. clearway line. The scale in feet is labeled CLEARWAY (FT). From this point, draw a straight line to the runway slope REF LINE. There is a downhill slope of 1%. From the runway slope REF LINE, draw a straight diagonal line to the -1 slope line. From this point, draw a straight line to the wind REF LINE. There is a tailwind of 4 kt. From the wind REF LINE, draw a diagonal line to the -4 (tailwind side) of the graph. From this point, draw a straight line to the Reference A scale. The drawn line should be at or near 1.94 of the Reference A scale. Answer (A) is incorrect. If the clearway reference was not used and the calculation line was drawn straight to the first REF LINE, the answer would be 2.12. Answer (B) is incorrect. The figure of 2.02 would require an actual runway length of 6,600 ft.

questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 440 on page 815.) With a Reference A of 3.00 and Reference B of 28.5, the takeoff weight is limited to
questions.choice_c: 75,000 pounds.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Refer to Fig. 440. Draw a straight line from 3.00 on the Reference A scale, straight up above the EXAMPLE A dashed line. From the left side of the chart on the Reference B scale, draw a straight line along the 28.5 line to the first REF LINE. From this point, draw a diagonal line, maintaining the proper curvature of the line, and stop at the 3.00 Reference A that was drawn first. From this point, draw a straight line across to the right to the Aircraft Weight scale. The line should be on 76,500 pounds. Answer (A) is incorrect. A takeoff weight of 78,500 pounds would require a Reference B of 29.2. Answer (C) is incorrect. A takeoff weight of 75,000 pounds would require a Reference B of 28.0.

questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 472 on page 817.) With a gross weight of 54,500 pounds, the final take-off climb speed is
questions.choice_c: 148 knots.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: At the bottom of Fig. 472, find the weight of 54,500 pounds. From this point, draw a line straight up to the diagonal reference line. From this point, draw a line straight across the left to the take-off climb speed scale. The line should have intersected the 145 IAS. Answer (A) is incorrect. A final take-off climb speed of 142 knots would require a gross weight of 52,250 pounds. Answer (C) is incorrect. A final take-off climb speed of 148 knots would require a gross weight of 56,500 pounds. Answer (A) is the best answer. (POH/AFM) DISCUSSION: Fig. 321 gives you the airport altitude (elevation) of 6,535 feet. On Fig. 471, you will find the given temperature of -5°C on the left side of the chart. Draw a straight line from the -5° point on the bottom of the graph up to the altitude about 1/4 way between the 6 and the 8 (6,000- 8,000 feet). From that point, draw a straight line to the right until you reach the first reference line. From that point, draw a straight angled line down to the right to the next reference line. The line you just drew will then follow the reference line angled up and to the right. The weight given is 49,000 pounds. This is across the bottom right of the graph. Mark a spot on the second hash mark to the right of 48. This is 49,000 pounds. From that point, draw a straight line up to the reference line you drew your last line to. From that point, draw a straight line to the right to the V2-KT IAS reference line. Your line should intersect the V2-KT IAS reference line a little more than halfway between 115 and 120, or very close to 117.5. The best answer is 118 knots. Answer (B) is incorrect. A speed of 120 knots would require a weight of 51,000 Ib. Answer (C) is incorrect. A speed of 122 knots would require a weight of 52,500 Ib.

Note where the STRUCTURAL LIMIT line is in regards to the maximum weight. Draw a straight line from the bottom of the chart to the top of the chart along the 40°C line. Now, interpolate where 2,643 ft. would be located between 2,000 and 3,000 ft. on the Airfield Altitude scale. From this intersecting point, draw a straight line to the right to the WEIGHT scale. That line should be 67,000 pounds. This is beyond the structural limit of the aircraft, which is 65,200 pounds. Therefore, the Maximum Permissible Take-Off Weight is 65,200 Ibs. Answer (A) is incorrect. The amount of 65,500 pounds is beyond the structural limit of the aircraft. Answer (C) is incorrect. The amount of 64,900 pounds is less than the maximum permissible takeoff weight. questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 460 on page 826.) At a weight of 77,500 pounds, and a landing elevation below 5,000 feet, the Veer is
questions.choice_c: 143 knots. 14.3 Climb and Cruise Performance
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Refer to Fig. 460. At the bottom of the chart, find the gross weight of 77,500 pounds. Draw a straight line from there to the diagonal line labeled 5,000 ft. and below. From that point, draw a straight line to the left until reaching the Landing Speed-Vper (KIAS) scale. That line should intersect 143 KIAS. Answer (A) is incorrect. An airspeed of 139 knots would require a gross weight of 73,000 pounds. Answer (B) is incorrect. An airspeed of 141 knots would require a gross weight of 75,000 pounds.

questions.study_unit_name: Bombardier CRJ and Q400 Operating/Performance Data
questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 273 on page 830 and Figure 474 on page 828.) With a reported temperature of 45°C, and a weight of 52,000 pounds, the first segment take-off gross climb gradient is
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Refer to Fig. 273. Interpolation is required. Look at the top of the instrument approach plate for the airport elevation of 1,135 feet. Refer to Fig. 474. Interpolate where 1,135 ft. is in the altitude graph area. Draw a straight line from the outside air temperature on the bottom left side of the chart, from 45°C up to 1,135 ft. (interpolate), in the altitude graph area. From that point, draw a straight line to the right to the REFERENCE LINE in the center of the chart. Before going further, it is easiest now to draw a straight line up along the 52,000-Ib. weight from the scale on the bottom of the chart. Draw this line straight up and beyond the top-most diagonal line. Go back to the center REFERENCE LINE where you left off earlier and draw a line diagonally from that point, following the contour of the other diagonal lines on the chart, until you intersect the 52,000-Ib. weight line you drew before this step. From this point, draw a straight line to the right to the Gross Climb Gradient scale. This line should intersect at or very close to 0.045%, so 0.043% is the best answer choice. Make sure your lines are straight and, where curved, follow the contour of the curved lines. Answer (A) is incorrect. The figure of 0.020% would require an airport elevation of about 8,250 ft. at 34°CAnswer (C) is incorrect. The figure of 0.032% would require an airport elevation of about 5,000 ft. at 40°C.

questions.study_unit_name: Bombardier CRJ and Q400 Operating/Performance Data
questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 475 on page 829 and Figure 273 on page 830.) With a reported temperature of 32°C, and a weight of 58,000 pounds, the second segment takeoff gross climb gradient is
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Refer to Fig. 273. Interpolation is required. Look at the top of the instrument approach plate for the airport elevation of 1,135 feet. Refer to Fig. 475. Draw a straight line from the outside air temperature on the bottom left side of the chart from 32°C up to 1,135 ft. (interpolate) in the altitude graph area. From that point, draw a straight line to the right to the REFERENCE LINE in the center of the chart. Before going further, it is easiest now to draw a straight line up along the 58,000-Ib. weight from the scale on the bottom of the chart. Draw this line straight up and beyond the top-most diagonal line. Go back to the center REFERENCE LINE where you left off earlier and draw a line diagonally from that point, following the contour of the other diagonal lines on the chart until you intersect the 58,000-Ib. weight line you drew before this step. From this point, draw a straight line to the right to the Gross Climb Gradient scale. The line should intersect at or very close to 0.062%. The FAA is not giving much room for error here, so make sure the lines are straight and, where curved, follow the contour of the curved lines. Answer (B) is incorrect. The figure of 0.057% would require an altitude of 2,000 ft. and a temperature of 34°. Answer (C) is incorrect. The figure of 0.034% would require an altitude of 6,500 ft. and a temperature of 32°C.

questions.study_unit_name: Bombardier CRJ and Q400 Operating/Performance Data
questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 469 on page 831.) With an OAT of -20°C at 20,000 feet and an IAS of 150, the Maximum Continuous Power Torque Setting is
questions.explanation: Refer to Fig. 469. Locate the OAT -20°C and draw a straight line upward-to the 20,000-feet line. From that point, draw a straight line to the REF LINE, which is also the 150 KT IAS line. From that REF LINE, continue to draw a straight line to the Torque scale. The line should be at the 66% line. Answer (A) is incorrect. The result of 120 KT IAS would be 64% Torque. Answer (C) is incorrect. The result of 180 KT IAS would be 68% Torque.

questions.study_unit_name: Bombardier CRJ and Q400 Operating/Performance Data
questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 297 on page 833 and Figure 478 on page 834.) With a reported temperature of 25°C, a weight of 55,000 pounds, and a V1/VR ratio of 0.95, the accelerate-stop distance required is
questions.choice_c: 5,900 feet.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Refer to Fig. 297 to obtain the airport TDZE elevation of 5,320 feet. Refer to Fig. 478. Begin on the temperature scale at the 25°C mark and draw a straight line up until reaching the interpolation point of 5,320 feet on the airfield altitude scale. From that point, draw a straight line to the right until reaching the reference line. From this point, drawn a line toward the upper right, following the contour of the curved lines until reaching the point where the 55,000 pounds weight line would be. From this point, draw a straight line to the right until reaching the next reference line. This time, a line will be drawn at an angle downward and to the left, following the angle of the other angled lines. Stop at the point where 0.95 would intersect with the line just drawn. From this point, draw a straight line toward the right until intersecting the Accelerate-Stop Distance Required scale. The line should be at the 5,500-feet mark. Answer (B) is incorrect. An airfield altitude of about 2,250 feet would require an accelerate-stop distance of 4,300 feet. Answer (C) is incorrect. An airfield altitude of about 5,800 feet would require an accelerate-stop distance of 5,900 feet.

questions.study_unit_name: Bombardier CRJ and Q400 Operating/Performance Data
questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 297 on page 833 and Figure 481 on page 835.) With a reported temperature of 0°C, at 500 feet AGL after takeoff, and an airspeed of 145 knots IAS, the radius of turn is
questions.choice_c: 9,700 feet.
questions.explanation: Refer to Fig. 297 and locate the airport elevation. Add 500 feet to this elevation for 5,855 feet. Refer to Fig. 481. Look at the bottom right of the chart for 145 KT IAS. Draw a straight line from 145 KT IAS up beyond the 10 that is located on the Radius Of Turn scale on the far right side of the chart. On the left side of the chart where the outside air temperature is, draw a line from 0°C straight up to where an approximate altitude of 5,855 feet would be (just before the 6,000 feet line of the Altitude blocks). From there, draw a straight line to the right to the REF LINE in the center of the chart. From the REF LINE, draw a line up at an angle to the right, following the slight curvature of the printed lines until you reach the 145 KT IAS line that was drawn. From that point, draw a straight line to the right until you reach the Radius Of Turn line. It should be right on or slightly above the 8. Your radius of turn is 8,000 feet. Answer (A) is incorrect. The figure of 6,650 feet would require an airspeed of about 134 KT IAS. Answer (C) is incorrect. ie ae a 9,700 feet would require an airspeed of about

questions.study_unit_name: Bombardier CRJ and Q400 Operating/Performance Data
questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 459 on page 836.) With a payload of 20,000 pounds, still air range is
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Refer to Fig. 459. On the left side of the figure is the payload scale. Draw a straight line from the 20 (1,000 Ib.) payload to the right until reaching the reference line noted by MTOW 82,500 Ib. From there, draw a straight line down to the Still Air Range (NM) line. The line should intersect slightly to the right side of 1400. The closest answer is 1,410 NM. Answer (A) is incorrect. The figure of 1,350 NM would require a payload of 21,500 Ib. Answer (C) is incorrect. The figure of 1,590 NM would require a payload of 18,500 Ib.

questions.study_unit_name: Bombardier CRJ and Q400 Operating/Performance Data
questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 459 on page 836.) Fora supplemental charter, a still air range of 2,250 NM is required. The payload for this non-stop trip is
questions.choice_c: 6,100 pounds. 14.5 Miscellaneous
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Refer to Fig. 459. Draw a straight line from 2,250 on the Still Air Range scale up to the reference line labeled Max Fuel 19450 LB. From that point, draw a straight line to the Payload/Range scale. The line should be on the third dot above the 5 for 5,600 pounds. Answer (A) is incorrect. A payload of 5,100 pounds would be required for a 2,270 NM range. Answer; (C) is incorrect. A payload of 6,100 pounds would be required for a 2,220 NM range.

questions.study_unit_name: Bombardier CRJ and Q400 Operating/Performance Data
questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 422 on page 838.) At a weight of 68,500 pounds with gear and flaps up, you find the reference stall speed to be
questions.choice_c: 142 knots.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Refer to Fig. 422. There are two different charts in this figure. The top chart is with flaps at 0 and the gear up. This is the chart that pertains to this question. At the bottom of that chart, locate the gross weight of 68,500 pounds. From that point, draw a straight line up to the diagonal reference line. From there, draw a straight line across the left of the chart to the reference stall speeds scale. The line should have intersected slightly above 142 KCAS. Answer (A) is incorrect. The stall speed of 148 knots would require a gross weight of 74,000 pounds. Answer (B) is incorrect. The stall speed of 145 knots would require a gross weight of 70,250 pounds.

questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 466 on page 841.) At a weight of
questions.choice_a: 159 knots. the right of 60 represents 60,500 pounds. There are three Vs
questions.choice_b: 148 knots. lines in the middle of the graph. Draw a line straight up along the 60,500 pound line until you intersect the 1.3 Vsp line. From
questions.choice_c: 163 knots. that point, draw a straight line to the left until you reach the
questions.explanation: 60,500 pounds with 5° flaps, the 1.3 Vs speed is DISCUSSION: Fig. 466 shows two graphs. The top graph is for no flaps and the bottom graph is for 5° flaps. On the bottom graph, the bottom of the graph is weight. The first tick mark to

questions.study_unit_name: Bombardier CRJ and Q400 Operating/Performance Data
questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 465 below.) At a weight of 60,000 pounds with 35° flaps, the reference stall speed is
questions.choice_a: 96 knots. 124 knots. 101 knots. REFERENCE 130 120 110 100 80 70 40 42 44 46 48 50 52 54 56 58 60 62 64 66 68 WEIGHT - 1000 LB : iilustrations and materials were used with permission from Bombardier.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Fig. 465 shows the weight of the aircraft at the bottom of the chart, the flap setting is on the right side of the chart, and the CAS is on the left side of the chart. Draw a straight line up, along the 60 (60,000 Ibs.), until intersecting the 35° flap angle line. At that point, draw a straight line across to the left until past the CAS-KT line. The line will be between the 90 and 100 for a reference stall speed of 96 kts. Answer (B) is incorrect. The speed of 124 kts. would be a reference stall speed at 60,000 Ibs. with no flaps. Answer (C) is incorrect. The speed of 101 kts. would be a reference stall speed at 60,000 Ibs. with 15° flaps. \ 400... The Quiet Qne STALL SPEEDS (V.,) Figure 465.

questions.question_text: What is the primary cause of all changes in the
questions.choice_a: aratons of solar energy at the Earth's pressure that result in all changes in the Earth’s weather. ; Answer (B) is incorrect. Changes in air pressure are a result
questions.choice_b: Changes in air pressure over the Earth’s of the unequal solar heating of the Earth’s surface. Answer (C) surface. is incorrect. Movement of air masses is a result of unequal solar heating of the Earth’s surface.
questions.choice_c: Movement of air masses from moist areas to dry areas.
questions.explanation: Earth’s weather? DISCUSSION: Every physical process of weather is accompanied by or is the result of a heat exchange. Unequal Allie : solar heating of the Earth’s surface causes differences in air

questions.question_text: What term describes an elongated area of low
questions.choice_b: Ridge. not low, pressure. Answer (C) is incorrect. A hurricane or typhoon is a tropical cycl ith hi i i
questions.choice_c: Hurricane or typhoon. bs reed cyclone (low) with highest sustained winds of 64 kt.
questions.explanation: pressure? DISCUSSION: A trough is defined as an elongated area of low pressure with the lowest'pressure along a line marking al Troten maximum cyclonic curvature. Answer (B) is incorrect. A ridge is an elongated area of high,

questions.question_text: Which weather condition is defined as an anticyclone?
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: An anticyclone is an area of high atmospheric pressure. The circulation is clockwise in the Northern Hemisphere, counterclockwise in the Southern Hemisphere, and undefined at the Equator. Answer (A) is incorrect. Calm is defined as the absence of wind or of apparent motion of the air. Answer (C) is incorrect. COL is the neutral area between two highs or two lows. It is also the intersection of a trough and a ridge.

questions.question_text: What is a feature of air movement in a high pressure area?
questions.choice_a: Ascending from the surface high to lower pressure at higher altitudes.
questions.choice_b: Descending to the surface and then outward.
questions.choice_c: Moving outward from the high at high altitudes and into the high at the surface.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: The air movement out of a high-pressure area depletes the quantity of air because the air is heavier than the low-pressure air surrounding it. Therefore, high-pressure areas are areas of descending air toward the surface, then outward from the high. Answer (A) is incorrect. Air descends, not ascends, from a high-pressure area, at all altitudes. Answer (C) is incorrect. The agbe moves outward, not inward, from a high, regardless of altitude.

questions.question_text: Where is the usual location of a thermal low?
questions.choice_b: Over the eye of a hurricane.
questions.choice_c: Over the surface of a dry, sunny region.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: A dry, sunny region becomes quite warm from intense surface heating, thus generating a surface low- pressure area known as a thermal low. Thermal lows are relatively shallow with weak pressure gradients and without well- defined cyclonic circulation. Answer (A) is incorrect. Thermal lows develop over dry, sunny regions, not in the Arctic. Answer (B) is incorrect. An eye of a hurricane marks the center of a well-developed tropical cyclone, not a thermal low.

questions.question_text: A low-pressure area is an area of
questions.choice_c: ascending air.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Air flows into low pressure areas to replace rising air. Answer (A) is incorrect. Stagnant air is air that is not moving or does not move. Answer (B) is incorrect. Descending air is found in a high-pressure area, not a low-pressure area.

questions.question_text: Which event usually occurs after an aircraft passes through a front into the colder air?
questions.choice_b: Wind direction shifts to the left.
questions.choice_c: Atmospheric pressure increases.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: A front lies in a pressure trough, and pressure generally is higher in cold air. Thus, when you cross a front directly into colder air, barometric pressure usually increases. Answer (A) is incorrect. The temperature/dew point spread usually differs across a front, but it may not decrease if you fly into a cold, dry air mass. Answer (B) is incorrect. In the Northern Hemisphere, the wind always shifts to the right, not left, due to the Coriolis force.

questions.question_text: What is a feature of a stationary front?
questions.choice_a: The warm front surface moves about half the speed of the cold front surface.
questions.choice_b: Weather conditions are a combination of strong cold front and strong warm front weather.
questions.choice_c: Surface winds tend to flow parallel to the frontal zone.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: The opposing forces exerted by adjacent air masses of different densities are such that the frontal surface between them shows little or no movement and a stationary front results. The surface winds tend to flow parallel to this frontal zone. Answer (A) is incorrect. The movement of a warm front surface at about half the speed of the cold front surface is a feature of the movement of a warm, not stationary, front when the general wind flow is the same in each case. Answer (B) is incorrect. Weather conditions that are a combination of strong cold front and strong warm front weather are a feature of an occluded, not stationary, front.

questions.question_text: Which atmospheric factor causes rapid movement of surface fronts?
questions.choice_a: Upper winds blowing across the front.
questions.choice_b: Upper low located directly over the surface low.
questions.choice_c: The cold front overtaking and lifting the warm front.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: The upper wind flow dictates to a great extent the movement of the front. Systems tend to move with the upper winds. When winds aloft blow across a front, it tends to move with the wind. Answer (B) is incorrect. An upper low located directly over a surface low is a factor in how extensive the weather will be, not in how fast it will move. Answer (C) is incorrect. A cold front overtaking and lifting the warm front is a characteristic of an advancing cold front.

questions.question_text: In which meteorological conditions can frontal waves and low pressure areas form?
questions.choice_b: Slow-moving cold fronts or stationary fronts.
questions.choice_c: Cold front occlusions.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Frontal waves and areas of low pressure usually form on slow-moving cold fronts or on stationary fronts. Answer (A) is incorrect. Occluded fronts are formed by frontal waves and areas of low pressure that cause a cold front to close together with a warm front. Frontal waves and low-pressure areas normally form on slow-moving cold, not warm, fronts. Answer (C) is incorrect. A cold front occlusion occurs when the air behind the cold front is colder than the air in advance of the warm front, lifting the warm front aloft.

questions.question_text: What type weather change is to be expected in an area where frontolysis is reported?
questions.choice_a: The frontal weather is becoming stronger.
questions.choice_c: The front is moving at a faster speed.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Frontolysis is the process in which the adjacent air masses modify, and as temperature and pressure equalize across a front, the front dissipates. Answer (A) is incorrect. Frontal weather becoming stronger is a weather change that is to be expected in an area where frontogenesis, not frontolysis, is reported. Answer (C) is incorrect. A front moving at a fast speed is a weather change expected in an area of frontogenesis, not frontolysis.

questions.question_text: Which weather condition is an example of a nonfrontal instability band?
questions.choice_c: Frontogenesis.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: An instability line is a narrow, nonfrontal line or band of convective activity. If this activity is fully developed thunderstorms, it is called a squall line. Answer (B) is incorrect. Advective fog occurs when warm, moist air moves over a cool surface. It forms in stable, not unstable, air. Answer (C) is incorrect. Frontogenesis is the generation of a frontal, not nonfrontal, zone.

questions.question_text: Dew point fronts are also called
questions.choice_c: dry lines.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: During a considerable part of the year, dew point fronts are common in western Texas and New Mexico northward over the plains states. Moist air flowing north from the Gulf of Mexico abuts the dryer and, therefore, slightly denser air flowing from the southwest. Except for moisture differences, there is seldom any significant air mass contrast across this “front;” and therefore, it is commonly called a “dry line.” Nighttime and early morning fog and low-level clouds often prevail on the moist side of the line, while generally clear skies mark the dry side. In spring and early summer over Texas, Oklahoma, and Kansas, and for some distance eastward, the dry line is a favored spawning area for squall lines and tornadoes. Answer (A) is incorrect. Dew point fronts are also called dry lines, not wet lines. Answer (B) is incorrect. Dew point fronts are also called dry lines, not point fronts.

questions.question_text: Frozen dew is
questions.choice_c: white and opaque.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Sometimes dew forms and later freezes. Frozen dew is easily distinguished from frost. Frozen dew is hard and transparent, while frost is white and opaque. Answer (A) is incorrect. Frozen dew is hard and transparent, not opaque. Answer (C) is incorrect. Frost, not frozen dew, is white and opaque.

questions.question_text: What weather difference is found on each side of a “dry line”?
questions.choice_c: Stratus versus cumulus clouds.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: A “dry line” is common in Western Texas and New Mexico northward over the Plains. Moist air flowing north from the Gulf of Mexico meets the drier and slightly denser air flowing from the southwest. Except for moisture (dew point) differences, there is seldom any significant weather difference found on each side. Answer (A) is incorrect. Except for a moisture difference, not an extreme temperature difference, there is seldom any significant air mass contrast across the “dry line.” Answer (C) is incorrect. The side with moisture may have clouds, while generally clear skies mark the dry side.

questions.question_text: Dew point fronts result from
questions.choice_b: air density differences due to humidity levels.
questions.choice_c: temperatures aloft.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: During a considerable part of the year, dew point fronts are common in western Texas and New Mexico northward over the plains states. Moist air flows north from the Gulf of Mexico and abuts the dryer and, therefore, slightly denser air flowing from the southwest. It is the moisture (humidity) difference that causes the air density differences. Answer (A) is incorrect. It is not the temperature, but the moisture difference, from which dew point fronts result. Answer (C) is incorrect. It is not the temperature aloft, but the moisture difference, from which dew point fronts result.

questions.question_text: If the winds aloft are blowing across the front,
questions.choice_a: visibility around the front will be poor due to precipitation.
questions.choice_b: the winds aloft can be expected to turn at the frontal boundary.
questions.choice_c: the front can be expected to move with the upper winds.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Systems tend to move with the upper winds. When winds aloft blow across a front, the front tends to move with the wind. When winds blow parallel to the front, little or no movement will occur. Answer (A) is incorrect. Visibility within the front may be poor due to precipitation, but this is not directly related to the winds aloft. Answer (B) is incorrect. The upper wind flow dictates to a great extent the movement of the front. Frontal boundary systems tend to move with the upper winds, not change their direction.

questions.question_text: Shallow frontal surfaces tend to give
questions.choice_a: extensive cloudiness and large areas of precipitation.
questions.choice_b: areas of cloudiness and scattered precipitation.
questions.choice_c: heavy precipitation developing along the leading edge of the front.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Shallow frontal surfaces tend to give extensive cloudiness with large precipitation areas. Answer (B) is incorrect. Shallow frontal surfaces tend to have widespread precipitation, which often causes low stratus and fog. Answer (C) is incorrect. Shallow frontal surfaces tend to give extensive cloudiness with large precipitation areas. Heavy frontal precipitation is usually associated with sharp frontal surfaces.

questions.question_text: Steep frontal surfaces are usually associated with
questions.choice_c: dry lines. 15.3 Coriolis Force
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: A fast moving, steep cold front forces upward motion of the warm air along its leading edge. The frontal slope is steep near the leading edge as cold air replaces warm air. Answer (A) is incorrect. A warm front is usually about half as fast as a cold front. Answer (C) is incorrect. A dry line is a boundary separating moist and dry air masses and an important factor in severe weather frequency in the Great Plains. It is not associated with a steep frontal surface.

questions.question_text: At lower levels of the atmosphere, friction causes the wind to flow across isobars into a low because the friction
questions.choice_c: creates air turbulence and raises atmospheric pressure.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Surface friction decreases the wind speed and Coriolis force but does not affect pressure gradient force. The stronger pressure gradient turns the wind across the isobars toward lower pressure. Answer (B) is incorrect. Friction does not affect the pressure gradient force. Answer (C) is incorrect. Surface obstructions, not surface friction, cause air turbulence.

questions.question_text: At which location does Coriolis force have the least effect on wind direction?
questions.choice_c: At the Equator.
questions.explanation: ‘ DISCUSSION: The Coriolis force affects the wind direction i} everywhere except immediately at the Equator. Answer (A) is incorrect. The Coriolis force has the most (not least) effect on wind direction at the poles. Answer (B) is incorrect. The effects of the Coriolis force are quite pronounced in middle latitudes.

questions.question_text: How does Coriolis force affect wind direction in the Southern Hemisphere?
questions.choice_a: Causes clockwise rotation around a low.
questions.choice_b: Causes wind to flow out of a low toward a high.
questions.choice_c: Has exactly the same effect as in the Northern Hemisphere.
questions.explanation: | DISCUSSION: Coriolis force deflects air to the left in the } Southern Hemisphere, which is the opposite from the Northern Hemisphere. In the Southern Hemisphere, the wind blows clockwise around a low. Answer (B) is incorrect. The wind flows from a high to a low, not a low to a high, in both the Northern and Southern Hemispheres. Answer (C) is incorrect. The Coriolis force deflects air to the left in the Southern Hemisphere, which is the opposite, not the same, effect as in the Northern Hemisphere.

questions.question_text: What minimum thickness of cloud layer is indicated if precipitation is reported as light or greater intensity? 4,000 feet thick. a 2,000 feet thick.
questions.choice_c: A thickness which allows the cloud tops to be higher than the freezing level.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: When arriving at or departing from an airport reporting precipitation of light or greater intensity, expect clouds to be at least 4,000 ft. thick. Answer (B) is incorrect. To produce significant precipitation (light or greater intensity), clouds are normally at least 4,000 ft., not 2,000 ft., thick. Answer (C) is incorrect. A cloud thickness resulting in cloud tops above the freezing level means ice droplets and supercooled water will develop.

questions.question_text: What is the lowest cloud in the stationary group associated with a mountain wave?
questions.choice_c: Low stratus.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: When moisture is sufficient to produce clouds on the lee side of a mountain, the lowest clouds associated with mountain wave are rotor clouds. These are found under each crest of the standing wave. Answer (B) is incorrect. The standing lenticular clouds mark the crest, i.e., the top, of each standing wave. Answer (C) is incorrect. Low stratus clouds are not associated with a mountain wave.

questions.question_text: Which condition produces weather on the lee side of a large lake?
questions.choice_a: Warm air flowing over a colder lake may produce fog.
questions.choice_b: Cold air flowing over a warmer lake may produce advection fog.
questions.choice_c: Warm air flowing over a cool lake may produce rain showers.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Warm air flowing over a colder lake may become saturated by evaporation from the water while also becoming cooler in the low levels by contact with the cooler water. Fog often becomes extensive and dense to the lee side of the lake. The lee side is the side to which the wind is going. Answer (B) is incorrect. Cold air flowing over a warmer lake may produce rain showers, not advection fog, on the lee side of the lake. Answer (C) is incorrect. Warm air flowing over a cool BS may produce fog, not rain showers, on the lee side of the ake.

questions.question_text: Which type clouds are indicative of very strong turbulence?
questions.choice_c: Cirrocumulus.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Standing lenticular clouds are formed on the crests of mountain waves. Wind can be quite strong blowing through these clouds, and their presence is a good indication of very strong turbulence. - Answer (A) is incorrect. Nimbostratus is a gray or dark massive cloud layer, diffused by continuous rain, snow, or ice pellets. The stratus feature indicates very little turbulence. Answer (C) is incorrect. Cirrocumulus describes thin clouds appearing as small white flakes or patches of cotton. Their presence indicates some turbulence and possible icing.

questions.question_text: When advection fog has developed, what may tend to dissipate or lift the fog into low stratus clouds?
questions.choice_c: Surface radiation.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Advection fog forms when moist air moves over colder ground or water. It deepens as wind speed increases up to about 15 kt. Wind stronger than 15 kt. lifts the fog into a layer of low stratus or stratocumulus. Answer (A) is incorrect. Winds stronger than 15 kt., not a temperature inversion, tend to dissipate or lift advection fog. Answer (C) is incorrect. Surface temperature radiation is a factor in radiation fog, not advection fog.

questions.question_text: How are haze layers cleared or dispersed?
questions.choice_a: By convective mixing in cool night air.
questions.choice_b: By wind or the movement of air.
questions.choice_c: By evaporation similar to the clearing of fog.
questions.explanation: Haze layers may be cleared or dispersed by the wind, or heating during the day may cause convective movement of the air, spreading the haze to a higher altitude. Answer (A) is incorrect. Convective mixing is caused by heating during the day, not by the cool night air. Answer (C) is incorrect. Haze must be dispersed by movement of air. It cannot evaporate in a manner similar to the clearing of fog.

questions.question_text: Which conditions are necessary for the formation of upslope fog?
questions.choice_a: Moist, stable air being moved over gradually rising ground by a wind.
questions.choice_b: Aclear sky, little or no wind, and 100 percent relative humidity.
questions.choice_c: Rain falling through stratus clouds and a 10- to 25-knot wind moving the precipitation up the slope.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Upslope fog is formed by moist, stable air being cooled as it is gradually moved up sloping terrain by a wind. Once the upslope wind ceases, the fog dissipates. Answer (B) is incorrect. Clear skies, calm wind, and high humidity are conditions necessary for the formation of radiation fog, not upslope fog. Answer (C) is incorrect. Upslope fog is formed by moist air being moved gradually over rising ground, not by rain falling through stratus clouds and not by a wind blowing the precipitation up the slope.

questions.question_text: Cumuliform clouds signify
questions.choice_c: unstable air conditions.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Unstable air favors convection. Cumulus (meaning heap) clouds form in a convective updraft and build upward. Within an unstable layer, clouds are cumuliform. The vertical extent of the cloud depends on the depth of the unstable layer. Answer (A) is incorrect. Lenticular clouds form over mountainous terrain. Answer (B) is incorrect. Cumuliform clouds can form in low or high temperatures depending on the ambient temperature.

questions.question_text: Cloud formation is a process of
questions.choice_c: advective cooling.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Of the three basic processes that cool air to saturation, expansional cooling is the major cause of cloud formation. Answer (A) is incorrect. Although stagnant air overlying a cooling surface is one of the basic processes that may cool air to saturation, it is not the major cause or process of cloud formation. Answer (C) is incorrect. Advective cooling is the process in which warm air blows over cool surfaces thereby cooling the air. This is not a process of cloud formation, this most often results in the formation of fog.

questions.question_text: Cloud bases can usually be estimated by dividing the surface Fahrenheit temperature and dew point difference by Op > IO)?
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Temperature and dew point in upward moving air converge at a rate of about 4°F (2.20°C) per 1,000 feet. Find the difference between the surface temperature and surface dew point, and then divide by 4°F or 2.20°C as appropriate. The resulting number provides an estimate of the cloud bases in thousands of feet. Answer (B) is incorrect. The rate of convergence of the surface temperature and dew point is 4°F per 1,000 feet. Answer (C) is incorrect. The rate of convergence of the surface temperature and dew point is 4°F per 1,000 feet.

questions.question_text: Middle clouds are considered to be the clouds between
questions.choice_c: 6,500 and 20,000 feet AGL.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: The middle cloud family consists of the altostratus, altocumulus, and nimbostratus clouds. These clouds are primarily water, much of which may be supercooled. The height of the bases of these clouds ranges from about 6,500 to 20,000 feet in middle latitudes. Answer (A) is incorrect. The height of 1,000 to 6,500 feet AGL more closely describes the low cloud family, which exist near the Earth’s surface to 6,500 feet AGL. Answer (B) is incorrect. The high, not the middle, cloud family is from about 20,000 feet AGL and above.

questions.question_text: Except for temperature, ice fog forms in conditions favorable to
questions.choice_c: Arctic fog.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Conditions favorable for this type of fog are the same as for Arctic fog except for the temperature. Radiation fog forms at night under clear skies with calm winds when heat absorbed by the Earth’s surface during the day is radiated into space. Provided a deep enough layer of moist air is present near the ground, as the Earth’s surface continues to cool, the humidity will reach 100% and fog will form. Radiation fog varies in depth from 3 feet to about 1,000 feet, is always found at ground level, and usually remains stationary. This type of fog can reduce visibility to near zero at times and makes flying very hazardous during takeoffs and landings. Answer (A) is incorrect. Advection fog often looks like radiation fog and is also the result of condensation. However, the condensation in this case is caused not by a reduction in surface temperature, but rather by the horizontal movement of warm moist air over a cold surface. Answer (C) is incorrect. Arctic fog appears in very cold air from the Arctic ice or frozen-land regions moving over the warmer water of open parts of the Arctic Ocean. Rapid heating induces convection currents that rise in the air. These carry moisture upwards from the water surface, and this becomes visible as the moisture quickly condenses again in the very cold surrounding air. From this, a fog of rising columns of condensing water vapor is formed. The fog is usually fairly shallow, wispy, and smoke-like. Arctic fog may be seen in winter over the open water of rivers when the air is colder than water by 10°C or more.

questions.question_text: Radiation fog forms
questions.choice_c: under cloudy skies.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Radiation fog is restricted to land because water surfaces cool little from nighttime radiation. Answer (B) is incorrect. Water surfaces cool little from nighttime radiation, therefore, radiation fog would not be close to water or over water. Answer (C) is incorrect. When rain soaks the ground, followed by clearing skies, radiation fog is not uncommon the following morning. Ground fog usually burns off rather rapidly after sunrise. Other radiation fog generally clears before noon unless clouds move in over the fog. Radiation fog does not form under cloudy skies. It always forms over land.

questions.question_text: Precipitation-induced fog
questions.choice_b: results from relatively cooler rain or drizzle falling through warmer air.
questions.choice_c: results from relatively warmer rain or drizzle falling through cooler air.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Precipitation-induced fog results from relatively warmer rain or drizzle falling through cooler air. This fog may extend over large areas, completely suspending air operations. Answer (A) is incorrect. Precipitation-induced fog can become quite dense and continue for an extended period of time. Answer (B) is incorrect. This type of fog is the result of warmer, not cooler, rain or drizzle falling through cooler air.

questions.question_text: Visibility above a haze layer is poor when looking
questions.choice_c: upward.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Haze occurs in stable air and is usually only a few thousand feet thick, but sometimes may extend as high as 15,000 feet. Downward visibility above a haze layer is poor. Answer (A) is incorrect. Forward visibility above a haze layer is normally good. Visibility in haze varies greatly depending upon whether the pilot is facing the sun. Answer (C) is incorrect. Downward visibility above a haze layer is poor. Visibility looking upward above a haze layer would be dependent on cloud conditions and be independent of the haze layer condition.

questions.question_text: Stratus clouds are known for
questions.choice_a: some turbulence and small amounts of icing.
questions.choice_b: little or no turbulence with hazardous icing at temperatures near or below freezing.
questions.choice_c: some turbulence and not significant icing.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Stratus clouds are known for very little turbulence but can pose a serious icing problem if temperatures are near or below freezing. Answer (A) is incorrect. Altocumulus clouds are known for some turbulence and small amounts of icing. Answer (C) is incorrect. Cumulus clouds are known for some turbulence and not significant icing.

questions.question_text: Flight in haze
questions.choice_a: after sunrise restricts visibility overhead. 2 is often hazardous when landing into the sun.
questions.choice_c: does not restrict visibility forward or below the aircraft.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Landing an aircraft into the sun is often hazardous if haze is present. Answer (A) is incorrect. Visibility overhead is normally not affected by haze. Answer (C) is incorrect. Haze definitely causes visibility restriction, especially when looking into or facing the sun.

questions.question_text: Advection fog usually deepens in winds up to about
questions.choice_c: 5 knots.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Advection fog is most common along coastal areas but can also develop deep in continental areas. Advection fog deepens as wind speed increases up to about 15 knots. Answer (B) is incorrect. Advection fog deepens as wind speed increases up to about 15 knots, not 10 knots. Answer (C) is incorrect. A wind speed of 5 knots would not cause advection fog to deepen, but 15 knots would.

questions.question_text: Significant precipitation usually requires clouds to be
questions.choice_c: 4,000 feet thick.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: To produce significant precipitation, clouds usually are 4,000 feet thick or more. Answer (A) is incorrect. To produce significant precipitation, clouds would have to be 4,000 feet, not 2,000 feet, thick. Answer (B) is incorrect. To produce significant precipitation, clouds would have to be 4,000 feet, not 3,000 feet, thick.

questions.question_text: When does minimum temperature normally occur during a 24-hour period?
questions.choice_c: At midnight.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Diurnal variation is the change in temperature within a 24-hr. period. The minimum temperature normally occurs after sunrise, sometimes as much as 1 hr. after. Answer (B) is incorrect. The minimum temperature normally occurs after, not before, sunrise. Answer (C) is incorrect. The minimum temperature normally occurs after sunrise, not at midnight.

questions.question_text: Which term applies when the temperature of the air changes by compression or expansion with no heat added or removed?
questions.choice_c: Adiabatic.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: When air expands, it cools; when compressed, it warms. These changes are adiabatic, meaning that no heat is removed from or added to the air. Answer (A) is incorrect. Katabatic is a wind blowing down an incline caused by cold, heavier air spilling down the incline displacing warmer, less dense air. Answer (B) is incorrect. Advection is the horizontal flow in a convective current, i.e., wind.

questions.question_text: What is the approximate rate unsaturated air will cool flowing upslope?
questions.choice_c: 4°C per 1,000 feet.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Unsaturated air moving upward and downward cools and warms at about 3°C (5.4°F) per 1,000 ft. This is the dry adiabatic rate of temperature change. Answer (B) is incorrect. Unsaturated air will cocl flowing upslope at 3°C, not 2°C, per 1,000 ft. Answer (C) is incorrect. Unsaturated air will cool flowing upslope at 3°C, not 4°C, per 1,000 ft.

questions.question_text: Which process causes adiabatic cooling? Expansion of air as it rises. @ Movement of air over a colder surface. . Release of latent heat during the vaporization process.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Adiabatic cooling is caused by the expansion of air as it rises. Anytime air is moved upward, it expands because of decreasing atmospheric pressure. When air expands, it cools. Answer (B) is incorrect. Adiabatic cooling means that no heat is removed from the air, as would be the case if the air were moved over a colder surface. Answer (C) is incorrect. Adiabatic is the process in which no heat is removed from or added to the air.

questions.question_text: What weather condition occurs at the altitude where the dewpoint lapse rate and the dry adiabatic lapse rate converge?
questions.choice_c: Stable air changes to unstable air.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: The altitude where the dew point lapse rate and the dry adiabatic lapse rate converge is where convective cloud bases form. Unsaturated air in a convective current cools at about 3.0°C (5.4°F) per 1,000 ft.; dew point decreases at about 5/9°C (1°F) per 1,000 ft. When they converge, cloud bases form. Answer (B) is incorrect. Precipitation starts when precipitation particles have grown to a size and weight that the atmosphere can no longer suspend and the particles fall as precipitation. Answer (C) is incorrect. Air stability depends on the ambient or existing temperature lapse rate, not the convergence of the dew point lapse rate and the dry adiabatic lapse rate.

questions.question_text: Which type wind flows downslope becoming warmer and dryer?
questions.choice_c: Katabatic wind.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Katabatic wind forms because cold, heavy air spills down sloping terrain displacing warmer, less dense air ahead of it. The cold, heavy air is adiabatically heated and dried as it flows downslope because of heating due to the downward air being compressed by increasing pressure. Answer (A) is incorrect. A land breeze is a wind that flows from the cooler land toward warmer water. Answer (B) is incorrect. A valley wind is wind flowing up out of a valley because colder, denser air settles downward and forces the warmer air near the ground up a mountain slope.

questions.question_text: What is indicated about an air mass if the temperature remains unchanged or decreases slightly as altitude is increased?
questions.choice_c: The air is stable.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: If the air temperature remains unchanged or decreases only slightly as altitude is increased, the air mass tends to be stable. Answer (A) is incorrect. Unstable air would have a uniform decrease (approaching 3°C/1,000 ft.) in temperature with an increase in altitude. Answer (B) is incorrect. In a temperature inversion, the temperature increases with increases in altitude.

questions.question_text: Which condition is present when a local parcel of air is stable?
questions.choice_a: The parcel of air resists convection.
questions.choice_b: The parcel of air cannot be forced uphill.
questions.choice_c: As the parcel of air moves upward, its temperature becomes warmer than the surrounding air.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Stable air resists convection. If a parcel of air is lifted, it is cooler than the surrounding air and will return to its original position when the lifting force is removed. Spontaneous convection is impossible in stable air. Answer (B) is incorrect. Stable air can be forced uphill to form a mountain wave. Answer (C) is incorrect. Rising air warmer than the surrounding air describes unstable, not stable, air.

questions.question_text: When saturated air moves downhill, its temperature increases
questions.choice_a: ata faster rate than dry air because of the release of latent heat.
questions.choice_b: ata slower rate than dry air because vaporization uses heat.
questions.choice_c: ataslower rate than dry air because condensation releases heat.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: When saturated air moves downhill, its temperature increases at a slower rate than dry air because vaporization uses heat known as the “latent heat of vaporization.” The water removal cools the air from which the water is removed. Answer (A) is incorrect. When saturated air moves downhill, its temperature increases at a slower, not faster, rate than dry air because of the absorption, not release, of latent heat. Answer (C) is incorrect. As air moves downhill, its temperature increases at a slower rate than dry air because vaporization uses heat, not because of the release of heat through condensation.

questions.question_text: Adiabatic warming is also described as
questions.choice_c: expansional heating.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Adiabatic warming (heating) is a process of heating dry air through compression. For example, as air moves down a slope it is compressed, which results in an increase in temperature. Answer (B) is incorrect. Solar warming is due to the sun transferring heat through radiation. Answer (C) is incorrect. Expansional heating would be a process of a liquid, such as water expanding from heat. It has nothing to do with adiabatic warming.

questions.question_text: How can the stability of the atmosphere be determined?
questions.choice_c: Surface temperature/dewpoint spread.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: The difference between the ambient temperature lapse rate of a given mass of air and the adiabatic rates of cooling in upward-moving air determines the stability of the air. For example, surface heating or cooling aloft tends to make the air unstable, while surface cooling or warming aloft tends to make the air more stable. Answer (B) is incorrect. The difference between ambient temperature and adiabatic lapse rate, not atmospheric pressure at various levels, determines stability. Answer (C) is incorrect. The surface temperature/dew point spread is used to indicate probability of fog, not atmospheric stability.

questions.question_text: Large areas of land
questions.choice_c: minimize temperature variations.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Large areas of land increase temperature variations. While a daily maximum and minimum temperature change may be only 10°C over water, a change of up to 50°C can occur over land. Answer (B) is incorrect. The troposphere is dramatically affected by the terrestrial surface. Solar radiation warms the land by heating the surface. The warm surface increases the temperature of the air above by terrestrial radiation. Answer (C) is incorrect. Large areas of land do not minimize temperature variations in the troposphere. Large land masses tend to increase the temperature variations in the troposphere.

questions.question_text: The air is 100% saturated with vapor at
questions.choice_c: the dew-point temperature.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Relative humidity is expressed as a percent. It relates the actual water vapor present to that which could be present. Air with 100% humidity is saturated and is the dew-point temperature. Answer (A) is incorrect. The wet-bulb temperature is the temperature a parcel of air would have if it were cooled to saturation (100% relative humidity) by the evaporation of water into it with the latent heat being supplied by the parcel. In other words, this is the coolest temperature a parcel of air can achieve when evaporation is the only method of cooling applied. Answer (B) is incorrect. Humidity index is an index of the effect on human comfort of temperature and humidity levels.

questions.question_text: Any wind blowing down an incline where the incline is influential in causing the wind is a
questions.choice_c: katabatic wind.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: A katabatic wind is any wind blowing down an incline when the incline is influential in causing the wind. Mountain wind is a katabatic wind. Answer (A) is incorrect. A land breeze flows from land to water and is much smaller and weaker than a katabatic wind. Answer (B) is incorrect. Katabatic wind flows down an incline of a mountain, where the valley wind may not reach the crest of the mountain and is basically within the valley area.

questions.question_text: Land surface heating on the coast results in
questions.choice_c: asea breeze.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: During the day, the wind that blows from the cool water to warm the land is a sea breeze. Answer (A) is incorrect. A chinook wind is a warm, dry wind that blows down the east side of the Rocky Mountains at the end of winter. Answer (B) is incorrect. At night the wind blows from cool land to warmer water and creates a land breeze.

questions.question_text: Land surface cooling on the coast results in
questions.choice_c: asea breeze.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: At night, the wind blows from cool land to warmer water and creates a land breeze. Answer (A) is incorrect. A chinook wind is a warm, dry wind that blows down the east side of the Rocky Mountains at the end of winter. Answer (C) is incorrect. During the day, the wind that blows from the cool water to warm the land is a sea breeze.

questions.question_text: Where is a common location for an inversion?
questions.choice_c: At the base of cumulus clouds.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Inversions may occur at any altitude and are common in the stratosphere. An inversion is an increase in temperature with an increase in altitude. Answer (A) is incorrect. A common location for an inversion is in the stratosphere, not at the tropopause. Answer (C) is incorrect. The base of cumulus clouds is where the dew point lapse rate and the dry adiabatic lapse rate converge. It is not a common location for an inversion.

questions.question_text: What feature is associated with a temperature inversion?
questions.choice_c: Air mass thunderstorms.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: A temperature inversion is defined as an increase in temperature with height; i.e., the normal lapse rate is inverted. Thus, any warm air rises to its own temperature and forms a stable layer of air. Answer (B) is incorrect. Instability occurs when the temperature decreases, not increases as in a temperature inversion, with an increase in altitude and the rising air continues to rise. Answer (C) is incorrect. Air mass thunderstorms result from instability. They do not occur when there is a temperature inversion.

questions.question_text: What condition produces the most frequent type of ground- or surface-based temperature inversion?
questions.choice_a: The movement of colder air under warm air or the movement of warm air over cold air.
questions.choice_b: Widespread sinking of air within a thick layer aloft resulting in heating by compression.
questions.choice_c: Terrestrial radiation on a clear, relatively calm night.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: An inversion often develops near the ground on clear, cool nights when the wind is relatively calm. The ground radiates heat and cools much faster than the overlying air. Air in contact with the ground becomes cold, while the temperature a few hundred feet above changes very little. Answer (A) is incorrect. The movement of colder air under warm air is what happens when a cold front is advancing, and the movement of warm air over cold air is the process of an advancing warm front. Answer (B) is incorrect. Widespread sinking of air describes compressional or adiabatic heating.

questions.question_text: What characterizes a ground-based inversion?
questions.choice_c: Poor visibility.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: A ground-based inversion is characterized by poor visibility as a result of trapping fog, smoke, and other restrictions into low levels of the atmosphere. Answer (A) is incorrect. Convective currents at the surface do not occur when there is a ground-based inversion. Answer (B) is incorrect. When the temperature is cold, it is difficult for the earth to radiate enough heat to become colder than the overlying air.

questions.question_text: Which is a necessary condition for the occurrence of a low-level temperature inversion wind shear?
questions.choice_a: The temperature differential between the cold and warm layers must be at least 10°C.
questions.choice_b: Acalm or light wind near the surface and a relatively strong wind just above the inversion.
questions.choice_c: Awind direction difference of at least 30° between the wind near the surface and the wind just above the inversion.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: A temperature inversion forms near the surface on a clear night with calm or light surface wind, and a relatively strong wind just above the inversion is necessary to form a low-level temperature inversion wind shear. Answer (A) is incorrect. Magnitude of temperature differential in the inversion is not important; the wind shear is caused by the variation in wind speed. Answer (C) is incorrect. Surface wind and a relatively strong wind just above the inversion, not a wind direction difference of at least 30°, are needed to form a low-level temperature inversion wind shear. The wind shear is caused by wind speed variation, not variation in wind direction.

questions.question_text: An inversion aloft with temperatures below freezing can
questions.choice_a: result in the possibility of freezing rain.
questions.choice_c: produce small hailstones. 15.7 Density, Pressure, and Corrected Altitude
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: A condition favorable for rapid accumulation of clear icing is freezing rain below a frontal surface. Rain forms above the frontal surface at temperatures warmer than freezing. It falls through air at temperatures below freezing and becomes supercooled. The supercooled drops freeze on impact with an aircraft surface. Answer (B) is incorrect. Supercooled drops resulting from an inversion aloft with temperatures below freezing freeze on impact with an aircraft surface producing ice, not frost. Answer (C) is incorrect. Supercooled drops resulting from an inversion aloft with temperatures below freezing are raindrops, not hailstones.

questions.question_text: En route at FL 270, the altimeter is set correctly. On descent, a pilot fails to set the local altimeter setting of 30.57. If the field elevation is 650 feet, and the altimeter is functioning properly, what will it indicate upon landing?
questions.choice_c: Sea level.
questions.explanation: If an altimeter is set to 29.92 for flight at FL 270 and is not adjusted to the correct altimeter setting of 30.57 during descent, the altimeter will indicate an altitude lower than actual by 650 ft. (1,000 ft. x the difference between the two altimeter settings, which is .65). Thus, if the airplane lands at an airport with a field elevation of 650 ft., the altimeter will indicate sea level (650 ft. airport elevation — 650 ft. altimeter setting error). Answer (A) is incorrect. This figure is obtained by subtracting 65 ft. rather than subtracting 650 ft. Answer (B) is incorrect. This figure is obtained by adding 650 ft. rather than subtracting 650 ft.

questions.question_text: If the ambient temperature is colder than standard at FL 310, what is the relationship between true altitude and pressure altitude?
questions.choice_a: They are both the same, 31,000 feet.
questions.choice_b: True altitude is lower than 31,000 feet.
questions.choice_c: Pressure altitude is lower than true altitude.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: At FL 310, the altimeter is set to pressure altitude (29.92). If the outside air temperature (ambient) is colder than standard, the air is compressed and heavier in weight per unit volume than on a warm day. Thus, in colder-than-standard air, the true altitude is lower than the pressure altitude. Answer (A) is incorrect. Both true and pressure altitudes are the same at FL 310 if the ambient air temperature is standard, not colder than standard. Answer (C) is incorrect. Pressure altitude is lower than true altitude in warmer-, not colder-, than- standard air temperature.

questions.question_text: If the ambient temperature is warmer than standard at FL 350, what is the density altitude compared to pressure altitude?
questions.choice_b: Higher than pressure altitude. Impossible to determine without information on possible inversion layers at lower altitudes.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Density altitude, by definition, is pressure altitude adjusted for nonstandard temperature. When the ambient air temperature is warmer than standard, the expanded air is lighter in weight per unit volume than on a cold day, and the pressure levels are raised. Thus, the pressure level where the altimeter indicates FL 350 is higher on a warm day than under standard conditions. Answer (A) is incorrect. Density altitude is higher, not lower, when air temperature is warmer than standard. Answer (C) is incorrect. Density altitude is pressure altitude corrected for nonstandard temperatures. Pressure altitude is based on a standard pressure atmosphere at a particular altitude, and inversion layers at lower levels have no effect on pressure altitude.

questions.question_text: What is corrected altitude (approximate true altitude)?
questions.choice_a: Pressure altitude corrected for instrument error.
questions.choice_b: Indicated altitude corrected for temperature variation from standard.
questions.choice_c: Density altitude corrected for temperature variation from standard.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Corrected altitude is the indicated altitude of an airplane’s altimeter corrected for temperature variation from standard atmospheric temperature. This is an approximation of true altitude. Answer (A) is incorrect. Pressure altitude corrected for instrument error is a nonsense concept. Answer (C) is incorrect. Density altitude is pressure altitude corrected for temperature variation from standard. Density altitude is a final figure and not subject to additional adjustments.
