questions.question_text: Which pressure is defined as station pressure? Altimeter setting. @ Actual pressure at field elevation. Station barometric pressure reduced to sea level.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Pressure can be measured only at the point of measurement. The pressure measured at a station or airport is “station pressure,” or the actual pressure at field elevation. Answer (A) is incorrect. Altimeter setting is the value to which the scale of a pressure altimeter is adjusted to read field elevation. Answer (C) is incorrect. Station barometric pressure reduced to sea level is a method to readily compare station pressures between stations at different altitudes.

questions.question_text: At an indicated altitude of 9,000 feet with a temperature of 85°F, density altitude is
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Using your E6B on the flight computer side, find the conversion for Celsius to Fahrenheit at the bottom of the flight computer. The temperature of 85°F is about 29.4°C. Now go to the pressure altitude scale and line up just past the mark for 29°C to 9,000 ft. pressure altitude. Note the density altitude arrow points to about 12,750 feet. The closest answer is 12,800 feet. Answer (A) is incorrect. A density altitude of 13,900 feet would require a temperature of about 44°C. Answer (C) is incorrect. A density altitude of 11,400 feet would require a temperature of about 15°C.

questions.question_text: At an indicated altitude of 1,000 feet with a temperature of 32°C, density altitude is
questions.choice_c: 3,400 feet.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Density altitude is calculated using Celsius. In the pressure altitude scale, line up 32°C over 1,000 feet of pressure altitude. The density altitude arrow is pointing to about 3,400 feet. Answer (A) is incorrect. A density altitude of 1,400 feet would require a temperature of about 15°C. Answer (B) is incorrect. A density altitude of 2,400 feet would require a temperature of about 25°C.

questions.question_text: What is the result when water vapor changes to the liquid state while being lifted in a thunderstorm?
questions.choice_a: Latent heat is released to the atmosphere.
questions.choice_b: Latent heat is transformed into pure energy.
questions.choice_c: Latent heat is absorbed from the surrounding air by the water droplet.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: In a thunderstorm, the updrafts (lifting action) result in water vapor changing to liquid or condensation. Condensation releases latent heat to the atmosphere, which partially offsets cooling in the saturated updraft and increases buoyancy within the cloud. Answer (B) is incorrect. Latent heat cannot create pure energy. Latent heat is returned to the surrounding atmosphere. Answer (C) is incorrect. This is the process of latent heat of vaporization, which is changing liquid water to vapor.

questions.question_text: Convective clouds which penetrate a stratus layer can produce which threat to instrument flight?
questions.choice_c: Embedded thunderstorms.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: A stratus layer may sometimes form in a mildly stable layer while convective clouds penetrate the layer and possibly form thunderstorms. These thunderstorms may be almost or entirely embedded in a massive stratus layer and pose an unseen threat to instrument flight. Answer (A) is incorrect. The formation of freezing rain is dependent on rain falling through colder air. Convective clouds that penetrate a stratus layer may or may not produce precipitation. Answer (B) is incorrect. Clear air turbulence is turbulence encountered in air where no clouds are present.

questions.question_text: What is indicated by the term “embedded thunderstorms”?
questions.choice_a: Severe thunderstorms are embedded in a squall line.
questions.choice_b: Thunderstorms are predicted to develop in a stable air mass.
questions.choice_c: Thunderstorms are obscured by other types of clouds.
questions.explanation: ‘DISCUSSION: The term “embedded thunderstorms” means that the thunderstorms are embedded in clouds or thick haze layers and cannot be seen. Answer (A) is incorrect. A squall line consists of severe thunderstorms, which can always be seen. Answer (B) is incorrect. Thunderstorms do not occur in stable air masses.

questions.question_text: What feature is normally associated with the cumulus stage of a thunderstorm?
questions.choice_a: Beginning of rain at the surface.
questions.choice_c: Continuous updraft.
questions.explanation: During the cumulus stage of a thunderstorm, the cumulus cloud is building, and there are continuous updrafts. Answer (A) is incorrect. The beginning of rain at a surface marks the beginning of the mature stage. Answer (B) is incorrect. Frequent lightning occurs after the downdrafts have developed, producing the static electricity that causes lightning.

questions.question_text: Which weather phenomenon signals the beginning of the mature stage of a thunderstorm?
questions.choice_a: The appearance of an anvil top.
questions.choice_b: The start of rain at the surface.
questions.choice_c: Growth rate of the cloud is at its maximum.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: The mature stage of a thunderstorm begins when rain begins falling at the surface. This means that downdrafts have developed sufficiently to carry water all the way through the thunderstorm. Answer (A) is incorrect. The appearance of an anvil top occurs during the dissipating stage. Answer (C) is incorrect. The growth rate of a thunderstorm is at its greatest during the cumulus stage.

questions.question_text: Why are downdrafts in a mature thunderstorm hazardous?
questions.choice_a: Downdrafts are kept cool by cold rain which tends to accelerate the downward velocity.
questions.choice_b: Downdrafts converge toward a central location under the storm after striking the surface.
questions.choice_c: Downdrafts become warmer than the surrounding air and reverse into an updraft before reaching the surface.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: During the mature stage, cold rain in the downdraft slows compressional heating, and the downdraft remains colder than the surrounding air. Thus, its downward speed is accelerated and may exceed 2,500 fpm. Answer (B) is incorrect. After striking the ground, the downdrafts will move away from the storm’s center (not converge). Answer (C) is incorrect. Downdrafts remain colder, not warmer, than the surrounding air and accelerate downward, not reverse into an updraft.

questions.question_text: During the life cycle of a thunderstorm, which stage is characterized predominately by downdrafts?
questions.choice_c: Mature.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Thunderstorms have three life cycles: cumulus, mature, and dissipating. In the dissipating stage, the storm is characterized by downdrafts as the storm rains itself out. Answer (A) is incorrect. The cumulus stage is the building stage characterized by updrafts. Answer (C) is incorrect. The mature stage has both updrafts and downdrafts, which create strong wind shears.

questions.question_text: Which type storms are most likely to produce funnel clouds or tornadoes?
questions.choice_b: Cold front or squall line thunderstorms.
questions.choice_c: Storms associated with icing and supercooled water.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Funnel clouds or tornadoes normally form with steady-state thunderstorms associated with cold fronts or squall lines. The mature stage updrafts in a steady-state | thunderstorm are stronger and last longer than those in air mass storms and may persist for several hours. j Answer (A) is incorrect. Even though air mass thunderstorms may produce funnel clouds or tornadoes, they are most likely to occur with steady-state thunderstorms. Answer (C) is incorrect. All thunderstorms that have updrafts and carry water above the freezing level can produce icing and supercooled water. But thunderstorms associated with cold fronts and squall lines are most likely to produce funnel clouds or tornadoes.

questions.question_text: What is a difference between an air mass thunderstorm and a steady-state thunderstorm?
questions.choice_a: Air mass thunderstorms produce precipitation which falls outside of the updraft.
questions.choice_b: Air mass thunderstorm downdrafts and precipitation retard and reverse the updrafts.
questions.choice_c: Steady-state thunderstorms are associated with local surface heating.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: The rain associated with an air mass thunderstorm falls through or immediately beside the updraft. Falling precipitation induces frictional drag, retards the updraft, and reverses it to a downdraft. During a steady-state thunderstorm, precipitation falls outside the updraft, allowing the updraft to continue unabated. Answer (A) is incorrect. Steady-state, not air mass, thunderstorms produce precipitation that falls outside the updraft. Answer (C) is incorrect. Air mass, not steady-state, thunderstorms are associated with local surface heating.

questions.question_text: Which type cloud is associated with violent turbulence and a tendency toward the production of funnel clouds?
questions.choice_c: Stratocumulus.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Cumulonimbus mamma is a cumulonimbus cloud having hanging protuberances like pouches, testes, or udders on the underside of the cloud, usually indicative of severe turbulence. This type of cloud can produce a tornado. Answer (B) is incorrect. Standing lenticular clouds mark mountain waves that are the product of stable air flowing over an obstruction. Answer (C) is incorrect. Stratocumulus clouds sometimes form from the breaking up of stratus or the spreading out of cumulus, and they are associated with some turbulence and possible icing at subfreezing levels.

questions.question_text: Where do squall lines most often develop?
questions.choice_c: Behind a stationary front.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: A squall line is a nonfrontal, narrow band of active thunderstorms. Most often squall lines develop ahead of a cold front in moist, unstable air. Answer (A) is incorrect. Squall lines most often develop ahead of a cold front, not in an occluded front. Answer (C) is incorrect. Squall lines most often develop ahead of a cold front, not behind a stationary front.

questions.question_text: Where can the maximum hazard zone caused by wind shear associated with a thunderstorm be found?
questions.choice_a: In front of the thunderstorm cell (anvil side) and on the southwest side of the cell.
questions.choice_b: Ahead of the roll cloud or gust front and directly under the anvil cloud.
questions.choice_c: Onall sides and directly under the thunderstorm cell.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Hazardous turbulence is present in all thunderstorms. The maximum hazard zone caused by wind shear associated with a thunderstorm is found on all sides and directly under the thunderstorm cell. Answer (A) is incorrect. The wind shear associated with a thunderstorm is on all sides and directly under the cell, not just in the front and on the southwest side. Answer (B) is incorrect. The wind shear associated with a thunderstorm is on all sides and directly under the cell. A roll cloud is not present in all thunderstorms, and when present it marks the eddies of the shear zone between the plow wind and surrounding air.

questions.question_text: Atmospheric pressure changes due to a thunderstorm will be at the lowest value
questions.choice_a: during the downdraft and heavy rain showers.
questions.choice_c: immediately after the rain showers have stopped.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Pressure usually falls rapidly with the approach of a thunderstorm, then rises sharply with the onset of the first gust and arrival of the cold downdraft and heavy rain showers, falling back to normal as the storm moves on. Answer (A) is incorrect. During the downdraft and heavy rain showers, the pressure rises sharply. Answer (C) is incorrect. Immediately after the rain showers have stopped, the pressure will return to normal.

questions.question_text: A clear area in a line of thunderstorm echoes on a radar scope indicates
questions.choice_a: the absence of clouds in the area.
questions.choice_b: an area of no convective turbulence.
questions.choice_c: an area where precipitation drops are not detected.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Weather radar detects droplets of precipitation. If a radarscope displays a clear area, it indicates an area where no precipitation drops are detected. Answer (A) is incorrect. Weather radar detects only precipitation drops, not clouds. Answer (B) is incorrect. Convective turbulence, which would not be detected by radar, could be found under cumulus clouds. Radar does not detect turbulence.

questions.question_text: When flying over the top of a severe thunderstorm, the cloud should be overflown by at least
questions.choice_c: 500 feet above any moderate to severe turbulence layer.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: The top of a known or suspected severe thunderstorm should be cleared by at least 1,000 ft. of altitude for each 10 kt. of wind speed at the cloud top. In some severe thunderstorms, this clearance may exceed the altitude capability of most aircraft. Answer (B) is incorrect. The cloud should be overflown by at least 1,000 ft. for each 10 kt. wind speed, which would normally be greater than 2,500 ft. with a severe thunderstorm. Answer (C) is incorrect. The exact location of a turbulence layer will not usually be known.

questions.question_text: Intense radar storm echos should be avoided by a margin of
questions.choice_c: 20 NM.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: The most intense echos are severe thunderstorms. Remember that hail may fall several miles from the cloud, and hazardous turbulence may extend as much as 20 NM from the cloud. Avoid the most intense echos by at least 20 NM. Answer (A) is incorrect. Avoid the most intense echos by at least 20 NM. Echos should be separated by 40 NM before flying between them. Answer (B) is incorrect. Avoid the most intense echos by at least 20 NM, though 25 NM would be a good “personal safety” item.

questions.question_text: Downdrafts in thunderstorms can be expected to exceed
questions.choice_c: 4,500 feet per minute.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: The downdraft signals the mature stage of a thunderstorm. Cold rain in the downdraft retards compressional heating, and the downdraft remains cooler than surrounding air. Therefore, its downward speed is accelerated and may exceed 2,500 feet per minute. Answer (B) is incorrect. Thunderstorm downdrafts can be expected to exceed 2,500 feet per minute, not 3,500 feet per minute. Answer (C) is incorrect. Thunderstorm downdrafts can be expected to exceed 2,500 feet per minute, not 4,500 feet per minute.

questions.question_text: Updrafts in the mature stage of a thunderstorm can exceed
questions.choice_c: 6,000 feet per minute.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Updrafts and downdrafts in close proximity create strong vertical shear and a very turbulent environment. Updrafts reach a maximum with speeds possibly exceeding 6,000 feet per minute. Answer (A) is incorrect. Updrafts can reach speeds possibly exceeding 6,000 feet per minute, not 8,000 feet per minute. Answer (B) is incorrect. Updrafts can reach speeds possibly exceeding 6,000 feet per minute, not 7,000 feet per minute.

questions.question_text: Steady-state thunderstorms are usually associated with
questions.choice_c: the mature stage.
questions.explanation: Weather systems such as fronts, converging winds, and troughs aloft force upward motion spawning these storms that often form into squall lines. Steady-state thunderstorms usually are associated with weather systems. Answer (B) is incorrect. Afternoon heating intensifies the already active steady-state thunderstorm. Answer (C) is incorrect. In a steady-state thunderstorm, precipitation falls outside the updraft; therefore the mature stage updrafts become stronger and last much longer than air mass storms.

questions.question_text: All ATC facilities using radar weather processors with the ability to determine precipitation intensity will describe the intensity as
questions.choice_a: light, moderate, heavy, intense, extreme, severe.
questions.choice_c: light, moderate, heavy, extreme, severe.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: All ATC facilities using radar weather processors with the ability to determine precipitation intensity will describe the intensity to pilots as light, moderate, heavy, or extreme. Answer (A) is incorrect. The words “intense” or “severe” are not used by ATC facilities using radar weather processors with the ability to determine precipitation intensity. Answer (C) is incorrect. The word “severe” is not used by ATC facilities using radar weather processors with the ability to determine precipitation intensity.

questions.question_text: Shear turbulence may be expected
questions.choice_a: usually within 20 miles of a severe thunderstorm.
questions.choice_b: no more than 10 miles from a severe thunderstorm.
questions.choice_c: probably not more than 15 miles from a severe thunderstorm.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Outside of the cloud, shear turbulence has been encountered several thousand feet above and 20 miles laterally from a severe storm. Answer (B) is incorrect. Shear turbulence has been encountered 20 miles laterally from a severe storm. Answer (C) is incorrect. Shear turbulence has been encountered 20 miles laterally from a severe storm.

questions.question_text: Storm gust fronts often move as much as
questions.choice_a: 10 miles ahead of the associated precipitation.
questions.choice_b: 15 miles ahead of the associated precipitation.
questions.choice_c: 20 miles ahead of the associated precipitation.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Gust fronts often move far ahead (up to 15 miles) of associated precipitation. The gust front causes a rapid and sometimes drastic change in surface wind ahead of an approaching storm. Answer (A) is incorrect. Gust fronts often move up to 15 miles, not 10 miles, ahead of associated precipitation. Answer (C) is incorrect. Gust fronts often move up to 15 miles, not 20 miles, ahead of associated precipitation.

questions.question_text: A thunderstorm first begins when
questions.choice_a: lifting forces carry cooler air inward and upward.
questions.explanation: The cumulus stage is the beginning of the life cycle of a thunderstorm. The key feature of this stage is the updraft. This updraft carries cooler air inward and upward. Growth rate of this stage may exceed 3,000 feet per minute. Answer (B) is incorrect. Rain begins at the second or mature stage of the life cycle of a thunderstorm, not the cumulus stage. Answer (C) is incorrect. The cumulus stage of a thunderstorm pares cooler air inward and upward. It is not a high temperature cycle.

questions.question_text: Downdrafts in a thunderstorm may exceed
questions.choice_c: 14 knots.
questions.explanation: Downdrafts can exceed 2,500 feet per minute (fpm). We convert that to knots by multiplying e 2,500 fpm x 60 minutes = 150,000 feet per hour ® 1 nautical mile = 6,076 feet @ 150,000 feet per hour + 6,076 feet = 24.69 knots Answer (A) is incorrect. The speed of 34 knots would require a downdraft of about 3,500 feet per minute. Answer (C) is incorrect. The speed of 14 knots would require a downdraft of only about 1,500 feet per minute.

questions.question_text: Freezing rain encountered during climb is normally evidence that
questions.choice_a: a climb can be made to a higher altitude without encountering more than light icing.
questions.choice_b: alayer of warmer air exists above.
questions.choice_c: ice pellets at higher altitudes have changed to rain in the warmer air below.
questions.explanation: Freezing rain is rain that falls through colder air and becomes supercooled, freezing on impact. If encountered, it means that there is a layer of warmer air above. Answer (A) is incorrect. Freezing rain means only that a layer of warmer air exists above. It does not indicate the amount of icing that may be encountered during a climb. Answer (C) is incorrect. Freezing rain is formed by rain falling through colder air, not from ice pellets melting through warmer air.

questions.question_text: What condition is indicated when ice pellets are encountered during flight?
questions.choice_c: Snow at higher levels.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Ice pellets always indicate freezing rain at higher altitudes. Ice pellets are formed when rain freezes during descent. Answer (A) is incorrect. Ice pellets always indicate freezing rain, not thunderstorms, at higher altitudes. Answer (C) is incorrect. Freezing rain, not snow, is indicated at higher altitudes when ice pellets are encountered.

questions.question_text: What temperature condition is indicated if precipitation in the form of wet snow occurs during flight?
questions.choice_a: The temperature is above freezing at flight altitude.
questions.choice_b: The temperature is above freezing at higher altitudes.
questions.choice_c: There is an inversion with colder air below.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Falling snow may melt in warmer layers of air. When wet snow is encountered, it indicates that the temperature is above freezing at flight altitude. Answer (B) is incorrect. Wet snow indicates above- freezing temperature at flight level, not at higher altitudes. The temperature was below freezing at the altitudes where the snow formed. Answer (C) is incorrect. Wet snow indicates falling snow that has begun to melt due to above-freezing temperature at flight level. An inversion may or may not be the cause of the warmer air.

questions.question_text: Which conditions result in the formation of frost?
questions.choice_a: The temperature of the collecting surface is at or below freezing and small droplets of moisture are falling.
questions.choice_b: Dew collects on the surface and then freezes because the surface temperature is lower than the air temperature.
questions.choice_c: Temperature of the collecting surface is below the dewpoint and the dewpoint is also below freezing.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Frost forms when both the temperature of the collecting surface is below the dew point and the dew point is also below freezing. Frost is the direct sublimation of water vapor to ice crystals. Answer (A) is incorrect. Moisture that falls on a collecting surface that is at or below freezing will form ice. Answer (B) is incorrect. Frozen dew is hard and transparent, while frost is the sublimation from vapor to ice and is white and opaque.

questions.question_text: When will frost most likely form on aircraft surfaces?
questions.choice_a: On clear nights with stable air and light winds.
questions.choice_b: On overcast nights with freezing drizzle precipitation.
questions.choice_c: Onclear nights with convective action and a small temperature/dewpoint spread.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Frost will most likely form on aircraft surfaces on clear nights with stable air and light winds. These are conditions that in all other respects make weather ideal for flying. Answer (B) is incorrect. Freezing drizzle would produce ice, not frost, on the aircraft surfaces. Answer (C) is incorrect. Stable air is required. Convective action requires unstable conditions.

questions.question_text: Which type precipitation is an indication that supercooled water is present?
questions.choice_c: Ice pellets.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Rain falling through colder air may become supercooled, freezing on impact as freezing rain. Supercooled water is liquid water that is colder than 0°C, and its impact on an object induces freezing. Answer (A) is incorrect. Wet snow is an indication that temperature is above freezing at present level. Answer (C) is incorrect. Ice pellets indicate that water has frozen, not become/ remained supercooled.

questions.question_text: What is a feature of supercooled water?
questions.choice_a: The water drop sublimates to an ice particle upon impact.
questions.choice_b: The unstable water drop freezes upon striking an exposed object.
questions.choice_c: The temperature of the water drop remains at 0°C until it impacts a part of the airframe, then clear ice accumulates.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Water droplets that are colder than 0°C are considered supercooled. When they strike an exposed object, they freeze on impact. Aircraft icing results if supercooled water strikes an airplane. Answer (A) is incorrect. Sublimation is the process of changing water vapor to ice crystals, not liquid water to ice (the latter is freezing). Answer (C) is incorrect. Supercooled water temperature is below, not at, 0°C.

questions.question_text: The following weather condition may be conducive to severe in-flight icing:
questions.choice_a: visible rain at temperatures below 0° C ambient air temperature.
questions.choice_b: visible moisture at temperatures below 5° C ambient temperature.
questions.choice_c: visible rain at temperatures below 10° C ambient temperature.
questions.explanation: | DISCUSSION: Visible rain at ambient temperatures below 0°C (32°F) indicates a condition that is conducive to severe in- flight icing. Answer (B) is incorrect. Visible moisture at ambient temperatures below 5°C (41°F) does not necessarily indicate a condition that is conducive to severe in-flight icing. Answer (C) is incorrect. Visible rain at ambient temperatures below 10°C (50°F) does not necessarily indicate a condition that is conducive to severe in-flight icing.

questions.question_text: What condition is necessary for the formation of structural icing in flight?
questions.choice_c: Visible water.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Two conditions are necessary for structural icing in flight: (1) The aircraft must be flying through visible water, such as rain or cloud droplets, and (2) the temperature at the point where the moisture strikes the aircraft must be 0°C or colder. Answer (A) is incorrect. Supercooled water drops increase the rate of icing but are not a condition necessary for the formation of structural icing. Answer (B) is incorrect. Water must be visible, not in a gaseous (vapor) state.

questions.question_text: Which type of icing is associated with the smallest size of water droplet similar to that found in low-level stratus clouds?
questions.choice_c: Rime ice.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Rime ice forms when drops are small, such as those found in low-level stratus clouds. Its irregular shape makes it very effective in decreasing aerodynamic efficiency of airfoils. Answer (A) is incorrect. Clear ice forms when drops are large, not small, as found in rain or cumuliform clouds. Answer (B) is incorrect. Frost is not a structural icing condition found in flight; it occurs to airplanes parked on the ground.

questions.question_text: The following weather may be conducive to severe inflight icing
questions.choice_a: droplets that splash or splatter on impact at temperatures below 0° C ambient temperature.
questions.choice_b: drops that impact at temperatures below -20° C ambient air temperature.
questions.choice_c: droplets that splash or splatter on impact at temperatures below 10° C and above 1° C ambient temperature.
questions.explanation: When water droplets splash or splatter on impact at temperatures at or below 0° C, severe icing is possible. Answer (B) is incorrect. At temperatures below -20° C precipitation is typically ice, not liquid that could freeze onto the airframe. Answer (C) is incorrect. The temperature range specified in this answer option is above freezing. Severe icing is not likely in temperatures that are consistently above freezing.

questions.question_text: The following weather may be conducive to severe inflight icing
questions.choice_a: visible moisture with temperature below 5° C.
questions.choice_b: visible rain with temperature below 0° C.
questions.choice_c: visible rain with temperature below 10° C.
questions.explanation: __ DISCUSSION: Pilots should be especially alert to for severe icing when there is visible rain and the static air temperature is 0°C or colder. __ Answer (A) is incorrect. Although visible moisture can cause icing, the weather that is most conducive to severe inflight icing includes visible rain and a static air temperature of 0° C or colder. Answer (C) is incorrect. While visible rain is a factor in the formation of severe inflight icing, the most conducive static air temperature is 0° C or colder, not 10° C.

questions.question_text: What happens to residual ice that remains after deice boots are inflated and shed ice?
questions.choice_a: Residual ice increases with a decrease in airspeed or temperature.
questions.choice_b: Residual ice remains the same until the aircraft exits icing conditions.
questions.choice_c: Residual ice decreases with a decrease in airspeed or temperature.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: The FAA recommends that the deicing system be activated at the first indication of icing. Because some residual ice continues to adhere between pneumatic boot system cycles, the wing is never entirely “clean.” The amount of residual ice increases as airspeed and/or temperature decrease due to the more favorable conditions for ice accumulation associated with these conditions. At airspeeds typical of small airplanes, it may take many boot cycles to effectively shed the ice. Answer (B) is incorrect. Residual ice left behind between deicing system cycles will increase in magnitude if either airspeed or temperature decrease due to the more favorable conditions for ice accumulation associated with these conditions. Answer (C) is incorrect. The amount of residual ice increases, not decreases, as airspeed and/or temperature decrease due to the more favorable conditions for ice accumulation associated with these conditions.

questions.question_text: If you detect icing accumulation in flight, especially if the aircraft is not equipped with a deicing system, you should
questions.choice_b: leave the area of precipitation, if able, or fly to an altitude where the temperature is above freezing.
questions.choice_c: fly to an area with liquid precipitation.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Regardless of the level of anti-ice or deice protection offered by the aircraft, the first course of action should be to leave the area of visible moisture. This might mean descending to an altitude below the cloud bases, climbing to an altitude that is above the cloud tops, or turning to a different course. If this is not possible, then the pilot must move to an altitude where the temperature is above freezing. Answer (A) is incorrect. The best action is to leave the area of precipitation, but, if that is not possible, the pilot should move to an altitude where the temperature is above freezing. Only during a temperature inversion would that be a higher altitude; usually it means descending to a lower altitude. Answer (C) is incorrect. Areas of liquid precipitation do not necessarily equate to areas where the temperature is above freezing. Freezing rain is an excellent example of liquid precipitation that is incredibly hazardous.

questions.question_text: When conditions favoring the formation of ice are present, pilots should check for ice accumulation prior to flight. The best way to do this is by
questions.choice_a: using a flashlight and watching for light reflection.
questions.choice_b: running your hand along all control surfaces.
questions.choice_c: using ice detection lights.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Because ice, particularly clear ice, is difficult to detect on the aircraft's surface, you can shine a flashlight along all visible surfaces of the aircraft and watch for light reflections. This would be an indication of ice on the aircraft's surface. Answer (B) is incorrect. Physically inspecting the control surfaces is a good tactic, but you should not limit your scan merely to the aircraft’s control surfaces as the skin can and will collect ice as well. Additionally, T-tail designs may make a physical inspection of the related control surfaces difficult. Answer (C) is incorrect. Ice detection lights are useful for checking for ice accumulation at night, but they are used during flight, not during the preflight inspection.

questions.question_text: In an aircraft equipped with a pneumatic deicing system, the appropriate technique for removing ice is to
questions.choice_a: operate the pneumatic deicing system several times.
questions.choice_b: operate the pneumatic deicing system once.
questions.choice_c: confirm that ice has accumulated prior to engaging the pneumatic boots.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: To clear accumulated ice as well as residual ice that may be left between cycles, you should operate a pneumatic deicing system several times. Answer (B) is incorrect. To clear accumulated ice as well as residual ice that may be left between cycles, you should operate a pneumatic deicing system several times, not just once. Answer (C) is incorrect. While this technique was formerly accepted as a rule of thumb, current advisory guidance recommends that deicing systems be activated at the first indication of icing rather than after any significant amount has accumulated.

questions.question_text: Clear ice generally forms in outside temperature ranges of
questions.explanation: ' DISCUSSION: Clear ice generally forms when outside temperatures range from 0° C to -10° C. Answer (A) is incorrect. The outside temperature range that is most conducive to clear ice formation is 0° C to -10° C, not colder than -25° C. Answer (B) is incorrect. The outside temperature range that is most conducive to clear ice formation is 0° C to -10° C, not -15 ° to -25° C.

questions.question_text: Freezing drizzle often forms by the
questions.choice_c: condensation-condensation process.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Freezing drizzle is more commonly formed by a process known as the collision-coalescence process. Some droplets in a cloud grow to approximately 30 micrometers in diameter. Then they begin to settle and fall fast enough so that they collide with some smaller droplets. If the droplets coalesce, the result is a larger droplet, which now has an even better chance of capturing smaller droplets. This process can produce drizzle-size drops in a supercooled cloud, usually near the top, where the larger droplets generally are found in any cloud. Answer (B) is incorrect. Freezing drizzle is more commonly formed by a process known as the collision-coalescence process. Answer (C) is incorrect. Freezing drizzle is more commonly formed by a process known as the collision- coalescence process.

questions.question_text: On initial climbout after takeoff with the autopilot engaged, you encounter icing conditions. In this situation you can expect
questions.choice_a: ice to accumulate on the underside of the wings due to the higher AOA.
questions.choice_b: the autopilot to hold the vertical speed, only if the anti-icing boots are working.
questions.choice_c: increased airflow under the wings to prevent the accumulation of ice. 15.10 High-Altitude Weather
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Airplanes are vulnerable to ice accumulation during the initial climbout in icing conditions because lower speeds often translate into a higher Angle of Attack (AOA). This exposes the underside of the airplane and its wings to the icing conditions and allows ice to accumulate further aft than it would in cruise flight. Answer (B) is incorrect. The autopilot will hold the vertical speed if the anti-icing boots are working or not. Therefore, extreme vigilance should be exercised while climbing with the autopilot engaged. Climbing in Vertical Speed (VS) mode in icing conditions is highly discouraged. Answer (C) is incorrect. Lower speeds and a higher AOA will expose the underside of the airplane and its wings to the icing conditions and allows the accumulation of ice; it does not prevent the accumulation of ice.

questions.question_text: What is a characteristic of the troposphere?
questions.choice_a: It contains all the moisture of the atmosphere.
questions.choice_b: There is an overall decrease of temperature with an increase of altitude.
questions.choice_c: The average altitude of the top of the troposphere is about 6 miles.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: The troposphere is the layer from the surface to an average altitude of 7 mi. It is characterized by an overall decrease of temperature with an increase in altitude. Answer (A) is incorrect. Moisture can be found in the stratosphere, as evidenced by some of the largest thunderstorms. Answer (C) is incorrect. The average altitude of the top of the troposphere is about 7 mi., not 6 mi.

questions.question_text: Where is the normal location of the jetstream relative to surface lows and fronts?
questions.choice_a: The jetstream is located north of the surface systems.
questions.choice_b: The jetstream is located south of the low and warm front.
questions.choice_c: The jetstream is located over the low and crosses both the warm front and the cold front.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Strong, long-trajectory jet streams usually are associated with well-developed surface lows and frontal systems beneath upper troughs or lows. The jet stream is to the north as a surface low develops, and the low moves nearer the jet stream as the low deepens. The occluding low moves north of the jet stream, and the jet stream crosses the frontal system near the point of occlusion. . Answer (B) is incorrect. The jet stream is located to the north, not south, of the low and warm front. Answer (C) is incorrect. The jet stream crosses the occlusion of the warm and the cold fronts at the point of occlusion.

questions.question_text: Which feature is associated with the tropopause?
questions.choice_b: Absolute upper limit of cloud formation.
questions.choice_c: Abrupt change of temperature lapse rate.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: The tropopause is characterized by an abrupt change in temperature lapse rate. The temperature above the tropical tropopause increases with height, and the temperature above the polar tropopause remains about constant with height. Answer (A) is incorrect. The jet stream (wind) and clear air turbulence are found extensively in the tropopause. Answer (B) is incorrect. Clouds can be present into the stratosphere, as in very large thunderstorms and cirrus clouds made up of ice crystals.

questions.question_text: What weather feature occurs at altitude levels near the tropopause?
questions.choice_a: Maximum winds and narrow wind shear zones.
questions.choice_b: Abrupt temperature increase above the tropopause.
questions.choice_c: Thin layers of cirrus (ice crystal) clouds at the tropopause level.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Temperature and wind vary greatly in the vicinity of the tropopause. Maximum winds generally occur near the tropopause. These winds create narrow zones of wind shear, which often generate hazardous turbulence. Answer (B) is incorrect. Abrupt temperature increase does not occur above the tropopause. Answer (C) is incorrect. Thin layers of cirrus (ice crystal) clouds can develop at altitudes below the tropopause level and extend into the lower stratosphere.

questions.question_text: Where do the maximum winds associated with the jetstream usually occur?
questions.choice_a: _\n the vicinity of breaks in the tropopause on the polar side of the jet core.
questions.choice_b: Below the jet core where a long straight stretch of the jetstream is located.
questions.choice_c: On the equatorial side of the jetstream where moisture has formed cirriform clouds.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Maximum winds associated with the jet stream occur in the vicinity of breaks between the tropical and polar tropopause. Maximum wind speed in the jet stream is found in the core on the polar side due to very strong temperature gradients. Answer (B) is incorrect. In the jet stream, the maximum winds are found in, not below, the core. Answer (C) is incorrect. When moisture is available, cirriform clouds will form on the upward motion of air of the jet stream on the equatorial side. This will occur in the slower winds of the jet stream.

questions.question_text: Where are jetstreams normally located?
questions.choice_a: \n areas of strong low pressure systems in the stratosphere.
questions.choice_b: Ina break in the tropopause where intensified temperature gradients are located.
questions.choice_c: Inasingle continuous band, encircling the Earth, where there is a break between the equatorial and polar tropopause.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: The jet stream is a narrow, shallow, meandering river of maximum winds extending around the Earth in a wavelike pattern. The jet stream typically occurs in a break in the tropopause in an area of intensified temperature gradients characteristic of the break. Answer (A) is incorrect. A jet stream is located in a break in the tropopause, not in the stratosphere. Answer (C) is incorrect. There may be more than one jet stream at any time; up to three at one time are not uncommon.

questions.question_text: A jet stream is a riverlike flow of high-altitude wind following the planetary atmospheric wave patterns with speeds of
questions.choice_c: 100 knots or greater.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: To be classified as a jet stream, the concentrated winds must be 50 knots or greater. Answer (B) is incorrect. The jet stream is classified as a riverlike flow of high-altitude wind with concentrated winds at a minimum of 50 knots, not 71 knots. Answer (C) is incorrect. The jet stream is classified as a riverlike flow of high-altitude wind with concentrated winds at a minimum of 50 knots, not 100 knots.

questions.question_text: Clear air turbulence associated with a jet stream is
questions.choice_a: similar to that associated with a tropical maritime front.
questions.choice_b: most commonly found in temperatures between -40° and -50° C.
questions.choice_c: most commonly found in the vicinity of the tropopause.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Clear air turbulence (CAT) associated with a jet stream is most often found in the vicinity of the tropopause, which is the boundary layer between the troposphere and the stratosphere. Answer (A) is incorrect. CAT associated with the jet stream is not most commonly associated with a tropical maritime front. Rather, it is most often found in the vicinity of the tropopause, which is the boundary between the troposphere and the stratosphere. Answer (B) is incorrect. Temperature is not a primary factor that affects CAT. However, CAT that occurs in the jet stream is often associated with proximity to the tropopause.

questions.question_text: Which type frontal system is normally crossed by the jetstream?
questions.choice_c: Occluded front.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: The development of a surface low usually is south of the jet stream and moves nearer as the low deepens. The occluding low moves north of the jet stream, and the jet stream crosses the frontal system near the point of occlusion. Answer (A) is incorrect. The jet stream crosses an occluded front, not both warm and cold fronts. Answer (B) is incorrect. The jet normally crosses an occluded, not a warm, front.

questions.question_text: Which type clouds may be associated with the jetstream?
questions.choice_a: Cumulonimbus cloud line where the jetstream crosses the cold front.
questions.choice_b: Cirrus clouds on the equatorial side of the jetstream.
questions.choice_c: Cirrostratus cloud band on the polar side and under the jetstream.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Air travels in a “corkscrew” path around the jet stream with upward motion on the equatorial side. Thus, when high-level moisture is available, cirrus clouds form on the equatorial side of the jet stream. Answer (A) is incorrect. Cirriform, not cumulonimbus, clouds are associated with the jet stream. Answer (C) is incorrect. Cirrostratus clouds form on the equatorial, not polar, side of the jet stream.

questions.question_text: A strong wind shear can be expected
questions.choice_a: on the low pressure side of a 100-knot jetstream core.
questions.choice_b: where the horizontal wind shear is 15 knots, in a distance equal to 2.5° longitude.
questions.choice_c: if the 5°C isotherms are spaced 100 NM or closer together.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Wind speed decreases outward from the jet stream core, and the rate of decrease is greater on the polar (low-pressure) side than on the equatorial (high-pressure) side. Thus, a strong wind shear can be expected on the low-pressure side of a 100-kt. jet stream core. Answer (B) is incorrect. On a tropopause data chart, a strong wind shear can be expected where the horizontal wind shear is greater than 18 kt., not 15 kt., in a distance equal to 2.5° longitude (or about 150 NM). Answer (C) is incorrect. On a tropopause data chart, a strong wind shear can be expected if the 20-kt. isotachs, not 5°C isotherms, are spaced 100 NM or closer. Answer (A) is the best answer. (AVW Chap 13) DISCUSSION: In a standard atmosphere, the tropopause has a temperature of -56.5°C and a height of about 36,000 feet above the Earth. While both the temperature and altitude ranges of the tropopause are quite large, this answer choice is the best answer to the question given its inclusion of the standard temperature for the tropopause. Answer (B) is incorrect. In a standard atmosphere, the tropopause has a temperature of -56.5°C, which is outside of the range defined by this answer choice. Answer (C) is incorrect. In a standard atmosphere, the tropopause has a temperature of -56.5°C, which is warmer than the temperature defined by this answer choice.

questions.question_text: The tropopause is generally found when the free air temperatures are
questions.choice_c: colder than -60° C.
questions.explanation: In mid-latitudes, wind speed in the jet stream averages considerably stronger in winter than in summer. Answer (A) is incorrect. The jet stream averages considerably less in summer. Answer (B) is incorrect. The jet stream averages considerably stronger in winter.

questions.question_text: In the mid-latitudes, wind speed in the jet stream averages considerably less in the
questions.choice_c: summer.

questions.question_text: The three jet streams are
questions.choice_a: the polar front jetstream, the subtropical jetstream, and the polar night jetstream.
questions.choice_b: the polar front jetstream, the subtropical jetstream, and the polar jetstream.
questions.choice_c: the polar jetstream, the Tropic of Cancer jetstream, and the Maritime jetstream.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: There are three jetstreams. The polar front jetstream is associated with the polar front or the division between the cold polar and warm tropical air masses. The subtropical jetstream is a very persistent circumpolar jetstream found on the northern periphery of the tropical latitudes between 20° and 30° north latitude. The polar night jetstream is found in the stratosphere in the vicinity of the Arctic Circle during the winter months. Answer (B) is incorrect. There is a polar front jetstream, but there is no polar jetstream. Answer (C) is incorrect. The Tropic of Cancer is the most northerly circle of latitude on Earth. At this location, the Sun may appear directly overhead at its highest or lowest point. There is no Maritime jetstream. There are maritime air masses which are moist air masses located over water.

questions.question_text: Which type jet stream can be expected to cause the greater turbulence?
questions.choice_a: Accurving jet stream associated with a deep low pressure trough.
questions.choice_b: Astraight jet stream associated with a high pressure ridge.
questions.choice_c: Ajet stream associated with a wide isotherm spacing.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: The jet stream cirrus shield is its association with turbulence. Extensive cirrus cloudiness often occurs with deepening surface and upper lows. These deepening systems produce the greatest turbulence. Answer (B) is incorrect. Jet streams are normally boomerang shaped and not straight. Answer (C) is incorrect. Wide isotherm spacing indicates wind speeds are lower than the contour lines that are closer together.

questions.question_text: The decrease in wind speed from the core of the jet stream is the greatest on
questions.choice_a: the acute angle side of the jet stream.
questions.choice_b: the equatorial side of the jet stream.
questions.choice_c: the polar side of the jet stream.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: The rate of decreased wind speed is considerably greater on the polar side than on the equatorial side. The magnitude of wind shear is greater on the polar side than on the equatorial side. Answer (A) is incorrect. The rate of decreased wind speed is considerably greater on the polar side. Answer (B) is incorrect. The rate of decreased wind speed is less on the equatorial side than on the polar side.

questions.question_text: An upper level wind cannot be considered a jet stream unless the wind speed is at least
questions.choice_c: 65 knots.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: By definition, the concentrated winds must be 50 knots or greater to be classified as a jet stream. Answer (A) is incorrect. To be classified as a jet stream, concentrated winds must be 50 knots or greater, not 35 knots. Answer (C) is incorrect. Although 65 knots winds may be present within a jet stream, to be classified as a jet stream, the concentrated winds must be 50 knots or greater.

questions.question_text: With high altitude moisture present, cirrus clouds will form
questions.choice_a: onthe acute angle side of the jet stream.
questions.choice_b: onthe equatorial side of the jet stream.
questions.choice_c: on the polar side of the jet stream.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Air travels in a corkscrew path around the jet core with upward motion on the equatorial side. When high level moisture is available, cirriform clouds form on the equatorial side of the jet stream. Answer (A) is incorrect. Cirriform clouds form on the equatorial side of the jet stream whenever high level moisture is present. Answer (C) is incorrect. Cirriform clouds form on the equatorial side of the jet stream.

questions.question_text: The tropopause varies in height from
questions.choice_a: 15,000 feet or below at the poles to 70,000 feet at the equator.
questions.choice_b: 20,000 feet or below at the poles to 65,000 feet at the equator.
questions.choice_c: 32,000 feet or below at the poles to 320,000 feet at the equator.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: The height of the tropopause varies in height from about 20,000 feet over the poles to about 65,000 feet over the equator. It is higher in the summer than in winter. Answer (A) is incorrect. The stratosphere is beyond 65,000 feet over the equator. Answer (C) is incorrect. Above 65,000 feet lie the stratosphere, mesosphere and thermosphere.

questions.question_text: Which area or areas of the Northern Hemisphere experience a generally east to west movement of weather systems?
questions.choice_c: Subtropical only.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: The high-pressure belt at about 30° north latitude forces air outward at the surface to the north and to the south. The southward moving air is deflected by the Coriolis force, becoming the well-known subtropical northeast (moving northeast to southwest) trade winds. In the arctic region (60° north latitude and above), the heavier, denser air moves south at a low level toward the Equator but is turned to the right by the Coriolis force, causing the low-level polar easterlies (moving east to west). Answer (A) is incorrect. In the Northern Hemisphere, both the arctic and subtropical areas experience a general east-to- west movement of weather systems. Answer (C) is incorrect. In the Northern Hemisphere, both the arctic and subtropical areas experience a general east-to-west movement of weather systems.

questions.question_text: Summer thunderstorms in the arctic region will generally move
questions.choice_a: northeast to southwest in polar easterlies.
questions.choice_b: southwest to northeast with the jetstream flow.
questions.choice_c: directly north to south with the low-level polar airflow.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: During summer afternoons, scattered cumulus clouds forming over the interior of the arctic region occasionally grow into thunderstorms. These thunderstorms generally move from northeast to southwest in the polar easterlies, which is opposite the general movement in midlatitudes. Answer (B) is incorrect. Thunderstorms in the Arctic generally move northeast to southwest in the polar easterlies, not southwest to northeast with the jet stream flow. Answer (C) is incorrect. Thunderstorms in the Arctic generally move northeast to southwest in polar easterlies, not directly north to south.

questions.question_text: Which arctic flying hazard is caused when a cloud layer of uniform thickness overlies a snow or ice covered surface?
questions.choice_c: Blowing snow.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Whiteout is a visibility-restricting phenomenon that occurs in the Arctic when a cloud layer of uniform thickness overlies a snow- or ice-covered surface. Parallel rays of the sun are broken up and diffused when passing through the cloud layer so that they strike the snow surface from many angles, resulting in a loss of depth perception. Answer (A) is incorrect. Ice fog forms in moist air during extremely cold conditions. It is not formed by a cloud layer overlying a snow-covered surface. Answer (C) is incorrect. Blowing snow is snow that is blown by light or greater winds, causing decreased visibility. It is not formed by a cloud layer overlying a snow-covered surface.

questions.question_text: Whiteout conditions occur when
questions.choice_a: light is reflected back and forth countless times between snow and clouds, eliminating all shadows.
questions.choice_b: the low angle of the sun’s rays strike the snow surface and eliminate shadows to the eye due to the “prism effect.”
questions.choice_c: snow eliminates the color contrast of the surface and clouds during daytime.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: “Whiteout” is a visibility restricting phenomenon that occurs in the Arctic when a layer of cloudiness of uniform thickness overlies a snow or ice covered surface. Parallel rays of the sun are broken up and diffused when passing through the cloud layer so that they strike the snow surface from many angles. The diffused light then reflects back and forth countless times between the snow and the cloud, eliminating all shadows. Answer (B) is incorrect. In the phenomenon known as “whiteout,” parallel rays of the sun are broken up and diffused when passing through the cloud layer so that they strike the snow surface from many angles. The diffused light then reflects back and forth countless times between the snow and the cloud, eliminating all shadows. Answer (C) is incorrect. The result of “whiteout” is a loss of depth perception. Buildings, people, and dark colored objects appear to float in the air, and the horizon disappears.

questions.question_text: A cyclone is
questions.choice_a: a hurricane force storm in the Indian Ocean with highest sustained winds of 64 knots or higher.
questions.choice_b: a tropical depression in the Northwest Pacific with sustained winds of 63 knots.
questions.choice_c: a tropical storm in the Atlantic with highest sustained winds of 35 through 64 knots.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Tropical cyclone is a general term for any low that originates over tropical oceans. Tropical cyclones are Classified according to their intensity based on average 1-minute wind speeds. Tropical cyclone international classifications are 1. Tropical Depression—highest sustained winds up to 33 knots; 2. Tropical Storm—highest sustained winds of 34 through 63 knots; and 3. Hurricane, Cyclone, or Typhoon-highest sustained winds 64 knots or more. Strong tropical cyclones are known by different names in different regions of the world. A tropical cyclone in the Atlantic and eastern Pacific is a “hurricane”; in the western Pacific, “typhoon”; near Australia and in the Indian Ocean, simply “cyclone.” Answer (B) is incorrect. A tropical depression is defined by having sustained winds up to 33 knots, not 63 knots. Answer (C) is incorrect. A tropical storm is classified according to intensity based on average 1-minute maximum sustained wind speed. At 64 knots, the cyclone will be designated as a hurricane.

questions.question_text: Which weather condition is present when the tropical storm is upgraded to a hurricane?
questions.choice_b: Aclear area or hurricane eye has formed.
questions.choice_c: Sustained winds of 64 knots or more.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Tropical cyclones are classified by their intensity based on average 1-min. wind speeds. To be classified as a hurricane, the cyclone must have sustained winds of 64 kt. or more. Answer (A) is incorrect. Tropical cyclones are classified based on the sustained winds, not the highest wind speed. Answer (B) is incorrect. A clear area, or eye, usually forms in the tropical storm stage and continues through the hurricane stage.

questions.question_text: What is the general direction of movement of a hurricane located in the Caribbean or Gulf of Mexico region?
questions.choice_b: Westerly, until encountering land, then easterly.
questions.choice_c: Counterclockwise over open water, then dissipating outward over land.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Hurricanes located in the Caribbean or Gulf of Mexico move in a direction between west and northwest while in low latitudes. As the hurricane moves toward the midlatitudes, the prevailing westerlies will gain control, and the hurricane will curve to the northeast. Answer (B) is incorrect. A hurricane will curve easterly because of prevailing winds, not because of land. Answer (C) is incorrect. The windflow in the hurricane is counterclockwise, not the general movement of the hurricane itself.

questions.question_text: A cyclone is similar to a tropical storm in
questions.choice_a: the northern Pacific with winds up to 64 knots.
questions.choice_b: the Indian Ocean with winds 65 knots or more.
questions.choice_c: the north Atlantic with winds 35 to 64 knots.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: A strong tropical cyclone (with winds of 65 knots or more) is known in the Indian Ocean as a “cyclone.” Answer (A) is incorrect. Systems with winds up to 64 knots are known as “tropical storms” and not “cyclones.” Also, this answer references the Pacific Ocean, but “cyclones” as referred to in this question exist in the Indian Ocean. Answer (C) is incorrect. Systems with winds from 35 to 64 knots are known as “tropical storms” and not “cyclones.” Also, this answer references the Atlantic Ocean, but “cyclones” as referred to in this question exist in the Indian Ocean.

questions.question_text: A hurricane has formed when
questions.choice_a: the highest sustained winds are 64 knots or more.
questions.choice_b: the winds around a pressure storm have averaged 48 knots for over 48 hours.
questions.choice_c: the barometric pressure in the center of the storm is 35 mb lower than pressure outside the weather feature.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: A hurricane or typhoon has the highest sustained winds of 64 knots or more. Answer (B) is incorrect. A tropical storm has a highest sustained wind of 34 through 63 knots. The length of time is not an issue. Answer (C) is incorrect. The category of a storm is based on the sustained wind speed, not mb pressures.

questions.question_text: Tropical storms form when
questions.choice_a: asystem of air surrounds an area with pressure of 48 mb.
questions.choice_b: asystem has sustained winds of 34 to 63 knots.
questions.choice_c: asystem has peak wind gusts that do not exceed 65 knots.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: The classification of a tropical storm is based upon sustained winds of 34 through 63 knots. Answer (A) is incorrect. Classification of tropical cyclones are not based on areas of pressure. They are based on sustained wind speed. Answer (C) is incorrect. Each tropical storm classification is based upon the lowest sustained wind speed to the highest sustained wind speed, not peak gusts that do not exceed a certain speed.

questions.question_text: A tropical cyclone is
questions.choice_a: a tropical storm in the north Atlantic west of Newfoundland with highest sustained winds of 25 through 33 knots.
questions.choice_b: a tropical depression in the Northern Pacific near Dutch Harbor with sustained winds of 63 knots.
questions.choice_c: a hurricane force storm in the Pacific Ocean southwest of Hawaii with highest sustained winds of 65 knots or higher.
questions.explanation: A hurricane force storm in the area stated, with sustained winds of 64 knots or higher, may be classified as a tropical cyclone. | Tropical cyclone is a general term for a rotating, organized system of clouds and thunderstorms that originates over tropical or subtropical waters. The tropical cyclone international classifications include: a. Tropical Depression — highest sustained winds up to 33 knots b. Tropical Storm — highest sustained winds of 34 to 63 knots c. Hurricane, Cyclone, or Typhoon - highest sustained winds 64 knots or more Answer (A) is incorrect. A tropical storm has highest sustained winds of 34 through 63 knots, not 25 through 34 knots. Answer (B) is incorrect. A tropical depression has sustained winds of up to 33 knots, not 63 knots.

questions.question_text: Gusts in tropical cyclones can exceed the average one-minute wind speed by as much as
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Tropical cyclones are classified according to their intensity based on average one-minute wind speeds. Wind gusts in these storms may be as much as 50% higher than the average one-minute wind speeds. Answer (B) is incorrect. Wind gusts in these storms may be as much as 50% higher than the average one-minute wind speeds, not 75%. Answer (C) is incorrect. Wind gusts in these storms may be as much as 50% higher than the average one- minute wind speeds, not 100%.

questions.question_text: Which weather condition is present when a tropical storm is upgraded to a typhoon?
questions.choice_a: Winds around the pressure center have averaged 48 knots or more for 48 continuous hours.
questions.choice_b: The storm has sustained winds of 64 knots or more.
questions.choice_c: Barometric pressure at the center is 35 mb lower than the surrounding pressure.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: To be categorized as a hurricane or a typhoon, the storm must have highest sustained winds of 64 knots or more. Answer (A) is incorrect. With winds of 48 knots, this storm would be a tropical storm, not a typhoon. Answer (C) is incorrect. Storm classification is not based on barometric pressure but on sustained wind speed.

questions.question_text: You know that a tropical depression has formed when
questions.choice_a: barometric pressure in the center of the storm is 10 mb less than the surrounding pressure.
questions.choice_b: winds around the storm have averaged 28 knots for 24 hours.
questions.choice_c: the highest sustained winds having reached 34 knots. END OF STUDY UNIT
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Tropical cyclones are classified by wind speed. A tropical depression has highest sustained winds of 34 knots. Answer (A) is incorrect. Winds of 28 knots are not categorized as any tropical cyclone. Answer (B) is incorrect. Barometiic pressure is not a criteria of classifying a tropical cyclone.

questions.question_text: Which type of weather can only be directly observed during flight and then reported in a PIREP?
questions.choice_c: Level of the tropopause and turbulence.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Pilots are urged to volunteer directly observed reports of thunderstorms, structural icing, turbulence, wind shear, cloud bases (tops and layers), visibility, precipitation, wind, and temperature at altitude. Answer (B) is incorrect. A pilot would not be able to determine from observation if jet stream-type winds were encountered. Answer (C) is incorrect. The level of the tropopause is determined by radiosondes released by ground weather observing stations. It is not a type of weather that can be directly observed by a pilot during flight.

questions.question_text: What type turbulence should be reported when it momentarily causes slight, erratic changes in altitude and/or attitude, one-third to two-thirds of the time?
questions.choice_c: Intermittent light turbulence.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Light turbulence is defined as turbulence that momentarily causes slight, erratic changes in altitude and/or attitude. Intermittent is defined as occurring from one-third to two- thirds of the time. Answer (A) is incorrect. Light chop does not cause any appreciable changes in altitude and/or attitude, and occasional is less than one-third of the time. Answer (B) is incorrect. Moderate chop does not cause any appreciable changes in altitude and/or attitude.

questions.question_text: What type turbulence should be reported when it causes slight, rapid, and somewhat rhythmic bumpiness without appreciable changes in attitude or altitude, less than one-third of the time?
questions.choice_c: Moderate chop.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Light chop is turbulence that causes slight, rapid, and somewhat rhythmic bumpiness without appreciable changes in attitude or altitude. Occasional is defined as occurring less than one-third of the time. Answer (B) is incorrect. Moderate turbulence causes a change in the aircraft's attitude and/or altitude. Answer (C) is incorrect. Moderate chop causes rapid, not rhythmic, bumps or jolts, which are not “slight.”

questions.question_text: What type turbulence should be reported when it causes changes in altitude and/or attitude more than two-thirds of the time, with the aircraft remaining in positive control at all times?
questions.choice_c: Intermittent moderate turbulence.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Moderate turbulence is defined as turbulence that causes changes in altitude and/or attitude, but the aircraft remains in positive control at all times. Continuous is defined as occurring more than two-thirds of the time. Answer (A) is incorrect. Severe chop is not a turbulence reporting term. Answer (C) is incorrect. Intermittent means that turbulence is occurring from one-third to two-thirds of the time.

questions.question_text: KFTW UA/OV DFW/TM 1645/FL100/TP PA30/ SK SCT031- TOP043/BKN060-TOP085/OVC097- TOPUNKN/WX FVOOSM RA/TA 07. This pilot report to Fort Worth (KFTW) indicates
questions.choice_a: the ceiling at KDFW is 6,000 feet.
questions.choice_b: that the top of the ceiling is 4,300 feet.
questions.choice_c: the aircraft is in light rain.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: A ceiling is defined as the height (AGL) of the lowest broken or overcast layer aloft or vertical visibility into an obscuration. The PIREP reports the lowest broken layer at 6,000 ft. MSL (BKNO60). This indicates the ceiling is 6,000 ft. A ceiling is normally defined as the height above ground, but in a PIREP all altitudes are MSL unless otherwise indicated. Answer (B) is incorrect. The top of the scattered (SCT) layer, not the broken (BKN) layer is 4,300 ft. A ceiling is the lowest broken or overcast layer aloft. Answer (C) is incorrect. The flight visibility and weather (/WX) section of the PIREP is coded as /WX FVOOSM RA, which means the flight visibility is O SM in rain. Light rain would be coded as -RA.

questions.question_text: Maximum Turbulence Potential forecasts
questions.choice_a: are only as accurate as the computer model.
questions.choice_b: exhibit the same accuracy during all 24 hours of the day.
questions.choice_c: display all turbulence well.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Accuracy is dependent on available PIREPs. As PIREPs decrease at night, and increase during the day, the accuracy of the forecast varies. Answer (B) is incorrect. The accuracy of the analysis is dependent on PIREPs, which are fewer at night, causing the accuracy of the forecast to decrease. Answer (C) is incorrect. The product is only for turbulence associated with upper level fronts and jet streams while other known causes of turbulence are not forecast.

questions.question_text: What determines how icing is reported on a PIREP?
questions.choice_c: Rate of accumulation. 16.2 In-Flight Weather Advisories
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Icing intensities are classified based on the rate of icing accumulation. Answer (A) is incorrect. The type of icing accumulation can be listed in the PIREP remarks, but not in the the primary icing group. Answer (B) is incorrect. The thickness of ice would likely not be reported in a PIREP since that information does not provide direct information to pilots on what conditions to expect. Also, it is usually very difficult for a pilot to accurately estimate the thickness of the accumulated ice.

questions.question_text: The Hazardous Inflight Weather Advisory Service (HIWAS) is a continuous broadcast over selected VORs of
questions.choice_a: SIGMETs, CONVECTIVE SIGMETs, AIRMETs, Severe Weather Forecast Alerts (AWW), and Center Weather Advisories (CWA).
questions.choice_b: SIGMETs, CONVECTIVE SIGMETs, AIRMETs, Wind Shear Advisories, and Severe Weather Forecast Alerts (AWW).
questions.choice_c: Wind Shear Advisories, Radar Weather Reports, SIGMETs, CONVECTIVE SIGMETs, AIRMETs, and Center Weather Advisories (CWA).
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: The Hazardous Inflight Weather Advisory Service (HIWAS) is a continuous broadcast service over selected VORs of in-flight weather advisories, i.e., SIGMETs, convective SIGMETs, AIRMETs, severe weather forecast alerts (AWW), and center weather advisories (CWA). Answer (B) is incorrect. A runway wind shear advisory is given by a tower controller, not HIWAS, to arriving and departing aircraft. Answer (C) is incorrect. Radar weather reports are available from Flight Service Stations (FSSs), not HIWAS.

questions.question_text: En route weather advisories should be obtained from an FSS on
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Flight Service Stations (FSSs) provide communications capabilities for aircraft flying en route on a common frequency of 122.2 MHz. FSSs are normally available from 6 a.m. to 10 p.m. local time. Answer (A) is incorrect. This is normally a remote frequency used by FSS at a NAVAID site. A pilot transmits on 122.1 MHz, and the FSS receives on this frequency. The FSS transmits back to the pilot on the NAVAID frequency. Answer (C) is incorrect. The CTAF frequency used by an FSS located on an airport without an operating control tower is 123.6 MHz.

questions.question_text: At what time are current AIRMETs broadcast in their entirety by the Hazardous Inflight Weather Advisory Service (HIWAS)?
questions.choice_a: 15 and 45 minutes after the hour during the first hour after issuance, and upon receipt.
questions.choice_b: Every 15 minutes until the AIRMET is canceled.
questions.choice_c: There is a continuous broadcast over selected VORs of Inflight Weather Advisories.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: HIWAS is a continuous broadcast over selected VORs of in-flight weather advisories including AIRMETs, SIGMETs, convective SIGMETs, center weather advisories, urgent PIREPs, and summarized severe weather forecast alerts. Answer (A) is incorrect. In those areas without HIWAS, an FSS will broadcast a current AIRMET in its entirety upon receipt and at 15 and 45 min. past the hour during the first hour of issuance. Answer (B) is incorrect. A HIWAS is a continuous broadcast, not every 15 min., of in-flight weather advisories.

questions.question_text: The Telephone Information Briefing Service (TIBS) recordings are provided by selected Automated Flight Service Stations and
questions.choice_b: are designed to replace the standard briefing given by a flight service specialist.
questions.choice_c: contain area briefings encompassing a 50 NM radius.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: TIBS recordings contain area briefings that may encompass a service area that has been configured to meet the individual facility’s needs, e.g., encompassing a 50-NM radius. In some instances, the service area is not defined by a nautical mile radius but rather as a geographic location, such as Northwest Nebraska. Answer (A) is incorrect. TIBS information is monitored continuously, with recordings being updated as often as twice per hour. Answer (B) is incorrect. TIBS recordings are provided as a preliminary briefing to assist in making a “go or no-go” decision. TIBS recordings are not intended as a substitute for a specialist- provided preflight briefing.

questions.question_text: What conditions are indicated on a Weather Depiction Chart?
questions.choice_a: Actual sky cover, visibility restrictions, and type of precipitation at reporting stations.
questions.choice_b: Forecast ceilings and visibilities over a large geographic area.
questions.choice_c: Actual en route weather conditions between reporting stations.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: The weather depiction chart is computer prepared from the observations reported by both manual and automated observation locations to provide a broad overview of observed conditions as of the time the chart was prepared. The chart indicates actual sky cover, visibility restrictions, and type of precipitation at reporting stations. Answer (B) is incorrect. The weather depiction chart is not a forecast but an overview of observed conditions at a specified time. Answer (C) is incorrect. The weather depiction chart indicates actual weather conditions at a reporting station only as of a specified time.

questions.question_text: What is indicated on the Weather Depiction Chart by a continuous solid line enclosing a shaded geographic area?
questions.choice_a: The entire area has ceilings less than 3,000 feet and/or visibility less than 5 miles.
questions.choice_b: More than 50 percent of the area enclosed by the smooth line is predicted to have IFR conditions.
questions.choice_c: Reporting stations within the enclosed area are all showing IFR conditions at the time of the report.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: A continuous solid line enclosing a shaded area indicates that all reporting stations within the enclosed area are reporting IFR weather conditions at the time of the report. Answer (A) is incorrect. The weather depiction chart indicates observed weather at reporting stations at the time of the chart. There may be some areas without a reporting station within the enclosed area that are not IMC. Answer (B) is incorrect. The weather depiction chart reflects only observed weather, not forecast weather, at reporting stations. Answer (C) is the best answer. (AWS Sect 5) DISCUSSION: The station in southwest Louisiana is reporting a sky cover that is scattered with the base of the lowest layer at 3,200 ft. Since the area is not contoured, the conditions are VFR. Answer (A) is incorrect. A station with the visibility data missing means only that visibility is greater than 6 SM, not 20 SM, and most of the coast is reporting clear skies, not ceilings at or above 20,000 ft. Answer (B) is incorrect. The station in southeast Louisiana is reporting overcast, not broken, skies with a ceiling of 1,600, not 3,200, ft.

questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 150 on page 897.) The Weather Depiction Chart indicates that the coastal sections of Texas and Louisiana are reporting
questions.choice_a: all ceilings at or above 20,000 feet with visibilities of 20 miles or more.
questions.choice_b: marginal VFR conditions due to broken ceilings of 3,200 feet.
questions.choice_c: VFR conditions with scattered clouds at 3,200 feet.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: The area off the northern tip of Michigan shows obscured skies, by the “X” in the station circle, and fog, by the “=” symbol to the left of the station circle. Since the area is shaded, the conditions are IFR. Answer (A) is incorrect. An “X” in the station model indicates an obscured, not overcast, sky, and “=” indicates fog, not haze. Answer (B) is incorrect. The weather depiction chart does not indicate convective action, only sky conditions and visibility.

questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 150 on page 897.) The IFR conditions in the vicinity of Lakes Superior, Huron, and Michigan were caused by
questions.choice_b: convective action during the front’s passage.
questions.choice_c: obscured skies and fog.

questions.question_text: The U.S. Low-Level Significant Weather Prognostic Chart depicts weather conditions
questions.choice_a: that are forecast to exist at a specific time shown on the chart.
questions.choice_b: as they existed at the time the chart was prepared.
questions.choice_c: that are forecast to exist 6 hours after the chart was prepared.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: A low-level prognostic chart contains four panels: the two on the left are for 12 hr. after the time of issuance, and the two on the right are for 24 hr. after issuance. The two on the top are significant weather progs, and the two on the bottom are surface progs. Answer (B) is incorrect. Prognostic charts forecast conditions, not report observed conditions (as does a weather _ depiction chart). Answer (C) is incorrect. The low-level prognostic chart forecasts conditions 12 and 24 hr., not 6 hr., after the time of issuance.

questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 151 on page 899.) The 12-Hour Significant Weather Prognostic Chart indicates that eastern Kentucky and eastern Tennessee can expect probable ceilings
questions.choice_a: less than 1,000 feet and/or visibility less than 3 miles.
questions.choice_b: less than 1,000 feet and/or visibility less than 3 miles, and moderate turbulence below 10,000 feet MSL.
questions.choice_c: less than 1,000 feet and/or visibility less than 3 miles, and moderate turbulence above 10,000 feet MSL.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: The 12-hour significant weather prognostic is on panel A of Fig. 151. Eastern Kentucky and eastern Tennessee are enclosed in a solid line, which indicates IFR, i.e., ceiling less than 1,000 ft. and/or visibility less than 3 SM. Answer (B) is incorrect. No turbulence is forecast for eastern Kentucky and eastern Tennessee on the 12-hr. significant weather prog. Turbulence is indicated by a dashed line, such as the one that includes parts of western Kentucky and Tennessee. Answer (C) is incorrect. No turbulence is forecast for eastern Kentucky and eastern Tennessee. Turbulence is indicated by a dashed line such as the one that includes parts of western Kentucky and Tennessee.

questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 151 on page 899.) The chart symbols over southern California on the 12-Hour Significant Weather Prognostic Chart indicate
questions.choice_a: expected top of moderate turbulent layer to be 12,000 feet MSL.
questions.choice_b: expected base of moderate turbulent layer to be 12,000 feet MSL.
questions.choice_c: light turbulence expected above 12,000 feet MSL.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: On the 12-hour significant weather prognostic chart (panel A of Fig. 151), the symbol “~A..” indicates moderate turbulence. The expected top and base of the turbulent layer appear above and below a short line in hundreds of ft. MSL. Absence of a figure below the line (as here) indicates turbulence from the surface up.
