No figure above the line indicates turbulence extending above the upper limit of the chart. Thus, in southern California, moderate turbulence is expected from the surface up to 12,000 ft. MSL. Answer (B) is incorrect. The top of the turbulence, not the base, is 12,000 ft. MSL. Answer (C) is incorrect. The symbol shows moderate, not light, turbulence that is below, not above, 12,000 ft. MSL.

questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 151 on page 899.) The 12-Hour Significant Weather Prognostic Chart indicates that West Virginia will likely experience
questions.choice_a: continuous or showery precipitation covering half or more of the area.
questions.choice_b: thunderstorms and rain showers covering half or more of the area.
questions.choice_c: continuous rain covering less than half of the area.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: The shaded area covering West Virginia on the 12-hour surface prognostic chart (panel B of Fig. 151) indicates 50% or more coverage (because of the shading). The two solid dots indicate continuous rain. The small dot over the triangle indicates rain showers. Answer (B) is incorrect. Thunderstorms are indicated by an R with an arrow on its front leg. Answer (C) is incorrect. Coverage of less than half of the area is indicated by the absence of shading. An area of continuous rain is outlined in a solid, not dash-dot, line.

questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 151 on page 899.) A planned low-altitude flight from central Oklahoma to western Tennessee at 1200Z is likely to encounter
questions.choice_a: continuous or intermittent rain or rain showers, moderate turbulence, and freezing temperatures below 8,000 feet.
questions.choice_b: continuous or showery rain over half or more of the area, moderate turbulence, and freezing temperatures above 10,000 feet.
questions.choice_c: showery precipitation covering less than half the area, no turbulence below 18,000 feet, and freezing temperatures above 12,000 feet.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: In Fig. 151, 1200Z is on the right panels. The lower panel (panel D) shows continuous rain as indicated by two dots in the shaded area. Additionally, rain showers are shown by the triangle with the dot above it. Now look on panel C. The route of flight appears to be halfway between the 8,000-ft. freezing level and the 12,000-ft. freezing level. Thus, freezing temperatures should be above 10;000 feet. Also, there is a symbol indicating moderate turbulence. Answer (A) is incorrect. The route of flight is south of the 8,000-ft. freezing level. Answer (C) is incorrect. The shading indicates more, not less, than 50% rain coverage, the turbulence IS up to, not above, 18,000 ft., and temperatures are probably freezing above 10,000 ft., not 12,000 feet.

questions.question_text: What wind direction and speed aloft are forecast by this WINDS AND TEMPERATURE ALOFT FORECAST (FB) for FL 390 - “750649”?
questions.choice_c: 150° at 6 knots.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Coded directions with wind speed greater than 100 kt. range from 51 to 86. Fifty must be subtracted from the direction group and 100 added to the speed group. In this forecast, the wind direction is 250° (75 — 50 = 25), and the wind speed is 106 kt. (100 + 06 = 106). Answer (A) is incorrect. Direction is the first two, not three, digits, and 50 must be subtracted from the first two digits. Speed is the second group of digits, not the fourth and fifth digits. Answer (C) is incorrect. Fifty must be subtracted from the first two digits and 100 added to the second two digits because the wind speed is forecast to be greater than 100 kt.

questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 149 on page 901.) What is the forecast temperature at ATL for the 3,000-foot level?
questions.choice_c: Not reported.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: In the Winds and Temperature Aloft Forecasts, no temperatures are forecast for the 3,000-ft. level or for a level within 2,500 ft. of station elevation. Answer (A) is incorrect. No temperature is reported at the 3,000-ft. level or for a level within 2,500 ft. of station elevation. The 06 indicates wind velocity of 6 kt. Answer (B) is incorrect. No temperature is reported at the 3,000-ft. level, and temperatures are depicted in degrees Celsius, not Fahrenheit. The 06 indicates wind velocity of 6 kt.

questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 149 on page 901.) What approximate wind direction, speed, and temperature (relative to ISA) are expected for a flight over TUS at FL 270?
questions.choice_c: 010° true; 5 knots; ISA +13°C.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: For conditions at FL 270 over TUS in Fig. 149, interpolate between values at FL 240 and FL 300. First decode the two given flight levels: FL 240 = 050° at OS5kt. and -17°C FL 300 = 330° at O5kt. and -33°C Difference = 80° at Okt. and -16°C Interpolation for each value gives the following: FL 270 = 010° at 5 kt. and —25°C Compare the temperature to standard. Compute the standard temperature by subtracting the lapse rate at FL 270 [(27,000 + 1,000) x 2° = 54°] from sea level standard of 15°C, which is —39°C. The interpolated temperature for FL 270 is approximately 14°C (39 — 25) warmer than standard. Due to variations, temperature is ISA +13°C. (ISA is the International Standard Atmosphere. ISA temperature at sea level is 15°C and decreases at a rate of 2°/1,000 ft. up to 36,000 ft. MSL.) Answer (A) is incorrect. Wind direction is degrees true, not peut! Answer (B) is incorrect. Wind direction is 010° true, not ° true.

questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 149 on page 901.) What approximate wind direction, speed, and temperature (relative to ISA) are expected for a flight over MKC at FL 260?
questions.choice_c: 260° magnetic; 42 knots; ISA +9°C.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: For conditions at FL 260 over MKC in Fig. 149, interpolate between values at FL 240 and FL 300. First decode the two given flight levels: FL 240 =. 260° at. 38kt. and -21°C FL 300 = 260° at 5Okt. and -36°C Difference O° at 12kt. and -15°C Interpolation for each value gives the following: FL 260 = 260° at 42 kt. and —-26°C To compare the temperature to standard, subtract the lapse rate at FL 260 [(26,000 + 1,000) x 2° = 52°] from sea level standard of 15°C to get FL 260 standard of —37°C. The interpolated temperature for FL 260 of about —26°C is 11°C warmer than standard. The best answer is 260° true; 43 kt.; ISA +10. (ISA is the International Standard Atmosphere. ISA temperature at sea level is 15°C and decreases at a rate of 2°/1,000 ft. up to 36,000 ft. MSL.) Answer (B) is incorrect. Forecast temperature is warmer, not colder, than ISA. Answer (C) is incorrect. Wind direction is degrees true, not magnetic.

questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 149 below.) What approximate wind direction, speed, and temperature (relative to ISA) are expected for a flight over OKC at FL 370?
(Refer to Figure 149 on page 903.) What will be the wind and temperature trend for a DEN-ICT-OKC flight at 11,000 feet?
questions.choice_c: Wind shift from calm to a westerly direction.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: To determine the wind and temperature trend, the winds and temperatures aloft must be interpolated for 11,000 ft. The table below shows data for 9,000 and 12,000 ft. and the interpolated data for 11,000 ft. 9,000 12,000 11,000 DEN 9900+09 9900+04 9900+06 ICT 0607+08 9900+04 0602+05 OKC 1106+10 9900+05 1102+07 The best answer is that wind speed increases slightly from light and variable in DEN to 2 kt. at ICT and OKC. Answer (A) is incorrect. The temperature increases at OKC to a temperature higher than DEN. Answer (C) is incorrect. The wind shifts from calm to an easterly, not westerly, direction.

questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 149 on page 903.) What will be the wind and temperature trend for a DSM-LIT-SHV flight at 12,000 feet?
questions.choice_c: Wind direction shift from northwest to southeast.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: In determining the wind and temperature trend, it is easier to view in a table format. Note that, in this question, no interpolation is needed since 12,000 ft. is an altitude at which data are supplied from the Winds and Temperatures Aloft Forecast. Below is a table of the data: DSM LIT SHV 12,000 ft. 3022+00 2808+06 2106+06 During this flight, the wind speed decreases from 22 kt. to 8 kt. to 6 kt. Answer (B) is incorrect. The temperature increases, not decreases, en route. Answer (C) is incorrect. The wind direction shifts from northwest to southwest, not southeast.

questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 149 on page 903.) What will be the wind and temperature trend for an SAT ELP TUS flight at 16,000 feet?
questions.choice_c: Wind direction shift from southwest to east.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: To determine the wind and temperature trend, the winds and temperatures aloft must be interpolated for 16,000 ft. The table below shows data for 12,000 and 18,000 ft. and the interpolated data for 16,000 ft. 12,000 18,000 16,000 SAT 1911+07 2006-07 2008-02 ELP 0615+08 0113-05 0314-01 TUS 0814+10 0810-05 0811+00 On this route, the wind direction shifts from southwest (200°) to east (080°). Answer (A) is incorrect. Temperatures increase, not decrease, slightly. Answer (B) is incorrect. Wind speed increases between SAT and ELP but decreases from ELP to TUS.

questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 149 on page 903.) What will be the wind and temperature trend for an STL-MEM- MSY flight at FL 330?
questions.choice_b: Wind shift from west to north.
questions.choice_c: Temperature increase 5°C.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: To determine the wind and temperature trend, the winds and temperatures aloft must be interpolated for FL 330. The table below shows data for FL 300 and FL 340 and the interpolated data for FL 330. FL 300 FL 340 FL 330 STL 265435 265744 265642 MEM 261934 262144 262142 MSY 990034 990043 990041 During this flight, the wind speed decreases from 56 kt. to light and variable. __ Answer (B) is incorrect. The wind remains westerly in direction and becomes light and variable; it does not shift to the Melee Answer (C) is incorrect. Temperature increases 1°C, not

questions.question_text: What wind direction and speed aloft are forecast by this WINDS AND TEMPERATURE ALOFT FORECAST (FB) report for FL 390 - “731960”?
questions.choice_c: 073° at 196 knots.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: The first two digits are the direction group, and since it is greater than 36, the wind speed must be 100 kt. or greater. Subtract 50 from the direction group to determine wind direction of 230° (73 — 50 = 23). The second two digits are the wind speed; since it was determined that the wind was 100 kt. or greater, add 100 to this group. Thus, the wind speed is 119 kt. (100 + 19 = 119). Answer (B) is incorrect. Coded directions with wind speed over 100 kt. range from 51 through 86. The direction is 230°, not 131°, at 119 kt., not 96 kt. Answer (C) is incorrect. Fifty must be subtracted from the first two digits and 100 added to the second two digits. The last two digits are the temperature.

questions.question_text: A station is forecasting wind and temperature aloft to be 280° at 205 knots; temperature —51°C at FL 390. How would this data be encoded in the FB?
questions.choice_a: 780051 the direction code. Here the direction is 78 for 280°. The last
questions.choice_b: 789951 two digits indicate the temperature, and minus signs are omitted : above 24,000 ft. MSL.
questions.choice_c: 280051 Answer (A) is incorrect. It indicates a wind at 280° at 100 kt. Answer (C) is incorrect. If the wind is 0 kt., the direction and wind group is coded “9900.”
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: At FL 390, a 280° wind at 205 kt. is encoded as 789951. The first two digits are the direction. The second two digits are velocity. When wind speed is forecast at 200 kt. or greater, the wind group is coded as 99, and 50 is added to

questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 145 on page 905.) What type of report is listed for Lubbock (KLBB) at 18182?
questions.choice_a: An aviation selected special weather report.
questions.choice_b: A special report concerning very low station pressure.
questions.choice_c: A special METAR weather observation, concerning significant weather changes.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: At 1818Z (or 1818 UTC), KLBB issued a SPECI report, which is an aviation selected special weather report. The SPECI is a nonroutine aviation weather report taken when any of the SPECI criteria have been observed. Answer (B) is incorrect. Pressure changes do not require a SPECI. Any rapidly rising or falling pressure is reported in the remarks section. Answer (C) is incorrect. A SPECI is an aviation selected special weather report, not a special METAR. Also, a SPECI may be issued for other than weather changes, such as a volcanic eruption or a notification of an aircraft mishap.

questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 145 on page 905.) What was the local Central Standard Time of the Aviation Routine Weather Report at Austin (KAUS)?
questions.explanation: The date/time after Austin (KAUS) on Fig. 145 is 1317532Z. The first two digits are the date and the last four digits are the time, followed by Z to denote coordinated universal time (UTC). Thus, the report was issued at 1753 UTC. To determine Central Standard Time, you must subtract 6 hr. from UTC; thus, the time is 11:53 a.m. (1753 — 6 = 1153) CST. Answer (B) is incorrect. This is UTC adjusted to 24-hr. clock time (1753 = 5:53 p.m.). Answer (C) is incorrect. Central Standard Time is 6 hr. behind, not 5 hr. ahead of, UTC.

questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 145 on page 905.) What weather improvement was reported at Lubbock (KLBB) between 1750 and 1818 UTC?
questions.choice_a: The wind shift and frontal passage at 1812Z.
questions.choice_b: The vertical visibility improved by 2,000 feet.
questions.choice_c: The temperature and dew point spread improved.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: The vertical visibility at KLBB at 1750 UTC was reported to be 1,000 ft. (VV010). At 1818 UTC, the vertical visibility was reported to be 3,000 ft. (VV030), an improvement of 2,000 ft. from the 1750 UTC report. Answer (A) is incorrect. While the wind did shift at 1812Z due to a frontal passage, wind is not a weather element. Answer (C) is incorrect. At 1750 UTC, the temperature was +3°C and the dew point was —01°C (03/M01), or a spread of 4°C. At 1818 UTC, both the temperature and dew point were —1°C (M01/M01), or a spread of 0°C. Thus, the temperature/dew point spread decreased, not increased or improved.

questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 145 on page 905.) What condition is reported at Dallas (KDAL)?
questions.choice_a: The tops of the overcast is 10,000 feet.
questions.choice_c: Altimeter setting is 30.07.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Altimeter settings are reported in a four-digit format in inches of mercury prefixed with an “A” to denote the units of pressure. KDAL altimeter setting is coded as A3007, which means 30.07 inches of mercury. Answer (A) is incorrect. The base, not the top, of the overcast layer is 10,000 ft. Answer (B) is incorrect. The temperature/dew point spread is 8°C, not 8°F.

questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 145 on page 905.) What condition is reported at Childress (KCDS)?
questions.choice_b: Heavy rain showers began 42 minutes after the hour.
questions.choice_c: The ceiling is solid overcast at an estimated 1,800 feet above sea level.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: In the METAR for KCDS is the code —SHRA, which means there are light (-) showers (SH) and the precipitation type is rain (RA). Answer (B) is incorrect. Light, not heavy, rain showers began at 42 min. past the hour. Answer (C) is incorrect. Cloud height is expressed in AGL, not MSL. KCDS reports the bases of an overcast layer at 18,000 ft., not 1,800 ft., and METARs do not state whether the bases are estimated or measured.

questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 145 on page 905.) The peak wind at KAMA was reported to be from 320° true at 39 knots,
questions.choice_c: with .43 of an inch liquid precipitation since the last report.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Whenever the peak wind exceeds 25 Kt., PK WND will be included in the remarks with three digits for direction and two or three digits for speed followed by the time (in hours and minutes or just minutes past the hour) of occurrence. The time of the METAR report for KAMA is 1755Z, and the remark PK WND 32039/43 means the peak wind 320° true at 39 kt. occurred at 43 min. past the hour, or at 1743Z. _ Answer (B) is incorrect. Gusts are not reported in a peak wind remark. Answer (C) is incorrect. The remark means that the peak wind occurred at 43 min. past the hour, not that .43 of an inch of precipitation has accumulated since the last report.

questions.subsection: 30.05 in. of Hg (A3005), and at 1820 UTC the altimeter setting
questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 146 on page 907.) What method was used to obtain the METAR at Tyler (KTYR) at AMOSZ?
questions.choice_a: Automated Surface Observing System (ASOS), having a precipitation discriminator.
questions.choice_b: Automatic Meteorological Observing Station (AMOS), with a precipitation discriminator.
questions.choice_c: Automated Weather Observing System (AWOS), without a precipitation discriminator.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: After the date/time group on the METAR for KTYR is the contraction AUTO, which means that the METAR is a fully automated report. Currently, an ASOS is the only automated station that performs the basic observing functions necessary to generate a METAR report. Additionally, the remark A02 means the automated station has a precipitation discriminator. Answer (B) is incorrect. An AMOS is used at some sites, but it is not able to generate a METAR report. Currently, ASOS stations generate METAR reports. Answer (C) is incorrect. Currently, only the ASOS, not the AWOS, can perform the functions necessary to generate a METAR report. In the future, selected AWOS sites may be incorporated into the nationwide data collection network, and these stations will generate METAR reports. Additionally, a remark of A01, not A02, means that the automated station is without a precipitation discriminator.

questions.subsection: 30.05 in. of Hg (A3005), and at 1820 UTC the altimeter setting
questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 146 on page 907.) What was the ceiling at Walnut Ridge (KARG)?
questions.choice_c: 1,000 feet MSL.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: The ceiling is the lowest broken or overcast layer (measured in hundreds of feet above ground level) or vertical visibility (measured in hundreds of feet) into an obscuration. The ceiling at KARG is reported to be overcast clouds at 1,000 ft. AGL (OVC010). Answer (B) is incorrect. The runway visual range for RWY 28 at KARG is 2,400 ft. (R28/2400 FT), not the ceiling. Answer (C) is incorrect. The ceiling is measured in hundreds of feet above ground level (AGL), not above mean sea level (MSL).

questions.subsection: 30.05 in. of Hg (A3005), and at 1820 UTC the altimeter setting
questions.question_text: SPECI KGLS 131802Z 10012G21KT 060V140 2SM +SHRA SCT005 BKNO35 OVCO50CB 24/23 A2980 RMK RAB57 WS TKO RWO9L WSHFT 58 FROPA. This SPECI report at Galveston (KGLS) indicates which condition?
questions.choice_a: Wind steady at 100° magnetic at 12 knots, gusts to 21.
questions.choice_b: Precipitation started at 57 after the hour.
questions.choice_c: 5,000 feet overcast with towering cumulus.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: The remarks (RMk) section of the SPECI report at KGLS indicates rain began at 57 min. past the hour (RAB57); wind shear occurred during takeoff on RWY O9L (WS TKO RWOS9L); and the wind shifted in direction at 58 min. past the hour due to a frontal passage (WSHFT 58 FROPA). Answer (A) is incorrect. The wind is variable, not steady, from 060° to 140° with the prevailing wind at 100°. Wind direction is referenced to true, not magnetic, north. Answer (C) is incorrect. The 5,000 ft. overcast layer is the base of cumulonimbus clouds (CB), not tower cumulus clouds (TCU).

questions.subsection: 30.05 in. of Hg (A3005), and at 1820 UTC the altimeter setting
questions.question_text: METAR KMAF 131756Z 02020KT 12SM BKNO25 OVC250 27/18 A3009 RMK RAE44. Which weather condition is indicated by this METAR report at Midland (KMAF)?
questions.choice_a: Rain of unknown intensity ended 16 minutes before the hour.
questions.choice_b: The ceiling was at 25,000 feet MSL.
questions.choice_c: Wind was 020° magnetic at 20 knots.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: The report at KMAF indicates RMK RAE44, which means remarks follow: rain ended 44 min. past the hour (or 16 min. before the hour). Answer (B) is incorrect. The ceiling is a broken layer at 2,500 ft. AGL (BKNO25), not 25,000 ft. MSL. Answer (C) is Weel r Wind direction is referenced to true, not magnetic, north.

questions.subsection: 30.05 in. of Hg (A3005), and at 1820 UTC the altimeter setting
questions.question_text: METAR KSPS 131757Z 09014KT 6SM -RA SCT025 OVC090 24/22 A3005. SPECI KSPS 131820Z 01025KT 3SM +RA FC OVC015 22/21 A3000. What change took place at Wichita Falls (KSPS) between 1757 and 1820 UTC?
questions.choice_c: A funnel cloud was observed.
questions.explanation: The special report (SPECI) for KSPS at 1820 UTC reports that a funnel cloud (FC) was observed. Answer (A) is incorrect. The rain intensity increased, not decreased, between 1757 and 1820 UTC. At 1757 UTC the rain was light (-RA), and at 1820 UTC the rain was heavy (+RA). Answer (B) is incorrect. The atmospheric pressure decreased, not increased. At 1757 UTC the altimeter setting was 30.05 in. of Hg (A3005), and at 1820 UTC the altimeter setting was 30.00 in. of Hg (A3000).

questions.subsection: 30.05 in. of Hg (A3005), and at 1820 UTC the altimeter setting
questions.question_text: METAR KHRO 131753Z 09007KT 7SM FEW020 BKNO40CB 30/27 A3001. SPECI KHRO 131815Z 13017G26KT 3SM +TSRA SCT020 BKNO45TCU 29/24 A2983 RMK RAB12 WS TKO LDG RW14R FRQ LTGICCG VC. What change has taken place between 1753 and 1815 UTC at Harrison (KHRO)?
questions.choice_a: The ceiling lowered and cumulonimbus clouds developed.
questions.choice_b: Thundershowers began at 12 minutes past the hour.
questions.choice_c: Visibility reduced to IFR conditions.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: The special report (SPECI) taken at 1815 UTC at KHRO reports thunderstorm with heavy rain _ (+TSRA), and the remarks state that the rain began at 12 min. past the hour (RAB12). Answer (A) is incorrect. The ceiling (BKN) increased, not decreased, from 4,000 ft. to 4,500 ft., and towering cumulus clouds (TCU), not cumulonimbus clouds (CB), were reported at 1815 UTC. Answer (C) is incorrect. Visibility reduced to 3 SM, which is VFR, not IFR.

questions.subsection: 30.05 in. of Hg (A3005), and at 1820 UTC the altimeter setting
questions.question_text: The prevailing visibility in the following METAR is METAR KFSM 131756Z AUTO O0000KT M1/4SM R25/0600V1000FT -RA FG VV004 06/05 A2989 RMK A02 $.
questions.choice_c: amean (average) of 1/4 statute mile.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Prevailing visibility is reported in statute miles with a space and then fractions of statute miles, as needed, with SM appended to it. This METAR was produced at an automated station, as indicated by the modifier AUTO. At an automated station, visibility of less than 1/4 SM is reported as M1/4SM. Answer (B) is incorrect. The letter M means that the visibility is less than 1/4 SM, not measured 1/4 SM. Answer (C) is incorrect. The letter M means that the visibility is less than 1/4 SM, not that the visibility is a mean (average) of 1/4 SM.

questions.subsection: 30.05 in. of Hg (A3005), and at 1820 UTC the altimeter setting
questions.question_text: The VV001 in the following METAR indicates METAR KFSM 131756Z AUTO O0000KT M1/4SM R25/0600V1000FT -RA FG VV001 A2989 RMK AO2 VIS 3/4 RWY19 CHINO RWY19 $.
questions.choice_a: an observer reported the vertical visibility as 100 feet.
questions.choice_c: the variability value is 100 feet.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: The sky condition in the METAR is shown as VV001, which means a vertical visibility (spoken as an indefinite ceiling) of 100 ft. Answer (A) is incorrect. The METAR was produced by a fully automated station without any human intervention, as indicated by the AUTO modifier. Thus, there is no observer. Answer (C) is incorrect. The vertical visibility, not variability value, is 100 ft.

questions.subsection: 30.05 in. of Hg (A3005), and at 1820 UTC the altimeter setting
questions.question_text: The symbol ($) at the end of the following METAR indicates that METAR KFSM 131756Z AUTO OOO00KT M1/4SM R25/0600V1000FT -RA FG VV004 06/05 A2989 RMK A02 §.
questions.choice_a: the latest information is transmitted over a discrete VHF frequency at KFSM.
questions.choice_b: the latest information is broadcast on the voice portion of a local NAVAID at KFSM.
questions.choice_c: maintenance is needed on the system.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: A maintenance indicator (dollar) sign, $, is included when an automated weather reporting station detects that maintenance is needed on the system. Answer (A) is incorrect. The $ symbol indicates that maintenance is needed on the system, not that the latest information is transmitted over a discrete VHF frequency at KFSM. Answer (B) is incorrect. The $ symbol indicates that maintenance is needed on the system, not that the latest a is broadcast on the voice portion of a local NAVAID at 2

questions.subsection: 30.05 in. of Hg (A3005), and at 1820 UTC the altimeter setting
questions.question_text: A squall is a sudden increase of at least 16 knots in average wind speed to a sustained speed of
questions.choice_a: 26 knots or more for at least 1 minute.
questions.choice_b: 20 knots or more for at least 1 minute.
questions.choice_c: 22 knots or more for at least 1 minute.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: A squall is a sudden increase of at least 16 kt. in average wind speed to a sustained speed of 22 kt. or more for at least 1 minute. Squalls are reported in the METAR as weather phenomena by the code SQ. Answer (A) is incorrect. A squall is a sudden increase of at least 16 kt. in average wind speed to a sustained speed of 22 kt., not 26 kt., or more for at least 1 minute. Answer (B) is incorrect. A squall is a sudden increase of at least 16 kt. in average wind speed to a sustained speed of 22 kt., not 20 kt., or more for at least 1 minute.

questions.subsection: 30.05 in. of Hg (A3005), and at 1820 UTC the altimeter setting
questions.question_text: If squalls are reported at the destination airport, what wind conditions exist?
questions.choice_a: Sudden increases in wind speed of at least 15 knots to a sustained wind speed of 20 knots, lasting for at least 1 minute.
questions.choice_b: Rapid variation in wind direction of at least 20° and changes in speed of at least 10 knots between peaks and lulls.
questions.choice_c: A sudden increase in wind speed of at least 16 knots, the speed rising to 22 knots or more for 1 minute or longer.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: A squall is a sudden increase in wind speed of at least 15 kt., the speed rising to 22 kt. or more, and lasting for at least 1 min. Answer (A) is incorrect. A squall is a sudden increase in wind speed of at least 15 kt., the speed rising to 20 kt. or more, and lasting for at least 1 min. Answer (B) is incorrect. A gust, not a squall, has a variation in wind speed of at least 10 kt. between peaks and lulls.

questions.subsection: 30.05 in. of Hg (A3005), and at 1820 UTC the altimeter setting
questions.question_text: METAR KFSO 031053Z VRBO2KT 7SM MIFG SKC 15/14 A3012 RMK SLP993 6//// T01500139 56012 In the above METAR, the SLP993 6//// indicates
questions.choice_a: sea-level pressure 999.3 hectopascals which in the last 6 hours has dropped .4 hectopascals.
questions.choice_b: sea-level pressure 999.3 hectopascals and an indeterminable amount of precipitation has occurred over the last 3 hours.
questions.choice_c: sea-level pressure 999.3 hectopascals and in the last 6 hours that four-tenths of an inch of precipitation has fallen.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: In the remarks (RMK) section of the METAR, the coded group SLP993 means the sea-level pressure (SLP) is 999.3 hectopascals. A coded element beginning with the number 6 indicates the 3- and 6-hr. precipitation amount. The “6” is followed by four digits indicating precipitation about using tens, units, tenths, and hundredths of an inch. When an indeter- minable amount of precipitation has occurred, four forward slashes will follow the number 6 (6////). Answer (A) is incorrect. The coded group 6//// indicates an indeterminable amount of precipitation has occurred over the last 3 hr., not that the pressure has dropped 0.4 hectopascals over the last 6 hr. Answer (C) is incorrect. A coded group for four-tenths of an inch of precipitation over the last 6 hr. would be coded as 60040, not 6////. The four slashes indicate an indeterminable amount of precipitation has occurred.

questions.subsection: 30.05 in. of Hg (A3005), and at 1820 UTC the altimeter setting
questions.question_text: Data that may be added (manual weather augmentation) to the Automated Weather Observing System (AWOS) report is limited to
questions.choice_a: the precipitation accumulation report, an automated variable visibility, and wind direction remark.
questions.choice_b: thunderstorms (intensity and direction), precipitation (type and intensity), and obstructions to visibility (dependent on the visibility being 3 miles or less).
questions.choice_c: density altitude, NOTAMs, and reported slant range visibility.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Manual weather augmentation remarks added to an AWOS-generated METAR are located in the remarks section. As a general rule, the manual remarks are limited to thunderstorms (intensity and direction), precipitation (type and intensity), and obstructions to vision when the visibility is 3 SM or less. Answer (A) is incorrect. Wind direction is an automated feature, not manually added data, of the AWOS. Answer (C) is incorrect. Density altitude is an automated, not a manually inputted, remark.

questions.subsection: 30.05 in. of Hg (A3005), and at 1820 UTC the altimeter setting
questions.question_text: METAR KFSO 031053Z VRBO2KT 7SM MIFG SKC 15/14 A3012 RMK SLP993 SNINCR 1/10 In the above METAR, the SNINCR 1/10 indicates
questions.choice_a: sea-level pressure 999.3 hectopascals and a snow depth of 10" and a 1" increase in the last hour.
questions.choice_b: sea-level pressure 999.3 hectopascals and 0.10" of snow on the runway landing zone during the last hour.
questions.choice_c: sea-level pressure 999.3 hectopascals and 1" of snow in the last 10 hours.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: SNINCR (Snow Increasing Rapidly) indicates the snow depth increase is 1 inch in the past hour with a total depth on the ground of 10 inches. Answer (B) is incorrect. SNINCR has to do with snow depth on the ground, not necessarily on the runway landing zone. Answer (C) is incorrect. SNINCR does not indicate the last 10 hours. It indicates the snow depth increase in the past hour and the total depth on the ground at the time of the METAR.

questions.question_text: Which primary source contains information regarding the expected weather at the destination airport at the ETA?
questions.choice_c: Terminal Aerodrome Forecast.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: A Terminal Aerodrome Forecast (TAF) is | a concise statement of the expected meteorological conditions at an airport during a specified period (usually 24 hr.). Thus, a TAF contains information regarding the expected weather at the destination airport at the ETA. Answer (A) is incorrect. A low-level prog chart is a forecast of significant weather for the United States. It is not a forecast for a specific destination. Answer (B) is incorrect. A weather depiction chart is a national weather map of observed, not forecast, weather at a specific time. It is useful for flight planning but does not provide specific information about a particular destination.

questions.question_text: Weather conditions expected to occur in the vicinity of the airport, but not at the airport, are denoted by the letters “VC.” When VC appears in a Terminal Aerodrome Forecast, it covers a geographical area of
questions.choice_a: a5-to-10 statute mile radius from the airport.
questions.choice_b: a5-mile radius of the center of a runway complex.
questions.choice_c: 10 miles of the station originating the forecast.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: When VC appears in a TAF, it applies to weather conditions expected to occur in an area within a 5- to 10- SM radius of the airport, but not at the airport itself. Answer (B) is incorrect. VC applies to weather conditions expected to occur in an area within a 5- to 10-SM radius from the airport, not within a 5-mile radius of the runway complex. Answer (C) is incorrect. VC applies to weather conditions expected to occur in the vicinity of the airport (within a 5- to 10-SM radius of the airport), not within 10 miles of the station originating the forecast.

questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 147 on page 911.) What type conditions can be expected for a flight scheduled to land at San Angelo (KSJT) at 15002?
questions.choice_a: Chance of 1 statute mile visibility and cumulonimbus clouds.
questions.choice_b: IFR conditions due to low visibility, rain, and mist.
questions.choice_c: IFR conditions due to low ceilings, rain, and fog.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: At KSJT from 0900 to 1800Z (UTC), the forecast is wind 170° at 20 kt. with gusts to 34 kt., visibility 2 SM, overcast cumulonimbus clouds at 1,000 ft. with rain and mist. Thus, the expected conditions are IFR conditions due to low visibility, rain, and mist. Answer (A) is incorrect. Visibility is forecast to be 2 SM, not 1 SM. Answer (C) is incorrect. IFR conditions are due to low visibility in rain and mist, not low ceilings and fog.

questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 147 on page 911.) At which time is IFR weather first predicted at Lubbock (KLBB)?
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: The forecast for KLBB between the hours of 0100Z to 0300Z is a 40% probability of visibility 1/8 SM (IFR) in thunderstorms and heavy rain showers, with snow and ice pellets, and an overcast layer at 300 ft. Thus, the first IFR weather predicted at KLBB is at 0100Z. Answer (A) is incorrect. At 2100Z, the forecast is wind from 040° at 15 kt., 5 SM visibility, broken clouds at 2,000 ft., and an overcast layer at 6,000 ft., which is VFR weather, not IFR weather. Answer (C) is incorrect. At 0400Z, the forecast is 3 SM pepty and an overcast layer at 1,000 ft., which is MVFR, not

questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 147 on page 911.) At what time is fog predicted to decrease the ceiling to 100 feet at KLBB?
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: The TAF entry for 1600Z predicts visibility of 1/8 mile and fog that constitutes a total obscuration with vertical limits to visibility of 100 feet. Answer (A) is incorrect. The 2100Z forecast makes no mention of either fog or a ceiling of 100 feet. The final entry on the line suggest an overcast layer at 300 feet. Answer (B) is incorrect. The 0900Z forecast is for fog and a ceiling of 200 feet, not 100 feet as the question posés.

questions.question_text: What weather is predicted by the term VCTS ina Terminal Aerodrome Forecast?
questions.choice_a: Thunderstorms are expected in the vicinity.
questions.choice_b: Thunderstorms may occur over the station and within 50 miles of the station.
questions.choice_c: Thunderstorms are expected between 5 and 25 miles of the runway complex.
questions.explanation: i DISCUSSION: The term VC (vicinity) applies to weather conditions expected within a 5- to 10-SM radius of the airport, but not at the airport itself. Thus, VCTS in a TAF means | thunderstorms are expected in the vicinity of the airport. j Answer (B) is incorrect. VC (vicinity) means within 5- to 10- SM from the airport, not 50 miles. Answer (C) is incorrect. A geographic area between 5 and 25 miles of the runway complex was the definition of the term vicinity in the old U.S. terminal forecast (FT), not the TAF.

questions.question_text: Which are the only cloud types forecast in the Terminal Aerodrome Forecast?
questions.choice_c: Stratocumulus.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: If cumulonimbus clouds are expected at the ; airport, the contraction CB is appended to the cloud layer that | represents the base of the cumulonimbus cloud(s). Cumulo- nimbus clouds are the only cloud type forecast in the TAF. Answer (A) is incorrect. The only cloud type forecast in the TAF is cumulonimbus, not altocumulus. Answer (C) is incorrect. The only cloud type forecast in the TAF is cumulonimbus, not stratocumulus.

questions.question_text: A calm wind that is forecast, in the International Terminal Aerodrome Forecast (TAF), is encoded as
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: In the International Terminal Aerodrome Forecast (TAF), a calm wind that is forecast is encoded as OOOOOKT. Answer (A) is incorrect. A variable wind is encoded as VRB when a forecast is for low wind speeds (3 kt. or less) followed by a wind speed, not two zeros. A calm wind will be encoded as OOOOOKT. Answer (C) is incorrect. A calm wind is encoded as OOOOOKT, not O0003KT.

questions.question_text: In the International Terminal Aerodrome Forecast (TAF), a variable wind direction is noted by “VRB” where the three digit direction usually appears. A calm wind appears in the TAF as
questions.choice_c: QOOOOKT.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: In the International Terminal Aerodrome Forecast (TAF), a calm wind appears in the TAF as OOOOOKT. Answer (A) is incorrect. In the TAF, a calm wind appears in the TAF as OOOOOKT, not OOO03KT. Answer (B) is incorrect. In the TAF, a calm wind appears in the TAF as OOOOOKT, not VRBOOKT. For low wind speeds (3 kt. or less), VRB is used for wind direction.

questions.question_text: A PROB40 (PROBability) HHhh group in an International Terminal Aerodrome Forecast (TAF) indicates the probability of
questions.choice_c: thunderstorms or high wind.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: A PROB40 (PROBability) HHhh group in a TAF indicates the probability of thunderstorms or other precipitation events. The PROB group is used when the occurrence of thunderstorms or precipitation is in the 30% to less than 50% range; thus, the probability value of 40 is appended to the PROB contraction. PROB40 is followed by a four-digit group giving the beginning time (HH) and ending time (hh) of the period during which thunderstorms or precipitation is expected. Answer (B) is incorrect. A PROB40 group in a TAF indicates the probability of thunderstorms or other precipitation events, not low visibility. Answer (C) is incorrect. A PROB40 group in a TAF indicates the probability of thunderstorms or other precipitation events, not high winds.

questions.question_text: What is the single source reference that contains information regarding volcanic eruption, turbulence, and icing conditions for a specific region?
questions.choice_b: In-Flight Weather Advisories. reduced to sea level.
questions.choice_c: Area Forecast. _ 63. Isobars on a surface weather chart represent lines of equal pressure ata given atmospheric pressure altitude.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: In-flight aviation weather advisories provide information on volcanic eruption, turbulence, and icing conditions for a specific region. Answer (A) is incorrect. A weather depiction chart is prepared from METAR reports and gives only a broad overview of the observed condition at the valid time of the chart. It does not provide any information on volcanic eruption, turbulence, and icing conditions for a specific region. Answer (C) is incorrect. In-flight weather advisories, not an area forecast, provides information on volcanic eruption, turbulence, and icing conditions for a specific region.

questions.question_text: A prognostic chart depicts the conditions
questions.choice_a: existing at the surface during the past 6 hours.
questions.choice_b: which presently exist from the 1,000-millibar through the 700-millibar level.
questions.choice_c: forecast to exist at a specific time in the future.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Prognostic charts show conditions as they are forecast to be at the valid time (UTC or Zulu) for the chart. The charts are issued four times daily. Answer (A) is incorrect. Prognostic charts relate to the future, not the past. Answer (B) is incorrect. Low-level prognostic charts are issued for the surface and 24,000 ft., which is 400 mb. One thousand mb is very close to the surface; i.e., sea level is 1,013 mb. Seven hundred mb is approximately 10,000 ft. MSL.

questions.question_text: For international flights, a U.S. High-Level Significant Weather Prognostic Chart is prepared for use
questions.choice_b: between 25,000 feet and 60,000 feet pressure altitude.
questions.choice_c: between FL 180 and FL 600.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Numerous High-Level Significant Weather Prognostic Charts are produced by the Aviation Weather Center (AWC) for international flights. They are prepared for flights between 25,000 ft. and 60,000 ft. pressure altitude (or FL 250 to FL 600). Answer (A) is incorrect. High-level prognostics for international flight are prepared for FL 250 to FL 600, not any altitude above 29,000. Answer (C) is incorrect. The base of the International High-Level Prognostic is FL 250, not FL 180.

questions.question_text: Forecast winds and temperatures aloft for an international flight may be obtained by consulting
questions.choice_a: Area Forecasts published by the departure location host country.
questions.choice_b: the current International Weather Depiction Chart appropriate to the route.
questions.choice_c: Wind and Temperature Aloft Charts prepared by the U.S. National Environmental Prediction (NCEP).
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Forecast winds and temperatures aloft for an international flight may be obtained by consulting Winds and Temperatures Aloft Charts prepared by the U.S. National Centers for Environmental Prediction (NCEP). Answer (A) is incorrect. Area forecasts are forecasts of general weather conditions over an area of several states and do not contain forecasts of the winds and temperatures aloft. Answer (B) is incorrect. The International Weather Depiction Chart indicates current weather and does not forecast winds and temperatures aloft.

questions.question_text: How will an area of thunderstorm activity, that | may grow to severe intensity, be indicated on the Severe Weather Outlook Chart?
questions.choice_c: SVRwithin any area.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: On a Severe Weather Outlook Chart, an area will be labeled APCHG to indicate probable general thunderstorm activity that may approach severe intensity. Approaching means winds greater than or equal to 35 kt., but less than 50 kt., and/or hail greater than or equal to 1/2 in. in diameter, but less than 3/4 in. (Surface conditions). Answer (A) is incorrect. A cross-hatched area identifies a tornado watch area. Answer (C) is incorrect. The term SVR is not used on the Severe Weather Outlook Chart.

questions.question_text: What information is provided by a Convective Outlook (AC)?
questions.choice_a: It describes areas of probable severe icing and severe or extreme turbulence during the next 24 hours.
questions.choice_b: It provides prospects of both general and severe thunderstorm activity during the following 24 hours.
questions.choice_c: It indicates areas of probable convective turbulence and the extent of instability in the upper atmosphere (above 500 mb).
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: The convective outlook describes the _ prospects for general thunderstorm activity during the following 24 hr. Areas with a high, moderate, or slight risk of severe thunderstorms are included, as well as areas where thunder- storms may approach severe limits. che‘ Answer (A) is incorrect. Severe icing and severe or extreme turbulence are the subjects of SIGMETs. Answer (C) is incorrect. This information describes a 500-mb Constant Pressure Analysis Chart.

questions.question_text: What sources reflect the most accurate information on current and forecast icing conditions?
questions.choice_a: Low-Level Sig Weather Prog Chart, RADAT’s, and the Area Forecast.
questions.choice_b: PIREP’s, Area Forecast, and the Freezing Level Chart.
questions.choice_c: PIREP’s, AIRMET Zulu’s, and SIGMET’s.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Pilot reports (PIREPs) can reflect the most current icing conditions for a specific area, including type and intensity. AIRMET Zulu is for icing and freezing levels for a specified time and normally an outlook. A SIGMET is issued for areas of severe icing. Answer (A) is incorrect. Low-level sig weather prog charts do not forecast icing conditions but do forecast freezing levels, and an area forecast does not provide any information on icing conditions. Answer (B) is incorrect. A freezing level chart cannot be used to determine current or forecast icing conditions, and an area forecast does not provide any information on icing conditions.

questions.question_text: A public severe thunderstorm watch implies
questions.choice_a: 58 mph winds or greater and/or surface hail of 1 inch or more in diameter.
questions.choice_b: 45 mph winds or greater and/or surface hail of 1 inch or more in diameter.
questions.choice_c: 50 knots or greater and/or surface hail of 1/2 inch or greater.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: The National Weather Service may issue a public severe thunderstorm watch when conditions are favorable to winds of 58 mph (50 kt.) or greater and/or surface hail of 1 inch or more in diameter. Answer (B) is incorrect. The threshold for winds that would trigger a severe thunderstorm watch is 58 mph, not 45 mph. Answer (C) is incorrect. Both the wind and hail values for a severe thunderstorm watch exceed those provided by this answer option. The correct values are 58 mph or greater and a 1-inch diameter or larger.

questions.question_text: Maximum turbulence potential charts (GTG-2) are issued
questions.choice_c: every morning at 0400. 16.10 Constant Pressure Analysis Charts
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Maximum turbulence potential charts (GTG-2) graphics are computer generated, four-dimensional forecasts of information related to the likelihood of encountering Clear Air Turbulence (CAT) associated with upper-level fronts and jet streams. This forecast is updated every hour and includes 06, 09, and 12-hour forecasts, which are updated every 3 hours, starting at OOZ. Answer (B) is incorrect. The maximum turbulence potential charts are updated hourly, not two times a day. Answer (C) is incorrect. This chart may come out every morning at 0400 hr., but it is updated hourly.

questions.question_text: Constant Pressure Analysis Charts contain contours, isotherms, and some contain isotachs. The contours depict
questions.choice_a: ridges, lows, troughs, and highs aloft.
questions.choice_b: highs, lows, troughs, and ridges on the surface.
questions.choice_c: highs, lows, troughs, and ridges corrected to MSL.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Constant pressure analysis charts contain contours and isotherms, and some contain isotachs. Contours are lines of equal height and depict highs, lows, troughs, and ridges aloft in the same manner as isobars on the surface chart. Answer (B) is incorrect. Contours depict highs, lows, troughs, and ridges aloft, not on the surface. Answer (C) is incorrect. Contour heights are expressed as pressure altitudes in a standard atmosphere, not corrected to MSL.

questions.question_text: On the constant pressure analysis chart, satellite and aircraft observations are used in the analysis, over areas of sparse data. A satellite observation is plotted using
questions.choice_a: a station circle at the cloud top location.
questions.choice_b: a square at the cloud top location.
questions.choice_c: astar at the cloud top location.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: On the constant pressure analysis chart, satellite and aircraft observations are used in analysis over areas of sparse data. A satellite observation is plotted using a star at the cloud top location, with the cloud height, wind estimate, and time of report. Answer (A) is incorrect. A station circle represents the location of a reporting station, not a satellite observation. Answer (B) is incorrect. A square is used to indicate an aircraft observation, not a satellite observation.

questions.question_text: On the constant pressure analysis chart, aircraft and satellite observations are used in the analysis, over areas of sparse data. An aircraft observation is plotted using
questions.choice_a: a Station circle at the aircraft location.
questions.choice_c: astar at the aircraft location.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: On the constant pressure analysis chart, aircraft and satellite observations are used in analysis over areas of sparse data. An aircraft observation is plotted using a square at the aircraft location, with the flight level of aircraft, temperature, and wind data. Answer (A) is incorrect. A station circle represents the location of a reporting station, not an aircraft location. Answer (C) is incorrect. A star represents a satellite observation, not an aircraft observation.

questions.question_text: Vertical wind shear can be determined by comparing winds on vertically adjacent constant pressure charts. The vertical wind shear that is critical for probability of turbulence is
questions.choice_c: greater than 8 knots per 1,000 feet.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: The vertical wind shear critical for probable turbulence is 6 kt. or more per 1,000 ft. Answer (A) is incorrect. The vertical wind shear critical for probable turbulence is 6 kt. or more per 1,000 ft., not 4 kt. or more per 1,000 ft. Answer (C) is incorrect. The vertical wind shear critical for probable turbulence is 6 kt. or more per 1,000 ft., not greater than 8 kt. per 1,000 ft.

questions.question_text: The horizontal wind shear, critical for turbulence (moderate or greater) per 150 miles is
questions.choice_c: nota factor, only vertical shear is a factor.
questions.explanation: The horizontal wind shear critical for turbu- lence (moderate or greater) is greater than 18 kt. per 150 NM. Answer (A) is incorrect. The horizontal wind shear critical for turbulence is greater than 18 kt., not 18 kt. or less. Answer (C) is incorrect. Both horizontal and vertical wind shears are critical for turbulence.

questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 153 on page 917.) What type ‘weather system is approaching the California Coast from the west?
questions.choice_c: Cold front.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: A three-dimensional picture can be developed by following the change in height of the height contours. These are the solid lines on Fig. 153, and the height is indicated in the black box. The contour that is approaching the California coast is lower than the contours over the southwestern and northwestern U.S. Thus, a low-pressure area must be approaching. Answer (B) is incorrect. The height contours would increase, not decrease, for a high. Answer (C) is incorrect. Pressure systems, not fronts, are depicted on a constant pressure chart.

questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 154 on page 918.) What is the height of the 300-millibar level at the low pressure center in Canada?
questions.choice_c: 11,850 meters MSL.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: On Fig. 154, the low-pressure center in Canada is marked by the bold “L” in the upper center of the chart. The height of the low-pressure center is indicated by the three bold numbers under the “L,” which are 912. For a 300-mb level, add a zero to the end of the number. Heights are expressed in meters MSL. Thus, 912 indicates 9,120 meters MSL. Answer (B) is incorrect. The height of the 300-mb level of the low-pressure center is 9,120 meters MSL, not 18,000 meters MSL. Answer (C) is incorrect. This is the answer you would get from a 200-mb, not 300-mb, pressure chart. The “185” below the “L’ indicates the height of the 200-mb low-pressure center to be 18,500 meters MSL.

questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 153 on page 917, Figure 154 on page 918, and Figure 155 on page 919.) What type weather is inferred by the almost vertical extent of the LOW in Canada?
questions.choice_a: A rapid-moving system with little chance of developing cloudiness, precipitation, and adverse flying conditions.
questions.choice_b: A slow-moving storm which may cause extensive and persistent cloudiness, precipitation, and generally adverse flying weather.
questions.choice_c: Arapid-moving storm, leaning to west with altitude, which encourages line squalls ahead of the system with a potential of severe weather.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: By comparing the 500-, 300-, and 200-mb pressure chart, it is determined that the low pressure is tilting little with height. The upper winds are not blowing across but are almost encircling the low. Because of these conditions, the storm moves very slowly and usually causes extensive and persistent cloudiness, precipitation, and generally adverse flying weather. Answer (A) is incorrect. The wind aloft is encircling the low, which means it is a slow-, not rapid-, moving system. Answer (C) is incorrect. The low is leaning very little, and the winds are encircling the low, which means it is a slow-, not rapid-, moving system.

questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 153 on page 917, Figure 154 on page 918, and Figure 155 on page 919.) Interpret the path of the jetstream.
questions.choice_a: Southern California, Nevada, Utah, Nebraska/ Kansas, and then southeastward.
questions.choice_b: Oregon, Idaho, Wyoming, Nebraska, lowa, and across the Great Lakes.
questions.choice_c: The Alaska area, across Canada to Montana, South Dakota, then across the Great Lakes area.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: To interpret the path of the jet stream, compare the isotachs on the 300- and 200-mb charts. The jet stream has winds of 50 kt. or greater. These greater wind speeds are found in a band from the Alaska area across Canada to Montana and South Dakota, then across the Great Lakes region. Answer (A) is incorrect. The isotachs in the Southern California area are between 30 and 50 kt. Answer (B) is incorrect. Oregon has isotachs between 10 and 30 kt.

questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 153 on page 917, Figure 154 on page 918, and Figure 155 on page 919.) What is the approximate temperature for a flight from southern California to central Kansas at FL 350?
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: You will need to use the 300-mb (FL 300) chart and the 200-mb (FL 390) chart. You will then need to interpolate for FL 350. The temperature is located in the upper left corner of the station on a constant pressure chart. On the 300-mb chart, the station in Southern California is reporting a temperature of -—30°C, and along the line to central Kansas, the temperature at the stations remains at -30°C. On the 200-mb chart, the Southern California station reports a temperature of —53°C, and the station in central Kansas reports a temperature of -47°C. The average is —50°C. | Interpolate for FL 350 for an approximate temperature of —41°C. Answer (A) is incorrect. This is the approximate temperature at FL 230, not FL 350, from Southern California to central Kansas. Answer (B) is incorrect. This is the approximate temperature at FL 340, not FL 350.

questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 153 on page 917, Figure 154 on page 918, and Figure 155 on page 919.) Determine the approximate wind direction and velocity at FL 240 over the station in central Oklahoma.
questions.choice_c: 330° at 13 knots.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: To determine the approximate wind direction, you must interpolate between values for FL 180 (500-mb chart) and FL 300 (300-mb chart). The wind direction and speed are determined by the flag at the reporting station. First decode the two given pressure levels: FL 180 (500 mb) 280) cee Okt FL 300 (300 mb) 360=="~at 10 kt. Difference 280% <at Okt. Interpolation for each value gives the following: FL 240 = 320° at 10 kt. Answer (A) is incorrect. This is the wind direction and speed at the 500-mb pressure level (FL 180). Answer (C) is incorrect. This is the interpolation between the 200- and 300-mb charts, not the 300- and 500-mb charts.

questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 153 on page 917, Figure 154 on page 918, and Figure 155 on page 919.) What is the relative moisture content of the air mass approaching the California coast?
questions.choice_c: Very wet with high potential for clouds and precipitation. 16.11 AIRMETs and SIGMETs
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Determine the moisture content by comparing the temperature/dew point spread of the stations along the California coast at the 200-, 300-, and 500-mb pressure levels. The spread, if given, is located at the lower left of the station symbol. Both the 500- and 200-mb charts indicate relatively wide spread dry conditions. The 300-mb chart shows that moisture is high in northern California. Overall, the system is relatively dry. Answer (B) is incorrect. There is very little moisture, as depicted by the wide temperature/dew point spread. Answer (C) is incorrect. The air mass is dry, not wet (note wide temperature/ dew point spreads).

questions.question_text: If a SIGMET alert is announced, how can information contained in the SIGMET be obtained?
questions.choice_a: ATC will announce the hazard and advise when information will be provided in the FSS broadcast.
questions.choice_b: By contacting a weather watch station.
questions.choice_c: By contacting the nearest FSS.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Pilots, upon hearing the alert notice, if they have not received the advisory or are in doubt, should contact the nearest FSS and ascertain whether the advisory is pertinent to their flights. Answer (A) is incorrect. ATC does not advise when to listen to an FSS broadcast; rather, they tell you to contact FSS. Answer (B) is incorrect. The pilot-may monitor an FSS broadcast or contact any FSS. A weather watch station is a nonsense term.

questions.question_text: Which type weather conditions are covered in the
questions.choice_b: Cumulonimbus clouds with tops above the Answer (B) is incorrect. Cumulonimbus clouds with tops tropopause and thunderstorms with 1/2-inch above the tropopause is not a weather condition covered in a hail or funnel clouds. convective SIGMET. Answer (C) is incorrect. Thunderstorms thunderstorms, and thunderstorms with 3/4-inch hail or tornadoes. i : must be at least VIP level 4, not level 2.
questions.choice_c: Any thunderstorm with a severity level of VIP 2
questions.explanation: Convective SIGMET? DISCUSSION: Convective SIGMETs are issued for (1) severe thunderstorm due to surface winds greater than 50 kt., hail at the surface equal to or greater than 3/4 in. in diameter, or tornadoes; (2) embedded thunderstorms; (3) line of thunderstorms; or (4) thunderstorms greater than or equal to VIP level 4 affecting 40% or more of an area at least 3,000 square mi.

questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 148 below.) Which system in
questions.choice_a: The storms in Texas and Oklahoma. do not have such extensive development (tops 30,000 ft.) as
questions.choice_b: The storms in Colorado, Kansas, and the storm NE of Memphis (MEM). Answer (B) is incorrect. The storms in Colorado, Kansas, and Oklahoma do not have such : extensive vertical development (tops of 38,000 ft.) as the storm
questions.choice_c: The isolated storm 50 miles northeast of NE of Memphis (MEM). Memphis (MEM).
questions.explanation: the Convective SIGMET listing has the potential of DISCUSSION: The isolated thunderstorm 50 mi. northeast of producing the most severe storm? MEM is a VIP level 5 (intense) storm. With a top above 45,000 ft., extensive vertical development is taking place. Answer (A) is incorrect. The storms in Texas and Oklahoma

questions.question_text: (Refer to Figure 148 below.) What time period
questions.choice_c: No more than 2 hours after the valid time. Answer (A) is incorrect. The outlook is for a period of 2 to 6 hr., not 24 hr., after the valid time. Answer (C) is incorrect. The outlook can cover up to 6 hr. after the valid time. MKCC WST 031755 CONVECTIVE SIGMET 42C VALID UNTIL 19552 TX OK FROM 5W MLC-PEQ-SJT-5W MLC AREA SCT EMBDD TSTMS MOVG LTL. TOPS 300. CONVECTIVE SIGMET 43C VALID UNTIL 19552 CO KS OK FROM AKO-OSW-30WNW OKC-AKO AREA SCT TSTMS OCNLY EMBDD MOVG FROM 3220. TOPS 380. CONVECTIVE SIGMET 44C VALID UNTIL 19552 5O0NE MEM ISOLD INSTD LVL5 TSTM DIAM 10 MOVG FROM 2625. TOP ABV 450. OUTLOOK VALID UNTIL 23552 TSTMS OVR TX AND SE OK WL MOV SEWD 15 KTS. TSTMS OVER CO, KS, AND N OK WL CONT MOVG SEWD 20 KTS. TSTM OVR TN WL CONT MOVG EWD 25 KTS. Figure 148. — Convective Sigmet.
questions.explanation: is covered by the outlook section of the Convective DISCUSSION: The outlook for a particular region is SIGMET? appended to the convective SIGMET. The outlook is a forecast and meteorological discussion for thunderstorm systems that are expected to require convective SIGMET issuances during a period 2 to 6 hr. after the valid time. Note that on Fig. 148 the outlook is good until 2355Z, which is 4 hr. after the valid time of 1955Z.

questions.question_text: A severe thunderstorm is one in which the surface wind is
questions.choice_a: 58 mph or greater and/or surface hail is 3/4 inch or more in diameter.
questions.choice_b: 50 knots or greater and/or surface hail is 1/2 inch or more in diameter.
questions.choice_c: 45 knots or greater and/or surface hail is 1 inch or more in diameter.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: A severe thunderstorm is one in which the surface winds are greater than or equal to 58 mph (50 kt.), hail at the surface is greater than or equal to 3/4 in. in diameter, or tornadoes are present. Answer (B) is incorrect. A severe thunderstorm is one in which the surface winds are 50 kt. or greater or hail at the surface is 3/4 in., not 1/2 in., or more in diameter. Answer (C) is incorrect. A severe thunderstorm is one in which the surface winds are 50 kt., not 45 kt., or greater or hail at the surface is 3/4 in., not 1 in., or more in diameter.

questions.question_text: When you hear a SIGMET on an ATC frequency forecasting severe icing conditions on the route to your destination, you plan for
questions.choice_a: the installed transport category airplane ice protection system protecting against all types and levels of icing as designed. very little airframe icing because of an OAT of -10°C or colder, the moisture is already frozen and cannot adhere to airplane surfaces. the possibility of freezing rain and freezing drizzle that can accumulate on and beyond the limits of any system.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Severe icing is defined by the rate of accumulation such that deicing/anti-icing equipment fails to reduce or control the hazard. Answer (A) is incorrect. The issuance of a severe icing SIGMET implies that ALL aircraft and their related deicing/ anti-icing equipment may not be sufficient to control the icing hazard. Answer (B) is incorrect. A SIGMET is an issued weather advisory. It locates an area of weather significant to the safety of all aircraft. If a SIGMET is issued that forecasts icing, plan for severe icing rather than very little airframe icing.

questions.question_text: SIGMETs may be issued for the contiguous U.S. and as convective SIGMETs for Alaska and Hawaii when squall lines are forecast. and as convective SIGMETs for Alaska when obscured thunderstorms are forecast. as convective SIGMETs when obscured thunderstorms are forecast.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Convective SIGMETs are issued for the conterminous U.S. (CONUS) instead of SIGMETs for thunderstorms. Any Convective SIGMET implies severe or greater turbulence, severe icing, and low-level wind shear. This includes the 48 states within the contiguous U.S. Answer (A) is incorrect. Conterminous and contiguous U.S. does not include Alaska and Hawaii. Answer (B) is incorrect. Obscured thunderstorms are covered by normal SIGMETs in Alaska, not convective SIGMETs.

questions.question_text: Convective SIGMETs are issued for a line of thunderstorms at least 100 miles long with severe thunderstorms affecting 50 percent of its length. 40 miles long with thunderstorms affecting 50 percent of its length. 60 miles long with thunderstorms affecting 40 percent of its length.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: A convective SIGMET will be issued when (1) a line of thunderstorms is at least 60 miles long with thunderstorms affecting at least 40% of its length or (2) an area of active thunderstorms is (a) affecting at least 3,000 square miles, (b) covering at least 40% of the area concerned, and (c) exhibiting a very strong radar reflectivity intensity or a significant satellite or lightning signature. Answer (A) is incorrect. A convective SIGMET will be issued when a line of thunderstorms is at least 60 miles long, not 100 miles long. Answer (B) is incorrect. A convective SIGMET will be issued when a line of thunderstorms is at least 60 miles long, not 40 miles long.

questions.question_text: Convective SIGMETs are issued for severe thunderstorms occurring for more than one hour of the valid period. occurring for more than 30 minutes of the valid period. affecting more than 1,000 square miles of airspace.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Convective SIGMETs are issued for embedded or severe thunderstorms expected to occur for more than 30 minutes during the valid period regardless of the size of the area. Answer (A) is incorrect. The time specified is for more than 30 minutes during the valid period. Answer (C) is incorrect. Convective SIGMETs are issued for an area of active thunderstorms affecting at least 3,000 square miles, not 1,000 square miles.

questions.question_text: A SIGMET is issued when weather affects
questions.choice_c: 5,000 square miles or more.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: A SIGMET may be issued in the contiguous U.S. when certain conditions are affecting or, in the judgment of the forecaster, are expected to affect an area of at least 3,000 square miles of an area judged to have a significant impact on the safety of aircraft operations. Answer (A) is incorrect. The correct answer is 3,000 square miles, not 1,000 square miles. Answer (C) is incorrect. A SIGMET may be issued in the contiguous U.S. when certain conditions are affecting or, in the judgment of the forecaster, are expected to affect an area of at least 3,000 square miles of an area, not 5,000 square miles or more.

questions.question_text: SIGMETs (other than domestic convective SIGMETs) may be valid for not more than
questions.choice_a: 4 hours for other phenomena and may be issued 2 hours before the valid time.
questions.choice_c: 6 hours for other phenomena.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: SIGMETs are issued for 6 hour periods for conditions associated with hurricanes and 4 hours for all other events. If conditions persist beyond the forecast period, the SIGMET is updated and reissued. Answer (A) is incorrect. An unscheduled SIGMET that is issued is valid at the time it is issued, not 2 hours before the valid time. Answer (C) is incorrect. SIGMETs may be valid for not more than 4 hours, not 6 hours.

questions.question_text: A SIGMET may be issued
questions.choice_a: for an area where conditions are deemed to have a significant effect on the safety of aircraft operations.
questions.choice_b: when expectations for dust storms or icing is predicted to be higher than 40% over a 1,000 square mile area.
questions.choice_c: for an area where conditions are likely to develop air turbulence above FL 310.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: A SIGMET may be issued when any of the specific conditions occur or are expected to occur in an area deemed to have a significant effect on the safety of aircraft operations. Answer (B) is incorrect. Two of the specific conditions for issuing a SIGMET are for severe icing not associated with thunderstorms and dust storms reducing visibility to 3 miles of less. However, there is no percentage or area requirement associated with this specific condition. Answer (C) is incorrect. SIGMETs are for hazardous weather to all aircraft, not necessarily about where conditions are likely to develop air turbulence above FL 310.

questions.question_text: The Mid-Level Significant weather (SIGWX) Chart is used to determine an overview of flying weather conditions between what altitudes?
questions.choice_c: 10,000 feet MSL and FL 450. 16.12 Flight Information Services Data Link (FISDL)
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Dispatchers and flight crew members can use the Mid-Level Significant Weather (SIGWX) Chart to determine an overview of selected flying weather conditions. The altitude areas for this chart are between 10,000 feet MSL and FL 450. This information can be used for flight planning and weather briefings before departure and during flight. Answer (A) is incorrect. The SIGWX Chart altitudes are in Mean Sea Level (MSL) and not Above Ground Level (AGL). FL 450 is not referred to as 45,000 feet. It is referred to as Flight Level (FL) at a specific barometric pressure. Answer (B) is incorrect. The altitude areas for the SIGWX Chart are in Mean Sea Level (MSL) and not Above Ground Level (AGL).

questions.question_text: The Federal Aviation Administration’s Flight Information Service Data Link (FISDL) is designed to provide data on a common frequency to flight crews from
questions.choice_a: 17,500 feet AGL down to 5,000 feet MSL.
questions.choice_b: 17,500 feet MSL down to 5,000 feet AGL.
questions.choice_c: 5,000 feet MSL to 17,500 feet MSL.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: The FAA’s Flight Information Service Data Link (FISDL) system is designed to provide coverage throughout the continental U.S. from 5,000 ft. AGL to 17,500 ft. MSL, except in those areas where this is unfeasible due to mountainous terrain. Answer (A) is incorrect. The FAA’s FISDL system is designed to provide coverage from 5,000 ft. AGL to 17,500 ft. MSL, not 5,000 ft. MSL to 17,500 ft. AGL. Answer (C) is incorrect. The FAA’s FISDL system is designed to provide coverage from 5,000 ft. AGL, not 5,000 ft. MSL, to 17,500 ft. MSL.

questions.subsection: 16.13 Terminal Weather Information for Pilot System (TWIP)
questions.question_text: The Federal Aviation Administration’s Flight Information Service Data Link (FISDL) provides what products?
questions.choice_c: Convective SIGMETs, PIREPs, AWWs, and NOTAMs.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: The FAA’s Flight Information Service Data Link (FISDL) provides, free of charge, METARs, SPEClIs, TAFs (and their amendments), SIGMETs, convective SIGMETs, AIRMETs, PIREPs (both urgent and routine), and AWWs (issued by the FAA or NWS). Answer (B) is incorrect. While the FAA’s FISDL does provide SPECls, SIGMETs, and AIRMETs, it does not provide NOTAM information. Answer (C) is incorrect. While the FAA’s FISDL does provide convective SIGMETs, PIREPs, and AWWs, it does not provide any information about NOTAMs.

questions.subsection: 16.13 Terminal Weather Information for Pilot System (TWIP)
questions.question_text: The Federal Aviation Administration’s Flight Information Service Data Link (FISDL) products, such as ground radar precipitation maps,
questions.choice_a: may be used instead of the aircraft radar.
questions.choice_b: are not appropriate for finding a path through a weather hazard area.
questions.choice_c: may be used to find a path through a weather hazard area. 16.13 Terminal Weather Information for Pilot System (TWIP)
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: FISDL products, such as ground-based radar precipitation maps, are not appropriate for use in tactical severe weather avoidance, such as negotiating a path through a weather hazard area. FISDL supports strategic weather decision making, such as route selection to avoid a weather hazard area in its entirety. Answer (A) is incorrect. FISDL products, such as ground- based radar precipitation maps, are not appropriate for use in tactical severe weather avoidance. Airborne weather radar is appropriate for this purpose under certain conditions. Answer (C) is incorrect. FISDL products, such as ground-based radar precipitation maps, are not appropriate for use in tactical severe weather avoidance, such as negotiating a path through a weather hazard area.

questions.subsection: 16.13 Terminal Weather Information for Pilot System (TWIP)
questions.question_text: What weather database can a dispatcher access concerning wind shear activity at an airport?
questions.choice_c: ATIS. END OF STUDY UNIT
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Dispatchers can access the TWIP database and send messages to specific aircraft whenever wind shear activity begins or ends at an airport. Answer (B) is incorrect. AWOS is an automated weather observation station used by pilots at uncontrolled airports or airports with low traffic volume. It is a recording, not a database. Answer (C) is incorrect. ATIS is an automatic terminal information service used by pilots entering controlled airspace. It is a recording, not a database.

questions.question_text: What is an important characteristic of wind shear?
questions.choice_a: \tis primarily associated with the lateral vortices generated by thunderstorms.
questions.choice_b: It usually exists only in the vicinity of thunderstorms, but may be found near a strong temperature inversion.
questions.choice_c: It may be associated with either a wind shift or a windspeed gradient at any level in the atmosphere.

questions.question_text: Which is a definition of “severe wind shear’?
questions.choice_a: Any rapid change of horizontal wind shear in excess of 25 knots; vertical shear excepted.
questions.choice_b: Any rapid change in wind direction or velocity Answer (A) is incorrect. A severe wind shear can occur with which causes airspeed changes greater than both horizontal and vertical shears. Answer (C) is incorrect. A 15 knots or vertical speed changes greater severe wind shear causes airspeed changes greater than 15 Kt., than 500 ft/min. Has ey Kt., or vertical speed changes greater than 500 ft/min., not min.
questions.choice_c: Any change of airspeed greater than 20 knots which is sustained for more than 20 seconds or vertical speed changes in excess of 100 ft/min.

questions.question_text: What information from the control tower is indicated by the following transmission? “SOUTH BOUNDARY WIND ONE SIX ZERO AT TWO FIVE, WEST BOUNDARY WIND TWO FOUR ZERO AT THREE FIVE.”
questions.choice_a: A downburst is located at the center of the airport.
questions.choice_b: Wake turbulence exists on the west side of the active runway.
questions.choice_c: There is a possibility of wind shear over or near the airport.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: The information for the transmission was provided by the Low-Level Wind Shear Alert System (LLWAS). This system detects the possibility of low-level wind shear over or near the airport. The system compares the wind measured around the airport with the wind measured at the center field location. When conditions exist, the tower controller will provide the site’s location and wind. Note that the transmission in the question indicates wind from different directions at the airport. Answer (A) is incorrect. A downburst is a vertical movement of air that is not measured by the LLWAS until it has horizontal movement. Also the wind direction is toward the center of the airport, not away from it. Answer (B) is incorrect. Wake turbulence does not produce wind. It is generated by an aircraft that is producing lift, which could be on either side of the active runway.

questions.question_text: What airport condition is reported by the tower when more than one wind condition at different positions on the airport is reported?
questions.choice_c: Frontal passage.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: When the tower reports more than one wind condition at different positions on the airport, it means that wind shear is probable. Various sensors of the Low-Level Wind Shear Alert System measure the wind, and when differences become excessive, the tower will provide more than one wind condition at different positions. Answer (A) is incorrect. Light and variable is used to report the wind conditions when wind speed is less than 5 kt. Answer (C) is incorrect. A frontal passage is normally indicated by a change in wind direction, but it is usually not reported by the tower.

questions.question_text: Which INITIAL cockpit indications should a pilot be aware of when a headwind shears to a calm wind?
questions.choice_a: Indicated airspeed decreases, aircraft pitches up, and altitude decreases.
questions.choice_b: Indicated airspeed increases, aircraft pitches down, and altitude increases.
questions.choice_c: Indicated airspeed decreases, aircraft pitches down, and altitude decreases.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: As the wind shears from a headwind to a calm wind, there is a quick reduction in the headwind component and a decrease in aircraft performance. The indications will be a decrease in indicated airspeed, a pitch down of the airplane’s nose, and a decrease in altitude. Answer (A) is incorrect. The aircraft will pitch down, not up, due to the relatively small angle of attack used during the headwind and the sudden decrease in the airflow over the wing when the wind shears to calm. Answer (B) is incorrect. Less power is required to maintain an indicated airspeed in a headwind than in calm air because of ram air; thus, a shear from a headwind to calm is indicated by a decrease in airspeed and a decrease, not an increase, in altitude.

questions.question_text: Which condition would INITIALLY cause the indicated airspeed and pitch to increase and the sink rate to decrease?
questions.choice_a: Sudden decrease in a headwind component.
questions.choice_b: Tailwind which suddenly increases in velocity.
questions.choice_c: Sudden increase in a headwind component.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: A sudden increase in a headwind component, i.e., a tailwind shearing to a headwind, is initially indicated by an increase in airspeed, pitch, and altitude. A sudden increase in a headwind component will increase aircraft performance. Answer (A) is incorrect. A sudden decrease in a headwind component decreases aircraft performance and is indicated by a decrease in airspeed, pitch, and altitude. Answer (B) is incorrect. An increase in tailwind velocity decreases performance and is indicated by a decrease in airspeed, pitch, and altitude.

questions.question_text: Which INITIAL cockpit indications should a pilot be aware of when a constant tailwind shears to a calm wind?
questions.choice_a: Altitude increases; pitch and indicated airspeed decrease.
questions.choice_b: Altitude, pitch, and indicated airspeed decrease.
questions.choice_c: Altitude, pitch, and indicated airspeed increase.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: When a constant tailwind shears to a calm wind, the shear increases indicated airspeed and thus increases performance. The airplane tends to pitch up and gain altitude. Answer (A) is incorrect. Pitch and indicated airspeed also increase. Answer (B) is incorrect. Altitude, pitch, and indicated airspeed decrease when a headwind, not tailwind, shears to a calm wind.

questions.question_text: Which wind-shear condition results in an increase in airspeed?
