Answer (A) is incorrect. Reevaluation is not a hazardous attitude in the ADM process. Answer (C) is incorrect. While peer pressure and stress levels may be factors contributing toward hazardous attitudes in the ADM process, they are not attitudes themselves.

questions.study_unit_name: Aeromedical Factors and Aeronautical Decision Making (ADM)
questions.subsection: 18.7 Human Factors and Aeronautical Decision Making (ADM)
questions.question_text: What should a pilot do when recognizing a thought as hazardous?
questions.choice_a: Correct this hazardous thought by making a thorough risk assessment.
questions.choice_b: Label the thought as hazardous and then correct that thought by stating the corresponding antidote.
questions.choice_c: Avoid allowing this hazardous thought to develop.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: When you recognize a hazardous thought, you should label it as hazardous; then correct the attitude by stating the corresponding antidote. Antidotes should be memorized for each of the hazardous attitudes so that they automatically come to mind when needed. Answer (A) is incorrect. When you recognize a hazardous thought, you should label it as hazardous and then correct the attitude by stating the corresponding antidote, not by making a thorough risk assessment. Answer (C) is incorrect. While you do not want the hazardous thought to develop, the way to avoid this is to recognize and label the thought as hazardous and then correct it by stating the corresponding antidote.

questions.study_unit_name: Aeromedical Factors and Aeronautical Decision Making (ADM)
questions.subsection: 18.7 Human Factors and Aeronautical Decision Making (ADM)
questions.question_text: In the aeronautical decision making (ADM) process, what is the first step in neutralizing a hazardous attitude?
questions.choice_b: Recognizing the invulnerability of the situation.
questions.choice_c: Making a rational judgment.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Hazardous attitudes, which contribute to poor pilot judgment, can be effectively counteracted by redirecting that hazardous attitude so that appropriate action can be taken. Recognition of hazardous thoughts is the first step in neutralizing them in the ADM process. Answer (B) is incorrect. Invulnerability is a hazardous attitude. The first step in neutralizing a hazardous attitude is to recognize it. Answer (C) is incorrect. Before a rational judgment can be made, the hazardous attitude must be recognized and then redirected so that appropriate action can be taken.

questions.study_unit_name: Aeromedical Factors and Aeronautical Decision Making (ADM)
questions.subsection: 18.7 Human Factors and Aeronautical Decision Making (ADM)
questions.question_text: Hazardous attitudes which contribute to poor pilot judgment can be effectively counteracted by
questions.choice_b: taking meaningful steps to be more assertive with attitudes.
questions.choice_c: early recognition of these hazardous attitudes.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Hazardous attitudes, which contribute to poor pilot judgment, can be effectively counteracted by redirecting them so that appropriate action can be taken. When you recognize a hazardous thought, you should label it as hazardous; then correct the attitude by stating the corresponding antidote. Answer (B) is incorrect. You should respond to a hazardous attitude with an antidote, not with steps to be more assertive with attitudes. Answer (C) is incorrect. While the first step in neutralizing a hazardous attitude is recognition, to counteract the attitude you must state the appropriate antidote.

questions.study_unit_name: Aeromedical Factors and Aeronautical Decision Making (ADM)
questions.subsection: 18.7 Human Factors and Aeronautical Decision Making (ADM)
questions.question_text: What is the antidote for a pilot with a “macho” attitude?
questions.choice_a: I’m not helpless. | can make a difference.
questions.choice_b: Follow the rules. They are usually right.
questions.choice_c: Taking chances is foolish.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Pilots exhibiting a macho attitude will try to prove themselves by taking risks in order to impress others. When this hazardous attitude is recognized, the pilot should state the antidote, “Taking chances is foolish.” Answer (A) is incorrect. The antidote for a resignation, not a macho, attitude is, “I’m not helpless. | can make a difference.” Answer (B) is incorrect. The antidote for an antiauthority, not a macho, attitude is, “Follow the rules. They are usually right.”

questions.study_unit_name: Aeromedical Factors and Aeronautical Decision Making (ADM)
questions.subsection: 18.7 Human Factors and Aeronautical Decision Making (ADM)
questions.question_text: Success in reducing stress associated with a crisis in the cockpit begins with
questions.choice_a: eliminating the more serious life and cockpit stress issues.
questions.choice_b: knowing the exact cause of the stress.
questions.choice_c: assessing stress areas in one’s personal life.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: If you hope to succeed in reducing stress associated with crisis management in the cockpit, it is essential to begin by making a personal assessment of stress in all areas of your life. Answer (A) is incorrect. In order to eliminate the more serious life and cockpit stress issues, you must first make an assessment of stress in all areas of your life to identify those stressors. Answer (B) is incorrect. In order to know the exact cause of the stress, you must first make an assessment of stress 2 all areas of your life to identify stressors and the causes of them.

questions.study_unit_name: Aeromedical Factors and Aeronautical Decision Making (ADM)
questions.subsection: 18.7 Human Factors and Aeronautical Decision Making (ADM)
questions.question_text: An air carrier aircraft flown into the ground while the crew is troubleshooting a landing gear fault is an example of
questions.choice_c: lack of aviation experience.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: This question describes Eastern Airlines Flight 401, which crashed into the Everglades causing 101 fatalities. The crew was working a landing gear problem and apparently lost situational awareness. That crash, while tragic, proves that even experienced crews can fall prey to a loss of situational awareness if they are not vigilant and do not take precautions to prevent that possibility. Answer (A) is incorrect. Neglect and reliance on memory would not cause a pilot or crew troubleshooting a landing gear fault to fly into the ground. Loss of situational awareness could and has resulted in that particular scenario, however. Answer (C) is incorrect. Lack of experience does not in and of itself cause pilots or crew members to fly into the ground while troubleshooting a landing gear or other system fault. Loss of situational awareness could and has resulted in that particular scenario, however, even when the pilots involved were experienced.

questions.study_unit_name: Aeromedical Factors and Aeronautical Decision Making (ADM)
questions.subsection: 18.7 Human Factors and Aeronautical Decision Making (ADM)
questions.question_text: To help manage cockpit stress, a pilot should think of life stress situations that are similar to those in flying. try to relax and think rationally at the first sign of stress. avoid situations that will degrade the ability to handle cockpit responsibilities.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: To help manage cockpit stress, you must condition (teach) yourself to relax and think rationally at the first sign of stress. Answer (A) is incorrect. Many of the stress coping techniques practiced for life stress management are not practical in flight. Answer (C) is incorrect. Avoiding situations that will degrade a pilot’s ability to handle cockpit responsibilities is considered a part of risk management, not cockpit stress management.

questions.study_unit_name: Aeromedical Factors and Aeronautical Decision Making (ADM)
questions.subsection: 18.7 Human Factors and Aeronautical Decision Making (ADM)
questions.question_text: The DECIDE process consists of six elements to help provide a pilot a logical way of approaching aeronautical decision making. These elements are to estimate, determine, choose, identify, detect, and evaluate. determine, evaluate, choose, identify, do, and eliminate. detect, estimate, choose, identify, do, and evaluate.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: The DECIDE model, comprised of six elements, is intended to provide a pilot with a logical way of approaching decision making. These six elements, using the acronym DECIDE, are detect, estimate, choose, identify, do, and evaluate. Answer (A) is incorrect. One of the elements of the DECIDE process is “do,” not “determine.” Answer (B) is incorrect. Two of the elements of the DECIDE process are “detect,” not “determine,” and “estimate,” not “eliminate.”

questions.study_unit_name: Aeromedical Factors and Aeronautical Decision Making (ADM)
questions.subsection: 18.7 Human Factors and Aeronautical Decision Making (ADM)
questions.question_text: When a recently certificated pilot decides to not wait any longer for the fog and low ceilings to lift, this pilot may be exhibiting the hazardous resigned attitude. macho attitude. impulsive attitude.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: A pilot waiting for weather to improve, who then decides, “I can do it,” without any sign of improving conditions, is exhibiting the macho attitude. Although this is commonly thought of as a male trait, women pilots can also fall prey to the macho attitude. Answer (A) is incorrect. Resignation is characterized by a pilot who gives up and displays an attitude that suggests (s)he has bad luck, or that someone is plotting against them. Answer (C) is incorrect. An impulsive pilot tends to do the first thing that comes to mind. While impulsivity could be an answer, it is not as accurate as the “I can do it” attitude of the macho pilot.

questions.study_unit_name: Aeromedical Factors and Aeronautical Decision Making (ADM)
questions.subsection: 18.7 Human Factors and Aeronautical Decision Making (ADM)
questions.question_text: An air carrier crew fixated on completing the last flight of a four day trip often may exhibit get-there-itis. staged decision-making. CG: naturalistic decision-making.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: The desire to satisfy a specific personal goal, like getting home on time after a four-day trip, is known as get- home-itis. This fixation with meeting a specific goal impairs pilot judgment, sometimes causing them to disregard any alternative course of action. Answer (B) is incorrect. The described scenario suggests get-there-itis, the desire to achieve a specific goal regardless of outside influences and conditions. It does not describe a staged decision-making process. Answer (C) is incorrect. The naturalistic decision-making process is a methodical approach to decision-making that includes analysis of the pilot, aircraft, environment, and external factors.

questions.study_unit_name: Aeromedical Factors and Aeronautical Decision Making (ADM)
questions.subsection: 18.7 Human Factors and Aeronautical Decision Making (ADM)
questions.question_text: Automatic Decision-Making is a reflexive type of decision-making. an impulsive type of decision-making. an internalized type of decision-making.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Automatic decision-making is a reflexive process that is anchored in training and experience. It is most often used in emergencies when there is no time to practice more analytical decision-making alternatives. Answer (B) is incorrect. Automatic decision-making is not impulsive or rash. While the process works quickly, it requires the pilot to correlate the current situation to similar situations experienced previously in order to choose an appropriate course of action. Answer (C) is incorrect. Automatic decision-making is reflexive in nature, not internalized. The automatic decision- making process requires the pilot to correlate the current situation to similar situations experienced previously in order to choose an appropriate course of action.

questions.study_unit_name: Aeromedical Factors and Aeronautical Decision Making (ADM)
questions.subsection: 18.7 Human Factors and Aeronautical Decision Making (ADM)
questions.question_text: When a pilot believes advanced avionics enable operations closer to personal or environmental limits,
questions.choice_a: greater utilization of the aircraft is achieved.
questions.choice_c: risk is decreased.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Pilots should not be lulled into a false sense of security simply because they have advanced avionics. Judgment and aeronautical decision making serve as the bridge between technology and safety. Any flight you make involves almost infinite combinations of pilot skill, experience, condition, and proficiency; aircraft equipment and performance; environmental conditions; and external influences. Both individually and in combination, these factors can compress the safety buffer provided by your baseline personal minimums. _ Consequently, you need a practical way to adjust your baseline personal minimums to accommodate specific conditions. Answer (A) is incorrect. While one may be able to use an aircraft in more challenging environments, the risks involved with such flights also increase. Therefore, the best answer choice is that risk is increased when a pilot believes advanced avionics enable operations closer to personal or environmental limits. Answer (C) is incorrect. Risk is increased, not decreased, when a pilot believes advanced avionics enable operations closer to personal or environmental limits.

questions.study_unit_name: Aeromedical Factors and Aeronautical Decision Making (ADM)
questions.subsection: 18.7 Human Factors and Aeronautical Decision Making (ADM)
questions.question_text: Automation in aircraft has proven
questions.choice_a: to present new hazards in its limitations.
questions.choice_c: effective in preventing accidents.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Most of the aviation community believes automation has made flying safer, but there is a fear that pilots fail to see that automation is a double-edged sword. Pilots need to understand the advantages of automation while being aware of its limitations. Experience has shown that automated systems can make some errors more evident while sometimes hiding other errors or making them less obvious. When passively monitoring an automated system for faults, abnormalities, or other infrequent events, humans perform poorly. The more reliable the system is, the worse the human performance becomes. It is a paradox of automation that technically advanced avionics can both increase and decrease pilot awareness. Answer (B) is incorrect. Automation is not necessarily flawless. Proper use of checklists and systematic training should be used to control common error-prone tasks and notice errors from automation before they become a threat to safety of flight. Answer (C) is incorrect. While automation has prevented accidents, it can also cause accidents from complacency. Therefore, it is not the best answer choice. When passively monitoring an automated system for faults, abnormalities, or other infrequent events, humans perform poorly. The more reliable the system is, the worse the human performance becomes. It is a paradox of automation that technically advanced avionics can both increase and decrease pilot awareness.

questions.study_unit_name: Aeromedical Factors and Aeronautical Decision Making (ADM)
questions.subsection: 18.7 Human Factors and Aeronautical Decision Making (ADM)
questions.question_text: A pilot’s attitude
questions.choice_a: has no effect on aviation decisions.
questions.choice_c: has no effect on pilot perceptions.
questions.explanation: DISCUSSION: Hazardous attitudes contribute to poor pilot judgment but can be effectively counteracted by redirecting the hazardous attitude so that correct action can be taken. After recognition as hazardous, the pilot should label it as hazardous, then state the corresponding antidote. Answer (A) is incorrect. Attitude has an effect on every aviation decision, whether hazardous or not. Answer (C) is incorrect. After recognition as hazardous, the pilot should label it as hazardous, then state the corresponding antidote. If this does not happen, the results could be catastrophic. If a pilot does not perceive a hazardous attitude as hazardous and takes corrective eee the effects of that pilot’s aviation decisions will always be unsafe.

questions.study_unit_name: Aeromedical Factors and Aeronautical Decision Making (ADM)
questions.subsection: 18.7 Human Factors and Aeronautical Decision Making (ADM)
questions.question_text: When a pilot fixates on the original goal or destination is
questions.choice_c: naturalistic decision-making.
questions.explanation: __ DISCUSSION: The definition of “get-there-itis” is the disposition that impairs pilot judgment through a fixation on the original goal or destination combined with a disregard for any alternative course of action. _ Answer (B) is incorrect. With get-there-itis, there is no thinking at all, analytical or other. It is “I have to get there, at this time, no matter what.” Answer (C) is incorrect. There is basically no decision-making process within “get-there-itis.”
